We used two different sets of X-ray images of the lungs in addition to two different sets of CT scans and the classification is done using a variety of networks that have been pre-trained such as VGG (16, 19), Densenet (121), Resnet (50, 50 V2, 101 V2), Mobile net (V2), Xception Inception (V3, Resnet V2), Efficient net (B0) and Nasnet (Large). On the X-ray and CT image datasets, Resnet and VGG architecture have shown the ability to properly differentiate COVID-19 from normal images, with an average accuracy of 95 to 98 percent respectively. Our acquired results on the classification datasets are competitive and superior to previously reported findings in the literature.

</details>


### [25] [Do Vision--Language Models Understand 3D Scenes or Just Catalogue Objects?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20448)
*Animesh Maheshwari,Divyansh Sahu,Nishit Verma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A large human-curated benchmark reveals a sharp dissociation in vision–language models between 3D spatial understanding and object-name grounding: models excel at planning rearrangements over visible layouts but perform poorly on depth-ordered occlusion and optical-geometry from reflections; white-box analysis implicates visual-token merger as the bottleneck in preserving spatial information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically evaluate whether vision–language models encode and reason about 3D scene layout beyond object naming, addressing a gap in embodied spatial understanding critical for manipulation and interaction tasks.

Method: A 3,034-sample benchmark targets depth-ordered occlusion, optical-geometry inference from reflections, and volumetric rearrangement planning. Six frontier/open-weight VLMs are evaluated via 18,204 annotator-provided responses (no LLM-as-judge). Occlusion is probed with three counterfactual operationalisations; reflections and volumetric rearrangement are assessed. An embodied-reasoning model is included. White-box analysis on Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking localizes failures to the visual-token merger.

Result: Across models, planning rearrangements over visible layouts achieves 53–97% accuracy with few collision violations, while occlusion reasoning reaches 6–45% and reflection reasoning under 7%. The embodied-reasoning model matches this profile. White-box analysis shows spatial information is lost after visual token compression and only recovered when post-merger activations are injected into the language decoder.

Conclusion: The bottleneck lies in the visual tokenizer/merger stage, where spatial information becomes inaccessible post-encoding and only reappears with clean post-merger activations. This uncovers a specific failure point in current VLMs that undermines robust 3D spatial understanding and embodied reasoning; improvements should target preserving spatial detail through token merging and decoder integration.

Abstract: Vision--language models reliably name objects in a scene, but do they represent the 3D layout those objects inhabit? We introduce a 3,034-sample human-curated benchmark targeting three components of spatial understanding: depth-ordered occlusion (probed via three independent counterfactual operationalisations), optical-geometry inference over visible reflections, and volumetric rearrangement planning. Six frontier and open-weight VLMs, scored by trained annotators on 18,204 responses with no LLM-as-judge, reveal a sharp dissociation: models that plan rearrangements over visible layouts at 53--97% accuracy and rarely violate collision constraints fall to 6--45% on occlusion and below 7% on reflections. An embodied-reasoning model reproduces the same profile. White-box analysis on Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking localises the failure to the visual-token merger: spatial information recoverable throughout the vision encoder becomes inaccessible after token compression and only stabilises again when clean post-merger activations are patched into the language decoder.

</details>


### [26] [ELEMENT: Multi-Modal Retinal Vessel Segmentation Based on a Coupled Region Growing and Machine Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20458)
*Erick O. Rodrigues,Aura Conci,Panos Liatsis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-modal retinal vessel segmentation framework (ELEMENT) leveraging grey-level and connectivity features with region-growing and ML, achieving high accuracy across DRIVE, STARE, CHASE-DB, VAMPIRE FA, IOSTAR SLO, and RC-SLO datasets, and outperforming state-of-the-art methods including deep learning models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retinal vessel segmentation is labor-intensive and error-prone when done manually or interactively; a fast, consistent, and accurate automated method across multiple imaging modalities is needed to aid diagnosis of ocular diseases.

Method: Feature extraction capturing grey-level information and vessel connectivity, integrated into a pixel-based classifier that uses region growing and machine learning; connectivity information is propagated across pixels during classification; supports multi-modal inputs (fundus photography, SLO, and FA).

Result: On DRIVE dataset: overall accuracy 97.40%, outperforming 25 of 26 state-of-the-art approaches (including six deep-learning methods). On STARE: 98.27%; CHASE-DB: 97.78%; VAMPIRE FA: 98.34%; IOSTAR SLO: 98.04%; RC-SLO: 98.35%—outperforming all reported methods.

Conclusion: ELEMENT reduces segmentation inconsistencies and increases throughput, delivering robust, multi-modal retinal vessel segmentation with strong cross-dataset performance and potential for clinical deployment.

Abstract: Vascular structures in the retina contain important information for the detection and analysis of ocular diseases, including age-related macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy and glaucoma. Commonly used modalities in diagnosis of these diseases are fundus photography, scanning laser ophthalmoscope (SLO) and fluorescein angiography (FA). Typically, retinal vessel segmentation is carried out either manually or interactively, which makes it time consuming and prone to human errors. In this research, we propose a new multi-modal framework for vessel segmentation called ELEMENT (vEsseL sEgmentation using Machine lEarning and coNnecTivity). This framework consists of feature extraction and pixel-based classification using region growing and machine learning. The proposed features capture complementary evidence based on grey level and vessel connectivity properties. The latter information is seamlessly propagated through the pixels at the classification phase. ELEMENT reduces inconsistencies and speeds up the segmentation throughput. We analyze and compare the performance of the proposed approach against state-of-the-art vessel segmentation algorithms in three major groups of experiments, for each of the ocular modalities. Our method produced higher overall performance, with an overall accuracy of 97.40%, compared to 25 of the 26 state-of-the-art approaches, including six works based on deep learning, evaluated on the widely known DRIVE fundus image dataset. In the case of the STARE, CHASE-DB, VAMPIRE FA, IOSTAR SLO and RC-SLO datasets, the proposed framework outperformed all of the state-of-the-art methods with accuracies of 98.27%, 97.78%, 98.34%, 98.04% and 98.35%, respectively.

</details>


### [27] [Pixel Wised Lesion Prediction on COVID-19 CT Imagery: A Comparative Analysis of Automated Image Segmentation Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20459)
*Sarmad Khan,Arslan Shaukat,Umer Asgher,Basim Azam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A benchmarking study compares four segmentation architectures (Unet, PSPNet, Linknet, FPN) with six pretrained encoders (VGG19, DenseNet121, Inception-ResNet-V2, MobileNet V2, SE-ResNet-101, EfficientNet B0) across three COVID-19 CT datasets for binary and multi-class segmentation, reporting up to 98% F1 in binary and 75–77% in multi-class. 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of standardized performance analysis and dataset heterogeneity in deep-learning-based medical image segmentation, providing a reference framework for segmentation in COVID-19 and other imaging scenarios.

Method: Systematic evaluation of four DL architectures using six pretrained encoders; experiments on binary and multi-class segmentation across three COVID-19 CT datasets; performance measured by F1-score.

Result: The study reports accurate segmentation with DL models; best binary F1 = 0.98; multi-class F1 = 0.75–0.77 on two datasets, indicating strong performance when using pretrained backbones across architectures.

Conclusion: Proposes a reproducible benchmarking approach that can guide COVID-19 CT segmentation and, more broadly, segmentation tasks in other imaging contexts, highlighting the value of combining diverse architectures with pretrained encoders.

Abstract: In recent years, there has been a notable increase in the level of attention that is given to algorithms based on deep learning in the context of medical image segmentation. Nevertheless, the reliability of the field has been hindered due to the absence of a standardized methodology for performance analysis and the utilization of different datasets in previous research. The primary objective of the research is to comprehensively evaluate contemporary segmentation frameworks combined with state-of-the-art pre-trained backbones in order to accurately predict COVID-19 lesions in CT images. Moreover, this evaluation can serve as a point of reference for the segmentation of images in various other imaging scenarios. In order to accomplish this, we integrate four distinct deep learning architectures, namely Unet, PSPNet, Linknet, and FPN, with six pre-trained encoders, including VGG 19, DenseNet 121, Inception ResNet V2, MobileNet V2, SeresNet 101, and EfficientNet B0. This approach enables the development of diverse testing architectures. In the context of image segmentation, our research encompassed both binary and multi-class experimentation. The findings derived from our analysis of three distinct COVID-19 CT segmentation datasets indicate that deep learning architectures yield precise and efficient segmentation outcomes. Significantly, a maximum F1-Score of 98% was attained for binary class segmentation, while multi-class segmentation yielded F1-Scores of 75% and 77% across two separate datasets. The utilization of artificial intelligence and deep learning enhances the diagnostic process for pandemic diseases across multiple dimensions.

</details>


### [28] [Understanding Model Behavior in Monocular Polyp Sizing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20461)
*Xinqi Xiong,Andrea Dunn Beltran,Junmyeong Choi,Sarah K. McGill,Marc Niethammer,Roni Sengupta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A cross-dataset audit of binary polyp size classification (<=5 mm vs >5 mm) across model families and input modalities finds consistent performance driven by non-metric cues tied to examination behavior, with limited gains from scale information and segmentation robustness under distribution shift as key bottlenecks; the work provides reusable evaluation tools and public code for auditing polyp sizing pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate polyp sizing is critical for appropriate surveillance decisions, but monocular colonoscopy provides no reliable metric reference. Understanding how current models fare across datasets and modalities, and identifying bottlenecks, is essential for improving true-scale reliability.

Method: A diagnostic audit across multiple public multi-center datasets, diverse model families, and patient-stratified cross-validation. Evaluates RGB appearance, relative depth, and photometric modalities. Introduces ground-truth scale at varying granularities to quantify gains from perfect scale information. Assesses the impact of segmentation under distribution shift and uses oracle-scale experiments on predicted masks. Provides evaluation tools (oracle scale ladders, shortcut partitions, mask substitution) and releases code.

Result: Model performance is moderately consistent across architectures and modalities, suggesting reliance on cues correlated with examination behavior rather than true metric scales. Ground-truth scale information yields limited improvements; current depth estimation and global calibration offer minimal gains. Segmentation errors under distribution shift erase much of the potential gains, with oracle scale under predicted masks recovering only baseline performance. Two independent bottlenecks identified: metric scale and mask robustness.

Conclusion: Metric scale accuracy and mask robustness are the primary bottlenecks in polyp sizing pipelines. The work offers reusable auditing tools and publicly available code to benchmark future approaches, guiding research toward true-scale reliability and robust segmentation under distribution shift.

Abstract: Accurate polyp size stratification guides surveillance decisions, with lesions larger than 5 mm typically requiring closer follow-up. However, monocular colonoscopy lacks a reliable metric reference. We present a diagnostic audit of binary polyp size classification (<=5 mm vs. >5 mm) across multiple public multi-center datasets, model families, and patient-stratified cross-validation. Across architectures and input modalities, including RGB appearance, relative depth, and photometry, model performance is moderately consistent, suggesting reliance on cues correlated with examination behavior rather than true metric scales. By providing ground-truth scale at varying granularities, we quantify the potential improvement from perfect scale information and show that current depth estimation and global calibration offer limited gains. We further demonstrate that segmentation errors under distribution shift eliminate most of this potential, with oracle scale under predicted masks recovering only baseline performance. These results highlight metric scale and mask robustness as two independent bottlenecks and provide reusable evaluation tools such as oracle scale ladders, shortcut partitions, and mask substitution for auditing future polyp sizing pipelines. Our code is publicly accessible at https://github.com/anaxqx/polyp-sizing-audit.

</details>


### [29] [HalluCXR: Benchmarking and Mitigating Hallucinations in Medical Vision-Language Models for Chest Radiograph Interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20469)
*Haoyu Wang,Zitong Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HalluCXR benchmarks six diverse vision-language models for chest X-ray interpretation across 856 MIMIC-CXR images, using an 8-category hallucination taxonomy and a two-layer detector; finds high hallucination rates, risk patterns, and shows ensembles reduce fabrication at cost of omissions; supports auditing and ensemble safety for clinical deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify and mitigate hallucinations (clinically dangerous errors) in vision-language models used for radiology, to establish benchmarks and safety protocols for clinical deployment.

Method: Dataset: 856 stratified MIMIC-CXR chest radiographs; six architecturally diverse VLMs; three query types; 15,408 evaluations. An eight-category hallucination taxonomy with clinical severity ratings. A two-layer detection pipeline validated against 250 human annotations (auto-detection F1=0.959; LLM judge F1=0.907).

Result: 61.9–82.3% of outputs contain hallucinations; up to 80.2% of errors are clinically dangerous. Three patterns: normal radiographs attract severe hallucinations; common findings over-fabricated; rare findings under-detected. Response length predicts hallucination risk (AUC up to 0.908). A six-model ensemble reduces fabrication by up to 84.8% at the cost of increased omissions; a three-model subset maintains comparable performance at half the cost.

Conclusion: Hallucination auditing, verbosity-based risk monitoring, and ensemble-based safety layers are prerequisites for clinical deployment of VLMs in radiology.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used for medical image interpretation, yet they frequently hallucinate, generating clinically plausible but factually incorrect findings that pose direct patient safety risks. We introduce HalluCXR, a benchmark evaluating six architecturally diverse VLMs across 856 stratified MIMIC-CXR chest radiographs and three query types, yielding 15,408 model evaluations. An eight-category hallucination taxonomy with clinical severity ratings and a two-layer detection pipeline are validated against 250 human annotations (auto-detection F1=0.959; LLM judge F1=0.907). We find that 61.9--82.3% of outputs contain hallucinations, with clinically dangerous errors in up to 80.2%. Three key patterns emerge: normal radiographs paradoxically attract the most severe hallucinations, common findings are systematically over-fabricated while rare findings go under-detected, and response length alone predicts hallucination risk (AUC up to 0.908). A six-model ensemble reduces fabrication by up to 84.8% at the cost of increased omission; a three-model subset retains comparable performance at half the cost. These results establish that hallucination auditing, verbosity-based risk monitoring, and ensemble-based safety layers are prerequisites for clinical deployment.

</details>


### [30] [EPC-3D-Diff: Equivariant Physics Consistent Conditional 3D Latent Diffusion for CBCT to CT Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20470)
*Alzahra Altalib,Chunhui Li,Haytham Al Ewaidat,Khaled Alawneh,Ahmad Qendel,Alessandro Perelli*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Physics-informed latent diffusion for CBCT→CT synthesis with projection-domain equivariance; achieves substantial PSNR/SSIM gains and better HU accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve HU accuracy and robustness of CBCT-based radiotherapy planning by correcting scatter/noise/artifacts using acquisition physics.

Method: 3D conditional latent diffusion in a compact latent space learned by a lightweight autoencoder; introduces a projection-domain equivariance loss: in-plane rotation of the volume corresponds to an angular shift in the projections; training enforces forward projections of rotated synthesized CT to match angle-shifted projections of target CT; evaluated with single/mixed domain training, ablations, and comparisons to diffusion and CycleGAN; uses paired phantom and clinical CBCT/CT data.

Result: Significant gains: +7.4 dB PSNR on phantom data and +1.8 dB PSNR on clinical data over state-of-the-art methods; improved SSIM and HU accuracy within tissue boundaries; demonstrated generalization and robustness; improved physics-consistency.

Conclusion: EPC-3D-Diff delivers physics-informed, robust CBCT→CT synthesis with improved HU fidelity, suitable for downstream radiotherapy workflows.

Abstract: Cone-beam CT (CBCT) is routinely acquired during radiotherapy for patient setup, but its quantitative reliability is degraded by scatter, noise, and reconstruction artifacts, limiting Hounsfield Unit (HU) accuracy. We propose EPC-3D-Diff, a novel conditional 3D latent diffusion framework for volumetric CBCT to CT synthesis that introduces a projection domain equivariance loss derived from acquisition physics. Unlike common image domain equivariance, we exploit the fact that an in plane rotation of the volume corresponds to an angular shift in its projections. During training, we enforce this relationship by forward projecting rotated synthesized CT volumes and matching them to appropriately angle shifted projections of the paired target CT, yielding a physics consistent equivariance constraint integrated into the diffusion objective. To capture full 3D context efficiently, conditional diffusion is performed in a compact latent space learnt by a lightweight 3D autoencoder, preserving axial depth while downsampling in plane resolution for stable training. We validate on a paired head CBCT/CT phantom dataset, including repeat scans, and paired clinical data using patient wise splits, and perform single and mixed domain training, ablations, and comparisons with diffusion and CycleGAN. EPC-3D-Diff generalizes well and achieved substantial improvements, +7.4 dB (phantom) and +1.8 dB (clinical data) in PSNR compared to state of the art methods, alongside improved SSIM and HU accuracy, within tissue boundaries. Overall, EPC-3D-Diff improves robustness and physics consistency, supporting HU aware synthesis for downstream radiotherapy workflows.

</details>


### [31] [Goodbye Drift: Anchored Tree Sampling for Long-Horizon Video-to-Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20476)
*Matthew Bendel,Stephen W. Bailey,Mithilesh Vaidya,Sumukh Badam,Xingzhe He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Anchored Tree Sampling (ATS), a training-free inference-time scheduler for long-horizon V2V generation that converts horizon drift into anchor-bounded drift by sparse-to-dense, tree-structured imputation, reducing causal steps and improving quality and drift stability without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon video generation suffers quality drift and continuity issues. Existing autoregressive methods tackle both but require training or complex inference; ATS aims to directly tackle drift via a scheduler that preserves anchors across the horizon.

Method: Anchored Tree Sampling (ATS): root call emits sparse anchors over the full horizon; recursive refinement generates intermediate anchors; final leaves synthesize between neighboring anchors. This creates a tree of anchor spans, reducing the critical path from K sequential steps to L+1 steps and enforcing anchor-bounded drift. Evaluated in static-camera V2V across five conditioning modalities (inpainting, outpainting, edge, pose, depth) on Wan 2.1 + VACE, plus long-horizon generation on LTX-2.3. No retraining required for the base model.

Result: ATS outperforms two autoregressive baselines in overall video quality and drift prevention across five modalities; demonstrates stable generation for 40+ minutes on LTX-2.3 across modalities.

Conclusion: Proposes extending ATS to arbitrarily long T2V generation and to dynamic-camera and multi-shot regimes, suggesting a path forward for longer, more flexible V2V generation without retraining.

Abstract: Long-horizon video generation suffers from two intertwined issues. First, there is drift, where video quality degrades over time. Second, there are continuity issues which manifest as object permanence issues, or improperly rendering transient content (e.g., an object that appears in non-consecutive frames changing color/style). Recent work has focused on autoregressive distillation techniques that attack both problems simultaneously. We instead choose to focus on drift directly and introduce \textbf{Anchored Tree Sampling (ATS)}: a training-free inference-time scheduler that replaces left-to-right rollout with sparse-to-dense, anchor-bounded imputation organized as a tree. A root call produces sparse anchors over the full horizon, recursive refinement generates intermediate anchors, and final leaf spans are synthesized between neighboring anchors. This reduces the critical path from $K$ sequential rollout steps to $L+1$ tree-hierarchical steps and converts horizon-compounding drift into anchor-bounded drift. We focus on V2V generation in the \emph{static-camera} regime, where sparse anchors over the horizon are well approximated by the dense conditioning signal, and the base model can produce them without retraining. We evaluate ATS against two contemporary autoregressive baselines on Wan $2.1$ $+$ VACE, across five conditioning modalities (inpainting, outpainting, edge, pose, depth). We show that ATS outperforms both competitors in overall quality, as well as in drift prevention. We additionally demonstrate stable $\geq 40$-minute generation on LTX-$2.3$ across the same five modalities. We conclude by proposing a path forward to extend ATS to arbitrarily long T2V generation, as well as the dynamic-camera and multi-shot regimes.

</details>


### [32] [Oracle Supervision Transfers for Hyperparameter Prediction in Model-Based Image Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20479)
*Jianmin Liao,Lixin Shen,Yuesheng Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HyperDn: a cross-configuration hyperparameter predictor for denoisers that transfers oracle supervision across source configurations to target configurations, enabling few- or zero-shot adaptation with near-oracle performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperparameter tuning for model-based image denoisers is costly because it requires oracle-labeled data for each configuration. A transfer-capable predictor could reuse supervision across configurations to reduce labeling burden.

Method: HyperDn is a single configuration-conditioned predictor that pools oracle supervision from source configurations and outputs hyperparameters for new denoiser–noise configurations. The authors test cross-paradigm transfer (TV/TGV variational methods to diffusion-based DiffPIR) and evaluate with very few target labels (as few as 2) and even zero-shot settings.

Result: With only 2 target oracle labels, HyperDn achieves 30.23 dB, within 0.90 dB of the oracle, and outperforms a 64-label-per-config baseline trained from scratch using 1/32 as many target labels. In zero-shot settings, it attains near-oracle PSNR on unseen mixtures and transfers from 96×96 source images to 512×768 targets without target labels.

Conclusion: Expensive oracle supervision for hyperparameter prediction can be transferred across configurations, significantly reducing the need to collect new target labels for each denoising configuration.

Abstract: Hyperparameter prediction is a critical practical bottleneck for model-based image denoisers, ranging from classical TV/TGV variational solvers to modern diffusion-based models such as DiffPIR. While existing learned predictors can achieve near-oracle performance, this approach scales poorly: each new configuration conventionally requires its own oracle-labeled training set, and each label requires a hierarchical grid search evaluated against clean ground truth. We therefore ask whether oracle supervision collected on source configurations can transfer to target configurations with few or no target oracle labels. We propose HyperDn, a single configuration-conditioned predictor that pools oracle supervision across source configurations and predicts heterogeneous hyperparameters for new denoiser--noise configurations. In a cross-paradigm experiment, HyperDn transfers from relatively cheap TV/TGV variational sources to more expensive diffusion-based DiffPIR. With only $2$ target oracle labels, it reaches $30.23$\,dB, within $0.90$\,dB of the oracle, and outperforms the $64$-label per-configuration predictor trained from scratch, using $1/32$ as many target labels as that baseline point. Without any target oracle labels, HyperDn also reaches near-oracle PSNR on two unseen mixtures of seen noise types and on transfer from relatively cheap $96\times 96$ source images to $512\times 768$ targets. Together, these results show that expensive oracle supervision for hyperparameter prediction can be transferred from source to new target configurations, reducing the need to rebuild oracle labels for each new denoising configuration.

</details>


### [33] [A Human-in-the-Loop Framework for Efficient Prompt Selection in Microscopy Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20495)
*Abhiram Kandiyana,Ankur Mali,Lawrence O. Hall,Peter R. Mouton,Dmitry Goldgof*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Formulates prompt-set construction as target-driven active learning to select unlabeled images for expert verification, enabling few-shot VLM prompts; achieves high performance with very few annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce annotation burden in microscopy image classification by leveraging vision-language models and human-in-the-loop caption verification/editing.

Method: Treat prompt-set construction as a target-driven active learning problem with three complementary selection criteria under strict low-resource constraints; use VLMs to draft captions for candidate exemplars, which experts verify/edit; evaluate on small unlabeled pools with end-to-end human-in-the-loop workflow.

Result: The methods reach the target performance with substantially fewer expert-verified images than random selection; achieves 100% test accuracy with around 20 annotated images on average.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a feasible human-centered use of generative AI for biomedical image analysis, reducing annotation cost while keeping experts actively involved; code and data will be released.

Abstract: Deep-learning pipelines for microscopy image classification often require expensive, labor- and time-intensive expert annotation to produce high-quality ground truth for training. Recent work has shown that prompt tuning of vision-language models (VLMs) can reduce manual annotation by constructing a small prompt set of expert-verified image-caption exemplars that is reused as few-shot context to classify all remaining images at inference time. To further reduce effort, the VLM can draft captions for candidate exemplars, which experts then verify and lightly edit instead of writing text de novo. However, two practical questions remain unaddressed: (1) which unlabeled images should be prioritized for verification, and (2) how many verified exemplars are needed to reach a performance target. In this work, we address these questions by formulating prompt-set construction as a target-driven active learning problem that prioritizes which images to annotate. We study three complementary selection criteria under strict low-resource constraints with small unlabeled pools. Experiments show that our methods reach the target performance with substantially fewer expert-verified images than random selection, achieving 100% test accuracy with as few as 20 annotated images on average. More broadly, our human-in-the-loop framework demonstrates a human-centered use of generative AI in biomedical image analysis, where experts remain actively involved in verifying and refining model output while significantly reducing annotation cost. Code and data will be publicly available.

</details>


### [34] [ShadeBench: A Benchmark Dataset for Building Shade Simulation in Sustainable Society](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20510)
*Longchao Da,Mithun Shivakoti,Xiangrui Liu,T Pranav Kutralingam,Yezhou Yang,Hua Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ShadeBench is a multimodal dataset and benchmark for urban shade understanding, enabling shade generation, segmentation, and 3D reconstruction across diverse urban scenes with temporally varying simulated shade maps, textual descriptions, satellite images, and 3D building data; includes evaluation protocols and baselines; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban heat exposure and shade patterns critically affect pedestrians and outdoor planning; a lack of large-scale, multimodal datasets and standardized evaluation frameworks hampers shade analysis.

Method: Assemble ShadeBench by collecting geographically diverse urban scenes; generate temporally varying simulated shade maps; provide textual descriptions; align satellite imagery, building skeletons, and 3D meshes; define evaluation protocols; implement baseline methods for shade generation, segmentation, and 3D reconstruction.

Result: Provides a public dataset and benchmark for shade-related tasks; establishes standardized evaluation protocols and baselines; supports scalable, fine-grained shade analysis for urban climate research.

Conclusion: ShadeBench lays a foundation for data-driven urban climate studies and aids heat-resilient urban planning, with potential extensions to real-world validation and broader multimodal tasks.

Abstract: Urban heat exposure is becoming an increasingly critical challenge due to the intensifying urban heat island effect. Fine-grained shade patterns, especially those induced by urban buildings, strongly influence pedestrians' thermal exposure and outdoor activity planning. However, accurately modeling and analyzing urban shade at scale remains difficult because of the lack of large-scale datasets and systematic evaluation frameworks. To address this challenge, we present ShadeBench, a comprehensive dataset and benchmark for urban shade understanding. ShadeBench contains geographically diverse urban scenes with temporally varying simulated shade maps and textual descriptions, together with aligned satellite imagery, building skeleton representations, and 3D building meshes. Built upon this multimodal dataset, ShadeBench supports a range of downstream tasks, including shade generation, shade segmentation, and 3D building reconstruction. We further establish standardized evaluation protocols and baseline methods for these tasks. By enabling scalable and fine-grained shade analysis, ShadeBench provides a foundation for data-driven urban climate research and supports future studies in heat-resilient urban planning and decision-making. The code and dataset are publicly available at https://darl-genai.github.io/shadebench/.

</details>


### [35] [NeuroQA: A Large-Scale Image-Grounded Benchmark for 3D Brain MRI Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20525)
*Mohammad H. Abbasi,Favour Nerrise,Shaurnav Ghosh,Ridvan Yesiloglu,Yuncong Mao,Bailey Trang,Mohammad Asadi,Merryn Daniel,Gustavo Chau Loo Kung,Ken Chang,Pavan Pinkesh Shah,Adam Turnbull,Kyan Younes,Seena Dehkharghani,Ehsan Adeli*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NeuroQA is a large-scale 3D brain MRI VQA benchmark with 56,953 QA pairs across 12 datasets, spanning ages 5–104 and five disorders, featuring 11 clinically grounded reasoning skills, and a two-tier public/restricted release with robust QA curation and an initial performance gap revealing current model limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of prior medical VQA that focus on 2D slices or narrow labels, by providing fully 3D-grounded VQA across multiple clinical domains, with rigorous validation against quantitative measurements and radiology metadata to prevent shortcuts and improve clinical relevance.

Method: Data collection from 12 MRI datasets to form 56,953 QA pairs over 12 domains; 3D volumes used for all QAs; 203 templates (131 image-grounded, 72 image-informed); 11 reasoning skills; answer-distribution refinement to reduce text-only shortcuts; image-grounding protocol; 38-rule deterministic pipeline with expert review verifying QA pairs against FreeSurfer metrics, metadata, or radiology reports; clinician evaluation on 100 items; held-out private test set; open-access QA release plus reproducible scripts; subject-level splits and online leaderboard.

Result: Best zero-shot vision-language model: 47.5% accuracy; supervised 3D CNN baseline: 43.7%; both below the 49.4% text-only majority-template floor; text-only accuracy after refinement reduced from >80% to 44.6%; QA pipeline produced zero same-subject contradictions; clinician evaluation supported by independent experts; two-tier release supports reproducible benchmarking.

Conclusion: NeuroQA provides a scalable, clinically grounded benchmark for 3D MRI VQA with rigorous data curation and an evaluation framework that exposes a substantial gap between current models and simple baselines, enabling reproducible research and clinically relevant progress.

Abstract: We present NeuroQA, a large-scale benchmark for visual question answering in 3D brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), with 56,953 QA pairs from 12,977 subjects across 12 datasets. It spans ages 5-104 and five clinical domains: Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, tumors, white matter disease, and neurodevelopment. Unlike prior medical Visual Question Answering (VQA) efforts that operate on 2D slices or rely on narrow diagnostic labels, NeuroQA pairs every item with a full 3D volume. It evaluates 11 clinically grounded reasoning skills across Yes/No, multiple-choice, and open-ended formats. Of the 203 templates, 131 are image-grounded (answerable from a 3-plane viewer) and 72 are image-informed (ground truth from quantitative volumetry or clinical instruments). To remove text-only shortcuts, we apply answer-distribution refinement, reducing closed-format text-only accuracy from $>$80% to 44.6%; image necessity is assessed separately through an image-grounding protocol released with the benchmark. A 38-rule deterministic pipeline and two rounds of expert review verify every QA pair against FreeSurfer measurements, metadata, or radiology report fields, with zero same-subject contradictions across templates. We conduct a clinician evaluation in which two clinicians independently assess 100 frozen test items on a three-plane viewer. On closed-format (Yes/No + multiple-choice) test-public items, the best zero-shot vision-language model and a supervised 3D CNN baseline reach 47.5% and 43.7% accuracy respectively, both below the 49.4% text-only majority-template floor. NeuroQA adopts a two-tier release with public QA pairs for open-access datasets and reproducible generation scripts for datasets restricted by data use agreements (DUAs), plus subject-level splits, a held-out private test set, and an online leaderboard.

</details>


### [36] [HADS-Net:A Hybrid Attention-Augmented Dual-Stream Network with Physics-Informed Augmentation for Breast Ultrasound Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20536)
*Chinedu Emmanuel Mbonu,Blessing Nwamaka Iduh,Joseph Ikechukwu Odo,Doris Chinedu Asogwa*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dual-stream HADS-Net achieves state-of-the-art accuracy for breast ultrasound lesion classification by combining physics-informed texture augmentation with a boundary-focused Sobel stream, fused via cross-attention, and optimized with focal loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Breast ultrasound classification is hindered by speckle noise, acoustic shadowing, and inter-class visual ambiguity; existing single-stream DL methods ignore ultrasound physics and boundary information, limiting performance and interpretability.

Method: Stream 1 applies physics-informed augmentation (speckle noise, acoustic shadowing, gain variation) before features are extracted via pretrained EfficientNet-B3 projected to 512 dimensions. Stream 2 processes Sobel edge maps through a lightweight CNN projected to the same 512-dim space. A cross-attention fusion module allows the texture stream to selectively query boundary features, yielding a joint representation classified by an MLP trained with adaptive class-weighted focal loss. Training uses five-fold stratified cross-validation with cosine annealing over 50 epochs; best checkpoint chosen by lowest validation loss on a held-out test set.

Result: On the BUSI dataset, accuracy 96.58%; macro ROC-AUC 0.9978; macro F1 0.9654; per-class F1: 0.970 (benign), 0.951 (malignant), 0.976 (normal). No malignant lesion misclassified as normal.

Conclusion: Modality-specific augmentation with cross-modal attention fusion effectively leverages texture and boundary cues for ultrasound-based breast cancer diagnosis, improving discriminability and robustness for clinical deployment.

Abstract: Accurate classification of breast ultrasound images into benign, malignant, and normal categories is a critical clinical task complicated by speckle noise, acoustic shadowing, and inter-class visual ambiguity. Existing deep learning methods rely on single-stream architectures with generic augmentation that ignores ultrasound acquisition physics, and no prior method dedicates a stream to the lesion boundary features identified as the most diagnostically significant visual cue. We propose HADS-Net, a Hybrid Attention-Augmented Dual-Stream Network exploiting global texture and local boundary cues through two parallel pathways. Stream 1 applies physics-informed augmentation simulating speckle noise, acoustic shadowing, and gain variation before extracting features via pretrained EfficientNet-B3 projected to 512 dimensions. Stream 2 extracts Sobel edge maps processed by a lightweight CNN projected to the same 512-dimensional space. A cross-attention fusion module allows the texture stream to selectively query boundary features, producing a jointly optimised representation classified by an MLP trained with adaptive class-weighted focal loss. Five-fold stratified cross-validation with cosine annealing over 50 epochs is used, with the globally best checkpoint selected by lowest validation loss evaluated on a held-out test set. On the BUSI dataset, HADS-Net achieves 96.58% accuracy, macro ROC-AUC of 0.9978, macro F1 of 0.9654, and per-class F1-scores of 0.970, 0.951, and 0.976 for benign, malignant, and normal. No malignant lesion is misclassified as normal. These results confirm that modality-specific augmentation with cross-modal attention fusion is an effective strategy for ultrasound-based breast cancer diagnosis.

</details>


### [37] [Continual Segmentation under Joint Nonstationarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20538)
*Prashant Pandey,Himanshu Kumar,Devineni Sri Venkatraya Chowdary,Brejesh Lall*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes gradient-adaptive stabilization and prototype anchored supervision to handle joint class-domain-label nonstationarity in continual segmentation, achieving robust performance with limited annotations and abundant unlabeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In evolving data streams, semantic segmentation faces coupled shifts in classes, domains, and supervision; existing continual learning methods rarely address these jointly; need unified, data-efficient solutions for dense prediction.

Method: Formalize continual segmentation under coupled shifts; introduce gradient-adaptive stabilization (parameter-wise regularization via gradient-scaled perturbations) to balance stability and plasticity; apply semi-supervised learning with prototype anchored supervision based on pseudo-labels validated by confidence and prototype consistency.

Result: Empirical evaluation across class-incremental, domain-incremental, and few-shot regimes shows consistent improvements over prior methods; reveals failure modes of existing approaches.

Conclusion: The approach enables learning under joint nonstationarity in continual segmentation and provides insights into robust dense predictors in evolving environments.

Abstract: Evolving data streams induce joint nonstationarity in continual semantic segmentation, where semantic classes, input distributions, and supervision availability change simultaneously over time. This setting reflects practical structured prediction systems, yet remains largely unexplored in prior continual learning work, which typically studies these factors in isolation. We formalize continual segmentation under coupled class, domain, and label shifts and investigate learning in heterogeneous dense prediction environments with limited annotations and abundant unlabeled data. To address instability and overfitting arising from few-shot supervision under distribution drift, we introduce gradient-adaptive stabilization, a parameter-wise regularization mechanism implemented via gradient-scaled stochastic perturbations that promotes a principled stability-plasticity tradeoff. We further leverage unlabeled data through semi-supervised learning and introduce prototype anchored supervision that validates pseudo-labels via joint confidence and prototype consistency. Together, these mechanisms enable learning under joint nonstationarity in continual segmentation. Extensive empirical evaluation across class-incremental, domain-incremental, and few-shot regimes demonstrates consistent improvements over prior methods in heterogeneous structured prediction settings. Our results expose fundamental failure modes of existing continual segmentation approaches and provide insight into learning robust dense predictors in dynamically evolving environments.

</details>


### [38] [Uncertainty-Guided Conservative Propagation for Structured Inference in Vessel Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20543)
*Huan Huang,Michele Esposito,Chen Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UGCP is a differentiable plug-in that iteratively refines vessel segmentation in logit space using predictive uncertainty, improving connectivity and accuracy (Dice, centerline Dice, HD95) across 2D/3D datasets with CNN/Transformer backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-pass vessel segmentation often fails on complex vascular patterns and ambiguous regions, leading to disconnections and unreliable predictions. An iterative refinement guided by uncertainty could propagate reliable information to uncertain areas while curbing drift.

Method: Introduce Uncertainty-Guided Conservative Propagation (UGCP): a small number of logit-space update steps that allow local predictions to interact. Uses predictive uncertainty to guide reliable regions to support ambiguous regions; employs structure-aware modulation and source-based stabilization to limit unreliable propagation. Differentiable and trainable end-to-end as a plug-in module for existing networks.

Result: Across four public datasets (2D/3D: retinal, coronary, cerebral vessels) and with both CNN- and Transformer-based backbones, UGCP yields consistent improvements in Dice, centerline Dice, and 95th percentile Hausdorff distance, while reducing vessel disconnections and improving structural consistency with limited additional computation.

Conclusion: UGCP provides a general, differentiable, plug-in strategy for iterative, uncertainty-guided refinement that enhances vessel segmentation without demanding extensive changes to base architectures.

Abstract: Accurate vessel segmentation is essential for medical image analysis, yet remains challenging due to complex vascular patterns and imaging ambiguity. Most deep models rely on single-pass prediction, limiting their ability to refine uncertain or disconnected regions during inference. To address this limitation, we propose Uncertainty-Guided Conservative Propagation (UGCP), a general plug-in module for vessel segmentation. Instead of directly using a one-shot output as the final prediction, UGCP performs a small number of logit-space update steps to refine the segmentation through local predictions interaction. Predictive uncertainty guides reliable regions to support ambiguous regions, while structure-aware modulation and source-based stabilization reduce unreliable propagation and excessive drift. The module is differentiable and can be trained end-to-end with different segmentation networks. We evaluate UGCP on four public vessel segmentation datasets covering 2D and 3D tasks, including retinal vessel, coronary artery, and cerebral vessel segmentation. Experiments with convolutional neural network-based and Transformer-based backbones show consistent improvements in Dice similarity coefficient, centerline Dice, and 95th percentile Hausdorff distance. Further analysis demonstrates that UGCP reduces vessel disconnections and improves structural consistency with limited additional computation. The code will be made available at https://github.com/chenzhao2023/UGC_PR.

</details>


### [39] [MAPS: A Synthetic Dataset for Probing Vision Models in a Controlled 3D Scene Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20549)
*Santiago Galella,Pamela Osuna-Vargas,Maren Wehrheim,Martina G. Vilas,Gemma Roig,Matthias Kaschube*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAPS introduces a scalable synthetic 3D scene library and Blender-based renderer to attribute vision model failures to scene parameters; it reveals a dominant effect of camera distance/elevation and shows architecture-level sensitivity patterns, not just CNN-vs-transformer differences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To disentangle how controllable scene properties influence model predictions, moving beyond global 2D perturbations and entangled real-world variations.

Method: Assemble 2,618 photorealistic 3D meshes, render images with a Blender pipeline under continuous variation of nine scene factors (background, camera, lighting), evaluate 20 models (CNNs and transformers) via regression-based sensitivity analysis.

Result: Camera distance and elevation dominate recognition failure; architecture clusters (modern CNNs and transformers) share sensitivity profiles distinct from older architectures; suggests fine-grained architectural design, not mere model type, shapes robustness to scene variation.

Conclusion: MAPS is scalable and extensible for attributing model behavior to scene factors; findings imply targeted robustness improvements should focus on how architectures utilize camera-related cues; the dataset enables controlled studies of 3D scene properties on vision models.

Abstract: Modern vision models achieve strong performance on standard benchmarks, yet their aggregate accuracy reveals little about which scene properties drive their predictions. Existing robustness benchmarks provide important stress tests, but typically manipulate global 2D image properties, rely on entangled real-world variation, or cover only a limited set of 3D objects and scene parameters. We introduce MAPS (Manifolds of Artificial Parametric Scenes), a scalable instrument for controlled attribution of vision model behavior to scene parameters. MAPS comprises 2,618 curated photorealistic 3D meshes validated for recognizability across 560 ImageNet classes and provides a Blender-based rendering pipeline for on-demand image generation under continuous variation of nine independent scene-factors spanning background, camera, and lighting, extensible to other factors. To showcase its applicability, we use MAPS to evaluate 20 convolutional and transformer-based models by quantifying their reliance on these scene factors through regression-based sensitivity analysis. We find a near-universal failure axis across all tested architectures: camera distance and elevation consistently dominate recognition failure regardless of ImageNet accuracy. However, the full sensitivity structure reveals that modern CNNs and transformers cluster together, distinct from older architectures, suggesting that fine-grained architectural design choices, rather than the coarse CNN-versus-transformer distinction, are the stronger determinant of sensitivity profiles.

</details>


### [40] [Faster or Stronger: Towards Flexible Visual Place Recognition via Weighted Aggregation and Token Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20551)
*Zichao Zeng,June Moh Goo,Junwei Zheng,Weijia Fan,Jiaming Zhang,Rainer Stiefelhagen,Jan Boehm*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WeiAD improves VPR by weighted aggregation of ViT patch clusters; WeiToP reduces ViT feature extraction cost via self-distilled token pruning, enabling on-demand accuracy–efficiency trade-offs, and outperforms existing pruning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cluster-based aggregation in ViT-based VPR often treats all clusters equally, ignoring that different clusters encode distinct spatial/semantic patterns that contribute unequally to retrieval; high latency of ViT feature extraction motivates cost-reduction strategies.

Method: WeiAD assigns learned weights to clusters during aggregation to form a discriminative global descriptor; WeiToP uses a self-distillation approach where aggregation-derived token importance guides a lightweight pruning module attached to an early transformer layer, enabling one-shot joint training and subsequent inference-time token pruning.

Result: WeiAD yields more discriminative global representations for VPR; WeiToP reduces feature extraction cost and enables flexible accuracy-efficiency trade-offs; WeiToP outperforms existing token pruning methods adapted from general vision tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed WeiAD and WeiToP jointly advance ViT-based VPR by (i) improving retrieval accuracy through weighted aggregation and (ii) tightening inference cost through token pruning with minimal training overhead and adaptable runtime control.

Abstract: Visual Place Recognition (VPR) aims to match a query image to reference images of the same place in a large-scale database. Recent state-of-the-art methods employ Vision Transformers (ViTs) as backbone foundation models to extract patch-level features that are robust to viewpoint, illumination, and seasonal variations, which are then aggregated into a compact global descriptor for retrieval. Most existing aggregation methods uniformly pool patch tokens into learned clusters, despite the fact that different clusters often encode distinct spatial or semantic patterns and contribute unequally to VPR performance. To address this limitation, we propose Weighted Aggregated Descriptor (WeiAD), which assigns weights to clusters during aggregation, producing more discriminative global representations. Beyond accuracy, retrieval latency is a critical concern for large-scale deployments and resource-constrained edge devices. Prior work mainly reduces latency by compressing global descriptors, while overlooking the cost of feature extraction, an issue exacerbated by ViT-based backbones. We therefore introduce WeiToP, a VPR-oriented token pruning framework that reduces feature extraction cost via self-distillation, where aggregation-induced token importance supervises a lightweight pruning module attached to an early transformer layer, enabling inference-time token pruning. After a single joint training phase, WeiToP enables plug-and-play token pruning at inference time, allowing flexible and on-demand control over the accuracy-efficiency trade-off without additional training. Moreover, WeiToP outperforms existing token pruning methods adapted from general vision tasks.

</details>


### [41] [End-to-End Unmixing with Material Prompts for Hyperspectral Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20569)
*Xu Han,Mohammad Aminul Islam,Lei Wang,Zekun Long,Guanmanyi Fu,Wangshu Cai,Kuldip K. Paliwal,Jun Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Joint end-to-end material-aware hyperspectral tracking via unmixing-informed localization and frequency-aware prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limited hyperspectral video data; need to couple material decomposition with tracking rather than decoupled pipelines; exploit intrinsic material information for robustness.

Method: Material representation decomposition module with adaptive frequency decomposition; dual-branch wavelet-enhanced material prompts; weighted target-oriented unmixing loss; model-agnostic to unmixing backbones.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art on standard hyperspectral tracking benchmarks; validates effectiveness; open-source code.

Conclusion: End-to-end material-aware tracking enhances robustness under appearance ambiguity and clutter; framework generalizes across backbones and unmixing strategies.

Abstract: Hyperspectral imagery encodes rich material properties that can improve tracking robustness under appearance ambiguity, illumination change, and background clutter. However, due to the limited availability of hyperspectral video data, many existing methods adapt pretrained RGB trackers via spatial or channel fusion strategies, largely neglecting the intrinsic material information in hyperspectral imagery. Moreover, the few material-aware approaches typically rely on external spectral unmixing pipelines that are decoupled from the tracking objective, limiting effective optimization of material representations for target localization. To address these limitations, we formulate hyperspectral object tracking as a joint optimization problem of material decomposition and target localization, coupling the two tasks via a weighted target-oriented unmixing loss that explicitly aligns material representations with localization accuracy. Specifically, we propose a material representation decomposition module for deep learning-based spectral unmixing with adaptive frequency decomposition. Building on the decomposed material representations, we further introduce a dual-branch wavelet-enhanced material prompt module that learns low- and high-frequency material prompts through efficient spatial-material interactions in the frequency domain. The framework is model-agnostic and can be seamlessly generalized to different unmixing backbones. Extensive experiments on standard hyperspectral tracking benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and validate the effectiveness of the proposed end-to-end material-aware tracking framework. Code is available at https://github.com/han030927/E2EMPT.

</details>


### [42] [$Δ$ynamics: Language-Based Representation for Inferring Rigid-Body Dynamics From Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20576)
*Chia-Hsiang Kao,Cong Phuoc Huynh,Chien-Yi Wang,Noranart Vesdapunt,Stefan Stojanov,Bharath Hariharan,Oleksandr Obiednikov,Ning Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ΔYNAMICS is a language-driven framework for inferring rigid-body dynamics from monocular videos by generating structured text scene configurations for physics simulation, achieving strong generalization and transfer to real-world data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current rigid-body dynamics inference methods depend on specific physical systems, object types, and camera setups, hindering generalization. A unified, flexible representation via language can bridge perception and simulation and improve generalization, aided by optical flow as input.

Method: A vision-language model that represents scene configurations as structured text for physics engines. It incorporates natural language motion reasoning and uses optical flow as a semantic-agnostic input. The model generates scene configurations which are then used in physics simulation. Additional boosts come from test-time sampling and evolutionary search.

Result: On CLEVRER, achieves segmentation IoU of 0.30, a 7x improvement over leading VLMs (InternVL3-8B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Claude-4-Sonnet). Test-time sampling yields +27% IoU; evolutionary search yields +120% IoU. Demonstrates transfer to a new dataset of 235 real-world rigid-body videos.

Conclusion: Language-driven inference can generalize better for physics reasoning and bridging perception and simulation, with demonstrated transfer to real-world data and potential for scalable, flexible physics perception.

Abstract: Inferring rigid-body physical states and properties from monocular videos is a fundamental step toward physics-based perception and simulation. Existing approaches assume specific underlying physical systems, object types, and camera poses, making them unable to generalize to complex real-world settings. We introduce $Δ$YNAMICS, a vision-language framework that uses language as a unified representation of rigid-body dynamics. Instead of directly predicting parameters, $Δ$YNAMICS generates scene configurations in a structured text format for physics simulation. We enhance the model's generalization by integrating natural language motion reasoning and leveraging optical flow as a semantic-agnostic input. On the CLEVRER dataset, $Δ$YNAMICS achieves a segmentation IoU of 0.30, a 7x improvement over leading VLMs (InternVL3-8B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B and Claude-4-Sonnet). Additionally, test-time sampling and evolutionary search further boost performance by 27% and 120% in segmentation IoU, respectively. Finally, we demonstrate strong transfer to a new dataset of 235 real-world rigid-body videos, highlighting the potential of language-driven physics inference for bridging perception and simulation.

</details>


### [43] [QwenSafe: Multimodal Content Rating Description Identification via Preference-Aligned VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20584)
*Dishanika Denipitiyage,Aruna Seneviratne,Suranga Seneviratne*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QwenSafe uses a vision-language model to detect Apple CRDs from app metadata and screenshots, aided by a data-synthesis pipeline (metadata2CRD); it is fine-tuned with supervised learning and Direct Preference Optimization, and outperforms baselines on 12 CRDs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for accurate, scalable, and consistent content rating disclosures in mobile app marketplaces amid multimodal app content (text plus visuals).

Method: Adapt Qwen3-VL-8B with supervised fine-tuning followed by Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Introduce metadata2CRD to synthesize descriptor-aligned QA pairs by combining app descriptions, screenshots, and formal CRD definitions. Evaluate against state-of-the-art VLMs (Qwen3-VL, LLaVA-1.6, Gemini-2.5-Flash) on 12 Apple-defined CRDs for binary classification of CRDs.

Result: QwenSafe outperforms baselines in binary CRD classification; positive-class recall improvements of approximately 111.8%, 36.1%, and 2.1% over the compared models, respectively.

Conclusion: Descriptor-aware multimodal alignment enhances automated CRD classification and demonstrates potential for scalable, consistent content rating in mobile app marketplaces.

Abstract: Mobile app marketplaces require developers to disclose standardized content rating descriptors (CRDs) to inform users about potentially sensitive or restricted content. Ensuring the accuracy and consistency of these disclosures remains challenging due to the multimodal nature of app content, which spans textual descriptions and visual interfaces. In this paper, we present QwenSafe, a Vision-Language Model (VLM) designed to automatically identify the presence of Apple-defined CRDs by jointly reasoning over app metadata and screenshots. To enable scalable training for this task, we introduce metadata2CRD, a data-construction pipeline that synthesizes descriptor-aligned question-answer pairs by combining app descriptions, screenshots, and formal descriptor definitions. We adapt Qwen3-VL-8B using supervised fine-tuning followed by Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to align model predictions with descriptor-specific evidence and explanations across visual and textual modalities. We evaluate QwenSafe on 12 Apple-defined content rating descriptors and compare it against state-of-the-art vision-language models, including Qwen3-VL, LLaVA-1.6, and Gemini-2.5-Flash. QwenSafe consistently outperforms all baselines in binary CRD classification, achieving improvements in positive-class recall of 111.8%, 36.1%, and 2.1%, respectively. Our results demonstrate that descriptor-aware multimodal alignment substantially improves automated content classification and highlights the potential of vision-language models to support scalable and consistent content rating in mobile app marketplaces.

</details>


### [44] [Head-Aware Key-Value Compression for Efficient Autoregressive Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20600)
*Guotao Liang,Baoquan Zhang,Zhiyuan Wen,Yunming Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HeadKV introduces a head-aware KV cache compression for autoregressive image generation. It allocates variable cache budgets per attention head based on local vs. global attention, identifies head types early without extra data/training, and uses a Stratified Token Eviction strategy to preserve long-range information, improving memory efficiency and throughput across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive visual generation suffers from high memory usage due to caching of generated tokens. Fixed per-head cache budgets ignore heterogeneity among attention heads, causing suboptimal memory use and throughput.

Method: Propose HeadKV: a head-aware KV cache compression framework that allocates smaller budgets to locality-biased heads and larger budgets to globally attentive heads. Identify head types during early generation when attention patterns are consistent per head, then reuse these types across tokens. Introduce Stratified Token Eviction to preserve long-range information. No additional training or dataset statistics required; method generalizes across inputs.

Result: Extensive experiments show the proposed approach is effective across multiple autoregressive image generation models, improving memory efficiency and throughput while maintaining generation quality.

Conclusion: Head-aware KV cache budgeting and early head-type identification enable more efficient autoregressive image generation, enabling scalable memory- and compute-efficient generation without extra training and with good generalization across inputs.

Abstract: Autoregressive (AR) visual generation has achieved remarkable performance but suffers from high memory usage and low throughput, as it requires caching previously generated visual tokens. Recent research has shown that retaining only a few lines of cache tokens can maintain high-quality images while significantly reducing memory usage and improving throughput. However, these methods allocate a fixed budget to each attention head, overlooking the heterogeneity among attention heads, leading to suboptimal memory allocation. In this paper, we observe that attention heads across different layers exhibit diverse attention patterns, where some heads focus on local neighborhoods while others capture broader contextual dependencies. Based on this insight, we propose a novel head-aware key-value (KV) cache compression framework for autoregressive image generation, called HeadKV, which assigns smaller budgets to locality-biased heads and larger budgets to heads with broader attention. A key challenge lies in identifying the type of each attention head to guide cache compression. We further observe that, within the same layer, each head exhibits consistent attention patterns across token positions, \emph{i.e.}, a head's behavior for early tokens remains consistent with that for later tokens. This insight suggests that head types can be identified during the early stage and reused for KV compression throughout generation. Its advantage is that it requires no additional training or dataset-level statistics and generalizes seamlessly across different inputs. Moreover, we design a Stratified Token Eviction strategy to effectively preserve long-range information. Extensive experiments demonstrate its effectiveness across multiple autoregressive image generation models.

</details>


### [45] [Mind Your Margin and Boundary: Are Your Distilled Datasets Truly Robust?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20606)
*Muquan Li,Yingyi Ma,Yihong Huang,Hang Gou,Ke Qin,Ming Li,Yuan-Fang Li,Tao He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: C^2R introduces an attack-aware curriculum and a class-balanced contrastive loss for robust dataset distillation, achieving superior robust accuracy across CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and ImageNet-1K subsets under six attacks, outperforming prior robust DD by ~2.8% on average.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the accuracy-robustness trade-off in dataset distillation by focusing training on near-margin adversaries and by explicitly enlarging inter-class boundary margins where attacks concentrate.

Method: Derives a perturbation score that approximates each sample's robust hinge to create an attack-aware curriculum emphasizing the smallest-margin adversaries; introduces a class-balanced contrastive robustness loss to enforce adversarial invariance and widen class boundary separation; integrates these components into the dataset distillation pipeline.

Result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and ImageNet-1K subsets under six attacks shows that C^2R achieves the best robust accuracy, outperforming prior robust DD by 2.8 percentage points on average.

Conclusion: C^2R delivers a principled improvement in robust dataset distillation by combining an attack-aware curriculum with a contrastive robustness objective, yielding stronger robustness across diverse datasets and attacks and improving the accuracy-robustness balance.

Abstract: Dataset distillation (DD) compresses a large training set into a small synthetic set for efficient training, but most DD methods optimize only clean accuracy and leave robustness uncontrolled. Recent robust DD methods improve robustness, yet they often suffer from a poor accuracy-robustness trade-off because they (i) treat all adversarially perturbed examples uniformly, despite robust risk being dominated by near-zero robust margins, and (ii) do not explicitly increase inter-class separation in the decision boundary where attacks concentrate. We present Contrastive Curriculum for Robust Dataset Distillation (C$^2$R), a framework that couples an attack-aware curriculum with a contrastive robustness objective. From a robust-margin perspective, we derive a perturbation score that approximates each sample's robust hinge, enabling a curriculum that prioritizes the smallest-margin adversaries that most directly drive robust error. In parallel, a class-balanced contrastive robustness loss enforces adversarial invariance while explicitly widening boundary separation across classes. Experiments on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and multiple ImageNet-1K subsets under six attacks show that C$^2$R achieves the best robust accuracy, outperforming prior robust DD by $2.8$% on average.

</details>


### [46] [Beyond Routing: Characterising Expert Tuning and Representation in Vision Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20610)
*Gene Tangtartharakul,Katherine R. Storrs*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MoE vision models reveal expert specialization that goes beyond simple category routing: an animate–inanimate partition dominates and remains stable across initializations, with broad continuous tuning and comparable category separability across experts, underscoring the value of expert-level tuning and representational analyses over gating alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Routing analyses alone do not reveal the content encoded by individual experts in mixture-of-experts (MoE) networks. The study seeks to characterize expert-level tuning, category separability, and cross-initialization stability, and to relate these to semantic dimensions beyond discrete categories.

Method: Train sparsely-gated convolutional MoE models with a contrastive objective on natural images. Analyze expert-level behavior by (i) measuring per-expert category separability, (ii) identifying most-exciting inputs for tuning, (iii) deriving semantic dimensions from THINGS human judgments, and (iv) applying representational similarity analysis to assess stability of expert allocations across independent initializations.

Result: The animate–inanimate distinction dominates expert partitioning from gating to expert readout and is stable across seeds. Routing appears relatively sparse and category-focused, yet expert-level analyses reveal broader tuning to continuous visual and semantic dimensions that extend beyond category boundaries. Experts display similar category separability despite distinct feature tuning, showing the value of moving beyond category-level explanations to probe fine-grained tuning and representational structure.

Conclusion: To understand vision MoEs, analyze expert-level tuning and representational geometry rather than relying solely on gating statistics. Expert specialization extends beyond category routing and is best explained by probing fine-grained tuning and the underlying representational structure.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models are often interpreted by analysing which categories are routed to which experts. However, routing alone does not reveal what each expert actually encodes. We train sparsely-gated convolutional MoE models with a contrastive objective on natural images and characterise expert specialisation using tools from visual neuroscience. Extending from gating-level to expert-level analyses, we measure per-expert category separability, and per-expert tuning using the most exciting inputs. Extending from category-level to feature-level explanations, we interpret tuning via semantic dimensions derived from a dataset of human behavioural judgements (THINGS). Finally, we use tuning and representational similarity analysis to assess the stability of expertise-allocation across independent initialisations. We find that an animate-inanimate distinction dominates expert partitioning, apparent from gating through to expert readout, and is stable across independently trained models. Although routing statistics suggest relatively sparse, categorical preferences, expert analyses reveal broader tuning to continuous visual and semantic dimensions that extend beyond category boundaries. Experts exhibit similar category-separability to one another, despite distinct feature tuning, demonstrating the explanatory benefits of moving beyond category-level analyses. Together, these results show that expert specialisation in vision MoEs extends well beyond category routing and is better understood by probing fine-grained expert-level tuning and representational structure.

</details>


### [47] [Accelerating Video Inverse Problem Solvers with Autoregressive Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20624)
*Taesung Kwon,Jonghyun Park,Hyungjin Chung,Jong Chul Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AVIS introduces an autoregressive video diffusion framework for streaming restoration that cuts latency and boosts throughput compared to non-autoregressive methods; AVIS Flash further accelerates by enforcing measurement consistency only on the first chunk, enabling near real-time performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based priors are powerful for zero-shot video inverse problems but suffer from high initial latency and low throughput due to holistic restoration and repeated pixel-space consistency steps. A streaming, autoregressive approach can mitigate these bottlenecks.

Method: Propose AVIS, an autoregressive video diffusion model that restores video in a streaming fashion. It initializes reverse diffusion with a measurement-consistent estimate to reduce required sampling steps. Two variants are introduced: AVIS (full pipeline) and AVIS Flash (enforces measurement consistency only on the first chunk).

Result: Significant latency reduction (114s to 4s) and throughput improvement (0.71 to 1.18 FPS) over leading non-autoregressive solvers; AVIS Flash achieves 5.91 FPS on a single RTX 4090 with competitive restoration quality and favorable efficiency–performance trade-offs.

Conclusion: Autoregressive streaming diffusion enables near real-time video restoration, with AVIS Flash offering a practical, high-throughput option suitable for real-time deployment while maintaining quality.

Abstract: Diffusion models provide powerful priors for zero-shot video inverse problems, but their real-time deployment is hindered by two inefficiencies: high initial latency caused by holistic video restoration, and low throughput resulting from multiple VAE passes to enforce measurement consistency in pixel space. To overcome these limitations, we propose Autoregressive Video Inverse problem Solver (AVIS). The AVIS framework leverages autoregressive video diffusion models to restore videos in a streaming manner, naturally eliminating latency bottlenecks. Specifically, AVIS initializes reverse diffusion with a measurement-consistent estimate, reducing the required sampling steps. Compared to leading non-autoregressive solvers, AVIS drastically reduces initial latency from 114s to 4s and increases throughput from 0.71 to 1.18 FPS while achieving superior restoration quality. We further introduce a highly accelerated variant, dubbed AVIS Flash, that enforces measurement consistency solely on the first chunk. AVIS Flash substantially boosts throughput to 5.91 FPS on a single RTX 4090 GPU while maintaining competitive performance and achieving a favorable efficiency-performance trade-off, paving the way toward real-time deployment.

</details>


### [48] [Pareto-Enhanced Portrait Generation: Vision-Aligned Text Supervision for Alignment, Realism, and Aesthetics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20640)
*Yunlong Wang,Jinjin Shi,Wenbin Gao,Xuran Xu,Runyu Shi,Ying Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A feature-supervised training paradigm for Multimodal Diffusion Transformers (MM-DiT) is proposed to overcome the text-image alignment, photorealism, and aesthetics trilemma in portrait generation. It adds a lightweight cross-modal alignment mechanism that derives vision-aligned text representations from SigLIP 2 and supervises the image branch during training with zero inference overhead, while also extracting aesthetic cues from vision foundation models to improve human-perceived aesthetics. The method preserves the base model's generalization and yields Pareto-frontier gains across all three objectives. 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models struggle to jointly optimize alignment, realism, and aesthetics in portrait generation. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) improves realism but risks overfitting, corrupts priors, and reduces alignment or aesthetics. There is a need for training-time guidance that improves photorealism without sacrificing alignment and aesthetics or requiring extra inference cost.

Method: Introduce a feature supervision framework for MM-DiT with a lightweight cross-modal alignment module that implicitly extracts multi-granularity vision-aligned text representations from SigLIP 2. This text guidance is applied to supervise the image branch during training only, incurring zero additional inference overhead. The approach injects vision-aligned text guidance to retain generalization and leverages implicit multi-granularity aesthetic signals from pretrained vision foundation models to optimize human-perceived aesthetics.

Result: Experimentation on MM-DiTs shows the proposed method pushes the Pareto frontier, achieving synergistic improvements in text-image alignment, photorealism, and human-perceived aesthetics, without degrading base-model generalization or requiring extra inference costs.

Conclusion: The proposed feature supervision paradigm enables a harmonious enhancement of all three objectives in portrait generation by leveraging cross-modal alignment at training-time and aesthetic cues from vision foundations, preserving generalization and avoiding the pitfalls of SFT.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models often face a severe trilemma in human portrait generation: text-image alignment, photorealism, and human-perceived aesthetics inherently inhibit one another. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is an effective method for enhancing the photorealism of image generation. However, it often leads to overfitting to the training dataset, corrupts pre-trained image priors, and degrades alignment or aesthetics. To break this bottleneck, we propose a feature supervision paradigm for Multimodal Diffusion Transformers (MM-DiT). Specifically, we introduce a lightweight cross-modal alignment mechanism that implicitly extracts multi-granularity vision-aligned text representations from SigLIP 2 and applies supervision to the image branch of MM-DiT during the training stage, with zero extra inference overhead. Our method injects vision-aligned text guidance while preserving the base model's original generalization, avoiding degradation caused by SFT. Furthermore, our method directly mines implicit multi-granularity aesthetic signals from pre-trained vision foundation models to optimize human-perceived aesthetics.
