Extensive experiments on MM-DiTs show that our method pushes the Pareto frontier and achieves synergistic improvements across text-image alignment, photorealism, and human-perceived aesthetics.

</details>


### [49] [Seeing Through Fog: Towards Fog-Invariant Action Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20645)
*Enqi Liu,Liyuan Pan,Zhi Gao,Lingzhi Li,Qing Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FogAct introduces the first foggy-action dataset and FogNet a two-stream CLIP model that learns fog-invariant representations via guidance from clean videos, achieving competitive results on FogAct and other datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fog degrades visibility and contrast in real-world videos, making action recognition unreliable; there is a need for a dedicated benchmark and methods that extract fog-invariant cues.

Method: Dataset: FogAct—paired clean and foggy videos (stereo) across 10 scenes, 55 actions, ~10k clips. Model: FogNet, a two-stream CLIP architecture that leverages clean-video guidance to learn robust representations for foggy videos, capturing shared structural and motion cues.

Result: Extensive experiments on FogAct and three other popular datasets show FogNet achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: FogAct provides a benchmark for foggy action recognition and FogNet demonstrates effective cross-domain guidance to obtain fog-invariant representations, enabling robust action recognition under degraded visibility.

Abstract: Foggy conditions are commonly encountered in real-world applications; however, existing action recognition approaches typically assume favorable weather and high-quality video inputs. On foggy days, unpredictable visibility degradation and reduced contrast obstruct the extraction of semantic cues, posing significant challenges for current action recognition methods. In this paper, we mitigate the issues faced in action recognition under foggy conditions by employing two strategies. First, we present FogAct, the first benchmark dataset for foggy action recognition, consisting of paired clean and foggy videos captured with a stereo camera system. The dataset spans 10 scenes and 55 action categories, comprising nearly 10,000 video clips. Second, we propose FogNet, a two-stream CLIP model that discovers fog-invariant semantic information hidden behind the degraded videos. FogNet learns robust representations of foggy videos with guidance from clean videos, effectively capturing shared structural and motion cues between clean and foggy videos. Extensive experiments on FogAct and three other popular datasets demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance compared with state-of-the-art (SOTA) approaches. Our FogAct and FogNet are given in our project page.

</details>


### [50] [Gaze into the Details: Locality-Sensitive Enhancement for OCTA Retinal Vessel Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20651)
*Tuopusen Huang,Ding Ma,Xiangqian Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LSENet enhances U-Net for OCTA vessel segmentation by introducing three modules: PIE (patch-wise attention to address vessel discontinuities), MFF (multiscale feature fusion to enrich features with multi-scale information from input and earlier layers), and CRD (connectivity refinement decoder with a large final kernel to reduce fragmentation), achieving state-of-the-art results with fewer parameters on OCTA-500, ROSE-1, and ROSSA datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OCTA vessel segmentation suffers from low local contrast and vessel fragmentation, leading to discontinuities and loss of detail when using standard U-Net-like architectures that emphasize holistic representations.

Method: Replace standard skip connections with Patch Information Enhance (PIE) for patch-wise attention, use Multiscale Feature Fusion (MFF) to inject rich multi-scale information from both original input and preceding layers into PIE, and deploy a Connectivity Refinement Decoder (CRD) with a large-kernel final convolution to refine features and reduce fragmentation across levels.

Result: LSENet achieves state-of-the-art performance on three public OCTA datasets (OCTA-500, ROSE-1, ROSSA) while using fewer parameters, indicating improved segmentation quality with model efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed PIE, MFF, and CRD modules collaboratively address low local contrast and discontinuities in OCTA vessel segmentation, delivering accurate, smooth vessel delineations with fewer parameters than prior architectures.

Abstract: Existing deep learning frameworks for Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography (OCTA) vessel segmentation are largely derived from the U-Net architecture, which serves as the foundation for most current designs. However, most of these methods focus only on holistic representation, struggling to address the problem of low local contrast unique to OCTA, which leads to vessel discontinuities and loss of detail. To address these problems, we propose LSENet, which builds upon the U-Net architecture by introducing three core innovative modules: To address vessel discontinuities, we introduce the Patch Information Enhance module (PIE), which replaces standard skip connections to execute patch-wise attention. To mitigate detail loss, the Multiscale Feature Fusion module (MFF) is proposed to feed the PIE module rich, multi-scale information by extracting visually interpretable features from both the original input and preceding layers. Finally, the Connectivity Refinement Decoder (CRD) is designed to refine features from all levels and utilize a large kernel in the final convolutional layer to reduce fragmentation. Experiments on three public datasets (OCTA-500, ROSE-1, and ROSSA) demonstrate that our proposed LSENet achieves state-of-the-art performance while requiring fewer parameters.

</details>


### [51] [RoPeSLR: 3D RoPE-driven Sparse-LowRank Attention for Efficient Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20659)
*Yuxi Liu,Zekun Zhang,Yixiang Cai,Renjia Deng,Yutong He,Kun Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RoPeSLR introduces a 3D RoPE-guided sparse-low-rank attention for Diffusion Transformers to achieve sub-quadratic complexity, decoupling attention into a high-frequency spike sparsity (~L^{3/2}) and a background low-rank (~d_h log L) continuum, enabling long video synthesis with significant FLOPs reductions and speedups while preserving fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-sequence diffusion-based video generation with quadratic attention limits scalability; existing sparse-linear approaches fail at extreme sparsity due to RoPE orthogonality loss; a mechanism that preserves 3D RoPE structure while scaling is needed.

Method: RoPeSLR uses head-wise low-rank parameterization with learnable 3D absolute positional embedding injection, guided by RoPE to maintain distance-aware representation; decouples attention into high-frequency semantic spike set plus low-rank background; achieves sub-quadratic sparsity and sub-linear rank growth.

Result: Empirical results show 10x FLOP reduction at 90% sparsity on Wan2.1-1.3B; 2.26x end-to-end speedup on 100K+ token HunyuanVideo-13B; less than 1.3% average VBench degradation, near-lossless fidelity.

Conclusion: RoPeSLR effectively enables scalable ultra-long video diffusion by preserving RoPE structure under sparse-low-rank attention, achieving substantial efficiency gains with minimal loss in quality.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have revolutionized high-fidelity video generation, yet their $\mathcal{O}(L^2)$ attention complexity poses a formidable bottleneck for long-sequence synthesis. While recent sparse-linear attention hybrids aim to mitigate this, their performance severely degrades at extreme sparsity due to the "RoPE Dilemma": standard linear attention fails to preserve the orthogonal relative-position structure of 3D Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE), neutralizing vital distance awareness. To address this, we propose \textbf{RoPeSLR}, a 3D RoPE-guided Sparse-LowRank attention framework. We establish that under empirically validated assumptions, the DiT attention manifold admits a decoupling into a high-frequency semantic spike set (bounded by $\mathcal{O}(L^{3/2})$ sparsity) and an extreme low-rank ($\mathcal{O}(d_h \log L)$) background continuum. Guided by this structural prior, RoPeSLR eschews standard linear attention for a head-wise low-rank parameterization equipped with a learnable 3D Absolute Positional Embedding (PE) injection, seamlessly synthesizing long-range relative distance decay. By guaranteeing sub-quadratic sparsity and sub-linear rank growth, RoPeSLR is exceptionally suited for scaling to ultra-long video inference. Extensive evaluations validate this scalable superiority: at 90\% sparsity, RoPeSLR achieves up to $10\times$ fewer FLOPs on Wan2.1-1.3B and delivers a $2.26\times$ end-to-end inference speedup on the ultra-long 100K+ token sequences of HunyuanVideo-13B, all while maintaining near-lossless generation fidelity (less than 1.3\% average VBench degradation).

</details>


### [52] [VSCD: Video-based Scene Change Detection in Unaligned Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20821)
*Jiae Yoon,Ue-Hwan Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Video-based Scene Change Detection (VSCD) for pixel-wise change masks between unconstrained indoor video pairs, with a large-scale 1.1M-frame dataset, a query-centric multi-reference model, and real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable robust change detection for long-term autonomy under arbitrary camera motion and non-synchronized views, where objects can appear or disappear.

Method: A query-centric multi-reference framework that implicitly learns temporal matching from change-mask supervision, aligns candidate reference features to the query via local patch correspondence, and fuses per-reference change features with frame- and patch-level confidence to decode a high-resolution change mask per frame.

Result: State-of-the-art performance against strong image- and video-based baselines on the VSCD benchmark, plus transferability to real-world data and successful deployment on a mobile robot for surveillance and object incremental learning.

Conclusion: The VSCD approach, with its large-scale annotated dataset and multi-reference, enables robust pixel-level change detection under unconstrained viewpoints, advancing long-term autonomy in dynamic environments.

Abstract: Detecting what has changed in an environment is essential for long-term autonomy, yet most change detection settings assume fixed viewpoints, mild misalignment, or only a few changed objects. We introduce Video-based Scene Change Detection (VSCD), which predicts a pixel-wise change mask for each query frame, given a reference and a query RGB video of the same indoor space recorded at different times under unconstrained camera motion. The two videos are not temporally synchronized, and many object instances may appear or disappear. To study this setting, we build a large-scale benchmark with over 1.1 million frames annotated with pixel-accurate change masks, together with a real-world test set for evaluating transfer beyond simulation. We propose a query-centric multi-reference model that learns temporal matching implicitly from change-mask supervision, aligns candidate reference features to the query via local patch correspondence, and fuses per-candidate change features using frame-level and patch-level confidence before decoding a high-resolution mask once per frame. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance against strong image- and video-based baselines, and we validate its real-world impact by deploying it on a mobile robot for two downstream applications -- visual surveillance and object incremental learning.

</details>


### [53] [LER-YOLO: Reliability-Aware Expert Routing for Misaligned RGB-Infrared UAV Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20667)
*Liming Hou,Yueping Peng,Hexiang Hao,Ji Wang,Xuekai Zhang,Wei Tang,Zecong Ye,Xin Ying,Yubo He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LER-YOLO presents a reliability-aware sparse mixture-of-experts framework for misaligned RGB-IR UAV detection, using an uncertainty-aware target alignment to estimate a spatial reliability map and a reliability-guided MoE to select from RGB-dominant, IR-dominant, and interactive experts, achieving state-of-the-art AP50 on MBU with strong ablations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting small UAVs from RGB-IR pairs is hindered by tiny target scale, clutter, and especially misalignment between heterogeneous sensors. Current bimodal detectors fuse features without accounting for local cross-sensor reliability, propagating mismatch artifacts into the detector head. A reliability-aware alignment and fusion mechanism is needed to suppress unreliable fusion and improve cross-modal interaction.

Method: An Uncertainty-Aware Target Alignment module resamples visible (RGB) features toward the infrared reference and estimates a spatial reliability map. A Reliability-Guided Sparse MoE Fusion module then adaptively selects k experts from three categories (RGB-dominant, IR-dominant, and interactive fusion experts) to enable trustworthy cross-modal interaction while suppressing unreliable fusion.

Result: On the public MBU benchmark with a YOLOv5s-family protocol, LER-YOLO achieves 89.7±0.2% AP50 across three independent seeds, with a best result of 89.9%. Extensive ablations, synthetic-shift tests, and complexity analyses indicate the improvements stem from reliability-guided expert routing rather than mere capacity gain.

Conclusion: Reliability-guided routing within a sparse mixture-of-experts framework effectively mitigates misalignment-induced artifacts and enables robust cross-modal fusion, yielding state-of-the-art-like performance on RGB-IR UAV detection while maintaining model efficiency.

Abstract: Detecting small unmanned aerial vehicles from RGB-infrared remote-sensing pairs remains challenging due to tiny target scale, cluttered backgrounds, and spatial misalignment between heterogeneous sensors. Existing bimodal detectors often align or fuse features without assessing the reliability of local cross-sensor correspondence, allowing mismatch artifacts to propagate into the detection head. To address this issue, we propose LER-YOLO, a reliability-aware sparse mixture-of-experts framework for misaligned RGB-infrared UAV detection. LER-YOLO first introduces an Uncertainty-Aware Target Alignment module that resamples visible features toward the infrared reference and estimates a spatial reliability map. This reliability prior is then used by a Reliability-Guided Sparse MoE Fusion module to adaptively select k experts from RGB-dominant, infrared-dominant, and interactive fusion experts, enabling trustworthy cross-modal interaction while suppressing unreliable fusion. Experiments on the public MBU benchmark under a YOLOv5s-family protocol show that LER-YOLO achieves 89.7+/-0.2% AP50 over three independent seeds, with a best result of 89.9%. Extensive ablations, parameter-matched comparisons, synthetic-shift evaluations, and complexity analysis demonstrate that the gains mainly come from reliability-guided expert routing rather than increased model capacity.

</details>


### [54] [LiteViLNet: Lightweight Vision-LiDAR Fusion Network for Efficient Road Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21007)
*Daojie Peng,Bingtao Wang,Fulong Ma,Liang Zhang,Jun Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LiteViLNet is a lightweight multi-modal road segmentation network that fuses RGB texture and LiDAR geometry with a dual-stream encoder, depthwise separable convolutions, MSFM fusion, and a large-kernel-bridge for linear-complexity long-range modeling, achieving strong accuracy with low parameters and real-time edge-device inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable accurate, real-time road segmentation on resource-constrained edge devices, addressing the high computational cost of transformer-based multi-modal methods.

Method: A dual-stream lightweight encoder extracts hierarchical features from RGB and LiDAR using depthwise separable convolutions. A Multi-Scale Feature Fusion Module (MSFM) enables cross-modal interaction at multiple levels, and a large-kernel-bridge module captures long-range dependencies with linear complexity.

Result: The model has 14.04M parameters, achieves 96.36% MaxF (best among CNN-based methods and comparable to larger transformers), and runs at 163.79 FPS on RTX 4060 Ti (22.18 FPS on Jetson Orin NX). This reflects superior inference speed while maintaining competitive accuracy on KITTI Road and real-world data.

Conclusion: LiteViLNet demonstrates the viability of real-time, embedded multi-modal road segmentation with strong accuracy and efficiency, suitable for autonomous driving and intelligent robotics.

Abstract: Road segmentation is a fundamental perception task for autonomous driving and intelligent robotic systems, requiring both high accuracy and real-time inference, especially for deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. Existing multi-modal road segmentation methods often rely on heavy transformer-based encoders to achieve state-of-the-art performance, but their enormous computational cost prohibits real-time deployment on embedded platforms. To address this dilemma, we propose \textbf{LiteViLNet}, a lightweight multi-modal network that fuses RGB texture information and LiDAR geometric information for efficient road segmentation. Specifically, we design a dual-stream lightweight encoder and depth-wise separable convolutions to extract hierarchical features from both modalities with minimal parameters. We further propose a Multi-Scale Feature Fusion Module (MSFM) to facilitate cross-modal interaction at different levels, and a large-kernel-bridge module to capture long-range dependencies with linear complexity. Extensive experiments on the KITTI Road dataset and real-world applications demonstrate that LiteViLNet achieves a promising balance between accuracy and efficiency. Notably, with only 14.04M parameters, our model attains a 96.36\% MaxF score, ranking the best among all CNN-based methods and being comparable to larger transformer-based models, and runs at 163.79 FPS in model-only inference on RTX 4060 Ti (22.18 FPS on Jetson Orin NX). It outperforms numerous heavy-weight methods in inference speed while maintaining highly competitive accuracy, fully validating the potential of LiteViLNet for real-time embedded deployment in autonomous driving and intelligent robotics.

</details>


### [55] [GSA-YOLO: A High-Efficiency Framework via Structured Sparsity and Adaptive Knowledge Distillation for Real-Time X-ray Security Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20669)
*Jiahao Kong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight, real-time detector (GSA-YOLO) for X-ray security that uses Group Lasso in the neck, Sparse Structure Selection in the head, and Adaptive Knowledge Distillation to boost robustness and efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art speed and improved mAP on HiXray and PIDray.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: X-ray security screening requires reliable detection under heavy occlusion and clutter with strict real-time performance; existing models trade accuracy for speed. There is a need for models that are both accurate and computationally efficient.

Method: Extend YOLOv8n with three components: (1) Group Lasso (GL) applied to the neck to enforce structured sparsity and robust feature extraction; (2) Sparse Structure Selection (SSS) applied to the detection head to prune redundant channels and slim the model; (3) Adaptive Knowledge Distillation (Ada-KD) to recover accuracy by distilling information from a stronger teacher adaptively during training. Evaluate on HiXray and PIDray datasets, reporting inference speed and FLOPs.

Result: GSA-YOLO achieves 189.62 FPS with reduced computation from 8.7G to 8.0G. It reports mAP50:95 of 0.531 (HiXray) and 0.679 (PIDray), corresponding to improvements of 2.4% and 1.8% over the baseline YOLOv8n. The approach maintains or enhances accuracy while improving efficiency across two X-ray datasets.

Conclusion: The integrated sparsity and knowledge-transfer strategy yields a robust, efficient detector suitable for practical X-ray security inspection, outperforming the baseline in accuracy with less computation and higher inference speed.

Abstract: X-ray security inspection requires accurate real-time detection of prohibited items, but existing models often struggle to balance the challenges of severe occlusion, complex clutter, and strict speed requirements. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes GSA-YOLO, a novel lightweight framework built upon the YOLOv8n architecture, specifically engineered to enhance detection robustness and inference efficiency. GSA-YOLO strategically integrates structured sparsity and adaptive knowledge transfer through three core components: Group Lasso (GL) applied to the network neck for robust feature extraction; Sparse Structure Selection (SSS) applied to the detection head for significant model slimming; and an Adaptive Knowledge Distillation (Ada-KD) mechanism for comprehensive accuracy recovery. This integrated approach synergistically enhances feature representation while pruning redundant channels, maximizing model efficiency without sacrificing performance. Rigorous evaluations on the HiXray and PIDray datasets confirm GSA-YOLO's comprehensive capability, achieving a leading inference speed of 189.62 FPS, accompanied by a reduction in computational cost from 8.7G to 8.0G. Crucially, GSA-YOLO secures mAP50:95 results of 0.531 and 0.679 on HiXray and PIDray, demonstrating 2.4% and 1.8% improvements over the baseline, respectively. Compared to other models, GSA-YOLO exhibits enhanced accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency, making it a promising solution for practical X-ray security inspection.

</details>


### [56] [Grounding Driving VLA via Inverse Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21061)
*Junsung Park,Hyunjung Shim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Recast Driving Visual Language Models as inverse kinematics; add next-visual-state prediction and a separate IK diffusion network; a 0.5B model matches 7–8B VLAs on NAVSIM-v2 and nuScenes, with improved visual grounding, especially in dynamic turns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLAs currently rely on ego status and textual shortcuts due to a structurally ill-posed trajectory recovery task that uses only current state as boundary condition; need boundary conditions that include future visual information to enable true visual grounding.

Method: (1) Next-visual-state prediction objective to predict future visual scene, providing dense visual supervision and discouraging shortcuts. (2) An inverse-kinematics network: a cross-attention-based conditional diffusion model that takes only current and future visual states as input to decode trajectories, suppressing reliance on ego status and text commands.

Result: A 0.5B-scale model attains trajectory planning performance comparable to 7–8B VLAs on NAVSIM-v2 and nuScenes, closing the gap via improved visual grounding. Absent longer training, the gains are attributed to recovered use of visual features, most pronounced during dynamic maneuvers like turning.

Conclusion: Framing trajectory recovery as an inverse IK problem with dense future supervision and a dedicated IK decoder mitigates shortcut pathways, enabling efficient, visual-grounded planning at smaller model scales and across challenging driving scenarios.

Abstract: Existing Driving VLAs predict trajectories while largely ignoring their visual tokens -- a phenomenon we trace not to insufficient training but to a structurally ill-posed task formulation. We show that trajectory recovery, when viewed through the lens of inverse kinematics, requires both a current and a future visual state as boundary conditions; existing VLAs supply only the former, which encourages the model to shortcut through ego status and text commands alone. To address this, we re-design Driving VLA in the style of an inverse kinematics solver. First, a next visual state prediction objective that requires the LLM to predict the future visual scene provides dense visual supervision and suppresses shortcut paths. Second, a separate Inverse Kinematics Network (a cross-attention-based conditional diffusion model) that takes only the current and future visual states as input is designed to suppress reliance on ego status and textual shortcuts during trajectory decoding. With this simple prescription alone, our 0.5B-scale model recovers visual grounding and reaches trajectory planning performance comparable to 7B--8B VLAs more than an order of magnitude larger, on both the closed-loop NAVSIM-v2 and the nuScenes benchmarks. Extensive analysis further shows that this improvement stems from a recovered ability to exploit visual features, with the effect being most pronounced in dynamic driving situations such as turning.

</details>


### [57] [VISTAQA: Benchmarking Joint Visual Question Answering and Pixel-Level Evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20676)
*Mozhgan Nasr Azadani,Yimu Wang,Yongpeng Zhu,Lihong Chen,Milan Ganai,Sean Sedwards,Marco Pavone,Krzysztof Czarnecki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces VISTAQA, a joint benchmark for answer correctness and pixel-level grounding in visual QA, plus GROVE as a joint evaluation metric; results show a gap between textual accuracy and visual grounding in current models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to explicitly align model reasoning with visual evidence in multimodal reasoning, as existing benchmarks evaluate textual accuracy or grounding in isolation, leading to opaque predictions.

Method: VISTAQA: 1,157 expert-curated samples across six task types and six visual domains; requires free-form answers plus precise segmentation masks; includes hallucination-aware examples. GROVE: a unified metric combining textual accuracy and grounding quality via per-sample geometric mean.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows even top systems perform poorly under GROVE, revealing a substantial gap between answer correctness and evidence alignment.

Conclusion: Joint evaluation of reasoning and grounding is essential; VISTAQA and GROVE reveal that current models struggle to provide reliable, grounded explanations, guiding future research toward better alignment between prediction and visual evidence.

Abstract: Establishing a clear link between model predictions and the visual evidence that supports them is critical for transparency and reliability in multimodal reasoning, yet current multimodal large language model (MLLM) evaluations do not explicitly enforce this alignment. Existing benchmarks assess either textual answer correctness or pixel-level localization in isolation, leaving the coupling of reasoning and grounding an open challenge. We introduce VISTAQA, a comprehensive benchmark for joint evaluation of free-form answer correctness and pixel-level evidence grounding in visual question answering. VISTAQA comprises 1,157 expert-curated samples spanning six task types and six visual domains, ranging from direct perception to compositional and relational reasoning. VISTAQA requires models to not only answer correctly, but to also provide precise segmentation masks that support their answers. It also includes hallucination-aware examples where no valid visual evidence exists. To support this enhanced evaluation, we introduce GROVE, a unified evaluation metric that enforces joint correctness by combining textual accuracy and grounding quality via a per-sample geometric mean, ensuring neither dimension can compensate for deficiencies in the other. Comprehensive experiments across grounding-aware models and hybrid pipelines with general-purpose MLLMs reveal that even the strongest systems achieve limited performance under GROVE, highlighting a substantial gap between answer accuracy and visual evidence alignment.

</details>


### [58] [Comparative Analysis of Military Detection Using Drone Imagery Across Multiple Visual Spectrums](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21157)
*Sourov Roy Shuvo,Prajwal Panth,Rajesh Chowdhury,Sorup Chakraborty,Sudip Chakrabarty,Prasant Kumar Pattnaik*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes evaluating drone object detection under four imaging modalities using a dataset derived from KIIT-MiTA, training YOLOv11-small to improve detection in diverse real-world conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address real-world variability in drone imagery (visibility, heat signatures, night conditions) and improve detection reliability for military surveillance and operations.

Method: Create four modality-specific subsets from KIIT-MiTA: Gray Scale, Thermal Vision, Night Vision, Obscura Vision. Train YOLOv11-small to detect objects across these settings; assess robustness and potential cross-modality generalization.

Result: The abstract claims improved performance and reliability of drone-based detection across the varied datasets, though it does not provide quantitative metrics.

Conclusion: The work contributes to robust object-detection capabilities for drone operations in defense contexts; it highlights the need for comprehensive evaluation across realistic conditions and raises consideration of ethical implications of militarized surveillance.

Abstract: In modern warfare, drones are becoming an essential part of intelligence gathering and carrying out precise attacks in different kinds of hostile environments. Their ability to operate in real-time and hostile environments from a safe distance makes them invaluable for surveillance and military operations. The KIIT-MiTA dataset is comprised of images of different military scenarios taken from drones, and these provide a foundation for detecting military objects, but it does not take into account the various types of real-world scenarios. With that in mind, to evaluate how the models are performing under varying conditions, four different types of datasets are created: Gray Scale, Thermal Vision, Night Vision, and Obscura Vision. These simulate the real-world environments such as low visibility, heat-based imagery, and nighttime conditions. The YOLOv11-small model is trained and used to detect objects across diverse settings. This research boosts the performance and reliability of drone-based operations by contributing to the development of advanced detection systems in both defensive and offensive missions.

</details>


### [59] [DarkShake-DVS: Event-based Human Action Recognition under Low-light andShaking Camera Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20680)
*Jiaqi Chen,Qinfu Xu,Liyuan Pan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes EIS-HAR, a two-module framework combining motion-compensated event streams and a four-stage hybrid HAR architecture, and introduces DarkShake-DVS as a large low-light, 6-DoF event-based HAR benchmark with synchronized IMU; shows superior performance against SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HAR in real-world settings often involves low-light conditions and unconstrained 6-DoF camera motion, which degrade quality and temporal coherence. Event cameras offer high sensitivity and microsecond-scale temporal resolution, and when paired with IMU data, can address these challenges. However, there is a lack of a benchmark integrating low-light, 6-DoF motion, and synchronized IMU data, and existing motion compensation techniques are inadequate.

Method: Introduce EIS-HAR with two components: (1) EIS module employing a non-linear warping function to reconstruct a motion-compensated input and reduce motion blur; (2) HAR module with a four-stage hybrid architecture to efficiently extract spatiotemporal features for action recognition. To mitigate data scarcity, present DarkShake-DVS, a large-scale event-based HAR benchmark consisting of 18,041 real-world clips captured under low-light and strong 6-DoF motion with synchronized IMU data.

Result: Extensive experiments on three datasets demonstrate that EIS-HAR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The approach provides a practical HAR solution under challenging conditions and introduces a benchmark to accelerate research in low-light, motion-rich, event-based action recognition with IMU information.

Abstract: Human Action Recognition (HAR) is a fundamental computer vision task with diverse real-world applications. Practical deployments often involve low-light environments and unconstrained 6-DoF camera motion, conditions that degrade visual quality, disrupt temporal coherence, and compromise reliability of existing methods. Event cameras, with high low-light sensitivity and microsecond-level temporal resolution, paired with an inertial measurement unit (IMU), present a promising solution. However, current research faces two key challenges: absence of a benchmark integrating low-light conditions, 6-DoF motion, and synchronized IMU data; and lack of effective motion compensation techniques. To address these, we propose Event-IMU Stabilized HAR (EIS-HAR), with two modules. The first is an EIS module that reduces motion blur via a non-linear warping function to reconstruct a motion-compensated input. The second is a HAR module with a four-stage hybrid architecture to efficiently extract spatiotemporal features for accurate action recognition. To alleviate data scarcity, we introduce DarkShake-DVS, the first large-scale event-based HAR benchmark that includes 18,041 realworld clips captured in low light and intense 6-DoF motion, supplemented by synchronized IMU data. Extensive experiments on three datasets demonstrate consistent superiority of EIS-HAR over state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [60] [Hyper-V2X: Hypernetworks for Estimating Epistemic and Aleatoric Uncertainty in Cooperative Bird's-Eye-View Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21309)
*Abhishek Dinkar Jagtap,Sanath Tiptur Sadashivaiah,Andreas Festag*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hyper-V2X uses a Bayesian hypernetwork with partial weight generation and a V2X context embedding module to produce stochastic BEV segmentation in a cooperative V2X setting, enabling uncertainty quantification with low overhead; architecture-agnostic and compatible with backbones like CoBEVT, showing calibrated uncertainties on OPV2V.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty quantification in cooperative V2X perception is under-explored. There is a need for reliable, calibrated uncertainty estimates to improve decision-making in multi-agent autonomous driving systems.

Method: A partial weight generation scheme plus a V2X context embedding module condition a Bayesian hypernetwork on fused multi-agent features to generate weight distributions for stochastic BEV segmentation. The framework is architecture-agnostic and can integrate with existing cooperative backbones such as CoBEVT.

Result: On the OPV2V benchmark, Hyper-V2X yields accurate, well-calibrated uncertainty estimates and improves overall perception reliability with low computational overhead.

Conclusion: Hyper-V2X provides efficient, architecture-agnostic uncertainty estimation for cooperative V2X perception, enabling more reliable autonomous driving without significant extra computation; code and benchmark are publicly available.

Abstract: Cooperative perception enabled by Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication enhances autonomous driving safety by creating a unified environmental representation through shared sensory data. While recent works have advanced multi-agent fusion for improved perception, uncertainty quantification in such cooperative frameworks remains largely unexplored. This paper introduces Hyper-V2X, a hypernetwork-based framework for estimating both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties in V2X-based perception. Specifically, we propose a partial weight generation scheme and V2X context embedding module that conditions a Bayesian hypernetwork on fused multi-agent features to generate weight distributions for stochastic Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) segmentation. Unlike existing deterministic BEV models, Hyper-V2X enables efficient uncertainty estimation with little computation overhead. Our approach is architecture-agnostic, and can be seamlessly integrating with modern cooperative backbones such as CoBEVT. Experiments on the OPV2V benchmark demonstrate that Hyper-V2X provides accurate, well-calibrated uncertainty estimates and improves overall perception reliability. Our code and benchmark are publicly available under an open-source license: https://github.com/abhishekjagtap1/Hyper-V2X

</details>


### [61] [IndusAgent: Reinforcing Open-Vocabulary Industrial Anomaly Detection with Agentic Tools](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20682)
*Rongbin Tan,Fangfang Lin,Zhenlong Yuan,Min Qiu,Kejin Cui,Mengmeng Wang,Yi Wang,Zijian Song,Zhiyuan Wang,Jiyuan Wang,Yue Wang,Shuhan Song§,Huawei Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes IndusAgent, a tool-augmented agent framework for open-vocabulary industrial anomaly detection; introduces Indus-CoT dataset and dynamic tool orchestration; employs a gated RL objective; achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot on five industrial benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of multimodal LLMs in open-vocabulary industrial anomaly detection, namely domain-misaligned reasoning and hallucinated inferences, by combining structured supervision with external tool usage.

Method: Construct Indus-CoT: a structured dataset with global visuals, high-resolution local patches, and expert normalcy priors for fine-tuning on industrial inspection trajectories. Develop IndusAgent to dynamically orchestrate tools (dynamic region cropping, high-frequency feature enhancement, prior retrieval) to resolve visual ambiguities and disentangle subtle anomalies. Introduce a gated reinforcement learning objective that jointly optimizes anomaly classification, localization accuracy, anomaly type reasoning, and efficient tool usage, ensuring tools are used only when beneficial.

Result: Empirical evaluation on five industrial anomaly benchmarks (MVTec-AD, VisA, MPDD, DTD, SDD) shows IndusAgent achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance among existing methods, indicating strong robustness and generalization.

Conclusion: IndusAgent offers a robust, scalable, tool-augmented framework for open-vocabulary industrial anomaly detection, improving detection/localization and reducing hallucinations, with demonstrated strong generalization across diverse industrial datasets.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown remarkable capability in bridging visual perception and textual reasoning, enabling zero-shot understanding across diverse industrial scenarios. However, their performance in open-vocabulary industrial anomaly detection (IAD) is often limited by domain-misaligned reasoning and hallucinated structural inferences. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{IndusAgent}, a tool-augmented agentic framework for open-vocabulary IAD. Specifically, we first construct \textbf{Indus-CoT}, a structured dataset that integrates global visual observations, high-resolution local patches, and expert normalcy priors, providing supervision for fine-tuning the model on rigorous industrial inspection trajectories. Building on this, IndusAgent dynamically orchestrates a set of external tools, including dynamic region cropping, high-frequency feature enhancement, and prior retrieval, thus enabling the agent to actively resolve visual ambiguities and disentangle subtle anomalies. Furthermore, we introduce a gated reinforcement learning objective that jointly optimizes anomaly classification, localization accuracy, anomaly type reasoning, and efficient tool usage, ensuring that tool invocation occurs only when beneficial. Extensive evaluations on five industrial anomaly benchmarks, including MVTec-AD, VisA, MPDD, DTD, and SDD, demonstrate that IndusAgent achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance among all existing methods, validating our robustness and generalization capacity.

</details>


### [62] [Closed Loop Dynamic Driving Data Mixture for Real-Synthetic Co-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21372)
*Hongzhi Ruan,Pei Liu,Weiliang Ma,Zhengning Li,Xueyang Zhang,Jun Ma,Dan Xu,Kun Zhan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AutoScale provides a fully automated closed-loop data engine for end-to-end driving leveraging synthetic data with a Graph-RAE representation, Cluster-GA for importance estimation, and cluster-guided retrieval to selectively sample high-value data under budget constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling real-world driving data is expensive and scene-biased; naive incorporation of abundant synthetic data causes inefficiency and distribution shifts. A guided, dynamic data mixture strategy is needed to maximize model performance within training budgets.

Method: Proposes AutoScale: a closed-loop data engine that unifies scene representation (Graph Regularized AutoEncoder), data mixture optimization (Cluster-aware Gradient Ascent for cluster-wise importance and reweighting), retrieval (cluster-guided vector selection), and model training/evaluation with feedback. Experiments on NavSim validate improvements over vanilla co-training and cross-domain baselines with fewer synthetic samples.

Result: AutoScale outperforms vanilla co-training and cross-domain baselines on NavSim, achieving better performance with fewer synthetic samples under constrained budgets.

Conclusion: Automated, closed-loop data optimization is effective for data-efficient, end-to-end driving; graph-regularized scene representations and cluster-guided sampling are key enablers for mitigating distribution shifts and maximizing model performance under data scarcity.

Abstract: Data scaling is fundamental to modern deep learning, and grows increasingly critical as autonomous driving shifts to end-to-end learning. Real-world driving data is expensive to annotate and scene-biased, making real-synthetic co-training with near-infinite synthetic data a promising direction. However, naively incorporating all available synthetic data is inefficient and leads to distribution shifts, and optimizing data mixture under practical training budgets remains a critical yet under-explored problem. In this sense, we claim that the mixture of training data requires clear guidance in terms of scene types and quantities. Particularly in this work, we conceptualize the data mixture approximately as a dynamic optimization process that iteratively adjusts the training data mixture to maximize model performance, guided by closed-loop evaluation feedback, and propose AutoScale, a fully automated closed-loop data engine unifying scene representation, data mixture optimization and retrieval, as well as model training and evaluation. Specifically, we propose Graph Regularized AutoEncoder (Graph-RAE) for driving scene representations, introduce Cluster-aware Gradient Ascent (Cluster-GA) for cluster-wise importance estimation and reweighting, and perform cluster-guided vector retrieval to select high-value samples. Experiments on NavSim demonstrate that AutoScale outperforms vanilla co-training and cross-domain baselines, achieving better performance with fewer synthetic samples under constrained budgets.

</details>


### [63] [Rethinking Cross-Layer Information Routing in Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20708)
*Chao Xu,Maohua Li,Qirui Li,Yixuan Xu,Yanke Zhou,Yunhe Li,Cuifeng Shen,Hanlin Tang,Kan Liu,Tao Lan,Lin Qu,Shao-Qun Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DAR replaces traditional residual connections in diffusion transformers with a learnable, timestep-adaptive, non-incremental cross-layer routing mechanism that aggregates history across sublayers, addressing residual-related issues to improve efficiency and sample quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard residual addition in diffusion transformers propagates information monotonically, causes gradient decay and block-wise redundancy across depth and timesteps, hindering training efficiency and model quality. A cross-layer routing mechanism that adaptively aggregates past activations could improve information flow and enable faster convergence.

Method: Propose Diffusion-Adaptive Routing (DAR) as a drop-in residual replacement that performs learnable, timestep-adaptive, non-incremental aggregation over the history of sublayer outputs. DAR is designed to be compatible with modern Transformer enhancements (e.g., REPA) and can be applied during pretraining and finetuning to re-route cross-layer information.

Result: On ImageNet 256×256, DAR improves SiT-XL/2 by 2.11 FID (7.56 vs 9.67) and matches converged quality with 8.75× fewer training iterations. When combined with REPA, it yields ~2× training acceleration in early stages. DAR is compatible with REPA and can be applied during finetuning, preserving high-frequency details during Distribution Matching Distillation.

Conclusion: Cross-layer information routing is a promising, orthogonal design axis for diffusion modeling. DAR effectively mitigates residual-related issues, improves sample efficiency and quality, and is compatible with existing Transformer enhancements, with applicability to pretraining and fine-tuning of large-scale diffusion models.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have become a de facto backbone of modern visual generation, and nearly every major axis of their design -- tokenization, attention, conditioning, objectives, and latent autoencoders -- has been extensively revisited. The residual stream that governs how information accumulates across layers, however, has been directly inherited from the original Transformer. In this paper, we present a systematic empirical analysis of cross-layer information flow in DiTs, jointly along depth and denoising timestep, and identify three concrete symptoms of traditional residual addition, namely monotonic forward magnitude inflation, sharp backward gradient decay, and pronounced block-wise redundancy. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose Diffusion-Adaptive Routing (\textsc{DAR}), a drop-in residual replacement that performs \emph{learnable, timestep-adaptive, and non-incremental} aggregation over the history of sublayer outputs. Moreover, the proposed \textsc{DAR} is compatible with many modern Transformer enhancement methods, such as REPA. On ImageNet $256\times256$, \textsc{DAR} improves SiT-XL/2 by $2.11$ FID ($7.56$ vs.\ $9.67$) and matches the baseline's converged quality with $8.75\times$ fewer training iterations. Stacked on top of REPA, it yields a $2\times$ training acceleration in the early stage, suggesting cross-layer information routing as an underexplored design axis in diffusion modeling, one that operates orthogonally to existing representation-alignment objectives. Beyond pretraining, \textsc{DAR} can also be applied during the fine-tuning stage of large-scale T2I models and preserves high-frequency details during Distribution Matching Distillation.

</details>


### [64] [SAVER: Selective As-Needed Vision Evidence for Multimodal Information Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20713)
*Miaobo Hu,Shuhao Hu,Bokun Wang,Rui Chen,Xin Wang,Xiaobo Guo,Daren Zha,Jun Xiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAVER: a selective vision-enabled framework for multimodal NER and relation extraction that activates vision only when necessary, using a conformal gate and a submodular selector, achieving improved F1 and reduced computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal information extraction in social media is hampered when posts contain multiple images that are weakly related, redundant, or misleading. Always-on fusion wastes computation and can amplify spurious cues; the core challenge is deciding when and which images to consult for each candidate span or entity pair.

Method: Introduce SAVER with a Conformal Groundability Gate (CGG) to estimate span-level visual groundability and pair-level activation; calibrate activation thresholds on a held-out split via a conformal-style procedure with Clopper–Pearson upper bounds. When activated, use a submodular relevance–diversity selector to pick a compact evidence subset across images, aggregate with a Set Transformer, and apply an energy-inspired joint scoring head combining text, optional visual evidence, text–image consistency, and sparse routing for entity typing or relation classification.

Result: SAVER consistently improves F1 over strong text-only and always-on multimodal baselines, while reducing AURC, increasing activation coverage at a fixed risk level, and lowering FLOPs and P90 latency.

Conclusion: Selective vision-as-needed with CGG and a compact evidence selector enables efficient and accurate multimodal information extraction by avoiding unnecessary vision use while leveraging trustworthy visual evidence when needed.

Abstract: Multimodal IE in social media is difficult because a post may attach multiple images that are weakly related, redundant, or even misleading with respect to the text. In this setting, always-on multimodal fusion wastes computation and can amplify spurious visual cues. The core challenge is to decide, for each candidate span or marked entity pair, whether vision should be consulted at all and, if so, which small subset of images provides trustworthy evidence.
  We propose SAVER, a selective vision-as-needed framework for multimodal named entity recognition and multimodal relation extraction. SAVER uses a Conformal Groundability Gate (CGG) to estimate span-level visual groundability in MNER, derive pair-level activation in MRE from the two marked entities, and calibrate the activation threshold on a held-out split via a conformal-style procedure with Clopper--Pearson upper bounds. When activated, a submodular relevance--diversity selector chooses a compact evidence subset across images, which is then aggregated by a Set Transformer. An energy-inspired joint scoring head combines text, optional visual evidence, text--image consistency, and sparse routing for entity typing or relation classification.
  Experiments show that SAVER consistently improves F1 over strong text-only and always-on multimodal baselines, while reducing AURC, increasing activation coverage at a fixed risk level, and lowering FLOPs and P90 latency.

</details>


### [65] [Holistic Reliability Propagation: Decoupling Annotation and Prediction for Robust Noisy-Label](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20725)
*Jingyang Mao,Ningkang Peng,Yanhui Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Disentangles reliability estimates for noisy labels using bilevel meta-learning (alpha for true labels, beta for pseudo-labels) and Holmistic Reliability Propagation (HRP) to route objectives with reliability-aware Mixup and beta-gated contrastive signals, yielding robust improvements in multimedia classification under label noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multimedia classification, external annotations and model predictions often both contribute to noisy supervision, yet their failure modes differ. A single reliability weight can be overwhelmed when one source fails. The work motivates learning separate reliabilities for actual labels and pseudo-labels to reduce negative interference and improve robustness to noise.

Method: Introduce bilevel meta-learning that outputs two per-sample batch-normalized scalars, alpha (label reliability) and beta (pseudo-label reliability), without enforcing sum-to-one. Apply Holistic Reliability Propagation (HRP) to route these reliabilities to distinct objectives: a reliability-aware Mixup with global input gating for the label branch, and a beta-gated set of pseudo-label positives for a contrastive branch.

Result: Empirical evaluations on synthetic and real-world benchmarks show that HRP yields higher average accuracy than strong baselines, and remains competitive at the highest noise rates.

Conclusion: Disentangling reliabilities for labels and pseudo-labels via bilevel meta-learning, combined with HRP routing, enhances robustness to noisy supervision and improves multimedia classification performance, particularly under high label noise.

Abstract: Learning with noisy labels in multimedia classification often combines external annotations and model predictions into a single reliability weight, even though the two sources can fail for different reasons. We instead estimate disentangled reliabilities: bilevel meta-learning produces two batch-normalized scalars per sample, alpha for the given label and beta for the pseudo-label, without constraining them to sum to one. Holistic Reliability Propagation (HRP) then routes them to different objectives, using reliability-aware Mixup with global gating on the input branch and beta-gated pseudo-label positives on the contrastive branch. On synthetic and real-world benchmarks, HRP improves average accuracy over strong baselines and remains competitive at the highest noise rates.

</details>


### [66] [GAMR: Geometric-Aware Manifold Regularization with Virtual Outlier Synthesis for Learning with Noisy Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20727)
*Ningkang Peng,Jingyang Mao,Xiaoqian Peng,Peirong Ma,Xichen Yang,Weiguang Qu,Yanhui Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Geometry-aware Manifold Regularization Paradigm (GMRP) that reshapes feature space by synthesizing virtual outliers to create energy barriers between data manifolds, enforcing intra-class compactness and inter-class separation to improve robustness to noisy labels and enhance OOD detection; claims SOTA on CIFAR-10 under asymmetric noise and strong Open-World reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Noisy labels cause overfitting in DNNs; passive sample filtering struggles to distinguish hard samples from mislabeled ones. A geometry-centric approach that actively reshapes the feature space to separate classes and hard/noisy samples is proposed.

Method: Introduce virtual outlier samples to create energy barriers between class manifolds and impose geometric regularization that promotes intra-class compactness and inter-class separation. Can be integrated as a standalone regularization into existing sample-selection frameworks, without relying on prior noise-pattern assumptions.

Result: Reported state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks including CIFAR-10 under asymmetric noise; notable improvement in challenging noise regimes and enhanced Open-World Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection.

Conclusion: Geometry-aware regularization effectively disentangles noisy and hard samples by shaping the data geometry, offering universal robustness to label noise and improved OOD reliability, and can be adopted as a modular component in existing pipelines.

Abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) experience significant performance degradation when processing noisy labels, primarily due to overfitting on mislabeled data. Current mainstream approaches attempt to mitigate this issue by passively filtering clean samples during training. However, simple sample filtering within feature spaces degraded by noise struggles to distinguish between challenging samples and noisy samples, creating a bottleneck for model performance. We highlight for the first time the fundamental importance of actively reshaping feature space geometry for learning from noisy data. We propose a novel Geometry-aware Manifold Regularization Paradigm whose core idea is to explicitly construct energy barriers between data manifolds by actively synthesizing virtual outlier samples. By imposing geometric constraints that promote intra-class compactness and inter-class separation, this approach enhances the discriminability between hard and noisy samples, leading to the learning of more robust representations. Our regularization mechanism exhibits high universality, with effectiveness independent of any prior assumptions about noise patterns. It can be integrated as a standalone mechanism into existing sample selection frameworks, providing stronger robustness against diverse noisy environments. Experiments demonstrate that our paradigm achieves performance surpassing current state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods on multiple benchmarks, including CIFAR-10, with particularly pronounced advantages under more challenging asymmetric noise conditions. Furthermore, this paradigm significantly enhances the model's capability in Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection, ensuring superior reliability and safety for deployment in open-world scenarios.

</details>


### [67] [Early High-Frequency Injection for Geometry-Sensitive OOD Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20728)
*Chuanjie Cheng,Ningkang Peng,Chenxi Liu,Yifan He,Peirong Ma,Yanhui Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Band-wise MMD^2 analysis shows high-frequency components yield stronger ID/OOD separability; EIHF exposes high-frequency evidence pre-convolution to boost post-hoc OOD detectors without changing training; gains on CIFAR-100 / ImageNet-100 with limitations on Places shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reassess the assumption that post-hoc OOD detectors rely on representation geometry by analyzing frequency content; link between input band frequency and ID/OOD discrepancy; develop input-side intervention (EIHF) to leverage high-frequency evidence.

Method: Perform band-wise MMD^2 analysis across CE, SimCLR, SupCon, and PALM representations; introduce EIHF as an input-side modification that exposes high-frequency information before the first convolution without altering the training objective; evaluate geometry changes via Mahalanobis score overlap; empirical evaluation on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100.

Result: Low-frequency bands exhibit weaker ID/OOD feature discrepancy; high-frequency bands provide stronger separability; EIHF reshapes class-conditional feature geometry and reduces ID/OOD Mahalanobis score overlap; experiments show gains on CIFAR-100 and best average FPR95 with second-best average AUROC on ImageNet-100; limitations observed on Places shift.

Conclusion: EIHF is an effective input-side intervention for geometry-sensitive OOD detection, leveraging high-frequency cues to improve post-hoc scoring without retraining; efficacy depends on dataset and task; code available.

Abstract: Post-hoc OOD detectors score logits or features after training, so their success depends on the geometry already encoded in the representation. We revisit this assumption through a band-wise MMD^2 analysis across CE, SimCLR, SupCon, and the OOD-oriented representation method PALM. In our diagnostic, low-frequency input bands induce weaker ID/OOD feature discrepancy, whereas higher-frequency bands tend to provide stronger separability. This observation motivates EIHF, an input-side intervention that exposes high-frequency evidence before the first convolution without changing the training objective. EIHF is strongest for geometry-sensitive OOD detection: under matched training and scoring settings, it reshapes class-conditional feature geometry and reduces ID/OOD Mahalanobis score overlap. Experiments on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 show gains on CIFAR-100 and the best average FPR95 with second-best average AUROC on ImageNet-100, while also revealing a limitation on the scene-centric Places shift. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/EIHF.

</details>


### [68] [TASTE: A Designer-Annotated Multi-Dimensional Preference Dataset for AI-Generated Graphic Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20731)
*Haonan Zhu,Elad Hirsch,Alexandria Minetti,Allison Nulty,Purvanshi Mehta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TASTE provides multi-criterion human judgments for text-to-image outputs, showing that current models and evaluators have limited alignment with professional designers; a criterion-agnostic framework demonstrates designer consensus on design quality; a small model head trained on TASTE narrows the gap to expert judgment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the collapse of multi-axial designer judgments into a single verdict when evaluating T2I outputs, and to establish a criterion-aware benchmark for evaluating design quality in generated images.

Method: Collect 1,600 ratings per criterion from 10 professional designers evaluating outputs from 4 T2I models across 9 criteria, across two cohorts, plus hallucination flags. Provide a criterion-agnostic evaluation framework using Kendall's tau, majority probability, Condorcet cycles against exact iid-uniform nulls with p=4 and R=5. Evaluate existing open-weight VLM judges and dedicated T2I scorers; train a small pairwise-difference head on TASTE.

Result: All nine criteria show designer agreement surpassing random nulls; no pre-trained evaluator achieves >0.55 macro agreement with the 5-designer majority; VLM judges exhibit position bias vs content sensitivity; a trained pairwise head reaches 0.611 macro agreement, about half-way to the single-rater ceiling of 0.741.

Conclusion: TASTE enables nuanced, criterion-aware assessment of T2I outputs, revealing gaps in current automated evaluators, and demonstrating the potential of simple, targeted models trained on human-judged data to improve alignment with professional design judgments.

Abstract: Text-to-image models produce graphic design at production scale, but their supervision comes from photo-style preference data with a single overall verdict per comparison. Designers evaluate along several distinct axes, including typography, visual hierarchy, color harmony, layout, and brief fidelity, and a single label collapses them. We release TASTE (Typography, Aesthetics, Spatial, Tone, Etc.): ten professional designers ranked outputs from four current text-to-image models on nine criteria across two disjoint cohorts, yielding 1,600 ratings per criterion plus per-image hallucination flags on the holistic-preference cohorts. We pair the dataset with three contributions. First, a criterion-agnostic signal test framework, using Kendall's tau, majority probability, and Condorcet cycles against exact iid-uniform nulls at p = 4 and R = 5, places designer agreement on graphic design between food and movie preferences and photo-style image quality, with every TASTE criterion rejecting the random-rater null. Second, no pre-trained system in our benchmark, including six open-weight VLM judges from 3B to 33B parameters and three dedicated T2I scorers, HPSv2.1, PickScore-v1, and LAION-Aesthetic-V2, exceeds 0.55 macro agreement with the 5-designer majority; VLM judges trade off position bias against content sensitivity, so scaling moves along this frontier without improving accuracy. Third, a small pairwise-difference head trained on TASTE reaches 0.611, closing roughly half the gap to the 0.741 single-rater ceiling.

</details>


### [69] [Deep Attention Reweighting: Post-Hoc Attention-Based Feature Aggregation in CNNs for Disentangling Core and Spurious Features under Spurious Correlations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20732)
*Kin Whye Chew,Jingxian Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DAR replaces GAP with a learnable attention-based aggregation, retrained with the classifier, to suppress spurious correlations and avoid entanglement of core and spurious features, outperforming Deep Feature Reweighting (DFR).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GAP collapses spatially distinct core and spurious features into a single vector, entangling signals and limiting the ability to suppress spurious correlations post-hoc; this hampers generalization and fairness.

Method: Introduce Deep Attention Reweighting (DAR): an attention-based aggregation module that replaces Global Average Pooling. DAR computes adaptive spatial weights across feature maps and is trained jointly with the classification head, enabling selective suppression of spurious features before the final aggregation.

Result: DAR consistently outperforms DFR across multiple datasets and metrics; ablation studies indicate the benefit stems from mitigating GAP-induced entanglement and enabling targeted suppression of spurious features, via joint training of the attention module and classifier.

Conclusion: Attention-based aggregation (DAR) effectively mitigates GAP-induced entanglement, providing a more robust post-hoc mechanism than DFR to reduce reliance on spurious correlations and improve generalization and fairness.

Abstract: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) often exploit spurious correlations in datasets, learning superficially predictive yet causally irrelevant features, leading to poor generalization and fairness issues. Deep Feature Reweighting (DFR) is a post-hoc technique that reduces a trained model's reliance on spurious correlations by retraining its classification head on a target dataset. However, we show that DFR is fundamentally constrained by operating on entangled features, limiting its ability to amplify the core features while simultaneously suppressing the spurious ones. We trace this entanglement to the ubiquitous Global Average Pooling (GAP) layer, which indiscriminately collapses spatially distinct core and spurious features into a single representation. To address this, we propose Deep Attention Reweighting (DAR), a post-hoc attention-based aggregation module that replaces GAP and is retrained jointly with the classification head. DAR computes an adaptive weighting of spatial locations across feature maps, enabling selective suppression of spurious features before the collapse into entangled features. Across various datasets, metrics, and ablations, DAR consistently outperforms DFR, demonstrating that our attention-based aggregation mitigates GAP-induced entanglement and reduces spurious reliance.

</details>


### [70] [Sketch2MinSurf: Vision-Language Guided Generation of Editable Minimal Surfaces from Hand-Drawn Sketches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20733)
*Wenda Wang,Anqi Liu,Junqi Yang,Lei He,Luying Wang,Jiachen Lu,Weixin Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hybrid vision-language + geometric optimization to convert hand-drawn sketches into editable 3D manifolds via minimal-surface theory, with a spatial-topological encoding and S2MS-Loss; achieves high topological similarity and artifact-free, editable surfaces; code/dataset available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for editable, topologically coherent 3D surfaces from sketches; existing generative models struggle with topology and editability in downstream workflows.

Method: Fuse vision-language guidance with minimal-surface theory; represent geometry as node-edge skeleton tuples; optimize with Sketch2MinSurf Structural Loss (reward-modulated) that enforces reconstruction and topology; test on 100 sketches; compare to baselines.

Result: Topological similarity 0.844 on 100 sketches; outperforms baselines; manifolds editable, no non-manifold artifacts; public installation; data and code released.

Conclusion: Shows viability of hybrid VL + geometric optimization for sketch-to-3D with topological control; enables human-intent-driven 3D form generation; resources released.

Abstract: Converting hand-drawn sketches into structured 3D geometries remains challenging due to the difficulty of representing non-Euclidean surfaces and maintaining topological consistency. Existing generative models such as GANs, NeRFs, and diffusion architectures often fail to produce editable manifolds directly usable in downstream design workflows. We present Sketch2MinSurf, a hybrid vision-language and geometric optimization framework that integrates vision-language guidance with minimal-surface theory to generate smooth and editable 3D surfaces from hand-drawn sketches. The core of our approach is a spatial-topological encoding that represents geometry as tuples of node coordinates and real/virtual edge skeletons, enabling stable topological control during generation. We further introduce the Sketch2MinSurf Structural Loss (S2MS-Loss), a reward-modulated objective that jointly constrains geometric reconstruction and topological coherence. On a test set of 100 sketches, Sketch2MinSurf achieves a topological similarity score of 0.844, outperforming existing sketch-to-shape baselines. The generated manifolds are directly editable and free from non-manifold artifacts. A public art installation at a university showcases the method's potential for human-intent-driven 3D form generation. The dataset and code are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Sketch2MinSurf/.

</details>


### [71] [Lowering the Barrier to IREX Participation: Open-Source Algorithms, Toolkit, and Benchmarking for Iris Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20735)
*Siamul Karim Khan,Patrick J. Flynn,Adam Czajka*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents two open-source iris recognition neural nets (TripletIris with Triplet loss; ArcIris with ArcFace loss) and re-implementations of two existing methods (HDBIF and CRYPTS) under IREX compliance, plus open-source segmentation and circle-estimation models. It benchmarks these on official IREX X program evaluations and multiple public iris datasets, aiming to improve reproducibility and ease of participation in IREX submissions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to assess open-source iris recognition solutions under standardized IREX testing protocols and to lower barriers for teams to submit methods to IREX, increasing reproducibility and comparability across methods.

Method: Develop two neural-network iris recognizers trained with Triplet loss (Ban–Batch-Hard Triplet mining) and ArcFace loss; provide IREX-compliant open-source C++ submissions; provide re-implementations of HDBIF (saliency-based iris filtering) and CRYPTS (Fuchs' crypts); release open-source segmentation and circle-estimation models; evaluate all methods in the official IREX X evaluation and on multiple standard benchmarks (QFIRE, Warsaw Post-Mortem Iris, CASIA datasets, IIT Delhi, IIITD, NDIris3D, Notre Dame VIQRE); ensure CRYPTS timing constraints on 1:N searches are considered.

Result: The authors have produced open-source, submission-ready iris recognition methods and reference implementations, and subjected them to official IREX X evaluation and extensive public benchmarks. They report successful IREX participation for most methods, with timing challenges noted for CRYPTS, and provide segmentation/circle-estimation models to support future work.

Conclusion: The work advances reproducibility and standardization in iris recognition by delivering open-source, IREX-ready methods and tooling, along with broad benchmark evaluation; it lowers barriers for new entrants to IREX and provides reusable components for segmentation and circle estimation.

Abstract: This paper proposes two new open-source iris recognition algorithms, providing both Python and IREX-compliant C++ implementations to be submitted to the official IREX X program. This work has two primary goals: (a) to conduct the first-ever assessment of open-source iris recognition solutions according to IREX testing protocols, and (b) to offer a model C++ submission that significantly facilitates the entry of other teams' open-source methods into the IREX evaluation. The new methods consist of two Neural Networks trained with: (i) Triplet loss with Batch-Hard Triplet mining (TripletIris), and (ii) ArcFace loss (ArcIris). The paper also provides open-source IREX-compliant C++ implementations of two existing methods: (a) an iris image filtering-based algorithm utilizing human saliency-driven kernels (HDBIF), and (b) a human-interpretable algorithm for detecting and comparing Fuchs' crypts (CRYPTS). Except for CRYPTS, which faced timing constraints during 1:N search, these methods have undergone the official IREX X evaluation and have also been assessed using several popular academic benchmarks: Quality-Face/Iris Research Ensemble, Warsaw-Biobase Post-Mortem Iris, CASIA-Iris-Thousand-V4, CASIA-Iris-Lamp-V4, IIT Delhi Iris Database, IIITD Contact Lens Iris Database, NDIris3D, and Notre Dame Variable Iris Image Quality Release 2. Finally, this paper also provides open-source models for iris segmentation and circle estimation that can be incorporated into any new iris recognition method.

</details>


### [72] [Resolving Long-Tail Ambiguity in Unsupervised 3D Point Cloud Segmentation with Language Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20737)
*Siqi Wei,Hongbin Xu,Feng Xiao,Tian Lan,Chun Li,Ming Li,Qiuxia Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LangTail introduces language-guided hierarchical learning to address long-tail ambiguity in unsupervised 3D point cloud segmentation by injecting language-derived semantic priors into hierarchical clustering, achieving large mIoU gains on ScanNet-v2, S3DIS, and nuScenes; code will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Purely visual similarity-based clustering in unsupervised 3D segmentation tends to collapse minority classes into dominant clusters, causing severe class imbalance due to long-tail ambiguity. Language models encode balanced, fine-grained world knowledge that can guide representation learning toward underrepresented categories.

Method: Construct entity-level semantic priors from language models and inject them into a hierarchical clustering framework via contrastive alignment. The priors guide multi-granularity semantic structure formation, preventing minor classes from being absorbed by dominant clusters and yielding more discriminative representations for underrepresented categories.

Result: LangTail consistently outperforms existing methods with substantial gains on three benchmarks—+13.5, +12.9, and +8.9 mIoU on ScanNet-v2, S3DIS, and nuScenes, respectively—demonstrating the effectiveness of language priors in improving minority-class representations. Code will be released at the provided repository.

Conclusion: Language priors enable effective mitigation of long-tail ambiguity in unsupervised 3D segmentation by enriching features with balanced semantic information, suggesting potential applicability to other 3D tasks and cross-modal unsupervised learning; reproducibility is supported by code availability.

Abstract: Existing approaches for unsupervised 3D point cloud segmentation predominantly rely on a purely visual similarity-based learning-by-clustering paradigm, which suffers from a fundamental limitation: long-tail ambiguity. In such a paradigm, features of minor classes are consistently absorbed by dominant clusters, leading to severely imbalanced predictions. To address this issue, we propose LangTail, a language-guided hierarchical learning framework that leverages the balanced world knowledge encoded in language models to mitigate long-tail ambiguity in unsupervised 3D segmentation. The key idea is to establish multi-level associations between language-derived semantic priors and visually underrepresented minor classes, thereby compensating for the biased attention of purely visual clustering toward dominant classes. Specifically, LangTail first constructs an entity-level semantic prior from language models, capturing balanced and fine-grained world knowledge across categories. These priors are injected into a hierarchical clustering framework via contrastive alignment. This guides multi-granularity semantic structure formation and prevents minor classes from being absorbed by dominant clusters, yielding more discriminative representations for underrepresented categories. Extensive experiments on ScanNet-v2, S3DIS, and nuScenes demonstrate that LangTail consistently outperforms existing methods by significant margins, \ie, +13.5, +12.9, and +8.9 mIoU, respectively. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of language priors in improving the representation of minority classes in 3D point clouds. The code will be released at: https://github.com/Whisky0129/langtail_official.

</details>


### [73] [STAR-IOD: Scale-decoupled Topology Alignment with Pseudo-label Refinement for Remote Sensing Incremental Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20738)
*Yaoteng Zhang,Qing Zhou,Junyu Gao,Qi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STAR-IOD introduces a dual-module framework to address RS-IOD: Subspace-decoupled Topology Distillation (STD) for aligning inter-class topology and reducing intra-class scale variations, and Clustering-driven Pseudo-label Generator (CPG) for dynamic, class-specific pseudo-label thresholds to handle missing annotations. It also provides DIOR-IOD and DOTA-IOD datasets, achieving 1.7% and 2.1% mAP gains over SOTA, and code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RS-IOD faces catastrophic forgetting in continual learning, large intra-class scale variations, and missing annotations which mislabel old-class instances as background. These issues hinder robust knowledge transfer and long-term performance on base and novel classes.

Method: STD transfers structural knowledge by aligning inter-class topology and mitigating intra-class representation shifts due to scale. CPG uses K-Means clustering to dynamically identify class-specific confidence thresholds, enabling more accurate discrimination between true targets and background under incomplete annotations. The framework is plug-and-play and evaluated on two RS-IOD datasets; code and datasets are released.

Result: STAR-IOD outperforms prior methods by 1.7% mAP on DIOR-IOD and 2.1% mAP on DOTA-IOD, demonstrating improved mitigation of catastrophic forgetting and preserved performance on both base and novel classes.

Conclusion: STAR-IOD effectively addresses RS-IOD challenges by transferring structural knowledge with STD and improving pseudo-label reliability with CPG, contributing to robust incremental detection in remote sensing. Code and datasets are publicly available.

Abstract: Remote sensing imagery typically arrives in the form of continuous data streams. Traditional detectors often forget previously learned categories when learning new ones; therefore, research on Remote Sensing Incremental Object Detection (RS-IOD) is of great significance. However, existing methods largely overlook the intra-class scale variations prevalent in remote sensing scenes, which undermines the effectiveness of knowledge transfer and old knowledge preservation. Moreover, RS-IOD also suffers from missing annotations, which cause the model to misclassify old-class instances as background. To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework, STAR-IOD. First, we introduce a Subspace-decoupled Topology Distillation (STD) module to transfer structural knowledge, explicitly aligning inter-class topological relationships and mitigating intra-class representation discrepancies induced by scale shifts. Furthermore, we introduce the Clustering-driven Pseudo-label Generator (CPG), a plug-and-play module that leverages K-Means clustering to dynamically identify class-specific thresholds, thereby guaranteeing an accurate distinction between true positive targets and background noise and alleviating the issue of missing annotations for old classes. We also constructed two Remote Sensing Incremental Object Detection datasets, DIOR-IOD and DOTA-IOD to facilitate research on RS-IOD.
