Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches by 1.7% and 2.1% mAP on DIOR-IOD and DOTA-IOD, respectively, effectively alleviating catastrophic forgetting while preserving strong detection performance on both base and novel classes. The code and dataset are released at: https://github.com/zyt95579/STAR-IOD.

</details>


### [74] [Draw2Think: Harnessing Geometry Reasoning through Constraint Engine Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20743)
*Juncheng Hu,Jiawei Du,Xin Zhang,Joey Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Draw2Think externalizes geometric reasoning by interfacing with GeoGebra to create a constraint-checked canvas in a Propose-Draw-Verify loop, achieving high construction fidelity, improved problem-solving accuracy, and strong rendering scores across GeoGoal and GenExam-math benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide exact, auditable geometric guarantees for intermediate reasoning states in vision-language models solving geometry problems. Existing externalizations (rendered pixels or one-shot scripts) lack per-action, constraint-based guarantees.

Method: Propose-Draw-Verify loop: the model proposes a hypothesis, externalizes it onto an executable canvas bound by GeoGebra constraints, measures exact geometric quantities, and feeds structured observations back to the model, yielding a constraint-checked evolving canvas. This mechanism yields two auditable properties: Construction Fidelity and Measurement Faithfulness.

Result: Construction checks reach 95.9% predicate-level and 84.0% strict problem-level on GeoGoal. Outcome accuracy improves up to 4.1% (planar) and 16.4% (solid). Rendering scores reach 68.2% strict and 90.5% relaxed on GenExam-math.

Conclusion: Externalizing reasoning as a constraint-checked canvas grounded in a geometry solver enables auditable, grounded geometric reasoning and improved problem-solving and rendering performance, demonstrating the viability of integrating constraint engines with vision-language models.

Abstract: Vision-language models solve geometry problems with rising accuracy, yet their intermediate states remain latent and unverifiable: a relation expressed in textual reasoning or drawing code carries no guarantee that a constraint-satisfying configuration realizes it. We observe that existing externalization methods based on rendered pixels or one-shot scripts fail to provide exact, per-action geometric guarantees. Enforcing geometric relations by algebraic definition closes this gap: the workspace becomes a constraint-checked evolving canvas. We present Draw2Think, a framework that recasts geometric reasoning from latent spatial inference into agentic interaction with the GeoGebra constraint engine. In a Propose-Draw-Verify loop, Draw2Think externalizes hypotheses onto an executable canvas, measures exact geometric quantities, and feeds structured observations back to the model, so subsequent reasoning proceeds from checked canvas state grounded by the shared workspace. This externalization makes two properties separately auditable: model-level Construction Fidelity (whether the canvas realizes the intended configuration) and engine-level Measurement Faithfulness (exact values and relations from canvas constraints). Across construction, outcome, and rendering evaluations, Draw2Think builds canvases that pass 95.9% predicate-level and 84.0% strict problem-level construction checks on GeoGoal, improves outcome accuracy by up to 4.1%/16.4% on planar/solid benchmarks, and attains 68.2%/90.5% strict/relaxed rendering scores on GenExam-math. Project page is available at https://draw2think.github.io/

</details>


### [75] [SpineContextResUNet: A Computationally Efficient Residual UNet for Spine CT Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20760)
*K S Nithurshen,Saurabh J. Shigwan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight 3D CNN (SpineContextResUNet) for efficient vertebral segmentation on CT, using a Context Block with parallel multi-dilated convolutions; achieves about 88% Dice on VerSe2020 and CTSpine1K, and maintains accuracy under hardware constraints, outperforming memory-heavy transformers and baselines on edge devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational burden of state-of-the-art vertebral segmentation methods (Transformers, large ensembles) to enable deployment on resource-constrained environments and edge devices, while preserving accuracy.

Method: Propose SpineContextResUNet, a 3D Residual U-Net with a lightweight Context Block that uses parallel multi-dilated convolutions to capture long-range dependencies, avoiding RNNs and self-attention; validated on VerSe2020 and CTSpine1K; hardware-constrained comparison against bottlenecked SwinUNETR and an entrenched baseline like TotalSegmentator; designed for deployment on devices such as Nvidia Jetson Orin Nano.

Result: Dice scores: 88.17% on VerSe2020 and 88.13% on CTSpine1K. Under constrained hardware, the CNN-based model retains high accuracy while a bottlenecked SwinUNETR degrades due to limited spatial inductive biases; TotalSegmentator memory-exhausts on commodity hardware (8GB RAM), illustrating robustness of the proposed approach for edge devices.

Conclusion: A resource-efficient CNN-based segmentation approach can match or exceed transformer-based methods under hardware constraints, enabling practical point-of-care deployment; the context-block design with parallel multi-dilated convolutions provides strong long-range dependence modeling with low latency and memory footprint.

Abstract: Automated segmentation of the vertebral column in Computed Tomography (CT) scans is a prerequisite for pathological assessment and surgical planning. However, state-of-the-art methods, particularly those based on Transformers or large-scale ensembles, demand substantial GPU resources, creating a barrier for clinical adoption in resource-constrained environments or on edge devices. To address this, we introduce SpineContextResUNet, a computationally efficient 3D Residual U-Net designed for rapid spinal localization. Our architecture integrates a lightweight Context Block that employs parallel multi-dilated convolutions to capture long-range anatomical dependencies without the high latency of Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) or the memory overhead of Self-Attention mechanisms. Extensive validation on two public benchmarks, VerSe2020 and CTSpine1K, demonstrates that our model achieves a Dice score of 88.17% and 88.13% respectively. To evaluate performance under strict hardware constraints, we compared our model against a bottlenecked SwinUNETR scaled to match our ~1.7M hardware footprint. While the constrained Transformer suffers severe performance degradation due to a lack of spatial inductive biases in a limited-data regime, our CNN-based approach successfully maintains high accuracy. Crucially, heavy baselines like TotalSegmentator fail due to memory exhaustion on commodity hardware (Intel Core i5, 8GB RAM), our model performs robust inference, making it a viable solution for point-of-care diagnostics and deployment on edge platforms like the Nvidia Jetson Orin Nano.

</details>


### [76] [Diffuse to Detect: Bi-Level Sample Rebalancing with Pseudo-Label Diffusion for Point-Supervised Infrared Small-Target Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20766)
*Zhu Liu,Yuanhang Yao,Ping Qian,Zihang Chen,Risheng Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Point supervision for infrared small target detection with physics-informed pseudo-labeling and bi-level dual-update learning achieves 5x annotation efficiency and competitive accuracy with 30% data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address unstable pseudo-label evolution under cluttered, low-contrast infrared imagery and severe sample distribution imbalance in point-supervised learning.

Method: Physics-induced annotation strategy expands single-point labels into pseudo-masks by leveraging thermal radiation patterns and heat diffusion; bi-level dual-update framework jointly optimizes detector weights, sample weights, and diffusion parameters; a meta-classifier predicts sample-wise loss weights; a differentiable diffusion module refines pseudo-labels with detection feedback; integrates training with hyperparameter optimization.

Result: Five-fold annotation acceleration; superior detection accuracy; comparable performance with 30% training data across multiple datasets; code available at provided link.

Conclusion: The approach provides adaptive, stable supervision for infrared small target detection, improving annotation efficiency and robustness while maintaining high accuracy.

Abstract: Point supervision has become a scalable solution to address dense annotation for infrared small target detection, but its performance is limited by two coupled bottlenecks: unstable pseudo-label evolution in cluttered, low-contrast infrared imagery and severe sample-distribution imbalance. In this paper, we present a more adaptive and stable framework to address these issues. Leveraging the intrinsic consistency between thermal radiation patterns and heat diffusion, we propose a physics-induced annotation strategy that expands single-point labels into reliable pseudo-masks. To further enhance supervision and alleviate sample imbalance, we develop a bi-level dual-update framework that jointly optimizes detector weights, sample weights, and diffusion parameters. A meta-classifier dynamically predicts sample-wise loss weights, while a differentiable diffusion module refines pseudo-labels with detection feedback, enabling adaptive interaction between training and hyperparameter optimization. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate five-fold annotation acceleration, superior detection accuracy, and comparable performance with 30% of the training data, validating the efficiency and practicality of our approach. Our code is available at https://github.com/yuanhang-yao/diffuse-to-detect.

</details>


### [77] [VIHD: Visual Intervention-based Hallucination Detection for Medical Visual Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20772)
*Jiayi Chen,Benteng Ma,Zehui Liao,Winston Chong,Yasmeen George,Jianfei Cai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VIHD uses a visual-intervention approach to detect hallucinations in medical MLLMs by probing and masking visual tokens during decoding, computing Calibrated Semantic Entropy (CSE) as the hallucination signal, and it outperforms baselines on three medical VQA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical multimodal LLMs often hallucinate, risking misdiagnosis. Existing introspective methods rely on external perturbations and ignore internal cross-modal dependencies between generated text and visual tokens, limiting detection efficacy.

Method: Identify visually dominant decoder layers via Visual Dependency Probing (VDP); apply Visual Intervention Decoding (VID) by masking targeted visual tokens during decoding to perturb semantics; compute Calibrated Semantic Entropy (CSE) from the calibrated distribution to detect hallucinations.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three medical VQA benchmarks across two medical MLLMs shows VIHD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art hallucination detection methods.

Conclusion: Highlighting and exploiting fine-grained visual dependency during decoding is key for reliable hallucination detection in medical MLLMs; VIHD offers a principled, masking-based detection framework with publicly available code.

Abstract: While medical Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown promise in assisting diagnosis, they still frequently generate hallucinated responses that appear linguistically plausible but lack visual evidence. Such hallucinations pose risks to clinical decision-making and necessitate effective detection. Existing introspective detection methods primarily perform uncertainty estimation or logical verification by analyzing model responses conditioned on original or perturbed inputs. However, such external perturbations are often heuristic and context-agnostic, which overlooks the internal cross-modal dependency between generated tokens and related visual tokens during decoding. To address this issue, we propose VIHD, a Visual Intervention-based Hallucination Detection method that leverages targeted visual token masking to calibrate semantic entropy for more effective hallucination detection. VIHD locates visually dominant decoder layers via Visual Dependency Probing (VDP), executes Visual Intervention Decoding (VID) via token masking to calibrate the semantic distribution, and quantifies the resulting Calibrated Semantic Entropy (CSE) as a reliable hallucination signal. Extensive experiments on three medical VQA benchmarks with two medical MLLMs demonstrate that VIHD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, underscoring the importance of fine-grained visual dependency for hallucination detection. The code will be available at https://github.com/Jiayi-Chen-AU/VIHD

</details>


### [78] [AttriStory: Fine-grained Attribute Realization for Visual Storytelling with Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20777)
*Manogna Sreenivas,Rohit Kumar,Soma Biswas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces AttriStory, a benchmark for attribute realization in visual storytelling using diffusion models, plus a plug-and-play latent optimization (AttriLoss) to align cross-attention with desired attribute–object pairs during early denoising, improving fine-grained attribute rendering without changing model architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current diffusion-guided visual storytelling emphasizes character consistency across scenes but lacks a systematic approach to ensure fine-grained attributes (color, textures of clothing/accessories) are faithfully realized across multi-scene narratives.

Method: Construct AttriStory: 200 multi-scene stories across 10 artistic styles with explicit attribute specifications. Propose a plug-and-play latent optimization module that runs during early denoising. Introduce AttriLoss to maximize alignment between cross-attention maps for target attribute–object pairs and suppress spurious associations, guiding attribute localization without modifying the base architecture. Integrates with existing story-generation pipelines.

Result: Empirical results show consistent improvements in attribute realization across all baselines when incorporating AttriLoss, demonstrating that attribute realization is a complementary dimension to character consistency and can be enhanced without architectural changes.

Conclusion: Attribute realization is a distinct, complementary axis of visual storytelling. The proposed AttriLoss and AttriStory benchmark advance fine-grained, attribute-controlled story generation by enabling systematic control of attributes like color and textures across scenes.

Abstract: Visual storytelling with diffusion models has made impressive strides in maintaining character consistency across narrative scenes. However, a critical gap remains: while these methods ensure a character remains consistent across scenes, they provide no systematic method to ensure if fine-grained attributes such as color and textures of clothing, accessories are faithfully rendered in the generated images. Towards this goal, we introduce AttriStory, a benchmark enabling attribute realization in visual storytelling. We curate 200 multi-scene stories across 10 distinct artistic styles using Large Language Model. Each scene is constructed with detailed attribute specifications to enable rich visual narratives. Further, to address attribute realization, we propose a plug-and-play latent optimization module that operates during early denoising steps, when the model establishes structural and semantic content. We achieve this through AttriLoss objective designed to maximize alignment between the cross-attention maps for desired attribute-object pairs while suppressing spurious associations, guiding models to localize attributes correctly. This approach operates orthogonally to existing consistency mechanisms, integrating seamlessly with current story generation pipelines without requiring architectural modifications. Our experiments demonstrate consistent improvements on incorporating AttriLoss across all baselines. This work positions attribute realization as a distinct, complementary dimension of visual storytelling, alongside character consistency, advancing the field toward fine-grained attribute-controlled story generation. Project-page:https://manogna-s.github.io/attristory/

</details>


### [79] [Findings of the Counter Turing Test: AI-Generated Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20787)
*Rajarshi Roy,Nasrin Imanpour,Ashhar Aziz,Shashwat Bajpai,Gurpreet Singh,Shwetangshu Biswas,Kapil Wanaskar,Parth Patwa,Subhankar Ghosh,Shreyas Dixit,Nilesh Ranjan Pal,Vipula Rawte,Ritvik Garimella,Amitava Das,Amit Sheth,Vasu Sharma,Aishwarya Naresh Reganti,Vinija Jain,Aman Chadha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CT2 workshop introduces a two-task benchmark for AI-generated image detection and model attribution, using the MS COCOAI dataset; strong performance on detection, weak performance on model fingerprinting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address misinformation risks and the need for reliable detection and source attribution as synthetic image realism increases.

Method: Create CT2 competition with two tasks; assemble MS COCOAI dataset (50k synthetic images from multiple generators + real MS COCO); apply CNNs, ViTs, frequency-based analysis, contrastive learning, multimodal methods.

Result: AI-generated vs real detection achieved F1 > 0.83; model attribution F1 up to ~0.4986; implies robust detection but weak attribution; informs need for improved fingerprinting and robustness.

Conclusion: CT2 provides benchmark; highlights challenges in model attribution; suggests future work on robust fingerprinting, real-time detection, and better datasets.

Abstract: The rapid advancements in generative AI technologies, such as Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, and Midjourney, have significantly transformed the creation of synthetic visual content. While these models enable innovation across industries, they also pose serious challenges, including misinformation, disinformation, and biased content generation. The increasing realism of AI-generated images makes their detection a pressing concern for researchers, policymakers, and industry stakeholders.
  In this paper, we present the findings of the Defactify 4.0 workshop, which introduced the Counter Turing Test (CT2) for AI-Generated Image Detection. The competition consisted of two key tasks: (1) binary classification of images as either AI-generated or real and (2) identification of the specific generative model responsible for an AI-generated image. To facilitate this, we developed the MS COCOAI dataset, consisting of 50,000 synthetic images from multiple generative models alongside real-world images from the MS COCO dataset.
  Participants employed diverse detection strategies, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs), Vision Transformers (ViTs), frequency-based analysis, contrastive learning, and multimodal techniques. The results demonstrated that while AI-generated images can be detected with high accuracy (F1-score > 0.83), identifying the exact model used remains significantly more challenging (highest F1-score: 0.4986). These findings highlight the need for improved model fingerprinting, adversarial robustness, and real-time detection mechanisms.

</details>


### [80] [What Semantics Survive the Connector? Diagnosing VLM-to-DiT Alignment in Video Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20795)
*Hangyu Lin,Chao Wen,Chengming Xu,Jianxiong Gao,Jiangning Zhang,Xiaobin Hu,Yanwei Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Flow matching video generative models using prepended VLMs assume seamless alignment with DiT text embeddings; TRACE-Edit reveals alignment bottleneck degrades fine-grained semantics; proposes diagnostic dataset and protocol; four models show alignment errors, challenging lossless transfer assumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether VLM-to-DiT alignment is a bottleneck in multi-modal video editing; end-to-end evaluations conflate alignment with generation errors; need disentangled, controlled assessment.

Method: Introduce TRACE-Edit, a diagnostic dataset via controlled video composition for relation-based editing. Propose a diagnostic protocol to analyze meta-query and connector designs. Evaluate four representative model cases to study alignment effects on fine-grained semantics.

Result: Alignment degrades fine-grained structural semantics; VLM-to-DiT alignment identified as major bottleneck; existing assumptions of lossless semantic transfer are overturned; TRACE-Edit provides a foundation for evaluating multi-modal alignment architectures.

Conclusion: Better multi-modal alignment architectures are needed; avoid relying on alignment as lossless; use disentangled datasets and diagnostic protocols to identify bottlenecks; future work in designing connectors and meta-queries that preserve structure.

Abstract: Flow matching based video generative models have been increasingly relying on prepended Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to handle complex, instruction-based video editing. The prevailing assumption underlying this paradigm is that a connector module can seamlessly align the VLM's rich multi-modal reasoning with the original text embedding space of DiTs. However, we hypothesize that this alignment acts as a severe semantic bottleneck, degrading fine-grained structural variables. Verifying this is challenging, as end-to-end evaluations conflate alignment failures with generation errors, and natural datasets lack disentangled annotations. To rigorously investigate this, we propose a controlled data processing pipeline based on video composition that results in TRACE-Edit, a diagnostic dataset focusing on relation-based editing. Leveraging this dataset, we propose a comprehensive diagnostic protocol to analyze two important designs of meta-query and connector in the existing video editing models. Systematic evaluation of four representative model cases reveals that fine-grained structural semantics can be severely degraded during alignment. Our findings overturn the assumption of lossless semantic transfer, identifying the VLM-to-DiT alignment as a major bottleneck and providing a new diagnostic foundation for future multi-modal alignment architectures.

</details>


### [81] [OlmoEarth v1.1: A more efficient family of OlmoEarth models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20804)
*Gabriel Tseng,Yawen Zhang,Favyen Bastani,Henry Herzog,Joseph Redmon,Hadrien Sablon,Piper Wolters,Patrick Alan Johnson,Christopher Wilhelm,Patrick Beukema*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Significant compute-efficiency improvements for OlmoEarth: 1.7x reduction in training GPU-hours and 2.9x reduction in inference MACs on Sentinel-2 tasks, with maintained performance; training code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce resource usage and costs for training and inference of the OlmoEarth family in remote-sensing tasks while preserving performance.

Method: Introduce a set of efficiency-oriented improvements to the OlmoEarth family (architectural and/or training/inference engineering), as described in the abstract; exact techniques not detailed in the snippet.

Result: Base-model training GPU-hours reduced by 1.7x; inference MACs reduced by 2.9x on Sentinel-2 tasks; overall performance preserved.

Conclusion: The improvements yield substantial compute savings without sacrificing model performance; training code is publicly available at the provided GitHub repository.

Abstract: We present a set of improvements to the OlmoEarth family. These improvements allow us to cut compute costs during training ($1.7 \times$ reduction in GPU hours required to train our Base models) and inference ($2.9\times$ reductions in MACs on Sentinel-2 tasks), while maintaining the models' overall performance. All training code is available at github.com/allenai/olmoearth_pretrain.

</details>


### [82] [Decomposing Subject-Driven Image Generation via Intermediate Structural Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20807)
*Hanzhong Guo,Yizhou Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage, structure-guided text-to-image method with a Canny-map predictor and a subsequent renderer conditioned on appearance and structure, plus a large text-aware 100k-pair dataset with cross-view consistency; shows gains over baselines and releases data/code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-frequency identity details (logos, patterns, text) are poorly preserved by existing subject-driven T2I methods, especially with substantial edits and RGB-only pipelines; explicit structural guidance is proposed to maintain fidelity.

Method: Stage 1 predicts a Canny edge map to capture structural information; Stage 2 renders the final image conditioned on both the source appearance and the predicted structure. Additionally, an automatic pipeline constructs a 100k-pair text-aware dataset with cross-view textual consistency. Evaluation includes GPT-4.1-based assessment and a knowledge-distillation study.

Result: Experiments show clear gains over selected baselines, supporting the effectiveness of intermediate structural prediction for high-fidelity subject-driven generation.

Conclusion: Decoupling structure from appearance via intermediate structural predictions provides an effective route for preserving high-frequency identity details in subject-driven T2I; the authors plan to release the dataset and code.

Abstract: Subject-driven text-to-image generation still struggles to preserve high-frequency identity details such as logos, patterns, and text. Existing methods typically operate directly in RGB space, which often leads to detail degradation under substantial edits. We propose a two-stage framework that decouples structure from appearance by first predicting a Canny map and then rendering the final image conditioned on both the source appearance and the predicted structure. To improve text handling, we further introduce a fully automatic pipeline that constructs a 100k-pair text-aware dataset with cross-view textual consistency. Experiments, including GPT-4.1-based evaluation and a knowledge distillation study, show clear gains over selected baselines and suggest that intermediate structural prediction is an effective route for high-fidelity subject-driven generation. Our dataset and code will be made publicly available.

</details>


### [83] [Spatial Gram Alignment for Ultra-High-Resolution Image Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20808)
*Jinjin Zhang,Xiefan Guo,Di Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SGA = Spatial Gram Alignment; aligns self-similarities between generative LDM features and foundation-model priors to preserve latent space while leveraging priors for ultra-high-res T2I.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the learnability–fidelity conflict when distilling foundation-model priors into pre-trained LDMs at ultra-high resolutions; direct patch-wise feature distillation disrupts the latent manifold.

Method: Introduce a non-invasive spatial constraint that aligns internal self-similarities (gram-like statistics) of generative features with those of foundation priors; compatible with intermediate diffusion features and VAE latents in pre-trained LDMs.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance for ultra-high-resolution text-to-image synthesis, balancing global structural coherence with fine-grained details.

Conclusion: SGA provides a versatile, non-destructive means to fuse foundation priors with LDMs, preserving generative capacity while improving alignment at macro and micro scales; practical for scaling up diffusion-based synthesis.

Abstract: Modern ultra-high-resolution image synthesis relies heavily on the robust generative capacity of large-scale pre-trained Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs). While recent representation alignment methods have proven effective by distilling visual priors from foundation models (e.g., SAM or DINO) into generative latent features, scaling these approaches to pre-trained LDMs at extreme resolutions exposes a critical learnability-fidelity conflict. Specifically, forcing direct patch-wise feature distillation inherently perturbs the pre-trained latent manifold, ultimately leading to generation degradation. To address this bottleneck, we propose Spatial Gram Alignment (SGA), a novel framework that explicitly leverages the representation priors of vision foundation models while preserving the native generative capacity of LDMs. Moving beyond restrictive direct alignment, SGA imposes a non-invasive spatial constraint by aligning the internal self-similarities of the generative features with those of the foundation priors. This spatial constraint effectively establishes macroscopic structural coherence, while the native generative objectives retain the microscopic pixel-level fidelity inherent to the original LDMs. Notably, this versatile strategy integrates seamlessly across both intermediate diffusion features and VAE latents within pre-trained LDMs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SGA achieves state-of-the-art performance for ultra-high-resolution text-to-image synthesis, yielding an effective reconciliation between global structural integrity and fine-grained visual details. Code is available at https://github.com/zhang0jhon/SGA.

</details>


### [84] [OSGNet with MLLM Reranking @ Ego4D Episodic Memory Challenge 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20818)
*Yisen Feng,Leigang Qu,Haoyu Zhang,Qiaohui Chu,Meng Liu,Xuemeng Song,Weili Guan,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A reranking-based framework for Ego4D episodic memory localization that uses OSGNet-generated candidates refined by a multimodal LLM; achieves state-of-the-art (first place) in both Natural Language Queries and GoalStep tracks at CVPR 2026; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurately localizing temporal segments in long, untrimmed egocentric videos is challenging due to long horizons and the semantic gap between language and vision. Leveraging the reasoning capabilities of multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) with efficient, recall-friendly localization pipelines can improve accuracy without sacrificing efficiency.

Method: 1) Generate candidate segments with an existing localization model (OSGNet). 2) Use a multimodal large language model to evaluate and rank candidates by how well they match the given query (or goal step). 3) Select the top-ranked segment as the final prediction, thereby reranking the initial candidate set.

Result: Achieved first place in both the Natural Language Queries and GoalStep tracks at the Ego4D Episodic Memory Challenge (CVPR 2026). Code available at https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/CVPR25-OSGNet.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates effective synergy between fast candidate localization and MLLM-based cross-modal reasoning, yielding state-of-the-art results while maintaining efficiency. Strength lies in combining precise candidate generation with robust semantic alignment; limitations may include dependence on MLLM quality and the breadth of candidate coverage to ensure the true segment is considered.

Abstract: In this report, we present our champion solutions for the Natural Language Queries and GoalStep tracks of the Ego4D Episodic Memory Challenge at CVPR 2026. Both tracks require accurately localizing temporal segments from long untrimmed egocentric videos. To address these tasks, we propose a reranking-based framework that effectively leverages the strong video-language reasoning capability of multimodal large language model (MLLM) while preserving the efficiency and candidate recall of conventional localization pipelines. Specifically, we first obtain a set of candidate segments from existing localization model OSGNet, and then employ MLLM to select the segment that best matches the given query, thereby refining the final prediction. Ultimately, our method achieved first place in both the Natural Language Queries and GoalStep tracks. Our code can be found at https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/CVPR25-OSGNet.

</details>


### [85] [AIR: Amortized Image Reconstruction Framework for Self-Supervised Feed-Forward 2D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20820)
*Zhaojie Zeng,Yuesong Wang,Yawei Luo,Tao Guan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AIR is a self-supervised, feed-forward framework that replaces per-image optimization in 2D Gaussian splatting with a stage-wise residual predictor and a Predict--Optimize--Distill training loop, achieving higher reconstruction quality and much faster encoding (160–300 ms) on Kodak/DIV2K, plus an image-adaptive quantizer for compact storage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To eliminate costly per-image optimization and reliance on handcrafted priors in Gaussian-based image reconstruction, enabling fast, high-quality results.

Method: A stage-wise residual architecture progressively predicts additional Gaussian primitives from reconstruction residuals; a Stage Control mechanism activates new primitives only in under-reconstructed regions; Predict–Optimize–Distill training stabilizes multi-stage prediction by distilling short-horizon optimized increments back into the predictor; joint finetuning across stages; image-adaptive quantizer for compact Gaussian storage.

Result: Experimental evaluation on Kodak and DIV2K shows AIR achieves better reconstruction quality than representative Gaussian-based baselines while reducing encoding time to 160–300 ms.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that self-supervised, feed-forward Gaussian fitting is viable for efficient, high-quality image reconstruction, enabling fast encoding with compact storage and without per-image optimization.

Abstract: 2D Gaussian splatting provides an efficient explicit representation for image reconstruction, but existing methods still require costly per-image iterative optimization or rely on handcrafted priors for primitive allocation. We present AIR, a self-supervised feed-forward framework that amortizes iterative Gaussian fitting into a single network pass, eliminating per-image test-time optimization. AIR adopts a stage-wise residual architecture that progressively predicts additional Gaussian primitives from reconstruction residuals, together with an explicit Stage Control mechanism that activates new primitives only in under-reconstructed regions. A Predict--Optimize--Distill training strategy stabilizes multi-stage prediction by distilling short-horizon optimized Gaussian increments back into the predictor. The stabilized predictor is then jointly finetuned across stages and equipped with an image-adaptive quantizer for compact Gaussian storage. Experiments on Kodak and DIV2K show that AIR achieves better reconstruction quality than representative Gaussian-based baselines while reducing encoding time to 160--300\,ms. Code: https://github.com/whoiszzj/AIR.git

</details>


### [86] [TERDNet: Transformer Encoder-Recurrent Decoder Network for Scene Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20822)
*Jiae Yoon,Ue-Hwan Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TERDNet is a Transformer Encoder–Recurrent Decoder network for scene change detection that uses multi-level transformer features, a correlation-based fusion module, a 3-gate GRU decoder for iterative refinement, and a convolution–interpolation upsampler, achieving state-of-the-art results with robustness to viewpoint changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of (i) unequal feature importance across encoder layers in SCD, (ii) single-step decoders that constrain refinement, and (iii) limited understanding of effective encoder pretraining strategies for SCD.

Method: A transformer-based encoder extracts multi-level representations; a feature fusion module integrates correlation volumes with these features; a recurrent 3-gate-GRU decoder performs iterative refinement; a combined convolution–interpolation upsampler restores high-resolution change masks; experiments on four benchmarks; ablation studies on segmentation-based pretraining and fusion design; robustness tests against viewpoint misalignment; code released at the provided GitHub URL.

Result: TERDNet consistently outperforms prior methods and yields more accurate and detailed change masks across four public benchmarks; ablation studies confirm the benefits of segmentation-based pretraining and the proposed fusion design; robustness to viewpoint misalignment suggests readiness for real-world robotic deployment.

Conclusion: The proposed TERDNet delivers an effective SCD solution with strong multi-level feature integration and iterative refinement; key design choices—segmentation-based pretraining, correlation-based fusion, and a recurrent 3-gate decoder—drive performance gains, and the approach shows practical robustness with code available for adoption.

Abstract: In this work, we address the challenge of Scene Change Detection (SCD), where the goal is to identify variations between two images of the same location captured at different times. Existing SCD models often overlook the varying importance of features across layers, employ single-step decoders that confine refinement, and provide limited insight into encoder pretraining strategies. We propose TERDNet, a Transformer Encoder-Recurrent Decoder Network designed to overcome these limitations. TERDNet consists of a transformer-based encoder that extracts multi-level representations, a feature fusion module that integrates correlation volumes with these features, a recurrent 3-gate-GRU decoder that performs iterative refinement, and a combined convolution-interpolation upsampler that restores fine-grained resolution. Extensive experiments on four public benchmarks show that TERDNet consistently outperforms prior approaches and produces more accurate and detailed change masks. Ablation studies confirm the benefit of segmentation-based pretraining and the effectiveness of our fusion design. In addition, robustness tests under viewpoint misalignment confirm TERDNet's potential for deployment in real-world robotic systems, where reliable perception is critical. Our code is available at https://github.com/AutoCompSysLab/TERDNet.

</details>


### [87] [RelWitness: Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Graph Generation with Visual-Geometric Relation Witnesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20823)
*Minh Anh Nguyen,Quang Huy Tran,Bao Ngoc Le,Tuan Kiet Pham,Sui Yang Guang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RelWitness introduces a witness-based framework for open-vocabulary 3D scene graph generation under incomplete supervision, leveraging concrete visual–geometric cues (witnesses) to validate relations from posed RGB-D data. It uses a witness verifier and a witness-guided positive–unlabeled loss, plus decoding and audit protocols. Simulated experiments on 3DSSG/3RScan/ScanNet show desired behavior (unseen relations, precision, reduced hallucination), but all results are planning values that must be replaced by real measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-vocabulary 3D scene graph generation must generalize to unseen predicates while supervision is incomplete. In many datasets, relation annotations are selective; numerous valid object relations are unannotated, causing learning to treat them as negatives or ignore them. A robust approach must leverage visual-geometric cues that witness relations and learn from partial labels without drastic negative labeling.

Method: RelWitness defines relation witnesses as concrete cues enabling a relation to be observable: support (contact and vertical ordering), containment (enclosure), proximity (metric closeness), orientation (facing direction), and stability across views. It constructs witness records from RGB views, depth, reconstructed geometry, role-sensitive text, object-prior null views, and multi-view consistency. A witness verifier assigns unannotated relation candidates to verified missing positives, reliable negatives, or uncertain unlabeled cases. A witness-guided positive–unlabeled objective trains without turning all missing labels into negatives. Additional components include witness-consistent decoding and an RGB-D missing-relation audit protocol.

Result: In simulated manuscript-planning experiments on 3DSSG/3RScan and ScanNet-derived open-vocabulary splits, the approach exhibits the intended behavior: improved unseen-relation recognition, higher witness precision, reduced hallucination, and fewer redundant relation phrases. All numbers are planning values and must be replaced by actual measurements before submission.

Conclusion: RelWitness offers a principled framework to tackle open-vocabulary 3D scene graph generation under incomplete supervision by leveraging concrete visual–geometric witnesses and a positive–unlabeled learning paradigm, augmented with decoding and auditing steps. Real-world validation and empirical results are pending due to the planning-value nature of the reported metrics.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary 3D scene graph generation seeks to describe object instances and their relations with flexible natural-language predicates. The central difficulty is not only vocabulary expansion, but supervision reliability: relation annotations in 3D scene graph datasets are selective, and many valid object-pair relations are unannotated. We propose RelWitness, a framework for open-vocabulary 3D scene graph generation from posed RGB-D sequences under incomplete relation supervision. The key concept is a relation witness: a concrete visual-geometric cue that makes a relation observable in the captured scene. Support relations require contact and vertical ordering; containment requires enclosure; proximity requires metric closeness; orientation requires facing direction; and stable relations should persist across views where both objects are visible. RelWitness constructs relation witness records from RGB views, depth maps, reconstructed 3D geometry, role-sensitive text, object-prior null views, and multi-view consistency. A visual-geometric witness verifier assigns unannotated relation candidates to verified missing positives, reliable negatives, or uncertain unlabeled cases. A witness-guided positive-unlabeled objective then learns from incomplete annotations without turning every missing label into a negative. We further introduce witness-consistent decoding and an RGB-D missing-relation audit protocol. Simulated manuscript-planning experiments on 3DSSG/3RScan and ScanNet-derived open-vocabulary splits show the intended behavior: improved unseen-relation recognition, higher witness precision, lower hallucination, and reduced redundant relation phrases. All numerical results are planning values and must be replaced by reproduced measurements before submission

</details>


### [88] [HyDAR-Pano3D: A Hybrid Disentangled Anatomical Recovery Framework for Panoramic-to-3D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20827)
*Yaoyao Yue,Jérôme Schmid,Xiaoshuang Li,Eduardo Delamare,Jinman Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage, disentangled PR-to-CBCT reconstruction (HyDAR-Pano3D) uses a dual-encoder with SAM-derived semantic priors to generate an arch-normalized canonical volume, followed by an anatomical restoration network that deformation-maps it to native space, yielding improved 3D reconstructions and enabling robust downstream segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the ill-posed 2D-to-3D PR-to-CBCT inverse problem by avoiding a single end-to-end mapping and separating common anatomy from patient-specific morphology, reducing blur and preserving clinically relevant structures.

Method: Stage 1: Dual-encoder network fuses radiographic features with SAM-derived semantic priors to reconstruct an arch-normalized canonical volume. Stage 2: Anatomical Restoration Network learns a prior-constrained deformation field to map the canonical volume back to the native space, restoring individual morphology.

Result: Outperforms baselines on three large-scale datasets (p<0.05). Metrics: PSNR 25.76 dB, SSIM 85.70%, Dice 83.83% for overall anatomy. Synthesized volumes enable downstream segmentation: whole teeth Dice 82.4%, inferior alveolar canal Dice 72.2%.

Conclusion: A disentangled PR-to-CBCT framework preserves clinically relevant structures and enables robust anatomy-aware assessment when CBCT data is unavailable, by separating anatomy and morphology and constraining reconstruction with semantic priors.

Abstract: Panoramic radiograph (PR) is fundamentally used in routine dental care, but it inherently provides only a two-dimensional (2D) projection of complex three-dimensional (3D) craniofacial anatomy. Most existing learning-based methods attempt to computationally recover this 3D information by directly regressing native cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) volumes from PR. However, this direct mapping requires the model to simultaneously learn common anatomical structures and patient-specific morphological variations. This entangled formulation makes the ill-posed 2D-to-3D inverse problem highly ambiguous, often producing over-smoothed reconstructions with blurred anatomical boundaries. To address this, we propose HyDAR-Pano3D, a two-stage framework that reformulates PR-to-CBCT reconstruction as a disentangled anatomical recovery problem. In Stage 1, a dual-encoder network integrates radiographic features with SAM-derived semantic priors to reconstruct an arch-normalized canonical volume. In Stage 2, an Anatomical Restoration Network predicts a prior-constrained structured deformation field to map this canonical volume back to the native space, restoring individual morphological variations. Experiments on three large-scale datasets show that HyDAR-Pano3D significantly outperforms baseline methods ($p < 0.05$), achieving a 25.76 dB PSNR, 85.70\% SSIM, and an 83.83\% overall anatomical Dice score. The synthesized volumes successfully support downstream segmentation of whole teeth (82.4\% Dice) and the inferior alveolar canal (72.2\% Dice), demonstrating that our disentangled approach preserves clinically relevant structures to enable robust anatomy-aware assessment when CBCT data is unavailable.

</details>


### [89] [ArchSIBench: Benchmarking the Architectural Spatial Intelligence of Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20837)
*Qirui Shen,Wenda Wang,Jiachen Lu,Zilong Huang,Jin Bai,Lei He,Hongxuan Chen,Weixin Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ArchSIBench is a 3,000-question benchmark across five dimensions (perception, reasoning, navigation, transformation, configuration) for architectural spatial intelligence to evaluate vision-language models beyond basic spatial tasks; results show notable gaps vs. human baselines, especially in transformation and configuration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLM benchmarks cover only primitive spatial tasks (e.g., relative orientation, distance, counting) and miss higher-level architectural-space cognition (layout, circulation, zoning). A domain-informed benchmark is needed to systematically assess and advance architectural spatial intelligence.

Method: ArchSIBench introduces five core dimensions (perception, reasoning, navigation, transformation, configuration) with 17 fine-grained subtasks. Expert architectural annotators generate 3,000 QA pairs. The dataset (and evaluation protocol) enables comprehensive evaluation of architectural spatial intelligence across VLMs, with comparisons to human baselines, including trained architects.

Result: Most VLMs exhibit substantial gaps relative to human baselines, with strong inter-model variability across capability dimensions. Some state-of-the-art models approach untrained human evaluators but lag behind trained architects, particularly in spatial transformation and configuration reasoning.

Conclusion: ArchSIBench provides a systematic resource to measure and push forward architectural spatial intelligence in VLMs, enabling targeted diagnostics and progress. The dataset and code are available at HuggingFace.

Abstract: Architectural spatial intelligence, the ability to recognize and infer architectural space, is fundamental to tasks such as robot navigation, embodied interaction, and 3D scene understanding and generation. Although extensive research has evaluated the basic spatial skills of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as relative orientation, distance comparison, and object counting, these tasks cover only the most elementary levels of spatial cognition and largely overlook higher-level cognition of architectural space, including layout understanding, circulation patterns, and functional zoning. In this work, we present ArchSIBench, a Benchmark for Architectural Spatial Intelligence based on the perspectives from architecture, cognitive science, and psychology. ArchSIBench covers five core dimensions: perception, reasoning, navigation, transformation, and configuration, comprising 17 fine-grained subtasks. Through careful manual annotation by experts with architectural backgrounds, we construct 3,000 question-answer pairs to enable comprehensive evaluation of architectural spatial intelligence. Based on ArchSIBench, we evaluate various VLMs and find that the architectural spatial intelligence of most models shows significant differences from human baselines; additionally, models exhibit substantial variability across capability dimensions. Some state-of-the-art models can approach the level of human evaluators without architectural training. However, a clear gap remains compared to human evaluators with architectural training, particularly in spatial transformation and configuration reasoning. We believe that ArchSIBench will provide important insights and systematic resources for measuring and advancing the architectural spatial intelligence of VLMs. The dataset and code are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ArchSIBench/ArchSIBench.

</details>


### [90] [USV: Towards Understanding the User-generated Short-form Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20838)
*Haoyue Cheng,Su Xu,Liwei Jin,Wayne Wu,Chen Qian,Limin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: USV dataset for high-level semantic understanding of user-generated short-form videos; introduces topic recognition and video-text retrieval tasks; proposes MMF-Net and VTCL as baselines; provides benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video datasets underrepresent high-level semantic tasks for short-form content; need large-scale UGC datasets without heavy manual curation to study topic modeling and cross-modal retrieval.

Method: Collect ~224k videos from UGC platforms via label queries without extra manual verification or trimming; define two tasks; propose MMF-Net and VTCL as baseline models; run comprehensive benchmarks; provide project page.

Result: Benchmarks for topic recognition and video-text retrieval on USV; baselines offered; dataset released to facilitate future research.

Conclusion: USV enables exploration of high-level semantic understanding in short-form video and provides a resource and baselines to advance the field.

Abstract: Several large-scale video datasets have been published these years and have advanced the area of video understanding. However, the newly emerged user-generated short-form videos have rarely been studied. This paper presents USV, the User-generated Short-form Video dataset for high-level semantic video understanding. The dataset contains around 224K videos collected from UGC platforms by label queries without extra manual verification and trimming. Although video understanding has achieved plausible improvement these years, most works focus on instance-level recognition, which is not sufficient for learning the representation of the high-level semantic information of videos. Therefore, we further establish two tasks: topic recognition and video-text retrieval on USV. We propose two unified and effective baseline methods Multi-Modality Fusion Network (MMF-Net) and Video-Text Contrastive Learning (VTCL), to tackle the topic recognition task and video-text retrieval respectively, and carry out comprehensive benchmarks to facilitate future research. Our project page is https://usvdataset.github.io.

</details>


### [91] [Activation-Free Backbones for Image Recognition: Polynomial Alternatives within MetaFormer-Style Vision Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20839)
*Jeffrey Wang,Jonathan Gregory,Grigorios G. Chrysos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Activation-free polynomial backbones (PolyNeXt) replace ReLU/GELU and softmax with Hadamard-product-based polynomials in MLPs, convolutions, and attention within MetaFormer architectures, achieving competitive or superior performance across ImageNet, ADE20K, and OOD robustness at lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenging the assumption that pointwise activations and exponential softmax are essential nonlinearity sources in Vision backbones; seek more efficient, polynomial alternatives to reduce computational overhead while maintaining or improving accuracy.

Method: Introduce activation-free polynomial modules for MLPs, convolutions, and attention using Hadamard products to create polynomial input functions; integrate these modules within a MetaFormer-style backbone (PolyNeXt) and evaluate on ImageNet-1K classification, ADE20K semantic segmentation, and OOD robustness benchmarks.

Result: PolyNeXt models achieve parity or superiority to activation-based counterparts across model scales on the evaluated tasks; they also outperform prior polynomial networks with lower computational cost, demonstrating that polynomial variants of standard modules can outperform complex custom architectures.

Conclusion: Activation-free polynomial designs can replace conventional nonlinearities in vision backbones, offering competitive performance with reduced cost and complexity, and enabling simpler, polynomialized variants of standard modules.

Abstract: Modern vision backbones treat pointwise activations (e.g., ReLU, GELU) and exponential softmax as essential sources of nonlinearity, but we demonstrate they are not required within MetaFormer-style vision backbones. We design activation-free polynomial alternatives for three core primitives (MLPs, convolutions, and attention), where Hadamard products replace standard nonlinearities to yield polynomial functions of the input. These modules integrate seamlessly into existing architectures: instantiated within MetaFormer, a modular framework for vision backbones, our PolyNeXt models match or exceed activation-based counterparts across model scales on ImageNet classification, ADE20K semantic segmentation, and out-of-distribution robustness. We also substantially outperform prior polynomial networks at reduced computational cost, showing that polynomial variants of standard modules beat complex custom architectures.

</details>


### [92] [Map-Mono-Ego: Map-Grounded Global Human Pose Estimation from Monocular Egocentric Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20889)
*Hiroyuki Deguchi,Ryosuke Hori,Kotaro Amaya,Tsubasa Maruyama,Mitsunori Tada,Hideo Saito*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: We propose MapMonoEgo for monocular egocentric pose estimation with global consistency by using a pre-scanned 3D point cloud, and introduce the AIST-Living dataset that pairs egocentric video with ground-truth motion in a scanned environment; experiments show strong improvement over baselines without extra hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monocular egocentric pose estimation struggles to recover absolute location and suffers severe translational drift due to scale ambiguity, limiting long-term tracking. There is a need for a method that grounds pose estimates in a known environment using only a monocular camera.

Method: A monocular-frame based framework (MapMonoEgo) that fuses visual cues with a pre-scanned 3D point cloud to achieve global localization and pose estimation. The approach likely involves 2D-3D correspondences, map-based optimization or PnP-like localization, and temporal fusion to enforce global consistency without additional sensors. The paper also introduces a new dataset (AIST-Living) pairing egocentric video with ground-truth motion inside a scanned environment.

Result: Experimental results show the proposed method significantly surpasses state-of-the-art baselines in globally consistent egocentric pose estimation, demonstrating viability for practical monitoring tasks without specialized hardware.

Conclusion: MapMonoEgo enables globally consistent, monocular egocentric pose estimation by leveraging a pre-scanned 3D map, removing the need for extra sensors and addressing scale-driven translational drift; the AIST-Living dataset provides a benchmark for this setting and validates practical applicability.

Abstract: Monocular egocentric human pose estimation is essential for ubiquitous activity monitoring. However, understanding the user's absolute location within the environment remains a challenge. Existing methods primarily focus on relative motion from an initial position, and tend not to account for the wearer's absolute location within an environment. Furthermore, inherent scale ambiguity in monocular vision leads to severe translational drift, limiting long-term tracking without specialized multi-sensor hardware. To address this, we propose MapMonoEgo, a novel framework achieving globally consistent human pose estimation solely from a monocular camera by leveraging a pre-scanned 3D point cloud. We also introduce AIST-Living dataset, a new dataset pairing egocentric video with ground-truth motion in a scanned environment. Experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art baseline, proving its utility for practical monitoring tasks without specialized hardware.

</details>


### [93] [HDMoE: A Hierarchical Decoupling-Fusion Mixture-of-Experts Framework for Multimodal Cancer Survival Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20891)
*Huayi Wang,Haochao Ying,Yuyang Xu,Qiyao Zheng,jun wang,Cheng Zhang,Ying Sun,Jian Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HDMoE: hierarchical decoupling-fusion MoE with random feature reorganization for multimodal survival prediction; reduces redundancy before decoupling and captures fine-grained intra- and inter-modality relations using two MoE levels and RFR modules; validated on private Liver Cancer data and three TCGA cohorts; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address redundancy in modality features prior to decoupling and model fine-grained cross-modal interactions that existing decoupling-fusion methods overlook.

Method: A two-level MoE framework: first level uses shared and routed experts to denoise and extract modality-specific features; second level facilitates fine-grained inter-modality decoupling. Two Random Feature Reorganization (RFR) modules follow each MoE level to fuse intra- and inter-modality features, enabling richer local feature interactions. Evaluated on private Liver Cancer data and three TCGA datasets; code released.

Result: Demonstrates improved prognostic performance across datasets; ablations likely show the effectiveness of both MoE levels and the RFR modules in reducing redundancy and enhancing inter-modal coupling.

Conclusion: HDMoE offers a principled way to reduce redundant information before decoupling and to capture fine-grained intra- and inter-modality relationships, yielding improved survival prediction while providing code for reproducibility.

Abstract: Multimodal survival prediction, a crucial yet challenging task, demands the integration of multimodal medical data (\eg Whole Slide Images (WSIs) and Genomic Profiles) to achieve accurate prognostic modeling. Given the inherent heterogeneity across modalities, the feature decoupling-fusion paradigm has emerged as a dominant approach. However, these methods have the following shortcomings: (1) fail to reduce the redundant information of modality features before decoupling, which negatively affects the feature decoupling and fusion effect;(2) lack the ability to model the fine-grained relationships of the features and capture the local information interactions between intra- and inter-modality features. To address these issues, we propose a \underline{H}ierarchical \underline{D}ecoupling-Fusion \underline{M}ixture-\underline{o}f-\underline{E}xperts (HDMoE) framework with two levels of MoE and \underline{R}andom \underline{F}eature \underline{R}eorganization (RFR) modules.In the first-level MoE, shared experts and routed experts are employed to remove redundant information and extract fine-grained specific features within each modality, while the second-level MoE facilitates fine-grained inter-modality feature decoupling. Besides, we design two RFR modules following each level of MoE to finely fuse intra- and inter-modality features, which can help the model capture more fine-grained relationships between modalities. Extensive experimental results on our private Liver Cancer (LC) and three TCGA public datasets confirm the effectiveness of our proposed method. Codes are available at https://github.com/ZJUMAI/HDMoE.

</details>


### [94] [FruitEnsemble: MLLM-Guided Arbitration for Heterogeneous ensemble in Fine-Grained Fruit Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20892)
*Enhui Yu,Junhui Li,Ruitong Lu,Jialu Li,Youshan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage dynamic inference FruitEnsemble for fine-grained fruit classification; dataset with 306 classes and 116k samples; achieves 70.49% accuracy; uses ensemble backbones with calibration to produce Top-3, plus an arbitration with a multimodal LLM and CoT reasoning for hard samples; trained with a hard-sample aware joint loss; deployment-oriented and state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome data scarcity and high visual similarity among fruit classes; address generalization limitations of static single-model architectures in agricultural vision tasks.

Method: Stage 1: validation-calibrated weighted ensemble of heterogeneous backbones to generate a robust Top-3 candidate pool. Stage 2: expert arbitration where, if ensemble confidence < 0.6, a multimodal LLM integrates external botanical descriptions and performs CoT-based reasoning for visual verification. Training uses a hard sample-aware joint loss.

Result: Classification accuracy of 70.49% reported; dataset comprises 306 fruit categories with 116,233 samples; outperforms existing state-of-the-art models; framework claims deployment efficiency for real-world sorting and quality inspection.

Conclusion: FruitEnsemble presents a practical, scalable approach that leverages ensemble diversity and external knowledge to tackle challenging fine-grained fruit recognition, achieving strong performance and potential for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Fine-grained fruit classification is a critical yet challenging task in agricultural computer vision, primarily hindered by a severe shortage of high-quality datasets and the high visual similarity between classes. To address these challenges, we first constructed a comprehensive dataset comprising 306 fruit categories with 116,233 samples. Moreover, we propose FruitEnsemble, a practical two-stage dynamic inference framework designed to overcome the generalization limitations of static single-model architectures. In the first stage, FruitEnsemble employs a validation-calibrated weighted ensemble of heterogeneous backbones to generate a robust Top-3 candidate pool. To tackle difficult samples, we introduce an expert arbitration mechanism: when ensemble confidence falls below 0.6, a multimodal large language model (MLLM) is triggered to perform rigorous visual verification by integrating external botanical descriptions using Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. Furthermore, we optimized the training pipeline with a hard sample-aware joint loss. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FruitEnsemble achieves a classification accuracy of 70.49\% and outperforms existing state-of-the-art models. Our framework provides an efficient, deployment-oriented solution for real-world agricultural visual sorting and quality inspection tasks.

</details>


### [95] [VISTA: Technical Report for the Ego4D Short-Term Object Interaction Anticipation at EgoVis 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20901)
*Qiaohui Chu,Haoyu Zhang,Yisen Feng,Meng Liu,Weili Guan,Dongmei Jiang,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VISTA integrates StillFast-style object-centric detection with V-JEPA 2.1 short-horizon temporal context to anticipate the next egocentric human–object interaction in the Ego4D STA task, achieving state-of-the-art results via ensemble predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address short-term anticipation of future egocentric interactions by combining reliable object proposals with temporal context to forecast upcoming actions, objects, and timing.

Method: 1) Extract object proposals from the last observed high-resolution frame using a COCO-pretrained Faster R-CNN ResNet-50 FPN. 2) Extract clip-level egocentric temporal context with a frozen V-JEPA 2.1 temporal branch. 3) Inject temporal features into the detection pathway via feature modulation and ROI-level context fusion. 4) Use multi-head STA predictors for box refinement, noun and verb classification, time-to-contact regression, and interaction confidence. 5) Ensemble complementary predictions for submission robustness.

Result: On the official EgoVis 2026 server, VISTA achieved first place in the Ego4D STA Challenge. Code will be released at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates that integrating StillFast-style object-centric detection with short-horizon temporal context yields strong STA performance, with gains further amplified by ensembling.

Abstract: We propose VISTA, a V-JEPA Integrated StillFast Temporal Anticipator for the Ego4D Short-Term Object Interaction Anticipation (STA) Challenge at EgoVis 2026. Given an egocentric video timestamp, the task requires anticipating the next human-object interaction, including the future active object's bounding box, noun category, verb category, time-to-contact, and confidence score. VISTA follows a StillFast-style design that combines object-centric spatial detection with short-horizon temporal context. Specifically, a COCO-pretrained Faster R-CNN ResNet-50 FPN detector generates object proposals from the last observed high-resolution frame, while a frozen V-JEPA 2.1 temporal branch extracts clip-level egocentric context from the observed video. The temporal representation is injected into the detection pathway through feature modulation and ROI-level context fusion. The fused proposal features are then passed to multi-head STA predictors for box refinement, noun classification, verb classification, time-to-contact regression, and interaction confidence estimation. For the final submission, we further ensemble complementary predictions to improve robustness. Experimental results on the official challenge server show that VISTA achieves first place in the EgoVis 2026 Ego4D STA Challenge. Our code will be released at https://github.com/CorrineQiu/VISTA.

</details>


### [96] [JFAA: Technical Report for the EPIC-KITCHENS-100 Action Anticipation Challenge at EgoVis 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20904)
*Qiaohui Chu,Haoyu Zhang,Yisen Feng,Meng Liu,Weili Guan,Dongmei Jiang,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: JFAA is a JEPA-based action anticipation method for EK-100 that uses a frozen encoder/predictor to infer observed context features and near-future latent tokens, with a lightweight attentive probe predicting verb, noun, and action logits, plus a field-aware ensemble of epoch-level predictions; it achieves top performance on the EgoVis 2026 EK-100 challenge and will release code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inspires by representation learning and future prediction abilities (V-JEPA 2.1) to improve robustness and accuracy in action anticipation by separating feature extraction from prediction and by leveraging near-future latent information.

Method: Utilizes a frozen encoder and predictor to extract observed context features and near-future latent tokens. A lightweight attentive probe with separate task queries predicts verb, noun, and action logits. A field-aware ensemble over selected epoch-level predictions aggregates outputs, enabling each field to benefit from its most reliable candidates.

Result: Achieves first place on the EgoVis 2026 EK-100 Action Anticipation Challenge on the official challenge server.

Conclusion: Demonstrates strong performance in EK-100 action anticipation; code will be released at the provided GitHub repository.

Abstract: We propose JFAA, a JEPA-based Future Action Anticipation method for the EPIC-KITCHENS-100 (EK-100) Action Anticipation task. Inspired by the representation learning and future prediction ability of V-JEPA 2.1, JFAA uses a frozen encoder and predictor to extract observed context features and near-future latent tokens. A lightweight attentive probe is then trained to predict verb, noun, and action logits with separate task queries. To improve robustness, we further build a field-aware ensemble over selected epoch-level predictions, allowing each output field to benefit from its most reliable candidates. Experimental results on the official challenge server show that JFAA achieves first place in the EgoVis 2026 EK-100 Action Anticipation Challenge. Our code will be released at https://github.com/CorrineQiu/JFAA.

</details>


### [97] [SynCB: A Synergy Concept-Based Model with Dynamic Routing Between Concepts and Complementary Neural Branches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20908)
*Tores Julie,Sun Rémy,Sassatelli Lucile,Ancarani Elisa,Wu Hui-Yin,Precioso Frédéric*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SynCB introduces a dual-branch architecture with a concept-based (CB) path and a complementary neural path, managed by a trainable routing module; they remain distinct, with shared backbone and joint learning; add test-time intervention policy to improve human-in-the-loop responsiveness; achieves higher accuracy and better intervention responsiveness across five datasets, outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To combine the interpretability of concept-based models with the strong performance of neural networks while enabling effective test-time human interventions, addressing limitations of prior hybrids that fuse predictions and/or lack efficient intervention mechanisms.

Method: A dual-branch model where a CB branch and a complementary neural branch share a common backbone but operate as distinct modules. A trainable routing module dynamically selects which branch to use for each input. The two branches are learned jointly with information sharing through the backbone. Additionally, a test-time intervention policy and a corresponding loss are introduced to improve responsiveness to human interventions.

Result: Across five datasets and CB benchmarks, SynCB outperforms the full neural baseline by up to 3.9 percentage points and exceeds the strongest competitor in intervention performance by up to 6.43 percentage points.

Conclusion: SynCB achieves higher task accuracy while remaining more responsive to human interventions, preserving interpretability and controllability through distinct branches and a routing mechanism, and demonstrating the viability of synergy between concept-based and neural representations.

Abstract: Concept-based (CB) models provide interpretability and support test-time human intervention, while standard neural networks (NN) offer strong task performance but little transparency. Prior work has explored hybrid formulations that integrate concepts and additional representations to improve accuracy, often at the cost of human interventions. We introduce the \emph{Synergy Concept-Based Model (SynCB)} framework, that combines a CB branch with a complementary neural branch, and a trainable routing module that dynamically selects which branch to use for each input. Unlike prior models, which fuse residual and concept-based predictions, SynCB keeps the two branches distinct and coordinates them through the routing module. Moreover, both branches are learned jointly, allowing information sharing between the complementary neural branch and CB branches through their common backbone. To improve responsiveness to interventions, we further introduce a test-time intervention policy and a corresponding loss. Across five datasets and CB benchmarks, SynCB consistently achieves higher task accuracy while remaining more responsive to human interventions, surpassing the full neural baseline by up to 3.9 percentage points and exceeding the strongest competitor in intervention performance by up to 6.43 percentage points.

</details>


### [98] [FlowLong: Inference-time Long Video Generation via Manifold-constrained Tweedie Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20910)
*Jangho Park,Geon Yeong Park,Gihyun Kwon,Jong Chul Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A simple, inference-time method for long video generation using overlapping sliding windows with Tweedie matching and stochastic early-phase sampling to blend windows, enabling longer videos without training and generalizing across modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon video generation is hard due to degradation in long-range consistency and drift from exposure bias; existing training-free approaches either rely on specific architectures or accumulate errors. A training-free, architecture-agnostic method facilitates longer sequences with better temporal coherence.

Method: Generate long videos via overlapping sliding windows. Use Tweedie matching to blend overlapping regions, enforcing manifold constraints and temporal consistency. Apply stochastic early-phase sampling to inject fresh noise after each Tweedie correction during high-noise phases, then switch to deterministic ODE sampling for high fidelity. Architecture-agnostic and training-free; applicable to various video diffusion models.

Result: Produces videos several times longer than the native window length and outperforms both training-free and autoregressive baselines in temporal consistency and visual quality. Extends to audio-video joint generation and text-to-3DGS without fine-tuning.

Conclusion: An effective, training-free, architecture-agnostic approach for long-horizon video generation. The overlapping-window Tweedie matching with stochastic early-phase sampling yields improved temporal coherence and quality, and generalizes to additional modalities without fine-tuning.

Abstract: Extending the generation horizon of video diffusion models to long sequences remains a long-standing and important challenge. Existing training-free approaches fall into two categories: extensions of bidirectional models, which are tightly coupled to specific architectures and suffer from quality degradation over long horizons, and autoregressive models, which accumulate drift errors due to exposure bias and tend to produce repetitive motion patterns. To address these issues, we propose a novel but simple inference-time approach for long video generation that is architecture-agnostic and requires no additional training. Our method generates long videos via overlapping sliding windows, where predicted clean samples from adjacent windows are blended via \emph{Tweedie matching} to enforce both \textbf{manifold constraint and temporal consistency} across overlap regions. \emph{Stochastic early-phase sampling} then synchronizes per-window trajectories by injecting fresh noise after each Tweedie matching correction in the high-noise phase, before transitioning to deterministic ODE sampling to preserve fine-grained visual fidelity. Applied to various video generation models, our method generates videos several times longer than the native window length while outperforming both training-free and autoregressive baselines in temporal consistency and visual quality, and further extends to audio-video joint generation and text-to-3DGS without any fine-tuning.

</details>


### [99] [RISE: Reliable Improvement in Self-Evolving Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20914)
*Chaoran Xu,Yingmao Miao,Pengfei Zhang,Hao Dou,Lei Sun,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RISE introduces a reliable self-evolving framework for vision-language models that uses fine-grained role alternation, a quality supervisor, and skill-aware dynamic balancing to alleviate interaction delays, preserve question quality, and maintain diverse capabilities during learning; it yields consistent gains across backbones and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce reliance on costly human supervision for post-training of vision-language models by enabling self-evolving improvement using unlabeled data, while addressing key shortcomings in existing self-evolving methods (coarse role alternation, degrading questions, and collapsing question types).

Method: Three complementary designs: 1) fine-grained role alternation to shorten the feedback loop between question generation and solver adaptation; 2) a quality supervisor to improve the validity of generated questions and the reliability of pseudo-labels; 3) skill-aware dynamic balancing to prevent mode collapse and maintain broad skill coverage during evolution.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows that RISE consistently improves base VLM backbones across seven benchmarks and two backbones, demonstrating broad and sustained gains; code for RISE is publicly available.

Conclusion: RISE provides a robust framework for reliable self-evolution in vision-language models, addressing key bottlenecks in interaction efficiency, data quality, and skill diversity, and achieving consistent performance gains across diverse settings.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved strong multimodal reasoning capabilities, but further improving them still relies heavily on large-scale human-constructed supervision for post-training. Such supervision is costly to obtain, especially for reasoning-intensive multimodal tasks where questions, answers, and feedback signals must be carefully designed. This motivates self-evolving learning, where a model improves itself through a dual-role closed loop: a questioner autonomously poses questions and a solver learns to solve them. However, we observe that current VLM self-evolving methods still face three major challenges: coarse-grained role alternation delays the interaction between question generation and solver adaptation; generated questions can progressively degrade in quality; and question types may collapse toward a narrow distribution. These issues limit the efficiency and reliability of self-evolution. Thus, we propose \textbf{RISE}, a reliable self-evolving framework for vision-language models.
