RISE is built on three complementary designs: fine-grained role alternation, which shortens the feedback loop between the questioner and the solver to improve efficiency; a quality supervisor, which improves question validity and pseudo-label reliability; and skill-aware dynamic balancing, which mitigates mode collapse and maintains broad skill coverage during evolution. Together, these components enable more reliable and effective self-evolution from unlabeled images. Experiments on two VLM backbones across seven benchmarks show that RISE consistently improves the base models, yielding broad and sustained gains. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/AMAP-ML/RISE.

</details>


### [100] [3D Reconstruction and Knowledge Distillation to Improve Multi-View Image Models to Explore Spike Volume Estimation in Wheat](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20940)
*Olivia Zumsteg,Jannis Widmer,Yann Bourdé,Norbert Kirchgessner,Andreas Hund,Lukas Roth,Paraskevi Nousi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hybrid 2D-3D framework estimates wheat spike volume efficiently by distilling 3D-informed knowledge into an image-only model, achieving faster inference and improved accuracy over a 3D-augmented 2D Transformer baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-throughput field phenotyping requires accurate spike volume estimation, but active 3D sensing is noisy and expensive; pure 2D methods lack explicit geometry. The work aims to fuse 3D geometric cues with efficient 2D inference via knowledge distillation.

Method: 1) Train a rigid-invariant point cloud network using distance-based histogram features for pose-robust 3D representations. 2) Combine this 3D model with a multi-view image-based regulated Transformer (RT) in an ensemble to estimate volume. 3) Distill the ensemble knowledge into a purely image-based student using feature-based or label-based distillation.

Result: The distilled RTs outperform the non-distilled RT: MAE improves from 654.31 to 639.93 mm^3 and 644.62 mm^3; correlation rises from 0.76 to 0.77 and 0.82. Inference time drops from 160 ms to 1.4 ms per spike. Distillation mitigates volume-dependent bias and yields geometry-aware latent representations in the image model.

Conclusion: 3D-informed training of a 2D Transformer enables scalable, efficient spike volume estimation suitable for high-throughput field phenotyping, combining robust geometry with fast image-only inference.

Abstract: Accurate estimation of wheat spike volume is important for yield component analysis and stress resilience assessment, yet field-based measurement remains challenging. Active 3D sensing methods such as Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) or time-of-flight (ToF) are sensitive to plant motion or poorly suited to outdoor conditions, while 3D reconstructions are computationally expensive. Direct 2D image processing would offer computational advantages, but image-based models lack explicit geometric information. We therefore propose a hybrid 2D-3D approach with knowledge distillation during training while enabling efficient image-only inference. First, we train a rigid-invariant point cloud network using distance-based histogram features to obtain pose-robust geometric representations. We then combine the 3D model with a proposed multi-view image-based regulated Transformer (RT) in an ensemble architecture. Finally, we distill the ensemble knowledge into a purely image-based student model using either feature-based or label-based distillation. The two distilled RTs reduce the mean absolute error (MAE) from 654.31 mm$^3$ of the non-distilled RT to 639.93 mm$^3$ and 644.62 mm$^3$, and increase correlation from 0.76 to 0.77 and 0.82, respectively. At the same time, inference time is reduced from 160 ms to 1.4 ms per spike. Distillation further mitigates volume-dependent bias and reshapes the latent representation of the image model toward a geometry-aware shape. Our results demonstrate that 3D-informed training of a 2D Transformer allows for scalable and efficient spike volume estimation for high-throughput field phenotyping.

</details>


### [101] [PaintCopilot: Modeling Painting as Autonomous Artistic Continuation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20941)
*Yunge Wen,Yuancheng Shen,Paul Pu Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PaintCopilot is a co-creative neural painting assistant that treats painting as an open-ended autoregressive process conditioned on evolving canvases, introducing three models (Target Predictor, Next Stroke Predictor, Region Sampler) and four interaction workflows, enabling fluid human–AI painting collaboration without a fixed reference image.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of reference-image reconstruction in neural painting by modeling painting as ongoing artistic dynamics, enabling open-ended, co-creative workflows similar to text generation.

Method: Three differentiable brush representations (Hard Round, Brush Tip, 2D Gaussian); ViT-based Target Predictor to infer artist intent from partial canvases; autoregressive Next Stroke Predictor using flow matching to generate coherent brushstrokes; VAE-based Region Sampler to synthesize semantically localized strokes on demand; four interactive workflows (Optimize History, Stroke Completion, Region Inpainting, Dynamic Brush).

Result: Qualitative case studies with professional artists show that PaintCopilot supports fluid, alternating control between human and AI, enabling open-ended creative exploration in painting workflows.

Conclusion: PaintCopilot demonstrates feasibility of open-ended, co-creative painting driven by learned artistic dynamics rather than reference optimization, highlighting potential for richer human–AI collaboration in art and suggesting avenues for evaluation, scalability, and broader brush representations.

Abstract: We present PaintCopilot, a co-creative neural painting assistant that models painting as an open-ended autoregressive artistic behavior conditioned on evolving canvas states and prior brushstroke history, without requiring a target image. Unlike existing neural painting methods that frame painting as pixel reconstruction toward a predefined reference, PaintCopilot predicts future strokes directly from learned artistic dynamics, analogous to how large language models continue text sequences from prior context.
  The framework proposes three complementary models: a ViT-based Target Predictor that infers artist intent from partial canvas observations, an autoregressive Next Stroke Predictor that generates temporally coherent brushstrokes via flow matching, and a VAE-based Region Sampler that synthesizes semantically localized stroke sequences on demand. Built on three differentiable brush representations (Hard Round, Brush Tip, and 2D Gaussian), the system supports four interactive workflows: Optimize History, Stroke Completion, Region Inpainting, and Dynamic Brush. Through case studies with professional artists, we demonstrate that PaintCopilot enables fluid co-creative painting workflows in which artists and AI continuously alternate control throughout the creative process.

</details>


### [102] [Bridging Structure and Language: Graph-Based Visual Reasoning for Autonomous Road Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20942)
*Lena Wild,Katie Z Luo,Marco Pavone*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces the Combined Road Substrate (CRS), a graph-grounded framework that jointly represents geometric road structure and open-vocabulary semantics to enable complex, traceable reasoning for road understanding; small, CRS-enriched data suffices to train compact models for improved structured reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between vision-language models (rich semantics but weak geometry/relations) and traditional modular road representations (geometry/topology but semantically rigid), enabling joint, executable road reasoning with open vocabulary.

Method: Develop CRS as a graph-grounded substrate combining geometric/topological road structure with semantic labels. Enable automatic generation of compositionally complex, linguistically varied QA via recursive graph queries. Introduce a 'grounding for free' mechanism for traceability to map elements and procedurally extract chain-of-thought supervision traces.

Result: CRS enables generation of complex QA pairs and, when used to train small 2–4B parameter models on 20–80 CRS-enriched scenes, yields stable gains in compositional reasoning across depths. Verifiable reasoning traces show CRS-trained models shift failures from relational scene understanding to attribute recognition, suggesting supervision scarcity—not model scale—is the primary bottleneck.

Conclusion: Structured, graph-grounded supervision is essential for robust road understanding. CRS demonstrates that integrating geometry/topology with open-vocabulary semantics, plus traceable supervision, improves structured reasoning beyond what scale alone achieves.

Abstract: Structured road understanding of lane geometry, topology, and traffic element relationships is foundational to safe autonomous driving. While vision-language models (VLMs) offer promising semantic flexibility, they lack the geometric and relational grounding required for precise road reasoning. Conversely, traditional modular systems, e.g., HD maps and topological road graphs, provide structural precision but remain semantically rigid. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Combined Road Substrate (CRS), a graph-grounded framework that makes geometric road structure and open-vocabulary semantics jointly executable in a single representation. CRS enables the automatic generation of compositionally complex and linguistically varied question-answer pairs via recursive graph queries, augmented with a "grounding for free" mechanism that ensures logical traceability to specific map elements, and procedurally extracted chain-of-thought supervision traces. We demonstrate that state-of-the-art VLMs - including large, closed-source models - struggle significantly with structured road reasoning, yet training a small 2- or 4-billion-parameter model with as few as 20 to 80 CRS-enriched scenes yields stable gains in compositional reasoning tasks of varying depth. Analysis of model behavior via verifiable reasoning traces reveals a systematic shift in failure modes: whereas baseline models fail at relational scene understanding, CRS-trained models reduce failures to attribute recognition, suggesting that the primary bottleneck in road understanding is not model scale, but the absence of structured supervision.

</details>


### [103] [Focus-then-Context: Subject-Centric Progressive Visual Token Reduction for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20950)
*Yulin Zhao,Yun Wang,Dehua Zheng,Borui jiang,Zheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SPpruner introduces a subject-centric progressive pruning paradigm for vision-language models, combining focus-driven visual subject identification with context-aware structural scanning to preserve subject-context relationships while reducing tokens, yielding substantial speedups with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-language models suffer from prohibitive inference costs due to massive visual token sequences. Existing token reduction methods often preserve an isolated visual subject aligned to the query, neglecting broader saliency and contextual relations.

Method: A two-stage design: (1) focus identification module that jointly models visual saliency and semantic relevance to extract a comprehensive spectrum of visual subjects; (2) context-aware structural scanning module that aggregates neighboring-region cues to restore global relational dependencies and preserve structural integrity of subjects.

Result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art gains: up to 2.53x speedup with only 22.2% visual tokens retained on Qwen2.5-VL, and a 67% FLOPs reduction on LLaVA, with a negligible 0.6% accuracy drop.

Conclusion: Subject-centric progressive reduction and the Focus-then-Context mechanism effectively reduce computation in VLM inference while maintaining performance, outperforming existing SOTA token-reduction methods.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) face a bottleneck of prohibitive computational costs arising from massive visual token sequences during inference. Existing vision token reduction methods alleviate this burden, but they unintentionally preserve the isolated visual subject strictly aligned with the user's query, which fails to substantially explore salient subjects and their contextual relationships. In this paper, we propose SPpruner, a subject-centric progressive reduction paradigm that emulates the \textit{Focus-then-Context} mechanism of the human visual perception system. Specifically, we first construct a focus identification module to explicitly model the interplay between visual saliency and semantic relevance. Herein, it can excavate the comprehensive visual subject spectrum to ensure a high-fidelity representation of visual input. Subsequently, a context-aware structural scanning module is developed to aggregate contextual cues from neighboring regions. As such, it can effectively restore global relational dependencies to uphold the structural integrity of the preserved subjects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our paradigm consistently outperforms SOTA methods, achieving up to 2.53 times speedup with only 22.2% of visual tokens retained in Qwen2.5-VL and a 67% FLOPs reduction on LLaVA with a negligible 0.6% accuracy drop.

</details>


### [104] [DrawMotion: Generating 3D Human Motions by Freehand Drawing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20955)
*Tao Wang,Lei Jin,Zhihua Wu,Qiaozhi He,Jiaming Chu,Yu Cheng,Junliang Xing,Jian Zhao,Shuicheng Yan,Li Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DrawMotion is a diffusion-based framework for text-to-motion generation that also accepts a freehand drawing condition to provide spatial control. It introduces a Multi-Condition Module (MCM) to fuse text and drawing conditions efficiently, and a training-free guidance mechanism that enables classifier-guidance gradients to align outputs with user intent while preserving fidelity. It reports substantial time savings (≈46.7%) in user studies; code and demos are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users struggle to precisely express intended motions using text alone; combining semantic (text) and spatial (drawing) cues can improve control and fidelity in text-to-motion generation.

Method: (1) Freehand drawing condition via automatically generated stickman sketches across diverse dataset formats; (2) Multi-Condition Module (MCM) integrated into the diffusion process to fuse multiple conditions with lower computational cost; (3) Training-free guidance leveraging a continuous intermediate feature space so classifier-guidance gradients can steer features toward user intent without retraining.

Result: Quantitative evaluations and user studies show that the freehand drawing condition reduces user time by about 46.7% in generating motions aligned with user imagination; the approach demonstrates efficient multi-condition fusion and alignment without additional training.

Conclusion: DrawMotion provides an effective, efficient framework for multi-condition text-to-motion generation, combining semantic and spatial cues through a diffusion model with a flexible fusion module and training-free guidance, validated by user studies and publicly available code/demos.

Abstract: Text-to-motion generation, which translates textual descriptions into human motions, faces the challenge that users often struggle to precisely convey their intended motions through text alone. To address this issue, this paper introduces DrawMotion, an efficient diffusion-based framework designed for multi-condition scenarios. DrawMotion generates motions based on both a conventional text condition and a novel hand-drawing condition, which provide semantic and spatial control over the generated motions, respectively. Specifically, we tackle the fine-grained motion generation task from three perspectives: 1) freehand drawing condition. To accurately capture users' intended motions without requiring tedious textual input, we develop an algorithm to automatically generate hand-drawn stickman sketches across different dataset formats; 2) multi-condition fusion. We propose a Multi-Condition Module (MCM) that is integrated into the diffusion process, enabling the model to exploit all possible condition combinations while reducing computational complexity compared to conventional approaches; and 3) training-free guidance. Notably, the MCM in DrawMotion ensures that its intermediate features lie in a continuous space, allowing classifier-guidance gradients to update the features and thereby aligning the generated motions with user intentions while preserving fidelity. Quantitative experiments and user studies demonstrate that the freehand drawing approach reduces user time by approximately 46.7% when generating motions aligned with their imagination. The code, demos, and relevant data are publicly available at https://github.com/InvertedForest/DrawMotion.

</details>


### [105] [Preserve, Reveal, Expand: Faithful 4D Video Editing with Region-Aware Conditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20961)
*Zhangchi Hu,Wenzhang Sun,Xiangchen Yin,Jiahui Yuan,Chunfeng Wang,Hao Li,Kun Zhan,Xiaoyan Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes PREX, a region-aware 4D editing framework that decouples Preserve, Reveal, Expand to align source evidence with rendered content; introduces PREBench benchmark; achieves reduced region errors while preserving quality and 4D control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address evidence-role mismatch in 4D video editing where reliable source evidence, uncertain rendered cues, and unsupported regions get entangled, causing preservation drift, ghosting, and unstable extrapolation.

Method: Decompose target spatiotemporal volume into Preserve, Reveal, Expand based on observation support and scene extent; construct calibrated, observation-backed appearance cues; inject via a region-aware adapter into a frozen video diffusion backbone; train with proxy tasks without requiring paired edited videos; introduce PREBench for targeted evaluation.

Result: Experiments show PREX reduces region-structured failures while maintaining strong visual quality and 4D edit-control capability; PREBench provides curated edits and region-role masks with human-aligned metrics.

Conclusion: PREX offers a region-aware solution to faithful 4D editing and introduces PREBench for targeted evaluation, enabling preservation of observed regions, disclosure of disoccluded content, and expansion of unseen areas without paired data.

Abstract: Existing 4D-driven video diffusion models primarily target plausible generation, but faithful 4D editing requires preserving source-observed regions while synthesizing disoccluded or out-of-view content. We identify Evidence-Role Mismatch: reliable source-backed evidence, unreliable rendered cues, and unsupported regions are entangled in a single conditioning signal, causing preservation drift, ghosting, and unstable extrapolation. We propose PREX (Preserve, Reveal, Expand), a region-aware framework that decomposes the target spatiotemporal volume into Preserve, Reveal, and Expand roles according to observation support and scene extent. PREX builds observation-backed appearance cues with calibrated confidence and injects them into a frozen video diffusion backbone through a region-aware adapter, trained with proxy tasks without requiring paired edited videos. We further introduce PREBench, a diagnostic benchmark with curated edits, region-role masks, and human-aligned metrics that complement global video-quality and 4D-control evaluations. Experiments show that PREX reduces region-structured failures while maintaining strong visual quality and 4D edit control capability. Project Page: https://ricepastem.github.io/PREX-Open

</details>


### [106] [Towards UAV Detection in the Real World: A New Multispectral Dataset UAVNet-MS and a New Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20963)
*Yihang Luo,Jun Chen,Chao Xiao,Yingqian Wang,Zhaoxu Li,Qiang Ling,Xu He,Nuo Chen,Gaowei Guo,Hongge Li,Miao Li,Longguang Wang,Yulan Guo,Li Liu,Wei An,Zhijie Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduction of UAVNet-MS dataset and MFDNet baseline for fine-grained small-UAV detection using RGB-MSI data, showing MSI adds complementary material cues and improves RGB baselines, with sizable dataset and evaluation against 20 detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB-based small-UAV detection struggles with small size, low contrast, and inter-type similarity; MSI encodes material signatures to aid discrimination; lack of dedicated MSI datasets for this task.

Method: Proposes UAVNet-MS dataset (15,618 RGB-MSI cubes, 1440x1080, with bounding boxes) and MFDNet dual-stream network that fuses spatial and spectral information while addressing array-induced parallax.

Result: MFDNet achieves +6.2% AP50 over best RGB-only methods; RGB+MSI beats RGB-only; MSI provides material evidence beyond spatial cues; establishes dataset and benchmark.

Conclusion: First multispectral dataset for fine-grained small-UAV detection; strong baseline; benchmark to drive multispectral UAV monitoring research.

Abstract: The proliferation of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) has created urgent demand for precise UAV monitoring. Existing RGB-based systems rely on spatial cues that degrade at small scales, particularly with high inter-type similarity, target-clutter ambiguity, and low contrast. Multispectral imaging (MSI) encodes material-aware spectral signatures, yet MSI-based fine-grained small-UAV detection remains underexplored due to lack of dedicated datasets. We introduce UAVNet-MS, the first multispectral dataset for fine-grained small-UAV detection, comprising 15,618 temporally synchronized RGB-MSI data cubes (1440x1080) with bounding box annotations. The dataset features challenging small objects (93.7% <= 32^2 pixels, average 18^2 pixels, ~0.02% image area) under low contrast. We propose MFDNet, a dual-stream baseline addressing array-induced parallax and spatial-spectral fusion. Extensive evaluation under RGB-only, MSI-only, and RGB+MSI protocols against 20 detectors shows MFDNet achieves +6.2% AP50 improvement over best RGB-only methods, demonstrating spectral cues provide complementary material evidence beyond spatial cues. This work provides foundational dataset, strong baseline, and benchmark for multispectral UAV monitoring research.

</details>


### [107] [Finding the Correct Visual Evidence Without Forgetting: Mitigating Hallucination in LVLMs via Inter-Layer Visual Attention Discrepancy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20965)
*Yutong Xie,Zhenglin Hua,Ran Wang,Wing W. Y. Ng,Xizhao Wang,Yuheng Jia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free ILVAD method mitigates LVLM hallucinations by exploiting inter-layer visual attention discrepancy to identify salient visual evidence and reinforce grounding during generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs often hallucinate due to insufficient or fading attention to correct visual evidence during generation. A lightweight, architecture-agnostic mitigation that leveragesLayer-wise attention dynamics could improve grounding without retraining.

Method: Compute attention weights from early generated tokens to visual tokens across layers to identify tokens repeatedly activated as visual evidence, creating a saliency map. Use the saliency map to enhance attention to visual evidence during generation and to score/weight generated text tokens grounded in vision. The approach is training-free and plug-and-play.

Result: Consistent reduction of hallucinations across five LVLMs and multiple architectures on various benchmarks; improved grounding; code release available.

Conclusion: ILVAD demonstrates that inter-layer visual attention dynamics can be leveraged to reduce visual forgetting and hallucination in LVLMs in a training-free, model-agnostic manner, with potential extensions to other multimodal settings.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have shown remarkable performance on a wide range of vision-language tasks. Despite this progress, they are still prone to hallucination, generating responses that are inconsistent with visual content. In this work, we find that LVLMs tend to hallucinate when they pay insufficient attention to the correct visual evidence and gradually forget it during the generation process. We empirically find that although LVLMs overall attend insufficiently to visual evidence, they exhibit sensitivity to the correct visual evidence in specific layers, with notable inter-layer discrepancy. Motivated by this observation, we propose a novel hallucination mitigation method that enhances visual evidence based on Inter-Layer Visual Attention Discrepancy (ILVAD). Specifically, we obtain the attention weights from early generated tokens to visual tokens across layers and identify the tokens that are repeatedly activated as visual evidence, forming a saliency map. We then enhance attention to visual evidence during generation through the saliency map to reduce visual forgetting. In addition, we leverage the saliency map to obtain attention scores of generated text to visual evidence, in order to select and emphasize text tokens that are strongly grounded in visual evidence. Our method is training-free and plug-and-play. Multiple benchmark evaluations conducted on five recently released models show that our method can consistently mitigate hallucinations in different LVLMs over various architectures. Code is available at https://github.com/ytx-ML/ILVAD.

</details>


### [108] [Comparative Evaluation of Deep Learning Models for Fake Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20971)
*Akhitha Pakala,Mohammed Mahir Rahman,Shahzad Memon,Tauseef Ahmed*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VGG16 achieves the highest accuracy (91%) among four pretrained CNNs (VGG16, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, XceptionNet) for fake image detection using a unified preprocessing/training pipeline; EfficientNetB0 shows higher fake-sensitivity but real-sample bias likely due to dataset imbalance; limitations include imbalance, overfitting, and limited interpretability; a reproducible baseline is established, underscoring the need for balanced data, stronger augmentation, and fairness-aware training for better cross-domain robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Digital-forensic detection of manipulated images is critical given rising GAN-based manipulation. This study benchmarks common CNN backbones under a unified pipeline to establish baselines and understand architecture-driven performance and bias in fake image detection.

Method: Four pretrained CNNs (VGG16, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, XceptionNet) were fine-tuned within a unified preprocessing and training pipeline. Images were resized, normalized, and augmented to address class imbalance and improve generalization. Models were evaluated on accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC with a discussion of bias/cross-domain robustness.

Result: VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy at 91%; XceptionNet, ResNet50, and EfficientNetB0 each achieved 90%. EfficientNetB0 exhibited stronger sensitivity to fake images but lower reliability on real samples, suggesting imbalance-driven bias. Limitations include dataset imbalance, potential overfitting, and limited interpretability, impacting cross-domain robustness. The work provides a reproducible baseline and highlights the need for balanced datasets, enhanced augmentation, and fairness-aware training to develop reliable fake-image detectors.

Conclusion: The study offers a reproducible baseline for fake image detection across four CNN backbones and emphasizes addressing data imbalance and interpretability to improve cross-domain robustness through balanced data, advanced augmentation, and fairness-aware training.

Abstract: The growing sophistication of GAN-based image manipulation presents significant challenges for digital forensics. This study compares the performance of four pretrained CNN architectures including VGG16, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, and XceptionNet for fake image detection using a unified preprocessing and training pipeline. A dataset of real and manipulated images was processed through resizing, normalization, and augmentation to address class imbalance and improve generalization. Models were evaluated using Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy at 91%, with XceptionNet, ResNet50, and EfficientNetB0 each reaching 90%. EfficientNetB0 showed stronger sensitivity to fake images but reduced reliability on real samples, reflecting imbalance-driven bias. Limitations include dataset imbalance, overfitting, and limited interpretability, which affect cross-domain robustness. The study provides a reproducible baseline and underscores the need for balanced datasets, advanced augmentation, and fairness-aware training to develop reliable fake image detection systems.

</details>


### [109] [Towards Integrated Rock Support Visualisation in 3D Point Cloud of Underground Mines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20973)
*Dibyayan Patra,Simit Raval,Pasindu Ranasinghe,Bikram Banerjee,Ismet Canbulat*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An automated, integrated 3D-visualisation framework for rock support in underground mines that combines structure mapping, rock bolt identification, discontinuity fitting, and bolt orientation in a single workflow, with stereographic analysis and bolt-quality metrics, demonstrated on real mine scans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discontinuity characterization and rock bolt identification are typically treated separately, hindering comprehensive assessment of rock-support performance; there is a need for integrated, accurate, and efficient automated tools that reduce manual measurement.

Method: A 3D point-cloud–based framework that unifies structure mapping, rock bolt identification, discontinuity plane fitting, and bolt orientation estimation. Outputs include integrated 3D visualisation of planes and bolt vectors, stereographic analysis of poles and orientations, and bolt-quality metrics (e.g., protrusion, deviation from roof normals). Demonstrated on real underground metal-mine scans and optimized for accuracy and computational efficiency.

Result: Accurate structure mapping and rock bolt identification achieved in medium-scale point clouds; integrated visualisation enables assessment of spatial intersections and geometric relationships; complementary stereographic analysis and bolt-quality metrics provided to evaluate installation effectiveness.

Conclusion: The study offers a practical step toward automated, integrated geotechnical assessment of rock support effectiveness without additional in-situ data, applicable to medium-scale point clouds and potentially scalable to broader mining contexts.

Abstract: The effectiveness of rock support in underground mines depends on the interaction between installed rock bolts and the structural fabric of the surrounding rock mass. However, discontinuity characterisation and rock bolt identification are commonly treated as separate tasks, limiting their value for integrated support assessment. This study presents an automated framework for integrated rock support visualisation using 3D point clouds of underground mine excavations. The framework integrates structure mapping, rock bolt identification, discontinuity plane fitting, and bolt orientation estimation into a unified workflow optimised for accuracy and computational efficiency. The outputs are used to generate an integrated 3D visualisation of fitted discontinuity planes and bolt vectors, enabling direct assessment of their spatial intersections and geometric relationships. A complementary stereographic analysis of discontinuity poles and bolt orientations is also performed to evaluate overall bolting geometric effectiveness relative to the mapped structural fabric. Additionally, bolt-level quality metrics, including exposed protrusion length and deviation from the local roof normal, are visualised to support assessment of installation quality. The proposed framework is demonstrated on real underground metal mine scans, producing accurate structure mapping and rock bolt identification results in medium-scale point clouds. Overall, the study provides a practical step towards automated, integrated geotechnical assessment of rock support effectiveness without requiring manual measurements or additional in-situ data acquisition.

</details>


### [110] [CHOIR: Contact-aware 4D Hand-Object Interaction Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20992)
*Hao Xu,Yilin Liu,Yinqiao Wang,Chi-Wing Fu,Niloy J. Mitra*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes CHOIR: contact-driven 4D HOI reconstruction from monocular video.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable scalable, open-world reconstruction of hand-object interactions (HOI) from monocular videos for interaction mining, scene-aware synthesis and planning. Challenges include reliance on known objects, clutter/occlusion, and misalignment between hands and objects.

Method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) initialize a coarse, contact-agnostic 4D HOI sequence from open-world priors; (2) apply a generative HOI spatial rectification module to predict ray-depth corrections and rectify hand-object relative placement, deriving initial per-frame contact correspondences on rectified geometry; (3) perform a contact-aware joint optimization with dynamically updated contact constraints to enforce geometric, temporal, and contact consistency.

Result: On controlled and challenging videos, CHOIR improves object reconstruction, physical plausibility, and temporal consistency relative to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Using contact as an explicit coupling signal enables more accurate, temporally coherent 4D HOI reconstruction from monocular video, enabling scalable mining of real interactions and supporting scene-aware synthesis and planning.

Abstract: We ask whether everyday open-world monocular videos can be turned into reusable 4D interaction primitives: articulated hand motion, object shape with 6D pose over time, and the when/where of contact. Such a capability would enable scalable mining of real interactions and, beyond reconstruction, support scene-aware synthesis and planning. However, reconstructing hand-object interaction (HOI) from challenging monocular videos remains difficult: methods often assume known objects or curated scenes, and separately estimated hands and objects easily become misaligned under clutter, occlusion, and unseen object geometries. Targeting this setting, we present CHOIR, a Contact-aware HOI Reconstruction framework for a monocular camera, using contact as an explicit coupling signal between hands and objects. CHOIR first initializes a coarse, contact-agnostic 4D HOI sequence from open-world visual priors. It then introduces a generative HOI spatial rectification module to predict ray-depth corrections and rectify hand-object relative placement, then derive initial per-frame contact correspondences on the rectified geometry. Last, a contact-aware joint optimization with dynamically updated contact constraints enforces geometric, temporal, and contact consistency. Experiments on controlled and challenging videos show that CHOIR improves object reconstruction, physical plausibility, and temporal consistency over state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [111] [Hybrid Machine Learning Model for Forest Height Estimation from TanDEM-X and Landsat Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20997)
*Islam Mansour,Ronny Haensch,Irena Hajnsek,Konstantinos Papathanassiou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Extending a physics-informed ML model for forest height estimation with Landsat multispectral data improves TanDEM-X-derived heights, reducing RMSE by 13.5% and MAE by 16.6% against LiDAR in Gabon.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve remaining ambiguities (height/structure and baseline/terrain slope) in coherence-based forest height retrieval; leverage complementary multispectral information to improve accuracy.

Method: Add Landsat-derived features to the PM-constrained ML framework; apply to multiple TanDEM-X datasets over Lopé, and validate vs airborne LiDAR.

Result: Notable accuracy gains: RMSE down by 13.5%, MAE down by 16.6%; confirms value of multispectral inputs.

Conclusion: Multispectral integration enhances model performance and can better resolve structural ambiguity in SAR-based height estimation; demonstrates effectiveness of a hybrid ML-PM approach.

Abstract: Integrating machine learning (ML) with physical models (PM) has emerged as a promising way of retrieving geophysical parameters from remote sensing data. In this context, a ML model for estimating forest height from TanDEM-X interferometric coherence measurements has recently been proposed, that constrains the learning process through a PM. While the features used for training and inversion where selected to ensure the physical consistency of the solutions, they could not resolve all height / structure and baseline / terrain slope ambiguities in the data. To improve this, the extension of the feature space with optical Landsat data is proposed able to provide complementary information on forest type or structure. The extended model is applied and validated on several TanDEM-X acquisitions over the Gabonese Lopé national park site and assessed against airborne LiDAR measurements. Results show a 13.5% reduction in RMSE and a 16.6% reduction in MAE compared to the original hybrid model, confirming the added value of multispectral inputs.

</details>


### [112] [DySink: Dynamic Frame Sinks for Autoregressive Long Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21028)
*Bo Ye,Xinyu Cui,Jian Zhao,Tong Wei,Min-Ling Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dynamic retrieval-based long-video memory with adaptive sinks (DySink) improves continuity and temporal quality by selectively storing and retrieving historical frames and using a sink anomaly gate to avoid collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed bounded-memory strategies retain outdated early frames and ignore potentially relevant intermediates, leading to biased or collapsed generation. Adaptive, retrieval-based long-range context aims to preserve relevant, diverse cues while maintaining efficiency.

Method: Introduce DySink: a retrieval-based framework with a compact memory bank that dynamically selects visually relevant historical frames as frame sinks. It pairs adaptive retrieval with a sink anomaly gate that detects excessive inter-head consensus over retrieved context and suppresses collapse-prone context.

Result: Empirical evaluation on minute-long videos shows DySink consistently improves dynamic degree over strong baselines and achieves higher temporal quality. Code and model weights will be released at the given repository.

Conclusion: Adaptive retrieval of relevant past frames, coupled with a mechanism to gate collapsing context, enhances both the dynamism and temporal fidelity of autoregressive long-video generation while preserving efficiency.

Abstract: Autoregressive long video generation often adopts bounded-memory streaming for efficiency, typically combining local windows for short-term continuity with static early-frame sinks as long-range anchors. However, this fixed allocation keeps early frames cached even when the current visual state has substantially diverged from them, while discarding potentially more relevant intermediate history. As a result, the retained long-range context may become less adaptive and bias generation toward outdated cues; in severe cases, RoPE-induced phase re-alignment can homogenize inter-head attention and cause sink collapse, where content regresses toward sink frames. We propose DySink, a retrieval-based framework that maintains a compact memory bank and selects visually relevant historical frames as dynamic frame sinks. DySink couples adaptive retrieval with a sink anomaly gate, which detects excessive inter-head consensus over retrieved context and suppresses collapse-prone context. Experiments on minute-long videos show that DySink consistently improves dynamic degree over strong baselines while also achieving higher temporal quality. The code and model weights will be released at https://github.com/yebo0216best/DySink.

</details>


### [113] [Towards Physically Consistent 4D Scene Reconstruction for Closed-loop Autonomous Driving Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21032)
*Bowyn Tan,Yutong Xie,Bai Huang,Fan Luo,Xiao Li,Naizheng Wang,Yang Guan,Shengbo Eben Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces an information-geometric framework to diagnose why existing 3D/4D street scene reconstruction methods struggle to jointly model static spatial structure and dynamic temporal evolution; proposes Orthogonal Projected Gradient (OPG) with a two-stage training scheme and a Temporal Regularization Strategy to disentangle and stabilize spatial and temporal components, achieving robust novel-view synthesis and temporal consistency in closed-loop driving simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the credit assignment dilemma between spatial and temporal parameters in single-source observations for 4D scene reconstruction, which leads to low-rank structures and null-space ambiguities that bias temporal updates away from accurate spatial estimation.

Method: 1) Diagnostic information-geometric framework identifying low-rank coupling and null-space ambiguity between static view-dependent and dynamic time-varying components. 2) Orthogonal Projected Gradient (OPG): a hierarchical training protocol that first stabilizes spatial representations, then restricts temporal updates to the spatial null space. 3) Temporal Regularization Strategy: imposes smoothness constraints on appearance evolution based on physical priors to ensure temporal consistency in closed-loop simulations.

Result: Extensive experiments show the proposed approach maintains stable novel-view synthesis and yields superior performance on traditional observation-reproducing metrics, suggesting improved temporal dynamics modeling alongside strong spatial reconstruction.

Conclusion: The proposed information-geometric diagnostics and orthogonal training paradigm effectively resolve the credit assignment dilemma between spatial and temporal components, enabling stable, physically consistent, and high-fidelity 4D street scene reconstruction for autonomous driving simulations.

Abstract: High-fidelity street scene reconstruction is pivotal for end-to-end autonomous driving simulation, where novel-view synthesis (NVS) and time-varying information modeling are two fundamental capabilities to facilitate closed-loop training. However, existing 3DGS methods and their 4D extensions fail to simultaneously achieve both. To bridge this gap, we establish an information-geometric diagnostic framework, revealing that this limitation stems from a credit assignment dilemma between spatial and temporal parameters. Specifically, the deterministic coupling between viewpoint and time in single-source observation creates a low-rank structure that induces massive null-space ambiguity between static view-dependent and dynamic time-varying components. Temporal information overshadows spatial cues, causing the estimation variance of spatial parameters to diverge. To address this issue, we propose Orthogonal Projected Gradient (OPG), a hierarchical training method designed to restore spatial identifiability. OPG prioritizes the integrity of spatial representations by securing them in an initial stage, then restricts temporal updates to the spatial null space, enabling proactive credit assignment. While OPG isolates temporal updates algebraically, Temporal Regularization Strategy is proposed to further refine the temporal solution space by imposing a smoothness constraint based on the physical prior of consistent appearance evolution, ensuring that the reconstructed scene remains physically consistent in closed-loop simulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method not only maintains stable NVS capabilities but also demonstrates superior performance in traditional observation-reproducing metrics, which indirectly reflect the capability of modeling temporal dynamics.

</details>


### [114] [Dynamic Video Generation: Shaping Video Generation Across Time and Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21042)
*Shikang Zheng,Jingkai Huang,Jiacheng Liu,Guantao Chen,Lixuan,Yuqi Lin,Peiliang Cai,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DVG dynamically allocates computation across time and space in diffusion-based video generation to enable automatic, content-aware acceleration without retraining, achieving substantial speedups with near-lossless quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video diffusion models are computationally heavy due to large token counts and the temporal dimension. Progressive resolution sampling helps, but scaling to video is challenging and often requires manual tuning or retraining. A dynamic, content-aware strategy aims to reduce computation while preserving quality.

Method: DVG jointly allocates computational effort along the temporal dimension and spatial dimensions, automatically selecting acceleration strategies based on content without manual tuning or retraining, effectively distributing resources where needed across frames and regions.

Result: Near-lossless acceleration with up to 7x speedup on HunyuanVideo and HunyuanVideo-1.5; up to 18x when combined with distillation.

Conclusion: DVG demonstrates a strong potential as a core component for efficient large-scale video generation systems, enabling significant speedups with minimal quality loss and broad applicability across models and tasks.

Abstract: Diffusion models have achieved impressive performance in video generation, but their iterative denoising process remains computationally expensive due to the large number of tokens processed at each timestep. Recently, progressive resolution sampling has emerged as a promising acceleration approach by reducing latent resolution in early stages. However, scaling this idea to video generation remains challenging, as the additional temporal dimension introduces diverse spatio-temporal demands across different videos, and compressing only a single dimension often leads to limited acceleration or degraded quality. Therefore, we propose DVG, a Dynamic Video Generation framework that jointly allocates computation across time and space, automatically selecting content-aware acceleration strategies without manual tuning or retraining. DVG achieves near-lossless acceleration across models and tasks, reaching up to 7 times speedup on HunyuanVideo and HunyuanVideo-1.5, and 18 times when combined with distillation, demonstrating its potential as a key component in today's large-scale efficient video generation systems. Our code is in supplementary material and will be released on Github.

</details>


### [115] [Multimodal LLMs under Pairwise Modalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21059)
*Yan Li,Yunlong Deng,Yuewen Sun,Gongxu Luo,Kun Zhang,Guangyi Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A framework to learn aligned latent representations for multimodal LLMs using only pairwise modality data, enabling cross-modal transfer and generation without requiring fully joint multimodal datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce data curation costs and improve scalability of MLLMs by relying on pairwise modality data to approximate the joint distribution, while enabling integration of new modalities.

Method: Two-stage representation learning. Stage 1: latent representation alignment using self-reconstruction and cross-modality contrastive learning, with an inductive bias via partial alignment and minimal latent specification. Stage 2: cross-modal recomposition by integrating encoders of new modalities with decoders of pre-trained modalities to enable cross-modal transfer and generation. A theoretical identifiability analysis under pairwise observations underpins the approach.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows that adding 3D point clouds and tactile modalities to pre-trained MLLMs across three modality pairs yields strong cross-modal performance, validating learning of an aligned latent space.

Conclusion: Pairwise-data-based representation learning can scale MLLMs to handle additional modalities and support cross-modal generation without requiring fully joint multimodal datasets, by ensuring identifiable shared latent space and effective cross-modal recomposition.

Abstract: Despite the impressive results achieved by multimodal large language models (MLLMs), their training typically relies on jointly curated multimodal data, requiring substantial human effort to construct multi-way aligned datasets and thereby limiting scalability across domains. In this work, we explore training MLLMs by only leveraging multiple paired modalities as a surrogate for the full joint multimodal distribution. Specifically, we first provide a theoretical analysis of the conditions under which the representations are identifiable with only observing pairwise modalities. Building on this analysis, we propose a representation learning framework for aligning latent representations across modalities using only pairwise data. The framework consists of two stages: latent representation alignment and cross-modal recomposition. Specifically, in the first stage, we learn the shared latent space across modalities by both self-modal reconstruction and pair-wise contrastive learning. We also incorporate an inductive bias in the contrastive learning process by partially aligning and minimal latent specification. In stage two, we integrate the encoder of newly introduced modalities with the decoders of the pre-trained modalities to facilitate cross-modal transfer and generation. We evaluate our method by newly adding 3D point clouds and tactile modalities into pre-trained MLLMs with three modality pairs and show that, by learning an aligned latent representation space, our model achieves strong cross-modal performance.

</details>


### [116] [Q-ARVD: Quantizing Autoregressive Video Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21072)
*Siao Tang,Xinyin Ma,Gongfan Fang,Xingyi Yang,Xinchao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Q-ARVD introduces frame- and weight-aware quantization for autoregressive video diffusion (ARVDs), addressing highly unbalanced frame sensitivity and heterogeneous weight outliers with a final-quality aware frame weighting and outlier-aware dual-scale quantization, achieving superior performance over prior schemes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ARVDs enable real-time streaming video generation but incur high inference costs; quantization is a natural efficiency approach, yet existing diffusion-transformer quantization methods do not translate well to ARVDs due to unique challenges in frame-wise sensitivity and weight outliers.

Method: Proposes a final-quality aware frame-weighting scheme integrated into the quantization objective to balance frame sensitivity, and an outlier-aware adaptive dual-scale quantization that detects and isolates outlier channels across arbitrary layers and block depths.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate Q-ARVD’s superiority over existing quantization approaches for ARVDs.

Conclusion: Q-ARVD provides a principled quantization framework for ARVDs by explicitly addressing frame-wise sensitivity and weight outliers, enabling accurate and efficient autoregressive video generation.

Abstract: Autoregressive video diffusion models (ARVDs) have emerged as a promising architecture for streaming video generation, paving the way for real-time interactive video generation and world modeling. Despite their potential, the substantial inference cost of ARVDs remains a major obstacle to practical deployment, making model quantization a natural direction for improving efficiency. However, quantization for ARVDs remains largely unexplored. Our empirical analysis shows that directly applying existing quantization schemes developed for standard diffusion transformers to ARVDs leads to suboptimal performance, revealing quantization behaviors that differ from those observed in bidirectional diffusion models. In this paper, we identify two critical challenges in quantizing ARVDs: (C1) Highly unbalanced frame-wise quantization sensitivity. Error accumulation during autoregressive generation can induce severely skewed quantization sensitivity across frames, following an exponential-like decay pattern. (C2) Prominent and heterogeneous outlier patterns in weights. Weight distributions exhibit pronounced outlier channels, whose patterns vary substantially across layer types and block depths. To address these issues, we propose Q-ARVD, a novel framework for accurate ARVD quantization. (S1) To tackle the highly unbalanced frame-wise sensitivity, Q-ARVD incorporates a final-quality aware frame-weighting mechanism into the quantization objective. (S2) To prevent heterogeneous outliers from degrading performance, Q-ARVD introduces an outlier-aware adaptive dual-scale quantization, which automatically detects the presence and quantity of outlier channels for an arbitrary layer, and isolates them to protect normal channels. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of Q-ARVD.

</details>


### [117] [SpectralEarth-FM: Bringing Hyperspectral Imagery into Multimodal Earth Observation Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21075)
*Nassim Ait Ali Braham,Aaron Banze,Conrad M. Albrecht,Julien Mairal,Jocelyn Chanussot,Xiao Xiang Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpectralEarth-FM: a hierarchical transformer that fuses hyperspectral imagery with co-located multisensor EO data via spectral tokenization, sensor-specific encoders, cross-sensor fusion, and a shared encoder, pretrained with a JEPA-like objective on the SpectralEarth-MM dataset to achieve state-of-the-art results on hyperspectral and EO benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HSI is underrepresented in EO foundation models and existing hyperspectral FMs are trained on HSI alone, hindering joint pretraining and fusion with co-located sensors; a scalable, cross-sensor architecture is needed to unlock richer EO representations.

Method: A hierarchical transformer with spectral tokenization for HSI, sensor-specific encoders, a cross-sensor fusion module, and a shared encoder; pretraining with a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA)-style objective that aligns global views with local single-sensor views. Data comes from SpectralEarth-MM, co-locating EnMAP, EMIT, DESIS HSI with Sentinel-2, Landsat-8/9 opticals, Landsat LST, and Sentinel-1 SAR over ~2M locations, 25M patches, >40TB. Evaluation follows the PANGAEA protocol on hyperspectral downstream tasks and EO benchmarks.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across hyperspectral downstream tasks and standard EO benchmarks under the PANGAEA protocol.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that joint HSI–multisensor foundation models are viable and beneficial; the hierarchical fusion approach and JEPA-style pretraining enable robust cross-sensor representations, with SpectralEarth-MM serving as a large-scale resource for future work.

Abstract: Earth observation (EO) foundation models (FMs) are increasingly trained on multisensor data, spanning multispectral imagery (MSI), synthetic aperture radar (SAR), and derived geospatial layers, but hyperspectral imagery (HSI) remains underrepresented. Conversely, existing hyperspectral FMs are trained on HSI alone, leaving joint pretraining and fusion of HSI with co-located EO sensors unexplored. We introduce SpectralEarth-FM, a hierarchical transformer for multisensor EO input with heterogeneous spectral dimensionality. The architecture combines spectral tokenization for hyperspectral inputs, sensor-specific encoders, a cross-sensor fusion module, and a shared hierarchical encoder, enabling joint processing of HSI and lower-channel observations. To pretrain SpectralEarth-FM, we curate SpectralEarth-MM, a dataset that co-locates HSI from three spaceborne sensors (EnMAP, EMIT, DESIS) with Sentinel-2, Landsat-8/9 optical imagery, Landsat land surface temperature (LST), and Sentinel-1 SAR, over common geographic footprints. It comprises approximately 2M globally distributed locations, 25M georeferenced patches, and over 40TB of data. Pretraining uses a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA)-style objective that matches representations between global views and single-sensor local views from the same location. We evaluate SpectralEarth-FM on hyperspectral downstream tasks and standard EO benchmarks following the PANGAEA protocol, achieving state-of-the-art results across both evaluation settings.

</details>


### [118] [VDFP: Video Deflickering with Flicker-banding Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21079)
*Zhiyi Zhou,Libo Zhu,Zihan Zhou,Yulun Zhang,Xiaokang Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces DeViD, a real-world screen-capture dataset, and VDFP, a deflickering framework using degradation field modeling (DFM) for rolling-shutter banding and a Flicker-aware, spatial-temporal prior (CPP) optimized with FA-MSE, with a zero-initialized augmented input layer to preserve priors. Reports state-of-the-art restoration of banding with preserved details and timing; dataset/code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Capturing digital screens with smartphones often yields structured, periodic luminance banding due to hardware sync mismatches. Existing restoration methods struggle with these artifacts and lack real-world data to train and validate models.

Method: 1) Degradation Field Modeling Based on Rolling Shutter Mechanism (DFM) to synthesize complex multi-banding scenarios. 2) Spatial-temporal continuous prior perception (CPP) to capture luminance transitions, trained with a Flicker-Aware MSE (FA-MSE) loss. 3) Zero-initialized augmented input layer to preserve pre-trained generative priors and temporal-spatial priors. 4) Training/test on the DeViD dataset; comprehensive benchmarks against baselines.

Result: VDFP significantly outperforms competing methods, achieving high-fidelity restoration with reduced banding, preserved spatial details, and consistent temporal coherence across diverse scenes in DeViD.

Conclusion: The work delivers a new real-world dataset and a perceptual, priors-guided deflickering framework that advances video restoration for screen-capture footage; code and dataset are slated for release to support reproducibility and further research.

Abstract: Capturing digital screens with smartphones frequently induces severe banding due to hardware synchronization mismatches. Existing video restoration methods struggle with these structured, periodic luminance fluctuations, often resulting in residual artifacts or over-smoothed textures. We firstly construct DeViD, a real-world dataset in various scenes to deal with the lack of available datasets.Then we propose VDFP (Video Deflickering with Flicker-banding Priors), a novel perception-guided generation framework. First, we introduce a Degradation Field Modeling Based on Rolling Shutter Mechanism (DFM) capable of synthesizing complex multi-banding scenarios. Second, we present a spatial-temporal continuous prior perception (CPP). Unlike traditional binary segmentation, this module is optimized via a Flicker-Aware Mean Squared Error (FA-MSE) to capture the luminance transitions. By zero-initializing an augmented input layer, our model preserves pre-trained generative priors as well as spatial-temporal prior perception. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VDFP significantly outperforms other methods, eliminating complex banding with high-fidelity spatial details and temporal consistency. Our dataset and code will be released at~ https://github.com/ZhiyiZZhou/VDFP.

</details>


### [119] [TextSculptor: Training and Benchmarking Scene Text Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21090)
*Yiheng Lin,Siyu Jiao,Xiaohan Lan,Wei Zhou,Qi She,Fei Yu,Heyun Chen,Zhengwei Wang,Jinghuan Chen,Moran Li,Yingchen Yu,Zijian Feng,Yao Zhao,Yunchao Wei,Yujie Zhong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TextSculptor presents a scalable data-generation pipeline and a standardized benchmark for scene text editing in multimodal LLMs, delivering 3.2M training samples (1.2M OCR-verified text-to-image, 2M edited pairs) and TextSculpt-Bench with four editing tasks, aiming to close the gap to proprietary systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scene text editing is underdeveloped due to scarce high-quality data and lacking standardized benchmarks; precise text modification with visual realism and background preservation remains challenging for open-source models.

Method: Automated data construction combining text-aware image synthesis with programmatic text rendering and compositing; OCR verification for text samples; creation of 2M source–target paired editing samples with strong background consistency; TextSculpt-Bench defines four tasks (add, replace, remove, hybrid) and a tailored evaluation protocol using OCR alignment, multimodal judgments, and background-region similarity.

Result: The framework reports that TextSculptor improves open-source text editing performance and narrows the gap to proprietary models; large-scale dataset and benchmark are released (TextSculpt-Data: 3.2M samples; TextSculpt-Bench).

Conclusion: Provides publicly available data and benchmarks to accelerate open-source progress in scene text editing, enabling more reliable evaluation and comparability across models.

Abstract: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) and diffusion-based generative models have substantially improved prompt-driven image editing. However, scene text editing remains challenging, as it requires models to precisely modify textual content while preserving visual realism and non-target regions. Current open-source models still lag behind proprietary systems, largely due to the scarcity of high-quality training data and the lack of standardized benchmarks tailored to text editing. To address these challenges, we present TextSculptor, a comprehensive framework for data construction and evaluation of scene text editing. We first develop an automated data construction pipeline that combines text-aware image synthesis with programmatic text rendering and compositing. Based on this pipeline, we build TextSculpt-Data, a large-scale dataset containing 3.2M training samples, including 1.2M OCR-verified text-to-image samples and 2M paired text editing samples with naturally aligned source-target images and strong background consistency. We further introduce TextSculpt-Bench, a benchmark covering four fundamental text editing tasks: text addition, text replacement, text removal, and hybrid editing. To support reliable evaluation, we design a tailored protocol that measures text accuracy, visual quality, and background preservation through OCR-based text alignment, multimodal judgment, and background-region similarity. Extensive experiments show that TextSculptor improves open-source text editing performance and narrows the gap to proprietary models. The data and benchmark are available at https://github.com/linyiheng123/TextSculptor.

</details>


### [120] [R2AoP: Reliable and Robust Angle of Progression Estimation from Intrapartum Ultrasound](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21099)
*Yuanhan Wang,Yifei Chen,Beining Wu,Mingxuan Liu,Xiaotian Hu,Chunbo Jiang,Yijin Li,Changmiao Wang,Feiwei Qin,Qiyuan Tian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: R2AoP is a robust angle-of-progression (AoP) estimation framework for intrapartum transperineal ultrasound that couples structurally informed segmentation with confidence-guided geometric modeling and a lightweight test-time adaptation to achieve stable, reproducible AoP measurements across heterogeneous acquisitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AoP estimation is highly sensitive to imaging noise, boundary ambiguities, and segmentation errors that are geometrically amplified, leading to unreproducible labor progression assessments; a robust, annotation-free adaptation mechanism across centers is needed.

Method: Three-branch local-structure-enhanced segmentation backbone delineates pubic symphysis and fetal head; confidence-weighted contour fitting reduces influence of unreliable boundary points in AoP computation; lightweight geometry-reliable test-time adaptation to cope with domain shifts without target annotations; evaluated on multi-center benchmarks with comparative analyses.

Result: Consistent reductions in AoP error and boundary metrics compared with state-of-the-art methods; source code released at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: The framework achieves stable, reproducible AoP measurements under diverse acquisitions, addressing noise, boundary ambiguity, and segmentation error amplification, with practical deployment potential indicated by open-source availability.

Abstract: Accurate estimation of the Angle of Progression (AoP) from intrapartum transperineal ultrasound is critical for objective assessment of labor progression, yet remains highly sensitive to imaging noise, boundary ambiguities, and the geometric amplification of local segmentation errors. We propose R2AoP, a reliable and robust AoP estimation framework that integrates structurally informed segmentation and confidence-guided geometric modeling to achieve stable and reproducible measurements. A three-branch local-structure-enhanced backbone improves the delineation of the pubic symphysis (PS) and fetal head (FH), while confidence-weighted contour fitting explicitly suppresses the influence of unreliable boundary points in AoP computation. To further improve performance under heterogeneous acquisition conditions, we introduce a lightweight geometry-reliable test-time adaptation strategy as an auxiliary component, enabling stable inference without target annotations. Extensive evaluations on multi-center benchmarks demonstrate consistent reductions in AoP error and boundary metrics compared with state-of-the-art AoP methods. Our source code is available at https://github.com/baiyou1234/R2AoP.

</details>


### [121] [RCGDet3D: Rethinking 4D Radar-Camera Fusion-based 3D Object Detection with Enhanced Radar Feature Encoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21112)
*Weiyi Xiong,Bing Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RCGDet3D is a real-time 3D detector for 4D radar that emphasizes efficient radar feature encoding. It introduces Ray-centric PGE (R-PGE) and a Semantic Injection (SI) module to improve geometric consistency and semantic richness, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and speed on VoD and TJ4DRadSet, outperforming heavy fusion strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 4D radar point clouds are sparse and existing radar–camera fusion methods hard-code complex fusion, trading speed for marginal gains. There is a need to enhance radar feature extraction to sustain real-time performance while improving accuracy.

Method: Build on Gaussian Splatting-based PGE from RadarGaussianDet3D. Introduce Ray-centric PGE (R-PGE) that predicts Gaussian attributes in ray-aligned coordinates before unifying to BEV, decoupling geometry from representation learning. Add a Semantic Injection (SI) module to fuse visual cues from images into radar features. Simplify multi-modal fusion and maintain BEV-centric representations to preserve efficiency.

Result: Empirical results on VoD and TJ4DRadSet show that RCGDet3D achieves higher accuracy and faster inference than state-of-the-art methods, establishing a new real-time benchmark for multi-modal 4D radar-based 3D detection.

Conclusion: Focusing on efficient radar feature encoding can rival or surpass elaborate fusion modules while preserving real-time performance; the proposed R-PGE and SI enable more accurate and semantically enriched radar features, guiding future real-time 4D radar detection research.

Abstract: 4D automotive radar is indispensable for autonomous driving due to its low cost and robustness, yet its point cloud sparsity challenges 3D object detection. Existing 4D radar-camera fusion methods focus on complex fusion strategies, trading inference speed for marginal gains. This trade-off hinders real-time deployment due to heavy computation on dense feature maps. In contrast, feature extraction from sparse radar points is less time-consuming but remains under-explored. This work uncovers that simply enhancing radar feature extraction can achieve comparable or even higher performance than elaborate fusion modules, while maintaining real-time performance. Based on this finding, we propose RCGDet3D, which centers on radar feature encoding and simplifies multi-modal fusion. Its encoder inherits from the efficient Gaussian Splatting-based Point Gaussian Encoder (PGE) in RadarGaussianDet3D with two key improvements. First, the Ray-centric PGE (R-PGE) predicts Gaussian attributes in ray-aligned coordinate systems before unifying them to Bird's-Eye View (BEV) space, significantly improving geometric consistency and reducing learning difficulty by decoupling the coordinate transformation from representation learning. Second, a Semantic Injection (SI) module incorporates visual cues from images, producing more geometrically accurate and semantically enriched radar features. Experiments on View-of-Delft (VoD) and TJ4DRadSet show that RCGDet3D outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and speed, setting a new benchmark for real-time deployment.

</details>


### [122] [ROAR-3D: Routing Arbitrary Views for High-Fidelity 3D Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21121)
*Hanxiao Sun,Mingxin Yang,Shuhui Yang,Zebin He,Xintong Han,Hongbo Fu,Chunchao Guo,Wenhan Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Upgrades a pretrained single-view 3D generator to handle arbitrary numbers of unposed images via ROAR-3D, introducing a token-based view router, dual-stream attention, and orientation perturbation, achieving state-of-the-art multi-view 3D generation with scalable test-time views and minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-view to 3D generative models suffer ambiguity about unseen regions; multi-view conditioning can reduce ambiguity but existing methods are heavy or fixed-view. The work aims to reuse 2D-to-3D grounding in pretrained models while avoiding pose dependence and expensive reconstructions.

Method: ROAR-3D introduces a token-wise view router to assign each 3D latent token to the most relevant view; dual-stream attention preserves the pretrained primary-view behavior while routing auxiliary views through a separate geometry-enrichment path; orientation perturbation decouples orientation control from geometry transfer; minimal trainable parameters and negligible inference overhead.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art multi-view 3D generation quality; supports test-time view scaling from 1 to 12+ views with consistent improvements; negligible inference overhead relative to the single-view baseline.

Conclusion: ROAR-3D provides an efficient, architecture-aware upgrade of pretrained single-view models to support multiple unposed views, yielding improved multi-view 3D generation without heavy reconstruction modules or pose inputs.

Abstract: Single-image-to-3D generative models can now produce high-quality geometry, yet conditioning on a single view inevitably introduces ambiguity about unseen regions. Multi-view conditioning can reduce this ambiguity, but existing methods either require fixed canonical viewpoints or rely on external reconstruction modules that impose heavy training costs and limit generation quality. We observe that pretrained single-view models already possess strong 2D-to-3D grounding that can be reused for multi-view conditioning. However, a closer analysis reveals that their conditioning mechanism entangles orientation control with geometry transfer, two functions that conflict when images from different viewpoints are naively combined. Based on this analysis, we propose ROAR-3D, a lightweight method that upgrades a pretrained single-view model to accept an arbitrary number of unposed images. A token-wise view router assigns each 3D latent token to its most relevant view, implicitly establishing 2D-to-3D correspondences without explicit pose input. A dual-stream attention design preserves the pretrained primary-view behavior while routing auxiliary views through a separate path dedicated to geometric enrichment. An orientation perturbation strategy ensures the auxiliary path learns orientation-independent geometry transfer. These components introduce minimal trainable parameters and add negligible inference overhead relative to the single-view baseline. ROAR-3D achieves state-of-the-art multi-view 3D generation quality and supports test-time view scaling from 1 to 12+ views with consistent improvements.

</details>


### [123] [Linear-DPO: Linear Direct Preference Optimization for Diffusion and Flow-Matching Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21123)
*Kesong Li,Yixuan Xu,Kuo-kun Tseng,Weiyi Lu,Kan Liu,Tao Lan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Generalizes Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to both diffusion and flow-matching text-to-image models via a unified reverse-time SDE, identifies suboptimality of standard DPO for T2I, and introduces Linear-DPO with a linear utility and EMA reference, showing superior performance over baselines on SD1.5, SDXL, and SD3-Medium.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DPO has proven effective for alignment in LLMs but does not directly extend to T2I generation. Prior work is limited to denoising diffusion models and uses NLP-based discrete objectives that mismatch the regression nature of image generation. A unified objective bridging diffusion and flow-matching is needed for better T2I alignment.

Method: Derive a generalized DPO objective within a unified reverse-time SDE framework that covers both diffusion and flow-matching models. From a gradient perspective, show that the standard sigmoid-based utility is suboptimal for text-to-image tasks. Propose Linear-DPO by replacing the aggressive sigmoid utility with a linear utility and incorporating an EMA-updated reference model. Validate on diffusion models SD1.5 and SDXL and flow-matching SD3-Medium through qualitative and quantitative experiments.

Result: The Linear-DPO approach demonstrates superior performance compared to existing baselines across both diffusion and flow-matching T2I models, with qualitative and quantitative gains reported in the experiments.

Conclusion: A unified DPO framework (Linear-DPO) effectively addresses objective mismatch in T2I alignment, extends DPO to both diffusion and flow-matching, and improves performance via a linear utility and EMA reference, indicating broad applicability for regression-based generative tasks beyond NLP.

Abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is successful for alignment in LLMs but still faces challenges in text-to-image generation. Existing studies are confined to denoising diffusion models while overlooking flow-matching, and suffer from an objective mismatch when applying discrete NLP-based DPO to regression-based generative tasks.\ In this paper, we derive a generalized DPO objective that covers both diffusion and flow-matching via a unified reverse-time SDE framework, and point out from a gradient perspective that the standard DPO objective is suboptimal for text-to-image generation. Consequently, we propose Linear-DPO, which replaces the aggressive sigmoid-based utility function with a sustained linear utility and incorporates an EMA-updated reference model. Qualitative and quantitative experiments on diffusion models (SD1.5, SDXL) and flow-matching model (SD3-Medium) demonstrate the superiority of our approach over existing baselines.

</details>


### [124] [VersusQ: Pairwise Margin Reasoning for Generalizable Video Quality Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21130)
*Shibei Meng,Binxin Yang,Yuan Liu,Jiexuan Zhang,Zhengyao Lv,Hubery Yin,Qiang Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VersusQ presents a pairwise, margin-based framework for video quality assessment with LMMs, replacing absolute scores with direct comparisons and a jointly optimized Margin-Coupled GRPO to align relational reasoning with continuous margins. It claims state-of-the-art, cross-domain generalization, and fine-grained ranking across VQA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Absolute, pointwise quality scores in video quality assessment are biased by dataset calibration, annotation protocols, and score distributions, hurting cross-domain generalization. A pairwise, relative approach may reduce this calibration bias and improve transferability.

Method: VersusQ performs LMM-based pairwise comparisons between two videos to reason about visual and temporal quality differences and outputs a signed continuous margin indicating preference and difference magnitude. Margin-Coupled GRPO jointly optimizes rollout-based relational reasoning with continuous margin regression to align interpretable rationales with numerical margins.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple public VQA benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance, robust cross-domain generalization, and reliable fine-grained ranking under heterogeneous evaluation scenarios.

Conclusion: Relative, comparison-based scoring with margin-aware relational reasoning improves generalization and ranking consistency over absolute-score methods; Margin-Coupled GRPO effectively ties qualitative rationales to quantitative margins, enabling precise, transferable VQA judgments.

Abstract: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have shown promise for video quality assessment, but most methods still predict an absolute score for each video. Such pointwise supervision often mixes perceptual quality with dataset-specific calibration, including annotation protocols, rating habits, and score distributions. As a result, the learned scoring rule may work well within a benchmark but transfer poorly across unseen domains. We argue that relative comparisons alleviate the absolute-scale calibration bias by focusing purely on perceptual differences rather than dataset-specific rating habits. Consequently, we propose \textbf{VersusQ}, a pairwise margin reasoning framework driven entirely by direct comparisons. Specifically, VersusQ performs LMM-based comparison between two videos, reasons about their visual and temporal quality differences, and predicts a signed continuous margin that captures both the preferred choice and the degree of difference. Furthermore, to align interpretable comparison rationales with fine-grained numerical differences, we introduce Margin-Coupled GRPO, which jointly optimizes rollout-based relational reasoning and continuous margin regression. Extensive experiments on multiple public VQA benchmarks demonstrate that VersusQ achieves state-of-the-art performance, strong cross-domain generalization, and reliable fine-grained ranking under heterogeneous evaluation scenarios.

</details>
