### [125] [UniT: Unified Geometry Learning with Group Autoregressive Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21131)
*Haotian Wang,Yusong Huang,Zhaonian Kuang,Hongliang Lu,Xinhu Zheng,Meng Yang,Gang Hua*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniT unifies online/offline dense 3D geometry perception via a Group Autoregressive Transformer that processes sensor groups, uses anchor-free and scale-adaptive learning, and achieves SOTA across multiple tasks with long-horizon memory management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge fragmented capabilities in geometry perception across online/offline, multi-modal, and metric-scale requirements within a single framework.

Method: Group Autoregressive Transformer over groups of sensor observations; online uses single-frame groups across steps; offline uses multi-frame groups; queue KV cache to bound memory; anchor-free relational modeling to discard outdated memory; scale-adaptive geometry loss coupling relative constraints with partial absolute scale; modal attention module for auxiliary modalities.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on ten benchmarks across seven tasks, demonstrating unified geometry perception and improved metric-scale generalization.

Conclusion: UniT provides a unified, scalable framework for dense 3D geometry from diverse sensor inputs, with improved memory efficiency and scale generalization, enabling broad applicability across perception tasks.

Abstract: Recent feed-forward models have significantly advanced geometry perception for inferring dense 3D structure from sensor observations. However, its essential capabilities remain fragmented across multiple incompatible paradigms, including online perception, offline reconstruction, multi-modal integration, long-horizon scalability, and metric-scale estimation. We present UniT, a unified model built upon a novel Group Autoregressive Transformer, which reformulates these seemingly disparate capabilities within a single framework. The key idea is to treat groups of sensor observations as the basic autoregressive units and predict the corresponding point maps in an anchor-free and scale-adaptive manner. More specifically, diverse view configurations in both online and offline settings are naturally unified within a single group autoregression process. By varying the group size, online mode operates over multiple autoregressive steps with single-frame groups, whereas offline mode aggregates a multi-frame group in a single forward pass. Meanwhile, a queue-style KV caching mechanism ensures bounded autoregressive memory over long horizons. This is enabled by reducing long-range dependencies on early frames through anchor-free relational modeling, thereby allowing outdated memory to be discarded on the fly. To improve metric-scale generalization across scenes, a scale-adaptive geometry loss is further introduced within this framework. It couples relative geometric constraints with a partial absolute scale term, implicitly regularizing global scale and inducing a progressive transition from scale-invariant geometry to metric-scale solutions. Together with a dedicated modal attention module for integrating auxiliary modalities, UniT achieves state-of-the-art performance in unified geometry perception, as validated on ten benchmarks spanning seven representative tasks.

</details>


### [126] [SurgOnAir: Hierarchy-Aware Real-Time Surgical Video Commentary](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21132)
*Jingyi He,Yue Zhou,Long Bai,Kun Yuan,Nassir Navab,Yuan Bi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SurgOnAir is a streaming vision-language model for real-time surgical narration, built on the SurgOnAir-11k dataset, with frame-to-token generation and explicit transition tokens to capture state changes across action/step/phase hierarchies, enabling immediate, hierarchical narration and real-time understanding; code and dataset to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time understanding of surgical workflow is critical for intelligent surgical systems. Existing methods are offline or operate at coarse temporal scales, limiting timely assistance and safety. A streaming, hierarchy-aware narration model with explicit transition signals can bridge perception and action in real time.

Method: Sequential frame processing without access to future frames; progressively generates narration tokens as frames arrive (frame-to-token generation). Learns multi-level textual outputs reflecting action, step, and phase hierarchies using a curated SurgOnAir-11k dataset with hierarchy-based supervision. Introduces special transition tokens to mark state changes. Trains a single vision-language model that unifies streaming across the hierarchy levels.

Result: Empirical results indicate real-time understanding and generation of superior, hierarchy-aware narrations. SurgOnAir unifies streaming across action, step, and phase levels, providing immediate signals of workflow transitions. Code and dataset are to be released publicly.

Conclusion: The approach enables real-time, hierarchy-aware surgical narration, supporting immediate perception-action coupling in intelligent surgical systems. The framework and dataset offer a foundation for broader real-time surgical AI applications, with potential for further evaluation and deployment in real-world OR settings.

Abstract: Understanding surgical workflow in real time is fundamental for intelligent surgical embodiment, where AI systems continuously perceive and respond as surgery proceeds. In the operating room, critical decisions depend on subtle, moment-to-moment changes, such as fine instrument movements and evolving tissue states, where even slight perceptual delays can limit assistance or compromise safety. Yet existing methods remain offline or operate at coarse temporal scales, generating descriptions only after processing clips, preventing immediate reaction. We address this by proposing SurgOnAir, a streaming vision-language model that processes frames sequentially without future access and progressively generates narration tokens as visual input arrives. SurgOnAir achieves fine-grained frame-to-token generation, enabling instant responsiveness to evolving surgical dynamics. Built upon our curated hierarchical dataset SurgOnAir-11k spanning action-, step-, and phase-level supervision, the model is trained to produce multi-level textual responses that reflect the inherent hierarchy of surgical procedures. Furthermore, special transition tokens are generated to explicitly mark state changes, allowing SurgOnAir to capture and signal key workflow transitions as they occur. Experiments show that SurgOnAir enables real-time understanding through a single vision-language model that unifies streaming across multiple hierarchies of the surgical workflow, generating superior and hierarchy-aware narrations. Code and dataset will be public.

</details>


### [127] [Distill to Think, Foresee to Act: Cognitive-Physical Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21139)
*Yang Wu,Qiang Meng,Zhaojiang Liu,Youquan Liu,Jian Yang,Jin Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoPhy advances autonomous driving by integrating cognitive (language-aided) and physical (future BEV-based sandbox) components into a reinforcement learning framework, using distillation of vision-language model knowledge into a BEV encoder and a dual-reward GRPO training with physical and cognitive objectives; it reports state-of-the-art results on NAVSIM benchmarks and safer, language-controllable behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Break the imitation-learning ceiling by combining a cognitive understanding of traffic semantics and driving intent with a forward-looking, safe action evaluation environment.

Method: 1) Distill VLM knowledge into the BEV encoder and discard VLM at inference, enabling zero-cost cognitive ability with an optional human-language command interface. 2) Build an auto-regressive BEV world model predicting future semantic maps conditioned on candidate actions to form an interpretable physical sandbox. 3) Optimize driving policy with GRPO using a dual-reward mechanism: a physical reward from BEV rollouts enforcing safety constraints, and a cognitive reward from a language-aligned scorer ensuring intent compliance.

Result: CoPhy achieves state-of-the-art results on NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks and demonstrates safer driving via cognitively informed scene compliance and flexible intent control through user-defined language instructions.

Conclusion: Integrating cognitive language-aligned guidance with a forward-looking BEV world model and dual-reward optimization yields safer, more controllable autonomous driving, with zero-inference-cost cognitive capabilities and plug-and-play language interfaces.

Abstract: Current end-to-end autonomous driving models are fundamentally constrained by the behavioral cloning ceiling of imitation learning. While reinforcement learning offers a path to smarter autonomy, it demands two missing pieces of infrastructure: (1) a cognitive foundation that understands traffic semantics and driving intent, and (2) a foresighted physical environment that can anticipate the consequences of candidate actions. To this end, we propose CoPhy, a CognitivePhysical reinforcement learning framework for autonomous driving. To distill to think, we distill VLM knowledge into the BEV encoder and then discard the VLM entirely, retaining cognitive ability at zero inference cost while releasing the cognitive channel as a pluggable interface for optional human language commands. To foresee to act, we build an auto-regressive BEV world model that explicitly predicts future semantic maps conditioned on candidate actions, serving as an interpretable physical sandbox from which safety metrics are directly derived. Built upon this dual infrastructure, we optimize the driving policy via GRPO with a novel dual-reward mechanism: a physical reward derived from BEV rollouts enforces hard safety constraints, while a cognitive reward from a language-aligned scorer ensures intent compliance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CoPhy not only achieves state-of-the-art results on NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks, but also enables safer driving via cognitively informed scene compliance and flexible intent control through user-defined language instructions.

</details>


### [128] [FTerViT: Fully Ternary Vision Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21171)
*Szymon Ruciński,Pietro Bonazzi,Engin Türetken,Simon Narduzzi,Michele Magno,Nadim Maamari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A fully ternarized Vision Transformer (FTerViT) with all weights and normalization ternarized, including patch embedding via TernaryBitConv2d and TernaryLayerNorm, trained with distillation and a quantization-aware recovery phase; achieves competitive accuracy with substantial memory savings and on-device ESP32-S3 deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce memory footprint and enable on-device deployment of Vision Transformers on resource-constrained devices. Prior ternarization targeted only the encoder; full-precision patch embeddings, LayerNorm, and classifier limit compactness.

Method: Introduce two operators: TernaryBitConv2d with per-channel scaling for patch embedding; TernaryLayerNorm. Train using knowledge distillation followed by a lightweight quantization-aware recovery phase.

Result: ImageNet-1K top-1 of 82.43% for W2A8 DeiT-III-S at 384×384, 6.09 MB (~15× compression, −2.42pp vs FP32); outperforms prior ternary ViTs by up to 8pp. Demonstrated on ESP32-S3 dual-core XTensa LX7: FTerViT-Small (224×224, 5.81 MB) achieves 79.64% accuracy.

Conclusion: FTerViT shows that full ternarization of ViTs is viable for edge devices, delivering substantial memory savings while maintaining high accuracy; introduces novel operators for patch embedding and normalization; enables practical on-device transformer inference on microcontrollers.

Abstract: Ternary Vision Transformers offer substantial model compression, however state-of-the-art methods only ternarize the encoder layers, leaving patch embeddings, LayerNorm parameters, and classifier heads in full precision. In compact models targeting resource-constrained processors, such as microcontrollers, these remaining full-precision components determine the total memory footprint, severely limiting deployment efficiency and on-device feasibility. In this work, we introduce a fully ternarized Vision Transformer in which \emph{all} weight matrices and normalization parameters are ternarized (FTerViT). To this end, we introduce two novel operators : TernaryBitConv2d with per-channel scaling for patch embedding and TernaryLayerNorm. FTerViT is trained using knowledge distillation, followed by a lightweight quantization-aware recovery phase. Our ternary W2A8 DeiT-III-S at 384$\times$384 resolution achieves 82.43\% ImageNet-1K top-1 at 6.09\,MB (${\sim}$15$\times$ compression, $-$2.42\,pp vs.\ FP32), outperforming prior ternary ViTs methods up to 8 pp. Finally, we demonstrate the first implementation of ternary vision transformers on a dual cores XTensa LX7 microcontroller inside the ESP32-S3 system-on-chip. By deploying FTerViT-Small (based on DeiT-III-Small at 224$\times$224 resolution, 5.81\,MB), we achieve 79.64\% ImageNet-1K top-1 accuracy.

</details>


### [129] [SAM-Sode: Towards Faithful Explanations for Tiny Bacteria Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21186)
*Wanying Tan,Shuo Yan,Dazhi Huang,Yazheng Liu,Zili Shao,Rufeng Chen,Hechang Chen,Mude Shi,Tianxing Ji,Sihong Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Transforms initial attribution maps into geometry-aware prompts using a foundation model (SAM3) to spatially refine and reconstruct explanations for tiny bacteria detection, with a dual-constraint denoising mechanism that aligns with physical significance and geometric structure to improve interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability for tiny object detection is compromised by blurred boundaries and strong background interference, hindering coherent morphological evidence critical for clinical auxiliary diagnosis.

Method: Introduce SAM-Sode: convert feature attribution maps into geometry-aware prompts; leverage SAM3 for spatial refinement and morphological reconstruction of explanations; apply a dual-constraint mechanism (physical significance and geometric alignment) for instance-level denoising to produce coherent, human-aligned explanations.

Result: Experiments on a self-constructed bacteria dataset (2,524 images) and public datasets show that the method suppresses background redundancy and substantially enhances decision-making transparency in tiny object detection.

Conclusion: The framework provides more coherent, morphology-consistent explanations by integrating geometry-aware prompt generation with dual-constraint denoising, improving XAI reliability in clinical tiny-object detection.

Abstract: Interpretability in object detection provides crucial confidence support for clinical auxiliary diagnosis. However, in tiny bacteria detection, traditional explanation methods often suffer from blurred foreground boundaries and diffuse feature attribution due to the extreme sparsity of target morphological features and severe interference from complex backgrounds. Such limitations hinder the provision of logically coherent morphological evidence. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel eXplainable AI (XAI) framework, SAM-Sode. The framework innovatively transforms initial feature attribution maps into geometry-aware prompts, leveraging the prior knowledge of the foundation model (SAM3) to achieve spatial refinement and morphological reconstruction of the explanatory mappings. Furthermore, we introduce a dual-constraint mechanism based on physical significance and geometric alignment to perform instance-level denoising, generating coherent explanations that better align with human expert intuition. Experimental results on our self-constructed bacteria dataset with complex circuit backgrounds (containing 2,524 images) and other public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method effectively suppresses background redundancy and significantly enhances the decision-making transparency of tiny object detection.

</details>


### [130] [Semantic Granularity Navigation in Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21190)
*Liangsi Lu,Minzhe Guo,Xuhang Chen,Yang Shi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NaviEdit is a training-free, inference-time controller that decouples edit progress from model-scale traversal in diffusion/flow image editing, reallocating a fixed step budget to intermediate scales for semantic edits while preserving structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a persistent trade-off between semantic editability and structural fidelity due to implicit coupling between edit progress and model scale; stronger edits push through high-noise states that destabilize layout before the semantic change localizes.

Method: Introduce NaviEdit with a strict self-consistency contract; operates at rollout level; keeps the pretrained model fixed; uses scale as a control input; allocates a fixed step budget toward semantically responsive intermediate scales instead of destructive high-noise regimes; training-free, inference-time controller.

Result: Empirical results show average gains across compatible editors and flow backbones, supporting decoupling as a portable inference-time control principle.

Conclusion: Decoupling edit progress from model-scale traversal is feasible and beneficial; NaviEdit provides a practical, model-agnostic, training-free approach for improved editing fidelity.

Abstract: Despite the generative capabilities of diffusion and flow models, real-image editing remains constrained by a persistent trade-off between semantic editability and structural fidelity. We trace a primary cause of this limitation to the implicit coupling of edit progress with model scale in existing paradigms. Under this coupling, stronger edits typically require visiting noisier states, which spends computation on destabilizing layout before the semantic change is well localized. We introduce NaviEdit, a training-free inference-time controller that decouples edit progress from model scale traversal through a strict self-consistency contract. NaviEdit operates at the rollout level and leaves the underlying pretrained model unchanged. It treats scale as a control input and reallocates a fixed step budget toward semantically responsive intermediate scales instead of destructive high-noise regimes. Experiments show positive average gains across compatible editors and flow backbones, supporting decoupling as a portable inference-time control principle.

</details>


### [131] [RankE: End-to-End Post-Training for Discrete Text-to-Image Generation with Decoder Co-Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21195)
*Siyong Jian,Siyuan Li,Luyuan Zhang,Zedong Wang,Xin Jin,Ying Li,Cheng Tan,Huan Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RankE is a first end-to-end post-training framework for discrete text-to-image (T2I) generation that co-evolves policy and VQ decoder to fix Latent Covariate Shift, surpassing policy-only optimization which improves rewards but harms image fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete AR T2I models suffer fidelity–alignment misalignment: upgrading the policy during post-training shifts token distributions away from the decoder's training distribution, harming pixel-space quality despite higher reward; an end-to-end co-evolution can align token and pixel spaces.

Method: Alternating optimization between policy and decoder using a ranking-based alignment objective for each, regularized by stability-preserving anchors suited to their parameter spaces, enabling co-evolution rather than a frozen decoder. Evaluated on LlamaGen-XL (775M) and Janus-Pro (1B) with MS-COCO 30K metrics.

Result: Policy-only RL improves CLIP but degrades FID; RankE achieves simultaneous gains (e.g., FID 15.21 and CLIP 33.76 on MS-COCO 30K for LlamaGen-XL), with consistent gains on Janus-Pro, indicating decoder co-evolution translates reward optimization into better pixel-space quality.

Conclusion: End-to-end decoder–policy co-evolution mitigates the fidelity–alignment trade-off in discrete T2I, enabling concurrent improvements in both alignment (CLIP) and image fidelity (FID) over frozen-decoder baselines.

Abstract: Discrete autoregressive (AR) text-to-image (T2I) models pair a VQ tokenizer with an AR policy, and current post-training pipelines optimize only the policy while keeping the VQ decoder frozen. Recent diffusion T2I work, exemplified by REPA-E, has shown that the VAE itself constitutes a key alignment bottleneck, yet no analogous investigation exists for discrete AR models. We show that policy-only optimization induces Latent Covariate Shift: as the policy evolves, the resulting token distribution diverges from the ground-truth distribution on which the decoder was trained, such that reward scores improve while decoded image quality degrades. To address this mismatch, we propose RankE, the first end-to-end post-training framework for discrete T2I generation. Rather than optimizing the policy against a fixed decoder, RankE co-evolves both components through alternating optimization: each module maximizes a ranking-based alignment objective while being regularized by a stability-preserving anchor suited to its parameter space. This co-evolution breaks the fidelity--alignment trade-off that plagues frozen-decoder approaches: on LlamaGen-XL (775M), standard RL improves CLIP but degrades FID, whereas RankE improves both simultaneously (FID 15.21, CLIP 33.76 on MS-COCO 30K). Consistent gains on Janus-Pro (1B) confirm that decoder co-evolution reliably converts reward optimization into pixel-space quality improvements.

</details>


### [132] [PGC: Peak-Guided Calibration for Generalizable AI-Generated Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21207)
*Xiaoyu Zhou,Jianwei Fei,Peipeng Yu,Jingchang Xie,Chong Cheng,Zhihua Xia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Peak-Guided Calibration (PGC) boosts AI-generated image detection by focusing on discriminative local peaks and calibrating the global decision; introduces CommGen15 dataset; achieves SOTA on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global detectors often overlook subtle local cues embedded in generated content, as dominant image content dominates representations. A peak-focused mechanism could recover discriminative local signals to improve reliability.

Method: Peak-sensitive aggregation selects the most discriminative local cues (peaks) from feature maps and uses them to calibrate the global detector decision, enabling recovery of subtle patterns submerged in global context. Also introduces CommGen15, a dataset with samples from 15 commercial models.

Result: On CommGen15, mean accuracy improves by 12.3%. On GenImage, AIGI, and UniversalFakeDetect benchmarks, improvements are +2.1%, +3.5%, and +1.7% respectively. Code is released at the provided URL.

Conclusion: PGC achieves state-of-the-art performance by leveraging salient local cues to refine global decisions; the CommGen15 dataset provides a more realistic threat model for evaluating detectors.

Abstract: The rapid evolution of generative AI, from GANs to modern diffusion models, has resulted in increasingly subtle discriminative clues. These fine-grained signals are often overshadowed by dominant, high-fidelity image content (e.g., the main subject), limiting the reliability of existing detectors that predominantly rely on global representations. To address this challenge, we propose the Peak-Guided Calibration (PGC) framework. PGC introduces a novel strategy that aggregates salient features via a peak-focusing mechanism. Specifically, by employing a peak-sensitive aggregation that accentuates the most discriminative local clues, PGC leverages these critical signals to calibrate the global decision. This approach recovers subtle patterns that would otherwise be submerged in the global context. Furthermore, to better simulate real-world threats, we introduce the CommGen15 dataset, a challenging benchmark comprising samples from 15 commercial models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PGC achieves state-of-the-art performance. Specifically, it improves mean accuracy by +12.3% on our CommGen15 dataset, and sets new records on standard benchmarks, including GenImage (+2.1%), AIGI (+3.5%), and UniversalFakeDetect (+1.7%). Code is available at https://github.com/xiaoyu6868/PGC.

</details>


### [133] [RePCM: Region-Specific and Phenotype-Adaptive Bi-Ventricular Cardiac Motion Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21237)
*Xuan Yang,Xiaohan Yuan,Hao Li,Lingyu Chen,Yanan Liu,Lei Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage framework RePCM completes full-cycle bi-ventricular mesh motion from a single end-diastolic frame by learning a data-driven region partition, region-specific VAE injection, and a phenotype-adaptive Mixture-of-Experts prior, yielding improved region dynamics across diseases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-diastolic frames are accessible, but obtaining temporally dense cardiac motion sequences is difficult. Global generative models tend to oversmooth due to strong regional and disease-specific differences; there is a need for explicit region structure and disease-aware dynamics to faithfully capture regional motion.

Method: Stage I: reconstruction network learns vertex-wise motion descriptors and performs clustering to derive a data-driven functional partition, revealing explicit motion regions. Stage II: Region-Specific Injection Module in a conditional VAE enforces masked, synchronized region exchange to preserve region-specific dynamics and limit cross-region mixing. A Phenotype-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts prior, conditioned on ED shape, uses anatomy-guided cues to model latent motion trends and inter-disease variability.

Result: Evaluations on three datasets across different cardiovascular diseases show consistent improvements in geometric and functional metrics and better preservation of region-specific dynamics.

Conclusion: RePCM provides region-aware, phenotype-adaptive motion synthesis that mitigates oversmoothing and better captures disease-specific regional dynamics when inferring full-cycle cardiac motion from a single ED frame.

Abstract: Cardiac motion over a cardiac cycle is crucial for quantifying regional function and is strongly affected by cardiovascular diseases. Since temporally dense mesh sequences are difficult to obtain in practice, we focus on leveraging the more accessible end-diastolic frame to infer a full-cycle sequence. Due to strong regional and disease-specific differences, traditional methods often oversmooth the data by relying on generative models that are optimized for global patterns. To address this problem, we propose Region-Aware and Phenotype-Adaptive Bi-Ventricular Cardiac Motion Synthesis (RePCM) for single frame Bi-ventricular mesh motion completion. In Stage I, a reconstruction network learns vertex wise motion descriptors and clustering yields a data driven functional partition, providing an explicit motion derived region structure. In Stage II, a Region-Specific Injection Module enforces masked, synchronized region exchange within a conditional VAE, preserving localized specific dynamics and restricting cross-region mixing. A Phenotype-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts prior conditioned on ED shape uses anatomy-guided cues to model latent motion trends and capture inter-disease variability. Experiments on three datasets covering different cardiovascular diseases show consistent gains in geometric and functional metrics and improved preservation of region specific dynamics.

</details>


### [134] [SR-Ground: Image Quality Grounding for Super-Resolved Content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21244)
*Artem Borisov,Evgeney Bogatyrev,Khaled Abud,Dmitriy Vatolin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SR-Ground, a large-scale dataset for fine-grained artifact segmentation in super-resolution (SR); 63k images, 6 artifact types, annotated at pixel level; validated by crowdsourcing; enables grounding-enabled IQA and a pipeline to reduce perceptible SR artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current IQA methods lack interpretability and cannot distinguish among different SR artifact types; there is a need for fine-grained, localized artifact information to guide evaluation and remediation of diffusion-based SR outputs.

Method: Construct SR-Ground by processing images from diverse state-of-the-art SR models, producing pixel-level annotations for multiple artifact categories; validate and refine automatic segmentations via a large crowdsourcing study with 1,062 participants, resulting in 63,000 high-quality images across 6 artifact types; train grounding-enabled IQA models on the dataset; develop a fine-tuning pipeline that uses the grounding model to suppress artifacts in SR outputs.

Result: A high-quality, large-scale dataset with pixel-level segmentation for six SR artifact types (63k images). Demonstrates that grounding-based IQA improves performance on downstream tasks and provides a practical fine-tuning pipeline to reduce perceptible SR artifacts.

Conclusion: Grounding-based, interpretable evaluation and targeted artifact mitigation become feasible for SR, with SR-Ground enabling more explainable IQA and actionable improvements to SR outputs.

Abstract: Super-Resolution (SR) has advanced rapidly in recent years, with diffusion-based models achieving unprecedented fidelity at the cost of introducing new types of visual artifacts. While existing Image Quality Assessment (IQA) methods provide holistic quality scores, they lack interpretability and fail to distinguish between different artifact types arising from modern SR approaches.
  To address this gap, we introduce SR-Ground, a large-scale dataset specifically designed for fine-grained artifact segmentation in super-resolved images. The dataset comprises images processed by a diverse set of state-of-the-art SR models, with pixel-level annotations for multiple artifact categories. We conduct a large-scale crowdsourcing study involving 1,062 participants to validate and refine automatically generated segmentations, resulting in a high-quality dataset of 63,000 images spanning 6 distinct artifact types.
  We demonstrate that training IQA models with grounding capabilities on SR-Ground significantly improves performance on downstream tasks. Furthermore, we introduce a fine-tuning pipeline that leverages our grounding model to reduce perceptible artifacts in SR outputs, showcasing the practical utility of our dataset.

</details>


### [135] [STiTch: Semantic Transition and Transportation in Collaboration for Training-Free Zero-Shot Composed Image Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21261)
*Miaoge Li,Dongsheng Wang,Zening Sun,Jinsen Zhang,Wenhan Luo,Jingcai Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free zero-shot CIR method that refines LLM-generated captions via a semantic transition vector and reinterprets retrieval as a discrete set-to-set alignment using bidirectional transportation distance, aiming to improve accuracy and diversity without training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-generated captions often introduce noise and miss the core modification intent due to semantic gaps between image details and text; pointwise alignment fails to capture diverse compositions in zero-shot CIR.

Method: 1) Refine LLM-inferred captions with a transition vector in embedding space guided by user instruction to emphasize core modification; 2) model caption and image as discrete distributions; 3) perform set-to-set alignment during retrieval; 4) compute retrieval score via a bidirectional transportation distance.

Result: Extensive experiments suggest the method is general, effective, and benefits various CIR tasks, outperforming baselines in zero-shot, compositional retrieval scenarios.

Conclusion: The framework addresses semantic gap and diversity issues in training-free zero-shot CIR by combining semantic refinement of captions and distribution-based, bidirectional alignment, offering a flexible, general approach for multimodal retrieval.

Abstract: Training-free zero-shot composed image retrieval models are recently gaining increasing research interest due to their generalizability and flexibility in unseen multimodal retrieval. Recent LLM-based advances focus on generating the expected target caption by exploring the compositional ability behind the LLMs. Although efficient, we find that 1) the generated captions tend to introduce unexpected features from the reference image due to the semantic gap between the input image and text modification, where the image contains much more details than the text; 2) the point-to-point alignment during the retrieval stage fails to capture diverse compositions. To address these challenges, we introduce a novel Semantic Transition and Transportation in collaboration framework for training-free zero-shot CIR tasks. Specifically, given the composed caption inferred by an LLM, we aim to refine it through a transition vector in the embedding space and make it closer to the target image. Combining LLMs with user instruction, the refined caption concentrates more on the core modification intent and thus filters out unnecessary noise. Moreover, to explore diverse alignment during the retrieval stage, we model the caption and image as discrete distributions and reformulate the retrieval task as a set-to-set alignment task. Finally, a bidirectional transportation distance is developed to consider fine-grained alignments across modalities and calculate the retrieval score. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method can be general, effective, and beneficial for many CIR tasks.

</details>


### [136] [Vision Transformers and Convolutional Neural Networks for Land Use Scene Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21268)
*Arun D. Kulkarni*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CNNs (e.g., AlexNet) offer robust performance for land-use scene classification on small datasets with strong local textures, while Vision Transformers (ViTs) can surpass CNNs in capturing global spatial relationships given sufficient data and computing resources; trade-offs include data requirements and computational cost across UC Merced and EuroSAT benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a concise, structured comparison to illuminate the relative strengths and limitations of CNNs and ViTs for remote sensing land-use scene classification, guiding model selection in practice.

Method: Benchmark comparison using a representative CNN (AlexNet) and a Vision Transformer (ViT) on UC Merced and EuroSAT datasets. Evaluate classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) and computational complexity; analyze performance under varying training data availability to contrast local-texture vs global-context capture.

Result: CNNs demonstrate robust performance with limited training samples and strong local texture cues; ViTs achieve superior performance in capturing global spatial relationships in complex scenes when ample training data are available, but require greater computational resources and larger datasets to reach optimal performance.

Conclusion: Model selection should be data-regime aware: prefer CNNs for data-scarce or texture-dominated LUSC tasks; opt for ViTs when ample labeled data and compute exist to leverage global context; consider data augmentation, transfer learning, or hybrid architectures to balance strengths.

Abstract: Land Use Scene Classification (LUSC) from remote sensing imagery plays a critical role in environmental monitoring, urban planning, and sustainable resource management. In recent years, deep learning methods have significantly advanced the state of the art, with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) dominating the field because of their strong ability to capture local spatial features. However, the emergence of Vision Transformers (ViTs) has introduced a new paradigm that models long-range dependencies through self-attention mechanisms, potentially enabling improved global context understanding. This paper presents a comparative assessment of Vision Transformers and CNN-based architecture for remote sensing land use scene classification. Representative CNN models, such as AlexNet, is evaluated alongside the Vision Transformer (ViT) using benchmark remote sensing datasets, including the UC Merced Land Use and EuroSAT Land Use datasets. The study examines classification accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and computational complexity to provide a comprehensive performance comparison. Experimental results demonstrate that CNNs perform robustly on datasets with limited training samples and strong local texture characteristics, whereas Vision Transformers exhibit superior performance in capturing global spatial relationships in complex scenes when sufficient training data are available. However, ViTs typically require greater computational resources and larger training datasets to achieve optimal performance. The findings of this study provide insights into the strengths and limitations of both architectures and offer guidance for selecting appropriate models for remote sensing land use scene classification applications.

</details>


### [137] [MONET: A Massive, Open, Non-redundant and Enriched Text-to-image dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21272)
*Benjamin Aubin,Gonzalo Iñaki Quintana,Onur Tasar,Sanjeev Sreetharan,Urszula Czerwinska,Damien Henry,Clément Chadebec*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MONET is a large, open, and safer 104.9M image-text dataset for text-to-image research, derived from 2.9B raw pairs and curated with filtering, de-duplication, and re-captioning; it includes precomputed embeddings and shows competitive performance when training a 4B-parameter latent diffusion model, thus lowering barriers to reproducible T2I research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training large text-to-image models requires high-quality, diverse datasets; collecting, filtering, deduplicating, and re-captioning at scale is costly, hindering open and reproducible research in the field.

Method: Construct MONET through a multi-stage pipeline: safety filtering, domain-based filtering, exact/near-duplicate removal, and re-captioning using multiple vision-language models to produce short-to-long descriptions; augment with synthetic samples; source data from heterogeneous open sources; provide precomputed embeddings/annotations; dataset released under Apache 2.0.

Result: MONET comprises ~104.9M image-text pairs derived from ~2.9B raw pairs. Models trained on MONET (a 4B-parameter latent diffusion model) achieved competitive GenEval and DPG scores, validating its effectiveness for scalable, reproducible T2I research.

Conclusion: MONET lowers the barrier to large-scale, reproducible text-to-image research by offering a well-filtered, diverse, captioned dataset with ready-to-use embeddings and annotations.

Abstract: Training large text-to-image models requires high-quality, curated datasets with diverse content and detailed captions. Yet the cost and complexity of collecting, filtering, deduplicating, and re-captioning such corpora at scale hinders open and reproducible research in the field. We introduce MONET, an open Apache 2.0 dataset of approx. 104.9M image--text pairs collected from 2.9B raw pairs across heterogeneous open sources through successive stages of safety filtering, domain-based filtering, exact and near-duplicate removal, and re-captioning with multiple vision-language models covering short to long-form descriptions, and further augmented with synthetically generated samples. Each image is shipped with pre-computed embeddings and annotations to accelerate downstream use. To validate the effectiveness of MONET, we train a 4B-parameter latent diffusion model exclusively on it and reach competitive GenEval and DPG scores, demonstrating that our dataset lowers the barrier to large-scale, reproducible text-to-image research.

</details>


### [138] [DriveMA: Rethinking Language Interfaces in Driving VLAs with One-Step Meta-Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21273)
*Weicheng Zheng,Yixin Huang,Qiao Sun,Derun Li,Hang zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes one-step meta-actions as a concise, high-signal interface for Driving VLAs, paired with DriveMA that uses action-centric supervision plus turn-level RL to optimize meta-action accuracy, trajectory quality, and consistency; achieves state-of-the-art results on Waymo End-to-End Driving Challenge (2B: 8.060 RFS; 4B: 8.079) and competitive NAVSIM performance; shows meta-actions beat language reasoning in expressiveness and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning-centric interfaces in Driving VLAs face bottlenecks: scarce high-quality reasoning annotations, difficulty for compact models to handle long reasoning chains, and high inference latency. A concise, automatically derivable meta-action interface could provide semantic grounding with low entropy, enabling scalable supervision and reliable trajectory conditioning.

Method: Introduce DriveMA, integrating action-centric supervised training with a turn-level credit-assignment reinforcement learning framework. The interface uses one-step meta-actions derived from expert trajectories, optimizing meta-action correctness, trajectory quality, and trajectory–meta-action consistency.

Result: State-of-the-art on the Waymo End-to-End Driving Challenge for a 2B model (RFS 8.060) and improved results with a 4B model (RFS 8.079); competitive results on NAVSIM. Ablations indicate one-step meta-actions balance expressiveness, predictability, and inference efficiency better than verbose natural-language reasoning or finer-grained action sequences.

Conclusion: Concise one-step meta-actions provide an effective and scalable interface for Driving VLAs, enabling strong performance with DriveMA; supports simpler, faster, and more reliable end-to-end driving systems, with released code/data/models forthcoming.

Abstract: Driving Vision-Language-Action Models (Driving VLAs) commonly introduce natural-language reasoning as an intermediate interface for end-to-end planning, but reasoning-centric interfaces face three practical bottlenecks: obtaining high-quality reasoning annotations is difficult, generating and understanding long reasoning chains is challenging for compact models, and inference latency is substantially increased. In this paper, we rethink the design of language interfaces in Driving VLAs and show that concise one-step meta-actions are a simple yet effective alternative to verbose reasoning. Meta-actions provide semantic decision grounding while remaining low-entropy, and being automatically derivable from expert trajectories, enabling scalable supervision and reliable trajectory conditioning. Building on this interface, we propose DriveMA, which combines action-centric supervised training with a turn-level credit-assignment reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes meta-action correctness, trajectory quality, and trajectory--meta-action consistency. Experiments show that DriveMA already achieves a new state of the art on the Waymo End-to-End Driving Challenge with a 2B model, reaching a Rater Feedback Score (RFS) of 8.060, while its 4B version further improves the state of the art to 8.079; DriveMA also obtains competitive performance on NAVSIM. Ablations demonstrate that one-step meta-actions offer a better practical trade-off between expressiveness, predictability, and inference efficiency than natural-language reasoning or finer-grained action sequences. Code, data, and models will be released to facilitate future research.

</details>


### [139] [Let EEG Models Learn EEG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21280)
*Yifan Wang,Yijia Ma,Wen Li,Chenyu You*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A continuous-time EEG generator (JET) uses conditional flow matching to model raw EEG as continuous trajectories, preserving spectral and temporal structure; achieves state-of-the-art TS-FID reductions on three benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data scarcity and privacy constraints in EEG modeling, plus inadequacy of discrete denoising objectives to capture continuous temporal dynamics and spectral structure.

Method: JET learns a smooth vector field that transports noise to EEG data via conditional flow matching, operating on raw continuous EEG sequences; enforces constraints to preserve spectral structure, temporal stationarity, and signal-level statistics.

Result: On three large-scale benchmarks, JET achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing TS-FID by over 40% compared to strong baselines, and captures key structural properties of neural dynamics.

Conclusion: A principled continuous-time generative framework for EEG that better models temporal dynamics and spectral properties, enabling scalable, high-fidelity EEG generation.

Abstract: High-fidelity EEG generation is critical for alleviating data scarcity and addressing privacy constraints in large-scale neural modeling. Despite recent progress, most existing approaches formulate EEG generation via discrete denoising objectives, which inadequately reflect the inherently continuous temporal dynamics and spectral structure of neural activity. As a result, these methods often struggle to preserve long-range temporal dependencies and exhibit mismatches in the spectral and temporal structure of the generated signals. In this work, we argue that effective EEG generation requires models that operate directly on the continuous evolution of neural signals. We introduce Just EEG Transformer (JET), a generative framework based on conditional flow matching that models EEG as raw sequences evolving along continuous trajectories. By learning a smooth vector field that transports noise to the EEG data distribution, JET captures temporal continuity and transient dynamics without relying on discretized denoising schemes or domain-specific representations. To ensure that the learned dynamics remain consistent with key properties of EEG signals, we introduce principled constraints that preserve spectral structure, temporal stationarity, and signal-level statistics. Across three large-scale benchmarks, JET consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing TS-FID by over 40% compared to strong baselines. Extensive analyses show that JET captures key structural properties of neural dynamics, providing a scalable and principled approach to EEG generation. Project page: https://y-research-sbu.github.io/JET/ .

</details>


### [140] [Reducing Object Hallucination in LVLMs via Emphasizing Image-negative Tokens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21300)
*Meng Shen,Minghao Wu,Deepu Rajan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Train-time mitigation of LVLM hallucinations by weighting tokens based on visual dependence and by filtering hallucination-prone data; validated across three LVLM variants; reduces hallucinations without increasing inference cost or reducing response length.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Object hallucination in large vision-language models (LVLMs) is a practical issue. The authors hypothesize that models over-prioritize text generation and underutilize visual information. They aim to rebalance training signals to improve visual grounding and reduce hallucinations.

Method: Classify generated tokens into image-positive, invariant, and negative based on their dependence on input image tokens. Adjust training weights for tokens according to these categories to reduce reliance on text-only generation. Additionally, filter training data to remove samples likely to induce hallucinations. Evaluate the approaches across three LVLM variants to test generality.

Result: Both training-time interventions reduce hallucinations without shortening response length or increasing inference cost. The methods show effectiveness and general applicability across three LVLM variants.

Conclusion: Rebalancing token-level training emphasis toward image-dependent information and removing hallucination-prone data are effective, generalizable strategies to mitigate hallucinations in LVLMs without impacting inference efficiency.

Abstract: Object hallucination is a significant challenge that hinders the application of large vision-language models (LVLMs) in practice. We hypothesize that one possible origin of hallucination is the model's tendency to prioritize text generation over meaningful interaction with images. To explore this, we examine the generation process and categorize text tokens into three groups: image-positive, invariant, and negative, based on their visual dependence on input image tokens. Our analysis reveals that most generated tokens are minimally influenced by the image information. This suggests that during the model's training stage, more emphasis is placed on learning how to follow textual instructions, rather than extracting information from images. Based on this finding, we propose adjusting the training weights of different tokens depending on their visual dependence to control hallucination. Additionally, we remove a portion of the training data that potentially contains more hallucinations as a data filtering strategy. Both methods achieve a reduction in hallucination without compromising response length or introducing additional computational costs during inference. We validate our methods across three LVLM variants, demonstrating the effectiveness and general applicability.

</details>


### [141] [OcclusionFormer: Arranging Z-Order for Layout-Grounded Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21343)
*Ziye Li,Henghui Ding*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SA-Z, a large-scale occlusion-annotated dataset, and OcclusionFormer, an occlusion-aware diffusion transformer that models Z-order by decoupling instances and volume rendering, with a queried alignment loss for per-instance supervision, achieving better handling of overlaps and structural consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Layout-to-image models struggle with inter-object occlusion due to missing explicit occlusion information in overlapping bounding boxes, leading to ambiguous regions and inconsistent layering.

Method: Create SA-Z with explicit occlusion ordering and pixel-level annotations. Propose OcclusionFormer, a diffusion Transformer that explicitly models Z-order by decoupling instances and compositing them via volume rendering. Introduce a queried alignment loss to supervise individual instances and enhance semantic consistency.

Result: Reduces ambiguity in overlapping regions, enforces correct occlusion dependencies, preserves structural integrity, and yields substantial accuracy gains across diverse scenes.

Conclusion: Occlusion-aware modeling with explicit ordering and per-instance supervision improves layout-to-image generation in occluded regions and supports more reliable, structurally consistent images.

Abstract: Recent layout-to-image models have achieved remarkable progress in spatial controllability. However, they still struggle with inter-object occlusion. When bounding boxes overlap, most existing methods lack explicit occlusion information, which makes the generation in intersection regions inherently ambiguous and hinders the determination of complex occlusion relationships. As a result, they often produce entangled textures or physically inconsistent layering in the overlapped areas. To address this issue, we first construct SA-Z, a large-scale dataset enriched with explicit occlusion ordering and pixel-level annotations. Building upon our proposed dataset, we introduce OcclusionFormer, a novel occlusion-aware Diffusion Transformer framework that explicitly models Z-order priority by decoupling instances and compositing them via volume rendering. Furthermore, to ensure fine-grained spatial precision, we introduce a queried alignment loss that explicitly supervises individual instances and enhances semantic consistency. The proposed method effectively reduces ambiguity in overlapping regions, enforces correct occlusion dependencies, and preserves structural integrity, leading to substantial accuracy gains across diverse scenes.

</details>


### [142] [A Non-Reference Diffusion-Based Restoration Framework for Landsat 7 ETM+ SLC-off Imagery in Antarctica](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21371)
*Leyue Tang,Jonathan Louis Bamber,Gang Qiao,Yuanhang Kong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes DiffGF, a non-reference diffusion-based framework to restore Landsat 7 SLC-off imagery in Antarctica using latent-space diffusion and pixel-space refinement; introduces SLCANT dataset; validated via quantitative/qualitative analyses and a downstream crevasse segmentation task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Antarctic optical data are scarce due to prolonged polar nights and frequent cloud cover; Landsat 7 SLC-off introduces ~22% missing pixels, and reference-based gap filling is hampered by rapid, large surface changes and lack of reliable references.

Method: Two-stage architecture: latent-space diffusion process to model missing data, followed by pixel-space refinement; trained on a dedicated Antarctic dataset (SLCANT) without external references; evaluation includes both restoration quality and a downstream crevasse segmentation task.

Result: Quantitative and qualitative results show high-fidelity restoration of Antarctic SLC-off imagery; downstream crevasse segmentation benefits from the restored data.

Conclusion: DiffGF enables effective use of Landsat 7 SLC-off archives in Antarctica, facilitating information extraction from historical records and supporting Antarctic studies.

Abstract: Acquiring usable optical imagery in Antarctica is inherently challenging due to prolonged polar nights and frequent cloud cover. Landsat provides the longest and most continuous optical observations and constitutes one of the most important remote sensing data sources for Antarctic studies. However, the scan-line corrector (SLC) failure in 2003 resulted in approximately 22% missing pixels in Landsat 7 ETM+ SLC-off imagery, severely limiting its usability. Unlike many non-polar environments, Antarctic surfaces undergo rapid and substantial changes, which makes it difficult to obtain reliable reference imagery and reduces the applicability of conventional reference-based gap-filling methods. To address this challenge, we propose DiffGF, a non-reference diffusion-based framework for restoring Landsat 7 SLC-off imagery without requiring any external reference data. DiffGF adopts a two-stage design consisting of a latent-space diffusion process and a pixel-space refinement. A dedicated Antarctic dataset, SLCANT, is constructed for training and evaluation. Quantitative and qualitative results demonstrate that DiffGF restores Antarctic SLC-off imagery with high fidelity. Its practical value is further examined through a downstream crevasse segmentation application. The results suggest that DiffGF provides a useful approach for exploiting Landsat 7 SLC-off archives in Antarctica, enabling the extraction of valuable information from historical records and supporting related Antarctic studies.

</details>


### [143] [Disentangling Generation and Regression in Stochastic Interpolants for Controllable Image Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21381)
*Yi Liu,Jia Ma,Wengen Li,Jihong Guan,Shuigeng Zhou,Yichao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiSI proposes a unified framework that disentangles the stochastic interpolant into independent generation and regression components, enabling a controllable spectrum from pure regression to fully generative. It uses two sampling trajectories, a unified few-step sampler, and a dual-branch pixel-space transformer to achieve competitive IR results with adjustable distortion-perception trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between fast, pixel-faithful regression-based image restoration and slow, texture-realistic generative methods by decoupling the generative process from regression in the stochastic interpolant.

Method: Introduce DiSI to decouple the stochastic interpolant into generation and regression components. Instantiate with two sampling trajectories and a unified sampler for few-step, high-quality inference on arbitrary trajectories. Employ a dual-branch U-Net style transformer in pixel space with a dedicated branch for stronger conditional guidance to maintain throughput.

Result: Demonstrates competitive performance on various IR tasks and uniquely enables inference-time control over the distortion-perception trade-off within a single model.

Conclusion: DiSI offers a versatile IR framework that smoothly transitions from regression to generative modes, enabling efficient high-throughput inference and flexible trade-off control across tasks.

Abstract: Recent advances in Image Restoration (IR) have been largely driven by generative methods such as Diffusion Models and Flow Matching, which excel in synthesizing realistic textures while suffering from slow multi-step inference and compromised pixel fidelity. In contrast, classical regression-based IR methods excel precisely in these aspects, offering single-step efficiency and high pixel-level reconstruction fidelity. To bridge this gap, we propose DiSI, a unified framework that Disentangles the underlying Stochastic Interpolant process into independent generation and regression components. This decoupling endows DiSI with remarkable versatility, enabling a continuous and controllable transition from a pure regression process to a fully generative one. Technically, we instantiate this framework with two specific sampling trajectories, accompanied by a unified sampler for high-quality, few-step inference on arbitrary trajectories. Furthermore, we design a dual-branch U-Net style transformer network in pixel space, using a dedicated branch to enhance conditional guidance while ensuring high throughput. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DiSI efficiently achieves competitive results on various IR tasks, while uniquely offering the inference-time flexibility to control the distortion-perception trade-off within a single model.

</details>


### [144] [RoadTones: Tone Controllable Text Generation from Road Event Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21411)
*Chirag Parikh,Siddhi Pravin Lipare,Ravi Kiran Sarvadevabhatla*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes RoadTones dataset/model/evaluation suite for tone-controllable road video captioning, including tone-conditioned CoT drafts and user-study validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video-language models produce factual road-event captions but lack control over tone and presentation, which is crucial for communication-critical contexts.

Method: Data: RoadTones-51K with tonal annotations and multi-tone captions. Model: RoadTones-VL-CoT that generates tone-conditioned captions plus intermediate tone-conditioned Chain-of-Thought drafts for interpretability. Evaluation: RoadTones-Eval jointly measures factual consistency and tone adherence; a user study validates caption quality, tone control, and factual consistency.

Result: Introduces dataset, model, and evaluation suite; conducts a user study; results support caption quality, tone controllability, and factual consistency.

Conclusion: Establishes foundation for context-sensitive, tone-controllable video captioning in road/video settings.

Abstract: Existing video-language models can generate factual descriptions of road events but lack control over how these events are expressed: their tone, urgency, or style. This limits deployment in communication-critical settings where the effectiveness of a message depends on both content and presentation, not just factual accuracy. To mitigate this, we introduce a comprehensive dataset-model-evaluation suite for tone-controllable road video captioning. Our human-validated data generation pipeline expands road-video corpora with diverse tonal annotations and multi-tone captions, yielding the RoadTones-51K dataset. We propose RoadTones-VL-CoT, a controllable video-to-text model that also generates tone-conditioned Chain-of-Thought intermediate drafts for interpretability. We also introduce RoadTones-Eval, a new evaluation suite that jointly measures factual consistency and tone adherence. In addition, we conducted a user study whose results validate caption quality, tone control, and factual consistency. Together, these contributions lay the foundation for context-sensitive tone-controllable video captioning.

</details>


### [145] [Ordering Matters: Rank-Aware Selective Fusion for Blended Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21417)
*Junghyun Lee,Hyunseo Kim,Hanna Jang,Junhyug Noh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A rank-aware multi-encoder fusion framework selectively combines multiple pre-extracted video and audio encoders for blended emotion recognition, using a gating mechanism to pick the top-n encoders and decouple presence and salience predictions with probability-level fusion, achieving 2nd place on BlEmoRE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emotions are often expressed as mixtures of subtle, overlapping multimodal cues; single encoders or naïve fusion fail to capture blended states and are brittle under distribution shifts; there is a need for selective, robust multimodal fusion that can handle varying cue informativeness.

Method: Introduce a rank-aware multi-encoder framework that embeds heterogeneous encoder outputs into a shared latent space; use an attention-based gating module to estimate sample-wise encoder importance and fuse only the top-n encoders; decouple prediction into presence and salience heads with probability-level fusion; apply feature-level unsupervised domain adaptation without pseudo-labeling to improve robustness.

Result: Empirical evaluation on the BlEmoRE challenge shows the proposed method outperforms strong individual encoders and naïve fusion baselines, ranking 2nd in the competition.

Conclusion: Rank-aware selective fusion effectively leverages complementary cues from diverse encoders to model blended emotions and offers robustness to distribution shifts without requiring pseudo-labels.

Abstract: Blended emotion recognition is challenging because emotions are often expressed as mixtures of subtle and overlapping multimodal cues rather than a single dominant signal. We propose a rank-aware multi-encoder framework that selectively combines complementary representations from diverse pre-extracted video and audio encoders. Our method projects heterogeneous encoder features into a shared latent space, estimates sample-wise encoder importance through an attention-based gating module, and fuses only the top-n most informative encoders. To better model blended emotions, we decouple prediction into presence and salience heads and align them through probability-level fusion. We further incorporate feature-level unsupervised domain adaptation without pseudo-labeling to improve robustness under distribution shift. Experiments on the BlEmoRE challenge show that the proposed framework outperforms strong individual encoders and naïve multi-encoder fusion baselines. Our final system ranked 2nd in the competition, supporting the effectiveness of rank-aware selective fusion for fine-grained blended emotion recognition.

</details>


### [146] [AIGaitor: Privacy-preserving and cloud-free motion analysis for everyone, using edge computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21421)
*Lauhitya Reddy,Trisha M. Kesar,Hyeokhyen Kwon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AIGaitor enables end-to-end monocular, on-device motion capture and gait analysis on a smartphone, addressing privacy, cost, and complexity barriers; it achieves on-device speed comparable to cloud pipelines when accounting for network transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical motion analysis is hampered by high cost, technical complexity, and privacy concerns. A survey of rehabilitation clinicians shows demand for accurate, affordable, easy-to-use gait analysis tools and highlights main barriers (cost, training, privacy).

Method: Develop on-device monocular motion-capture pipeline with 2D/3D pose estimation, pose optimization, skeleton-based deep-learning analysis, and a vision-language model. Implemented on iPhone 14 era hardware using lightweight models (e.g., ViTPose-s) and Time-Priority end-to-end processing of a 10 s 4K 60 fps video. Benchmarks compare on-device pipeline to cloud-based NVIDIA H200 with and without network transfer, reporting end-to-end times (77 s on-device; 94 s uplink; 66 s wifi) and real-time/keypoint performance with sub-millisecond gait classification.

Result: The on-device pipeline matches or surpasses the cloud pipeline when network transfer is included. AIGaitor is claimed as the first monocular system to demonstrate end-to-end on-device motion capture and downstream DL analysis, delivering clinically applicable movement analysis that is low-cost, private, and smartphone-accessible.

Conclusion: AIGaitor presents a novel, privacy-preserving, on-device gait analysis platform capable of end-to-end monocular motion capture and DL-based analysis on consumer smartphones, potentially enabling broader clinical adoption. Future work could address robustness across conditions, energy efficiency, and broader clinical validation.

Abstract: Motion capture is the gold standard for measuring human movement, but clinical use remains limited by cost, technical complexity, and privacy concerns. AIGaitor is a privacy-preserving, cloud-free motion analysis system that runs markerless monocular motion-capture pipelines and downstream deep-learning analysis entirely on a consumer smartphone using on-device neural accelerators. To motivate its design, we surveyed 74 rehabilitation clinicians: 92 percent said they would adopt an accurate, cost-effective, easy-to-use AI gait analysis tool, while 79.7 percent cited operating cost, 68.9 percent insufficient training, and 64.9 percent privacy concerns as leading barriers. We then optimized and benchmarked mobile iOS implementations of current monocular pipeline components, including 2D and 3D pose estimation, pose optimization, skeleton-based deep-learning analysis, and a vision-language model. A Time-Priority end-to-end on-device pipeline processes a 10 s 4K 60 fps video clip in 77 s on an iPhone 14, matching or beating the same pipeline on a high-end NVIDIA H200 cloud server when network transfer is included: 94 s at global mobile-average uplink and 66 s at developed-world Wi-Fi. Lightweight models such as ViTPose-s achieve real-time keypoint extraction, and skeleton-based action-recognition models provide sub-millisecond gait classification on the same clip. To our knowledge, AIGaitor is the first monocular system to demonstrate end-to-end on-device motion capture and downstream deep-learning analysis, supporting clinically applicable movement analysis that is low-cost, private, and accessible to smartphone users.

</details>


### [147] [iTryOn: Mastering Interactive Video Virtual Try-On with Spatial-Semantic Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21431)
*Jun Zheng,Zhengze Xu,Mengting Chen,Jing Wang,Jinsong Lan,Xiaoyong Zhu,Kaifu Zhang,Bo Zheng,Xiaodan Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Interactive Video Virtual Try-On (Interactive VVT) and iTryOn, a diffusion-based framework with spatial hand priors and semantic caption guidance; achieves state-of-the-art on VVT benchmarks and leads in interactive scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video virtual try-on (VVT) methods focus on non-interactive garment showcasing and struggle with active human-garment interaction. There is semantic ambiguity in interactions from pose, and garment deformation learning is data-sparse when interactions are brief. A framework that models both spatial hand-garment contact and temporal semantic cues is needed.

Method: iTryOn builds on a large-scale video diffusion Transformer with a multi-level interaction injection mechanism: (1) spatial level uses a garment-agnostic 3D hand prior to guide precise hand-garment contact and resolve spatial ambiguity; (2) semantic level uses global captions for overall context and time-stamped action captions for localized interactions, synchronized via Action-aware Rotational Position Embedding (A-RoPE).

Result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance on traditional VVT benchmarks and a commanding lead in the new interactive setting, enabling more dynamic and controllable virtual try-on experiences.

Conclusion: Presents a significant step toward dynamic, controllable VVT with interactive capabilities; introduces novel spatial-semantic guidance and temporal alignment mechanisms; future work may explore broader interactions, efficiency, and generalization to diverse garments and body types.

Abstract: Video Virtual Try-On (VVT) aims to seamlessly replace a garment on a person in a video with a new one. While existing methods have made significant strides in maintaining temporal consistency, they are predominantly confined to non-interactive scenarios where models merely showcase garments. This limitation overlooks a crucial aspect of real-world apparel presentation: active human-garment interaction. To bridge this gap, we introduce and formalize a new challenging task: Interactive Video Virtual Try-On (Interactive VVT), where subjects in the video actively engage with their clothing. This task introduces unique challenges beyond simple texture preservation, including: (1) resolving the semantic ambiguity of interactions from standard pose information, and (2) learning complex garment deformations from video where interactive moments are sparse and brief. To address these challenges, we propose iTryOn, a novel framework built upon a large-scale video diffusion Transformer. iTryOn pioneers a multi-level interaction injection mechanism to guide the generation of complex dynamics. At the spatial level, we introduce a garment-agnostic 3D hand prior to provide fine-grained guidance for precise hand-garment contact, effectively resolving spatial ambiguity. At the semantic level, iTryOn leverages global captions for overall context and time-stamped action captions for localized interactions, synchronized via our novel Action-aware Rotational Position Embedding (A-RoPE). Extensive experiments demonstrate that iTryOn not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on traditional VVT benchmarks but also establishes a commanding lead in the new interactive setting, marking a significant step towards more dynamic and controllable virtual try-on experiences.

</details>


### [148] [ReMATF: Recurrent Motion-Adaptive Multi-scale Turbulence Mitigation for Dynamic Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21440)
*Zhiming Liu,Zhicheng Zou,Nantheera Anantrasirichai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes ReMATF, a lightweight two-frame recurrent framework for atmospheric turbulence video restoration that achieves real-time performance with per-pixel temporal fusion, outperforming multi-frame transformer baselines in quality and speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Atmospheric turbulence distorts video with geometric warping, blur, and flicker. State-of-the-art methods rely on transformer/3D architectures using multi-frame inputs, which are computationally heavy and poorly suited for real-time, resource-constrained scenarios.

Method: ReMATF uses a two-frame recurrent design with a multi-scale encoder-decoder, temporal warping, and a motion-adaptive temporal fusion module that per-pixel fuses the warped previous output with the current prediction without expanding the temporal window.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real turbulence datasets show consistent improvements in PSNR/SSIM and perceptual quality (LPIPS), with substantially faster inference compared to multi-frame transformer baselines.

Conclusion: ReMATF provides an efficient turbulence mitigation solution suitable for resource-constrained deployment, balancing spatial detail, temporal coherence, and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Atmospheric turbulence severely degrades video quality by introducing distortions such as geometric warping, blur, and temporal flickering, posing significant challenges to both visual clarity and temporal consistency. Current state-of-the-art methods are based on transformer, 3D architectures and require multi-frame input, but their large computational cost and memory usage limit real-time deployment, especially in resource-constrained scenarios. In this work, we propose ReMATF, a lightweight recurrent framework that restores videos using only two frames at a time while preserving spatial detail and temporal stability. ReMATF combines a multi-scale encoder-decoder with temporal warping and a motion-adaptive temporal fusion module that performs per-pixel fusion between the warped previous output and the current prediction to enhance coherence without enlarging the temporal window. This design reduces flicker, sharpens details, and remains efficient. Experiments on synthetic and real turbulence datasets show consistent improvements in PSNR/SSIM and perceptual quality (LPIPS), along with substantially faster inference than multi-frame transformer baselines, making ReMATF suitable turbulence mitigation in resource-constrained scenarios.

</details>


### [149] [TempGlitch: Evaluating Vision-Language Models for Temporal Glitch Detection in Gameplay Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21443)
*Yakun Yu,Ashley Wiens,Adrián Barahona-Ríos,Benedict Wilkins,Saman Zadtootaghaj,Nabajeet Barman,Cor-Paul Bezemer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TempGlitch is a video benchmark to evaluate temporal glitch detection in vision-language models for games, revealing current models struggle with temporal cues; dataset and code are released for robust temporal reasoning evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLM evaluations treat glitches as static frames, missing temporal reasoning capabilities; a dedicated benchmark is needed to assess models' ability to detect glitches that emerge over time in gameplay videos.

Method: Five temporal glitch types with balanced samples are compiled into TempGlitch, with paired glitch-free videos; 12 proprietary/open VLMs are evaluated under multiple frame-sampling settings, assessing temporal detection performance.

Result: Current VLMs perform near chance on TempGlitch; models exhibit conservative or over-sensitive behavior; denser frame sampling and larger models do not consistently improve detection; TempGlitch serves as a focused testbed for temporal reasoning in gameplay understanding.

Conclusion: TempGlitch fills a gap in evaluating temporal reasoning for video QA with VLMs and provides a resource for developing models capable of robust temporal glitch detection in games; code and data are publicly available.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly being explored for video game quality assurance, especially gameplay glitch detection. Most existing evaluations, however, treat glitches as static visual anomalies, asking models to detect failures from a single frame. We argue that this framing misses a key distinction: some glitches are spatial and visible in an isolated frame, whereas others are temporal and become evident only through changes across ordered frames. A preliminary study confirms this gap, showing that temporal glitches are substantially harder for VLMs to detect than spatial ones. To enable systematic evaluation of this underexplored setting, we introduce TempGlitch, a controlled gameplay video benchmark for temporal glitch detection. TempGlitch covers five temporal glitch types with balanced per-category samples, together with paired glitch-free videos that enable reliable binary evaluation. We evaluate 12 proprietary and open-weight VLMs across multiple frame-sampling settings. Our results show that current VLMs remain near chance on TempGlitch, often collapsing into either overly conservative behavior that misses most glitches or overly sensitive behavior that flags clean videos as glitchy. Moreover, denser frame sampling and larger model size do not reliably resolve these failures. TempGlitch provides a focused testbed for temporal reasoning, robust gameplay understanding, and automated glitch detection with VLMs. Code and data are available at the project website.

</details>


### [150] [ProtoPathway: Biologically Structured Prototype-Pathway Fusion for Multimodal Cancer Survival Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21454)
*Amaya Gallagher-Syed,Costantino Pitzalis,Myles J. Lewis,Michael R. Barnes,Gregory Slabaugh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An interpretable multimodal framework ProtoPathway for cancer survival that unifies whole slide imaging and transcriptomics through learnable prototypes and pathway-aware GNNs, enabling cross-modal attention-based interpretability across genes, pathways, prototypes, and tissue morphology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cancer prognosis can benefit from jointly modeling histopathology and molecular data, but existing multimodal methods often lack stable, interpretable representations and scalable fusion. ProtoPathway aims to provide biologically grounded, end-to-end trainable representations with native interpretability.

Method: Two branches: (1) histology uses K learnable morphological prototypes trained with a survival objective; patches are softly assigned to prototypes to produce fixed-size tokens. (2) genomics uses a bipartite graph neural network over a shared gene–pathway Reactome hierarchy to produce pathway embeddings via bidirectional message passing. Cross-modal attention operates on a prototype × pathway matrix, enabling prototypes to query pathways. The attention output provides interpretable attribution from genes to pathways to prototypes to tissue maps.

Result: Evaluated on five TCGA cancer cohorts, achieving competitive or superior survival prediction with substantially improved biological interpretability and reduced computational cost. Interpretability validated via fold-stratified rank-based population-level analysis. Code, weights, Reactome data, and unified baselines are released.

Conclusion: ProtoPathway demonstrates that stable, biologically grounded representations across histology and transcriptomics can be fused with interpretable cross-modal attention to yield strong survival predictions and meaningful attribution across the biological hierarchy, with open-source resources for replication.

Abstract: We introduce ProtoPathway, an interpretable-by-design multimodal framework for cancer survival prediction that unifies whole slide imaging and transcriptomics through encoders producing biologically grounded representations on both sides of the fusion. On the histopathology side, $K$ learnable morphological prototypes, trained end-to-end with the survival objective, serve as the slide representation itself: patches flow into prototype tokens via soft assignment, compressing variable-length patch sets into fixed task-adaptive tokens.
