On the genomic side, a bipartite graph neural network encodes gene expression within the Reactome pathway hierarchy, producing pathway embeddings that reflect both constituent genes and their broader biological context through bidirectional message passing over a shared gene--pathway graph. Cross-modal attention then operates over a compact prototype $\times$ pathway matrix in which prototypes query pathways, modeling the biological direction in which molecular programs give rise to tissue morphology. Because both axes carry stable task-learned identity, the attention matrix is itself an interpretability output, yielding native inference-time attribution across the full biological hierarchy, from genes through pathways and prototypes to spatial tissue maps. We evaluate on five TCGA cancer cohorts, demonstrating competitive or superior survival prediction with substantially improved biological interpretability and reduced computational cost, with interpretability claims validated through fold-stratified rank-based population-level analysis. Our source code, model weights, and Reactome pathways, together with a unified codebase reimplementing all multimodal survival baselines under identical preprocessing and evaluation, are available at: https://github.com/AmayaGS/ProtoPathway.

</details>


### [151] [StreamGVE: Training-Free Video Editing via Few-Step Streaming Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21466)
*Guanlong Jiao,Chenyangguang Zhang,Jia Jun Cheng Xian,Zewei Zhang,Renjie Liao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StreamGVE is a noise-to-data, streaming-generation-based video editing framework that preserves few-step sampling while injecting source-video conditioning, using dual-branch fast sampling, a self-attention bridge, cross-attention grounding/boosting, source-oriented guidance, and visual prompting; it achieves superior results with low time cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video editing largely follows a data-to-data paradigm, which incurs costly iterations and conflicts with modern generative models. A noise-to-data approach can better leverage pre-trained streaming generators and reduce editing time while maintaining quality.

Method: Build on pre-trained streaming generation models. StreamGVE employs dual-branch fast sampling with a self-attention bridge and cross-attention grounding/boosting to meet sampling and conditioning needs. It adds source-oriented guidance to improve target-generation quality and a visual prompting strategy to enhance editing flexibility. It uses cross-attention to integrate source-video conditions during editing.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse video editing tasks show StreamGVE consistently outperforms existing approaches, even in few-step settings with minimal time cost, demonstrating robustness and generalizability across different models.

Conclusion: StreamGVE provides an effective, robust, and practical solution for video editing by leveraging a noise-to-data streaming generation paradigm, achieving high-quality, conditioned edits with low computational cost and broad applicability.

Abstract: Although existing video editing methods are generally feasible, they often require many costly iterations and still struggle to deliver high-quality yet satisfying editing results. We attribute this limitation to the prevalent data-to-data paradigm, which is less compatible with modern generative models than noise-to-data generation. To address this gap, we revisit video editing from a noise-to-data perspective and propose Streaming-Generation-based Video Editing (StreamGVE), which preserves few-step sampling while seamlessly injecting source-video conditions. Built on pre-trained streaming generation models, StreamGVE introduces dual-branch fast sampling with a self-attention bridge and cross-attention grounding/boosting to satisfy both sampling and conditioning requirements. We further propose source-oriented guidance to improve target-generation quality, and a visual prompting strategy to enhance editing flexibility and practicality. The method is effective, robust, and generalizable across different models. Extensive experiments on diverse video editing tasks show that StreamGVE consistently outperforms existing approaches, even in few-step settings with minimal time cost.

</details>


### [152] [Stream3D: Sequential Multi-View 3D Generation via Evidential Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21472)
*Kaichen Zhou,Zeyang Bai,Xinhai Chang,Mengyu Wang,Paul Liang,Fangneng Zhan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Stream3D turns a frozen view-conditioned 3D generator into a streaming model with constant cross-chunk memory by maintaining a compact evidential memory that caches informative historical frames. No retraining or architectural changes are required.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address temporal inconsistency and unbounded memory when applying single-view 3D generators to long monocular streams; provide a training-free streaming mechanism.

Method: Maintain a compact evidential memory that selectively caches informative frames using an evidence score. The memory size is fixed and updated as the stream progresses, ensuring constant memory usage across chunks. The approach leaves the generator frozen and does not rely on retraining or auxiliary losses.

Result: Empirically, Stream3D outperforms latent-transport baselines (KV-cache reuse, flow-based feature editing) on realistic and synthetic streaming benchmarks, across photometric and geometric metrics.

Conclusion: Stream3D provides a practical, memory-bounded streaming solution for view-conditioned 3D generation without retraining, mitigating temporal drift and memory growth on long streams.

Abstract: View-conditioned 3D generators such as SAM 3D, TRELLIS and Hunyuan3D produce high-quality object reconstructions from a single view, but real-world visual observation often arrives as long monocular streams. Naively applying these generators to each streaming frame independently leads to severe temporal inconsistency in the generated results. To address this problem, we propose Stream3D, the first training-free streaming mechanism that turns a frozen view-conditioned 3D generator into a streaming generator with constant cross-chunk memory. Stream3D achieves this by maintaining a compact evidential memory, which selectively caches the most informative historical frames based on a proposed evidence score mechanism. As the stream progresses, the memory dynamically updates to retain a fixed number of informative frames, preventing the memory footprint from growing linearly with sequence length. This also prevents degradation over long sequences and keeps the underlying generator completely unchanged without retraining, architectural modifications, or auxiliary losses. Evaluated on both realistic and synthetic streaming benchmarks, Stream3D outperforms latent-transport baselines, including KV-cache reuse and flow-based feature editing, across both photometric and geometric metrics. More details can be found at: https://anonymous-submission-20.github.io/streaming3D.github.io/.

</details>


### [153] [Latent Dynamics for Full Body Avatar Animation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21478)
*Shichong Peng,Chengxiang Yin,Fei Jiang,Zhongshi Jiang,Lingchen Yang,Qingyang Tan,Amin Jourabloo,Jason Saragih,Ke Li,Christian Häne*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A temporal dynamics model for pose-driven 3D Gaussian avatars improves realism of loose clothing by learning a latent dynamics process that evolves with pose history, enabling diverse, coherent motion with interpretable force terms and negligible runtime overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pose-driven avatars struggle with history-dependent dynamics of loose clothing; explicit garment modeling is costly or impractical, and prior data-driven approaches lack explicit temporal dynamics or control.

Method: Augment a pose-conditioned 3D Gaussian avatar with a transformer-based decoder and a dynamics residual latent. A learned latent dynamics model evolves the residual latent from a short pose history and previous latent state, with updates decomposed into driving, restoring, and dissipative forces. Inference uses short pose history to roll out temporally coherent dynamics at minimal cost; initial conditions yield diverse trajectories and the force decomposition affords controllable stiffness.

Result: Evaluated on nine captured sequences of everyday motion with diverse loose garments. Quantitative metrics and a perceptual user study show improved animation quality over recent data-driven baselines, with improved temporal coherence and detail due to the dynamics-aware latent; the approach produces diverse plausible rollouts depending on initial conditions.

Conclusion: Incorporating a learnable dynamics latent and transformer-based decoding into pose-driven Gaussian avatars yields temporally coherent, high-fidelity renderings for loose clothing, with interpretable force components and negligible runtime impact, demonstrated across multiple everyday-motion sequences.

Abstract: Pose-driven full-body avatars built on neural rendering produce high-quality novel views of a captured subject. Yet loose clothing and other dynamic elements deform in ways pose alone cannot explain: the same pose can correspond to many different states, because their motion depends on history, inertia, and contact. Explicit simulation and layered-garment methods can model such dynamics, but they require either a dedicated garment template, which raw multi-view capture does not naturally provide, or a test-time physics simulator with non-trivial runtime cost. A parallel line of work learns data-driven clothing avatars that avoid explicit garment layers. These methods add an auxiliary latent for variation beyond pose; at inference, they fix it, regress it from pose, or retrieve it from training data, without explicitly modeling how the latent evolves with its own dynamics. Additionally, even in everyday motion with loose clothing, existing architectures often struggle to capture fine-grained detail, producing blurry renderings and temporal artifacts. We augment a pose-conditioned 3D Gaussian avatar with a transformer-based decoder and a dynamics residual latent that captures temporal appearance and geometry variation beyond the driving signals. At inference, a learned latent dynamics model evolves the residual latent from a short pose history and the previous latent state. The model decomposes each update into driving, restoring, and dissipative forces, producing temporally coherent, history-dependent rollouts with negligible added cost. Different initial conditions yield diverse yet plausible motion trajectories, and the force decomposition exposes controls such as stiffness. Across nine captured sequences of everyday motion with diverse loose garments, quantitative metrics and a perceptual user study show improved animation quality over recent data-driven baselines.

</details>


### [154] [WikiVQABench: A Knowledge-Grounded Visual Question Answering Benchmark from Wikipedia and Wikidata](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21479)
*Basel Shbita,Pengyuan Li,Anna Lisa Gentile*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WikiVQABench is a knowledge-grounded VQA benchmark built from Wikipedia images, captions, and Wikidata, using LLMs to generate MC questions and human curation to ensure external-knowledge-based reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many real-world VQA tasks require external knowledge beyond the image; existing perception-focused benchmarks fail to evaluate knowledge-aware reasoning.

Method: A pipeline uses large language models to generate candidate multiple-choice QA pairs from Wikipedia images, captions, and Wikidata; human annotators verify factual correctness, visual-text consistency, and the necessity of external knowledge for correct resolution.

Result: WikiVQABench yields a substantial collection of knowledge-enabled VQA items; evaluation across 15 VLMs (ranging from 256M to 90B parameters) shows wide performance variability (∼24.7%–75.6%), validating the benchmark's discriminative capability; dataset and code are publicly available.

Conclusion: The benchmark effectively assesses knowledge-aware vision-language models and provides a publicly accessible resource for evaluating and advancing knowledge-grounded VQA.

Abstract: Visual Question Answering (VQA) benchmarks have largely emphasized perception-based tasks that can be solved from visual content alone. In contrast, many real-world scenarios require external knowledge that is not directly observable in the image to answer correctly. We introduce WikiVQABench, a human-curated knowledge-grounded VQA benchmark constructed by systematically combining Wikipedia images, their associated article captions, and structured knowledge from Wikidata. Our pipeline uses large language models (LLMs) to generate candidate multiple-choice image-question-answer sets. All generated instances are subsequently reviewed and curated by human annotators to ensure factual correctness, visual-text consistency, and that each question requires external knowledge in addition to visual evidence for correct resolution. WikiVQABench comprises a substantial collection of Wikipedia images with curated multiple-choice questions designed to benchmark knowledge-aware vision-language models (VLMs). Evaluation of fifteen VLMs (256M-90B parameters) reveals a wide performance range (24.7%-75.6% accuracy), demonstrating that the benchmark effectively discriminates model capabilities on knowledge-intensive reasoning. The dataset and benchmarking code are publicly available.

</details>


### [155] [One-Step Distillation of Discrete Diffusion Image Generators via Fixed-Point Iteration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21484)
*Chaoyang Wang,Yunhai Tong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A fully differentiable, one-step distillation framework for discrete diffusion models called Fixed-Point Distillation (FPD) that uses local correction targets from a teacher, lifts tokens to continuous space with a multi-bandwidth drift loss, and employs a straight-through estimator to backprop through the discrete bottleneck; optionally includes an adversarial loss; achieves competitive one-step generation and narrows the gap to multi-step teachers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the inefficiency of slow iterative decoding in discrete diffusion models and limitations of prior one-step distillation approaches (which either double compute via auxiliary score nets or rely on multi-stage pipelines).

Method: Partial corruption of the student’s one-step draft to form local correction targets; lift discrete tokens to continuous features; apply multi-bandwidth drift loss to accumulate corrections; use straight-through estimator to sample hard tokens for forward pass to teacher/decoder while backpropagating gradients to student logits; end-to-end differentiable; optional unconditional adversarial objective for realism.

Result: FPD achieves competitive visual fidelity and structural alignment in a single inference step, narrowing the gap to multi-step teacher models and outperforming existing discrete distillation baselines, validated on class- and text-conditional generation.

Conclusion: FPD offers a simple, end-to-end, fully differentiable single-step distillation framework for discrete diffusion models, with a flexible training objective (including optional adversarial term) that yields strong performance without multi-stage pipelines.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models excel at visual synthesis but rely on slow, iterative decoding. Existing single-step distillation methods attempt to bypass this bottleneck, either by training auxiliary score networks that effectively double compute, or by introducing specialized parameterizations and multi-stage pipelines that fragment optimization. In this paper, we introduce Fixed-Point Distillation (FPD), an end-to-end framework that constructs local correction targets by partially corrupting the student's one-step draft and refining it with a single teacher step. To compute the training objective in a semantically meaningful space, we lift discrete tokens into continuous features and apply a multi-bandwidth drift loss that iteratively accumulates these corrections. To backpropagate through the discrete bottleneck, we employ a straight-through estimator that feeds exact hard-sampled tokens to the teacher and decoder during the forward pass, ensuring that training and inference operate on the same codebook manifold, while routing continuous gradients back to the student logits. This fully differentiable pathway additionally accommodates an optional unconditional adversarial objective to enhance perceptual realism. Evaluations on both class- and text-conditional generation validate the effectiveness of our framework. FPD achieves competitive visual fidelity and structural alignment within a single inference step, narrowing the gap to multi-step teachers while outperforming existing discrete distillation baselines.

</details>


### [156] [Uni-Edit: Intelligent Editing Is A General Task For Unified Model Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21487)
*Dian Zheng,Manyuan Zhang,Hongyu Li,Hongbo Liu,Kai Zou,Kaituo Feng,Hongsheng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Uni-Edit proposes a single-task approach to tune Unified Multimodal Models using automated image-editing data, achieving simultaneous gains in understanding, generation, and editing capabilities with one dataset and training stage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mixed multi-task training for UMMs causes task conflicts and requires complex pipelines/data balancing, limiting mutual reinforcement. A single general task could unlock joint benefits.

Method: Develop Uni-Edit via an automated data-synthesis pipeline that converts diverse VQA data into complex, nested-editing instructions; produce Uni-Edit-148k; tune UMMs on this data in one stage, without auxiliary operations.

Result: Tuning solely with Uni-Edit yields comprehensive improvements across all three abilities on BAGEL and Janus-Pro, without extra data or stages.

Conclusion: Uni-Edit serves as a general, scalable task for UMM tuning, enabling cross-task reinforcement and simplifying the training paradigm.

Abstract: Currently, enhancing Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) with image understanding, generation, and editing capabilities mainly relies on mixed multi-task training. Due to inherent task conflicts, such strategy requires complex multi-stage pipelines, massive data mixing, and balancing tricks, merely resulting in a performance trade-off rather than true mutual reinforcement. To break this paradigm, we propose Uni-Edit, an intelligent image editing task that serves as the first general task for UMM tuning. Unlike complex mixed pipelines, Uni-Edit improves performance across all three abilities at once using only one task, one training stage, and one dataset. Specifically, we first identify image editing as an inherently ideal general task, as it naturally demands both visual understanding and generation. However, existing editing data relies on simplistic instructions that severely underutilize a model's understanding capacity. To address this, we introduce the first automated and scalable data synthesis pipeline for intelligent editing, transforming diverse VQA data into complex and effective editing instructions with embedded questions and nested logic. This yields Uni-Edit-148k, pairing diverse reasoning-intensive instructions with high-quality edited images. Extensive experiments on BAGEL and Janus-Pro demonstrate that tuning solely on Uni-Edit achieves comprehensive enhancements across all three capabilities without any auxiliary operations.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [157] [GraphDiffMed: Knowledge-Constrained Differential Attention with Pharmacological Graph Priors for Medication Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20188)
*Krati Saxena,Tomohiro Shibata*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphDiffMed combines dual-scale differential attention (intra- and inter-visit) with pharmacological constraints to robustly recommend safe medication combinations from noisy, long EHR trajectories, outperforming baselines on MIMIC-III with favorable safety metrics; the best configuration relies only on demographic features in the reported setup and the code is open-sourced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical AI in medication recommendation is hindered by long, noisy, and heterogeneous patient trajectories. There is a need to jointly capture temporal dynamics and pharmacological knowledge (e.g., DDIs) while suppressing noise to ensure safety and clinical relevance.

Method: A knowledge-constrained recommendation framework (GraphDiffMed) built on dual-scale Differential Attention v2, applying attention within each visit and across visits to filter noise, with pharmacological constraints integrated during learning. Evaluations include experiments on MIMIC-III and ablation studies; open-source release.

Result: The approach consistently improves recommendation quality and ranking over strong baselines and achieves a more favorable safety-performance balance. Ablations show the strongest configuration uses only demographic auxiliary features in their setting.

Conclusion: Integrating noise-aware attention with pharmacological constraints yields more reliable and clinically meaningful medication recommendations, demonstrating the value of combining temporal noise suppression and domain knowledge in EHR-based drug recommendation.

Abstract: Recommending safe and effective medication combinations from electronic health records (EHRs) is a core clinical AI problem, yet it remains difficult because patient trajectories are long, noisy, and clinically heterogeneous. Existing methods typically excel at either temporal modeling across visits or pharmacological knowledge integration (e.g., drug-drug interactions, DDIs), but rarely achieve both while robustly suppressing noise. We present GraphDiffMed, a knowledge-constrained medication recommendation framework built on dual-scale Differential Attention v2. Differential attention is applied at both intra-visit and inter-visit levels to filter spurious signals within encounters and across longitudinal history, while pharmacological constraints are incorporated during learning. Experiments on MIMIC-III and ablation studies show that this design consistently improves recommendation quality and ranking over strong baselines while achieving a more favorable safety performance balance. We further find that the strongest-performing configuration uses only demographic auxiliary features under our experimental setting. Overall, GraphDiffMed demonstrates that combining noise-aware attention with pharmacological constraints yields more reliable and clinically meaningful medication recommendation. We open-source our code at https://github.com/saxenakrati09/GraphDiffMed.

</details>


### [158] [TabPFN-MT: A Natively Multitask In-Context Learner for Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20234)
*Cormac Cureton,Narges Armanfard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TabPFN-MT enables efficient multitask learning for tabular data by expanding the y-encoder and using a shared decoder, achieving state-of-the-art multitask accuracy on 344 small-to-medium datasets with constant-time inference per task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PFNs perform well in single-task tabular prediction but do not share information across targets. There is a need for multitask in-context learning that captures inter-task dependencies within a context, especially for small-to-medium datasets where gradient-based training is less practical.

Method: Train on an expanded multi-target synthetic prior; use an expanded y-encoder and a shared decoder head to enable multitask in-context learning; rely on in-context learning rather than traditional gradient-based training; operate effectively in small-to-medium data regimes (fewer than ~1,000 samples).

Result: On 344 datasets, TabPFN-MT achieves state-of-the-art performance for deep tabular multitask learning. It attains an overall multitask Accuracy rank of 4.89 (best average rank). It remains competitive with top single-task ensembles despite joint optimization, and reduces inference from O(T) to O(1) forward passes for T tasks, offering major computational efficiency.

Conclusion: TabPFN-MT delivers strong multitask tabular learning with significant inference efficiency, making it well-suited for multi-target tabular applications in small-to-medium data regimes.

Abstract: Prior-Data Fitted networks (PFNs) have been very successful in tabular contexts, handling prediction tasks in context. However, they are designed for single-task inference, meaning that predicting several target values within a context requires repeated forward calls and precludes inter-task information sharing. We propose TabPFN-MT, which is trained on an expanded multi-target synthetic prior to capture inter-task dependencies in context. This model uses an expanded $y$-encoder and a shared decoder head to enable multitask in-context learning and simultaneous inference. The model is uniquely specialized for small-to-medium datasets by relying on in-context learning rather than traditional gradient-based training. Within this regime (averaging fewer than 1,000 samples), extensive evaluations across 344 datasets demonstrate that TabPFN-MT establishes a new state-of-the-art for deep tabular multitask learning. Furthermore, despite the inherent compute asymmetry of joint optimization, our model remains highly competitive with the latest state-of-the-art single-task ensembles. Notably, on multitask datasets it achieves an overall Accuracy rank of 4.89, the highest average rank among all models tested. Crucially, TabPFN-MT delivers this highly competitive performance while reducing the inference cost for $T$ tasks from $O(T)$ to $O(1)$ forward passes, offering a massive computational efficiency improvement for multi-target tabular applications.

</details>


### [159] [Provably Learning Diffusion Models under the Manifold Hypothesis: Collapse and Refine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20235)
*Wei Huang,Andi Han,Mingyuan Bai,Huanjian Zhou,Qixin Zhang,Taiji Suzuki,Kenji Fukumizu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SiLD shows that diffusion score learning on data on low-dimensional manifolds collapses to the manifold and then refines the intrinsic density, via a two-stage latent diffusion learned by a single score-matching objective. Theoretical sample complexity scales with intrinsic dimension; empirically competitive with or better than VAE-based LDMs, with improved reconstructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why diffusion models can efficiently learn scores despite high ambient dimensionality when data lie on a low-dimensional manifold, and move beyond KL-based latent regularization by using a score-based objective.

Method: Propose a collapse-and-refine mechanism and a two-stage Score-induced Latent Diffusion (SiLD) framework. Both manifold learning and density estimation arise from a single denoising score matching objective, with theoretical analysis showing ambient-dimensional blow-up is avoided; empirical evaluation on stacked MNIST, CelebA variants, and molecular generation benchmarks.

Result: Theoretical result: sample complexity depends on intrinsic dimension, not ambient. Empirical result: SiLD matches or outperforms VAE-based LDMs in generation quality and consistently improves reconstruction.

Conclusion: Provides a principled, efficient latent diffusion approach by leveraging score-based learning on manifolds; achieves competitive generation and superior reconstruction with a KL-regularization-free objective.

Abstract: Diffusion models generate high-dimensional data with remarkable quality, yet how their training efficiently learns the score function, bypassing the curse of dimensionality when data is supported on low-dimensional manifolds, remains theoretically unexplained. We identify a collapse-and-refine mechanism driven by the geometry of the score function itself: at small noise scales, the diverging singularity of the score drives a rapid dimensional collapse of the induced denoising map onto the data manifold projection; at moderate noise scales, training refines the intrinsic density on the learned manifold. We instantiate this principle as Score-induced Latent Diffusion (SiLD), a two-stage framework in which both manifold learning and density estimation emerge from a single denoising score matching objective, replacing the heuristic KL regularization of VAE-based latent diffusion models. We prove that the resulting sample complexity depends on the intrinsic dimension rather than the ambient dimension. Experiments on Stacked MNIST, CelebA variants, and molecular generation benchmarks show that SiLD matches or outperforms VAE-based LDMs in generation quality and consistently improves reconstruction, validating our theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [160] [MagBridge-Battery: A Synthetic Bridge Dataset for Li-ion Magnetometry and State-of-Health Diagnostics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20240)
*Sakthi Prabhu Gunasekar,Prasanna Kumar Rangarajan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Synthetic MagBridge-Battery v1.0 dataset linking real magnetic morphology with SOH labels for battery health diagnostics; 6,760 samples; benchmark tasks: SOH regression, second-life classification, anomaly detection; ablation confirms meaningful encoding; released on Zenodo (CC-BY-4.0) with code (Apache-2.0).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address lack of public magnetic-field datasets with degradation labels; demonstrate utility of magnetic sensing beyond terminal measurements; enable benchmarking of ML models for battery health from magnetic signals.

Method: Combine Mohammadi-Jerschow magnetic morphology (real) with PulseBat SOH labels to create 5,600 conditioned samples, 600 synthetic anomaly samples, 560 Regime-B extrapolation samples; ensure disjoint cells; define three primary tasks plus auxiliary; perform label-shuffle ablation.

Result: Creates a public dataset and benchmark suite; shows non-artifactual encoding (R^2 ~ 0.77 drop to ~0 with shuffle); release details; ready for evaluation.

Conclusion: Fills gap in public datasets; enables magnetic-sensing battery diagnostics research; still scarcity of paired measurements remains.

Abstract: Battery health diagnostics today rely overwhelmingly on electrochemical signals measured at the cell terminals. A parallel literature has shown that magnetic sensing can resolve information that terminal-only measurements miss, but method development is limited by the absence, to the best of our knowledge, of public battery magnetic-measurement datasets paired with degradation labels. We release MagBridge-Battery v1.0, a synthetic dataset of 6,760 magnetic-field signatures that bridges real magnetic morphology from the Mohammadi-Jerschow Open Science Framework (OSF) archive with state-of-health (SOH) labels from the PulseBat dataset. The release contains 5,600 PulseBat-conditioned grounded samples, 600 synthetic sensor-anomaly samples derived from clean parents, and 560 low-voltage Regime-B extrapolation samples. A cell-disjoint, parent-child-leakage-free primary benchmark split is verified to contain zero overlapping cells, zero cross-split parent-child pairs, and zero sample-ID overlap. We define three primary benchmark tasks: SOH regression, second-life classification, and anomaly detection, plus an auxiliary anomaly-subtype classification task. A controlled label-shuffle ablation collapses SOH regression from R^2 approximately 0.77 to approximately 0, confirming that the bridge encodes input SOH non-trivially rather than producing label-aligned artifacts. The dataset is released on Zenodo under CC-BY-4.0, and the bridge code and benchmark suite are released under Apache-2.0. This work provides a public benchmark for magnetic-sensing battery diagnostics while paired magnetic-electrochemical measurements remain scarce.

</details>


### [161] [Geometry-Lite: Interpretable Safety Probing via Layer-Wise Margin Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20241)
*Woo Seob Sim,Yu Rang Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geometry-Lite is a compact, multi-layer probe that maps each layer's final prompt-token representation to margins via centroid, local-neighborhood, and supervised linear-boundary readers; it shows that safety evidence is dominated by persistent boundary-position margins across layers, not layer-to-layer motion, with limited gains from drift or structural summaries, but some benefits under distribution shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the geometry of safety evidence across model layers beyond aggregate AUROC, including how evidence forms, which geometric patterns support low false-positive rates, and how these patterns hold up under benchmark shift.

Method: Introduce Geometry-Lite: map each layer's final prompt-token representation to signed margins using centroid, local-neighborhood, and supervised linear-boundary readouts; summarize margins by boundary position, layer-to-layer change, and coarse shape; evaluate on nine instruction-tuned backbones (1.2B–70B) across seven safety benchmarks; compare against single-layer probes and multi-layer score stacking; analyze drift and shift behavior.

Result: Safety evidence is expressed primarily through persistent boundary-position geometry: final or extremal margins and unsafe-side layer occupancy dominate aggregated detection performance; finite-difference drift and structural summaries contribute little to pooled AUROC, though drift yields small recall gains under shifted low-FPR thresholds; under benchmark shift, optimized linear boundaries are sharp on the training mixture whereas class-conditional mean geometry remains separative on a predefined hard held-out subset.

Conclusion: Prompt-level safety evidence is not mainly a layer-to-layer motion signal, but a persistent layer-wise margin geometry whose informative components and readout biases become visible in decision-critical regimes; Geometry-Lite provides a practical tool for dissecting multi-layer safety signals across models and benchmarks.

Abstract: Prompt-level safety probes for large language models use hidden-state representations to separate safe from unsafe prompts, but strong average detection performance does not explain the geometry of this separation. In particular, it remains unclear how safety evidence is formed across layers, which aspects of that layer-wise geometry support low-false-positive decisions, and which geometric biases remain stable under benchmark shift. We study this as an empirical decomposition problem and introduce Geometry-Lite, a compact prompt-level probe that maps each layer's final prompt-token representation to signed margins under centroid, local-neighborhood, and supervised linear-boundary readouts, then summarizes the resulting margin profiles by boundary position, layer-to-layer change, and coarse shape. Across nine instruction-tuned backbones ($1.2$B--$70$B) and seven safety benchmarks, Geometry-Lite improves over single-layer probes while remaining close to raw multi-layer score stacking, making it a useful instrument for analyzing the multi-layer safety signal. The decomposition shows that safety evidence is expressed primarily through persistent boundary-position geometry: final or extremal margins and unsafe-side layer occupancy dominate aggregate detection performance. In contrast, finite-difference drift and structural summaries add little to pooled AUROC, although drift can provide small recall-oriented corrections under shifted low-FPR thresholds. Under benchmark shift, optimized linear boundaries are sharp on the training mixture, whereas class-conditional mean geometry retains separation more reliably on a predefined hard held-out subset. Overall, prompt-level safety evidence is not primarily a layer-to-layer motion signal, but a persistent layer-wise margin geometry whose useful components and readout-level biases become visible in decision-critical regimes.

</details>


### [162] [LEAP: A closed-loop framework for perovskite precursor additive discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20242)
*Xin-De Wang,Zhi-Rui Chen,Ze-Feng Gao,Peng-Jie Guo,Cheng Mu,Zhong-Yi Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Domain-specialized LLM-driven active-learning framework (LEAP) for perovskite additive prioritization, combining mechanism-grounded descriptors with Bayesian optimization and expert review, yielding improved PCEs in experimental validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient discovery of precursor additives is hampered by enormous chemical space; need data-efficient, mechanism-aware screening to boost perovskite solar cell performance.

Method: Domain-specialized LLM extracts mechanism-relevant knowledge from literature and encodes candidates with interpretable descriptors; these descriptors feed a Bayesian optimization loop with uncertainty under low-data; expert-in-the-loop feasibility review guides selections.

Result: Domain-specialized model outperforms general models in mechanism-consistent reasoning; in iterative screening across three rounds, later-round devices achieved average PCEs of 20.13% (6-CDQ) and 20.87% (2-CNA) vs 19.25% control; champion PCE 21.32%.

Conclusion: Literature-grounded mechanistic descriptors, when integrated with Bayesian optimization and expert feasibility review, can enable mechanism-aware additive prioritization in perovskite photovoltaics and potentially accelerate discovery in the precursor-additive space.

Abstract: Efficient discovery of precursor additives is essential for improving the performance of perovskite solar cells, yet the large chemical space makes conventional trial-and-error screening inefficient. We develop LEAP(LLM-driven Exploration via Active Learning for Perovskites), an expert-in-the-loop closed framework that couples a domain-specialized large language model(LLM) with active learning for iterative additive prioritization. The LLM is trained to extract mechanism-relevant knowledge from the perovskite additive literature and to represent candidate molecules through interpretable descriptors, which are further integrated into a Bayesian optimization workflow for uncertainty-aware prioritization under low-data conditions. Benchmark results on unseen literature show that the domain-specialized model outperforms general-purpose models in mechanism-consistent reasoning. Experimental validation in an expert-in-the-loop proof-of-concept study suggests improved additive prioritization across three screening rounds, leading to average device PCEs of 20.13% and 20.87% for the later-round 6-CDQ- and 2-CNA-treated devices, respectively, compared with 19.25% for the control, with a champion PCE of 21.32%. These results provide preliminary evidence that literature-grounded mechanistic descriptors, when coupled with Bayesian optimization and expert feasibility review, can support mechanism-aware additive prioritization in perovskite photovoltaics.

</details>


### [163] [GROW: Aligning GRPO with State-Action Modeling for Open-World VLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20246)
*Xiongbin Wu,Zhihao Luo,Shanzhe Lei,Lechao Zhang,Xuhong Wang,Jie Yang,Zhonglong Zheng,Yuanjie Zheng,Xin Tan,Wei Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces GROW, a sample-based GRPO framework for open-world VLM agents; decomposes trajectories into state-action samples to compute advantages, enabling effective multi-turn RL; achieves SOTA on 800+ Minecraft tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO requires full trajectories which causes excessive context length and noise in long-horizon, open-world VLM tasks; reliance on supervised fine-tuning limits RL effectiveness.

Method: Decompose collected trajectories into state-action samples and compute inter-sample advantages rather than treating an entire trajectory as one entity. Provide surrogate analysis showing preserved GRPO signal under simplifying assumptions despite differing local states. Empirically validate on 800+ Minecraft tasks.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on more than 800 Minecraft tasks, demonstrating the effectiveness of the GROW RL framework for open-world VLM agents.

Conclusion: GROW offers a scalable RL framework that enables effective GRPO-based training for open-world vision-language agents, addressing long-horizon, multi-turn tasks and outperforming SFT baselines.

Abstract: Recently, vision-language model (VLM) agents have shown promising progress in open-world tasks, where successful task completion often requires multiple turns of visual perception and action execution. However, existing methods still rely primarily on Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with expert demonstrations, while the advanced reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm, specifically Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), has not been effectively employed for multi-turn RL in these tasks because standard GRPO requires full trajectories as training samples which leads to excessively long context and noise. To address this issue, we propose GROW, a RL framework for open-world VLM agents that decomposes collected trajectories into state-action samples, and computes advantages between these samples rather than treating a full trajectory as a single entity. We further provide a surrogate analysis indicating that, even though the grouped samples are conditioned on different local states rather than an identical prompt context, the objective can preserve the core relative policy optimization signal of GRPO under simplifying assumptions. Experiments on more than 800 Minecraft tasks show that our method achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of our proposed RL framework for open-world VLM agents.

</details>


### [164] [CP-MoE: Consistency-Preserving Mixture-of-Experts for Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20247)
*Yang Liu,Toan Nguyen,Flora D. Salim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CP-MoE introduces a transient expert into Mixture-of-Experts continual learning to preserve previously learned knowledge while enabling cross-task transfer. It uses a consistency-preserving routing bias and transient-guided regularisation to reduce forgetting and maintain performance across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Catastrophic forgetting is a core challenge for continual learning with MoE-based LLMs/VLMs. Existing LoRA-based MoE methods force a trade-off between isolating experts (limiting transfer) and updating shared parameters (causing forgetting). A mechanism that preserves historical knowledge while allowing early task-specific updates to influence stable experts is needed.

Method: CP-MoE adds a transient expert that captures early task-specific updates and informs routing and regularisation. The consistency-preserving routing bias uses the transient expert to estimate representation similarity with stable experts, steering routing toward more compatible experts. A transient expert-guided regularisation protects important historical parameters during merging.

Result: Empirical validation on unimodal and multimodal continual learning benchmarks shows strong performance. On SuperNI, CP-MoE achieves state-of-the-art results and improved zero-shot transfer to unseen tasks. On VQA v2, it scales to multimodal reasoning, consistently reduces forgetting, and outperforms strong MoE baselines.

Conclusion: CP-MoE reduces parameter interference and forgetting while preserving cross-task knowledge transfer, enabling robust continual learning for both LLM-based and VLM-based MoE systems.

Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting remains a major obstacle to continual learning in large language models (LLMs) and vision--language models (VLMs). Although Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures offer an efficient path to scaling, existing LoRA-based MoE continual learning methods still face a fundamental trade-off: they either isolate experts too aggressively, limiting knowledge transfer across tasks, or allow task-specific updates to overwrite important existing parameters, leading to severe forgetting. To address this, we propose CP-MoE, a continual learning framework built around a transient expert that captures early task-specific updates and guides their integration into stable experts. CP-MoE introduces a consistency-preserving routing bias, which uses the transient expert to estimate representation similarity with stable experts and steer routing towards more compatible expert selection, and a transient expert-guided regularisation mechanism, which selectively protects important historical parameters during merging. Together, these components reduce parameter interference and forgetting while preserving cross-task knowledge transfer. We validate CP-MoE on both unimodal and multimodal continual learning benchmarks with LLM-based and VLM-based MoE models. On SuperNI benchmark, spanning diverse sequential language tasks, CP-MoE achieves state-of-the-art performance and stronger zero-shot transfer to unseen tasks. On VQA v2 dataset, it scales effectively to multimodal visual reasoning, consistently reduces forgetting, and outperforms strong MoE baselines.

</details>


### [165] [Graph Transductive Sharpening: Leveraging Unlabeled Predictions in Node Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20248)
*Brown Zaz,Mar Gonzàlez I Català,Ferran Hernandez Caralt,Moshe Eliasof,Pietro Liò*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Transductive Sharpening (TS), a loss-level regularizer that leverages unlabeled-node predictions in transductive GNNs by minimizing entropy on unlabeled nodes while balancing with labeled nodes, yielding performance gains without architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In transductive semi-supervised graph learning, predictions for unlabeled nodes during training carry useful signal, but standard supervised losses discard them. Exploiting this signal through a principled objective could improve accuracy.

Method: Introduce TS: modify the training loss by exploiting the decomposition of cross-entropy into a label-dependent alignment term and a label-independent entropy term. Minimize entropy on unlabeled nodes (to sharpen predictions) while counterbalancing this on labeled nodes. This loss-level adjustment requires no changes to the backbone architecture.

Result: Across a wide range of node-classification benchmarks, TS yields consistent performance improvements without modifying the underlying GNN architecture. Code is released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: Prediction-level regularization via entropy minimization on unlabeled nodes can provide reliable gains in transductive semi-supervised node classification while remaining architecture-agnostic.

Abstract: In the transductive setting, where the full graph is observed but node labels are only partially available, progress in semi-supervised node classification has largely focused on architectural innovation. In this paper, we revisit an orthogonal axis: the training objective. We start from a simple observation: transductive models produce predictions for every node during training, including nodes without labels. These unlabeled-node predictions may contain useful training signal, but standard supervised objectives discard them because no ground-truth labels are available. Inspired by the decomposition of cross-entropy into a label-dependent alignment term and a label-independent entropy term, we propose prediction confidence as a natural way to extract this signal in the absence of labels. This motivates Transductive Sharpening (TS): a loss-level modification that minimizes prediction entropy on unlabeled nodes while counterbalancing this effect on labeled nodes. We evaluate Transductive Sharpening across a wide range of node-classification benchmarks and observe consistent performance improvements without requiring any changes to the backbone architecture. Code is available at https://github.com/transductive-sharpening/tunedGNN.

</details>


### [166] [Automated Kernel Discovery Towards Understanding High-dimensional Bayesian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20249)
*Taeyoung Yun,Woocheol Shin,Inhyuck Song,Jaewoo Lee,Jinkyoo Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Kernel Discovery, an LLM-driven evolutionary framework for high-dimensional Bayesian optimization that expands the GP kernel space beyond additive/multiplicative rules, using a two-stage LLM pipeline to generate novel kernel forms and executeable code, with LOO-CRPS for selection; achieves top performance (avg rank 1.2/17) on five benchmarks and analyzes kernel types responsible for gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Kernel design is the bottleneck in high-dimensional Bayesian optimization. Existing automated methods are constrained by limited kernel composition rules and by approaches that rely on conditioning on raw observations, which is problematic given context-length limits and noisy patterns. There is a need for scalable, automated kernel discovery that can explore diverse forms without depending on raw data.

Method: Kernel Discovery employs a two-stage LLM framework. Stage 1: the LLM proposes novel mathematical kernel forms beyond predefined compositions. Stage 2: a second LLM converts each proposed form into validated, executable kernel code. The approach operates as an evolutionary framework to explore a broad kernel space and crucially does not condition on raw observations. Kernel selection uses Leave-One-Out Continuous Ranked Probability Score (LOO-CRPS) to penalize overfitted kernels.

Result: On five high-dimensional BO benchmarks, the method achieves an average rank of 1.2 out of 17, outperforming competitive baselines. The paper also analyzes the discovered kernels to identify which kernel forms contribute to improvements in high-dimensional BO.

Conclusion: Kernel Discovery broadens the search space for GP kernels in high-dimensional BO, enabling automatic discovery of effective kernel forms without relying on fixed composition rules or raw-data conditioning. The two-stage LLM pipeline, combined with LOO-CRPS, yields strong empirical performance and provides insights into which kernels drive gains.

Abstract: Gaussian Process (GP) kernels are central to Bayesian optimization (BO), yet designing effective kernels for high-dimensional problems still relies on extensive manual engineering. Existing automated approaches struggle in high dimensions for two bottlenecks: their kernel search space is limited to additions and multiplications of base kernels, and LLM-based approaches require conditioning on raw observations, which becomes infeasible due to context-length limits and the difficulty of extracting meaningful patterns. We introduce \textbf{Kernel Discovery}, a LLM-driven evolutionary framework for high-dimensional BO that searches a broader kernel space beyond predefined composition rules and does not require conditioning on observations. Motivated by the observation that directly prompting an LLM to generate kernel code yields syntactically varied but functionally identical kernels, we adopt a two-stage approach: an LLM first proposes novel mathematical forms, then a second LLM call converts each form into validated, executable code. We also propose a leave-one-out continuous ranked probability score (LOO-CRPS) as a selection criterion that penalizes overfitted kernels. On five high-dimensional BO benchmarks, our method achieves an average rank of \textbf{1.2 out of 17}, outperforming competitive baselines.
  We further analyze the discovered kernels to identify which kernels lead to improvements in high-dimensional BO.

</details>


### [167] [Physics-informed convolutional neural networks for fluid flow through porous media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20250)
*Rafał Topolnicki,Paweł Dłotko,Maciej Matyka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A CNN-based framework predicts pore-scale velocity fields directly from geometry, using an encoder–decoder with skip connections and physics-informed losses to ensure incompressibility, boundary conditions, and tortuosity consistency; it generalizes to unseen geometries and accelerates Lattice-Boltzmann solver convergence. 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate pore-scale flow simulation is computationally expensive due to complex pore geometries and Navier–Stokes dynamics, especially when many realizations are needed. A fast, data-driven surrogate can reduce wall-clock time for repeated simulations while preserving essential physical constraints.

Method: A convolutional encoder–decoder with skip connections predicts velocity fields from geometry. The loss combines velocity reconstruction with incompressibility, no-flow inside solids, periodicity, and agreement with the global tortuosity index. Several CNN backbones are evaluated to identify robust architectures. Generalization is tested on out-of-distribution samples with changes in obstacle geometry, boundary conditions, porosity, and realistic porous structures. The predicted fields are used as warm-starts for Lattice-Boltzmann simulations to accelerate convergence.

Result: The surrogate yields accurate velocity predictions consistent with the physical constraints and generalizes to unseen geometries and boundary specifications. Using the predicted velocity fields as initial conditions for LB simulations substantially accelerates convergence, reducing the number of iterations in over 90% of tested cases.

Conclusion: A physics-informed, data-driven surrogate can efficiently predict pore-scale velocity fields from geometry, providing a practical warm-start for LB solvers and enabling faster parametric studies of porous-media flows.

Abstract: Accurate simulation of fluid flow in porous media is challenging due to complex pore-space geometries and the computational cost of solving the Navier-Stokes equations. This difficulty is particularly important when repeated simulations are required, as standard numerical solvers may converge slowly in intricate porous domains. We present a neural-network-based framework for predicting pore-scale velocity fields directly from sample geometry. The method uses a convolutional encoder-decoder architecture with skip connections to preserve spatial detail while extracting multi-scale features. Physical consistency is encouraged through a custom loss function combining velocity reconstruction with incompressibility, no-flow conditions inside solids, periodicity constraints, and agreement with the global tortuosity index. We analyze the influence of the corresponding loss weights and quantify the contribution of individual loss components to prediction accuracy. Several CNN backbones are evaluated to identify architectures providing accurate and robust predictions. The generalization ability of the trained model is tested on samples outside the training distribution, including changes in obstacle geometry, boundary conditions, porosity, and realistic porous structures. Finally, we demonstrate a practical use of the predicted velocity fields as initial conditions for Lattice-Boltzmann simulations. This warm-start strategy accelerates solver convergence, reducing the number of iterations in over 90% of tested cases.

</details>


### [168] [Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Safe Autonomous Driving Under Pedestrian Behavioral Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20255)
*Prakash Aryan,Kaushik Raghupathruni,Timo Kehrer,Sebastiano Panichella*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A MARL framework (MAPPO) co-trains an autonomous vehicle and 12 pedestrians, incorporating latent, per-pedestrian jaywalking traits to produce more realistic interactions than fixed pedestrian policies; results show improved goal achievement but higher collision risk near jaywalkers, with a measurable behavior gap in trajectories; co-training reduces collisions versus single-agent RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance realism and safety evaluation of self-driving cars by modeling heterogeneous human crossing behavior, including latent traits that influence jaywalking, which fixed policies fail to capture.

Method: A MARL environment in which an SDC and 12 pedestrians are trained with MAPPO. Pedestrians follow scripted Dijkstra paths for movement; SDC makes high-level go/wait decisions via RL. Jaywalking probability is assigned per-pedestrian from a latent personality trait at episode start (hidden from the SDC). Evaluated over 500 episodes; includes trajectory-based measures such as speed near jaywalkers.

Result: Co-trained SDC reaches 78% of goals with 14% collisions; best rule-based baseline yields 35% goals and 33% collisions. The SDC travels 2.65 m/s faster near jaywalkers at close range (0-3 m). Jaywalking accounts for 13% of crossings but 62% of collisions. MARL co-training reduces collisions by 30% relative to single-agent RL, as pedestrians learn to wait when the SDC approaches at speed.

Conclusion: Joint MARL training with latent pedestrian traits yields more realistic interactions and can reduce collisions compared with single-agent RL, but jaywalking remains a critical risk when unobserved by the SDC. The study demonstrates a measurable interaction gap via trajectory data and highlights the value of MARL in safety evaluation over fixed pedestrian policies.

Abstract: Simulation-based testing of self-driving cars (SDCs) typically relies on scripted or simplified pedestrian models that do not capture the heterogeneity and uncertainty of real human crossing behavior. This limits the realism of safety assessments, especially in scenarios involving jaywalking, which is governed by latent personality traits that the vehicle cannot observe. We hypothesize that jointly training pedestrians and the SDC with multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) produces more realistic interaction scenarios than training the SDC against fixed pedestrian policies, and that the resulting behavior gap between predictable and unpredictable crossings can be measured directly from trajectories. This paper describes a MARL environment in which an SDC and 12 pedestrians are co-trained using Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO). Pedestrian locomotion follows scripted Dijkstra pathfinding, while an RL policy controls high-level go/wait decisions. Jaywalking probability depends on a per-pedestrian personality trait sampled at episode start and hidden from the SDC. In 500-episode evaluations, the co-trained SDC reached 78% of goals with a 14% collision rate, compared to 35% goals and 33% collisions for the best rule-based baseline. A speed differential metric shows that the SDC traveled 2.65 m/s faster near jaywalkers than near crosswalk users at close range (0-3 m), indicating that jaywalking encounters were not anticipated. Jaywalking accounted for 13% of crossing events but was associated with 62% of collisions. Co-training with MARL pedestrians reduced collisions by 30% relative to single-agent RL, as pedestrians learned to wait when the SDC approached at speed.

</details>


### [169] [FBOS-RL: Feedback-Driven Bi-Objective Synergistic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20256)
*Xikai Zhang,Yongzhi Li,Likang Xiao,Yingze Zhang,Yanhua Cheng,Quan Chen,Peng Jiang,Wenjun Wu,Liu Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FBOS-RL introduces Feedback-Guided Exploration Enhancement and two mutually reinforcing objectives, EPA and ECC, to address gradient stagnation in GRPO by leveraging environment feedback; it yields faster learning and a higher performance ceiling with higher policy entropy and smoother optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO's single-origin rollout sampling can fail to provide informative gradients when tasks exceed the model's current capability, leading to training stagnation. A feedback-driven, bi-objective framework aims to utilize environment cues to guide exploration and exploitative alignment, improving learning efficiency and ultimate performance.

Method: FBOS-RL adds Feedback-Guided Exploration Enhancement based on environment feedback and defines two training objectives: Exploitation-oriented Policy Alignment (EPA) and Exploration-oriented Capability Cultivation (ECC). These objectives are designed to reinforce each other, creating a positive feedback loop (flywheel) that enhances both exploration and policy alignment.

Result: Empirical results show EPA and ECC mutually reinforce, yielding a faster learning trajectory than GRPO and feedback baselines under the same rollout budget, a higher performance ceiling, and training characteristics such as higher policy entropy and lower gradient norms.

Conclusion: The proposed feedback-driven bi-objective framework mitigates gradient stagnation in RL for large-scale model alignment, achieving more efficient training and greater ultimate capability by leveraging environment feedback through EPA–ECC synergy.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has become a cornerstone for aligning and unlocking the reasoning capabilities of large-scale models. At its core, the training loop of GRPO and its variants alternates between rollout sampling and policy update. Unlike supervised learning, where each gradient step is anchored to an explicit ground-truth target, the optimal gradient direction for updating model parameters in this setting is not known a priori; the high-quality rollouts drawn during the sampling stage therefore act as the implicit "teacher" that guides every parameter update. However, GRPO adopt a simple sampling scheme that conditions all rollouts on the same original prompt. When a task lies beyond the policy model's current capability, this sampling scheme rarely yields a high-quality rollout, leaving the policy model without a meaningful gradient direction when updating its parameters, which causes training to stall. To address this issue, we propose FBOS-RL, a Feedback-Driven Bi-Objective Synergistic reinforcement learning framework. Specifically, we let the model perform Feedback-Guided Exploration Enhancement based on the feedback provided by the environment, and on top of this we design two mutually reinforcing training objectives: Exploitation-oriented Policy Alignment(EPA) and Exploration-oriented Capability Cultivation(ECC). Extensive experiments demonstrate that EPA and ECC can mutually reinforce each other, forming a positive flywheel effect that significantly improves both the training efficiency and the final performance ceiling of reinforcement learning. Specifically, under an identical number of rollouts, FBOS-RL learns substantially faster than GRPO and feedback-based baselines and ultimately attains a higher performance ceiling, while exhibiting higher policy entropy and lower gradient norms throughout training.

</details>


### [170] [Instance Discrimination for Link Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20257)
*Valentin Cuzin-Rambaud,Mathieu Lefort,Rémy Cazabet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adapts instance-discrimination SSL to graph link prediction, introducing link-based models L-GRACE and L-BGRL with community-structure augmentation; achieves competitive results, notably on unattributed graphs, in both supervised and self-supervised settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend the success of instance-discrimination SSL from nodes to links in graphs, and to understand how augmentation affects link-prediction performance.

Method: Perform a rigorous evaluation of existing self-supervised models for link prediction; introduce a community-structure based augmentation; propose two link-representation models, L-GRACE and L-BGRL, outperforming baselines on unattributed graphs and matching SOTA in both supervised and self-supervised contexts.

Result: Existing SSL models' success is highly dependent on augmentation; L-GRACE and L-BGRL yield improved link representations, especially for unattributed graphs, achieving parity with state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Shifting to link representations and incorporating community-structure augmentations yields strong performance for link prediction, extending SSL techniques to new graph tasks and data regimes.

Abstract: Recently, instance discrimination models have emerged as a major solution for self-supervised learning. Having already demonstrated its effectiveness in the image domain, instance discrimination learning is now proving equally convincing in the graph domain, in particular for node classification. However, fewer contributions have tackled the link prediction task. In this contribution, we propose to adapt existing methods to this context. We first provide a rigorous evaluation of existing self-supervised models in the field of link prediction, showing that the main performance depends on the augmentation process (like in computer vision). We then propose a new structural augmentation based on the community structure that is relevant for link prediction. Our main contribution introduces two new models, L-GRACE and L-BGRL, based on link representations instead of node representations, which improve the performance of the existing methods, especially on unattributed graphs, and we show that they perform on par with the state of the art, both in supervised and self-supervised contexts.

</details>


### [171] [It Takes Two: Complementary Self-Distillation for Contextual Integrity in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20258)
*Sangwoo Park,Woongyeong Yeo,Seanie Lee,Yumin Choi,Hyomin Lee,Kangsan Kim,Jinheon Baek,Seong Joon Oh,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SELFCI offers a two-pronged self-distillation approach to CI alignment in LLMs: decoupling task utility from privacy disclosure via two reverse KL divergences from different teacher distributions, producing a Product-of-Experts target that balances capability and privacy. It outperforms online RL baselines and generalizes to out-of-domain agentic workflows without external supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy-utility trade-offs are critical when LLMs act as personal agents handling sensitive workflows. Contextual Integrity requires information flows to follow domain norms, but frontier models struggle with disclosure decisions, often hurting task performance when mitigations are applied.

Method: Proposes SELFCI, a complementary self-distillation framework that independently optimizes two reverse KL divergences over teacher distributions derived from feedback. One KL promotes preserving task-relevant information (utility), the other minimizes disclosure (privacy). The combined objective yields a Product-of-Experts target representing the intersection of capability and privacy constraints.

Result: Empirical evaluations show SELFCI outperforms competitive baselines such as online reinforcement learning methods (e.g., GRPO) without requiring costly external supervision. The approach also extends to out-of-domain, agentic workflows with accumulated private context, indicating robust CI alignment potential.

Conclusion: Decoupling information suppression from task resolution via dual feedback-driven teachers and a PoE target provides a practical path toward CI-aligned LLMs, balancing privacy and utility without external supervision and with better generalization.

Abstract: Contextual Integrity (CI) defines privacy not merely as keeping information hidden, but as governing information flows according to the norms of a given context. As large language models are increasingly deployed as personal agents handling sensitive workflows, adhering to CI becomes critical. However, even frontier models remain unreliable in making disclosure decisions, and existing mitigation strategies often degrade underlying task performance. To overcome this privacy-utility trade-off, we propose SELFCI, a complementary self-distillation framework that decouples information suppression from task resolution. SELFCI jointly optimizes two independent reverse KL divergences over distinct teacher distributions derived from feedback: one encourages preserving task-relevant information for utility, while the other enforces minimal and appropriate disclosure. This complementary formulation induces a Product-of-Experts (PoE) target, aligning the policy with the intersection of capability and privacy requirements. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that SELFCI, without relying on costly external supervision, consistently outperforms competitive baselines such as online reinforcement learning algorithms (e.g., GRPO). These trends further extend to out-of-domain settings involving agentic workflows and accumulated private context, suggesting that SELFCI provides a practical path toward CI alignment.

</details>


### [172] [Residual Paving: Diagnosing the Routing Bottleneck in Selective Refusal Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20262)
*Bryce Hinkley,Peyman Najafirad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Residual Paving enables selective editing of refusal prompts in frozen instruction-tuned transformers by routing-first control (gate) and residual edit capacity (experts). It isolates route selectivity from edit capacity, achieving large reductions in edit refusals while preserving benign and harmful distributions, and showing consistent gains across six backbones with diagnostic oracle routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve three-way control over model refusals: induce desired refusals on designated edit prompts, maintain benign behavior, and preserve harmful refusals outside the edit set. The bottleneck appears to be route selectivity rather than edit capacity.

Method: Introduce Residual Paving: a routed residual editing framework with an early-layer router predicting a scalar gate and an expert mixture. When active, prompt-conditioned bottleneck residual experts apply later-layer residual updates while freezing the backbone. An oracle-routing diagnostic replaces the learned gate with the held-out label to assess the routing component in isolation. Evaluation on Gemma-3-4B-IT held-out split, plus six backbones for generalization. Includes one-direction steering controls and trajectory diagnostics to analyze movement toward edit-target continuations.

Result: On Gemma-3-4B-IT held-out, learned Residual Paving reduces edit refusal from 88.6% to 4.0%, with 95.5% benign distribution preservation and 87.3% harmful distribution preservation. One-direction steering controls are weaker on edit success, leaving edit refusal at 86.8% for Edit-target ActAdd and 78.9% for DIM-style refusal steering. Off-target harmful-keep degradation remains: harmful refusal 65.3% vs. frozen-base 81.6%. Across six backbones, oracle routing improves the keep-side diagnostic score on every reported row, median gain +12.9 percentage points. Trajectory diagnostics on two backbones suggest movement toward edit-target continuations rather than general refusal suppression.

Conclusion: Residual Paving identifies route selectivity as the main bottleneck in three-way editing and demonstrates that isolating routing from editing capacity yields strong improvements in targeted edit performance with robust preservation of benign/harmful distributions. The approach generalizes across multiple backbones and benefits from oracle-routing diagnostics, suggesting a viable path toward more reliable selective editing.

Abstract: We study selective refusal editing as a three-way control problem: induce non-refusal on designated edit prompts while preserving benign behavior and harmful refusals outside the edit set. We introduce Residual Paving, a routed residual editing method for frozen instruction-tuned transformers that separates route selectivity, whether to intervene, from residual-edit capacity, what edit to apply. An early-layer router predicts a scalar gate and expert mixture; when active, prompt-conditioned bottleneck residual experts apply later-layer residual updates while leaving the backbone unchanged. This decomposition supports an oracle-routing diagnostic where only the learned scalar gate is replaced with the held-out edit/keep label, leaving the residual editor and frozen backbone fixed. On the primary Gemma-3-4B-IT held-out split, learned Residual Paving reduces edit refusal from 88.6% to 4.0%, with 95.5% benign distribution preservation and 87.3% harmful distribution preservation. Same-protocol one-direction steering controls are much weaker on edit success, leaving edit refusal at 86.8% for Edit-target ActAdd and 78.9% for DIM-style refusal steering. The remaining failure is off-target harmful-keep degradation: harmful refusal remains below the frozen-base rate, 65.3% vs. 81.6%. Across six backbones, oracle routing improves the keep-side diagnostic score on every reported row, with median gain +12.9 pp, supporting the interpretation that learned route selectivity is the main observed bottleneck. Trajectory diagnostics on two backbones further suggest directed movement toward edit-target continuations rather than generic refusal suppression.

</details>


### [173] [Chronicle: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Joint Language and Time Series Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20268)
*Paul Quinlan,Jeremy Levasseur,Qingguo Li,Xiaodan Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chronicle: a 324M decoder-only transformer jointly trained on text and time series within a single backbone, achieving competitive unimodal results and superior multimodal forecasting without late fusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world data often come with accompanying text; current multimodal models either retrofit LM adapters post hoc or are not challenged against strong unimodal foundation models, leaving the value of joint training unproven.

Method: A 324M decoder-only transformer with shared blocks, attention, and residual stream is trained from scratch on text and time series. Most pretraining uses unimodal batches; a brief alignment stage weaves the modalities together, enabling cross-modal capability through shared parameters rather than special adapters.

Result: Chronicle matches Gemma-3-270M-PT on 19 NLU tasks; establishes a new record for frozen-embedding time series classification on 24 UCR/UEA datasets; yields multimodal forecasts on Time-MMD that outperform all supervised fusion baselines.

Conclusion: Joint pretraining on text and time series with a single backbone is effective, enabling strong unimodal performance and robust multimodal capabilities without dependence on pretrained cross-modal adapters; Chronicle is the first such model and sets new benchmarks across domains.

Abstract: Real-world time series come with text: metadata, descriptions, news, reports. Yet time series foundation models process numerical sequences in isolation, and the multimodal text-and-time-series models that attempt to bridge the two all adapt a pretrained language model post hoc, inheriting representations shaped without ever seeing temporal data. These models are also evaluated almost exclusively against other multimodal baselines, not against the strongest unimodal foundation models in either domain, leaving open whether joint training is needed at all. We present Chronicle, a compact 324M-parameter decoder-only transformer trained from scratch on natural language and time series within a single unified architecture. Both modalities share the same transformer blocks, attention mechanism, and residual stream; the bulk of pretraining uses unimodal batches so cross-modal capability emerges purely from shared parameters, with a short alignment stage that interleaves the two. To our knowledge, Chronicle is the first model jointly pretrained on text and time series from scratch, and the first multimodal model evaluated against dedicated foundation models in both domains. It matches Gemma-3-270M-PT on 19 NLU tasks, sets a new bar for frozen-embedding time series classification on 24 UCR/UEA datasets, and produces multimodal forecasts on Time-MMD that beat every supervised fusion baseline, all from a single backbone.

</details>


### [174] [Catching a Moving Subspace: Low-Rank Bandits Beyond Stationarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20269)
*Hamed Khosravi,Xiaoming Huo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Piecewise-stationary, low-rank linear contextual bandits with scalar feedback are analyzed. The work characterizes an identification boundary, proposes a rank-aware algorithm (SPSC) with online change-point detection, and achieves intrinsic-rank dynamic regret, backed by experiments across 11 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rewards lie in a low-dimensional latent subspace that drifts over time. Existing methods treat either low-rank structure or nonstationarity in isolation; there is a gap for joint handling in piecewise-stationary settings with minimal feedback.

Method: Introduce SPSC: alternating isotropic probes with windowed projected ridge-UCB within a learned r-dimensional subspace; a CUSUM-like mechanism detects segment boundaries online. Provide (i) an identification boundary under three probe-side conditions, (ii) a regret bound of ~Õ(r√T) + Õ(T^{2/3}) + O(W V_in), and (iii) empirical validation on 11 benchmarks.

Result: The approach attains intrinsic-rank dynamic regret, demonstrates necessity and sufficiency of the three conditions for identifiability, and shows empirical gains over baselines when ambient-dimension-to-rank gap is moderate to large (d−r ≥ T^{1/6}).

Conclusion: This work uniquely characterizes the identifiability frontier and achieves the intrinsic-rank dynamic-regret rate in piecewise-stationary low-rank linear contextual bandits, validated empirically and suggesting practical applicability across domains where subspace drift occurs.

Abstract: Many bandit deployments (recommendation, clinical dosing, ad targeting) share two facts prior work handles only in isolation: rewards live on a low-dimensional latent subspace, and that subspace drifts. Stationary low-rank bandits exploit rank but break under subspace change; non-stationary linear bandits adapt to drift but pay ambient rate $\widetilde{O}(d\sqrt{T})$. We study piecewise-stationary low-rank linear contextual bandits with scalar feedback: $θ_t = B_k^\star w_t$ with rank-$r$ factor $B_k^\star\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times r}$ constant within each of $K$ unknown segments and able to shift at boundaries. Our results are tight along three axes. (i) Identification boundary. With single-play scalar rewards, the moving subspace is recoverable through quadratic functionals of rewards iff three probe-side conditions hold: known noise variance, bounded state-noise coupling, and full-dimensional probe support. Each is necessary in the unrestricted-second-moment problem, and jointly they are sufficient, characterizing the boundary of the solvable region. (ii) Algorithm and dynamic regret. SPSC interleaves isotropic probes with windowed projected ridge-UCB exploitation inside the learned $r$-dimensional subspace; a CUSUM-style variant discovers segment boundaries online. The costed dynamic regret is $\widetilde{O}(r\sqrt{T})+\widetilde{O}(T^{2/3})+O(W\,V_{\mathrm{in}})$, replacing the ambient $d\sqrt{T}$ rate with the intrinsic rank. (iii) Empirics.
