On eleven benchmarks spanning synthetic, UCI/MovieLens, semi-synthetic clinical, and ZOZOTOWN production-log data, SPSC outperforms non-stationary and low-rank baselines whenever $d-r\gtrsim T^{1/6}$, matching the analytical crossover. To our knowledge, this is the first work to characterize the identification boundary and attain the intrinsic-rank dynamic-regret rate in this setting.

</details>


### [175] [Conformal Selective Acting: Anytime-Valid Risk Control for RLVR-Trained LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20270)
*Hamed Khosravi,Xiaoming Huo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conformal Selective Acting (CSA) is a per-round, deployment-side wrapper for RLVR-tuned local LLMs that achieves pathwise selective risk guarantees with anytime validity and horizon-independent release-rate, filling an empty cell in the test framework for per-round safety certification under regulated constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regulated deployments require per-round safety certification with no pooling across deployments and no waiting for long-run averages. Existing conformal methods either require exchangeability, bound long-run averages, or control marginal risk rather than selective risk, leaving a gap for per-round, non-refusing deployment in online, non-exchangeable streams with adaptive updates.

Method: Introduce CSA: a per-threshold Ville-type e-process maintained on a Bonferroni grid, evaluated against the RLVR filtration. It enforces selective risk with anytime-pathwise validity by operating as a per-round wrapper, leveraging predictable updates and isotonic-calibrated monotone risk. Prove four results: (i) anytime-pathwise selective-risk bound R_T^{act} <= α + O(N_T^{-1/2}); (ii) rate-optimal certification Θ(arη^{-2} log(1/δ)); (iii) horizon-independent release-rate gap; (iv) empirical validation across diverse benchmarks and live RLVR cells.)

Result: The paper proves rigorous theoretical guarantees for CSA under the stated conditions and demonstrates empirical superiority across extensive benchmarks: CSA uniquely satisfies pathwise validity and non-refusing deployment in all tested cells among ten methods, and achieves state-of-the-art selective risk control and certification rates.

Conclusion: CSA complements the modeling side (LLM/training) by providing a deployment-side safeguard that preserves per-round safety without altering the underlying model or API access; it enables operators in regulated settings to deploy RLVR-tuned LLMs with per-round safety certificates.

Abstract: A local specialist LLM, fine-tuned with reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) on operator-local data, is installed in a regulated organization with per-deployment error budget $α$. The operator needs a safety certificate for this deployment's stream at every round: no pooling across deployments, no waiting for a long-run average. Existing wrappers cannot deliver this on adaptive, online-updated streams: offline conformal-risk methods require exchangeability; online-conformal methods bound only long-run averages; non-exchangeable extensions are marginally valid; and the closest anytime wrapper, A-RCPS, controls marginal rather than selective risk. Using a (test statistic, validity guarantee, deployment rule) framework, we identify one empty cell forced by deployment requirements: e-process per threshold, selective risk, anytime-pathwise validity, max-certified-threshold rule. Conformal Selective Acting (CSA) fills it as a per-round wrapper maintaining a Ville-type e-process per threshold on a Bonferroni grid, evaluated against the RLVR filtration. Under predictable updates and isotonic-calibrated monotone risk we prove (i) an anytime-pathwise selective-risk bound $R_T^{\mathrm{act}}\leα+O(N_T^{-1/2})$, (ii) rate-optimal certification matching $Θ(\barη^{-2}\log(1/δ))$, and (iii) a horizon-independent release-rate gap. Across eight specialist benchmarks ($480$ streams), sixteen adversarial distribution-shift cells ($160$ streams), and five live Expert-Iteration RLVR cells with online LoRA over four base models in three architecture families ($10{,}300$ rounds), CSA is the only method among ten compared that satisfies pathwise validity and non-refusing deployment on every cell. We do not propose a new LLM, training algorithm, or policy class; CSA is the deployment-side complement, orthogonal to the model, for operators who cannot use a frontier API.

</details>


### [176] [Smaller Abstract State Spaces Enable Cross-Scale Generalization in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20272)
*Nasehatul Mustakim,Lucas Lehnert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical model for OOD generalization in RL under partial observability, using state abstraction and successor-weighted model reduction; derives performance bounds showing smaller abstract state spaces improve OOD generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization to more complex tasks is hard for RL; a theoretical foundation for OOD generalization under partial observability is needed to guide architecture design.

Method: Extend state abstraction to POMDPs; introduce successor-weighted model reduction enabling compression to smaller abstract spaces; derive a bound on OOD test performance that decomposes error into approximation and estimation components.

Result: Derived conditions under which OOD generalization is achievable; evidence that reducing the abstract state space size improves test performance and generalization to more complex tasks.

Conclusion: Constraining agents to a small, finite set of abstract states appears necessary for generalization across tasks of varying complexity; motivates research into scalable RL architectures across complexity levels.

Abstract: While humans readily generalize abstract concepts to more complex or larger tasks, building Reinforcement Learning (RL) systems with this ability remains elusive. Here, we present the first theoretical model of how such Out-of-Distribution (OOD) generalization can be achieved in RL agents. Our approach considers Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) and assumes that an intelligent agent uses an abstraction function to determine which experiences can be treated as equivalent and which must be distinguished. First, we extend the existing state abstraction framework and proof techniques to POMDPs. Then, we define a successor-weighted model reduction, a model reduction variant that enables compression into smaller abstract spaces than prior definitions allow. We derive a bound on the agent's OOD test performance, thereby defining the conditions under which OOD generalization is achievable. This bound decomposes an agent's performance loss into approximation and estimation errors, revealing how reducing an agent's abstract state space size improves test performance and OOD generalization. Our analysis suggests that constraining an agent to operate over a small, finite set of abstract states is necessary for achieving generalization to more complex tasks. Our results motivate further research into learning RL architectures that scale across tasks of varying complexity levels.

</details>


### [177] [Modality-Decoupled Online Recursive Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20273)
*Siyuan Li,Youyuan Zhang,Fangming Liu,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: M-ORE is a modality-decoupled online recursive editor for lifelong, efficient adaptation of multimodal LLMs, addressing cross-modal and long-horizon interference with a closed-form, Sherman–Morrison-based update in an orthogonal low-rank subspace and module-wise locality statistics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-only editors for LLMs underperform on multimodal LLMs due to visually dominant activations skewing updates and sequential writes causing cross-edit interference; need online lifelong editing under tight compute/memory budgets.

Method: Derives a unified proximal-projection formulation enabling a closed-form update via Sherman–Morrison recursion. Maintains module-wise locality statistics for the text stack and visual projector to prevent visually dominated updates, and updates in a fixed orthogonal low-rank edit subspace to mitigate long-horizon interference.

Result: Empirical evaluation across multiple MLLM backbones and online-editing benchmarks shows consistent improvements in reliability, generality, and locality over strong baselines, with favorable quality–efficiency scaling.

Conclusion: M-ORE provides a modality-aware, efficient online editing framework for lifelong MLLM adaptation, achieving robust updates with constant per-edit overhead and publicly released code.

Abstract: Online model editing for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) requires assimilating a stream of corrections under tight compute and memory budgets. Yet editors developed for text-only LLMs often degrade on MLLMs: visually dominant activations skew the statistics that shape updates, causing cross-modal conflict, while sequential writes become entangled in a shared edit space and amplify long-horizon interference, causing inter-edit interference. To address these, we propose M-ORE, a modality-decoupled online recursive editor for lifelong MLLM adaptation. M-ORE is derived from a unified proximal-projection formulation and admits a closed-form update with a Sherman-Morrison recursion, yielding constant per-edit overhead. It maintains module-wise locality statistics for the text stack and the visual projector to avoid visually dominated update shaping and performs continual updates in a fixed orthogonal low-rank edit subspace via a Sherman-Morrison recursion to mitigate long-horizon interference. Experiments on multiple MLLM backbones and online editing benchmarks show that our M-ORE method consistently improves reliability, generality, and locality over strong baselines, while achieving favorable quality-efficiency scaling. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/lab-klc/M-ORE.

</details>


### [178] [OmniISR: A Unified Framework for Centralized and Federated Learning via Intermediate Supervision and Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20276)
*Wei-Bin Kou,Guangxu Zhu,Ming Tang,Chen Zhang,Lisheng Wu,Lei Zhou,Yujiu Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OmniISR is a unified ISR-based framework that blends centralized learning (CL), federated learning (FL), and their hybrid CL-FL training via multi-layer intermediate supervision and regularization, using mutual information and negative entropy signals. It provides non-asymptotic convergence guarantees, drift/gradient alignment bounds, and experimental gains across CL and FL settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CL and FL operate under incompatible optimization regimes due to data governance constraints, causing unbiased global gradients with internal covariate shift in CL vs. biased local updates and drift in FL. Naively mixing them lacks rigorous guarantees. A principled framework is needed to align representations and control uncertainty across heterogeneous regimes.

Method: Introduce OmniISR: fuse pure CL, pure FL, and hybrid CL-FL modes via intermediate supervision using mutual-information (MI) to align shifting covariates and client-drift, and intermediate regularization via negative-entropy (NE) to penalize overconfident predictions. Theoretical contributions include (i) an ISR-agnostic, non-asymptotic O(1/√T) convergence bound, (ii) a federated drift bound quantifying ISR-driven drift reduction, (iii) a gradient-alignment guarantee for non-conflicting CL/FL updates under mild bias, and (iv) an explicit escape-time bound showing hybrid CL-FL increases stochasticity and accelerates saddle-escape. Empirically, OmniISR improves performance across CL and FL, reduces the CL-FL gap by 22.60%, and yields 37/48 paired metric wins across multiple FL algorithms.

Result: The framework delivers theoretical guarantees (convergence, drift reduction, gradient alignment, and saddle-escape acceleration) and practical improvements, demonstrating robust CL/FL integration with a reduced gap and strong metric gains across several FL settings.

Conclusion: OmniISR offers a principled, theory-backed pathway to unify CL and FL, delivering both convergence guarantees and empirical performance gains, and enabling effective operation under diverse data governance constraints.

Abstract: The global deployment of edge intelligence operates across heterogeneous legal frameworks. While some regions permit centralized learning (CL) via cloud data aggregation, others enforce strict data localization, necessitating federated learning (FL). This operational dichotomy introduces two incompatible optimization regimes (i.e., unbiased global gradients yet coupled with internal covariate shift in CL versus biased, drift-prone local updates in FL), resulting in that any naive integration of the two lacks rigorous theoretical guarantees. To fill this gap, we propose OmniISR, a unified framework that fuses pure CL, pure FL, and hybrid CL-FL training modes via equipping intermediate supervision and regularization (ISR) signals at multiple hidden layers. Specifically, we propose (i) to use mutual-information (MI) as intermediate supervision to align shifting internal covariate in CL and client-drifting representations in FL, and (ii) to adopt negative-entropy (NE) as intermediate regularizer to penalize overconfident prediction, preserve representational uncertainty, and avoid device-specific collapse. On the theory side, we derive (i) a unified, ISR-agnostic, and non-asymptotic O(1/sqrt(T)) convergence bound that shows the introduced ISR does not violate standard SGD convergence, (ii) a federated drift-bound that quantifies the ISR-reduced client drift, (iii) a gradient-alignment guarantee that ensures non-conflicting CL and FL updates under mild bias, and (iv) an explicit escape-time bound that indicates that CL-FL hybrid mixing enlarges effective stochasticity and accelerates escape from strict saddles. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OmniISR consistently improves model performance in both centralized and federated paradigms, reduces the CL-FL gap by 22.60%, and yields 37/48 paired metric wins across multiple FL algorithms.

</details>


### [179] [ClaimDiff-RL: Fine-Grained Caption Reinforcement Learning through Visual Claim Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20278)
*Tianle Li,Xuyang Shen,Yan Ma,Rongxin Guo,Shaoxiang Chen,Jiacheng Chen,Haochen Wang,Hongyang Tang,Yucong Zhou,Yu Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ClaimDiff-RL uses reference-conditioned atomic claim differences as per-difference rewards for RL-based long-form image captioning, enabling fine-grained feedback and improved fidelity-coverage tradeoffs; achieves better hallucination-omission balance and strong performance on several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the reward granularity problem in RL for long-form captioning, where sequence-level signals mask local factual errors; current metrics compress local errors, hindering balanced optimization.

Method: Introduce ClaimDiff-RL; given image, caption, and reference; a multimodal judge Enumerates visually grounded differences, verifies each against the image, assigns open-vocabulary error types and severity, and outputs per-difference statistics to compose the RL reward.

Result: Improves hallucination vs missing facts balance; preserves general capability; on a 160-image diagnostic benchmark, public captioning benchmarks, and VQA benchmarks; sometimes surpasses Gemini-3-Pro-Preview on fine-grained dimensions like object counting, spatial relations, scene recognition.

Conclusion: Typed, verifiable claim differences are effective reward units for fine-grained, diagnosable caption RL, enabling controllable tradeoffs and better debugging of fidelity and coverage in long-form captioning.

Abstract: Long-form image captioning exposes a reward granularity problem in RL: captions are judged as whole sequences, while the important errors occur at the level of individual visual claims. A good dense caption should be both faithful and informative, avoiding hallucination without omitting salient details. Yet pairwise preferences, reference-based metrics, and holistic scalar rewards compress these local errors into a single sequence-level signal, obscuring the tradeoff between factuality and coverage. We introduce ClaimDiff-RL, a framework that uses reference-conditioned atomic claim differences as the reward unit for caption RL. Given an image, an actor caption, and a reference caption, a multimodal judge enumerates visually grounded differences, verifies each difference against the image, assigns open-vocabulary error types and severity levels, and produces per-difference statistics for reward composition. This makes hallucinated claims and omitted salient facts separately measurable and tunable. Experiments show that holistic scalar rewards can reduce hallucination by increasing missing facts, while ClaimDiff-RL exposes this faithfulness and coverage tradeoff and enables more balanced operating points. On a 160-image human-labeled diagnostic benchmark, public captioning benchmarks, and VQA benchmarks, ClaimDiff-RL improves the hallucination--missing-fact balance, preserves general capability, and even surpasses Gemini-3-Pro-Preview on several fine-grained Capability dimensions such as object counting, spatial relations, and scene recognition. These results suggest that typed, verifiable claim differences are an effective reward unit for fine-grained and diagnosable caption RL.

</details>


### [180] [Introspective X Training: Feedback Conditioning Improves Scaling Across all LLM Training Stages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20285)
*Brandon Cui,Ximing Lu,Jaehun Jung,Syeda Nahida Akter,Hyunwoo Kim,Yuxiao Qu,David Acuna,Shrimai Prabhumoye,Yejin Choi,Prithviraj Ammanabrolu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introspective Training (IXT) uses a reward-conditioned feedback loop to annotate data with natural language critique, enabling quality-aware training from early stages and achieving up to 2.8x compute efficiency in large LLM scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve data efficiency and scaling of LLM training by leveraging insights from later stages (post-training) to guide earlier stages (pre-training).

Method: Train a thinking reward model to generate natural-language critiques; prepend-conditioning of training data with the generated feedback; applicable across training stages (pre-training to post-training); inspired by offline reward-conditioned reinforcement learning.

Result: On 7.5–12B dense LLMs trained from scratch up to 18 trillion tokens, IXT yields up to 2.8x compute efficiency and achieves performance gains in domains like math and code that are hard to reach with standard training.

Conclusion: IXT can bend scaling curves by enabling quality-aware training from early stages, delivering substantial efficiency gains and enhanced performance across challenging domains.

Abstract: We tackle the question of how to scale more efficiently across the many, ever-growing stages of current LLM training pipelines. Our guiding intuition stems from the fact that the dynamics of later stages of the pipeline, e.g. post-training, can be used to inform earlier stages such as pre-training. To this end, we propose Introspective Training (or IXT), inspired by offline reward-conditioned reinforcement learning and applicable to any stage of training. IXT uses a thinking reward model to annotate data with natural language critique based feedback, enabling quality aware training from the earliest stages of the pipeline. Models are then trained by prefix-conditioning the data with the generated feedback -- ensuring that not all tokens are treated equally starting much earlier in training than usual. Comprehensive experiments on 7.5-12B transformer-based dense LLMs trained from scratch all the way up to 18 Trillion tokens seen show that our method: bends scaling curves resulting in up to 2.8x more compute efficiency generally; and reaches performance levels unachievable for models trained otherwise in domains such as math and code.

</details>


### [181] [FusionCell: Cross-Attentive Fusion of Layout Geometry and Netlist Topology for Standard-Cell Performance Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20287)
*Haoyi Zhang,Kairong Guo,Bojie Zhang,Yibo Lin,Runsheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FusionCell is a dual-modality predictor that jointly models routed layout geometry and netlist topology for fast, accurate standard-cell delay/power prediction, outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Characterizing standard cells requires capturing both fine-grained layout geometry (including coupling and layout-dependent effects) and netlist topology, but existing methods either rely on slow simulations or ignore layout geometry, hindering accurate predictions.

Method: A dual-modality model: a DeiT encoder processes three-layer routed layouts; a heterogeneous graph transformer models device/net graphs; a topology-guided fusion uses the netlist as a structural map to actively query relevant physical regions, enabling joint geometric and topological reasoning. Dataset: ~19.5k cells, 149 types on 7nm ASAP7; targets six metrics (rise/fall delay, transition, power).

Result: FusionCell reduces regression error with an average MAPE of 0.92% and improves ranking metrics (Spearman/Kendall) over baselines; it speeds up characterization by orders of magnitude compared to circuit simulation.

Conclusion: Joint geometry-topology modeling via topology-guided fusion yields accurate, fast standard-cell characterization, scalable to large cell libraries and technology nodes.

Abstract: Standard cells form the building blocks of digital circuits, so their delay and power critically influence chip-level performance; yet characterization still relies on slow simulation sweeps, and many fast predictors ignore layout geometry, missing coupling and layout-dependent effects. The challenge is to jointly represent layout geometry and netlist topology so models capture fine-grained spatial details together with structural connectivity for accurate performance prediction. We introduce FusionCell, a dual-modality predictor that treats routed layout geometry and netlist topology as inputs and fuses them explicitly in a unified model. A DeiT encoder processes three-layer routed layouts, while a graph transformer models heterogeneous device/net graphs. The modalities are integrated through a topology-guided mechanism, where the netlist acts as a structural "map" to actively query relevant physical regions in the layout for joint geometric and topological reasoning. We build a 7nm dataset based on the ASAP7 PDK with over 19.5k cells spanning 149 types using automatic tools, targeting six metrics: signal rise/fall delay, transition, and power. Experimental results demonstrate that FusionCell reduces regression error, with an average MAPE of 0.92 percent, and improves Spearman/Kendall ranking over baselines, while accelerating the characterization process by orders of magnitude compared to circuit simulation.

</details>


### [182] [Plug-and-Play Spiking Operators: Breaking the Nonlinearity Bottleneck in Spiking Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20289)
*Xinzhe Yuan,Xiang Peng,Bin Gu,Huan Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A plug-and-play spike-friendly approximation framework for nonlinear Transformer operations enabling ANN-to-SNN conversion for LLMs with minimal accuracy loss (<1%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nonlinear operators in Transformers (Softmax, SiLU, normalization) require divisions, exponentials, and norms that are hard to implement efficiently on LIF-based neuromorphic hardware; existing ANN-to-SNN pipelines mainly handle linear operations, limiting neuromorphic compatibility.

Method: Decompose nonlinear computations into three primitives (division, exponentiation, and l2-norms) and realize them via population coding with LIF neuron groups, plus lightweight bit-shift scaling to avoid floating-point arithmetic. This modular operator-block approach plugs into existing ANN-to-SNN pipelines without fine-tuning and supports nonlinearities such as Softmax, SiLU, and normalization.

Result: Empirical evaluation on a range of Transformer-based LLMs shows less than 1% accuracy drop when selectively replacing the targeted nonlinear operators across various tasks.

Conclusion: A modular, spike-friendly primitives framework enables accurate ANN-to-SNN conversion for Transformers, enabling broader neuromorphic compatibility without fine-tuning and with minimal accuracy degradation.

Abstract: ANN-to-SNN conversion offers a practical, training-free route to spiking large language models. However, current pipelines primarily focus on spike-driven realizations for Transformer linear-algebra operations, while providing limited support for key nonlinear operators. This gap limits compatibility with neuromorphic-style execution constraints, where such nonlinearities typically require division, exponentiation, or norm computations that are not naturally supported by standard leaky integrate-and-fire dynamics. To solve this problem, we propose a plug-and-play framework that implements spike-friendly approximations for Transformer nonlinearities and integrates into existing ANN-to-SNN pipelines. Our method decomposes these nonlinear computations into three recurring primitives -- division, exponentiation, and $\ell_2$ norms -- and realizes them via population computation using LIF neuron groups, combined with lightweight bit-shift scaling to avoid floating-point arithmetic. By composing these primitives as modular operator blocks, our framework supports common Transformer nonlinearities (e.g., Softmax, SiLU, and normalization) without any fine-tuning. Experiments on a range of LLMs Transformers show that selectively replacing the targeted nonlinear operators incurs less than a $1\%$ accuracy drop across all evaluated tasks.

</details>


### [183] [Weasel: Out-of-Domain Generalization for Web Agents via Importance-Diversity Data Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20291)
*Fatemeh Pesaran zadeh,Seyeon Choi,Xing Han Lù,Siva Reddy,Gunhee Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Weasel offers efficient offline training for web agents by selecting a fixed-budget subset of trajectory steps, pruning AXTrees around targets, and using model-generated rationales to align styles, yielding large speedups (9.7–12.5x) and better out-of-domain performance across multiple datasets and models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language model web agents struggle to generalize out-of-domain and require expensive offline training due to noisy trajectories and long AXTree states. There is a need for sample-efficient offline training that preserves diversity and target-relevant content while reducing cognitive load on the model.

Method: 1) Greedy trajectory selection optimizing a joint objective of unary importance and pairwise diversity over states, websites, and interaction patterns to select a fixed budget of trajectory steps. 2) Target-centered AXTree pruning to retain content surrounding the ground-truth action target. 3) Replace expert traces with model-generated, style-consistent rationales to reduce style mismatch for reasoning-native models.

Result: On AgentTrek and NNetNav, evaluated on WebArena, WorkArena, and MiniWob, with Qwen2.5-7B, Gemma3-4B, and Qwen3-8B. Weasel improves out-of-domain performance and reduces training cost, achieving roughly 9.7–12.5× training speedups over standard fine-tuning. Code released at GitHub.

Conclusion: Weasel is a practical, workflow-efficient approach to offline fine-tuning for web agents, combining selective trajectory sampling, content-targeted pruning, and rationale alignment to improve cross-domain performance at substantially reduced cost. Potential extensions include exploring broader model families, diverse task settings, and further ablation studies on the proposed components.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have enabled web agents that follow natural language goals through multi-step browser interactions. However, agents fine-tuned on specific trajectories and domain often struggle to generalize out of domain, and offline training can be compute-inefficient due to noisy, redundant trajectories and long accessibility-tree (AXTree) states. To address both issues, we propose Weasel, a trajectory selection method for offline training of web agents. Weasel selects a fixed-budget subset of trajectory steps by optimizing an objective that balances unary importance with pairwise diversity over states, websites, and interaction patterns, solving efficiently with a greedy algorithm. We further improve efficiency with target-centered AXTree pruning that keeps only content around the ground-truth action target, and we mitigate style mismatch for reasoning-native models by replacing expert traces with model-generated, style-consistent rationales. Across AgentTrek and NNetNav training datasets, evaluations in WebArena, WorkArena, and MiniWob, and experiments with Qwen2.5-7B, Gemma3-4B, and Qwen3-8B, Weasel improves out-of-domain performance while reducing training cost, producing roughly 9.7-12.5$\times$ training speedups over standard fine-tuning. We make the code available at https://github.com/fatemehpesaran310/weasel.

</details>


### [184] [TreeText-CTS: Compact, Source-Traceable Tree-Path Evidence for Irregular Clinical Time-Series Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20292)
*Kwanhyung Lee,Juhwan Choi,Jongheon Kim,Joohyung Lee,Hyeongwon Jang,Eunho Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TreeText-CTS converts irregular EHR trajectories into readable, source-traceable tree-path evidence units, routes through frozen XGBoost, selects evidence, and uses a language-model encoder for prediction, achieving state-of-the-art among text-based EHR interfaces across PhysioNet and MIMIC datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between powerful numerical time-series models and human-readable, verifiable evidence by producing compact, source-traceable explanations aligned with time windows, without patient-level summarization.

Method: Convert multi-scale window summaries into threshold-based tree-path evidence units; pass these through frozen XGBoost models to obtain activated paths; verbalize activated paths as deterministic evidence units; assemble a subset via an evidence selector; feed into a language-model encoder for final prediction.

Result: Achieves best AUROC and AUPRC among evaluated text-based EHR time-series interfaces on PhysioNet 2012 mortality, MIMIC-III mortality, and PhysioNet 2019 sepsis-onset datasets; AUPRC improvement of 6.0–9.7 percentage points over strongest prior text-based interface; performance competitive with numerical time-series models.

Conclusion: TreeText-CTS provides inspectable, source-traceable evidence for predictions; ablations show the contributions of tree-path construction, evidence selection, and LM composition to performance.

Abstract: Numerical time-series models can effectively process irregular electronic health record (EHR) trajectories, but they do not naturally expose the measurements and temporal patterns supporting each risk estimate as readable evidence. Existing text-based interfaces improve readability, but typically rely on either raw serialization, which is lengthy and redundant, or patient-level free-form summaries, which are difficult to trace to source measurements and time windows. To bridge this gap, we introduce TreeText-CTS (Clinical Time-Series), which converts irregular EHR trajectories into human-readable, compact, source-traceable tree-path evidence units without patient-level summarization or inference-time autoregressive decoding. TreeText-CTS routes multi-scale window summaries through frozen XGBoost models and verbalizes activated tree paths as deterministic, source-traceable evidence units composed of threshold conditions. An evidence selector assembles an informative subset of these units, which a language-model encoder then integrates for prediction. Across PhysioNet 2012 mortality, MIMIC-III mortality, and PhysioNet 2019 sepsis-onset forecasting, TreeText-CTS achieves the best AUROC and AUPRC among evaluated text-based EHR time-series interfaces, improving AUPRC by 6.0 to 9.7 absolute percentage points over the strongest prior text-based interface while remaining competitive with numerical time-series models. Ablations show that tree-path evidence construction, evidence selection, and language-model composition each contribute to performance. Because every span passed to the language-model encoder is constructed from activated tree-path threshold conditions, TreeText-CTS makes the evidence supplied to the final predictor inspectable and source-traceable.

</details>


### [185] [Closed-form predictive coding via hierarchical Gaussian filters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20293)
*Aleksandrs Baskakovs,Sylvain Estebe,Kenneth Enevoldsen,Kristoffer Nielbo,Chris Mathys,Nicolas Legrand*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identity-precision in predictive coding (PC) slows training and degrades with depth; by formulating PC as deep hierarchical Gaussian filters (HGFs) with precision-weighted messages, online learning of activations, weights, and precisions is enabled under a single free-energy objective, removing the need for global error signals or iterations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome slow, depth-sensitive training in PC networks caused by a fixed identity precision, by reinstating precision-weighted inference to achieve fast, local, Bayesian updates.

Method: Recast PC as deep hierarchical Gaussian filters and implement precision-weighted message passing; jointly learn activations, weights, and precisions under a single free-energy objective without iterative relaxation or backpropagation with a global error signal; use Hebbian-like update rules.

Result: On FashionMNIST, performance nears backpropagation in epoch-level wall-clock cost and converges in fewer epochs; additionally, it improves online learning, data efficiency, and handling of concept drift.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that closed-form variational inference with online precision learning provides a tractable foundation for deep PC networks, preserving biological interpretability while avoiding iterative relaxation or global error signals.

Abstract: Predictive coding (PC) offers a local and biologically grounded alternative to backpropagation in the training of artificial neural networks, yet to date, it remains slower, and performance degrades sharply as network depth increases. We trace both problems to a single simplification: current PC networks fix the precision matrix to the identity, discarding precision-weighted prediction errors that the variational derivation requires to be fast, local, and Bayesian. We close this gap by expressing predictive coding networks as deep hierarchical Gaussian filters (HGFs) and restore precision-weighted message passing, yielding dynamic uncertainty estimates and Hebbian-compatible update rules at every layer. The resulting networks can simultaneously learn activations, weights, and precisions under a single free-energy objective, with no global error signal, and resolve inference without requiring iterations or automatic differentiation. On FashionMNIST, our solution approaches backpropagation in epoch-level wall-clock cost while converging in fewer epochs, and outperforms it on online, data efficiency, and concept-drift tasks. We thus establish that closed-form variational inference with online precision learning provides a tractable foundation for deep predictive coding networks, retaining biological and interpretative advantages, without requiring iterative relaxation or global error signals.

</details>


### [186] [Quant.npu: Enabling Efficient Mobile NPU Inference for on-device LLMs via Fully Static Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20295)
*Jinghe Zhang,Daliang Xu,Chenghua Wang,Weikai Xie,Tao Qi,Yun Ma,Mengwei Xu,Gang Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Quant.npu introduces an integer-only, fully static quantization framework for NPUs, leveraging learnable quantization parameters and rotation matrices. It includes a rotation-and-bit-width-aware initialization and a distribution-aware two-stage optimization, plus a sensitivity-guided adaptive mixed-precision scheme. It achieves accuracy on par with SOTA PTQ methods while reducing mobile-NPU latency up to 15.1%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NPU hardware constraints require static, integer-only quantization, but most PTQ methods rely on dynamic activation quantization, causing incompatibility. A static, high-fidelity PTQ approach is needed for efficient, on-device LLM inference.

Method: Proposes Quant.npu: an integer-only, fully static quantization framework with learnable quantization parameters and rotation matrices. Introduces rotation-and-bit-width-aware initialization and a distribution-aware selective optimization (two-stage pipeline) for rotated vs unrotated tensors, plus a sensitivity-guided adaptive mixed-precision scheme.

Result: Extensive experiments on real-world mobile NPUs show Quant.npu achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art methods and reduces inference latency by up to 15.1%.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of stable, static integer quantization for mobile NPUs with reparameterizable learnable quantizers and rotations, offering a practical path toward efficient on-device LLM inference.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed on mobile devices, where Neural Processing Units (NPUs) necessitate fully static quantization for optimal inference efficiency. However, existing post-training quantization (PTQ) methods predominantly rely on dynamic activation quantization, rendering them incompatible with NPU hardware constraints. To bridge the gap between high-fidelity PTQ and NPU-constrained inference, we propose Quant.npu, a integer-only fully static quantization framework. It incorporates learnable quantization parameters and rotation matrices, enabling low-bit activation-weight quantization without runtime quantization parameters re-computation. Crucially, we identify that initialization and selective optimization of quantization parameters is pivotal for optimization stability, as improper initialization and naive joint optimization induce gradient instability that disrupts the optimization of rotation matrices. To address this, we propose a rotation-and-bit-width-aware initialization tailored to diverse activation profiles and a distribution-aware selective optimization (two-stage quantization pipeline) tailored to rotated and unrotated tensors. Furthermore, we introduce a sensitivity-guided adaptive mixed-precision scheme to balance accuracy with inference efficiency. Extensive experiments on real-world mobile NPUs demonstrate that Quant.npu achieves comparable accuracy to state-of-the-art methods, while reducing inference latency by up to 15.1%.

</details>


### [187] [Spectral Unforgetting: Post-Hoc Recovery of Damaged Capabilities Without Retraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20296)
*Aarash Abro,Muhammad Tahir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DG-Hard is a data-free, post-hoc spectral repair that filters the weight-update delta with Donoho-Gavish hard thresholding to recover after fine-tuning, maintaining target task gains and held-out improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning; need to repair model capabilities without access to training data, while preserving target-task gains and beneficial held-out improvements.

Method: Let Δ = W_ft - W_base. Treat Δ as a low-rank, task-aligned signal embedded in noise. For each weight-delta matrix, apply the Donoho-Gavish hard singular-value threshold to keep the high-energy, structured component and remove the spectral bulk. This is a closed-form SVD filtering step with no data-dependent tuning.

Result: Across 14 (model, task) settings and 9 cross-domain held-out benchmarks, DG-Hard delivers the strongest balanced repair among post-hoc baselines. It also restores safety alignment degraded by benign fine-tuning on three independent axes, despite using no alignment data.

Conclusion: Fine-tuning-induced capability loss appears, at least in part, as removable spectral residue in the weight-update; DG-Hard offers an effective, data-free repair that preserves target-task gains and beneficial held-out improvements.

Abstract: Fine-tuning a language model for a target task routinely degrades capabilities the training data never explicitly threatened. We study this phenomenon, known as catastrophic forgetting, and propose a post-hoc repair solution that uses only the pretrained checkpoint $W_{\mathrm{base}}$ and its fine-tuned descendant $W_{\mathrm{ft}}$. The goal is not merely to revert the model toward the base checkpoint, but to recover capabilities damaged by fine-tuning while preserving both the target-task gains and any beneficial held-out improvements. We introduce DG-Hard, a checkpoint-only spectral repair method for the fine-tuning update $Δ= W_{\mathrm{ft}} - W_{\mathrm{base}}$. DG-Hard treats $Δ$ as a low-rank task-aligned signal embedded in an IID-like noise residual that gradient descent has no incentive to remove, and applies the Donoho-Gavish hard singular-value threshold to each weight-delta matrix, keeping the structured high-energy part of the update and removing the spectral bulk. This reduces repair to a closed-form SVD filtering step requiring no data-dependent tuning. A central difficulty is evaluation: average accuracy hides per-benchmark failures, while naive recovery scores reward models that simply revert toward the base. We therefore introduce a partition-conditional metric that separately tracks healing, preservation, non-damage, and target-task retention. Across $14$ (model, task) settings and nine cross-domain held-out benchmarks, DG-Hard achieves the strongest balanced repair among post-hoc baselines. DG-Hard also restores safety alignment degraded by benign fine-tuning on three independent safety axes, despite using no alignment data. These results suggest that part of fine-tuning-induced capability loss is not an unavoidable consequence of specialization, but a removable spectral residue in the weight update itself.

</details>


### [188] [Mechanisms of Misgeneralization in Physical Sequence Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20299)
*Kento Nishi,Raphael Tang,Karun Kumar,Core Francisco Park,Hidenori Tanaka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Generative sequence models for physical trajectories can misgeneralize: individual trajectories seem plausible but their aggregate distribution over a physical quantity (e.g., distance, energy) is distorted. A data deviation kernel is introduced to diagnose and predict which quantities gain or lose mass, enabling kernel-informed mitigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Engineers curate demonstrations to fix the expected distribution over physical quantities, but standard models can shift mass of these quantities despite plausible per-trajectory outputs. A mechanistic explanation and mitigation are needed for reliable planning in physical domains.

Method: Use controlled synthetic tasks to study error propagation: show local model errors propagate through physical measurements to shift recovered distributions. Introduce a data deviation kernel to quantify this effect and predict mass shifts of physical quantities. Empirically validate on synthetic maze navigation and double-pendulum motion tasks, and discuss mitigation strategies.

Result: Demonstrate physical misgeneralization: aggregated distributions over physical quantities deviate from the intended, despite plausible individual trajectories. The data deviation kernel effectively predicts which quantities gain or lose mass across tasks and guides kernel-informed mitigation strategies.

Conclusion: A mechanistic interpretation clarifies when and why misgeneralization occurs; kernel-informed interventions offer a structurally promising path to align dataset-induced distributions with intended physical constraints, complementing per-trajectory plausibility checks.

Abstract: Generative sequence models are often trained to plan motion in physical domains, from robotics to mechanical simulations. When constructing a dataset to train such a model, engineers may curate demonstrations to specify how trajectories should be distributed over a physical quantity like travel distance or mechanical energy. For example, a roboticist building a maze navigation agent might choose demonstrations whose travel distances cover a fixed range uniformly, hoping to constrain the agent's expected power usage. We find that standard deep learning can violate this intent: each generated trajectory can seem plausible on its own, but the aggregate distribution over the physical quantity is wrong. We call this failure physical misgeneralization, and develop an account of its mechanism. Using controlled synthetic tasks, we show that physical misgeneralization arises when local errors typical of the model class propagate through the physical measurement to shift the recovered distribution. We estimate these errors with a data deviation kernel, and we use it to predict which physical quantities gain or lose mass in both our synthetic and more applied maze navigation and double-pendulum motion tasks. Finally, our mechanistic interpretation helps identify which mitigation strategies are structurally promising, and we use it to propose a kernel-informed intervention.

</details>


### [189] [Robust Subspace-Constrained Quadratic Models for Low-Dimensional Structure Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20300)
*Zheng Zhai,Xiaohui Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A robust subspace-constrained quadratic model (SCQM) is proposed for learning low-dimensional structure from high-dimensional data, generalizing SQMF to accommodate broad noise models (generalized Gaussian and radial Laplace). It uses a gradient-based optimization with backtracking line-search and provides loss-function sensitivity analysis, with experiments showing superior robustness and reconstruction accuracy over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve reliable subspace learning under diverse noise regimes, including heavy-tailed and light-tailed distributions, by generalizing the noise model and providing robust optimization and analysis.

Method: Introduce SCQM building on SQMF to model a broad class of noise distributions; develop a gradient-based algorithm with backtracking line-search for stable convergence; perform sensitivity analysis comparing lp^p and l2 losses.

Result: Theoretical insights on robustness and convergence are supported by extensive numerical experiments, which show the proposed method consistently outperforms existing approaches in robustness and reconstruction accuracy.

Conclusion: SCQM provides a robust, efficient framework for subspace learning under general noise models, combining a flexible modeling approach with effective optimization and loss-function analysis.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a robust subspace-constrained quadratic model (SCQM) for learning low-dimensional structure from high-dimensional data. Building upon the subspace-constrained quadratic matrix factorization (SQMF) framework, the proposed model accommodates a broad class of noise distributions, including generalized Gaussian and radial Laplace models. This generalization enables reliable performance under both heavy-tailed and light-tailed noise, thereby substantially enhancing robustness across diverse data regimes. To efficiently address the resulting nonconvex optimization problem, we develop a gradient-based algorithm equipped with a backtracking line-search strategy that ensures stable and efficient convergence. In addition, we present a sensitivity analysis of the $\ell_p^p$ and $\ell_2$ loss functions, elucidating their distinct behaviors under varying noise characteristics. Extensive numerical experiments corroborate the theoretical analysis and demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms existing methods in terms of robustness and reconstruction accuracy.

</details>


### [190] [Neural Collapse by Design: Learning Class Prototypes on the Hypersphere](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20302)
*Panagiotis Koromilas,Theodoros Giannakopoulos,Mihalis A. Nicolaou,Yannis Panagakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper reframes supervised learning as prototype contrast on the unit hypersphere, unifying CE and SCL. It introduces normalized losses NTCE and NONL to fix CE's shortcomings, and shows SCL yields class-mean classifiers, making linear probes redundant. Empirically, these methods approach Neural Collapse (NC) and improve transfer and robustness, while fixed-prototype SCL can match linear probing performance without extra classifier training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NC defines a theoretical optimum for supervised classification, but existing paradigms (CE and SCL) fail to achieve it in practice. The work seeks a unified framework and practical losses that realize NC-like geometry during training and evaluation.

Method: Recast CE and SCL as prototype contrast on the unit hypersphere. From the CE side, propose NTCE and NONL with large negative sets and decoupled alignment/uniformity terms. From the SCL side, prove that SCL optimizes for a classifier whose weights are class mean embeddings, rendering linear probing redundant. Validate on four benchmarks including ImageNet-1K, comparing accuracy, convergence, and transfer/robustness metrics; analyze geometry and provide empirical + theoretical insights.

Result: NTCE and NONL outperform standard CE accuracy, closely approximate NC (≥95%), and reach CE-like NC convergence within only 7.5% of CE iterations on most metrics. SCL with fixed prototypes matches linear probing without lengthy classifier training. Transfer gains of +5.5% mean relative improvement (up to +8.7% under severe class imbalance), and lower mCE on ImageNet-C are reported, all consistent with prototype/hypersphere geometry.

Conclusion: Supervised learning can be recast as prototype learning on the hypersphere, achieving Neural Collapse by design via two complementary paths: to improve CE with normalized, contrastive-inspired losses (NTCE/NONL), and to reinterpret SCL as preserving a classifier formed by class means. This yields robustness and transfer benefits and suggests a practical framework for unified, efficient training without relying on post hoc linear probing.

Abstract: Supervised classification has a theoretical optimum, Neural Collapse (NC), yet neither of its two dominant paradigms reaches it in practice. Cross entropy (CE) leaves radial degrees of freedom unconstrained and converges to a degenerate geometry, while supervised contrastive learning (SCL) drives features toward NC during pretraining but discards this structure in a post hoc linear probing phase. We show that both paradigms are different appearances of the same method, prototype contrast on the unit hypersphere, and that closing the gap requires fixing each at its specific point of failure. From the CE side, we propose NTCE and NONL, two normalized losses that import contrastive optimization's missing ingredients into classifier learning: a large effective negative set and decoupled alignment and uniformity terms. From the SCL side, we prove that SCL's objective already optimizes throughout training for a principled classifier whose weights are the class mean embeddings, making linear probing both redundant and harmful. Empirically, on four benchmarks including ImageNet-1K, NTCE and NONL surpass CE accuracy, closely approximate NC ($\geq 95\%$), and match CE's converged NC on 4/5 metrics in under $7.5\%$ of its iterations, while SCL with fixed prototypes matches linear probing without the hours-long classifier training phase. The learned geometry yields $+5.5\%$ mean relative improvement in transfer learning, up to $+8.7\%$ under severe class imbalance, and lower mCE on ImageNet-C, recasting supervised learning as prototype learning on the hypersphere, with NC reached by design on both paths.

</details>


### [191] [AirfoilGen: A valid-by-construction and performance-aware latent diffusion model for airfoil generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20303)
*Zhijie Yang,Min Tang,Qiang Zou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AirfoilGen: a valid-by-construction, performance-aware latent diffusion model for airfoil design using a circle sweeping representation and a performance-conditioned latent diffusion process, plus a large 200k-airfoil dataset; achieves high shape validity and ~98.41% conditioning accuracy on aerodynamic performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep generative methods for airfoils often produce geometrically invalid shapes and offer limited control over aerodynamic performance, hindering practical design integration.

Method: Introduce circle sweeping representation to constrain geometry; use a transformer to encode airfoil shapes into latent embeddings; apply a conditional diffusion model that denoises Gaussian noise into latent embeddings conditioned on target aerodynamic performance (e.g., lift/drag); assemble a new large-scale airfoil dataset (~200k) for training.

Result: Demonstrates substantial improvements in geometric validity and controllability over prior methods; achieves an average performance-conditioning accuracy of 98.41% on target aerodynamics.

Conclusion: AirfoilGen enables valid-by-construction, performance-aware airfoil generation; the large dataset supports robust training of modern generative models and the approach offers practical integration into design pipelines.

Abstract: Airfoil shape design is a fundamental task in aerospace engineering, with a direct impact on flight stability and fuel consumption. Deep learning has recently emerged as a promising tool for this task, but existing deep generative approaches remain limited in both geometric validity and physical controllability. They offer little control over the generated shapes, yielding invalid geometries, and they typically do not condition effectively on aerodynamic performance. To address these issues, this paper proposes AirfoilGen, a valid-by-construction and performance-aware latent diffusion model for airfoil. It first introduces a novel airfoil representation scheme, the circle sweeping representation, to constrain the generative process so that output shapes respect essential airfoil characteristics. It then enables explicit control over aerodynamic performance (e.g., lift and drag coefficients) by operating in a learned latent space: a transformer model encodes airfoil shapes into vector embeddings, and a conditional diffusion model denoises Gaussian noise into these latent embeddings while incorporating target aerodynamic performance. In addition, this paper presents a new dataset of over 200,000 airfoils, which is substantially larger than the widely used UIUC airfoil dataset (1,650 airfoils) and more suitable for training modern deep generative models. Experiments demonstrate that AirfoilGen enables airfoil generation with far greater geometric validity and aerodynamic performance controllability than previously achievable, with an average performance-conditioning accuracy of 98.41%.

</details>


### [192] [WaveGraphNet: Physics-Consistent Guided-Wave Damage Localization through Coupled Inverse-Forward Graph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20311)
*Vinay Sharma,Aditya Bharade,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph-based coupled inverse-forward learning (WaveGraphNet) for damage localization in CFRP plates with sparse guided-wave sensing; uses a transducer graph and physics-consistent forward regularizer to improve extrapolation to unseen regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Guided-wave damage localization with sparse sensors is underconstrained; standard methods struggle to generalize to unseen damage locations.

Method: Model transducers as graph nodes with paths defining connectivity. An inverse branch maps graph-structured spectral descriptors of differential guided-wave responses to damage coordinates; a forward branch predicts path-wise energy-deviation patterns for a candidate location. The forward branch serves as a physics-consistent regularizer during training to enforce agreement with observed energy redistribution.

Result: The graph-based approach yields strong localization for sparse sensing and improved robustness when extrapolating to held-out regions, outperforming non-graph and traditional graph baselines.

Conclusion: Coupled inverse-forward graph learning is an effective strategy for guided-wave damage localization under limited spatial coverage in CFRP plates.

Abstract: Guided-wave structural health monitoring enables damage localization in composite plates using sparse networks of bonded piezoelectric transducers. However, inferring the spatial location of defects from pitch-catch measurements remains weakly constrained when only a limited set of damage locations is available for training. As a result, models trained to predict defect locations may perform well on seen cases but generalize poorly to unseen regions of the structure.
  This paper proposes WaveGraphNet, a coupled inverse--forward graph learning framework for guided-wave damage localization in Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP) plates. The sensing layout is explicitly modeled as a graph, where transducers are represented as nodes and measured propagation paths define the graph connectivity. An inverse branch maps graph-structured spectral descriptors of differential guided-wave responses to a damage location, while a forward branch predicts the path-wise energy-deviation patterns of measured wave responses associated with a candidate location. During training, the forward branch serves as a physics-consistent regularizer, discouraging location estimates that are numerically plausible but inconsistent with the measured redistribution of wave-response energy. This coupling encourages agreement between inferred damage coordinates and the underlying wave propagation behavior.
  Within this benchmark, the proposed graph-based formulation provides a strong localization model for sparse guided-wave sensing and demonstrates improved robustness in extrapolation to held-out regions compared to both non-graph and graph baselines. These results highlight the potential of coupled inverse-forward graph learning as an effective strategy for guided-wave localization under limited spatial coverage.

</details>


### [193] [Less Data, Faster Training: repeating smaller datasets speeds up learning via sampling biases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20314)
*Jingwen Liu,Ezra Edelman,Surbhi Goel,Bingbin Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Smaller datasets with more repetitions can yield compute savings in training, driven by layer-wise growth due to sampling biases; this phenomenon is theoretically and empirically supported and can act as a deliberate inductive bias for optimization, especially in reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: A persistent small-vs-large gap shows that repeating on fewer samples reduces compute while maintaining or improving performance, a phenomenon not fully captured by existing theory. Understanding the mechanism could enable proactive, compute-efficient training strategies.

Method: The authors provide theoretical analysis of sampling biases and layer-wise growth, and validate these insights with extensive empirical studies across diverse tasks, architectures, and optimizers, using interventions that vary dataset size and repetition to observe training dynamics and compute efficiency.

Result: Theoretical and empirical evidence indicate that speedups originate from layer-wise growth enabled by sampling biases, which are more pronounced with smaller datasets. A smaller dataset with more repeats can lead to favorable optimization inductive biases rather than merely acting as a fallback under data scarcity.

Conclusion: Smaller datasets with higher repetition can be used as a proactive inductive bias to guide optimization, offering compute-efficient training strategies, with notable promise for reasoning tasks.

Abstract: This work investigates the ``small-vs-large gap'', where repeating on fewer samples can lead to compute saving during training compared to using a larger dataset. This is observed across algorithmic tasks, architectures and optimizers and cannot be explained using prior theory. We argue that the speedup comes from appropriate layer-wise growth enabled by sampling biases, which is more pronounced when the dataset size is smaller. We provide both theoretical analysis and empirical evidence from various interventions. Our results suggest that using a smaller dataset with more repetitions is not just a fallback strategy under data scarcity, but can be proactively leveraged as a favorable inductive biases for optimization, particularly in reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [194] [Causal Unlearning in Collaborative Optimization: Exact and Approximate Influence Reversal under Adversarial Contributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20341)
*Ali Mahdavi,Azadeh Zamanifar,Amirfarhad Farhadi,Omid Kashefi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HF-KCU enables fast, accurate data-deletion in federated learning by approximating influence functions with Krylov-subspace conjugate gradient, reducing complexity to O(kd) (k<<d). A causal weighting scheme localizes updates to only affected clients, with robustness to bounded Hessian/gradient perturbations, and convergence guarantees for the Krylov approximation. Validated across CNNs and ViT on CIFAR-10, MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, achieving large speedups (47.75x on CIFAR-10 with Dirichlet alpha=0.5) and minimal accuracy loss (~0.6%), plus privacy restoration evidenced by membership-inference attack results. Interpretability is preserved as updates trace back to the deleted data.



<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data deletion requests in federated learning are regulatory requirements but retraining from scratch is prohibitively expensive, especially in asynchronous, resource-constrained FL settings. There is a need for surgical, efficient deletion that preserves model utility while providing privacy guarantees and interpretability.

Method: Approximate the influence function via conjugate gradient iterations within Krylov subspaces (Krylov-based CG). Reduce computational complexity from O(d^3) to O(kd) with k << d. Introduce a causal weighting mechanism so that only clients who contributed deleted data receive parameter updates, avoiding shifts for unaffected clients. Account for bounded perturbations to the Hessian and gradient for robustness; provide convergence guarantees for the Krylov approximation error: O((k^1/2-1)/(k^1/2+1)) where k is the Hessian condition number. Demonstrated on CNNs (ResNet-18, SimpleCNN) and ViT-Lite across CIFAR-10, MNIST, Fashion-MNIST; assesses with Dirichlet partition (alpha=0.5).

Result: HF-KCU achieves 47.75x speedup over retraining on CIFAR-10 with Dirichlet alpha=0.5. Test accuracy remains within 0.60% of retrained baseline (71.16 vs 71.76%). Membership inference attacks on the forget set yield success rates of 0.499, matching retrained model, indicating effective privacy restoration. Provides convergence guarantees for Krylov error decay and demonstrates surgical updates that preserve model quality for unaffected clients and improve stability in asynchronous settings. Updates are directly traceable to the deleted data.

Conclusion: HF-KCU offers an efficient, precise, and interpretable mechanism for data deletion in production federated systems, enabling asynchronous deletion requests under constrained budgets while preserving performance for unaffected clients and maintaining privacy through near-perfect resistance to leakage via membership inference.

Abstract: Federated learning systems must support data deletion requests to comply with privacy regulations, yet retraining from scratch after each deletion is computationally prohibitive. We present HF-KCU, a method that removes a client's contribution by approximating the influence function through conjugate gradient iterations in Krylov subspaces, reducing complexity from O(d^3) to O(kd) where k<<d.A causal weighting mechanism ensures that only clients holding the deleted data receive parameter updates, preventing spurious changes to unaffected clients. Our method is designed to handle bounded adversarial perturbations to the Hessian and gradient, providing graceful degradation under realistic threat models. We validate HF-KCU across convolutional (ResNet-18, SimpleCNN) and transformer (ViT-Lite) architectures on CIFAR-10, MNIST, and Fashion-MNIST. On CIFAR-10 under Dirichlet (alpha=0.5) partitioning, HF-KCU achieves 47.75 times speedup over retraining while maintaining test accuracy within 0.60% of the rational baseline(71.16 vs 71.76 %). Membership inference attacks on the forget set yield success rates of 0.499 matching the retrained model and confirming effective privacy restoration. We provide convergence guarantees showing that the Krylov approximation error decreases as O((k ^1/2-1)/(k^1/2+1)) where k is the Hessian condition number. The causal weighting mechanism ensures surgical updates, where only clients holding deleted data are modified, preserving model quality for unaffected participants and avoiding the instability of gradient-based approaches in asynchronous federated settings. This design provides interpretability as each update is directly traceable to the influence of the deleted data. The method's efficiency and precision make it suitable for production federated systems where deletion requests arrive asynchronously and computational budgets are constrained.

</details>


### [195] [Symmetrization of Loss Functions for Robust Training of Neural Networks in the Presence of Noisy Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20347)
*Alexandre Lemire Paquin,Brahim Chaib-Draa,Philippe Giguère*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a symmetric decomposition of multi-class losses; identifies the unique convex symmetric loss (multiclass unhinged) via symmetrization of cross-entropy; introduces SGCE and alpha-MAE interpolations with beta-smoothness; shows competitive results on noisy-label benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Labeling data for supervised learning is costly and error-prone, motivating robust loss designs. Symmetry provides theoretical guarantees against label noise. The work analyzes how a loss can be decomposed into symmetric and class-insensitive parts, and how symmetrization yields a principled multi-class robust loss.

Method: Decompose any multi-class loss into a symmetric component and a class-insensitive term. Symmetrize cross-entropy to obtain a linear multi-class extension of the unhinged loss. Coefficients in the multi-class case must satisfy the symmetry condition; under suitable assumptions, prove the multi-class unhinged loss is the unique convex symmetric loss. Show that the linear approximation of any symmetric loss around equal-score vectors equals the multi-class unhinged loss. Introduce SGCE and alpha-MAE, two losses interpolating between the multi-class unhinged loss and MAE with tunable beta-smoothness.

Result: The multi-class unhinged loss is established as the unique convex symmetric loss under the stated assumptions. The linear (local) approximation property links any symmetric loss to the unhinged form. Empirical results on standard noisy-label benchmarks indicate competitive performance relative to existing robust losses.

Conclusion: The symmetry-based framework clarifies robustness properties of multi-class losses, deriving a principled unique convex symmetric loss and practical interpolants (SGCE, alpha-MAE) with controllable smoothness, backed by competitive empirical results.

Abstract: Labeling a training set is often expensive and susceptible to errors, making the design of robust loss functions for label noise an important problem. The symmetry condition provides theoretical guarantees for robustness to such noise. In this work, we study a symmetrization method arising from the unique decomposition of any multi-class loss function into a symmetric component and a class-insensitive term. In particular, symmetrizing the cross-entropy loss leads to a linear multi-class extension of the unhinged loss. Unlike in the binary case, the multi-class version must have specific coefficients in order to satisfy the symmetry condition. Under suitable assumptions, we show that this multi-class unhinged loss is the unique convex multi-class symmetric loss. We also show that it has a fundamental local role: the linear approximation of any symmetric loss around score vectors with equal components is equivalent to the multi-class unhinged loss. We then introduce SGCE and alpha-MAE, two loss functions that interpolate between the multi-class unhinged loss and the Mean Absolute Error while allowing control of the beta-smoothness of the loss. Experiments on standard noisy-label benchmarks show competitive performance compared with existing robust loss functions.

</details>


### [196] [Consistently Informative Soft-Label Temperature for Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20357)
*Hoang-Chau Luong,Nghia Van Vo,Kaiqi Zhao,Lingwei Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CIST introduces sample-wise adaptive temperatures for both teacher and student in knowledge distillation to produce consistently informative soft labels, reweighting the distillation loss by teacher confidence and student difficulty, with theoretical justification and empirical gains across vision and language tasks over standard KD with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard KD with a fixed global temperature yields globally inconsistent soft labels due to varying logit scales and learning difficulties across samples, and rigid teacher–student logit alignment when using the same temperature for both models.

Method: Assign separate, per-sample adaptive temperatures to both teacher and student in the distillation objective. Use these temperatures to smooth predictions, reweight the KD loss by teacher confidence and student learning progress, and provide theoretical insight that teacher-label entropy is governed by the max logit to temperature ratio.

Result: Empirically, CIST reduces entropy inconsistency caused by fixed temperature and achieves consistent improvements over standard KD and strong baselines in both vision and language distillation, with negligible computational overhead.

Conclusion: Adaptive, sample-wise smoothing via CIST yields more informative soft labels, relaxes rigid logit-scale matching, and improves KD performance in a theory-supported and practically efficient manner.

Abstract: Knowledge distillation (KD) transfers knowledge from a high-capacity teacher to a compact student by matching their predictive distributions, with temperature scaling serving as a central mechanism for smoothing teacher predictions and exposing informative "dark knowledge" beyond the hard label. However, the standard fixed-temperature design is inherently sample-agnostic. Since samples differ in logit scale and learning difficulty, a single global temperature produces teacher soft labels with highly inconsistent entropy: some predictions remain overly sharp and provide limited inter-class information, whereas others become over-smoothed and lose class-discriminative information. Moreover, sharing the same temperature between teacher and student further imposes rigid logit-scale alignment despite their capacity mismatch. To address these limitations, we propose CIST (Consistently Informative Soft-label Temperature), which assigns separate sample-wise adaptive temperatures to the teacher and student. This design produces consistently informative teacher soft labels while relaxing rigid teacher--student logit-scale matching. It also reweights the distillation objective according to teacher confidence and student learning difficulty. Theoretically, we show that teacher-label entropy is largely governed by the ratio between the maximum teacher logit and the temperature, providing a principled basis for adaptive smoothing. Empirically, CIST mitigates the inconsistency induced by fixed temperature, and experiments on both vision and language distillation tasks show consistent improvements over standard KD and strong baselines with negligible computational overhead.

</details>


### [197] [Nonlocal operator learning for fMRI encoding and decoding tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20389)
*Andreas Kramer,Saugat Acharya,Alice Giola,Emanuele Zappala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent neural integral operators with fixed-point iterations improve fMRI decoding/encoding by exploiting nonlocal spatiotemporal context; longer temporal windows yield more structured latent representations and better performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: fMRI data possess high-dimensional spatiotemporal structure that challenges prediction and decoding; capturing distributed nonlocal dependencies and longer temporal context may enhance modeling of brain dynamics.

Method: Propose latent neural integral operator framework that performs fixed-point iterations in an auxiliary space; classification and stimulus prediction are performed via a decoder; evaluate on two open-source fMRI datasets for decoding stimuli and encoding fMRI dynamics from stimulus representations; compare short vs long temporal windows and visual cortex vs whole-brain recordings.

Result: Longer temporal windows generally improve results and produce more structured latent representations; decoding yields clearer class separation in latent space than in raw data; encoding gains with longer windows though absolute performance remains moderate due to task difficulty; benefits observed across tasks and datasets.

Conclusion: Neural integral operators are a promising framework for modeling fMRI dynamics; broader spatiotemporal context benefits prediction and representation learning; effective modeling of distributed nonlocal brain dynamics requires architectures designed to capture such dependencies.

Abstract: Functional MRI data exhibit high-dimensional spatiotemporal structure, making both prediction and decoding challenging. In this work, we investigate neural integral-operator-based models for encoding and decoding tasks in fMRI, with particular emphasis on the role of nonlocal spatiotemporal context. We implement a latent neural integral operator framework that performs fixed point iterations in an auxiliary space from which classification and stimuli prediction is performed via a decoder. We evaluate our model on two open-source fMRI datasets.
  Our experiments examine both decoding of stimuli from fMRI recordings and encoding of fMRI dynamics from stimulus representations. A main focus is the effect of spatiotemporal context: we systematically compare short and long temporal windows, as well as the use of visual cortex vs whole brain recordings, and analyze their influence on performance and latent-space geometry. Across tasks and datasets, larger temporal windows generally improve results and produce more structured learned representations. In decoding experiments, the learned latent space often provides clearer class separation than the raw data. In encoding experiments, although absolute performance remains moderate due to the difficulty of the task, longer temporal windows still yield consistent gains.
  These findings suggest that neural integral operators provide a promising framework for modeling fMRI dynamics and that broader spatiotemporal context can be beneficial for both prediction and representation learning. More broadly, the results indicate that exploiting distributed nonlocal structure in brain dynamics requires model architectures specifically designed to capture such dependencies.

</details>


### [198] [Score-Based Causal Discovery of Latent Variable Causal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20396)
*Ignavier Ng,Xinshuai Dong,Haoyue Dai,Biwei Huang,Peter Spirtes,Kun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces score-based causal structure learning for models with latent variables, with identifiability guarantees, score equivalence, and consistency, plus exact and continuous scoring schemes and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identifying latent variables and their causal relationships is crucial across disciplines; constraint-based methods suffer from order-dependency and error propagation, while extending score-based learning like GES to latent-variable settings is challenging.

Method: Formulates a scoring function for latent-variable causal models that attains score equivalence and consistency; characterizes the degrees of freedom of the marginal distribution over observed variables under multiple structural assumptions; develops both exact and continuous score-based learning procedures; offers a unified view linking constraint-based methods to latent-variable settings.

Result: Establishes identifiability guarantees, score equivalence, and consistency for latent-variable structure learning; provides a principled framework with exact and continuous scoring schemes; empirical experiments validate effectiveness.

Conclusion: The work provides a unified, theoretically grounded framework for learning latent-variable causal graphs using score-based methods, bridging existing constraint-based approaches and enabling reliable structure discovery under various latent-structure assumptions.

Abstract: Identifying latent variables and the causal structure involving them is essential across various scientific fields. While many existing works fall under the category of constraint-based methods (with e.g. conditional independence or rank deficiency tests), they may face empirical challenges such as testing-order dependency, error propagation, and choosing an appropriate significance level. These issues can potentially be mitigated by properly designed score-based methods, such as Greedy Equivalence Search (GES) (Chickering, 2002) in the specific setting without latent variables. Yet, formulating score-based methods with latent variables is highly challenging. In this work, we develop score-based methods that are capable of identifying causal structures containing causally-related latent variables with identifiability guarantees. Specifically, we show that a properly formulated scoring function can achieve score equivalence and consistency for structure learning of latent variable causal models. We further provide a characterization of the degrees of freedom for the marginal over the observed variables under multiple structural assumptions considered in the literature, and accordingly develop both exact and continuous score-based methods.
