This offers a unified view of several existing constraint-based methods with different structural assumptions. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

</details>


### [199] [Decomposing MXFP4 quantization error for LLM reinforcement learning: reducible bias, recoverable deadzone, and an irreducible floor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20402)
*Xiaocan Li,Shiliang Wu,Zheng Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decomposes MXFP4 quantization error into three additive components—scale bias from power-of-two rounding, deadzone truncation from truncating small values, and grid noise from 4-bit rounding. Each component drives a distinct RL-training failure mode; targeted corrections (Macro-block scaling, Outlier Fallback, Adaptive Quantization Noise) mitigate them. On Qwen2.5-3B dense and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base MoE, BF16 accuracy is recovered within 0.7% and 3.0%, respectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MXFP4 quantization error in RL post-training of LLMs is typically treated as a single noise term, obscuring distinct mechanisms by which error affects training. A decomposition clarifies the failure modes and enables targeted remediation.

Method: Provide an exact three-way decomposition of quantization error into scale bias, deadzone truncation, and grid noise; empirically validate the decomposition and its associated failure modes; implement RL-targeted corrections—Macro-block scaling, Outlier Fallback, and Adaptive Quantization Noise (AQN); evaluate on Qwen2.5-3B dense and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base MoE models.

Result: Each component dominates a distinct RL failure mode: scale bias affects gradient accuracy via the backward pass, deadzone truncation degrades rollout quality, and grid noise increases policy entropy. Targeted corrections recover BF16 accuracy to within 0.7% (BF16 baseline) on Qwen2.5-3B dense and within 3.0% on Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base MoE.

Conclusion: A component-aware analysis of MXFP4 quantization error yields effective, mode-specific corrections that restore training fidelity in RL post-training scenarios for large LLMs.

Abstract: MXFP4 arithmetic can dramatically accelerate reinforcement learning (RL) post-training of large language models (LLMs), yet the quantization error introduces severe accuracy degradation. Existing work treats the quantization error as a monolithic noise term, missing the distinct mechanisms upon interpreting how quantization error damages training. We prove an exact three-way decomposition of quantization error and show how each component dominates a distinct RL training pathway. Our theoretical and empirical analysis decomposes the MXFP4 quantization error into three additive components: "scale bias" from power-of-two rounding, "deadzone truncation" from zeroing small values, and "grid noise" from rounding to the nearest 4-bit grid. Each component dominates a distinct RL failure mode: scale bias accumulates multiplicatively through the backward pass, affecting gradient accuracy; deadzone truncation degrades rollout quality; and grid noise raises the policy's entropy. We combine corrections that are RL failure mode-targeted but not component-exclusive: Macro-block scaling to reduce scale bias, Outlier Fallback recovers deadzone entries, but also partially reduces scale bias induced error, and Adaptive Quantization Noise (AQN) for controlling the policy entropy. On Qwen2.5-3B dense and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base mixture-of-experts model, the targeted corrections recover BF16 accuracy to within 0.7% and 3.0% respectively.

</details>


### [200] [Spectral Souping: A Unified Framework for Online Preference Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20408)
*Yinlam Chow,Guy Tennenholtz,Ted Yun,James Harrison,Arthur Gretton,Andre Barreto,Bo Dai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spectral Souping: learn a basis of specialized policies offline for fine-grained preferences and online 'soup' those policies by merging outputs or parameters during inference, leveraging a universal spectral representation to efficiently adapt LLMs to individual user preferences without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mismatch between aggregated human preferences in RLHF and diverse/conflicting individual user needs, enabling fast, scalable personalisation without costly online retraining.

Method: Two-phase approach: offline learning of a basis of specialized policies across distinct preference dimensions; online adaptation by merging (souping) outputs or parameters of these policies at inference time to tailor behavior to a user.

Result: Benchmarks show significant improvements over state-of-the-art RLHF baselines, with scalable and computationally efficient adaptation to individual preferences.

Conclusion: Spectral Souping offers a practical framework for fast, online personalization of LLMs by exploiting a universal spectral representation to merge specialized policies, reducing retraining costs while achieving better alignment with diverse user needs.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) effectively aligns Large Language Models (LLMs) with aggregate human preferences but often fails to address the diverse and conflicting needs of individual users. To overcome this issue, we introduce Spectral Souping, a unified framework for efficient, online preference alignment. Our contribution is the discovery of a universal spectral representation within LLMs, which is proven to be highly amenable to model merging. This theoretical insight enables a two-phase methodology: we first learn a basis of specialized policies offline, each focused on a distinct, fine-grained preference dimension. An online adaptation algorithm then efficiently ``soups'' these policies at inference time, either by merging their outputs or parameters, enabling rapid model adaptation without the need for costly online retraining w.r.t. tailored preference rewards. Experiments on online preference alignment benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves significant performance improvements over existing state-of-the-art approaches, presenting a scalable and computationally efficient solution for dynamically adapting LLMs to individual user preferences.

</details>


### [201] [Supervised Latent Restructuring for Small-Data Quantum Learning in Plant Phenomics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20413)
*Alakananda Mitra,David H. Fleisher,Vangimalla Reddy,Chittaranjan Ray*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compressing 1280-d deep embeddings to 11-d supervised latent improves linear separability but offers mixed results for nonlinear classifiers and quantum kernel methods under small-data constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In extremely small-data, high-dimensional biological data, kernel methods struggle to preserve class structure after dimensionality reduction. The study investigates whether a supervised latent restructuring can recover discriminative geometry and support quantum kernel learning.

Method: Pipeline: 1280-d deep image embeddings -> PCA to 64 -> LDA to 11 (supervised latent) -> GPU-accelerated Quantum Kernel Alignment (QKA) on NVIDIA L40S. Evaluate with Linear SVM, XGBoost, RBF-SVM, and Random Forest; assess separability with Silhouette coefficient.

Result: Silhouette improves from 0.003 (raw) and -0.006 (PCA-64) to 0.197 in the LDA-11 space. Linear SVM and XGBoost improve in the restructured space; RBF-SVM and Random Forest degrade at the 11-dimensional bottleneck. QKA remains challenging under a constrained optimization budget; latent geometry is not alone sufficient for strong trainable quantum performance.

Conclusion: Latent geometry is a central design variable for small-data quantum learning, but aggressive compression can hinder recovery of nonlinear discriminative structure; achieving practical quantum advantage requires reconsidering representation strategies beyond simple linear separability.

Abstract: High-dimensional biological data often exhibit a severe mismatch between feature dimensionality and sample size, making reliable classification difficult in extremely small-data regimes. In these settings, kernel methods can lose discriminative power when latent compression fails to preserve class-separating structure. We study this problem in fine-grained plant phenomics and propose a hybrid workflow that compresses 1280-dimensional deep image embeddings into a 64-dimensional PCA space and then restructures them into an 11-dimensional supervised latent space using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), followed by GPU-accelerated Quantum Kernel Alignment (QKA) on NVIDIA L40S hardware. Empirically, supervised latent restructuring substantially improves the geometric separability of the compressed representation, increasing the Silhouette coefficient from 0.003 in the raw embedding space and -0.006 in PCA-64 to 0.197 in the supervised LDA-11 space. However, downstream classical evaluation reveals a clear compression trade-off: Linear SVM and XGBoost improve in the restructured latent space, whereas RBF-SVM and Random Forest degrade under the same 11-dimensional bottleneck. Under a constrained optimization budget, QKA in this regime remains challenging, indicating that latent geometry alone is not sufficient for strong trainable quantum performance. These findings position representation geometry as a central design variable in small-data quantum learning and expose the practical difficulty of recovering nonlinear discriminative structure from aggressively compressed biological representations.

</details>


### [202] [Miller-Index-Based Latent Crystallographic Fracture Plane Reasoning with Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20416)
*Qinwu Xu,Yifan Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MLLMs can use Miller-index latent priors to reason about fracture planes, performing latent inference when physics supports it and abstaining when not, across synthetic and real fracture data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine if multimodal LLMs can leverage structured latent representations and physics-based priors for fracture geometry, using crystallographic plane indices (Miller indices) as a latent variable to guide reasoning.

Method: Formulate z=(h,k,l) as a latent variable for idealized planar fractures. Evaluate two capabilities: latent inference (map visuals to plane hypotheses under physical constraints) and latent applicability assessment (decide if such a latent representation is meaningful for a given fracture image). Test on synthetic data, controlled 2D–3D geometric pairs, and real fracture images across ceramics, glass, metals, and concrete. Assess inference accuracy and the ability to reject non-physical latent representations.

Result: MLLMs reliably perform latent inference in idealized settings and can reject the latent representation when underlying physics do not support it, across multiple material classes.

Conclusion: MLLMs can act as physics-aware reasoning systems conditioned on structured latent priors, provided the domain of validity is explicitly modeled.

Abstract: We study whether multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can leverage crystallographic plane indices (Miller indices) as a structured latent representation for reasoning about fracture geometry. We formulate Miller indices $z = (h,k,l)$ as a latent variable governing idealized planar fracture and evaluate two complementary capabilities: (i) latent inference, where the model maps visual observations to plane hypotheses under physically valid conditions, and (ii) latent applicability assessment, where the model determines whether such a representation is meaningful for a given fracture image.
  Through extensive experiments spanning synthetic data, controlled 2D--3D geometric pairs, and real-world fracture images across multiple material classes -- including ceramics, glass, metals, and concrete -- we show that MLLMs can reliably perform latent inference in idealized settings and, critically, can reject the latent representation when the underlying physics does not support it. These results suggest that MLLMs can act as physics-aware reasoning systems conditioned on structured latent priors, provided that the domain of validity is explicitly modeled.

</details>


### [203] [Can Conversational XAI Improve User Performance? An Experimental Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20439)
*Sven Kruschel,Julian Rosenberger,Lasse Bohlen,Mathias Kraus,Patrick Zschech*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An experimental design to evaluate explanation assistance in XAI, comparing conversational vs Q&A-based support; findings show users outperform the model in both conditions, with no difference between assistance types; suggests focusing on engagement and mechanisms in future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address a gap where XAI explanations often fail to improve objective performance; assess whether explanation-based assistance, particularly conversational interfaces, can boost prediction accuracy, model understanding, and error identification.

Method: Utilizes an explainable-by-design prediction model; creates conditions where users can outsmart the model by identifying and compensating for systematic errors; compares two assistance modes—conversational and Q&A-based—across measures of prediction accuracy, model understanding, and error identification; preliminary study with N=42.

Result: Both assistance types significantly outperformed the model; no performance differences between conversational and Q&A-based support; engagement was modest.

Conclusion: The findings inform refinements for the full study, including enhanced engagement interventions and deeper investigation into the mechanisms that drive improved human predictions; future work should clarify when and why conversational vs Q&A assistance may differ in effectiveness.

Abstract: Explainable AI (XAI) techniques aim to provide insights into predictive models and enhance user performance, yet they often fall short of these expectations. Conversational XAI assistants promise to overcome such limitations, but empirical evidence on their impact on objective performance measures remains limited. We propose an experimental design for evaluating explanation assistance through prediction accuracy, model understanding, and error identification. Using an explainable-by-design prediction model, we create conditions where users can outperform the model by identifying and compensating for systematic errors. We compare conversational assistance against Q&A-based assistance to assess which better supports users in working with model explanations. Preliminary results from testing our experimental design show that participants (N=42) in both treatments significantly outperformed the model but reveal no performance differences between assistance types and modest engagement overall. These findings inform refinements for our planned full study, including enhanced engagement interventions and investigation of the mechanisms driving improved predictions.

</details>


### [204] [Group-Algebraic Tensors: Provably-optimal Equivariant Learning and Physical Symmetry Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20440)
*Paulina Hoyos,Shashanka Ubaru,Dongsung Huh,Vasileios Kalantzis,Kenneth L. Clarkson,Misha Kilmer,Haim Avron,Lior Horesh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Star_G tensor algebra endows a finite group G with a multiplication rule so equivariance is built into the algebra; it yields optimal, symmetry-preserving decompositions and data-driven symmetry discovery, with a Lean formalization and a Kronecker factorization to combine symmetries. Empirically recovers Wigner–Eckart rules on QM9 and achieves far fewer parameters than MLPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Make equivariance an intrinsic algebraic property, enabling provably optimal symmetry-preserving compression, per-irrep interpretability, and data-driven symmetry discovery beyond architectural constraints.

Method: Define star_G algebra; prove Eckart–Young optimality for star_G-SVD (polynomial-time, symmetry-preserving approximation); establish a Kronecker factorization replacing F_G with F_{G1}⊗F_{G2} to compose multiple symmetries; provide a Lean 4 formalization (≈600 lines).

Result: Achieves closed-form per-irreducible-representation decomposition of predictions; enables data-driven discovery of the symmetry group; recovers Wigner–Eckart selection rules from data on QM9’s chiral octahedral subgroup; full QM9 results show star_G-SVD with ridge regression uses ~50–90× fewer parameters than parameter-matched MLPs.

Conclusion: Algebraic equivariance provides capabilities beyond architectural equivariance, delivering provably optimal symmetry-preserving compression, per-irrep interpretability, and data-driven symmetry discovery; effective empirical demonstration on molecular property predictions demonstrates fundamental advantages over standard ENNs.

Abstract: We introduce the $\star_G$ tensor algebra, in which any finite group $G$ defines the multiplication rule, making equivariance an intrinsic algebraic property rather than an architectural constraint. The framework rests on three machine-verified theoretical pillars: (i)~an Eckart-Young optimality guarantee for the $\star_G$-SVD: the first such result for symmetry-preserving tensor approximation, exact and polynomial-time; (ii)~a Kronecker factorization that composes multiple symmetries by replacing $F_G$ with $F_{G_1} \otimes F_{G_2}$ with no architectural redesign; and (iii)~a 600-line Lean~4 formalization of the $\star_G$ algebra. The framework provides capabilities that equivariant neural networks (ENNs) structurally cannot: a closed-form per-irreducible-representation decomposition of every prediction, and data-driven discovery of the symmetry group that best fits a dataset. As a non-trivial empirical demonstration, decomposing QM9 molecular geometry over the chiral octahedral subgroup of SO(3) recovers the Wigner--Eckart selection rules of angular momentum from data alone, with no quantum mechanical input: scalar properties are A$_1$-dominated, dipole components are T$_1$-dominated, the isotropic polarizability is uniquely insensitive to $l\!=\!1$ as the rank-2-trace decomposition $l\!=\!0 \oplus l\!=\!2$ requires, and the T$_1$/A$_1$ predictive-power ratio separates vector observables from scalar observables by a factor of five. On full QM9 (130{,}831 molecules), $\star_G$-SVD with ridge regression provides closed form predictions at $\sim50-90\times$ fewer parameters than parameter-matched MLPs. Algebraic equivariance thus complements architectural equivariance not as a faster-better-cheaper alternative but as a different mathematical affordance: provably-optimal symmetry-preserving compression, per-irrep interpretability, and data-driven physical discovery.

</details>


### [205] [Weight Decay Regimes in Grokking Transformers: Cheap Online Diagnostics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20441)
*Lucky Verma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Weight decay in small transformers trained on modular arithmetic acts as a scalar control parameter that governs sharp transitions among memorization, developmental grokking, and collapse. Two cheap online diagnostics (mean pairwise attention-head cosine similarity and entropy standard deviation) track training dynamics from attention activations. Across 11 conditions and three model scales (0.82M–85M params), a logistic boundary at λ_c ≈ 0.0158 (CI [0.0109, 0.0200], N=210) and a power-law exponent ν ≈ 0.757 (CI [0.725, 0.799]) separate memorization from grokking; universality remains unresolved. Findings replicate across horizon-matched multi-task replication and across architectures, with λ_c values architecture-dependent; paired reinitialization affects Phase-2 amplitude, while weight-norm clipping does not. Conclusions are scoped to modular arithmetic in small transformer attention models; broader universality/class claims require further finite-size scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify a low-cost, generalizable control parameter and online diagnostics for phase-like transitions in transformer training on structured modular arithmetic tasks, and assess robustness across architectures and settings.

Method: Sweep weight decay λ across 11 experimental conditions and 3 model scales (0.82M–85M params). Monitor online diagnostics from attention activations: mean pairwise attention-head cosine similarity and entropy standard deviation. Fit a near-transition logistic model to locate the memorization→development boundary (λ_c) and a power-law to extract an empirical exponent ν. Validate findings via horizon-matched multi-task replication (n=280, four modular ops) and paired attention-head re-initialization at λ=0.05, plus weight-norm clipping and cross-architecture probes (4L MLP, 4L LSTM, 4L Mamba; n=70 each). Scope checks: main claims about modular arithmetic in small transformer attention models; non-attention experiments are scope probes; universality-class claims deferred to future work.

Result: Weight decay acts as the empirical control parameter separating memorization, developmental grokking, and collapse. The boundary is best described by a near-transition logistic fit with λ_c ≈ 0.0158 (CI [0.0109, 0.0200], N=210) and a power-law fit with ν ≈ 0.757 (CI [0.725, 0.799]). Reference exponents (ν=1/2 or ≈0.63 for 3D Ising) fall outside the empirical CI under the four-bin grid, so ν is reported as empirical without a universal-class claim yet. A horizon-matched replication preserves the weight-decay control pattern; paired attention-head reinitialization at λ=0.05 significantly changes Phase-2 amplitude (Cohen's d = -1.190, p = 4.5e-3, n=10), while weight-norm clipping is ineffective. Cross-architecture probes (4L MLP, 4L LSTM, 4L Mamba; n=70 per probe) replicate the transition with architecture-specific λ_c values. Conclusions are constrained to modular arithmetic in small transformer attention models; broader universality claims require further finite-size-scaling work.

Conclusion: Weight decay serves as an empirical, low-cost control parameter organizing training dynamics into memorization, grokking, and collapse in modular arithmetic tasks, with two online attention-based diagnostics providing real-time tracking. While the λ_c and ν estimates are robust across several settings, universal critical behavior remains unestablished pending deeper finite-size scaling and denser experiments; the approach is validated across tasks and architectures within the stated scope.

Abstract: Transformers trained on modular arithmetic exhibit sharp transitions between memorization, generalization, and collapse. We show that weight decay acts as a scalar empirical control parameter for these regimes, and introduce two cheap online diagnostics, mean pairwise attention-head cosine similarity and entropy standard deviation, that track training dynamics from attention activations alone and complement loss-landscape diagnostics at lower compute cost. Across eleven experimental conditions and three model scales (0.82M to 85M parameters), the weight-decay axis separates memorization, developmental grokking, and collapse. A near-transition logistic fit localizes the memorization-to-developmental boundary at $λ_c=0.0158$ (95% CI [0.0109, 0.0200], N=210); a power-law fit gives an empirical exponent $ν=0.757$ (CI [0.725, 0.799]). Reference exponents $ν=1/2$ and 3D Ising $ν\approx 0.63$ lie outside this empirical CI under our four-bin grid, so we report $ν$ as empirical and defer universality-class identification to denser finite-size-scaling work. A horizon-matched multi-task replication (n=280, four modular operations) preserves the weight-decay control pattern; a paired attention-head re-initialization experiment at $λ=0.05$ changes Phase-2 amplitude (Cohen's $d=-1.190$, n=10, $p_t=4.5 \times 10^{-3}$), while matched weight-norm clipping does not. Three cross-architecture probes (4L MLP, 4L LSTM, and 4L Mamba; each n=70) replicate the weight-decay-controlled transition with architecture-specific $λ_c$ values. Main diagnostic claims are scoped to modular arithmetic in small transformer attention models; the non-attention experiments are scope probes, and architecture-wide, language-model, and universality-class claims are out of scope.

</details>


### [206] [LLM Pretraining Shapes a Generalizable Manifold: Insights into Cross-Modal Transfer to Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20449)
*Alexis Roger,Prateek Humane,Zhenghan Tai,Gwen Legate,Andrei Mircea,Vasilii Feofanov,Irina Rish*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Language pretraining builds a reusable manifold that enables time-series forecasting without paired data; finetuning aligns to task directions, suggesting LLM priors provide geometric structure for time-series dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand cross-modal transfer from language-model priors to time-series forecasting and whether predictive structure exists before fine-tuning.

Method: Linear probes on frozen LLM states to decode time-series trajectories without paired supervision; retrieval in the projected latent space for forecasting; analysis of pretrained initialization (coherent gradients, anisotropic loss landscape); fine-tuning as low-dimensional alignment with evidence from low-rank updates, subspace alignment, and shared features for periodicity, trend, and repetition.

Result: A linear probe can decode plausible time-series dynamics from frozen LLM representations; retrieval in the learned projected space yields competitive forecasts; pretrained initialization improves optimization (coherent gradients, anisotropic loss landscape); fine-tuning reuses existing directions rather than learning temporal primitives, evidenced by low-rank updates and subspace alignment; shared features capture periodicity, trend, and repetition.

Conclusion: Language pretraining constructs a geometric manifold that preconditions time-series training; finetuning projects numerical dynamics onto task-relevant directions, supporting a geometric account of LLM-to-time-series transfer.

Abstract: Can language-pretrained transformers become effective time-series forecasters, and why? In this paper, we show that cross-modal transfer arises because language pretraining preconditions time series training with a reusable manifold. A linear probe on frozen LLM states decodes realistic time-series trajectories without paired supervision, and retrieval in this projected space yields competitive forecasts, showing that structure and dynamics exist before finetuning. Pretrained initialization also improves optimization, producing coherent gradients and a highly anisotropic loss landscape unlike random initialization. Finetuning then acts as low-dimensional alignment, reusing existing directions rather than learning temporal primitives from scratch, as evidenced by low-rank updates, subspace alignment, and shared features for periodicity, trend, and repetition. Together, these results support a geometric account of LLM-to-time-series transfer: language pretraining builds the manifold, and finetuning projects numerical dynamics onto task-relevant directions.

</details>


### [207] [Mechanistic Interpretability for Learning Assurance of a Vision-Based Landing System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20607)
*Romeo Valentin,Olivia Beyer Bruvik,Marc R. Schlichting,Mykel J. Kochenderfer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A representation-level assurance approach for a vision-based runway landing system is proposed by enforcing content–style separation in a vision transformer; per-patch embeddings are decomposed into interpretable atoms via K-SVD, showing contentful atoms align with runway structure and stylistic atoms with appearance; a novel OOMS runtime assurance monitors the model's situation representation, providing the first concrete representation-level evidence in line with EASA guidance and advocating mechanistic interpretability for safety cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EASA learning-assurance guidance requires evidence that data-driven aviation systems build and monitor their own situation representations; neural network evidence for this requirement is lacking.

Method: Train a vision transformer on runway keypoint regression using the LARDv2 dataset; decompose per-patch embeddings with K-SVD into interpretable atoms; qualitatively visualize and assess the alignment of contentful vs stylistic atoms with task and domain; analyze the regression head weights; define and implement out-of-model-scope (OOMS) monitoring to track the model's situation representation at test and runtime.

Result: Contentful atoms track runway structure; stylistic atoms capture domain appearance; the regression head relies mostly on contentful atoms; OOMS provides runtime and test-time monitoring of representation, offering representation-level evidence for assurance; first concrete demonstration of the required evidence for EASA guidance.

Conclusion: This work delivers the first concrete piece of representation-level evidence demanded by EASA learning-assurance guidance and argues that mechanistic interpretability can be a practical building block for aviation safety cases; OOMS complements existing design-domain and output-space monitoring.

Abstract: EASA's learning-assurance guidance requires data-driven aviation systems to build and monitor their own situation representation, yet for neural networks the technical means to provide such evidence remain an open problem. We address this gap for a vision-based aircraft landing system: we propose that a minimally assurable model must at least be shown to separate content from style in its own situation representation. Showing that the model's predictions then rely largely on the contentful representation components leads to a concrete assurance path. To demonstrate this assurance path on a concrete model we train a vision transformer model for runway keypoint regression on the LARDv2 dataset. The model, which acts as the subject for our assurance demonstration, produces per-patch embeddings that we decompose into interpretable atoms via K-SVD sparse dictionary learning. A qualitative visualization confirms that contentful atoms track task-relevant runway structure and stylistic atoms track domain-specific appearance, and the regression head is shown to place almost all of its linear weight on contentful atoms. We further build on the content/style separation and define out-of-model-scope (OOMS) detection, a novel runtime assurance approach directly monitoring the model's situation representation. OOMS monitoring is complementary to operational design domain and output-space out-of-distribution monitoring and addresses concrete requirements of the recent EASA guidance. By directly analyzing a model's situation representation both at test time and runtime, this work delivers the first concrete piece of the representation-level evidence that EASA learning-assurance guidance demands, and points to mechanistic interpretability as a practical building block of future aviation safety cases.

</details>


### [208] [CASCADE Conformal Prediction: Uncertainty-Adaptive Prediction Intervals for Two-Stage Clinical Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20468)
*Ricardo Diaz-Rincon,Muxuan Liang,Adolfo Ramirez-Zamora,Benjamin Shickel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A cascade conformal prediction framework CASCADE uses epistemic uncertainty from a PD medication-change classifier to adapt predictive intervals for LEDD changes, yielding narrower intervals for confident cases and expanded intervals for uncertain cases, with calibrated coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty quantification for LEDD predictions often treats all decisions equally. There is a need for calibrated, adaptive prediction intervals that reflect both the likelihood of a medication change and the magnitude of the predicted change.

Method: CASCADE propagates epistemic uncertainty from a screening classifier to dynamically scale the prediction intervals of a secondary regression task. It uses Venn-Abers multi-probabilistic uncertainty mapped directly to non-conformity scores, enabling a cascade: classification uncertainty informs regression interval width rather than relying on auxiliary residual regression.

Result: The framework achieves highly efficient, narrower intervals for confident patients (38.9% narrower than standard conformal baselines) while expanding intervals to maintain robust coverage for uncertain cases, enabling continuous risk adaptation.

Conclusion: CASCADE bridges discrete clinical decision-making and continuous dose forecasting in PD by delivering calibrated, adaptive prediction intervals that reflect both the decision task and the regression outcome.

Abstract: Effective medication management in Parkinson's Disease (PD) is challenging due to heterogeneous disease progression, variable patient response, and medication side effects. While AI models can forecast levodopa equivalent daily dose (LEDD) as a measure of medication needs, standard uncertainty quantification often fails to communicate the reliability of these predictions, treating high and low confidence clinical decisions identically. We introduce CASCADE (Calibrated Adaptive Scaling via Conformal And Distributional Estimation), a novel conformal prediction framework that propagates epistemic uncertainty from a screening classifier to adapt downstream predictions. Unlike standard conformal methods that rely on auxiliary residual regression, we leverage epistemic uncertainty from a primary classification task (identifying whether a medication change is needed) to dynamically scale the prediction intervals of a secondary regression task (predicting how much change). By mapping Venn-Abers multi-probabilistic uncertainty directly to non-conformity scores, our framework achieves continuous risk adaptation. We demonstrate that this ``cascade effect'' produces highly efficient intervals for confident patients (38.9% narrower than standard conformal baselines) while automatically expanding intervals to ensure robust coverage for uncertain cases, bridging the gap between discrete clinical decision-making and continuous dose forecasting in PD.

</details>


### [209] [Design for Manufacturing: A Manufacturability Knowledge-Integrated Reinforcement Learning Framework for Free-Form Pipe Routing in Aeroengines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20644)
*Caicheng Wang,Zili Wang,Shuyou Zhang,Yongzhe Xiang,Zheyi Li,Liangyou Li,Jianrong Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FPRO is a manufacturability-aware reinforcement-learning framework for free-form aeroengine pipe routing. It encodes paths as curvature and torsion in a Frenet frame, enforces manufacturability constraints, optimizes with PPO, and maps results to six-axis bending die trajectories. It achieves collision-free, manufacturable, smoother paths with faster convergence and close real-world geometry, outperforming Cartesian methods and RL baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current pipe routing is decoupled from downstream manufacturing, causing labor-intensive iteration to achieve manufacturability. A unified design–manufacturing framework is needed to reduce iterations and ensure manufacturability.

Method: Represent the pipe path using curvature and torsion in the Frenet frame, with cubic Hermite interpolation to generate profiles. Embed domain-specific manufacturability constraints on curvature/torsion. Optimize via proximal policy optimization with stochastic exploration and a stage-guided reward. Use a unified mapping to convert the optimized path into bending-die motion trajectories for six-axis fabrication.

Result: FPRO consistently yields collision-free, manufacturable paths with smoother geometry and faster convergence. It outperforms state-of-the-art RL baselines in terminal alignment, path length, obstacle avoidance, and manufacturability. Real-world validation shows close geometric correspondence between manufactured pipe and digital design.

Conclusion: FPRO effectively bridges design and manufacturing for aeroengine piping by integrating manufacturability knowledge into RL-based routing in the Frenet frame, enabling direct fabrication and improved performance over Cartesian-based and baseline methods.

Abstract: Design for manufacturing plays a critical role in advanced aeroengine development, where complex components necessitate careful consideration of manufacturability. However, current practices in pipe routing remain largely decoupled from down-stream manufacturing, leading to labor-intensive, trial-and-error iterations to achieve manufacturable designs. To address this problem, this study proposes the Frenet-based pipe routing optimization (FPRO) framework, a manufacturability knowledge-integrated reinforcement learning approach for free-form pipe design in aeroengines. FPRO formulates the routing problem as a boundary value problem in the Frenet frame. In this framework, the pipe path is represented by curvature and torsion profiles, which are generated using cubic Hermite interpolation. To integrate design and manufacturing, domain-specific manufacturing knowledge is embedded as constraints on the permissible ranges of curvature and torsion. The path optimization is performed using the proximal policy optimization algorithm with stochastic exploration and a stage-guided reward mechanism. A unified mapping formulation then translates the optimized path into motion trajectories for the bending die, enabling direct fabrication on a six-axis free-bending machine. Experimental results demonstrate that FPRO consistently generates collision-free, manufacturable paths with smoother geometric profiles compared to Cartesian-based methods. It also achieves faster convergence and superior performance in terminal alignment, path length, obstacle avoidance, and manufacturability compared to state-of-the-art reinforcement learning baselines. Real-world validation confirms the close geometric correspondence between the manufactured pipe and its digital design, validating the practical feasibility of FPRO.

</details>


### [210] [Training Language Agents to Learn from Experience](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20477)
*Yuval Shalev,Zifeng Ding,Mateja Jamnik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces In-context Training (ICT) to study cross-task self-improvement in language agents; trains reflectors via RL to generate system prompts from actor trajectories, improving performance on unseen tasks; demonstrates cross-task generalization and provides MetaGym library.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether experience from interactive tasks can be distilled into reusable, cross-task lessons that improve performance on future unseen tasks, addressing the limitation of reflection methods confined to a single task instance.

Method: Propose ICT as a framework where a reflector model observes trajectories produced by an actor and generates system prompts intended to boost the actor on future tasks. Train reflectors with a reinforcement-learning pipeline that learns reflections directly from experienced trajectories, without human-provided examples. Evaluate on ALFWorld and MiniHack, comparing against an untrained baseline, and assess generalization to different environments. Introduce MetaGym, a Python library for constructing meta-environments to facilitate future research.

Result: Trained reflectors outperform an untrained baseline on most held-out task families in ALFWorld and MiniHack, indicating that the ability to learn from experience can itself be learned. In some cases, reflectors generalize beyond the training benchmark to substantially different environments.

Conclusion: Learning to reflect from experience is achievable and beneficial for cross-task self-improvement in language agents. The work also provides a reusable toolkit (MetaGym) to foster further research in this area.

Abstract: Language agents can adapt from experience in interactive environments, but current reflection-based methods can only self-correct within a single task instance. Whether such experience can be distilled into reusable lessons that improve performance on future unseen tasks remains unclear. We address this problem by introducing the In-context Training (ICT) task, a framework for evaluating cross-task self-improvement in language agents. In ICT, a reflector model observes trajectories collected by an actor model and generates system prompts intended to improve the actor's performance on future unseen tasks. We then propose an RL-based training pipeline for learning such reflections directly from experience, without human-provided examples. Across ALFWorld and MiniHack, our trained reflectors outperform an untrained baseline on most held-out task families, showing that the ability to learn from experience can itself be learned. In some cases, we observe generalisation beyond the benchmark on which the reflector was trained, to substantially different environments. Finally, we introduce MetaGym, a generic Python library for constructing meta-environments, enabling future research on self-improving language agents.

</details>


### [211] [Quadratic Characterizations for Reachability Analysis of Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20482)
*Elias Khalife,Mazen Farhood,Pierre-Loic Garoche*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework to construct verified quadratic characterizations of scalar relations in 2D using local convex QPs from samples and global SOS verification, yielding sound domain overapproximations compatible with QC/IQC methods and applicable to smooth activations and ReLU networks, reducing conservatism and enabling reachability analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome conservatism of generic quadratic characterizations on bounded domains for nonlinearities and uncertainties by producing domain-dependent, verifiable quadratic constraints that integrate with QC/IQC-based analysis and neural-network verification.

Method: Step 1: generate candidate quadratic inequalities by solving convex quadratic programs using samples from the relation and exterior points. Step 2: globally verify these candidates using sum-of-squares certificates over exact semialgebraic descriptions or relaxed polynomial descriptions for nonpolynomial relations. The resulting constraints provide a sound overapproximation of the relation on the domain. The constraints are embedding-ready for QC-based SDPs for reachability/safety analyses of feedforward networks and can leverage neuron-dependency and tighter local bounds to reduce conservatism (notably for ReLU). For smooth activations (e.g., tanh), the approach yields domain-dependent QCs as alternatives to sector/slope bounds. For nonpolynomial cases, relaxations enable verification.

Result: A set of verified quadratic constraints that overapproximate scalar relations on given domains with soundness guarantees. These constraints harmonize with existing QC/IQC analysis frameworks and can be used in SDP-based reachability/safety analyses for neural networks. Demonstrated improvements include tighter reachability for smooth activations, reduced conservatism for ReLU networks through dependency tightening and local bounds, and applicability to non-network contexts such as saturation.

Conclusion: The framework provides a practical, verifiable method to obtain domain-dependent quadratic characterizations that integrate with QC/IQC-based analysis, enabling tighter, less conservative analysis of nonlinearities and neural networks while remaining compatible with existing semidefinite-programming tools.

Abstract: Quadratic constraints (QCs) are widely used to characterize nonlinearities and uncertainties, but generic analytical characterizations can be conservative on bounded domains. This paper develops a framework for constructing verified quadratic characterizations of scalar relations in the two-dimensional real plane. Candidate quadratic inequalities are locally generated by solving convex quadratic programs using samples from the relation and exterior sample points. They are then verified globally using sum-of-squares certificates over an exact semialgebraic description or, in the case of nonpolynomial relations, over relaxed polynomial descriptions. The resulting verified constraints define a sound overapproximation of the scalar relations over the considered domains. These constraints are directly compatible with existing analysis frameworks based on QCs and pointwise integral quadratic constraints (IQCs) for static nonlinearities and uncertainties, and they can also be embedded in QC-based semidefinite programs for reachability and safety analysis of feedforward neural networks. For smooth activations such as $\tanh$, the method yields domain-dependent quadratic characterizations that constitute an alternative to generic sector- or slope-based descriptions. For ReLU networks, we give methods to reduce conservatism in QC-based reachability analysis of feedforward networks by exploiting dependencies between neurons and tighter local bounds. Numerical examples demonstrate improved reachability results for smooth activations, reduced conservatism for ReLU networks, and applicability beyond neural networks through an example involving saturation.

</details>


### [212] [ZEBRA: Zero-shot Budgeted Resource Allocation for LLM Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20485)
*May Hamri,Inbal Talgam-Cohen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes ZEBRA, a zero-shot framework for allocating budgets across multi-phase autonomous pipelines by reducing the problem to a continuous nonlinear knapsack solved via water-filling; LLM controller estimates phase-wise utility curves; unifies additive and multiplicative aggregations under one solver; outperforms LLM-direct on APPS and HotpotQA; robust to estimation noise and adapts to pipeline structure, showing improvement in economic behavior of multi-agent systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As autonomous agents execute end-to-end tasks under fixed budgets, the key question becomes how to spend the budget effectively across the composing phases at inference time, not merely whether the budget is respected.

Method: Zero-shot approach where an LLM controller estimates per-phase utility curves; formulate budget allocation as a continuous nonlinear knapsack problem; solve via water-filling on the Lagrange multiplier to obtain per-phase splits; aggregator types (additive/multiplicative) are unified under the same solver.

Result: Empirical evaluation on a 150-task APPS coding benchmark shows ZEBRA variants outperform LLM-direct on all aggregate metrics; at α = 0.5, ZEBRA achieves 94.4% of unconstrained quality vs 88.1% for LLM-direct. Statistically significant and transferable to non-coding tasks (HotpotQA) where ZEBRA beats LLM-direct by 14.3 percentage points; budget splits differ according to pipeline (near-balanced for HotpotQA, skewed toward refinement for APPS) indicating adaptation to structure; robustness to curve-estimation noise.

Conclusion: Lightweight, inference-time algorithmic guidance (ZEBRA) can meaningfully improve the economic efficiency of autonomous multi-agent systems by optimally distributing budget across pipeline phases without additional training.

Abstract: As autonomous agents increasingly execute end-to-end tasks under fixed monetary budgets, the pressing open question shifts from whether the budget is respected, to how to spend it effectively. Existing budget-aware methods typically control reasoning step-by-step within a single agent, or learn resource allocation policies via RL. None address how to split a budget across the composing phases of a multi-agent pipeline at inference time. We propose ZEBRA, a zero-shot framework that reduces multi-phase budget allocation to a continuous nonlinear knapsack problem: an LLM controller estimates per-phase utility curves, and a water-filling search on the Lagrange multiplier returns the per-phase split. Additive and multiplicative aggregations are unified under the same solver. On a $150$-task APPS coding benchmark, both ZEBRA variants outperform LLM-direct (budget allocation directly by an LLM) on every aggregate metric. At a budget of $α= 0.5$ of the unconstrained spend, ZEBRA recovers $94.4\%$ of unconstrained quality, versus $88.1\%$ for LLM-direct. The advantage is statistically significant and transfers beyond coding: on a $3$-phase HotpotQA pipeline, ZEBRA beats LLM-direct by $14.3$pp, with allocations empirically robust to curve-estimation noise. On HotpotQA, ZEBRA arrives at a different budget split (near-balanced) compared to the APPS one (skewed towards a refinement phase), showing adaptation to the pipeline structure. More broadly, we show that lightweight algorithmic guidance at inference time can improve the economic behavior of autonomous multi-agent systems.

</details>


### [213] [Tippett-minimum Fusion of Representation-space Diffusion Models for Multi-Encoder Out-of-Distribution Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20502)
*Neelkamal Bhuyan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An encoder-agnostic OOD detector (EncMin2L) fuses per-encoder diffusion-based detectors to cover global domain changes, semantic shifts, texture differences, and covariate corruptions. It uses ID-only diagnostics to assess encoder sensitivity, and a two-level min-gate to calibrate and combine per-encoder scores, with Tippett p-value aggregation. It achieves ≥0.94 AUROC across shifts with ~2.3x fewer parameters than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OOD detection must be robust to a wide spectrum of distribution shifts. Existing monolithic or single-encoder detectors struggle to generalize across all shift types. The goal is an encoder-agnostic, calibration-stable method that leverages per-encoder representations without requiring OOD labels.

Method: Train multiple per-encoder diffusion-based likelihood detectors (RDMs) and fuse them with EncMin2L, a two-level min-gate that calibrates combinations of per-encoder detectors. Encoder sensitivities to shift types are inferred from ID data using η^2 (class-conditional F-test) and Δμ (log-likelihood changes under synthetic corruptions). Per-encoder scores are aggregated via a Tippett minimum p-value combination to produce a single OOD signal, avoiding OOD labels.

Result: EncMin2L achieves AUROC ≥ 0.94 across all four shift types simultaneously and outperforms state-of-the-art representation-space diffusion OOD detectors on overlapping benchmarks, while requiring ~2.3x fewer parameters than monolithic multi-encoder baselines.

Conclusion: EncMin2L offers a scalable, calibration-stable, encoder-agnostic approach to diffusion-based OOD detection across diverse distribution shifts. It leverages ID-based encoder diagnostics and a two-level gating strategy to outperform baselines with lower parameter cost.

Abstract: We address out-of-distribution (OOD) detection across the full spectrum of distribution shifts -- global domain changes, semantic divergence, texture differences, and covariate corruptions -- through a multi-encoder fusion of per-encoder representation-space diffusion models (RDMs). We statistically identify each encoder's sensitivity to specific shift types from ID data alone and introduce EncMin2L -- an encoder-agnostic two-level $\min(\cdot)$-gate that combines and calibrates per-encoder diffusion-based likelihood detectors without OOD labels, outperforming monolithic multi-encoder baselines at $2.3\times$ lower parameter cost. Two ID-data diagnostics: $η^2$ (class-conditional F-test) and $Δμ$ (log-likelihood shift under synthetic corruptions) -- quantify encoder specialization, while a Tippett minimum $p$-value combination aggregates per-encoder scores into a single, calibration-stable OOD signal. EncMin2L achieves $\geq 0.94$ AUROC across all four shift types simultaneously, outperforming the state-of-the-art representation-space diffusion OOD detectors across overlapping benchmarks.

</details>


### [214] [Reinforcing Human Behavior Simulation via Verbal Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20506)
*Weiwei Sun,Xuhui Zhou,Jiarui Liu,Weihua Du,Haojia Sun,Yiqing Xie,Qianou Ma,Sihao Chen,Mengting Wan,Longqi Yang,Pei Zhou,Sherry Wu,Sean Welleck,Graham Neubig,Yiming Yang,Maarten Sap*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Training LLMs with verbal feedback as a first-class reinforcement signal (DITTO) and a unified human-behavior benchmark (SOUL) yields substantial gains over base models and competitive performance with GPT-5.4 across diverse social-behavior tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL approaches for LLMs underuse verbal, subjective feedback, limiting the ability to simulate human-like behavior; there is a need to leverage natural language signals for nuanced social reasoning and persona-based tasks.

Method: DITTO uses verbal feedback after each rollout to generate improved, feedback-conditioned rollouts. It is trained with GRPO (policy optimization) and distills verbal guidance into the base policy without needing feedback at test time. SOUL provides a multi-task benchmark across theory of mind, role play, social skills, learner/user simulation across six categories.

Result: On SOUL benchmarks, DITTO achieves ~36% average improvement over the base model and surpasses GPT-5.4 on 6 of 10 tasks.

Conclusion: RL with verbal feedback is a promising avenue for training LLMs to emulate human behavior; success relies on quality/coverage of verbal feedback and effective distillation; future work could expand feedback types and benchmarks.

Abstract: Humans learn social norms and behaviors from verbal feedback (e.g., a parent saying "that was rude" or a friend explaining "here's why that hurt"). Yet, learning from feedback for LLMs has largely focused on domains like code and math, where RL rewards are directly verifiable and condensed into scalar values. As LLMs are increasingly used to simulate human behavior, e.g., standing in for users, patients, students, and other personas, there is a pressing need to make them more human-like, which requires embracing a fundamentally different kind of signal: feedback that is verbal, subjective, and multi-faceted. We present DITTO, a model trained by treating verbal feedback as a first-class signal in reinforcement learning. After each rollout, DITTO receives verbal feedback and generates a feedback-conditioned improved rollout; both outputs are jointly optimized with GRPO, distilling verbal guidance into the base policy without requiring feedback at test time. We also introduce SOUL (Simulation gym Of hUman-Like behavior), a unified benchmark and training data suite spanning 10 tasks across six categories: Theory of Mind, character role play, social skill, learner simulation, user simulation, and persona simulation. DITTO achieves an average 36% improvement over the base model and exceeds GPT-5.4 on 6 of 10 SOUL benchmarks, demonstrating that RL with verbal feedback is a promising direction for training LLMs to simulate human behavior.

</details>


### [215] [Fast Reconstruction of Exact Maxwell Dynamics from Sparse Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20514)
*Dan DeGenaro,Xin Li,Obed Amo,Michael Pokojovy,Sarah Adel Bargal,Markus Lange-Hegermann,Bogdan Raiţă*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A shallow, exact-by-construction neural network (FLASH-MAX) for Maxwell's equations: each hidden unit encodes an exact solution, guaranteeing zero PDE residual and universal approximation; demonstrates fast training and high accuracy from sparse data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve data efficiency and training speed in scientific ML by embedding governing physics into the network architecture rather than the loss, ensuring PDE satisfaction by construction.

Method: A shallow network where each hidden neuron represents a distinct exact solution to Maxwell's equations (exact-by-construction). Trains end-to-end on sparse pointwise observations with a proof of universal approximation on arbitrary domains; zero PDE residual is guaranteed by construction.

Result: Achieves sub-1% relative validation error from about 1K sparse observations in seconds, with zero PDE residual; maintains single-digit errors even with only 100 observations in 3D; universal approximation holds on arbitrary domains.

Conclusion: Moving governing structure from the loss into the hypothesis class can dramatically improve the precision-optimization speed trade-off in scientific machine learning.

Abstract: We introduce FLASH-MAX, a shallow, exact-by-construction neural network architecture for predicting homogeneous electromagnetic fields from sparse pointwise observations. Each hidden neuron represents a separate exact solution to Maxwell's equations, so that the network satisfies the governing equations symbolically by construction and can be trained end-to-end from sparse data within seconds. We prove a universal approximation result showing that this exact model class remains universal on arbitrary domains. FLASH-MAX reaches sub-1% relative validation error from about 1K sparse pointwise observations in seconds, all while maintaining a zero PDE residual, and keeps single-digit errors even for only 100 observations sampled from 3D space. These results suggest that moving governing structure from the loss into the hypothesis class can dramatically improve the trade-off between precision and optimization speed in scientific machine learning.

</details>


### [216] [Online Conformal Prediction with Corrupted Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20515)
*Bowen Wang,Matteo Zecchin,Osvaldo Simeone*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies online conformal prediction (OCP) under corrupted feedback, proposing two robust schemes—filtering and active compensation—and provides explicit miscoverage guarantees with empirical validation showing improved calibration and smaller prediction sets under corruption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To maintain reliable, calibrated uncertainty estimates in online, non-stationary settings when feedback about past predictions can be corrupted by noise, failures, or adversaries, which threatens OCP guarantees.

Method: Model feedback corruption as an arbitrary binary flip sequence. Analyze degradation of miscoverage in standard OCP. Propose two robust schemes: (1) robust OCP via filtering that uses the predictive threshold structure to filter corrupted feedback; (2) robust OCP via active compensation that actively mitigates corrupted feedback. Derive explicit miscoverage guarantees, specialized for an independent stochastic flip model and for a memory-bounded arbitrary error model.

Result: Establish explicit miscoverage guarantees for both robust schemes. Empirical validation on real-world datasets shows markedly improved calibration and significantly smaller prediction sets compared with baseline OCP under corrupted feedback.

Conclusion: Robust OCP methods effectively mitigate the adverse impact of corrupted feedback, restoring reliable calibration in online settings and demonstrating practical viability with theoretical guarantees and empirical support.

Abstract: Modern artificial intelligence systems require calibrated uncertainty estimates that remain reliable in sequential and non-stationary environments. Online conformal prediction (OCP) addresses this challenge through adaptively updated prediction sets that provide deterministic long-run miscoverage guarantees. These guarantees, however, hinge on the assumption of perfect feedback about the coverage of past prediction sets. In practice, the observed miscoverage indicator may be corrupted by noise, communication failures, or adversarial manipulation, which can severely degrade OCP's calibration guarantees. In this paper, we study OCP under corrupted feedback. We first model feedback corruption as an arbitrary binary flip sequence, and analyze how feedback corruption affects and degrades the miscoverage performance of standard OCP. We then propose two robust schemes: robust OCP via filtering, which leverages the structural properties of the predicted threshold to filter corrupted feedback, and robust OCP via active compensation, which incorporates an active compensation mechanism to mitigate the effect of corrupted feedback. For both methods, we establish explicit miscoverage guarantees, which are further specialized for an independent stochastic flip model and for an arbitrary error model with memory bounds. Experiments on real-world datasets validate the proposed approach, showing markedly improved calibration and significantly smaller prediction sets compared with baseline OCP methods under corrupted feedback.

</details>


### [217] [An exponential mechanism based on quadratic approximations for fine-tuning machine learning models with privacy guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20521)
*Hoang Tran,Jorge Ramirez,Jiayi Wang,Alberto Bocchinfuso,Christopher Stanley,M. Paul Laiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Privacy-preserving fine-tuning using the exponential mechanism with closed-form MVN sampling; introduces random-projection for scalability; demonstrates competitive DP performance on MNIST and MIMIC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning pretrained models on small, sensitive datasets risks memorization and data leakage. There is a need for differential privacy guarantees during fine-tuning to protect individual data points while maintaining utility.

Method: Formulate a utility function that blends a local quadratic approximation of the pretrained model with information from the new dataset. Use the exponential mechanism to sample updates, which, with this utility, allows exact sampling from a multivariate normal distribution in closed form. Introduce a random-projection strategy to scale to high-dimensional models.

Result: The approach comes with theoretical privacy guarantees, bounds on sensitivity, and accuracy estimates. Empirical evaluation on MNIST and MIMIC shows competitive performance relative to existing differentially private fine-tuning techniques.

Conclusion: The exponential-mechanism-based DP fine-tuning is effective and scalable for high-dimensional models, offering solid privacy guarantees and competitive accuracy; random projection enables practical deployment in large models.

Abstract: Fine-tuning adapts a pretrained machine learning model to a small, sensitive dataset, but this process risks memorizing individual new data points, making the model vulnerable to adversaries who seek to extract sensitive information. In this work, we develop a randomized algorithm based on the exponential mechanism for fine-tuning while ensuring differential privacy. Our key idea is to construct a simple utility function that combines a local quadratic approximation of the pretrained model with information from the new dataset. The resulting exponential mechanism admits exact sampling from a multivariate normal distribution in closed form. We establish theoretical privacy guarantees, sensitivity bounds, and accuracy estimations for our method. We further introduce a random-projection strategy that makes the approach scalable to high-dimensional models. Numerical experiments on the MNIST benchmark and the MIMIC clinical dataset demonstrate competitive performance against existing differentially private fine-tuning techniques.

</details>


### [218] [Machine-Learning-Enhanced Non-Invasive Testing for MASLD Fibrosis: Shallow-Deep Neural Networks Versus FIB-4, Tabular Foundation Models, and Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20523)
*Athanasios Angelakis,Gabriele De Vito,Eleni-Myrto Trifylli,Filomena Ferrucci*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML-enhanced non-invasive testing (MLE-NIT) using five variables (age, FIB-4, AST, platelets, ALT) can improve advanced fibrosis detection in MASLD over FIB-4 alone; a shallow neural network (s-DNN) with only 354 parameters delivered best external performance and good calibration, outperforming larger models like TabPFN and GPT-4o in external cohorts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FIB-4's fixed formula may underutilize diagnostic information; leverage ML to extract additional signal from the same clinical variables without increasing data collection burden.

Method: Three biopsy-confirmed MASLD cohorts (China, Malaysia, India; n=784). China split into 486 training and 54 internal validation; external evaluation on Malaysia (n=? not given) and India. Five variables: age, FIB-4, AST, platelets, ALT. Comparisons: FIB-4, s-DNN, TabPFN, GPT-4o.

Result: External ROC-AUCs: FIB-4 0.75 (Malaysia) and 0.60 (India); TabPFN 0.69 and 0.66; GPT-4o 0.75 and 0.63; s-DNN 0.77 and 0.67. s-DNN had 354 parameters vs TabPFN 7,244,554; s-DNN showed more balanced external performance; calibration: Brier scores 0.18 (Malaysia) and 0.22 (India). Permutation importance: AST and FIB-4 dominant.

Conclusion: Compact non-linear MLE-NITs can enhance FIB-4-based fibrosis assessment without extra data; extremely small models (e.g., s-DNN) may outperform larger ML models in external validation while maintaining interpretability and calibration.

Abstract: Advanced fibrosis is a major determinant of liver-related morbidity in metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD). FIB-4 is widely used as a first-line non-invasive test, but its fixed formula may underuse diagnostic information contained in age, aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, and platelet count. We evaluated whether machine-learning-enhanced non-invasive testing (MLE-NIT) can improve advanced fibrosis detection while preserving this FIB-4 variable space.
  We used three biopsy-confirmed MASLD cohorts from China, Malaysia, and India (n=784). The Chinese cohort was split into 486 training and 54 internal validation/tuning patients; final performance was reported only on the Malaysian and Indian external cohorts. Models used five variables: age, FIB-4, aspartate aminotransferase, platelet count, and alanine aminotransferase. We compared FIB-4 with a shallow-deep neural network (s-DNN), TabPFN, and gpt-4o-2024-08-06.
  FIB-4 achieved external ROC-AUCs of 0.75 and 0.60 in Malaysia and India, respectively. TabPFN achieved 0.69 and 0.66, fine-tuned GPT-4o achieved 0.75 and 0.63, and the s-DNN achieved 0.77 and 0.67, respectively. The s-DNN contained only 354 trainable parameters, compared with 7,244,554 for TabPFN, yet provided a more balanced external operating profile. Calibration showed s-DNN Brier scores of 0.18 and 0.22, and permutation importance identified AST and FIB-4 as dominant variables. Compact non-linear MLE-NITs may enhance FIB-4-based fibrosis assessment without increasing clinical data requirements.

</details>


### [219] [Ada2MS: A Hybrid Optimization Algorithm Based on Exponential Mixing of Elementwise and Global Second-Moment Estimates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20533)
*Meng Zhu,Quan Xiao,Weidong Min*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Ada2MS introduces a continuum optimizer that blends AdamW-like stability with Momentum SGD-like generalization by exponentially interpolating between elementwise and global second-moment estimates, yielding competitive results on visual tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the trade-offs between AdamW and Momentum SGD—robust, stable updates vs. strong generalization with careful tuning—by providing a tunable interpolation between the two regimes.

Method: Ada2MS uses a continuous exponential interpolation between elementwise second-moment estimates (as in AdamW) and a global second-moment estimate (as in momentum methods), enabling a smooth transition between AdamW-like and Momentum SGD-like behavior.

Result: On visual tasks, Ada2MS achieves competitive performance under a unified optimizer-comparison protocol.

Conclusion: Ada2MS successfully balances the strengths of AdamW and Momentum SGD through a tunable interpolation mechanism, suggesting robustness and good generalization can be jointly achieved depending on the interpolation setting.

Abstract: Optimization algorithms are core methods by which machine learning models iteratively minimize loss functions, update parameters, learn from data, and improve performance. Momentum SGD and AdamW represent two important optimization paradigms. AdamW produces stable updates and usually has strong robustness across training scenarios, but its generalization performance is sometimes weaker than that of momentum methods. Momentum SGD can often obtain better generalization after careful tuning, but it is more sensitive to gradient-scale variation and hyperparameter settings. To balance the strengths and weaknesses of the two paradigms, this paper proposes Ada2MS, an optimization algorithm that achieves a smooth transition between AdamW-like behavior and momentum-SGD-like behavior through continuous exponential interpolation between elementwise second-moment estimates and global second-moment estimates. On the visual tasks evaluated in this study, Ada2MS obtains competitive results under a unified optimizer-comparison protocol. The code will be released at https://github.com/mengzhu0308/Ada2MS

</details>


### [220] [Axiomatizing Neural Networks via Pursuit of Subspaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20534)
*Mehmet Yamac,Mert Duman,Ugur Akpinar,Felix Rojas Casadiego,Serkan Kiranyaz,Marcel van Gerven,Moncef Gabbouj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Pursuit of Subspaces (PoS) as an axiomatic geometric framework to explain neural networks, unifying representation, computation, and generalization; derives geometric explanations for core phenomena and advances toward a principled theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep nets are powerful but poorly understood and often treated as black boxes; a principled geometric/axiomatic framework is needed to link empirical performance to theory.

Method: Introduce PoS hypothesis with geometric postulates; derive consequences; apply to shallow and deep architectures to yield a unified perspective on representation, computation, and generalization.

Result: The framework provides geometric explanations for representation structure, architectural mechanisms, and generalization; yields a coherent perspective across architectures and tasks; demonstrates potential to explain fundamental questions in deep learning.

Conclusion: PoS offers a principled step toward a coherent theoretical foundation for deep learning, bridging empirical success with geometric reasoning and guiding future research.

Abstract: While deep neural networks have achieved remarkable success across a wide range of domains, their underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood, and they are often regarded as black boxes. This gap between empirical performance and theoretical understanding poses a challenge analogous to the pre-axiomatic stage of classical geometry. In this work, we introduce the Pursuit of Subspaces (PoS) hypothesis, an axiomatic framework that formulates neural network behavior through a set of geometric postulates. These axioms, together with their derived consequences, provide a unified perspective on representation, computation, and generalization in both shallow and deep architectures. We show that this framework yields geometric explanations for fundamental questions in deep learning, including representation structure, architectural mechanisms, and generalization behavior, offering a principled step toward a coherent theoretical foundation.

</details>


### [221] [OpenSeisML: Open Large-Scale Real Seismic and well-log Dataset for Generative AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20539)
*Ipsita Bhar,Huseyin Tuna Erdinc,Thales Souza,Charles Jones,Felix J. Herrmann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OpenSeisML is a curated set of real seismic datasets from publicly available UK surveys, designed to support generative AI workflows for seismic inversion by enabling uncertainty quantification and reproducible data preparation through an automated pipeline, including time-to-depth conversion via checkshot and velocity model interpolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Realistic velocity models are scarce due to proprietary data in oil & gas; ML/Gen‑AI methods require large, representative datasets. OpenSeisML aims to democratize data access, enable statistically consistent realizations, and provide priors for seismic inversion.

Method: Curate real seismic surveys from the UK National Data Repository; perform time-to-depth conversion using checkshot data; interpolate to construct velocity models for accurate conversion of post-stack data; develop an automated data curation pipeline to ensure reproducibility; train a generative model to learn the distribution of subsurface properties and generate multiple realizations for uncertainty quantification.

Result: Introduction of OpenSeisML datasets and an automated pipeline for seismic data preparation; enables generation of multiple statistically consistent realizations to serve as priors for seismic inversion and uncertainty quantification.

Conclusion: OpenSeisML addresses data scarcity and reproducibility issues in seismic inversion by providing publicly available, realistically distributed datasets and a pipeline to produce uncertainty-aware realizations for Gen-AI workflows.

Abstract: The advent of machine learning (ML) and computer vision has significantly accelerated seismic inversion workflows by reducing the computational cost of traditionally expensive iterative methods. However, the development and evaluation of ML methods remain limited by the scarcity of realistic velocity models, as most high-quality data are privately owned by oil and gas companies. To address this gap, we present OpenSeisML, a collection of real seismic datasets designed to support generative AI (Gen-AI) workflows for seismic inversion. The datasets are curated from publicly available surveys in the UK National Data Repository (NDR). When seismic volumes are in the time domain and wells are in depth, a time-to-depth conversion is required. We use checkshot data to establish the time-depth relationship and construct a velocity model through interpolation for accurate conversion of post-stack seismic data. Here, we present an automated data curation pipeline that enables seismic data preparation while ensuring reproducibility. The objective is to train a generative model that captures the statistical distribution of subsurface properties, enabling the synthesis of multiple statistically consistent realizations for uncertainty quantification which can act as a prior for seismic inversion.

</details>


### [222] [Latent Process Generator Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20547)
*Lukas Billera,Hedwig Nora Nordlinder,Ben Murrell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent Process Generator Matching (LPGM): learn a generator on the image space X_t = Φ(Y_t) that matches the one-time marginals of the projected latent process, enabling time-dependent latent conditioning without sampling auxiliary states at generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address training-time use of auxiliary stochastic dynamics in flow-matching and diffusion models, while keeping generation-time outputs in the observed space; generalize Generator Matching from static latent variables to time-dependent latent processes.

Method: Model Y_t as a tractable Markov process with X_t = Φ(Y_t) deterministic; train a generator on the image space to reproduce the same one-time marginal distributions as the projected process; show the framework subsumes discrete latent results and extends to time-dependent latent conditional processes.

Result: Theoretical result showing that a generator on the image space can achieve identical one-time marginal distributions to the projected latent process; unifies and extends Generator Matching to time-dependent latent frameworks.

Conclusion: Latent Process Generator Matching broadens Generator Matching to a rich family of time-dependent latent conditional processes, enabling flexible conditioning while keeping outputs in the observed space.

Abstract: Many recent flow-matching and diffusion-style generative models rely on auxiliary stochastic dynamics during training: a richer process is simulated to define conditional targets, but the auxiliary state is either intractable to sample at generation time or simply not part of the desired output. Existing Generator Matching theory formalises conditioning on static latent random variables, and several recent papers prove special cases of projection results for particular augmented-state constructions. We introduce latent process generator matching, a general framework that treats the observed generative state as a deterministic image $X_t=Φ(Y_t)$ of a tractable Markov process $Y_t$. We show that in this setting one may learn the generator of a stochastic process on the image space which has the same one-time marginal distributions as the projected process. This generalizes and subsumes the discrete latent process results from the literature, and extends Generator Matching from static latent variables to a rich family of time-dependent latent conditional processes.

</details>


### [223] [Complementing reinforcement learning with SFT through logit averaging in the post training of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20555)
*Xingwei Gan,Ying Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A logit-averaging method over a frozen reference policy and a trainable policy is integrated into GRPO, avoiding KL regularization and critics, to maintain SFT formatting while leveraging the trainable policy's reasoning; evaluated on MATH, cn-k12, and MMLU with accuracy comparable or superior to KL-regularized GRPO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To combine the benefits of a frozen reference policy (SFT) with a trainable policy without KL penalties or critics, preserving the SFT formatting advantages while enhancing reasoning via the trainable model.

Method: Introduce a logit-averaging mechanism that couples a frozen reference policy and a trainable policy within Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). The approach avoids KL regularization and critics; the two policies are coupled through averaging their logits, acting as an anchor while allowing the trainable policy to contribute reasoning.

Result: Empirical evaluation on MATH, cn-k12, and MMLU shows higher accuracy or at least comparable accuracy compared with canonical KL-regularized GRPO.

Conclusion: Logit averaging between a frozen reference policy and a trainable policy within GRPO yields competitive performance without KL regularization or a critic, offering a simple yet effective way to preserve SFT formatting while benefiting from trainable policy reasoning.

Abstract: We introduce a novel method that averages the logits of a frozen reference policy (e.g., SFT) and a trainable policy, and incorporate the method into Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). In contrast to Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods, our proposal does not involve a Kullback Leibler (KL) regularization or critic; the trainable policy and the reference anchor are coupled through the logit averaging structure to leverage the reasoning expertise of the trainable policy while maintaining the formatting advantage of SFT.
