Our method is evaluated on MATH, cn-k12, and MMLU, and the results show a higher accuracy or at least comparable accuracy relative to the canonical KL-regularized GRPO.

</details>


### [224] [Deep Learning Surrogates for Emulating Stochastic Climate Tipping Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20580)
*Adeline Hillier,Jennifer Sleeman,Jay Brett,Caroline Tang,Jenelle Millison,Anand Gnanadesikan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dynamics-informed Temporal Fusion Transformer serves as a fast, differentiable surrogate for complex ocean-transport simulations, achieving 465x speedup while accurately predicting timing of regime collapses and capturing ensemble uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for scalable, differentiable surrogates for computationally intensive Earth system models that produce multi-variate, non-stationary time series and initial-condition covariates, enabling rapid forecasting and uncertainty quantification of regime transitions.

Method: Develop a dynamics-informed TFT with architectural and objective-function modifications to handle up to 21 non-stationary time series plus static covariates, forecast tip/transition timings over thousands of steps, and quantify stochastic timing uncertainty across ensembles while maintaining differentiability with respect to inputs.

Result: The learned surrogate accurately anticipates Atlantic and Pacific collapse timings, captures scheduled and stochastic transition timing across ensemble predictions, and achieves ~465× speedup relative to the full numerical simulator, with preserved differentiability to parameters and initial conditions.

Conclusion: A TFT-based, dynamics-informed surrogate is effective for fast, differentiable emulation of complex Earth-system simulations, enabling scalable speedups and uncertainty quantification in multivariate, non-stationary settings.

Abstract: This work explores a dynamics-informed Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) as a data-driven surrogate for computationally intensive Earth system simulations. Focusing on multivariate time series describing global ocean transport, we demonstrate the surrogate's ability to forecast tip events across thousands of time steps. The data involve up to 21 non-stationary time series in addition to static covariates describing free parameters and initial conditions. Modifications to the architecture and objective function yield a surrogate that anticipates the timing of Atlantic and Pacific collapses to high fidelity and captures the stochastic uncertainty in transition timing across ensemble predictions. The learned surrogate achieves a 465x computational speedup over the numerical simulator while maintaining differentiability with respect to parameters and initial conditions.

</details>


### [225] [TriForces: Augmenting Atomistic GNNs for Transferable Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20581)
*Ali Ramlaoui,Alexandre Duval,Hannah Bull,Victor Schmidt,Hugues Talbot,Fragkiskos D. Malliaros,Joseph Musielewicz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TriForces is a model-agnostic, three-stream framework that disentangles composition and structure in ML interatomic potentials, using self-supervised learning to preserve transferability; it improves performance on benchmarks (MatBench, QM9) without DFT labels and shows substantial data-efficient gains (57% energy MAE improvement on OMat24 at 20k samples).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLIPs struggle to transfer across domains due to loss of composition/structure information; small, task-specific datasets require effective representation and data-efficient learning.

Method: Three-stream architecture that separates composition and structure information, combined with self-supervised learning to preserve transferable representations; model-agnostic, with pretrained variants released for multiple MLIP architectures.

Result: Improved performance on MatBench and QM9 without DFT labels; improved energy and force MAE on OMat24 in limited-data regime; enables efficient retrieval of similar structures via latent space.

Conclusion: TriForces offers a flexible, data-efficient approach to cross-domain MLIP transfer, preserving transferable representations and enabling practical deployment across architectures; code released.

Abstract: Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) achieve excellent accuracy when trained on large Density Functional Theory (DFT) data. To be useful in practice, they must often be adapted to target chemistries using small and expensive task-specific datasets. However, MLIPs transfer inconsistently across domains, with representations that often loose accessible composition and structure information. To address this, we present TriForces, a model-agnostic three-stream framework that separates composition and structure information, combined with self-supervised learning to preserve transferable representations. TriForces improves performance on MatBench and QM9 over baselines without needing DFT labels and enables efficient similar structure retrieval through its learned latent space. On OMat24, in limited-data training regime, TriForces reduces energy MAE by 57% at 20K samples only and improves force MAE across sample sizes. We release pretrained TriForces variants across multiple MLIP architectures with code at https://github.com/Ramlaoui/triforces.

</details>


### [226] [ReversedQ: Opportunities for Faster Q-Learning in Episodic Online Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20592)
*Sofia R. Miskala-Dinc,Aviva Prins*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReversedQ combines three fast-learning levers for model-free posterior-sampling Q-learning in finite-horizon MDPs, achieving large empirical gains over RandomizedQ on BDCL and chain MDPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the reliance on delayed learning in nascent model-free posterior-sampling methods. Identify actionable levers to accelerate learning: (i) value-function update order, (ii) update frequencies, (iii) value-function initialization.

Method: Extend Wang et al.'s RandomizedQ with three modifications (update order, update frequency, initialization), study their individual and combined effects through empirical experiments on BDCL and chain MDPs.

Result: Individual and especially their combination (ReversedQ) improve scaled mean cumulative reward significantly: BDCL from 9.53% to 78.78%; chain MDP from 21.76% to 61.81%.

Conclusion: Faster learning in model-free posterior-sampling Q-learning is attainable via deliberate design choices for value-function updates, frequencies, and initialization, with ReversedQ offering substantial empirical gains and guiding future theoretical analysis.

Abstract: We study model-free Q-learning in finite-horizon episodic Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with stationary dynamics across episodes. We identify a central issue in nascent model-free posterior-sampling works: the reliance on delayed learning in order to prove theoretical guarantees. In particular, we identify three opportunities for faster learning - (i) value-function update order, (ii) update frequencies, and (iii) value-function initialization. Using Wang et al.'s RandomizedQ as a basis, we illustrate these changes and their individual (as well as cumulative) impact in multiple empirical studies. We find that our combined modifications, termed ReversedQ, improve scaled mean cumulative reward compared to RandomizedQ, from 9.53% to 78.78% in the Bidirectional Diabolical Combination Lock (BDCL), and from 21.76% to 61.81% in a chain MDP.

</details>


### [227] [Unsupervised clustering and classification of upper limb EMG signals during functional movements: a data-driven](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20599)
*L. F. Salazar Álvarez,D. Escobar-Saltarén,M. B. Salazar Sánchez,S. C. Henao-Aguirre*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A four-stage sEMG pipeline for clustering and classifying 52 gestures from NINAPRO DB4, achieving low-latency control with a 200 ms window; feature reduction to five; six representative gestures via Mahalanobis-based clustering; top models are Extra Trees and ANN, supporting real-time myoelectric control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for robust, scalable, and low-latency upper-limb sEMG gesture recognition to enable real-time myoelectric prosthesis control and reduce computational load through feature and gesture selection.

Method: A four-stage pipeline: (1) preprocessing with a 4th-order low-pass filter at 0.6 Hz and Hilbert envelope; (2) extraction of 26 temporal and frequency-domain features; (3) feature refinement via visual analysis, mutual information, PCA, and decision-tree importance scores, yielding five key features; (4) gesture selection using hierarchical clustering with Mahalanobis distance to choose six representative gestures; temporal segmentation using a 200 ms window. Classifier evaluation involved an automated PyCaret-based comparison identifying Extra Trees (ET) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) as top performers, followed by independent training to confirm stability and generalization.

Result: The pipeline yielded six representative gestures and a five-feature subset; ET and ANN emerged as top classifiers with demonstrated stability and generalization. ANN showed progressive learning, while ET maintained robust, consistent performance, supporting low-latency, adaptive control for myoelectric prostheses.

Conclusion: The proposed scalable, low-latency sEMG processing and classification workflow is suitable for real-time myoelectric control and can be generalized to similar gesture datasets, aiding rapid deployment in adaptive prosthetic systems.

Abstract: This study presents a comprehensive approach for the clustering and classification of upper-limb surface electromyography (sEMG) signals during functional reach and grasp movements. The methodology was applied to the NINAPRO DB4 dataset, which provides multichannel EMG recordings of 52 gestures. A four-stage pipeline was designed, including signal preprocessing, fea-ture extraction, gesture selection via hierarchical clustering, and comparative model evaluation. Preprocessing involved a fourth-order low-pass filter (0.6 Hz) and Hilbert envelope transformation, effectively reducing noise and enhancing signal clarity. Feature extraction yielded 26 temporal and frequency-domain met-rics, which were later refined using visual analysis, mutual information, principal component analysis, and decision tree importance scores. A final subset of five key features was selected for classification tasks. Gesture selection was per-formed through hierarchical clustering using Mahalanobis distance, resulting in six representative movements that balanced biomechanical diversity and compu-tational efficiency. A 200 ms window was identified as optimal for temporal seg-mentation based on stability and physiological plausibility. Classifier models were evaluated in two stages. Automated comparison using PyCaret identified Extra Trees (ET) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) as top performers. Sub-sequent independent training confirmed their stability and generalization capac-ity, with ANN showing progressive learning and ET maintaining robust, con-sistent results. The findings support the implementation of adaptive, low-latency control strategies for myoelectric prostheses and provide a scalable pipeline for future real-time applications.

</details>


### [228] [Compositional Transduction with Latent Analogies for Offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20609)
*Junseok Kim,Dohyeong Kim,Mineui Hong,Songhwai Oh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes analogy transduction for compositional generalization in offline goal-conditioned RL, introducing a novel analogy representation that isolates task-change under execution and is invariant to context, enabling generalization to unseen analogy-context pairs; demonstrates empirical gains on OGBench manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of trajectory stitching for compositional generalization in offline GCRL by enabling composition via task-endogenous analogies across contexts.

Method: Formalize analogy transduction and develop a tailored analogy representation; propose an offline GCRL method that enables transduction beyond seen pairs; conduct empirical evaluation on OGBench manipulation environments.

Result: The approach substantially outperforms prior methods that do not perform analogy transduction.

Conclusion: Analogy transduction is a viable mechanism to achieve compositional generalization in offline GCRL and the proposed representation and learning method enable generalization to unseen analogy-context pairs, with strong empirical results on OGBench.

Abstract: Compositional generalization is essential for reaching unseen goals under novel contextual variations in offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL), where a generalist goal-reaching agent must be learned from limited data. Most prior approaches pursue this via trajectory stitching over temporally contiguous segments, which limits composing behaviors across varying contexts. To overcome this limitation, we formalize analogy transduction as synthesizing new plans by composing task-endogenous analogies with given contexts and propose a novel analogy representation tailored for it. Grounded in our theory, this analogy representation captures what changes under optimal task execution, remains invariant to contextual variations, and is sufficient for optimal goal reaching. We further contend that generalization to unseen analogy-context pairs is a practical obstacle in analogy transduction, and introduce a new approach for offline GCRL that enables analogy transduction beyond seen pairs to unseen combinations. We empirically demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on OGBench manipulation environments, substantially outperforming prior methods that do not perform analogy transduction. Project page: https://rllab-snu.github.io/projects/CTA/

</details>


### [229] [Matryoshka Concept Bottleneck Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20612)
*Ziye Chen,Hongbin Lin,Xinyue Xu,Jie Li,Lijie Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MCBM presents a hierarchical, nested concept bottleneck model enabling adaptive concept usage within a single model, reducing test-time intervention costs from linear to O(log K) while preserving or matching performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the high cost of test-time human interventions in CBMs by avoiding a fixed concept set or training multiple budgets; enable a single model to operate efficiently across varying levels of conceptual granularity.

Method: Construct a Matryoshka-inspired hierarchy of concepts organized by maximum relevance and minimum redundancy. Inference can proceed at multiple granularity levels without retraining. Provide theoretical O(log K) intervention-cost guarantees and monotonic performance improvement. Validate empirically that the model matches the performance of independently trained models while enabling dynamic expert interaction.

Result: Intervention costs scale logarithmically with the number of concepts, O(log K), with monotonic performance gains. Empirical results show the single MCBM matches the performance of multiple independently trained concept bottleneck models and supports dynamic expert interaction.

Conclusion: MCBM offers a unified, adaptive framework for CBMs that reduces intervention costs and preserves or improves predictive performance, enabling practical deployment with variable concept budgets.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) have emerged as a prominent paradigm for interpretable deep learning, learning by grounding predictions in human-understandable concepts. However, their practical deployment is hindered by the high cost of test-time intervention, as correcting model errors typically requires human experts to manually inspect and verify a large set of predicted concepts. Existing approaches suffer from a fundamental structural limitation: they either adopt a single static concept set, forcing experts to exhaustively annotate concepts and incurring prohibitive intervention costs, or train multiple models tailored to different concept budgets, resulting in substantial computational and maintenance overhead. To address this challenge, we propose the Matryoshka Concept Bottleneck Model (MCBM), a unified architecture that enables adaptive concept utilization within a single model. Inspired by Matryoshka Representation Learning, MCBM organizes concepts into a nested hierarchy based on maximum relevance and minimum redundancy, allowing inference at multiple levels of conceptual granularity without retraining. Theoretically, we show that MCBM reduces the expected intervention costs from linear to logarithmic order, $O(\log K)$, while guaranteeing monotonic performance improvement. Empirically, extensive experiments demonstrate that MCBM matches the performance of independently trained models while enabling dynamic and efficient expert interaction.

</details>


### [230] [SURF: Steering the Scalarization Weight to Uniformly Traverse the Pareto Front](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20619)
*Liuyuan Jiang,Chentong Huang,Lisha Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SURF samples scalarization weights to achieve uniform Pareto-front coverage by inverting the arc-length-based CDF of the scalarization path; provides closed-form for structured problems (bi-objective bandits) and iterative reconstruction for general cases, with linear convergence to a finite floor; empirically improves PF uniformity across bandits, multi-objective Gym, and multi-objective LLM alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform coverage of the Pareto front is desirable but standard weight sampling yields non-uniform coverage; need a principled method to align weight sampling with PF traversal dynamics.

Method: Geometric analysis of how scalarization paths trace the PF as weights vary; define arc-length metric and CDF of traversal speed; invert to obtain weight samples; derive closed-form CDF maps for structured problems; iterative SURF for general problems; prove linear convergence to finite floor.

Result: Demonstrates more uniform PF coverage than baselines; provides closed-form CDF for structured cases; empirical validation on bandits, multi-objective gym, and multi-objective LLM alignment.

Conclusion: SURF offers a principled, scalable approach to PF-aware scalarization weight sampling, achieving improved uniform coverage with provable convergence properties and broad applicability; it may incur a finite-sampling floor under certain conditions.

Abstract: Scalarization is widely used in multi-objective optimization owing to its simplicity and scalability. In many applications, the goal is to generate solutions that represent diverse user preferences, ideally with uniform coverage of the Pareto front (PF). However, uniformly sampling scalarization weights usually induces non-uniform coverage of the PF. We explain this mismatch through a geometric analysis of the scalarization path. As the scalarization weight varies, the corresponding solutions trace the PF with a generally non-uniform traversal speed. This speed induces an arc-length cumulative distribution function (CDF); inverting this CDF map yields a principled rule for selecting weights that produce uniform PF coverage. Building on this insight, we propose SURF (Sampling Uniformly along the PaReto Front). For structured problems, including bi-objective bandits, we derive closed-form expressions for this CDF map and the resulting PF-aware weight sampling rule. For general problems, SURF alternates between CDF reconstruction and weight sampling. Theoretically, we show that under provable conditions, SURF converges linearly to an unavoidable finite-sampling floor. Empirically, experiments on bandits, multi-objective-gymnasium, and multi-objective LLM alignment demonstrate that SURF efficiently achieves more uniform PF coverage than baselines.

</details>


### [231] [Dynamic Shapley Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20620)
*Xuan Yang,Hsi-Wen Chen,Ming-Syan Chen,Jian Pei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic Shapley data valuation using a task-by-player matrix with locality-based updates; D-Shap enables fast, structured updates and self-valuation to bootstrap the matrix.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Shapley-based data valuation is accurate but computationally heavy; dynamic tasks and players require reusable, fast updates rather than one-shot, recomputed scores.

Method: Represent Shapley values as a task-by-player matrix; leverage task/coalition locality (small relevant player subsets, similar tasks yield similar valuations); perform structured matrix maintenance: interpolate new task valuations via structure-aware methods and limit player-induced updates to local blocks; introduce self-valuation to build the initial matrix directly from data using subset reuse and coverage-aware anchor selection.

Result: D-Shap updates tasks in milliseconds and reduces cost of updating players by up to 1,000×, while achieving valuation quality competitive with full recomputation across diverse models.

Conclusion: Dynamic, locality-aware matrix maintenance enables scalable, real-time Shapley data valuation; self-valuation eliminates the need for predefined tasks and supports robust initialization.

Abstract: Shapley-based data valuation provides a principled way to quantify the contribution of training data, but its high computational cost makes it impractical in dynamic settings where tasks and training players evolve. Existing methods treat Shapley computation as a one-shot process and collapse contributions into aggregated scores, preventing reuse and requiring recomputation under any change. We introduce a new perspective that represents Shapley values as a player-by-task matrix and formulates dynamic valuation as a structured matrix maintenance problem. We exploit the fact that each task depends on a small subset of training players and that similar tasks yield similar valuations, leading to utility locality and coalition locality. Based on these insights, we propose D-Shap, a dynamic valuation framework that enables efficient updates by modifying only a small portion of the matrix: new task valuations are inferred via structure-aware interpolation, while updates induced by new players are confined to affected local matrix blocks. To eliminate the need for pre-specified evaluation tasks, we introduce self-valuation, which constructs the initial matrix directly from training data, supported by scalable subset reuse and coverage-aware anchor selection. Experiments across diverse models show that D-Shap performs task updates in milliseconds and reduces the cost of player updates by up to three orders of magnitude, while achieving valuation quality competitive with full recomputation.

</details>


### [232] [The General Theory of Localization Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20635)
*Congwei Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unifying localization-method framework built on localization kernels and local means that reinterpret self-attention, connects to many ML methods, and can construct Transformers via hierarchical local models; offers adaptive, hierarchical, and non-local extensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a rigorous theoretical foundation for self-attention and localization-based learning; unify diverse ML approaches under a single framework; enable data-adaptive, flexible model design and architecture design, including deriving Transformer from hierarchical local models.

Method: Formalize the local(-ized) model and localization trick; define localization kernels and local means; analyze connections to kernel methods, lazy learning, MeanShift, relaxation labeling, Hopfield networks, LLE, fuzzy inference, and denoising autoencoders; extend to adaptive kernels, hierarchical local models, non-local models; show Transformer can be constructed from hierarchical local models.

Result: Mapping that situates many ML techniques within the localization framework; evidence that Transformers emerge from hierarchical local models; a theoretical lens to reinterpret and generalize state-of-the-art architectures; practical toolset for designing data-adaptive systems.

Conclusion: Localization method provides a unified theoretical framework and practical design toolkit for local and non-local modeling, enabling reinterpretation of existing models and the construction of flexible, adaptive architectures (including Transformers).

Abstract: This paper proposes a general machine learning framework called the localization method, which is fundamentally built on two core concepts: localization kernels and local means -- key components that underpin the self-attention mechanism. To establish a rigorous theoretical foundation, the framework is formally defined through two essential pillars: the formulation of the local(-ized) model and the localization trick. We systematically investigate the connections between the localization method and a wide range of existing machine learning models/methods, including (but not limited to) kernel methods, lazy learning, the MeanShift algorithm, relaxation labeling, Hopfield networks, local linear embedding (LLE), fuzzy inference, and denoising autoencoders (DAEs). By dissecting these relationships, we clarify the broader theoretical significance of the localization method and demonstrate its practical applicability across diverse machine learning tasks. Furthermore, we explore advanced extensions of the framework, such as adaptive kernels, hierarchical local models, and non-local models. Notably, we show that the Transformer -- a cornerstone of modern sequence modeling -- can be constructed using hierarchical local models, revealing the ability of the localization method to unify and generalize state-of-the-art architectures. This work not only provides a unified theoretical lens to reinterpret existing models but also offers new methodological tools for designing flexible, data-adaptive learning systems.

</details>


### [233] [Same Target, Different Basins: Hard vs. Soft Labels for Annotator Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20642)
*Mirerfan Gheibi,Gashin Ghazizadeh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hard-label delivery methods (multipass and stochastic label sampling) can outperform soft-label training when annotations per example are sparse; with full annotator distributions they match soft-label training. Multipass is strong when raw votes are available; SLS is a lightweight alternative that also matches soft-label training when full distributions exist.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Annotator disagreement can reflect epistemic uncertainty. The work investigates hard-label delivery as an alternative to collapsing votes or training on the empirical soft-label distribution, especially under sparse annotation regimes.

Method: Two hard-label methods: multipass (cycles through observed votes with fixed dataset size) and stochastic label sampling (SLS) (sample one label per example at the start of each epoch). Experiments on CIFAR-10H; ablations include deterministic control for multipass and shuffled SLS as a control. Comparisons with soft-label cross-entropy; analysis that SLS and soft-label cross-entropy optimize the same expected objective; additional analyses on optimization landscapes and OOD detection (SVHN, CIFAR-100).

Result: Hard-label delivery outperforms soft-label training when few annotations per example are available; gain increases as the sparse empirical target diverges from the full annotator distribution. With full annotator distributions, both hard-label methods achieve performance matching soft-label training. SLS and soft-label cross-entropy share the same expected objective. Hard-label delivery yields flatter loss basins, with supporting OOD evidence from SVHN and CIFAR-100.

Conclusion: Multipass is a strong practical default when raw vote counts are available; SLS provides a lightweight, competitive alternative under sparse labeling and matches soft-label training when full distributions are available.

Abstract: When annotators disagree, that disagreement can reflect epistemic uncertainty rather than simple label noise. We study hard-label delivery as an alternative to the usual choices of collapsing votes to a single label or training directly on the empirical soft-label distribution. We focus on two primary hard-label methods: multipass, which cycles through observed votes while keeping the dataset size fixed, and stochastic label sampling (SLS), which samples one label per example at the start of each epoch. On CIFAR-10H, we find that when only a small number of annotations per example is available, hard-label delivery improves over soft-label training, with larger improvements where the sparse empirical target is farther from the full annotator distribution. When full annotator distributions are available, both hard-label methods match soft-label training. We use deterministic control as an ablation of multipass and shuffled SLS as a control that breaks the example-to-distribution match. We also show that SLS and soft-label cross-entropy optimize the same expected objective. Hard-label delivery also converges to flatter basins, with supporting descriptive evidence from OOD detection on SVHN and CIFAR-100. Overall, these results suggest that multipass is a strong practical default when raw vote counts are available, while SLS offers a lightweight alternative that remains competitive when only a few votes per example are available and matches soft-label training when full annotator distributions are available.

</details>


### [234] [AVSD: Adaptive-View Self-Distillation by Balancing Consensus and Teacher-Specific Privileged Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20643)
*Duy Nguyen,Hanqi Xiao,Archiki Prasad,Zaid Khan,Anirban Das,Austin Zhang,Sambit Sahu,Hyunji Lee,Elias Stengel-Eskin,Mohit Bansal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive-View Self-Distillation (AVSD) uses multiple privileged-information views in self-distillation to reconstruct token-level supervision by separating cross-view consensus from view-specific residuals, enabling robust updates and outperforming single-view baselines on math and code tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-distillation with a privileged teacher creates asymmetry and dependency on view choice; leveraging multiple views can provide a stable consensus while exploiting view-specific residuals when appropriate.

Method: AVSD identifies a consensus signal shared across privileged views to guide updates, then selectively adds view-specific residual signals only when they align with the consensus and scale with its magnitude, effectively reconstructing token-level supervision.

Result: On math benchmarks (AIME24, AIME25, HMMT25) AVSD consistently outperforms single-view self-distillation and GRPO, with Avg@8 gains of 3.1% (Qwen3-8B) and 2.2% (Qwen3-4B). On code-generation benchmarks (Codeforces, LiveCodeBench v6) with Qwen3-8B, AVSD improves by 2.4% on average over the single-view baseline.

Conclusion: Adaptive multi-view self-distillation is effective across domains, mitigating the风险 of relying on a potentially mismatched privileged view and leveraging cross-view consensus to drive robust token-level supervision.

Abstract: Self-distillation enables language models to learn on-policy from their own trajectories by using the same model as both student and teacher, with the teacher being conditioned on privileged information unavailable to the student. Such information can come in different types or views, such as solutions, demonstrations, feedback, or final answers. This setup provides dense token-level feedback without relying on a separate external model, but creates a fundamental asymmetry: the teacher may rely on view-specific information that the student cannot access at inference time. Moreover, the best type of privileged information is often task-dependent, making it difficult to choose a single teacher view. In this work, we address both these challenges jointly by introducing AVSD (Adaptive-View Self-Distillation), a novel method of self-distillation with multiple privileged-information views, which reconstructs token-level supervision by separating stable cross-view consensus from view-specific residual signals. AVSD identifies the consensus signal shared across views, which provides a reliable update direction, and then selectively adds the view-specific residual signal to adjust the update magnitude when it both aligns with the consensus direction and remains proportionate to the consensus signal. Experiments on math competition benchmarks (AIME24, AIME25, and HMMT25) show that AVSD consistently outperforms both single-view self-distillation baselines and GRPO, achieving average Avg@8 gains of 3.1% and 2.2% over the strongest baselines on Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-4B, respectively. Moreover, on code-generation benchmarks (Codeforces, LiveCodeBench v6) using Qwen3-8B, AVSD outperforms the single-view self-distillation baseline by 2.4% on average.

</details>


### [235] [LT2: Linear-Time Looped Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20670)
*Chunyuan Deng,Yizhe Zhang,Rui-Jie Zhu,Yuanyuan Xu,Jiarui Liu,T. S. Eugene Ng,Hanjie Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LT2 enables linear-time looped transformers by replacing quadratic attention with linear-time or sparse attention, and uses hybrids to balance quality and efficiency; demonstrated gains on recall, state-tracking, language modeling, including a competitive Ouro-hybrid-1.4B model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the quadratic complexity of full attention in looped transformers to enable scalable, efficient small-language-models and broaden practical applicability.

Method: Propose LT2 family: LT2-linear (linear attention), LT2-sparse (sparse attention), and LT2-hybrid variants (interleaving attention types: GDN+DSA; Full+GDN). The authors provide theoretical formalizations of iterative memory refinement and expanding receptive field in looped settings, perform controlled recalls, state-tracking, and language modeling experiments, and demonstrate how to convert pre-trained LT to LT2-hybrid. They train Ouro-hybrid-1.4B (~1B tokens) and compare against industry 1B/4B models.

Result: Looping synergizes with linear and sparse attention: iterative memory refinement in linear attention and progressive receptive-field expansion in sparse attention. LT2-hybrid configurations (GDN+DSA and Full+GDN) achieve near-full-attention quality at linear-time cost or surpass standard looped transformers in both performance and efficiency. Ouro-hybrid-1.4B outperforms industry-level 1B models and is competitive with 4B models while retaining speed advantages.

Conclusion: LT2 provides a scalable pathway to efficient looped transformers, enabling effective small-language-models through linear-time or sparse attention, with hybrid variants offering favorable quality-efficiency trade-offs and practical model conversion.

Abstract: Looped Transformers (LT) have emerged as a powerful architecture by iterating their layers multiple times before decoding the final token. However, pairing them with full attention retains quadratic complexity, making them computationally expensive and slow. We introduce LT2 (Linear-Time Looped Transformers), a family of looped architectures that replace quadratic softmax attention with subquadratic, linear-time attention. We study two variants: LT2-linear with linear attention and LT2-sparse with sparse attention. We find that looping uniquely synergizes with these variants: it enables iterative memory refinement in linear attention and progressively expands the effective receptive field in sparse attention. We formalize these benefits theoretically and demonstrate consistent empirical gains across controlled recall, state-tracking, and language modeling tasks. We then explore LT2-hybrid, which combines different attention variants in a looped setting. Two variants are especially promising: LT2-hybrid (GDN+DSA), which interleaves linear and sparse attention to maximize efficiency and matches the standard looped transformer's quality at fully linear-time cost; and LT2-hybrid (Full+GDN), which interleaves GDN with a small fraction of full attention layers to maximize quality, surpassing the standard looped transformer in both performance and efficiency. We also show how to convert a pre-trained LT into an LT2-hybrid model. With about 1B tokens of training, our converted model, Ouro-hybrid-1.4B, outperforms industry-level 1B models and is competitive with industry-level 4B models while retaining the speed benefits of linear-time attention. Together, these results show a clear path toward making looped transformers more scalable and advancing efficient, capable small language models.

</details>


### [236] [Modular Multimodal Classification Without Fine-Tuning: A Simple Compositional Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20674)
*Herman Bergström,Aditya Mehrotra,Rahul G. Krishnan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A modular multimodal classification approach (CoMET) that freezes pretrained backbones, compresses features with PCA, and feeds them to a Tabular Foundation Model (TFM); introduces PALPooling for better token representation; claims state-of-the-art results without training and strong scalability across large class spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To simplify multimodal learning by leveraging strong frozen representations and a lightweight adaptor, avoiding expensive end-to-end fine-tuning while achieving robust, scalable performance across diverse modalities.

Method: Process each modality through a frozen pretrained backbone, apply PCA to the resulting embeddings, concatenate into a single feature vector, and input to a Tabular Foundation Model (TFM) for prediction. PALPooling is proposed as an adaptive token pooling step when CLS tokens align poorly with downstream tasks. No (or minimal) training is required; the approach combines fixed representations with a pretrained tabular model.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on diverse multimodal benchmarks without training. PCA alone suffices as an adaptor across modalities. PALPooling consistently improves representation quality when CLS alignment is weak. Demonstrates fast, scalable classification on hierarchical tasks with large class spaces (e.g., >500k samples, ~2000 classes) without fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Composing foundation models via frozen backbones and simple adapters is a powerful, out-of-the-box solution for multimodal learning, challenging the necessity of end-to-end training pipelines for new problems.

Abstract: We introduce CoMET, \textit{\textbf{C}omposing \textbf{M}odality \textbf{E}ncoders with \textbf{T}abular foundation models}, a simple yet highly competitive method for multimodal classification: pass each modality through a frozen pre-trained backbone, compress the resulting embeddings with PCA, and concatenate as input into a Tabular Foundation Model (TFM) for prediction. We show that PCA alone suffices to act as an adaptor yielding strong, robust performance across modalities. When the \texttt{CLS} tokens of the foundation model align poorly with downstream tasks, we propose \textbf{PALPooling}, a lightweight adaptive token pooler that consistently improves representation quality. By composing strong frozen representation learning backbones with TFMs, our approach achieves state-of-the-art results across diverse multimodal benchmarks without any training. On hierarchical tasks with large fine-grained class spaces, our approach enables fast and scalable classification, handling datasets with over 500,000 samples and 2,000 classes without any fine-tuning. Overall, our results show that the composition of foundation models is a simple, yet powerful, out-of-the-box solution for multimodal learning, challenging the necessity of complex, end-to-end training pipelines for new problems.

</details>


### [237] [Dynamic TMoE: A Drift-Aware Dynamic Mixture of Experts Framework for Non-Stationary Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20678)
*Jiawen Zhu,Shuhan Liu,Di Weng,Yingcai Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic TMoE unifies architectural evolution with temporal continuity for non-stationary time series by dynamically instantiating and pruning mixture-of-experts (MoE) using MMD-based distribution-shift detection and a temporal memory router with an anomaly repository, achieving state-of-the-art results (MSE -10.4%, MAE -7.8%) on nine benchmarks; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationary time series forecasting is challenged by evolving distribution shifts that static models fail to capture. Mixture-of-Experts offer decoupled handling of drift but are limited by fixed expert pools and memoryless routing. There is a need for adaptive, continuous, and test-time stable decision-making in the presence of abrupt regime shifts.

Method: Detect distribution shifts with Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) to dynamically instantiate heterogeneous experts and prune redundant ones, optimizing model capacity. Introduce a temporal memory router that leverages recurrent states and an anomaly repository to select experts in a context-aware, stable manner without test-time updates. Training-phase architectural evolution with a memory-based routing mechanism to maintain temporal continuity.

Result: Empirical evaluation on nine benchmarks shows state-of-the-art performance, reducing MSE by 10.4% and MAE by 7.8% relative to strong baselines. Code is released at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: The framework integrates architectural evolution and temporal continuity to better adapt to distribution shifts in non-stationary time series, achieving improved predictive accuracy without requiring test-time model updates.

Abstract: Non-stationary time series forecasting is challenged by evolving distribution shifts that static models struggle to capture. While Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures offer a promising paradigm for decoupling complex drift patterns, existing approaches are limited by fixed expert pools and memoryless routing, hampering their ability to adapt to abrupt regime shifts. To address this, we propose Dynamic TMoE, a framework that unifies architectural evolution with temporal continuity during learning phase. By detecting distribution shifts via Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD), we dynamically instantiate heterogeneous experts and prune redundant ones to optimize capacity. Additionally, a temporal memory router leverages recurrent states and an anomaly repository to ensure stable, context-aware expert selection without requiring test-time updates. Experiments on nine benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, reducing MSE by 10.4% and MAE by 7.8%. Code is available at https://github.com/andone-07/Dynamic-TMoE.

</details>


### [238] [Distributed Direct Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20696)
*Zhanhong Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First convergence and complexity analysis of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) in federated and decentralized RL, with personalized offline RL modeling and spectral-graph insights; shows how drift, communication and heterogeneity affect convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand theoretical behavior and provide guarantees for DPO in distributed, non-IID, communication-constrained settings to aid alignment with human judgments.

Method: Provide convergence and time-complexity analysis for federated DPO (client drift, communication frequency, heterogeneity) and decentralized DPO (graphs, spectral connectivity); model user-specific preference distributions; derive rates; conduct empirical validation on alignment benchmarks.

Result: Derived convergence rates and time-complexity results for federated and decentralized DPO; established dependence on heterogeneity, communication, and graph connectivity; empirical validation on standard benchmarks showing both guarantees and practical robustness.

Conclusion: This work delivers theoretical guarantees for DPO in distributed RL and demonstrates practical scalability; provides insights for designing communication-efficient, personalized preference-based RL systems.

Abstract: Preference-based reinforcement learning (RL) is a key paradigm for aligning policies with human judgments, yet its theoretical behavior in distributed settings where preference data are fragmented across heterogeneous users remains poorly understood. Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) avoids explicit reward modeling but lacks convergence guarantees under federated and decentralized training, where communication constraints and non-IID preferences fundamentally alter optimization dynamics. We provide the first convergence and time-complexity analysis of DPO in distributed environments. Modeling personalized offline RL with user-specific preference distributions, we characterize the induced global optimization landscape. For federated DPO, we derive convergence rates that quantify the impact of client drift, communication frequency, and preference heterogeneity; for decentralized DPO, we establish convergence over general communication graphs and show how spectral connectivity governs optimization speed and consensus. Empirically, we corroborate our theoretical insights on standard alignment benchmarks, demonstrating that our proposed methods not only enjoy strong theoretical guarantees but also deliver robust and scalable performance in practice. The code base is available here.

</details>


### [239] [Decision-Path Patterns as Tree Reliability Signals: Path-based Adaptive Weighting for Random Forest Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20716)
*Youngjoon Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reweight random forest votes using a class-conditional ratio based on the topology of root-to-leaf paths to estimate per-tree reliability; achieves significant accuracy gains on 30 binary benchmarks, robust to forest size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploit a signal in how the dominant class label flips along a tree's root-to-leaf decision path to assess tree reliability for per-sample weighting, while avoiding bias that correlates with the predicted class.

Method: Introduce a class-conditional ratio weighting scheme that guarantees zero expected class bias by construction. Evaluate under a shared-forest, shared-split protocol on 30 binary benchmarks (30 repeats) against RF, weighted RF, KNORA-Eliminate, and KNORA-Union.

Result: The proposed method is the sole scheme among the four comparisons to yield a statistically significant accuracy improvement over RF (p=0.018; Wilcoxon). Other methods show no significant gain (p>0.5). It avoids majority-recall regressions; minority-recall regressions occur on only 3/30 datasets. Gains are robust across forest sizes from 100 to 1000 trees.

Conclusion: Class-conditional ratio weighting provides a bias-free, robust per-tree reweighting that improves RF accuracy on binary benchmarks and is resilient to forest size, highlighting a limitation of classical dynamic ensemble selection methods which exhibit recall regressions.

Abstract: Random forests aggregate tree votes by simple majority, treating all trees as equally informative. We observe that the topological pattern along each tree's root-to-leaf decision path -- where and how often the dominant class label flips along it -- carries a signal of tree reliability that is exploitable for per-sample reweighting. The naive use of this signal is structurally confounded with the predicted class, so we propose a class-conditional ratio weighting that guarantees zero expected class bias by construction. On 30 binary classification benchmarks under a shared-forest, shared-split protocol with 30 repeats, the proposed method is the only one among four compared schemes -- RF, weighted RF, KNORA-Eliminate, KNORA-Union -- to yield a statistically significant accuracy improvement over RF (Wilcoxon p = 0.018), while the three alternatives all fail to do so (p > 0.5). It is also the only scheme without majority-recall regressions, with minority-recall regressions limited to 3/30 datasets -- a one-sided loss to which classical dynamic ensemble selection methods are susceptible. The gain is robust across forest sizes from 100 to 1000 trees.

</details>


### [240] [Memory-Efficient Partitioned DNN Inference on Resource-Constrained Android Crowds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20723)
*Lakshani Manamperi,Disumi Pathirana,Thiwanka Pathirana,Nipun Premarathna,Kutila Gunasekera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CROWDio's DNN pipeline scheduling enables practical ONNX inference on memory-constrained Android devices by distributing memory pressure across devices using five mechanisms, achieving low per-device RAM usage and reduced latency for DistilBERT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address memory and energy limitations for deploying large transformer models on commodity mobile hardware; enable distributed inference without modifying models.

Method: DNN pipeline scheduling subsystem with five mechanisms: JIT deferred partition loading; single-partition-resident constraint; 4-tier affinity scheduler; zlib-compressed tensor transport; streaming 1:1 dependency model; evaluated on DistilBERT across five Android devices; non-modified ONNX inference.

Result: Per-device RSS ~43±2 MB; battery draw ~50±3 mAh per run; streaming concurrency reduces batch latency ~34% below barrier synchronization.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility and effectiveness of distributed mobile inference under memory constraints with modest hardware; enables edge collaboration for transformer models without altering model architectures.

Abstract: Deploying large deep neural networks on memory-constrained mobile devices is a central challenge in edge ML. While compression, pruning, and quantization reduce per-parameter cost, transformer-based models remain too large for the 3.3-7.4 GB RAM envelope of commodity Android handsets. We present the DNN pipeline scheduling subsystem of CROWDio, which achieves practical ONNX inference across resource-constrained Android workers without model modification, by distributing memory pressure across devices via five mechanisms: JIT deferred partition loading, a single-partition-resident constraint, a 4-tier affinity scheduler, a zlib-compressed tensor transport, and a streaming 1:1 dependency model. Evaluated on DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019) (approximately 67 M parameters, SST-2) across five Android handsets over ten runs, our system holds peak per-device RSS to 43+-2 MB and limits battery draw to 50+-3 mAh per run, while streaming concurrency cuts batch latency 34% below barrier synchronisation.

</details>


### [241] [Hack-Verifiable Environments: Towards Evaluating Reward Hacking at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20744)
*Amit Roth,Ankur Samanta,Matan Halevy,Yoav Levine,Yonathan Efroni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Hack-Verifiable TextArena, a benchmark where reward-hacking opportunities are embedded by design to allow deterministic measurement; shows cross-model analysis and releases open-source tooling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward hacking undermines alignment and is hard to measure reliably at scale with post hoc analyses; a scalable, verifiable evaluation framework is needed.

Method: Embed detectable reward-hacking opportunities directly into environments (TextArena), creating Hack-Verifiable TextArena for automated, verifiable measurement. Evaluate across diverse language models and settings; release code openly.

Result: Empirical analysis of reward-hacking behavior across language models within the benchmark; demonstration of reliable measurement and reproducibility; provision of open-source code and benchmark.

Conclusion: The verifiable-in-environment approach enables scalable, reliable measurement of reward hacking, facilitating robust evaluation of alignment methods and reproducibility across models and tasks.

Abstract: Aligning autonomous agents with human intent remains a central challenge in modern AI. A key manifestation of this challenge is reward hacking, whereby agents appear successful under the evaluation signal while violating the intended objective. Reward hacking has been observed across a wide range of settings, yet methods for reliably measuring it at scale remain lacking. In this work, we introduce a new evaluation paradigm for measuring reward hacking. Whereas prior studies have primarily analyzed it post hoc by inspecting agent trajectories, we instead embed detectable reward hacking opportunities directly into environments. This makes their exploitation verifiable by design, enabling deterministic and automated measurement of whether and how agents exploit such vulnerabilities. We instantiate this approach in $\textit{TextArena}$ and release $\textit{Hack-Verifiable TextArena}$, a testbed in which reward hacking can be measured reliably. Using this benchmark, we analyze reward hacking behavior across language models in diverse environments and settings. We open source the code at https://github.com/MajoRoth/hack-verifiable-environments/.

</details>


### [242] [The Hidden Signal of Verifier Strictness: Controlling and Improving Step-Wise Verification via Selective Latent Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20745)
*Yefan Zhou,Yilun Zhou,Austin Xu,Soroush Vosoughi,Shafiq Joty,Jiang Gui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes VerifySteer to calibrate verifier strictness via latent signals and sample-level routing, achieving better verification performance with lower compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need to calibrate step-wise verification so verifiers neither miss errors nor over-reject correct steps; identify hidden-state markers that control strictness.

Method: Identify boundary-local hidden-state signal in verification paragraphs; demonstrate how hidden-state steering modulates strictness without fine-tuning; design VerifySteer to route samples and intervene selectively at paragraph boundaries using latent correctness cues.

Result: On ProcessBench and Hard2Verify, VerifySteer outperforms prompt optimization and activation steering baselines; competitive with self-consistency while requiring 4–7x less inference compute; complementary to fine-tuning improvements with additional gains.

Conclusion: Latent correctness signals can be exploited for efficient, targeted verification control, offering a practical alternative to full fine-tuning or brute-force prompting; VerifySteer provides a scalable mechanism for calibrating verifier behavior.

Abstract: Generative verifiers have emerged as a promising paradigm for step-wise verification, but their verification behavior is often poorly calibrated: they may be under-critical and miss erroneous steps, or over-critical and reject correct reasoning. We refer to this tendency to be overly lenient or overly critical as verifier strictness. In this work, we study whether verifier strictness can be controlled through hidden-state intervention. We uncover a verification-specific hidden-state signal: in step-wise verification, a verifier's tendency to accept or reject a solution step is encoded near the boundary of the corresponding verification paragraph. Exploiting this signal, we show that hidden-state steering can directly modulate verifier strictness without fine-tuning. However, uniform steering induces a trade-off between error detection and correctness certification. To address this, we propose VerifySteer, which exploits latent correctness signals for sample-level routing and selectively intervenes on paragraph boundaries. Experiments on ProcessBench and Hard2Verify show that VerifySteer outperforms prompt optimization and activation steering baselines, and is competitive with self-consistency while requiring 4-7x less inference compute. VerifySteer is also complementary to verification fine-tuning, providing further gains on top of fine-tuned verifiers. The code is available at https://github.com/YefanZhou/VerifySteer.

</details>


### [243] [The Devil is in the Condition Numbers: Why is GLU Better than non-GLU Structure?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20749)
*Xingyu Lyu,Qianqian Xu,Zhiyong Yang,Peisong Wen,Qingming Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GLU reshapes the NTK spectrum in two-layer networks, yielding a smaller condition number and a more compact eigenvalue distribution, which accelerates optimization and leads to faster convergence; GLU offers limited improvement to the generalization gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite empirical gains, the mechanism behind GLU's advantage is unclear. An NTK analysis of two-layer networks can reveal how GLU affects training dynamics and spectrum, clarifying whether gains arise from optimization speed or generalization changes.

Method: Analyze two-layer networks in the neural tangent kernel (NTK) regime with GLU gates. Examine the NTK spectrum, condition number, and eigenvalue distribution. Compare training dynamics and loss curves between GLU and non-GLU models. Validate generalization impact on architectures like ViT and GPT-2 to assess if GLU reduces the generalization gap.

Result: GLU reshapes the NTK spectrum, producing a smaller condition number and a more compact eigenvalue distribution. This spectral reshaping correlates with faster convergence and a characteristic loss-crossing between GLU and non-GLU models. Empirically, GLU has limited impact on reducing the generalization gap across models such as ViT and GPT-2.

Conclusion: The primary benefit of GLU lies in accelerated optimization due to altered spectral properties of the NTK, rather than substantial improvements in generalization. This explains the prevalent use of GLU in LLM architectures where training speed is critical.

Abstract: Gated Linear Units (GLU) and their variants are widely adopted in modern open-source large language model architectures and consistently outperform their non-gated counterparts, yet the underlying reasons for this advantage remain unclear. In this work, we study GLU by analyzing two-layer networks in the neural tangent kernel (NTK) regime. Our analysis reveals that the GLU structure reshapes the NTK spectrum, leading to a smaller condition number and a more compact eigenvalue distribution. Building on this finding, we further analyze the resulting training dynamics and show how the reshaped spectrum leads to faster convergence of GLU models, including a characteristic loss-crossing phenomenon observed between GLU and non-GLU models. Finally, we empirically observe that GLU has limited impact in reducing the generalization gap on various models, including ViT and GPT-2, suggesting that its primary benefit lies in accelerating optimization rather than reducing the generalization gap.

</details>


### [244] [Correcting Stochastic Update Bias in Preconditioned Language Model Optimizers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20756)
*Nikhil Nayak,Julia White,Urchade Zaratiana,Kelton Zhang,Henrijs Princis,Dhruv Atreja,Henry Fawcett,Matthew Thomas,George Hurn-Maloney,Ash Lewis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bias-corrected preconditioned optimizers via cross-fitting and variance-corrected inversion improve finite-sample bias and hold-out loss across AdamW, Sophia, Shampoo.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finite-sample biases in preconditioned SGD arise from gradient–preconditioner coupling and nonlinear inversion; correcting them yields better training efficiency and generalization.

Method: Single-batch bias correction: cross-fitting decouples gradient and preconditioner by estimating numerator and preconditioner from independent microbatch groups; variance-corrected inversion subtracts the leading delta-method bias term using microbatch variability; applicable to diagonal moment, diagonal curvature, and matrix preconditioning; instantiated in AdamW, Sophia, Shampoo.

Result: Held-out loss on Qwen2.5-0.5B pretrained models reduced by 0.15, 0.07, and 0.11 nats respectively; effects on mixed-quality pretraining and downstream instruction tuning are neutral-to-positive.

Conclusion: Bias correction is a practical technique to reduce finite-sample biases in preconditioned optimizers and improve training performance.

Abstract: Preconditioned optimizers are central to language model training, but their stochastic update rules are usually treated as direct approximations to population preconditioned descent. We show that this view misses two finite-sample biases. First, the gradient and preconditioner are typically estimated from the same minibatch, introducing gradient--preconditioner coupling bias. Second, even when the preconditioner estimate is unbiased, its inverse or inverse-root is generally biased because inversion is nonlinear. We propose a single-batch bias-correction framework that addresses both effects: cross-fitted preconditioning estimates the numerator and preconditioner from independent microbatch groups, while variance-corrected inversion uses microbatch variability to subtract the leading delta-method bias term. The framework applies to diagonal moment, diagonal curvature, and matrix preconditioning methods, instantiated in AdamW, Sophia, and Shampoo. Bias correction reduces held-out pretraining loss on Qwen2.5-0.5B by $0.15$, $0.07$, and $0.11$ nats, respectively; the effects on mixed-quality pretraining and downstream instruction tuning are consistently neutral-to-positive. Together, these results establish bias correction as a practical mechanism for reducing finite-sample update bias and improving the performance of preconditioned optimizers.

</details>


### [245] [ShapeBench: A Scalable Benchmark and Diagnostic Suite for Standardized Evaluation in Aerodynamic Shape Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20763)
*Shaghayegh Fazliani,Krissh Chawla,Jack Guo,Yiren Shen,Matthias Ihme,Madeleine Udell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ShapeBench is a comprehensive, open-source ASO benchmark with 103 tasks across 8 shape categories, offering surrogates and occasional CFD verification; results show optimizer rankings vary widely and no single method dominates; suggests need for general-purpose approaches like ShapeEvolve and further study before benchmark saturation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable fair, standardized evaluation of aerodynamic shape optimization (ASO) methods across diverse shape classes and objective formulations, addressing fragmentation and non-comparability in current benchmarks.

Method: ShapeBench provides a unified API with 103 tasks across eight shape categories and multiple optimization regimes. Each task includes a validated surrogate for fast search and, when feasible, a high-fidelity CFD pipeline for final verification. A consistent budget metric and reproducible protocol enable fair comparison of classical and LLM-driven methods, including ShapeEvolve, a domain-specialized evolutionary LLM baseline.

Result: Results show substantial variance in optimizer rankings across shape categories and formulations; mean pairwise Spearman ρ = 0.013, indicating poor cross-task generalization of single-task conclusions. The benchmark is far from saturation; classical methods are not universally applicable across all tasks, underscoring the need for more general approaches.

Conclusion: ShapeBench enables reproducible, fair cross-task comparisons in ASO and highlights the necessity for general-purpose optimization strategies and further activity across diverse shape classes and formulations; supports evaluating both traditional and LLM-driven approaches such as ShapeEvolve.

Abstract: Rapid progress in aerodynamic shape optimization (ASO) has outpaced currently-available standardized evaluation frameworks. Fair comparison requires a unified benchmark spanning diverse shape classes, objective formulations, and matched-budget state-of-the-art baselines. We introduce ShapeBench, an open-source ASO benchmark with a unified API spanning 103 tasks across eight shape categories and multiple optimization regimes. Each ShapeBench task includes a validated surrogate for fast search; when feasible, a high-fidelity Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) pipeline for final verification is available, enabling systematic fidelity-gap analysis. ShapeBench provides a reproducible protocol with well-configured baselines to compare fairly using a consistent budget metric, allowing for comparison among both classical and LLM-driven methods, including general-purpose optimizers and a new domain-specialized evolutionary LLM baseline, ShapeEvolve. Results on ShapeBench demonstrate substantial variance in optimizer rankings across shape categories and problem formulations, with mean pairwise Spearman $ρ= 0.013$, so single-task conclusions do not reliably generalize across problem classes. The benchmark is also far from saturation; classical methods are rarely applicable across all shape categories and tasks, further highlighting the need for more general-purpose approaches.

</details>


### [246] [Cumulative Meta-Learning from Active Learning Queries for Robustness to Spurious Correlations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20771)
*Kin Whye Chew,Jingxian Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CAML is a cumulative active meta-learning framework for active learning that meta-learns an inductive bias across rounds to counter spurious correlations, improving minority-group accuracy and generalization beyond standard active-learning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spurious correlations cause models to rely on irrelevant patterns; standard AL updates only likelihood, which can be diluted in deep models. A cumulative, meta-learned inductive bias could steer adaptation toward core features across rounds.

Method: Frame each AL round as a meta-learning task. Treat the current labeled data as meta-train for adaptation, and the newly queried batch as meta-test for evaluating generalization. Maintain a cumulative inductive bias across rounds to capture sequential dependencies, with a theoretical analysis showing interaction terms between earlier inductive biases and later query objectives. Empirically validate on spurious-correlation benchmarks with multiple AL strategies.

Result: CAML yields substantial gains in minority-group accuracy: 27.8% on Dominoes, 29.9% on Waterbirds, 14.3% on SpuCo, and 24.0% on CivilComments.

Conclusion: A cumulative meta-learning approach to active learning can better align model adaptation with core features across rounds, improving robustness to spurious correlations and fairness across benchmarks.

Abstract: Spurious correlations in real-world datasets cause machine learning models to rely on irrelevant patterns, undermining reliability, generalization, and fairness. Active learning offers a promising way to address this failure mode by querying informative samples that distinguish core features from spurious ones. However, standard active-learning methods simply append queried examples to the labeled set, effectively updating only the likelihood term. In deep learning regimes, the influence of these informative samples can be diluted by the larger labeled set and memorized by overparameterized models. We propose Cumulative Active Meta-Learning (CAML), an active-learning framework that uses queried examples to meta-learn the prior, or inductive bias, governing how the model adapts. CAML casts each active-learning round as a meta-learning task: the current labeled set serves as meta-train data for adaptation, while the newly queried batch serves as meta-test data for evaluating generalization. Unlike conventional meta-learning, which treats tasks as independent and identically distributed, CAML exploits the sequential dependence between active-learning rounds by maintaining a cumulative inductive bias that is progressively refined. Theoretically, we show that this cumulative formulation introduces interaction terms that couple earlier meta-learned inductive biases with later query-induced objectives, capturing dependencies absent from standard meta-learning. Empirically, CAML improves minority-group accuracy across spurious-correlation benchmarks and acquisition strategies, with gains of up to 27.8% on Dominoes, 29.9% on Waterbirds, 14.3% on SpuCo, and 24.0% on CivilComments.

</details>


### [247] [Learning to Think in Physics: Breaking Shortcut Learning in Scientific Diffusion via Representation Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20780)
*Haozhe Jia,Pengyu Yin,Wenshuo Chen,Shaofeng Liang,Lei Wang,Bowen Tian,Xiucheng Wang,Nanqian Jia,Yutao Yue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: REPA-P is a teacher-free, architecture-agnostic framework that enforces physics in diffusion models by attaching lightweight 1x1 projection heads to select intermediate layers to predict physical states and applying PDE residual losses during training; heads are discarded at inference, yielding zero overhead. It improves convergence, PDE residuals, and robustness across four PDE tasks and backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PDE constraints are usually applied only to final outputs, allowing intermediate representations to shortcut under distribution shifts; enforcing physics in early representations can improve generalization and sample efficiency.

Method: Attach small 1x1 projection heads to selected layers; decode activations into physical quantities; compute PDE residual losses during training on these predictions; train with both intermediate residuals and standard output losses; discard heads at inference; test on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer backbones across four PDE tasks.

Result: Accelerates convergence by up to 2x; reduces physics residuals by up to 66.4%; improves out-of-distribution robustness by up to 49.3%; gains consistent across backbones; ablations show only a small set of layers are enough; zero overhead inference.

Conclusion: REPA-P effectively enforces physics in intermediate representations, providing strong, architecture-agnostic improvements for physics-informed diffusion models with negligible inference cost.

Abstract: Physics-informed diffusion models typically enforce PDE constraints only on final outputs, leaving intermediate representations unconstrained and prone to shortcut learning under shifted boundary conditions. We introduce **REPA-P**, a teacher-free, architecture-agnostic framework that aligns intermediate features with physical states using first-principles residuals. REPA-P attaches lightweight $1{\times}1$ projection heads to selected layers, decodes hidden activations into physical quantities, and applies PDE residual losses during training. These heads are discarded at inference, introducing **zero overhead**. Across four PDE tasks, including Darcy flow, topology optimization, electrostatic potential, and turbulent channel flow, REPA-P accelerates convergence by up to $2{\times}$, reduces physics residuals by up to $66.4\%$, and improves out-of-distribution robustness by up to $49.3\%$, with consistent gains on both U-Net and Diffusion Transformer backbones. Ablations show that supervising a small set of intermediate layers captures most benefits and complements output-level physics losses. Code is available at [https://github.com/Hxxxz0/REPA-P](https://github.com/Hxxxz0/REPA-P).

</details>


### [248] [Causal Machine Learning Is Not a Panacea: A Roadmap for Observational Causal Inference in Health](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20782)
*Donna Tjandra,Trenton Chang,Sonali Parbhoo,Rajesh Ranganath,Andre Kurepa Waschka,William Mitchell,Maggie Makar,Shalmali Joshi,Finale Doshi-Velez,Leo Anthony Celi,Jenna Wiens*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Roadmap for applying causal ML to observational data, emphasizing validity assumptions, responsible use, and a template to improve rigor and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing availability of large observational datasets and challenges of running randomized trials have spurred interest in causal ML for causal inference; need for clear guidelines to apply it responsibly.

Method: Conceptual roadmap outlining how to assess validity assumptions within data, bridge clinical experts and ML practitioners, and provide a template to strengthen rigor and interpretability.

Result: Highlights that causal ML limitations are under-appreciated across disciplines, identifies knowledge gaps and risks of biased results if assumptions are not met; proposes guidance for responsible use.

Conclusion: Causal ML can generate causal hypotheses when assumptions and modeling choices are justified; the paper offers a template to enhance rigor and interpretability of causal analyses.

Abstract: Objective: The growing availability of large-scale observational clinical datasets and challenges in conducting randomized controlled trials have spurred enthusiasm in using causal machine learning (ML) for causal inference in observational data. We present a roadmap for applying causal ML to observational data. Materials and methods: We outline the importance of assessing validity assumptions within available data and applying causal ML responsibly for clinical experts using causal ML and ML practitioners with limited clinical expertise. Observations: Despite advances in causal ML, its limitations remain largely under-appreciated across disciplines. This gap in shared knowledge may impact the validity of findings. Discussion: Causal assumptions must be satisfied and modeling choices justified. Otherwise, these approaches risk producing biased or misleading results, with consequences for clinical research and patient care. Conclusion: Causal ML can be a powerful tool for generating causal hypotheses. We provide a template to strengthen the rigor and interpretability of causal analyses.

</details>


### [249] [Beyond Numerical Features: CNN-Driven Algorithm Selection via Contour Plots for Continuous Black-Box Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20797)
*Yiliang Yuan,Xiang Shi,Mustafa Misir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Contour-map representations fed to a CNN regressor for per-instance algorithm selection in black-box optimization; achieves SBS-like or better performance and is competitive with feature-based baselines on BBOB 2009, and remains effective under DeepELA with windowed views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on handcrafted numerical descriptors (ELA/Deep-ELA) by leveraging image-based representations that capture spatial structure of probed landscapes for per-instance solver selection.

Method: Create multiple contour-view visualizations of probed landscapes; feed stacked or encoded per-view images into a CNN regressor that predicts per-solver performance; select the solver with the predicted best value; evaluate on BBOB 2009 single-objective, then a bi-objective DeepELA setting using windowed contour views.

Result: Selectors outperform the single best solver and are competitive with feature-based baselines in single-objective BBOB; the image-based approach also proves competitive in a bi-objective DeepELA setting.

Conclusion: Simple vision models can harness spatial structure in probed landscapes for algorithm selection without handcrafted ELA features, offering a viable alternative to traditional descriptor-based methods.

Abstract: The present paper introduces a new representation-driven approach to per-instance algorithm selection, applied to black-box optimization, for automatically choosing the most promising solver from a fixed portfolio. Prior work in continuous optimization largely relies on numerical descriptors, including Exploratory Landscape Analysis features and learned embeddings such as Deep-ELA. This work studies a complementary representation: contour-map visualizations of probed landscapes. A CNN regressor takes multiple instance-specific contour views (stacked or encoded per view and aggregated) and predicts per-solver performance, enabling selection by the predicted best value. On the standard BBOB 2009 single-objective protocol, the resulting selectors significantly outperform the single best solver (SBS) and are competitive with feature-based baselines. A subsequent bi-objective evaluation under the DeepELA setting further indicates that the same image-based principle can be competitive when using windowed contour views. Overall, the results suggest that simple vision models can exploit spatial structure in probed landscapes for algorithm selection without handcrafted ELA features.

</details>


### [250] [Most Transformer Modifications Still Do Not Transfer at 1-3B: A 2020-2026 Update to Narang et al. (2021) with Downstream Evaluation and a Noise Floor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20798)
*Yang Zhao,Jiahao Lu,Bin Huang,Guhua Zhang,Jie Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Most post-2021 Transformer modifications fail to transfer at the 1.2B–3B scale under iso-data/compute/recipe controls; only a couple transfer, with stability issues for some. Downstream evaluation and cross-scale testing are essential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reassess Narang et al. (2021) conclusions in the current landscape of larger models and new modification types, using strict fair-comparison controls and downstream metrics.

Method: Evaluate 20 post-2021 Transformer modifications at 1.2B and 3B under iso-data, iso-compute, and iso-recipe; use multi-seed baseline noise floor; CLIMB-12 downstream evaluation as primary metric; apply Bonferroni correction; assess stability and loss-downstream gaps.

Result: Reproduces the earlier finding: most modifications do not transfer. Of 20, only two show transfer at 1.2B after correction; one of these fails to train stably at 3B under the shared recipe. Loss-downstream gap enlarges for attention-output modifications; two failures approach baseline loss but lose 6–16 CLIMB points.

Conclusion: Noise-floor reporting, downstream evaluation, and cross-scale stability testing are prerequisites for architecture comparisons at 1–3B.

Abstract: Narang et al. (2021) evaluated 40+ Transformer modifications at T5-base scale and concluded that most did not transfer. Five years later, the typical working regime has moved to 1-3B parameters, downstream evaluation has replaced pretraining perplexity, and a substantially different catalogue of modifications has emerged. We revisit their question by testing 20 post-2021 Transformer modifications at 1.2B and 3B under strict iso-data, iso-compute, iso-recipe control, with a multi-seed baseline noise floor and CLIMB-12 downstream evaluation as the primary metric. The central finding reproduces theirs at this curated set: most modifications do not transfer. Of the 20 modifications, only two clear Bonferroni correction at 1.2B; one of those two further fails to train stably at 3B under the shared recipe. We also find that the loss-downstream gap reported by Tay et al. (2023) enlarges several-fold for attention-output modifications: two significant failures converge to within 2-3% of baseline validation loss yet drop 6-16 CLIMB-points.
