We conclude that noise-floor reporting, downstream evaluation, and cross-scale stability testing are now prerequisites for architecture comparisons at 1-3B.

</details>


### [251] [Tunable MAGMAX: Preference-Aware Model Merging for Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20803)
*Kei Hiroshima,Kento Uchida,Shinichi Shirakawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tunable MAGMAX enables preference-aware merging of task-specific parameters in continual learning, using a tunable preference vector to control task-wise performance, with automatic construction of preferences from limited target data; it achieves competitive results and adapts to deployment environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current continual learning with model merging often optimizes average accuracy and neglects deployment-specific constraints or user preferences. There is a need for mechanisms to tailor multi-task performance to diverse environments and automate preference specification using limited data.

Method: Introduce a preference vector that selects the number of elements from each task vector during MAGMAX merging, enabling tunable, task-wise performance. Develop an automatic method to construct suitable preference vectors using small amounts of target environment data and datasets from training tasks.

Result: Experiments on continual learning benchmarks show Tunable MAGMAX can control task-wise performance and adapt merged models to diverse environments, achieving superior or comparable performance to baseline methods.

Conclusion: Tunable MAGMAX provides a practical, adaptable solution for deploying continual learning models across environments with varying task-performance preferences, though further work on scalability and robustness could be explored.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) aims to train models sequentially on multiple tasks while mitigating catastrophic forgetting of previously learned knowledge. Recent advances in large pre-trained models (LPMs) and model merging techniques, such as MAGMAX, have demonstrated effective CL performance by combining task-specific parameters. However, existing methods primarily focus on average performance across all tasks and do not adequately address how to construct models accommodating different deployment environments or varying user preferences. This paper proposes a model merging framework, termed Tunable MAGMAX, which enables preference-aware control of task-specific performance in CL. Our method introduces a preference vector that controls the number of elements selected from each task vector during model merging, allowing us to adjust the merged model performance according to their deployment needs. We further propose a method for automatically constructing appropriate preference vectors by leveraging small amounts of target environment data and datasets from model training tasks, thereby eliminating the need for manual specification. The experimental result on CL benchmark tasks demonstrates that Tunable MAGMAX effectively controls task-wise performance and successfully adapts merged models to various target environments. The proposed Tunable MAGMAX achieves superior or comparable performance to baseline methods, making it a practical solution for deploying CL models to various environments where the preferences of each task performance differ.

</details>


### [252] [Markovian Circuit Tracing for Transformer State Dynamic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20824)
*Abdullah X*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MCT provides a benchmark and pipeline to test whether transformer activations encode coarse state transitions, using synthetic HMM tasks to quantify how well small transformers approximate Bayes-like predictions and recover latent state structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable interpretable evaluation of transformer internal dynamics by aligning activations with known latent-state transitions, and provide a controlled benchmark for state-dynamics interpretability.

Method: Create synthetic HMM tasks with known latent states, transitions, Bayesian beliefs, Bayes-optimal predictions, and counterfactual targets. Train tiny causal transformers on next-token prediction. Analyze residual activations for latent-state information. Apply state abstraction extraction to recover coarse transition signals. Perform state-forcing by patching recovered-state centroids and evaluate KL divergence to counterfactual targets, with multiple regimes and controls.

Result: Transformers achieve near-Bayes next-token predictions with small mean excess loss over Bayes (~0.0138). Residual activations contain partial Bayes-belief information. State abstractions recover coarse transition signals strongest in persistent/low-state regimes; weaker in ambiguous-emission and six-state regimes. State-forcing (patching centroid) reduces KL divergence from 0.1957 to 0.0532 on average, beating wrong-state, mean-activation, random-activation, and shuffled-label controls.

Conclusion: MCT provides a controlled benchmark and simple pipeline for evaluating transformer state dynamics and interpretability, enabling quantifiable assessments of how internal activations align with latent-state transitions.

Abstract: Many sequence computations are easier to study as movement through internal states than as isolated local circuits. We introduce Markovian Circuit Tracing (MCT), a diagnostic pipeline for testing whether transformer activations contain coarse state-transition structure. The benchmark uses synthetic Hidden Markov Model (HMM) tasks where latent states, transition matrices, Bayesian belief vectors, Bayes-optimal predictions, and forced-state counterfactual targets are known exactly. Across six HMM families and three seeds per family, tiny causal transformers learn near-Bayes next-token predictors, with mean excess loss over Bayes of 0.0138. Residual activations contain partial Bayesian belief information in this controlled synthetic benchmark. State abstractions extracted from these activations recover coarse transition signal, strongest in persistent and lower-state regimes, and weaker in ambiguous-emission and six-state regimes. The clearest result comes from state forcing. Patching a recovered-state centroid reduces KL to the exact HMM counterfactual target from 0.1957 in the unpatched model to 0.0532 on average, beating wrong-state, mean-activation, random-activation, and shuffled-label controls. The contribution is a controlled benchmark and evaluation framework for transformer state-dynamics interpretability, with MCT as a simple reference pipeline

</details>


### [253] [Finite-Time Regret Analysis of Retry-Aware Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20854)
*Bingkui Tong,Junpei Komiyama,Soichiro Nishimori,Paavo Parmas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReMax optimizes a posterior-based sampling to maximize the expected maximum reward across M virtual draws; for Gaussian rewards (M=2) it achieves the first sublinear regret bound and reveals an underestimation effect that can cause over-exploitation; experiments show competitive gains over KL-UCB and TS with mild underestimation and variance scaling helps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand regret properties and practical performance of retry-aware objectives (pass@k, max@k) in Bayesian bandits, and clarify why ReMax can behave differently from standard exploration strategies.

Method: Characterize the optimal ReMax distribution under Gaussian rewards with M=2 via an expected-improvement balance condition; decompose regret into saturation of suboptimal arms and an arm underestimation effect; provide sublinear regret analysis; supplement with experiments comparing against KL-UCB and Thompson sampling and analyzing posterior-variance scaling.

Result: First sublinear regret bound for ReMax in the Gaussian M=2 setting; separation of saturation and underestimation effects; explanation of ReMax's exploitative tendency; empirical results showing advantages under mild underestimation; variance scaling mitigates severe underestimation.

Conclusion: This work clarifies the conditions under which ReMax can outperform standard bandit algorithms and demonstrates the necessity of accounting for underestimation in the regret analysis; variance-aware adjustments improve robustness.

Abstract: We study a stochastic bandit algorithm motivated by retry-aware objectives that value the best outcome among multiple attempts, such as pass@$k$ and max@$k$. Given a posterior over arm values, ReMax chooses a sampling distribution that maximizes the posterior expected maximum reward over $M$ virtual draws. Although this objective was introduced in reinforcement learning as an exploration mechanism under uncertainty, its regret properties in bandit problems have remained unclear. For Gaussian rewards and the first nontrivial case $M=2$, we characterize the optimal ReMax distribution through an expected-improvement balance condition and prove the first sublinear regret bound for ReMax. Our analysis separates the usual saturation behavior of suboptimal arms from a ReMax-specific underestimation effect, in which the optimal arm may be sampled too rarely after an unfavorable estimate. This explains why ReMax can be more exploitative than Thompson sampling (TS) and why its regret analysis is technically delicate. Experiments support this picture: ReMax often outperforms KL-UCB and Thompson sampling under mild underestimation, while posterior-variance scaling empirically mitigates severe underestimation.

</details>


### [254] [Multi-Step Likelihood-Ratio Correction for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20865)
*Deokgyu Yoon,Hyungkyu Kang,Joongkyu Lee,Byeongchan Kim,Gyungin Shin,Sungrae Park,Min-hwan Oh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes N-step forward trace to augment PPO for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), yielding NFPO; a tunable bridge between PPO and true policy gradient with theoretical bias-variance control and empirical gains on reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the local, biased nature of PPO surrogate objectives in RLVR, which can hinder optimal policy improvement; introduce a mechanism to better balance bias and variance while allowing a continuum from PPO to the exact gradient.

Method: Introduce the N-step forward trace that augments the PPO surrogate with the cumulative likelihood ratio of the next N-1 tokens; embed this into a masked policy gradient framework to form the NFPO algorithm, establishing a continuous bridge between PPO and the exact policy gradient.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows that, for an appropriate choice of N, NFPO yields a tighter policy-improvement bound than PPO; empirical experiments on comprehensive reasoning benchmarks demonstrate consistent performance gains over baselines.

Conclusion: NFPO provides a principled bias-variance trade-off control in RLVR, with theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements, by smoothly interpolating between PPO and the exact policy gradient.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) plays a pivotal role in improving the reasoning ability of large language models. However, widely used PPO surrogate objectives are fundamentally local, as they rely on a local approximation of the exact policy gradient objective. While this approximation improves stability by reducing the variance induced by importance sampling, it also introduces structural bias into the surrogate objective, which must be controlled through trust region mechanisms. In this work, we introduce the $N$-step forward trace, which augments the PPO surrogate objective using the cumulative likelihood ratio of the next $N-1$ tokens. Building on this idea, we propose $N$-Step Forward-Trace Policy Optimization (NFPO), a practical RLVR algorithm that integrates the $N$-step forward trace into the masked policy gradient framework. NFPO provides a continuous bridge between the PPO surrogate objective and the exact policy gradient objective, offering a principled mechanism for controlling the bias-variance trade-off. Our theoretical analysis shows that, with an appropriate choice of $N$, the proposed objective yields a tighter policy-improvement bound than the standard PPO surrogate. Experiments on comprehensive reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that NFPO consistently improves performance, supporting our theoretical findings.

</details>


### [255] [LOSCAR-SGD: Local SGD with Communication-Computation Overlap and Delay-Corrected Sparse Model Averaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20866)
*Yassine Maziane,Ammar Mahran,Artavazd Maranjyan,Peter Richtárik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LOSAR-SGD is a heterogeneous Local SGD method that sparsifies communication and overlaps computation with communication, using a delay-corrected merge to incorporate delayed information. It provides convergence guarantees for smooth non-convex objectives and demonstrates practical efficiency improvements over naive methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Communication is the main bottleneck in distributed and federated learning, especially at scale and on slow networks. A practical approach combines compression, local updates, and communication-computation overlap, but there is limited theory for their joint use, particularly under worker heterogeneity.

Method: Introduce LOSCAR-SGD, a Local SGD variant where only a sparse subset of model coordinates is communicated and local optimization continues during communication. A delay-corrected merge rule integrates delayed synchronized information without discarding progress, accommodating heterogeneous numbers of local steps per worker.

Result: The authors provide convergence guarantees for smooth non-convex objectives and analyze how sparsity, overlap, and worker heterogeneity affect convergence rate. They claim this is the first theoretical treatment of this combination of ingredients. Experiments show that overlap reduces training time and the delay-corrected merge outperforms naive overwriting.

Conclusion: This work delivers the first theory for combining compression, local updates, and overlap in a heterogeneous setting, with practical benefits demonstrated in reduced training time and improved merge behavior via delay correction.

Abstract: Communication is a major bottleneck in distributed learning, especially in large-scale settings and in federated learning environments with slow links. Three standard ways to reduce this cost are communication compression, local training, and communication-computation overlap. Methods that combine these ingredients are used in practice and have been found to be effective for large-scale training, but there is little theory for methods that combine all three. We study a heterogeneous-compute setting in which different workers may take different numbers of local steps, and we propose LOSCAR-SGD, a Local SGD method that communicates only a sparse subset of model coordinates and continues optimizing while communication is in flight. A key ingredient is a delay-corrected merge rule that incorporates delayed synchronized information without discarding the progress made during the overlap phase. We give convergence guarantees for smooth non-convex objectives and show how sparsity, overlap, and worker heterogeneity affect the rate. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first theory for this combination of ingredients. Experiments further show that communication-computation overlap reduces training time and that the delay-corrected merge outperforms naive overwriting.

</details>


### [256] [Runtime-Certified Bounded-Error Quantized Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20868)
*Dean Calver*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Runtime-certified attention with a tiered KV cache: INT8 keys and INT4 values on GPU, FP16 originals in RAM; online per-head/per-step error bounds guide adaptive precision and multi-stage fallback to exact dense attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate approximation error from KV cache quantization and provide runtime detection/recovery beyond empirical averages.

Method: Tiered storage (INT8 keys, INT4 values in GPU; FP16 originals in RAM) with a two-term error decomposition (key-quantization distortion and value reconstruction error). Online per-head/per-step error bounds drive adaptive precision selection and a multi-stage fallback ladder to guarantee recovery to exact dense attention when needed.

Result: Benchmarking on PG-19, NIAH, and RULER with LLaMA 3.1-8B contexts up to 128K shows the system matches dense FP16 KV quality within noise for language modeling and retrieval, while recovering catastrophic failures seen in naive INT8/INT4 baselines. Value-sensitive tasks at short context reveal a trade-off between compression and fidelity, addressable via tighter value tolerances or FP16-value fallback; certification is local (per-head/per-step) and ensures bounded or exact recovery, reframing KV cache quantization as runtime-verified computation.

Conclusion: The work presents a runtime-verified KV cache approach that prioritizes safe, quality-constrained aggressive compression over raw speedups, enabling robust deployment of quantized KV caches with deterministic fallback guarantees.

Abstract: KV cache quantization reduces the memory cost of long-context LLM inference, but introduces approximation error that is typically validated only empirically. Existing systems rely on average-case robustness, with no mechanism to detect or recover from failures at runtime. We present a tiered KV cache architecture that enables runtime-certified attention: INT8 keys and INT4 values are stored in GPU memory, while FP16 originals are retained in system RAM for deterministic fallback. A two-term error decomposition yields per-head, per-step bounds on (i) attention distribution distortion from key quantization and (ii) value reconstruction error. These bounds are computed online and used to drive adaptive precision selection and a multi-stage fallback ladder, which guarantees recovery to the exact dense attention output when required. Across PG-19, NIAH, and RULER benchmarks on LLaMA~3.1-8B with contexts up to 128K, the system matches dense FP16 KV quality within noise for language modelling and retrieval tasks, while recovering catastrophic failures observed in naive INT8/INT4 baselines. Value-sensitive tasks at short context expose a controlled trade-off between compression and fidelity, which can be eliminated via tighter value tolerances or FP16-value fallback. The certification is local (per-head, per-step) and does not guarantee end-to-end model correctness, but ensures that each attention computation is either bounded relative to an FP16 reference or exactly recovered via fallback. This reframes KV cache quantization as a runtime-verified computation rather than a fixed approximation. The goal is not raw speedups, but enabling safe deployment of aggressive KV compression under strict quality constraints.

</details>


### [257] [CIG: Exploration via Conditional Information Gain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20878)
*Tim Joseph,Marcus Fechner,Philipp Stegmaier,Karam Daaboul,J. Marius Zöllner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Conditional Information Gain (CIG) as a scalable, principled intrinsic reward for RL exploration. It uses a log-determinant of an ensemble disagreement kernel to produce per-step, causally conditioned rewards that depend on both the replay buffer and the rollout prefix, enabling high-dimensional state spaces. Demonstrated in model-based RL with short rollouts across 12 tasks (MiniGrid and OGBench) and both clean and stochastic settings; it matches or exceeds prior methods and is robust to distractors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing intrinsic reward approaches either separate lifelong and episodic signals, rely on heuristic balances, or require dynamics models (e.g., Gaussian processes) that do not scale to high-dimensional states. Trajectory-level information gain is intractable for deep models when conditioning on multiple factors (replay buffer and rollout prefix). A scalable, per-step intrinsic reward that preserves conditioning on both sides and scales to high-dimensional spaces is needed.

Method: Derive Conditional Information Gain (CIG) as a tractable surrogate by formulating a log-determinant objective over an ensemble disagreement kernel. Efficient Cholesky factorization yields per-step rewards that are causal and respect both the replay buffer and the rollout prefix conditioning. Implemented in a model-based setting with short rollouts; internal within-rollout corrections are left largely unexplored.

Result: Empirical evaluation across twelve tasks spanning discrete MiniGrid and continuous OGBench, in both clean and stochastic-distractor settings: CIG matches or outperforms prior exploration methods and shows robustness to stochastic distractors.

Conclusion: CIG provides a scalable, principled intrinsic reward that captures both conditioning sets (replay buffer and rollout prefix) and delivers per-step guidance suitable for high-dimensional state spaces in model-based RL with short rollouts, demonstrated across diverse task domains.

Abstract: Intrinsic rewards for exploration in reinforcement learning condition on different contexts: lifelong rewards score each transition against accumulated experience but ignore within-rollout redundancy; episodic rewards penalize intra-trajectory repetition but discard lifetime progress. Hybrid methods combine both signals through heuristic weights or require Gaussian-process dynamics that do not scale beyond low-dimensional state spaces. Trajectory-level information gain decomposes into per-step terms that condition on the replay buffer and rollout prefix simultaneously, but remains intractable for deep models. We derive the Conditional Information Gain (CIG) reward as a tractable surrogate: a log-determinant objective over an ensemble disagreement kernel whose Cholesky factorization yields causal per-step rewards that retain both conditioning sets while scaling to high-dimensional state spaces. We instantiate CIG in a model-based setting, where rollouts are short and within-rollout corrections remain largely unexplored. Across twelve tasks spanning discrete (MiniGrid) and continuous control (OGBench), in both clean and stochastic-distractor settings, CIG outperforms or matches prior exploration methods while remaining robust to stochastic distractors.

</details>


### [258] [NeighborDiv: Training-free Zero-shot Generalist Graph Anomaly Detection via Neighbor Diversity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20879)
*Kaifeng Wei,Teng Liu,Liang Dong,Xiubo Liang,Yuke Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A training-free GGAD framework, NeighborDiv, uses neighbor-to-neighbor diversity (variance of inter-neighbor feature similarities) to detect anomalies, achieving state-of-the-art performance with cross-domain stability and no training data dependency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GGAD methods predominantly rely on node-to-neighbor consistency, which leads to complex pipelines, heavy data requirements, high computation, and unstable cross-domain generalization. A shift to the neighbor-to-neighbor diversity paradigm seeks a simpler, more robust anomaly signal.

Method: Introduce NeighborDiv, quantifying neighbor diversity via the variance of inter-neighbor feature similarities to capture how a node organizes its local graph, operating in a training-free manner and decoupled from node-to-neighbor consistency frameworks.

Result: NeighorDiv achieves state-of-the-art performance on two standard GGAD evaluation paradigms, with relative gains of 10.25% (AUC) and 17.78% (AP) over the second-best baseline under Single-Domain Independent Training (SDIT), and 6.89% (AUC) and 9.58% (AP) under Unified Multi-Domain Training (UMDT). It also exhibits zero performance volatility across datasets, indicating no training-set dependency and a lightweight, practical framework.

Conclusion: NeighborDiv provides a robust, training-free generalist graph anomaly detection approach that outperforms baselines and offers cross-domain stability and practicality for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Graph Anomaly Detection (GAD) is increasingly shifting to Generalist GAD (GGAD) for cross-domain "one-for-all" detection, but existing GGAD methods predominantly rely on the neighbor consistency principle, falling into the \textbf{Node-to-Neighbor Consistency Paradigm} for anomaly quantification. These methods suffer from complex training pipelines, heavy training data dependency, high computational costs, and unstable cross-domain generalization. To address these limitations, we propose NeighborDiv, a training-free generalist graph anomaly detection framework based on neighbor diversity. Departing from the dominant Node-to-Neighbor Consistency Paradigm, we shift the focus to the \textbf{Neighbor-to-Neighbor Diversity Paradigm}, and uncover that the internal structural dispersion of a node's neighbor set is a powerful, independently discriminative anomaly signal. We quantify neighbor diversity via the variance of inter-neighbor feature similarities, which captures how a node organizes its local graph environment, and operates independently of conventional node-to-neighbor consistency frameworks. Extensive experiments under two standard GGAD evaluation paradigms show NeighborDiv achieves state-of-the-art performance, with relative gains of 10.25% in average AUC and 17.78% in average AP over the second-best baseline under Single-Domain Independent Training (SDIT), and 6.89%/9.58% in AUC/AP under Unified Multi-Domain Training (UMDT), respectively. Notably, NeighborDiv yields zero performance volatility across all datasets, eliminating training-set dependency and establishing a lightweight and highly practical GGAD framework.

</details>


### [259] [Learning fMRI activations dictionaries across individual geometries via optimal transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20883)
*Sonia Mazelet,Rémi Flamary,Bertrand Thirion*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FGW-based dictionary learning for fMRI that accounts for inter-subject geometric variability via amortized optimization to predict transport plans and FGW-aware dictionary atoms dependent on the trade-off between feature alignment and structural similarity; demonstrated on HCP data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Handle subject-specific brain geometry variability in fMRI without losing subject information, enabling interpretable representations via dictionary learning.

Method: Employ Fused Gromov-Wasserstein distance to compare graphs with different geometries and features. Use amortized optimization to train a neural network that predicts approximate optimal transport plans for scalability. Learn dictionary atoms parameterized by the FGW trade-off between feature alignment and structural consistency.

Result: On the HCP dataset, the method captures different levels of geometric variability and yields representations that preserve essential information for downstream analysis.

Conclusion: FGW-based, amortized dictionary learning provides a scalable, geometry-aware representation for fMRI data, balancing alignment of features with preservation of geometric structure, suitable for population-level and brain-state analyses.

Abstract: Dictionary learning is a powerful tool for creating interpretable representations. When applied to functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data, the resulting patterns of brain activity can be used for various downstream tasks, such as brain state classification or population-level analysis. However, a major challenge is the variability in brain geometry across individuals. This is usually addressed by projecting each individual brain geometry onto a common template, which removes subject-specific information. In this work, we introduce a novel approach to dictionary learning on fMRI data that explicitly accounts for this variability. We use the optimal transport-based Fused Gromov-Wasserstein (FGW) distance to compare graphs with different geometries and features. To address the challenge of computing multiple FGW distances for large graphs such as those arising from fMRI data, we rely on amortized optimization to learn a neural network that predicts an approximation of the optimal transport plans, which substantially reduces the computational cost. Additionally, we learn dictionary atoms that depend on the FGW trade-off parameter, which controls the balance between feature alignment and structural consistency. Numerical experiments on the HCP dataset demonstrate that the proposed approach captures different levels of geometric variability in the data and provides representations that preserve essential information.

</details>


### [260] [Training distribution determines the ceiling of drug-blind cancer sensitivity prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20885)
*Taekyung Heo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Global Pearson r overestimates predictive gains in drug-sensitivity by capturing between-drug potency differences; per-drug r shows drug encodings do not outperform cell-only features; mechanism-stratified training and response matching recover key predictive signal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the observed stagnation in drug-blind sensitivity prediction and determine whether it stems from representational bottlenecks or metric artifacts.

Method: Systematic analysis across four independent datasets comparing global vs per-drug Pearson correlation, plus a controlled experiment swapping mechanism-of-action (MoA) identity as a drug feature vs a training-distribution constraint; implement mechanism-stratified training and response matching.

Result: Global r is dominated by between-drug potency; a trivial drug-mean predictor performs as well as complex drug encodings; per-drug r shows no improvement from drug encodings over cell-only features; MoA-as-feature yields little benefit, but MoA-based training stratification boosts per-drug r for targeted kinase inhibitors by preserving pathway-specific signals; proposed strategies recover major predictive gain.

Conclusion: Metric choice and training design critically shape apparent predictive ability in drug-blind sensitivity; mechanism-stratified training and response matching provide practical, deployable paths to recover true predictive signal despite the metric artifact.

Abstract: Precision oncology requires predicting which drugs will suppress a specific tumor from its molecular profile, but drug-blind sensitivity prediction has plateaued despite increasingly complex drug representations. Here we show that this stagnation reflects a metric artifact rather than a representational bottleneck. The standard benchmark, global Pearson r, is dominated by between-drug potency differences that a trivial drug-mean predictor captures without any cell-specific learning. Per-drug Pearson r, which isolates within-drug cell ranking, reveals that no drug encoding improves over cell-only features across four independent datasets. A controlled experiment channeling mechanism-of-action identity as either a drug feature or a training-distribution constraint identifies the cause. Supplying MoA as a feature yields negligible benefit, whereas using it to stratify training raises per-drug r substantially for targeted kinase inhibitors, because pan-cancer co-training suppresses pathway-specific sensitivity signals. Mechanism-stratified training and response matching from pilot observations provide two deployable strategies that together recover the principal sources of predictive gain in drug-blind sensitivity prediction.

</details>


### [261] [Winfree Oscillatory Neural Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20922)
*Jiawen Dai,Yue Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WONN is a Winfree-dynamics–based oscillatory neural network operating on the torus to learn representations, achieving scalable, parameter-efficient performance on vision and reasoning tasks, including ImageNet-1K and Maze-hard with a tiny parameter footprint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the scalability and efficiency gaps in prior synchronization-based architectures which were confined to niche tasks, by developing a scalable oscillatory neural architecture applicable to standard benchmarks and complex reasoning.

Method: Generalized Winfree dynamics on the torus (S^1)^d with structured oscillatory interactions. Interactions are instantiated as either fixed trig mappings or learnable neural networks, enabling phase-based inductive biases and hierarchical connectivity.

Result: Competitive or superior results across CIFAR, ImageNet, Maze-hard, and Sudoku. Notably, first synchronization-based oscillatory architecture to scale to ImageNet-1K; Maze-hard achieves 80.1% with only 1% of the parameters of prior SOTA models.

Conclusion: Structured oscillatory dynamics provide a scalable, parameter-efficient alternative to conventional neural architectures, with demonstrated applicability to both vision and reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Oscillations and synchronization are widely believed to play a fundamental role in representation and computation. However, existing machine learning approaches based on synchronization dynamics have largely been confined to specialized settings such as object discovery, with limited evidence of scalability to standard vision benchmarks or logic reasoning tasks. We propose the Winfree Oscillatory Neural Network (WONN), a dynamical neural architecture based on generalized Winfree dynamics. WONN evolves representations on the torus $(S^1)^d$ through structured oscillatory interactions, combining phase-based inductive biases with flexible and hierarchical interaction mechanisms instantiated as either fixed trigonometric mappings or learnable neural networks. We evaluate WONN on image recognition and complex reasoning tasks, including CIFAR, ImageNet, Maze-hard, and Sudoku. Across these domains, WONN achieves competitive or superior performance with strong parameter efficiency. In particular, WONN is, to our knowledge, the first synchronization-based oscillatory architecture to scale competitively to ImageNet-1K. Furthermore, on Maze-hard, WONN achieves 80.1% accuracy using only 1% of the parameters of prior state-of-the-art models. These results suggest that structured oscillatory dynamics provide a scalable and parameter-efficient alternative to conventional neural architectures.

</details>


### [262] [DASH: Fast Differentiable Architecture Search for Hybrid Attention in Minutes on a Single GPU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20936)
*Weizhe Chen,Miao Zhang,Junpeng Jiang,Yaping Li,Weili Guan,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DASH enables fast differentiable search for hybrid attention architectures, achieving high-quality designs with minutes-level search and dramatically reduced token usage, outperforming Jet-Nemotron-style baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce search cost and time for discovering efficient hybrid attention architectures in LLM inference, addressing the token-heavy, staged PostNAS approach (Jet-Nemotron).

Method: Continuous relaxation of discrete layer-wise attention placement into architecture logits; prepare reusable teacher-aligned linear candidates; perform architecture-only search with model and operator weights frozen to maximize search efficiency; token budget ~12.3M.

Result: On Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, DASH outperforms selector-style baselines and achieves stronger RULER performance than released Jet-Nemotron models; competitive on overlapping short-context and general benchmarks.

Conclusion: Differentiable, architecture-only search yields high-quality hybrid attention architectures with drastically reduced search cost, enabling practical, routine design and promising directions for hybrid architecture development.

Abstract: Hybrid attention architectures are becoming an increasingly important paradigm for improving LLM inference efficiency while preserving model quality, making hybrid architecture design a central problem. Existing designs often rely on manual empirical rules or proxy-based selector signals for layer-wise operator allocation. Recent NAS-style systems such as Jet-Nemotron demonstrate the promise of automated hybrid architecture search. However, Jet-Nemotron's PostNAS search stages alone use 200B tokens, making such search pipelines difficult to use as routine methods for hybrid architecture design. We introduce DASH, a fast differentiable search framework for hybrid attention architecture design, which relaxes discrete layer-wise attention operator placement into continuous architecture logits, prepares reusable teacher-aligned linear candidates, and performs architecture-only search with model and operator weights frozen to significantly enhance search efficiency. On Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, DASH consistently outperforms a comprehensive suite of existing selector-style hybrid attention design baselines, showing that direct differentiable search can discover stronger hybrid architectures. Moreover, DASH achieves stronger RULER performance than released Jet-Nemotron models while remaining competitive on overlapping short-context and general benchmarks. Notably, each DASH search run uses only 12.3M tokens and takes about 20 minutes on a single RTX Pro 6000 GPU, corresponding to merely 0.006% of the PostNAS search tokens reported by Jet-Nemotron. These results suggest that high-quality hybrid attention architectures can be obtained through minutes-level differentiable search, providing a promising direction for hybrid architecture design.

</details>


### [263] [A Deployment Audit of Release-Side Risk in Conformal Triage under Prevalence Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20956)
*Chengze Li,Xiao Liu,Hanrong Zhang,Haiyang Peng,Yanghao Ruan,Huanhuan Ma,Chunyu Miao,Qichao Zhou,Xiangrong Qi,Philip Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Leakage-aware deployment audit for release-side conformal triage; assesses safety of releasing event-positive patients under prevalence shift by separating roles (prevalence correction, conformal calibration, held-out release-safety evaluation) and examining release outcomes, calibration data sufficiency, and safety-review trade-offs; illustrated with a retrospective NSCLC pilot; classwise branch acts as a scarcity diagnostic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Under prevalence shift, standard summaries of marginal coverage and review rates miss whether truly event-positive patients are released without review; a leakage-aware audit focuses on release safety.

Method: Partition subjects into three non-overlapping roles; perform leakage-aware audit on release decisions; measure: number of event-positive patients released without review, sufficiency of event labels for calibration, shift in safety-review balance; apply to retrospective NSCLC pilot; analyze interactions among prevalence correction, conformal calibration, and review.

Result: Demonstrates that after prevalence correction, the pooled conformal branch lowers review by releasing more patients, some event-positive; classwise branch reveals scarcity of event labels; audit explains why lower review can be misleading and diagnoses when safe low-review release cannot be certified.

Conclusion: The leakage-aware deployment audit provides a direct assessment of release safety under prevalence shift and identifies data-label scarcity as a barrier to safe low-review release; separation into roles enables targeted diagnostics and safer deployment decisions.

Abstract: Conformal triage converts predictive scores into deployment actions that either release a case, flag it for urgent attention, or defer it to human review. Under prevalence shift, however, the usual summaries of marginal coverage and human-review rate can miss the safety-critical question of whether patients who truly experience the target event are released without review. To address this gap, we introduce a leakage-aware deployment audit for release-side conformal triage. It first assigns target subjects to three non-overlapping roles: prevalence correction, conformal calibration, and held-out release-safety evaluation. This separation then lets the audit evaluate release directly: how many event-positive patients are cleared without review, whether the pilot has enough event labels for calibration, and how the safety-review trade-off shifts. Applying this audit to a retrospective NSCLC pilot shows why lower review can be misleading: after prevalence correction, the pooled conformal branch lowers review by releasing more patients, some of whom are event-positive. Within the audit, the classwise branch acts as a scarcity diagnostic: the pilot has too few event labels to certify safe low-review release.

</details>


### [264] [Choose Wisely and Privately: Proactive Client Selection for Fair and Efficient Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20975)
*Adda Akram Bendoukha,Heber Hwang Arcolezi,Nesrine Kaaniche,Aymen Boudguiga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proactively selects a fixed-size subset of clients for federated learning by maximizing a Potential Federation Loss that balances data utility and cross-feature fairness, using mutual information from privately released contingency tables and simulated annealing under strong differential privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-IID data in Federated Learning leads to slow convergence and degraded accuracy; reactive, reward-based client weighting wastes computation and increases privacy exposure. A proactive federation design aims to pre-select beneficial client sets before training.

Method: Compute cross-feature mutual information from differentially private contingency tables to assess dataset relevance. Define Potential Federation Loss (PFL) over fixed-size federations to balance data utility and fairness of cross-feature correlations. Solve the client subset selection as an optimization problem over PFL using simulated annealing, with strong differential privacy guarantees on clients' local statistics.

Result: Experiments on four benchmarks show that models trained on optimally found federations converge faster, achieve higher accuracy, and exhibit improved fairness compared to uniform sampling and competitive with state-of-the-art adaptive aggregation methods.

Conclusion: Proactive, DP-guaranteed client selection via PFL and simulated annealing can yield faster, fairer, and more accurate FL models by preemptively forming beneficial client groups, reducing wasted computation and privacy exposure.

Abstract: Federated Learning enables collaborative model training across decentralized data sources without data transfer. Averaging-based FL is limited by the presence of non-IID data, which negatively impacts convergence speed and final model accuracy. Conventional alternatives suffer from significant inefficiency. Clients with noisy or highly heterogeneous data contribute expensive gradient computations that are either discarded or heavily down-weighted before aggregation. These reactive approaches waste computational resources, require more communication rounds and result in unnecessary privacy exposure. In this paper, we propose a proactive client selection framework that aims to find an optimal federation of clients whose combined data match utility and fairness requirements before training begins. Our method relies on mutual information computed from differentially private contingency tables to quantify the relevance of cross-feature correlations in the union dataset. We introduce a Potential Federation Loss (PFL) over the set of fixed-size federations, which balances two objectives. Maximizing collective data utility while ensuring fair cross-features correlations to prevent group unfairness. Client selection is expressed as an optimal subset search problem over the PFL objective, which we solve using simulated annealing under strong differential privacy guarantees for clients' local statistics. Experimental results on four benchmarks show faster, fairer, and more accurate models trained on optimally found federations, compared to uniform sampling, even when state-of-the-art adaptive aggregation or sampling strategies are employed.

</details>


### [265] [Point Cloud Sequence Encoding for Material-conditioned Graph Network Simulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20978)
*Philipp Dahlinger,Balázs Gyenes,Niklas Freymuth,Luca Geminiani,Tobias Würth,Johannes Mitsch,Nadja Klein,Luise Kärger,Gerhard Neumann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PEACH enables zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for graph-network simulators by conditioning on in-context point-cloud sequences via a spatio-temporal encoder and auxiliary supervision, outperforming mesh baselines in accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph-network simulators often assume known material parameters (e.g., stiffness, viscosity), which is unrealistic in experiments. Inferring these properties from mesh trajectories is challenging, limiting real-world applicability. A parameter-agnostic, practical adaptation method is needed.

Method: Introduce in-context learning on point clouds with a novel spatio-temporal point-cloud sequence encoder. Use two forms of auxiliary supervision to improve simulation fidelity and adapt the learned simulator to unseen physical properties during inference.

Result: Demonstrates accurate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on dynamic scenes. In simulation experiments, PEACH outperforms mesh-based baselines in prediction accuracy and is more practical for real-world deployment.

Conclusion: PEACH provides a scalable, accurate approach to adapting graph-network simulators to unseen physical properties via point-cloud context, enabling effective sim-to-real transfer without explicit parameter estimation.

Abstract: Graph Network Simulators (GNSs) have emerged as powerful surrogates for complex physics-based simulation, offering inherent differentiability and orders-of-magnitude speedups over traditional solvers. However, GNSs typically assume access to the underlying material parameters, such as stiffness or viscosity, severely limiting their utility in realistic experimental settings. While recent meta-learning approaches address the parameter dependency by inferring properties from mesh trajectories, reconstructing a mesh from an observed scene is challenging. In this work, we introduce Point Cloud Encoding for Accurate Context Handling (PEACH), a novel framework that applies in-context learning on point clouds to adapt a learned simulator to unseen physical properties during inference. Our approach relies on a novel spatio-temporal point cloud sequence encoder, as well as two forms of auxiliary supervision to help improve simulation fidelity. We demonstrate that PEACH is capable of accurate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on a challenging, dynamic scene. Experiments on simulation scenes show that PEACH even outperforms mesh-based baselines on prediction accuracy, while being much more practical for real-world deployment.

</details>


### [266] [A Sharper Picture of Generalization in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20988)
*Paul Lintilhac,Sair Shaikh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates PAC-Bayes-based generalization bounds for transformers on boolean functions, leveraging Fourier spectra sparsity and low-degree components. It contrasts with Rademacher-based bounds and proposes flat minima that implement sparse boolean functions, enabling a non-vacuous, low-sharpness PAC-Bayes bound. Empirically validated with mechanistic interpretability support.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in theoretical generalization analyses for transformers on boolean-like domains by exploring PAC-Bayes bounds informed by the Fourier spectrum of target functions and the role of low-degree sparsity in enabling good generalization.

Method: 1) Analyze target functions’ Fourier spectra and identify sparsity on low-degree components. 2) Propose the existence of flat minima that implement any boolean function of sparsity up to the context length. 3) Construct a PAC-Bayes generalization bound around an idealized low-sharpness learner. 4) Empirically evaluate predictions and perform a mechanistic interpretability study to validate the theoretical construction in real transformers.

Result: Demonstrates that sparse, low-degree Fourier spectra can yield non-vacuous PAC-Bayes generalization bounds via flat-minima constructions. Provides empirical predictions aligned with theory and mechanistic interpretability findings that support the proposed construction’s realism in practical transformers.

Conclusion: PAC-Bayes bounds can complement Rademacher-based analyses for transformer generalization on boolean-like tasks when the target function exhibits low-degree spectral sparsity, with the caveat that the key assumptions (flat minima for sparse boolean functions and an idealized low-sharpness learner) are critical to the results and require empirical substantiation.

Abstract: We study transformers' generalization behavior on boolean domains from the perspective of the Fourier Spectra of their target functions. In contrast to prior work (Edelman et al., 2022; Trauger and Tewari, 2024), which derived generalization bounds from Rademacher complexity, we investigate the feasibility of obtaining generalization bounds via PAC-Bayes theory. We show that sparse spectra concentrated on low-degree components enable low-sharpness constructions with good generalization properties. Our idea is to show the existence of flat minima implementing any boolean function of sparsity no greater than the context length, and then apply a PAC-Bayes bound to an idealized low-sharpness learner, resulting in a non-vacuous generalization bound. We evaluate predictions empirically and conduct a mechanistic interpretability study to support the realism of our theoretical construction in real transformers.

</details>


### [267] [Modeling Temporal scRNA-seq Data with Latent Gaussian Process and Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20989)
*Mehmet Yigit Balik,Harri Lähdesmäki*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Generative heteroscedastic GP with OT alignment for scRNA-seq temporal inference, handling heterogeneity via cell-specific latent time and cell-type conditioning, achieving state-of-the-art interpolation/extrapolation and perturbation-trajectory inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Infer temporal dynamics from static single-cell transcriptomics, a challenge due to biological variability, heterogeneous cell populations, and lack of true trajectories. Existing neural differential equation approaches can overfit and neglect variability.

Method: A generative framework modeling population trends with a latent heteroscedastic Gaussian process (HP-GP) approximated by Hilbert space methods (HSGP). An optimal transport objective aligns generated and observed population distributions. Cell-specific latent time and cell-type conditioning disentangle temporal asynchrony and cell-type trajectories. A novel gradient-based strategy infers perturbation trajectories.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on complex interpolation and extrapolation benchmarks.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling heterogeneity and aligning distributions via OT improves temporal inference in scRNA-seq, enabling robust interpolation/extrapolation and the inference of perturbation trajectories.

Abstract: Single-cell RNA sequencing provides insights into gene expression at single-cell resolution, yet inferring temporal processes from these static snapshot measurements remains a fundamental challenge. Current approaches utilizing neural differential equations and flows are sensitive to overfitting and lack careful considerations of biological variability. In this work, we propose a generative framework that models population trends using a latent heteroscedastic Gaussian process (GP) approximated by Hilbert space methods. To address the absence of genuine cell trajectories, we leverage an optimal transport (OT) objective that aligns generated and observed population distributions. Our method explicitly captures biological heterogeneity by incorporating cell-specific latent time and cell type conditioning to disentangle temporal asynchrony and trajectories to different cell types. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on complex interpolation and extrapolation benchmarks and introduce a novel gradient-based strategy for inferring perturbation trajectories.

</details>


### [268] [Beyond the Bellman Recursion: A Pontryagin-Guided Framework for Non-Exponential Discounting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20996)
*Hojin Ko,Jeonggyu Huh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces PG-DPO, a non-recursive RL framework for non-exponential discounting, combining Pontryagin Maximum Principle with MC rollouts; shows improvements over standard DP and critic baselines on hyperbolic/survival discounting tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exponential discounting is fragile under non-exponential time preferences and survival processes; Bellman-style recursions rely on time-homogeneity and multiplicativity, which break with non-exponential discounting; a variational approach is needed.

Method: Proposes Pontryagin-Guided Direct Policy Optimization (PG-DPO), a variational framework that abandons recursion and couples the Pontryagin Maximum Principle with Monte Carlo rollouts via an Adjoint-MC projection enforcing pointwise Hamiltonian maximization.

Result: Across multi-dimensional hyperbolic and survival-discount benchmarks, PG-DPO improves accuracy and stability where equation-driven solvers and critic-based baselines diverge.

Conclusion: PG-DPO provides a robust alternative to dynamic programming for settings with non-exponential discounting, delivering more stable and accurate policy optimization when standard methods fail.

Abstract: Most value-based and actor--critic reinforcement learning methods rely on Bellman-style recursions, yet these recursions collapse under non-exponential discounting common in human preferences and survival processes. We show the breakdown is structural: exponential discounting sits at a fragile intersection of multiplicativity and time homogeneity, and violating either property breaks standard dynamic programming. To overcome this, we propose Pontryagin-Guided Direct Policy Optimization (PG-DPO), a variational framework that abandons recursion and couples the Pontryagin Maximum Principle with Monte Carlo rollouts via an Adjoint-MC projection enforcing pointwise Hamiltonian maximization. Across multi-dimensional hyperbolic and survival-discount benchmarks, PG-DPO improves accuracy and stability where equation-driven solvers and critic-based baselines diverge.

</details>


### [269] [Efficient Banzhaf-Based Data Valuation for $k$-Nearest Neighbors Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21033)
*Guangyi Zhang,Lutz Oettershagen,Lixu Wang,Aristides Gionis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes exact and approximate algorithms to compute Banzhaf data values for kNN classifiers; proves #P-hardness, then leverages locality to derive a DP framework with a pseudo-polynomial time O(W k n^2) for weighted kNN and an O(n k^2) algorithm for unweighted, plus Monte Carlo estimators; validated on real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify each data point's contribution to model performance (data valuation) using fair, principled measures like the Banzhaf value; however, exact computation is intractable for general models. The paper targets kNN due to its locality, seeking practical algorithms.

Method: 1) Prove #P-hardness of computing Banzhaf values for kNN. 2) Develop a dynamic programming framework exploiting kNN locality to compute exact Banzhaf values. 3) Derive a pseudo-polynomial O(W k n^2) algorithm for weighted kNN (W = max sum of top-k weights). 4) Derive a specialized O(n k^2) algorithm for unweighted kNN. 5) Provide Monte Carlo randomization estimators to improve efficiency. 6) Validate on real-world datasets with experiments.

Result: The framework delivers substantial computational gains: a pseudo-polynomial DP for weighted kNN and a linear-in-n, O(n k^2) algorithm for unweighted kNN, plus Monte Carlo estimators. Empirical results demonstrate practical speedups and effective data-valuation outcomes on real datasets.

Conclusion: Although Banzhaf-based data valuation for kNN is #P-hard in general, the locality of kNN enables practical, exact DP algorithms and efficient approximations. The work offers actionable methods for fair data valuation in ML pipelines and highlights the feasibility of exact/approximate valuation computations in contact with real-world data.

Abstract: Data valuation, the task of quantifying the contribution of individual data points to model performance, has emerged as a fundamental challenge in machine learning. Game-theoretic approaches, such as the Banzhaf value, offer principled frameworks for fair data valuation; however, they suffer from exponential computational complexity. We address this challenge by developing efficient algorithms specifically tailored for computing Banzhaf values in $k$-nearest neighbor ($k$NN) classifiers. We first establish the theoretical hardness of the problem by proving that it is \#P-hard. Despite this intractability, we exploit the locality properties of $k$NN classifiers to develop practical exact algorithms. Our main contribution is a dynamic programming framework that achieves significant computational improvements: we present a pseudo-polynomial algorithm with $O(Wkn^2)$ time complexity for weighted $k$NN classifiers, where $W$ is the maximum sum of top-$k$ weights, and a specialized algorithm for unweighted $k$NN that achieves $O(nk^2)$ time complexity, that is, linear in the number of data points. We also offer efficient Monte Carlo estimation methods. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the practical efficiency of our approach and its effectiveness in data valuation applications.

</details>


### [270] [A Dialogue between Causal and Traditional Representation Learning: Toward Mutual Benefits in a Unified Formulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21058)
*Yan Li,Yuewen Sun,Shaoan Xie,Gongxu Luo,Yunlong Deng,Kun Zhang,Guangyi Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A proposal for unifying causal representation learning (CRL) with traditional representation learning via a two-component framework: a task component (what to preserve) and a constraint component (latent structure). It includes theoretical insight from CRL and practical guidance from traditional learning, supported by experiments (CausalVerse) showing the impact of task–constraint pairing on the effectiveness of causal constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CRL and traditional representation learning have evolved separately, leading to terminology, problem formulation, and evaluation gaps. A dialogue between the fields could yield theoretical and practical benefits, advancing both directions.

Method: Propose a unified formulation with two components: (1) task component specifying preserved information, and (2) constraint component specifying latent-space structure. Conduct empirical study evaluating how different task components interact with various structured constraints, using CausalVerse to assess when causal constraints are advantageous depending on the task.

Result: The framework enables bidirectional gains: CRL offers tools to know when structured latent constraints are useful; traditional representation learning informs task design and objective choices to improve CRL methods. Empirical results indicate that the effectiveness of causal constraints depends on the paired task, demonstrating the importance of task–constraint alignment.

Conclusion: A unified, dialogue-driven framework bridges CRL and traditional representation learning, guiding task design and choice of structural constraints to enhance causal representation methods and, conversely, informing traditional learners about the value of structured latent spaces.

Abstract: Causal representation learning (CRL) and traditional representation learning have largely developed along different trajectories. Traditional representation learning has been driven mainly by applications and empirical objectives, whereas CRL has focused more on theoretical questions, particularly identifiability. This difference in emphasis has created a gap between the two fields in terminology, problem formulation, and evaluation, limiting communication and sometimes leading to disconnected or redundant efforts. In this paper, we argue that these two fields should be brought into dialogue rather than treated as separate paradigms. To this end, we introduce a unified formulation in which the representation learning is characterized by two components: a task component, which specifies what information the learned representation is required to preserve, and a constraint component, which specifies what structure is imposed on the latent space. Under this formulation, the benefits run in both directions. CRL provides theoretical tools for understanding when structured latent constraints are useful or necessary, while traditional representation learning offers practical insights on task design and objective choice that can improve the development of CRL methods. To illustrate this interaction, we experimentally study how different task components affect the behavior of CRL methods under different structured constraints. Results on CausalVerse show that the effectiveness of causal constraints depends strongly on the tasks with which they are paired.

</details>


### [271] [Divide et Calibra: Multiclass Local Calibration via Vector Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21060)
*Cesare Barbera,Lorenzo Perini,Giovanni De Toni,Andrea Passerini,Andrea Pugnana*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compositional, region-aware multiclass calibration via VQ-induced partitions and shareable Dirichlet factors for improved local calibration without sacrificing global performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global calibration methods assume homogeneous calibration errors across the latent space, which is often false. Local methods risk information loss due to latent-space reductions. There is a need for region-specific calibration that shares parameters to remain data-efficient, especially in sparse regions.

Method: Propose a compositional calibration framework where region-specific calibration maps are built from shared codeword-dependent factors. Instantiate this via Vector Quantization (VQ) to create a structured partition of the representation space and use an indexed parameterization of Dirichlet concentrations to enable parameter sharing across regions, enabling heterogeneous yet coherent calibration maps.

Result: Experiments on benchmark datasets show significant improvements in local calibration while maintaining competitive global calibration and predictive performance.

Conclusion: The approach enables region-aware calibration with parameter sharing, improving local calibration in sparse regions without compromising global performance, and is scalable to diverse latent-space regions.

Abstract: Accurate and well-calibrated Machine Learning (ML) models are mandatory in high-stakes settings, yet effective multiclass calibration remains challenging: global approaches assume calibration errors are homogeneous across the latent space, while local methods often rely on latent-space dimensionality reduction, which leads to information loss. To address these issues, we propose a compositional approach to multiclass calibration, where region-specific calibration maps are constructed from shared codeword-dependent factors. We instantiate this idea via Vector Quantization (VQ), which induces a structured partition of the representation space, and an indexed parameterization of Dirichlet concentrations that enables parameter sharing across regions. Our approach learns heterogeneous calibration maps that generalize well even to sparse regions of the latent space. Experiments on benchmark datasets show significant improvements in local calibration while maintaining competitive global calibration and predictive performance.

</details>


### [272] [Robust Personalized Recommendation under Hidden Confounding in MNAR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21066)
*Zongyu Li,Wanting Su,Tianyu Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes PUID and BPUID to address hidden confounding in recommendations by estimating user-item specific sensitivity bounds, using adversarial optimization; improves deconfounding without RCT data and outperforms global-bound methods on three real datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Observational recommender data suffer from selection bias due to users' selective interactions. Global sensitivity bounds assume homogeneous effects of unmeasured confounders and often rely on costly RCTs. Personalizing bounds aims to capture heterogeneity and relax strong homogeneity assumptions.

Method: Introduce Personalized Unobserved-Confounding-aware Interaction Deconfounder (PUID) that estimates user-item level sensitivity bounds. Uses adversarial optimization to robustly bound propensity effects under unmeasured confounding. BPUID extends PUID with a benchmark-guided variant incorporating pre-trained models as stabilizing references.

Result: Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets show that PUID/BPUID significantly outperform global sensitivity bound methods under hidden confounding, without requiring RCT data, improving robustness and predictive accuracy.

Conclusion: Personalized sensitivity bounds alleviate the homogeneity limitation of global bounds, enabling more accurate deconfounding in recommendations; BPUID provides additional stability via benchmark guidance.

Abstract: Recommender systems often rely on observational user--item interaction data, which is prone to selection bias due to users' selective interactions with items. Inverse propensity weighting and doubly robust estimators effectively mitigate selection bias under observed confounding, but are unreliable in the presence of hidden confounders. Existing approaches relying on randomized controlled trials (RCTs) or global sensitivity bounds are constrained in practice: RCTs demand costly experimental data, while global sensitivity bounds presume a uniformly bounded effect of unmeasured confounders on propensities through sensitivity analysis, thereby neglecting heterogeneity across user--item interactions. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel framework, which estimates user--item level sensitivity bounds, thereby substantially relaxing the homogeneity assumption inherent in global sensitivity bounds named Personalized Unobserved-Confounding-aware Interaction Deconfounder (PUID). To ensure both robustness and predictive accuracy, we further develop an adversarial optimization strategy and propose a benchmark-guided variant (BPUID) that incorporates pre-trained models as stabilizing references. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms global methods under hidden confounding, without requiring RCT data.

</details>


### [273] [Towards Understanding Self-Pretraining for Sequence Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21070)
*Omar Coser,Loredana Zollo,Paolo Soda,Antonio Orvieto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-pretraining with masked token prediction improves Transformer sequence classification by shaping Attention via proximity-biased cues, revealing a bottleneck where supervised labels alone cannot learn key query–key patterns from random initialization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how SPT improves optimization beyond supervised training in Transformers and why standard supervision can fail, by dissecting the mechanism behind Amos et al.'s results.

Method: Replicate and ablate Amos et al.'s SPT pipeline on Long-Range Arena with minimal setup; analyze how label supervision learns query–key Attention patterns, identify learning proximity interactions that convert absolute positional encodings into proximity-based Attention, and use a simplified theoretical setup to compare label supervision versus masked reconstruction.

Result: A central bottleneck is that label supervision alone struggles to learn useful query–key Attention patterns from random initialization. Ablations show that proximity-biased Attention arising from absolute positions is a key contribution of SPT. In a simplified theory, label supervision is locally blind to certain Attention-score directions that masked reconstruction can detect, explaining the benefits of SPT beyond standard supervision.

Conclusion: SPT helps Learn Attention patterns that supervision alone cannot acquire, due to alignment constraints at random initialization; masked reconstruction reveals complementary directions in Attention scores. The findings point to proximity-based Attention as a core mechanism and suggest combining reconstruction objectives with supervision to improve optimization in Transformers.

Abstract: Amos et al. (2024) showed that the accuracy of Transformer models in sequence classification can be significantly improved by first pretraining with a masked token prediction objective without external data or augmentation, a procedure referred to as self-pretraining (SPT). While the primary objective of Amos et al. (2024) was to showcase that Transformers can achieve strong performance on the Long-Range Arena (LRA), their pipeline raises more fundamental questions: How does SPT drive optimization to better solutions? Why can standard supervised training fail in Transformers? To better understand this, we replicate and systematically ablate the findings of Amos et al. (2024). Our ablations suggest that a central bottleneck in the studied settings is not depth or generalization alone, but the ability of label supervision to learn useful query-key Attention patterns from random initialization. With a minimal setup, we identify learning proximity interactions - turning absolute positional encodings into proximity-biased Attention scores - as a key source of the improvements brought by SPT. Finally, in a simplified theoretical setup, we show that label supervision can be locally blind to certain Attention-score directions that are instead detectable through masked reconstruction.

</details>


### [274] [Reviving Error Correction in Modern Deep Time-Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21088)
*Minh Hoang Nguyen,Dai Do,Huu Hiep Nguyen,Dung Nguyen,Kien Do,Hung Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A plug-in, architecture-agnostic error correction module (UEC-STD) for deep time-series forecasting that separately corrects trend and seasonal components to mitigate autoregressive error accumulation, without retraining the base forecaster; validated across multiple backbones and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive error accumulation degrades long-term forecasts; traditional ECMs are inadequate for deep models; need a practical, non-intrusive correction mechanism.

Method: Introduce Universal Error Corrector with Seasonal-Trend Decomposition (UEC-STD). The model explicitly decomposes predictions into trend and seasonal parts and trains a corrector to adjust each separately; integrates with any forecaster without retraining; code provided.

Result: Significant improvements in correction accuracy and robustness across 4 backbones and 10 datasets; shows practicality and generality.

Conclusion: UEC-STD offers a scalable tool to mitigate autoregressive errors in deep time-series forecasting and yields new insights into ECM usage for deep models; code available.

Abstract: Modern deep-learning models have achieved remarkable success in time-series forecasting. Yet, their performance degrades in long-term prediction due to error accumulation in autoregressive inference, where predictions are recursively used as inputs. While classical error correction mechanisms (ECMs) have long been used in statistical methods, their applicability to deep learning models remains limited or ineffective. In this work, we revisit the error accumulation problem in deep time-series forecasting and investigate the role and necessity of ECMs in this new context. We propose a simple, architecture-agnostic error correction model that can be integrated with any existing forecaster without requiring retraining. By explicitly decomposing predictions into trend and seasonal components and training the corrector to adjust each separately, we introduce the Universal Error Corrector with Seasonal-Trend Decomposition (UEC-STD), which significantly improves correction accuracy and robustness across 4 backbones and 10 datasets. Our findings provide a practical tool for enhancing forecasts while offering new insights into mitigating autoregressive errors in deep time-series models. Code is available at https://github.com/DA2I2-SLM/UEC-STD.

</details>


### [275] [UOTIP: Unbalanced Optimal Transport Map for Unpaired Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21094)
*Donggyu Lee,Taekyung Lee,Jaewoong Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unpaired image inverse problem solver using Unbalanced Optimal Transport Map (UOTIP) that learns a map from noisy measurements to clean signals with a likelihood-based cost; introduces a quadratic cost to guarantee existence/uniqueness under twist condition; robust to multi-level noise and class imbalance; achieves state-of-the-art on unpaired benchmarks for linear and nonlinear inverse problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address unpaired inverse problems where paired noisy-clean data is unavailable, requiring a method that is robust to varying observation noise, adaptable to class imbalance, and generalizable to diverse noise types.

Method: Propose UOTIP: learn an Unbalanced Optimal Transport (UOT) map from the distribution of noisy measurements to the distribution of clean signals. Incorporates a likelihood-based cost and relaxes exact marginal constraints via the unbalanced OT framework. Adds a quadratic cost term to satisfy the twist condition, ensuring existence (and, under appropriate conditions, uniqueness) of the transport map for ill-posed problems.

Result: Experimental results indicate state-of-the-art performance on unpaired image inverse problem benchmarks, across both linear and nonlinear inverse problems.

Conclusion: UOT provides robustness, adaptability, and generalizability for unpaired inverse problems. The quadratic cost term yields theoretical guarantees (existence/uniqueness) of the transport map under the twist condition, supporting the method’s applicability to ill-posed problems.

Abstract: We investigate unpaired image inverse problems, a challenging setting where only independent, non-paired sets of noisy measurements and clean target signals are available for training. We propose a novel inverse problem solver based on Unbalanced Optimal Transport, called Unbalanced Optimal Transport Map for Inverse Problems (UOTIP). Our method formulates the reconstruction task, predicting clean target signals from noisy measurements, as learning a UOT Map from noisy measurement distribution to clean signal distribution by incorporating a likelihood-based cost function. By relaxing the exact marginal constraint, the UOT framework provides key advantages to our model: robustness to multi-level observation noise, adaptability to class imbalance between noisy and clean datasets, and generalizability to diverse noise-type scenarios. Furthermore, we theoretically demonstrate that incorporating a quadratic cost term ensures the existence and uniqueness of the transport map by satisfying the twist condition, even for ill-posed inverse problems. Our experiments demonstrate that UOTIP achieves state-of-the-art performance on unpaired image inverse problem benchmarks, across linear and nonlinear inverse problems.

</details>


### [276] [A Typed Tensor Language for Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21103)
*Theofilos Mailis,Kalliopi-Christina Despotidou,Konstantinos Filippopolitis,Yannis Foufoulas,Thanasis-Michail Karampatsis,Andreas Ktenidis,Evdokia Mailli,Theodore Papamarkou,Yannis Ioannidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a typed tensor language for federated learning that separates federated tensors from globally shared tensors, and proves a shared-state factorization theory for one-round and iterative programs; links encode/merge/decode to gradient-based learning within a fixed-dimensional shared state.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify disparate FL protocols under a formal, compositional framework to enable rigorous reasoning about computation, communication, and learning in federated settings.

Method: Define a typed tensor language with federated tensors (partitioned records across clients) and shared tensors (globally available), with semantics referenced to a virtual global tensor. Prove a shared-state factorization: one-round programs factor through a fixed-dimension shared state independent of client count; establish a converse: encoders/decoders realizable in the language correspond to typed one-round programs; extend to iterative programs with shared cross-round state. Develop a differentiable fragment where per-record losses/gradients map to a global gradient via per-record aggregation, yielding server-side gradient descent and second-order updates within the language.

Result: Formal shared-state factorization theory for a broad class of FL computations; expressiveness equivalence between encodable programs and typed programs; fixed-size shared state enabling scalable analysis; differentiable fragment supporting gradient-based learning within the framework.

Conclusion: The work provides a formal, extensible framework that captures encode/merge/decode computations in federated learning and analytics, clarifying how many FL computations can be realized with fixed-dimensional shared state and enabling principled derivation of gradient-based optimization within that space.

Abstract: Federated learning and analytics are often described as collections of separate protocols, even when they share the same mathematical form: client-local tensor computation, mergeable aggregation into shared state, and shared-only post-processing. We introduce a typed tensor language that formalizes this structure. The language distinguishes federated tensors, whose records are partitioned across clients along a tracked record axis, from shared tensors, which are available globally. Its semantics are defined by comparison with a virtual global tensor, used only as a reference object. The main result is a shared-state factorization theory. We show that typed one-round programs factor through fixed-dimensional shared state whose size is independent of the number of clients and records, computed from client-local tensor expressions and merged across clients.
