We also prove a converse representability result; factorizations whose encoders and decoders are expressible in the language are realized by typed one-round programs, and the correspondence extends to iterative programs whose cross-round state is shared. This gives a formal account of the computations in the language that can be expressed as encode, merge, and decode procedures. We then develop a differentiable fragment for learning. If a per-record loss and its per-record gradient are represented by client-local tensor expressions, the global gradient is represented by record-axis summation of the federated gradient tensor. This yields typed iterative programs for server-side gradient descent and shared-linear-algebra second-order updates. The framework characterizes a broad class of federated learning computations whose communication passes through fixed-dimensional shared state.

</details>


### [277] [HORST: Composing Optimizer Geometries for Sparse Transformer Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21104)
*Tom Jacobs,Rohan Jain,Rebekka Burkholz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A modular optimizer called HORST combines adaptive stability with L1 sparsity bias via a hyperbolic mirror map to enable effective sparse training of transformers, outperforming AdamW across sparsity levels, especially at high sparsity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparsifying transformers faces conflicting optimization biases: adaptive methods provide stability (L_infty bias) but sparsity requires an L1 bias. A principled way to blend these is needed.

Method: Model optimizer steps as non-commutative operators and compose them; use a hyperbolic mirror map to induce L1 sparsity bias while inheriting stability from adaptive methods; HORST is a modular optimizer.

Result: Empirical results on both vision and language transformers show HORST consistently and significantly outperforms AdamW baselines across all sparsity levels, with larger gains at higher sparsity.

Conclusion: HORST offers a stable, sparsity-friendly optimization framework for sparse transformer training, enabling robust sparsity-induced performance gains across vision and language tasks.

Abstract: Sparsifying transformers remains a fundamental challenge, as standard optimizers fail to simultaneously encourage sparsity and maintain training stability. Effective adaptive optimizers exhibit an implicit $L_{\infty}$ bias favoring stability, yet, sparsity requires an $L_1$ bias. To integrate sparsity, we propose a composition of optimizer steps, which we cast as non-commutative operators to analyze and combine their optimization geometry in a principled way. This yields HORST (Hyperbolic Operator for Robust Sparse Training), a modular optimizer that inherits stability from adaptive methods while inducing $L_1$ sparsity bias through a hyperbolic mirror map. Our experiments demonstrate its utility for sparse training of transformers on both vision and language tasks. HORST consistently and significantly outperforms AdamW baselines across all sparsity levels, with large gains at higher sparsity.

</details>


### [278] [Improved Guarantees for Constrained Online Convex Optimization via Self-Contraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21107)
*Dhruv Sarkar,Abhishek Sinha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A simple projection-based COCO algorithm achieves logarithmic regret and CCV for both strongly convex and convex losses, with exponential CCV improvement in the strongly convex case, leveraging a geometric result on self-contracted curves.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce both regret and cumulative constraint violation under adversarially chosen constraints in Constrained Online Convex Optimization (COCO). Improve CCV scaling beyond existing bounds while preserving optimal regret.

Method: Projection-based online update that handles adversarial constraints; maintains a feasible/controlled sequence via projections; employs a geometric lemma on self-contracted curves to bound CCV.

Result: Strongly convex losses: O(log T) regret and O(log T) CCV. Convex losses: O(sqrt(T)) regret and O(sqrt(T)) CCV. These improve prior O(sqrt(T) log T) CCV for strongly convex and O(sqrt(T) log T) CCV for convex cases, with exponential CCV improvement in the strongly convex setting.

Conclusion: Introduces a simple yet effective projection-based approach and bases the analysis on a novel geometric result for self-contracted curves, which may be of independent interest and potentially applicable to other online optimization problems.

Abstract: We consider Constrained Online Convex Optimization (COCO) with adversarially chosen constraints. At each round, the learner chooses an action before observing the loss and constraint function for that round. The goal is to achieve small static regret against the best point satisfying all constraints while also controlling cumulative constraint violation ($\mathsf{CCV}$). For strongly convex losses, state-of-the-art algorithms achieve $O(\log T)$ regret and $O(\sqrt{T \log T})$ $\mathsf{CCV}.$ The corresponding best-known bounds for convex losses is $O(\sqrt{T})$ regret and $O(\sqrt{T} \log T)$ $\mathsf{CCV}$. In this paper, we give a simple projection-based algorithm that simultaneously achieves $O(\log T)$ regret and $O(\log T)$ $\mathsf{CCV}$ for strongly-convex losses, yielding an exponential improvement in the $\mathsf{CCV}$. For the convex losses, our algorithm improves the $\mathsf{CCV}$ to $O(\sqrt{T})$ while maintaining the optimal $O(\sqrt{T})$ regret. The key to our improvement is a recent geometric result for self-contracted curves, which may be of independent interest.

</details>


### [279] [A Unified Framework for Uncertainty-Aware Explainable Artificial Intelligence: A Case Study in Power Quality Disturbance Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21114)
*Yinsong Chen,Samson S. Yu,Zhong Li,Chee Peng Lim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Formalizes explanation distribution as the push-forward of a Bayesian neural network posterior through Lipschitz attribution operators, and introduces UA-RAO to summarize this distribution (mean, variance, coefficient of variation, quantiles, and set-theoretic aggregations). Provides Monte Carlo accessibility and Wasserstein bounds. Evaluated on a 15-class PQD benchmark showing improved localization with deep ensembles and informative uncertainty patterns; framework is domain-agnostic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-hoc XAI methods yield deterministic attributions, while BNNs yield distributions over explanations. Capturing this variability is crucial for uncertainty-aware decision making. The work formalizes this as an explanation distribution and proposes a general uncertainty-aware framework.

Method: Define the explanation distribution as the push-forward of the BNN posterior through a Lipschitz-continuous attribution operator. Introduce UA-RAO (uncertainty-aware relevance attribution operator), a family of operators that summarize the explanation distribution using mean, variance, coefficient of variation, quantiles, and set-theoretic aggregation measures. Provide Monte Carlo accessibility and Wasserstein approximation bounds. Empirically evaluate with three BNN approximations and three attribution operators on a 15-class PQD benchmark, using relevance mass accuracy and intersection-over-union as localization metrics.

Result: Deep ensembles with the mean UA-RAO improve localisation over the deterministic baseline. Other UA-RAO summaries reveal uncertainty patterns not visible in point-attributions. Qualitative results suggest these patterns generalize beyond the training distribution.

Conclusion: Framework is domain-agnostic and applicable to any BNN paired with a Lipschitz-continuous attribution operator.

Abstract: Post-hoc explainable AI (XAI) methods typically produce deterministic attribution maps, whereas Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) induce a distribution over explanations. Capturing the variability of this distribution is important for uncertainty-aware decision-making. This paper formalises the \emph{explanation distribution} as the push-forward measure of the BNN posterior through any Lipschitz-continuous attribution operator. It further proposes the uncertainty-aware relevance attribution operator (UA-RAO), a general family of operators that summarises the explanation distribution using the mean, variance, coefficient of variation, quantiles, and set-theoretic aggregation measures. Theoretical support is provided through Monte Carlo accessibility and Wasserstein approximation bounds. The framework is evaluated on a 15-class power quality disturbance (PQD) classification benchmark, comparing three BNN approximations paired with three attribution operators using relevance mass accuracy and intersection-over-union as localisation metrics. Results show that deep ensembles with the mean UA-RAO improve localisation over the deterministic baseline, while other UA-RAO summaries reveal uncertainty patterns absent from point-estimate attributions. Qualitative results on measured signals further suggest that these patterns generalise beyond the synthetic training distribution. The framework is domain-agnostic and can be applied to any BNN paired with a Lipschitz-continuous attribution operator.

</details>


### [280] [Advantage Collapse in Group Relative Policy Optimization: Diagnosis and Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21125)
*Xixiang He,Qiyao Sun,Ao Cheng,Xingming Li,Xuanyu Ji,Hailun Lu,Runke Huang,Qingyong Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diagnose and mitigate gradient stagnation in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for LLM reasoning by introducing Advantage Collapse Rate (ACR) and the Adaptive Virtual Sample Policy Optimization (AVSPO). ACR predicts training stagnation; AVSPO injects virtual reward samples guided by ACR, reducing collapse by 58-63% and boosting accuracy by 4-6 percentage points across 0.5B–14B models with good out-of-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO can suffer from advantage collapse, where homogeneous rewards yield near-zero advantages and vanishing gradients, hindering learning and final performance in LLM reasoning tasks. A diagnostic and a practical remedy are needed to improve training stability and outcomes.

Method: Define Advantage Collapse Rate (ACR) as the proportion of training batches with ineffective gradients (near-zero advantages). Monitor ACR in real time during GRPO training. Propose Adaptive Virtual Sample Policy Optimization (AVSPO), a lightweight extension that injects virtual reward samples guided by current ACR, enabling learning from homogeneous groups without extra model rollouts.

Result: ACR correlates with training stagnation and final performance across models from 0.5B to 14B parameters on math reasoning benchmarks. AVSPO reduces advantage collapse by 58–63% relative to GRPO and yields consistent accuracy gains of 4–6 percentage points across scales, while preserving generalization to an out-of-domain task. Code and datasets are provided by the authors.

Conclusion: ACR provides a valuable diagnostic for GRPO training dynamics, and AVSPO offers an effective, low-overhead remedy that stabilizes learning and improves performance on reasoning tasks without additional rollouts.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a prominent algorithm within the Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) framework, has achieved strong results in improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). However, GRPO is prone to advantage collapse, a failure mode where homogeneous rewards within a group (e.g., all correct or all incorrect answers) yield near-zero advantages and vanishing gradients. To address this, we introduce the Advantage Collapse Rate (ACR), the first diagnostic metric quantifying the proportion of training batches with ineffective gradients. Across models from 0.5B to 14B parameters on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, we show that ACR strongly predicts training stagnation and final performance. We then propose Adaptive Virtual Sample Policy Optimization (AVSPO), a lightweight extension of GRPO that injects virtual reward samples, guided by real-time ACR monitoring, to enable learning from homogeneous groups without additional model rollouts. AVSPO reduces advantage collapse by 58-63% relative to GRPO and yields consistent accuracy gains of 4-6 percentage points across all model scales, while maintaining generalization on the evaluated out-of-domain task. Code and datasets are available at https://qingyonghu.github.io/AVSPO.

</details>


### [281] [Reasoning-Trace Collapse: Evaluating the Loss of Explicit Reasoning During Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21127)
*Lukas Twist,Helen Yannakoudakis,Jie M. Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fine-tuning can erase explicit reasoning traces, even as final answers stay plausible. A structural evaluation framework reveals tracing validity (valid/empty/missing/truncated) separate from task performance. Loss-masking can mitigate trace collapse without teacher traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mismatch between explicit reasoning training and instruction-tuning data lacking traces, which causes reasoning-trace collapse and unreliable evaluation.

Method: Propose a framework that separately measures reasoning trace validity and task performance conditioned on valid traces; apply to four open-weight reasoning models and compare standard supervised fine-tuning to masking-based strategies; test without requiring teacher-generated traces.

Result: Fine-tuning rapidly suppresses valid reasoning traces; answer-only metrics mask degradation as conditional performance on valid traces remains high; simple loss-masking substantially mitigates trace collapse without needing teacher traces.

Conclusion: Evaluation of fine-tuned reasoning models should report structural reasoning reliability metrics (valid/empty/missing/truncated) alongside final-answer performance, especially when adaptation data lacks explicit traces; masking techniques offer a practical mitigation.

Abstract: Explicit reasoning models are trained to produce intermediate reasoning traces before final answers, but downstream fine-tuning is often performed on ordinary instruction-response data that contains no such traces. We show that this mismatch can induce reasoning-trace collapse: a fine-tuned model continues to produce plausible final answers while losing the structurally valid explicit reasoning traces that made it a reasoning model in the first place. We introduce a structural evaluation framework that separates answer correctness from reasoning-trace validity, measuring valid, empty, missing, and truncated reasoning alongside reasoning-conditioned task performance. Using this framework, we study four open-weight reasoning models and find that standard supervised fine-tuning can rapidly suppress valid reasoning traces, and that answer-only metrics can substantially obscure this failure: in several settings, performance conditional on valid reasoning remains high while the rate of valid reasoning falls sharply. We further show that simple loss-masking strategies can substantially mitigate collapse without requiring teacher-generated reasoning traces. These results suggest that evaluations of fine-tuned reasoning models should report structural reasoning reliability metrics in addition to final-answer performance, especially when adaptation data does not contain explicit reasoning traces.

</details>


### [282] [Learning First Integrals via Backward-Generated Data and Guided Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21160)
*Jingfeng Zhong,Zhengxiang Liu,Zhijie Wang,Shuai Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FISolver uses backward generation and RL fine-tuning to learn to discover first integrals for differential equations, achieving strong, data-efficient performance versus large LLMs and Mathematica.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data scarcity and reliance on mathematical intuition hinder automatic discovery of first integrals; there is a need for scalable, data-driven approaches.

Method: Backward Generation creates (differential equation, first integral) pairs by deriving equations from sampled integrals; supervised fine-tuning on a compact mathematical model; reinforcement learning with a Levenshtein Distance-based shaped reward; data synthesis and blending strategies to cover difficult problem families.

Result: Experiments show FISolver outperforms larger mathematical LLMs and Mathematica on challenging benchmarks while using substantially lower computational cost, indicating a data-driven route for automated discovery of first integrals.

Conclusion: A data-driven, scalable pipeline can advance automated discovery of first integrals in dynamical systems; results motivate applying similar strategies to broader invariant discovery, with future work focusing on dataset diversity, reward design, and generalization to wider problem classes.

Abstract: The discovery of first integrals is of fundamental scientific importance for understanding conservation laws in dynamical systems. However, existing symbolic computation tools and Large Language Models (LLMs) remain limited on this task because high-quality training data are scarce and successful solutions often depend on mathematical intuition. This paper presents FISolver, an LLM-based solver developed to address this challenge. First, we introduce a "Backward Generation" algorithm that systematically builds large-scale datasets of (differential equation, first integral) pairs by deriving differential equations from sampled integrals, thereby alleviating the data scarcity bottleneck. Second, we apply supervised fine-tuning to a compact mathematical model and further improve its performance through reinforcement learning with a Levenshtein Distance-based shaped reward. In addition, we design data synthesis and blending strategies that support effective adaptation to difficult problem families from sparse examples. Experiments show that FISolver, while requiring substantially lower computational cost, significantly outperforms larger mathematical LLMs and commercial solvers such as Mathematica on challenging benchmarks, indicating a new data-driven route for automated discovery of first integrals.

</details>


### [283] [Behavior-Consistent Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21214)
*Marcel Hussing,Liv G. d'Aliberti,Claas Voelcker,Benjamin Eysenbach,Eric Eaton*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Q-value Expectile Disagreement (QED) uses a state-dependent temperature in maximum-entropy RL to bound cross-run policy divergence, reducing performance variance across runs with modest costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning often exhibits high variance across training runs, causing unreliable performance and deployment challenges. Cross-run policy divergence can be mitigated by anchoring policies via entropy regularization; however, naive entropy increase may hinder optimization and amplify off-policy error. A principled approach is needed to achieve behavior-consistency without sacrificing learning efficiency.

Method: Introduce Q-value Expectile Disagreement (QED), a state-dependent temperature schedule for Boltzmann policies. Temperature is tied to Q-disagreement and uses double-critic disagreement as a single-run proxy for cross-run disagreement. The approach derives a KL-divergence bound for pairwise policies and highlights the trade-offs between entropy and off-policy error. Empirically, evaluate on continuous-control tasks with 18 environments.

Result: QED reduces cross-run policy divergence by about two orders of magnitude and lowers return variance across runs, while maintaining comparable performance. There is a modest cost to sample efficiency.

Conclusion: QED provides a practical mechanism to achieve behavior-consistent reinforcement learning by stabilizing cross-run policy behavior through a state-dependent entropy temperature informed by Q-disagreement, enabling more reliable deployment in real-world settings.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) often exhibits high variance across training runs, leading to unreliable performance and posing a major challenge to deployment in real-world domains. In this work, we address the challenge of cross-run policy divergence by formalizing the problem of behavior-consistent RL, where the objective is to obtain policies that are both high-performing and distributionally similar across training runs. Our key observation is that maximum-entropy RL provides a direct mechanism for controlling behavioral divergence by anchoring runs to a common (uniform) prior. We prove that, for Boltzmann policies, choosing the temperature proportional to $Q$-function disagreement bounds the pairwise KL divergence between the induced policies. However, we also show that naïvely increasing entropy might impair policy optimization while amplifying off-policy error. Building upon these observations, we propose $Q$-value Expectile Disagreement (QED), a state-dependent temperature schedule that uses double-critic disagreement as a single-run proxy for cross-run disagreement. Empirically, we demonstrate that across 18 continuous-control tasks, QED reduces across-run divergence by two orders of magnitude without sacrificing performance, resulting in a considerable reduction in return variance at modest sample-efficiency costs.

</details>


### [284] [PREFINE: Preference-Based Implicit Reward and Cost Fine-Tuning for Safety Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21225)
*Richa Verma,Bavish Kulur,Sanjay Chawla,Balaraman Ravindran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PREFINE is a preference-based fine-tuning method that adapts Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to sequential decision making, aligning a pre-trained RL policy with trajectory-level cost preferences to improve safety while preserving rewards in continuous control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To make a pre-trained RL policy safety-aware without full retraining, using only a small dataset of trajectory-level preferences (low-cost vs high-cost) to guide fine-tuning and reduce costs without sacrificing performance.

Method: Constructs policy-sampled counterfactual trajectories to create meaningful preference contrasts and jointly optimizes for reward retention and safety alignment. Adapts DPO from NLP to RL by applying preference-based objective to trajectories, enabling offline-style fine-tuning with limited data and improved efficiency.

Result: Empirically reduces constraint violations and catastrophic failures by over 60% while maintaining the original reward, and achieves low-cost, high-reward behavior with better data and computational efficiency than full offline RL or imitation learning.

Conclusion: PREFINE demonstrates that preference-based fine-tuning can bridge safety alignment and reward preservation in continuous control, offering a data- and computation-efficient path to safe policy adaptation without re-training from scratch.

Abstract: We address the problem of making a pre-trained reinforcement learning (RL) policy safety-aware by incorporating cost constraints without retraining it from scratch. While costs could be numerically encoded, we assume a more general setting is when costs are provided as preferences. Given a reward-optimized policy and a small dataset of preferred (low-cost) and dispreferred (high-cost) trajectories, our goal is to fine-tune the policy to generate low-cost behaviors while retaining high rewards. Unlike standard RLHF in language models, where preferences are defined over responses to the same prompt, our setting involves trajectory-level preferences in continuous control environments. We introduce PREFINE: Preference-based Implicit Reward and Cost Fine-Tuning for Safety Alignment which is a preference-based fine-tuning method that adapts Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), which is now widely used for LLM fine-tuning, to the sequential decision making setting. PREFINE constructs policy-sampled counterfactual trajectories to establish meaningful preference contrasts and jointly optimizes for reward retention and safety alignment. Empirically, PREFINE reduces constraint violations and catastrophic failures by over 60% while maintaining original reward behavior. PREFINE produces policies that achieve low-cost, high-reward performance with significantly improved data and computational efficiency compared to full offline RL or imitation learning, bridging preference alignment and safe policy adaptation in continuous domains.

</details>


### [285] [ChunkFT: Byte-Streamed Optimization for Memory-Efficient Full Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21177)
*Yongkang Liu,Zijing Wang,Mengjie Zhao,Ercong Nie,Mingyang Wang,Qian Li,Feiliang Ren,Shi Feng,Daling Wang,Hinrich Schütze*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ChunkFT is a memory-efficient fine-tuning method that computes gradients for arbitrary sub-tensors using a dynamically activated working set, without altering the network architecture, achieving competitive results with full fine-tuning and outperforming memory-efficient baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce memory consumption during fine-tuning of large language models and enable sub-network optimization without changing model architecture.

Method: Reformulate full-parameter fine-tuning around a dynamically activated working set, enabling gradient computation for arbitrary sub-tensors and sub-networks without dense gradient computations; provides a theoretical convergence analysis in the deterministic setting.

Result: Demonstrates memory savings (e.g., 7B model with 1K input length uses 13.72GB on a single RTX 4090), successful fine-tuning of Llama 3-8B and 3-70B on modest hardware, superior memory efficiency and competitive or superior optimization quality compared to baselines, and comparable-to-superior performance relative to full-parameter fine-tuning; downstream tasks show improvements across language understanding, mathematical reasoning, and MT-Bench; code repository provided.

Conclusion: ChunkFT offers a viable, memory-efficient alternative to full-parameter fine-tuning with strong empirical performance and a theoretical convergence foundation, enabling effective fine-tuning on memory-constrained hardware.

Abstract: This work presents \textsc{ChunkFT}, a memory-efficient fine-tuning framework that reformulates full-parameter fine-tuning around a dynamically activated working set. \textsc{ChunkFT} enables gradient computation for arbitrary sub-tensors without modifying the network architecture, providing an algorithmic foundation for optimizing arbitrary sub-networks while avoiding standard dense gradient computation. We provide a theoretical convergence analysis of \textsc{ChunkFT} in the deterministic setting. Empirically, we apply \textsc{ChunkFT} to fine-tune Llama 3-8B and Llama 3-70B using a single RTX 4090-24GB GPU and 2$\times$ H800-80GB GPUs, respectively. Full-parameter fine-tuning of a 7B model with a 1K input length requires only 13.72GB of GPU memory. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of \textsc{ChunkFT} in memory usage, running time, and optimization quality. Moreover, downstream evaluations on language understanding, mathematical reasoning, and MT-Bench show that \textsc{ChunkFT} consistently outperforms existing memory-efficient baselines. Notably, \textsc{ChunkFT} achieves performance comparable to, and in some cases exceeding, full-parameter fine-tuning. Our repository is on https://github.com/misonsky/chunk.

</details>


### [286] [OCTOPUS: Optimized KV Cache for Transformers via Octahedral Parametrization Under optimal Squared error quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21226)
*Mark Boss,Vikram Voleti,Simon Donné,Shimon Vainer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OCTOPUS jointly quantizes rotated coordinate triplets for KV caches using an octahedral mapping and per-triplet Lloyd-Max quantization, yielding a data-oblivious online codec with nonuniform bit allocation that outperforms prior rotation codecs across modalities and bitrates, with no decode-time bandwidth impact thanks to on-the-fly reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV caches dominate memory bandwidth in long-context autoregressive inference. Prior rotation-based codecs (TurboQuant, PolarQuant) showed that structured random rotations with per-coordinate scalar quantizers are near-optimal; OCTOPUS extends this by quantizing coordinate triplets jointly to improve rate-distortion under a fixed total key dimension.

Method: Apply a structured random rotation to rotated coordinates, group coordinates into triplets, map the triplet direction to a square via an octahedral parameterization, and quantize the two coordinates and the triplet norm using Lloyd-Max quantizers matched to analytically tractable marginals. Optimize per-triplet squared error to yield a non-uniform bit allocation that depends only on the total key dimensionality. Demonstrate a finite-dimensional quality optimum that is constant across decoders. The codec is data-oblivious, online, and deterministic given a seed. A fused Triton implementation reconstructs keys on the fly without materializing uncompressed keys, adding no decode-time bandwidth or latency. 

Result: OCTOPUS matches or surpasses all prior rotation-based codecs across text, video, and audio at every reported bit width and metric. The lead over competing codecs grows as the bitrate decreases for extreme compression. The finite-dimensional optimum is observed to be constant across real decoders tested. 

Conclusion: OCTOPUS provides a practical, high-performance KV cache compression solution for long-context inference: data-oblivious, online, and deterministic with on-the-fly reconstruction, delivering improved rate-distortion across modalities and bitrates while incurring no additional decode-time bandwidth or latency. A project page is provided for details and implementation.

Abstract: The key-value (KV) cache dominates memory bandwidth and footprint in long-context autoregressive inference. Recent rotation-preconditioned codecs (TurboQuant, PolarQuant) show that a structured random rotation followed by a per-coordinate scalar quantizer matched to an analytically tractable marginal is a near-optimal recipe for KV compression. OCTOPUS advances this paradigm through joint quantization of rotated coordinate triplets. Each triplet's direction is mapped to a square via an octahedral parameterization, and the two resulting coordinates and the triplet norm are Lloyd-Max quantized against implementation-matched marginals. Optimizing the per-triplet squared error gives a strictly non-uniform bit allocation depending only on the total dimensionality of the keys. We find the finite-dimensional quality optimum with sweeps to be constant on every real decoder we test. The codec is data-oblivious, online, and deterministic given a seed. Across text, video, and audio, OCTOPUS matches or beats every prior rotation codec at every reported bit width and metric, with a lead that grows as bits drop for extreme compression. Furthermore, a fused Triton implementation reconstructs keys on the fly without materializing the uncompressed key, so the codec adds no decode-time bandwidth or latency over the existing dequantization. Project Page: https://octopus-quant.github.io/

</details>


### [287] [Domain-Adaptable Reinforcement Learning for Code Generation with Dense Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21180)
*Erfan Aghadavoodi Jolfaei,Daniel Maninger,Abhinav Anand,Mert Tiftikci,Mira Mezini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A reinforcement learning framework using proximal policy optimization to fine-tune LLMs for domain-aware code generation, improving functional correctness and simulator executability on MBPP/MBPP+ and RoboEval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lack of guarantees in code-generated outputs under domain-specific constraints; in robotics, environment awareness and physical constraints are critical, necessitating mechanisms to ensure correctness, safety, syntax, security, and executability.

Method: Fine-tune pre-trained LLMs with proximal policy optimization using a customizable, execution-aware reward that captures syntax, functional correctness, code style, security, and simulator executability; employs a token-level reward mapping to credit tokens based on execution outcomes; evaluates on MBPP/MBPP+ and RoboEval.

Result: Substantial improvements in functional correctness and simulator executability, including an absolute pass@1 increase of 19% on MBPP and a 51% reduction in execution failures on RoboEval.

Conclusion: Structured reinforcement learning can effectively align language models to correct program generation and domain-specific requirements.

Abstract: Large language models show strong potential for automated code generation, but lack guarantees for correctness, quality, safety, and domain-specific constraints. For instance in robotics, where code generation is increasingly being used for planning and executing actions, awareness of the environment and physical constraints is critical. To facilitate the adaption of code-generating LLMs to diverse requirements, including domain-specific ones, we present a reinforcement learning framework that fine-tunes pre-trained LLMs using proximal policy optimization. Our customizable execution-aware reward formula captures and optimizes syntax, functional correctness, code style, security, and simulator executability. A token-level reward mapping mechanism enables effective credit assignment from execution outcomes to generated tokens. The framework is evaluated on general-purpose code generation (MBPP/MBPP+) and robotic program synthesis (RoboEval). The results show substantial improvements in functional correctness and simulator executability, including an absolute pass@1 increase of 19% on MBPP and a reduction in execution failures by 51% on RoboEval. These findings demonstrate that structured reinforcement learning can effectively align language models to correct program generation and domain-specific requirements.

</details>


### [288] [APEX: Autonomous Policy Exploration for Self-Evolving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21240)
*Yibo Li,Jiashuo Yang,Zhi Zheng,Zhiyuan Hu,Yuan Sui,Shizun Wang,Yufei He,Bryan Hooi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: APEX introduces an explicit strategy-space exploration mechanism for self-evolving LLM agents to combat exploration collapse. It builds a directed acyclic graph (strategy map) of milestones with prerequisites, uses Fork Discovery to expand unexplored directions, and employs Policy Selection to balance exploration and exploitation. It outperforms baselines on Jericho text-adventure games and WebArena, with ablations supporting each component.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-evolving agents accumulate memory and reflection but suffer exploration collapse as memory grows, leading to over-reliance on familiar high-reward routines. There is a need for sustained, explicit exploration strategies that remain effective during long-horizon tasks.

Method: Construct a strategy map (a DAG of milestones with prerequisite edges) to represent an explicit strategy space. Fork Discovery adds evidence-grounded, unexplored directions to the map. Policy Selection performs planning with a balance between exploration and exploitation over the map. Evaluated on nine Jericho text-adventure games and WebArena; extensive ablations validate component contributions and robustness.

Result: APEX outperforms all baselines on the evaluated tasks. The approach is robust across diverse settings, and ablations confirm the contribution of each component (strategy map, Fork Discovery, Policy Selection) to sustained exploration.

Conclusion: Explicit strategy-space exploration via a strategy map with Fork Discovery and balanced Policy Selection effectively mitigates exploration collapse in self-evolving agents, enabling improved long-horizon decision making in interactive environments.

Abstract: LLM agents have shown strong performance across a wide range of complex tasks, including interactive environments that require long-horizon decision making. But these agents cannot learn on the fly at test time. Self-evolving agents address this by accumulating memory and reflection across episodes rather than requiring model-weight updates. However, these agents often suffer from exploration collapse: as memory grows, behavior concentrates around familiar high-reward routines, reducing the chance of discovering better alternatives. To address this problem, we propose Autonomous Policy EXploration (APEX), which builds and maintains an explicit strategy space through a strategy map-a directed acyclic graph of milestones with prerequisite dependency edges. In APEX, Fork Discovery expands the map with evidence-grounded unexplored directions, while Policy Selection balances exploration and exploitation during planning. Evaluated on nine Jericho text-adventure games and WebArena, a realistic web interaction benchmark, APEX outperforms all baselines. Extensive ablations validate each component's contribution and demonstrate robustness across diverse settings, demonstrating APEX's effectiveness for sustained exploration in self-evolving agents.

</details>


### [289] [How Much Online RL is Enough? Informative Rollouts for Offline Preference Optimization in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21266)
*Richa Verma,Balaraman Ravindran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: G2D shows offline DPO with a short GRPO warm-up can match/outperform online GRPO at substantially lower compute, reframing RLVR gains as a data informativeness problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce compute cost of online GRPO in RLVR while preserving performance by leveraging informative offline data via DPO.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) a short GRPO warm-up, (2) construct a static preference dataset from online data, (3) offline fine-tuning with DPO. Evaluate across different online-step counts (K) on Qwen2.5-7B and Llama-3.1-8B; analyze impact of number of preference pairs vs informativeness; emphasize calibrated uncertainty from warm-up.

Result: For Qwen2.5-7B, K=150 yields 62.4% on MATH-500 vs GRPO 51.6% (≈4x lower compute). For Llama-3.1-8B, K=500 yields 49.4% (surpasses GRPO). Performance correlates with informativeness of preferences, not just pair count; moderate warm-up provides better contrastive signals, while excessive warm-up causes overconfident policies and less informative data.

Conclusion: The offline-online RLVR gap is primarily a data informativeness problem; a short, calibrated online warm-up enables a compute-efficient offline DPO to match or exceed online RL performance.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for reasoning in language models, with GRPO as its primary example. However, GRPO requires continuous online rollout generation, making it computationally expensive and difficult to scale. While Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) offers a stable and efficient offline alternative, it is typically expected to underperform w.r.t. online RL methods such as GRPO when trained on rollouts from a cold supervised fine-tuned (SFT) policy. We introduce G2D (GRPO to DPO)}, a three-stage pipeline that performs a short GRPO warm-up, constructs a static preference dataset, and fine-tunes a model offline with DPO. Across a set of values of the number of online steps (K) in GRPO on Qwen2.5-7B and Llama-3.1-8B, we find that offline DPO with moderate warm-up matches or outperforms GRPO at substantially lower compute cost in our setting. On Qwen2.5-7B, G2D at K=150 achieves 62.4% on MATH-500, outperforming GRPO (51.6%) by 10.8% at ~4x lower compute. On Llama-3.1-8B, G2D at K=500 achieves 49.4%, surpassing GRPO in our experimental setting. We show that performance is not governed by the number of preference pairs, which does not vary much w.r.t. K, but by their informativeness. Moderate warm-up produces rollouts with calibrated uncertainty, yielding stronger contrastive signal, while excessive warm-up leads to overconfident policies and less informative data. Our results recast the offline-online gap in RLVR as primarily a data informativeness problem, and identify short online RL warm-up with appropriate difficulty calibration of the fine-tuning dataset as a compute-efficient alternative to online RL.

</details>


### [290] [Divide and Contrast: Learning Robust Temporal Features without Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21241)
*Abdul-Kazeem Shamba,Kerstin Bach,Gavin Taylor*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Di-COT is a self-supervised framework for time-series representation that contrasts informative substructures within a window, avoiding data augmentation and multiple encoder passes, achieving state-of-the-art results while being computationally efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on labeled data in time-series tasks while maintaining downstream performance; existing SSL methods are often costly or rely on assumptions that fail across diverse temporal dynamics.

Method: Per-iteration stochastic partitioning of each window into overlapping sub-blocks; contrastive learning on sub-blocks rather than timesteps; loss computation scales with batch size and number of sub-blocks, independent of sequence length; no data augmentation or multiple encoders.

Result: Extensive experiments on six large-scale real-world datasets plus UCR/UEA benchmarks show Di-COT learns semantically structured, transferable representations, achieving state-of-the-art performance in classification, clustering, kNN, and cross-dataset transfer, with substantially reduced training time.

Conclusion: Di-COT offers an efficient, scalable, and transferable SSL approach for time-series by focusing on substructure-level contrasts, removing the need for augmentation and multiple encoders; source code is publicly available.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning for time-series representation aims to reduce reliance on labeled data while maintaining strong downstream performance, yet many existing approaches incur high computational costs or rely on assumptions that do not hold across diverse temporal dynamics. In this work, we introduce Divide and Contrast (Di-COT), an unsupervised framework that avoids data augmentation and multiple encoder passes by contrasting informative substructures within a window rather than individual timesteps. Di-COT stochastically partitions each window into a small number of overlapping sub-blocks per iteration, enabling efficient and meaningful contrast while mitigating false positives during temporal transitions. To further improve scalability, we adopt a contrastive objective whose computation depends on the batch size and the number of sub-blocks, making loss computation independent of sequence length. Extensive experiments on six large-scale real-world datasets, as well as the UCR and UEA benchmarks, demonstrate that Di-COT learns semantically structured and transferable representations, achieving state-of-the-art performance on classification, clustering, $k$NN, and cross-dataset transfer, while substantially reducing training time. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/sfi-norwai/Di-COT.

</details>


### [291] [Graph Navier Stokes Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21247)
*Zexing Zhao,Guangsi Shi,Yu Gong,Tianyu Wang,Shirui Pan,Hongye Cheng,Yuxiao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A graph neural network that uses a Navier–Stokes inspired convection term to complement diffusion, mitigating oversmoothing and improving accuracy across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs with diffusion-based message passing suffer from oversmoothing as depth increases and may struggle with varying levels of node homophily. A convection mechanism could enhance information propagation.

Method: Introduce Graph Navier Stokes Networks (GNSN) with a dynamic velocity field on the graph to govern convection. The model adaptively balances convection and diffusion, enabling efficient and direct message propagation across graphs with different homophily.

Result: GNSN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on twelve real-world datasets for node classification, and experiments show effective alleviation of oversmoothing.

Conclusion: Incorporating a convection term via a graph-based Navier–Stokes framework yields a more expressive propagation mechanism, robust to varying homophily and effective at mitigating oversmoothing while improving accuracy.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as a cornerstone of deep learning, with most existing methods rooted in graph signal processing and diffusion equations to model message passing. However, these approaches inherently suffer from the oversmoothing problem, where node features become indistinguishable as the network depth increases. Inspired by the Navier Stokes equations, we introduce Graph Navier Stokes Networks (GNSN), a novel architecture that transcends conventional diffusion-based message passing by incorporating convection into graph structures. GNSN defines a dynamic velocity field on the graph to govern convection, enabling more efficient and direct message propagation. By adaptively balancing convection and diffusion, GNSN is able to efficiently handle datasets with varying levels of homophily. Extensive evaluations across twelve real-world datasets demonstrate that GNSN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in classification accuracy. Moreover, experimental results further emphasize its effectiveness in alleviating the oversmoothing problem.

</details>


### [292] [TimeSRL: Generalizable Time-Series Behavioral Modeling via Semantic RL-Tuned LLMs -- A Case Study in Mental Health](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21295)
*Yuang Fan,Lilin Xu,Millie Wu,Jingping Nie,Qingyu Chen,Yuzhe Yang,Zhuo Zhang,Xin Liu,Subigya Nepal,Xiaofan Jiang,Xuhai "Orson" Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TimeSRL is a two-stage LLM framework applying a semantic bottleneck to longitudinal sensing, achieving cross-dataset generalization in mental-health prediction via RL-tuned semantic abstractions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Longitudinal passive sensing suffers from cross-dataset distribution shifts and cohort-specific artifacts; LLMs struggle with long heterogeneous time-series; need semantically robust generalization.

Method: Two-stage: stage 1 abstracts raw signals into high-level natural language; stage 2 predicts outcomes using abstractions. Training uses Group Relative Policy Optimization with Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards, no gold intermediate annotations.

Result: On mental-health prediction under LOSO, TimeSRL achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing MAE compared to strong baselines; anxiety: 3.1–10.1% and 9.5–44.1%; depression: 3.2–9.6% and 27.4–57.6%; p<0.05; robust cross-benchmark transfer without target-domain fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Semantic abstractions are reusable; RL-tuned LLMs enable generalizable behavior modeling via semantic bottlenecks; potential new direction for cross-cohort generalization in longitudinal data.

Abstract: Longitudinal passive sensing enables continuous health prediction, yet models often fail under cross-dataset distribution shifts. Traditional ML overfits cohort-specific artifacts, while Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle to reason reliably over long, heterogeneous time-series. We introduce TimeSRL, a two-stage LLM framework that routes predictions through an explicit semantic bottleneck. The model first abstracts raw signals into high-level natural language, then predicts behavioral outcomes from these abstractions alone. This forces the model to reason over semantic concepts that we argue generalize better than raw numbers. We optimize this process end-to-end using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), learning outcome-aligned abstractions without gold intermediate annotations. Instantiated on mental-health prediction, TimeSRL achieves state-of-the-art performance on a benchmark designed to stress-test cross-cohort generalization under a rigorous leave-one-dataset-out (LOSO) protocol, reducing mean absolute error (MAE) over strong non-LLM ML and LLM baselines by 3.1--10.1% and 9.5--44.1% for anxiety, and 3.2--9.6% and 27.4--57.6% for depression (all $p$s<0.05). TimeSRL significantly outperforms prior methods in cross-benchmark transfer across different sensing pipelines, rivaling its own within-domain performance without target-domain fine-tuning. These results demonstrate that semantic abstractions are reusable and point to a new direction for generalizable behavior modeling via RL-tuned LLMs.

</details>


### [293] [On the Cost and Benefit of Chain of Thought: A Learning-Theoretic Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21260)
*Yue Zhang,Zhiyi Dong,Tommaso Cesari,Yongyi Mao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Canonical decomposition of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning risk into two opposing terms: oracle-trajectory risk (OTR) and trajectory-mismatch risk (TMR). OTR captures CoT benefits and reduces to target-domain risk; TMR captures error accumulation along mismatched reasoning trajectories and can blow up without structure. With stability, TMR is tightly bounded by an amplification factor delineating linear, bounded, and exponential growth regimes. The work clarifies when CoT helps or hurts and what governs the transition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a rigorous learning-theoretic framework to understand CoT by formalizing the interaction between an answer map and a chain-rule that generates intermediate steps, and to quantify when CoT improves or degrades performance.

Method: Model CoT as an autoregressive interaction between an answer map and a chain rule; define reasoning risk of a hypothesis; derive a tight canonical decomposition into OTR and TMR; analyze stability conditions and derive a tight upper bound on TMR with an exact amplification factor; characterize regimes of error growth (bounded/linear/exponential).

Result: Two-term decomposition of CoT risk (OTR and TMR); TMR can be arbitrarily large in absence of stability even if OTR is zero; under stability, a tight bound on TMR is given by an amplification factor that separates bounded, linear, and exponential growth regimes; provides a precise theory of when CoT helps, when it hurts, and the transition between regimes.

Conclusion: The paper offers a rigorous framework to understand the utility of CoT, clarifying the conditions under which CoT is beneficial and how stability controls the magnitude of its potential harm, thereby illuminating how to design CoT systems that avoid detrimental error growth.

Abstract: We develop a learning-theoretic framework for understanding Chain of Thought (CoT). We model CoT as the interaction between an answer map and a chain rule that generates intermediate questions autoregressively, and define the reasoning risk of a hypothesis under this interaction. Our first result is a tight canonical decomposition of this risk into two terms with opposing roles: an oracle-trajectory risk (OTR), which captures the benefit of CoT and reduces to a target-domain risk in a domain adaptation problem, and a trajectory-mismatch risk (TMR), which captures the cost of CoT through error accumulation along mismatched reasoning trajectories. We then show that this cost is unavoidable without structure: if any one of the loss, the hypothesis answer map, or the chain rule lacks stability, the TMR can be arbitrarily large even when the OTR is zero and the hypothesis is uniformly close to the ground truth. Conversely, under stability, we prove a tight upper bound on the TMR governed by an exact amplification factor that identifies bounded, linear, and exponential error-growth regimes. Together, these results give a precise theory of when CoT helps, when it hurts, and what controls the transition between the two.

</details>


### [294] [From Circuit Evidence to Mechanistic Theory: An Inductive Logic Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21303)
*Nura Aljaafari,Danilo S. Carvalho,Andre Freitas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A formal coherence layer for cumulative mechanistic science in neural networks, using Causal Functional Signatures (CFS) and Inductive Logic Programming (ILP)–derived architectural signatures to enable cross-model comparison and transfer via θ-subsumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic interpretability often yields isolated findings with no shared formal representation of what circuits compute or how findings relate. This work aims to make mechanistic claims explicit, comparable, and portable across model scales.

Method: Define Causal Functional Signature (CFS) grounded in causal attribution evidence and token-role profiles; learn architectural signature τ_arch via inductive logic programming from scale-invariant structural predicates; use θ-subsumption as a coherence criterion; evaluate transfer across model scales and architecture families; compare ILP signatures to graph kernels and feature-vector baselines.

Result: CFS reveals qualitatively distinct task-specific strategies (e.g., attention-mediated copying vs MLP-mediated binding). ILP signatures achieve substantially better structural separation than graph-kernel and feature-vector baselines and support principled transfer across model scales and architecture families.

Conclusion: A formal, portable coherence layer enables cumulativity in mechanistic interpretability by making circuit-level claims explicit and comparable across models, fostering cross-scale transfer and unified representations of circuit mechanisms.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability produces circuit-level causal analyses of neural network behaviour, but discovered circuits often remain isolated experimental artefacts: there is no shared formal representation for what circuits compute, how they relate, or when two findings provide evidence for the same mechanism. This work provides a formal infrastructure for cumulative mechanistic science by treating circuit interpretation as inductive theory construction. Each circuit is characterised at two levels: a Causal Functional Signature (CFS), which grounds component behaviour in causal attribution evidence and token role profiles, and an architectural signature $τ_{\mathrm{arch}}$, learned by inductive logic programming (ILP) from scale-invariant structural predicates. Together, these constitute a formal coherence layer that makes mechanistic claims explicit, comparable via $θ$-subsumption, and portable across model scales. CFS reveals qualitatively distinct computational strategies across task types, including attention-mediated copying versus MLP-mediated binding. ILP signatures achieve substantially better structural separation than graph kernel and feature-vector baselines, and support principled transfer across model scales and architecture families.

</details>


### [295] [Nonparametric Learning and Earning with One-Point Feedback under Nonstationarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21263)
*Xiangyu Yang,Feng Xu,Jian-Qiang Hu,Jiaqiao Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An online, nonparametric dynamic pricing framework that learns from single-period revenue, uses restarts to handle nonstationarity, and a meta-learning layer to hedge restarting schedules; it provides regret guarantees and demonstrates empirical effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the dual challenge of learning the revenue–demand relationship from limited feedback (one observation per period) and adapting to potentially nonstationary market conditions, without imposing a parametric demand form.

Method: Update prices via revenue-based gradient approximations constructed from one observation per period. Introduce a restarting mechanism to periodically refresh the learning process and discount outdated information. When nonstationarity level is unknown, add a meta-learning layer to hedge across multiple restarting schedules. Provide theoretical performance guarantees (regret bounds) as a function of time horizon and variation magnitude, plus empirical validation on synthetic and real-world data.

Result: Establish performance guarantees showing cumulative revenue regret scales with horizon and market variation. Demonstrate effectiveness through simulation studies on synthetic data and real-world data.

Conclusion: The proposed procedures enable effective learning and revenue achievement in nonstationary environments without parametric demand assumptions, combining gradient-based updates, restarts, and meta-learning to adapt and perform robustly.

Abstract: Firms increasingly rely on dynamic pricing to respond to evolving customer demand, yet in many applications they observe only the revenue generated by a single posted price in each period. At the same time, market conditions may shift gradually or abruptly due to changes in customer preferences, competition, or external shocks. These features create two intertwined challenges: learning the revenue--demand relationship from limited feedback and adapting pricing decisions to a changing environment. We study how a seller can learn and earn effectively under these constraints, without assuming a specific parametric form for demand. We develop a learning framework that updates prices using revenue-based gradient approximations constructed from one observation per period. To address environmental changes, we incorporate a restarting mechanism that periodically refreshes the learning process so that outdated information is discounted. When the degree of nonstationarity is unknown, we further introduce a meta-learning layer to adaptively hedge across multiple restarting schedules. We provide performance guarantees for our approach, showing how cumulative revenue loss relative to a fully informed benchmark depends on both the time horizon and the magnitude of market variation. Simulation experiments using synthetic and real-world data illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed procedures.

</details>


### [296] [FedCoE: Bridging Generalization and Personalization via Federated Coordinated Dual-level MoEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21264)
*Penglin Dai,Fulian Li,Xincao Xu,Junhua Wang,Lixin Duan,Xiao Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedCoE presents a coordinated dual-level mixture-of-experts framework for federated learning, balancing global generalization and local personalization with an adaptive cold-start mechanism; it outperforms baselines on both global and personalized metrics and requires no local fine-tuning for new clients in cold-start scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-IID data in federated learning leads to parameter divergence with simple averaging, while personalization methods risk overfitting to local data and poor generalization to new clients (cold-start). A coordinated mixture-of-experts can route data to specialized models while maintaining global coherence.

Method: Maintain multiple independent global expert models on the server and a shared gating network that dynamically models client-expert correlations during aggregation. Introduce an adaptive mechanism enabling new clients to leverage the global expert pool with minimal local training, aiming to mitigate expert drift and gating inconsistency.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows FedCoE achieves 78.00% global accuracy and 89.32% personalized accuracy on average, outperforming baselines by 8.82% (global) and 29.19% (personalized). In cold-start tests, it reaches 77.27% accuracy without local fine-tuning, exceeding baselines by over 12.54%.

Conclusion: FedCoE effectively balances global generalization and local personalization, mitigates expert drift and gating inconsistency, and provides a practical solution to cold-start in FL through an adaptive, coordinated mixture-of-experts approach.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for privacy-preserving distributed learning. However, existing FL methods face a fundamental challenge. Traditional averaging-based approaches suffer from parameter divergence under non-IID conditions, while personalized FL methods overfit to local data and fail to generalize to new clients (cold-start problem). Mixture-of-Experts naturally addresses this by routing heterogeneous data to specialized experts rather than forcing uniform aggregation. In this paper, we propose FedCoE, a Federated Coordinated dual-level mixture-of-Experts framework that effectively balances global generalization with local personalization. FedCoE maintains multiple independent global expert models on the server and employs a shared gating network to dynamically model client-expert correlations during aggregation, effectively mitigating expert drift and gating inconsistency. To address the cold-start challenge, we introduce an adaptive mechanism that enables new clients to immediately leverage the global expert pool without extensive local training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedCoE achieves 78.00% global accuracy and 89.32% personalized accuracy on average, outperforming the baseline by 8.82% and 29.19%, respectively. In cold-start scenarios, FedCoE delivers 77.27% accuracy without any local fine-tuning, outperforming baselines by over 12.54%.

</details>


### [297] [DeCoR: Design and Control Co-Optimization for Urban Streets Using Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21311)
*Bibek Poudel,Lei Zhu,Kevin Heaslip,Sai Swaminathan,Weizi Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DeCoR is a two-stage RL framework that jointly optimizes crosswalk layout and network-level signal control using flow observations, achieving substantial QoS improvements on a real-world corridor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between perception outputs (flows) and urban design decisions, enabling scalable, joint optimization of pedestrian network design and traffic signal control.

Method: Stage 1 (design): encode pedestrian network as a graph and use a generative policy to parameterize a Gaussian mixture model over crosswalk location and width; sample new crosswalks. Stage 2 (control): a shared policy learns adaptive signal timings to minimize jointly pedestrians’ and vehicles’ delays for each proposed layout. Trained/evaluated on a 750 m real-world corridor with demand from video and Wi‑Fi data.

Result: Layout optimization: pedestrian arrival time to the nearest crosswalk reduced by 23% using fewer crosswalks. Control optimization: pedestrian wait time reduced by 79% and vehicle wait time reduced by 65% relative to fixed-time signaling. The control policy generalizes to unseen demand and is robust to layout changes without retraining.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a feasible, scalable approach for jointly optimizing urban design and traffic control via reinforcement learning, with substantial mobility efficiency gains and transferability; practical deployment would require data quality, computational resources, and validation under diverse conditions.

Abstract: Modern vision systems can detect, track, and forecast urban actors at scale, yet translating perception outputs to urban design remains limited. We introduce DeCoR, a two-stage reinforcement learning framework that leverages flow observations to co-optimize crosswalk layout and network-level signal control. The design stage encodes the pedestrian network as a graph and learns a generative policy that parameterizes a Gaussian mixture model over crosswalk location and width, from which new crosswalks are sampled. For each layout, a shared control policy learns adaptive signal timings to minimize joint pedestrian and vehicle delay. On a 750 m real-world urban corridor with demand sensed from video and Wi-Fi logs, DeCoR learns a layout that reduces pedestrian arrival time to their nearest crosswalk by 23% while using fewer crosswalks than existing configurations. On the control side, DeCoR reduces pedestrian and vehicle wait time by 79% and 65%, respectively, relative to fixed-time signalization. Further, the control policy generalizes to demands outside of training and is robust to layout changes without retraining.

</details>


### [298] [Data-Efficient Neural Operator Training via Physics-Based Active Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21348)
*Alicja Polanska,Lorenzo Zanisi,Vignesh Gopakumar,Stanislas Pamela*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A physics-informed active learning method using PDE residuals as acquisition scores improves data efficiency for neural operators solving PDEs, validated on Burgers and Navier–Stokes; outperforms random sampling and is competitive with state-of-the-art while injecting a physics bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural operators for PDEs require large training datasets; data-efficient training remains a bottleneck. An acquisition strategy informed by physics can selectively sample the most informative states where the model's physics understanding is weakest.

Method: Introduce physics-based acquisition: a physics-informed active learning algorithm that uses the PDE residual to guide data selection. The framework iteratively queries the most informative samples based on residuals and retrains the neural operator. Validated on 1D Burgers equation and 2D compressible Navier–Stokes equations.

Result: Physics-based acquisition consistently outperforms random acquisition in data efficiency and matches state-of-the-art methods. It also injects a physics inductive bias into training, concentrating simulation effort where the model’s physical understanding is weakest.

Conclusion: The proposed physics-based acquisition improves data efficiency and generalizes across different PDE systems by targeting regions with high residuals, thus focusing learning where physical understanding is weakest and reducing simulation costs.

Abstract: Solving partial differential equations with neural operators significantly reduces computational costs but remains bottlenecked by high training data requirements. Active learning offers a natural framework to mitigate this by selectively acquiring the most informative samples in an iterative manner. We introduce physics-based acquisition - a novel physics-informed active learning algorithm that leverages the partial differential equation residual to guide data selection. We validate the method by presenting numerical experiments for the 1D Burgers equation and the 2D compressible Navier-Stokes equations. We show that, in our experiments, physics-based acquisition consistently outperforms random acquisition and matches the state of the art in data efficiency. At the same time, it has the unique advantage of injecting a physics inductive bias into the training process, ensuring that simulation cost is spent where the model's physical understanding is weakest.

</details>


### [299] [A Mechanistic Study of Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21288)
*Marin Biloš,James T. Wilson,Anderson Schneider,Yuriy Nevmyvaka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Architecturally diverse tabular foundation models converge in accuracy; they implement distinct, testable in-context readouts and invariance mechanisms, with causal interventions confirming their behavior and perturbations revealing robust failure modes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify whether similar accuracy across models reflects the same underlying in-context algorithms, identify the sources of permutation invariances, and assess robustness under adversarial perturbations.

Method: Combine causal interventions with targeted perturbations to probe three readout schemes (attention-weighted vote, class-conditional mean, etc.), analyze permutation invariances linked to positional parameters, and test refit baselines using hub/rank attacks across tabular models.

Result: Models exhibit qualitatively different similarity-based readouts; representation collapse is not practically harmful; permutation invariances derive from particular positional parameters; perturbations reproduce predicted failures; hub and rank attacks isolate readouts from baselines; provides a mechanistic account of biases and failures in tabular foundation models.

Conclusion: Identifies the inductive biases governing accuracy and failure modes, enabling better understanding and evaluation of tabular foundation models and guiding future architectural and robustness-focused design.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models with different architectures converge in accuracy across a range of classification and regression tasks. This raises questions a leaderboard cannot answer: (i) whether the models execute the same in-context algorithm, (ii) where row, column, and class-permutation invariances originate, and (iii) how robust they are under perturbations engineered against the inferred mechanism. We characterize all three. The model families realize qualitatively distinct similarity-based readouts: from an attention-weighted vote over context labels to a class-conditional mean readout, each confirmed by causal intervention. We find that the representation collapse highlighted in prior work is not a practical concern for them. Each model's permutation invariances trace to specific positional parameters whose removal preserves accuracy and makes approximate invariance exact. Perturbations engineered against each readout reproduce predicted failure modes; hub and rank attacks isolate them from refit baselines. Together these results give a mechanistic account of contemporary tabular foundation models and identify which inductive biases govern both their accuracy and characteristic failures.

</details>


### [300] [On the Regularity and Generalization of One-Step Wasserstein-guided Generative Models for PDE-Induced Measures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21388)
*Likun Lin,Zhongjian Wang,Jack Xin,Zhiwen Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a theoretical framework showing doubling properties for PDE-induced target measures, Hölder regularity of optimal transport from uniform to target, and justification for one-step Wasserstein-guided generative models (e.g., DeepParticle) with excess-risk and robustness results, supported by experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite empirical success, theory of statistical accuracy and generalization of generative models for PDE-driven distributions is limited. The work seeks regularity of transport maps and generalization for one-step Wasserstein-guided learning in scientific computing.

Method: Show that normalized target densities from linear elliptic/parabolic equations and diffusion/Fokker–Planck equations on torus satisfy doubling conditions. Use regularity theory for optimal transport between doubling measures to deduce Hölder continuity of the optimal transport map from a uniform source to the target. Derive excess-risk bounds for DeepParticle and robustness estimates under target shift; validate with numerical experiments.

Result: Target measures satisfy doubling; the optimal transport map from a uniform source to the target is Hölder continuous; excess-risk bounds for the learned map (DeepParticle) are established; robustness under target shift proven; experimental results support the theoretical rates.

Conclusion: The work provides an approximation-theoretic justification for one-step transport-based generative models for PDE-induced distributions, demonstrates robustness and generalization properties, and confirms the theory with experiments.

Abstract: Despite the remarkable empirical success of generative models, the available theory on their statistical accuracy in scientific computing remains largely pessimistic. This paper develops a theoretical framework for understanding the regularity of transport maps and the generalization properties of one-step Wasserstein-guided generative models for PDE-induced probability measures. We consider normalized target densities associated with linear elliptic and parabolic equations on bounded domains, as well as diffusion and Fokker--Planck equations on the torus. Under standard structural assumptions, we prove that these target measures satisfy doubling conditions. By combining this fact with regularity theory for optimal transport between doubling measures, we show that the optimal transport map from a uniform source measure to the target measure is Hölder continuous. This regularity yields an approximation-theoretic justification for one-step generative models that learn PDE-induced distributions via a single pushforward map. As a representative instance, we study DeepParticle and derive excess-risk bounds characterizing the discrepancy between the learned map and the population-optimal map. We also establish a robustness estimate under target shift and illustrate the theory with experiments which support the derived rates.

</details>


### [301] [Automatic Discovery of Disease Subgroups by Contrasting with Healthy Controls](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21301)
*Robin Louiset,Edouard Duchesnay,Benoit Dufumier,Antoine Grigis,Pietro Gori*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Deep UCSL, a contrastive, EM-optimized deep framework to discover disease-specific subgroups by contrasting patients with controls, improving subgroup quality on MNIST and medical imaging data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To isolate pathological factors by removing shared variability with healthy controls, enabling interpretable, homogeneous subgroups in biomedical data.

Method: A deep feature extractor learns a discriminative space; a novel conditional joint-likelihood loss over latent clusters and patient/control labels is optimized via EM, with a regularizer to emphasize disease-specific variability and suppress shared variability.

Result: Empirically, Deep UCSL yields higher-quality subgroups than prior methods on a MNIST example and four real medical imaging datasets; code and data are publicly available.

Conclusion: Contrastive, EM-based subgroup discovery can disentangle disease-specific factors from nuisance variation, enabling interpretable subgroups with practical applicability in biomedical imaging.

Abstract: In biomedical Subgroup Discovery, practitioners are interested in discovering interpretable and homogeneous subgroups within a group of patients. In this paper, assuming that healthy subjects (i.e., controls) share common but irrelevant factors of variation with the patients, we motivate and develop a Contrastive Subgroup Discovery method, entitled Deep UCSL. By contrasting patients with controls, Deep UCSL identifies subgroups driven solely by pathological factors, ignoring common variability shared with healthy subjects. Our framework employs a deep feature extractor to learn a discriminative representation space. Mathematically, we derive a novel loss based on the conditional joint likelihood of latent clusters and patient/control labels, optimized via an Expectation-Maximization strategy alternating between subgroup inference and feature encoder updates. A regularization term further encourages representations to capture disease-specific variability while ignoring variability shared with controls. Compared to previous related works, our approach quantitatively improves the quality of the estimated subgroups, as demonstrated on a MNIST example and four distinct real medical imaging datasets. Code and datasets are available at: https://github.com/rlouiset/deep_ucsl.

</details>


### [302] [FedCritic: Serverless Federated Critic Learning-based Resource Allocation for Multi-Cell OFDMA in 6G](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21418)
*Amin Farajzadeh,Melike Erol-Kantarci*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedCritic: a serverless federated multi-agent actor-critic framework for distributed downlink resource management in 6G ultra-dense networks, using gossip-based critic sharing to handle interference and long-term QoS constraints; shows performance gains and stable training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In 6G ultra-dense networks with aggressive frequency reuse, inter-cell interference (ICI) is severe. Joint subcarrier scheduling and power control across neighboring cells are strongly coupled, requiring distributed strategies that enforce long-term QoS without centralized coordination.

Method: Proposes FedCritic with virtual-queue deficit weights for QoS, decentralized execution, and federated critic via gossip-based parameter averaging over the interference graph; avoids centralized training and coordination; retains local policies.

Result: Simulation in interference-rich reuse-1 setting shows improvements in mean SINR, cell-edge rate, network-wide average sum-rate, and fairness relative to non-coordinated and CTDE baselines; more stable training and reduced coordination overhead.

Conclusion: FedCritic provides a viable, scalable solution for distributed downlink resource management under interference coupling with long-term QoS guarantees, achieving performance gains and stable training.

Abstract: In sixth-generation (6G) ultra-dense networks, aggressive frequency reuse amplifies inter-cell interference (ICI), making multi-cell orthogonal frequency-division multiple access (OFDMA) scheduling and power control strongly coupled across neighboring cells. We study distributed downlink resource management -- joint subcarrier scheduling and power allocation -- under interference coupling and long-term per-user quality-of-service (QoS) minimum-rate constraints. By using virtual-queue deficit weights to enforce long-term QoS, we develop FedCritic, a serverless federated multi-agent actor-critic framework with decentralized execution. Unlike centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) approaches that require centralized critic learning and joint trajectory aggregation, FedCritic federates the critic through lightweight gossip-based parameter averaging over the interference graph, enabling stable value estimation without a central coordinator while keeping policies local. Simulations in an interference-rich reuse-1 setting show that FedCritic improves mean signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) and cell-edge rate, increases network-wide average sum-rate and fairness relative to non-coordinated and CTDE baselines, and achieves more stable training with lower coordination overhead.

</details>


### [303] [HiRes: Inspectable Precedent Memory for Reaction Condition Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21420)
*Shreyas Vinaya Sathyanarayana,Raja Sekhar Pappala,Deepak Warrier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HiRes is a retrieval-augmented hierarchical representation model for reaction-condition prediction that achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on USPTO-Condition tasks and provides interpretable chemical precedents via a k-NN retrieval memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In practical synthesis planning, chemists need both accurate condition recommendations and justification through precedents; existing purely parametric models lack interpretable precedent memory.
