Method: HiRes uses a graph encoder, transformation-aware cross-attention, multi-stream reaction fusion, and a k-NN retrieval layer. The learned reaction space serves as both a classifier feature and an inspectable precedent memory, trained end-to-end on USPTO-Condition data.

Result: Acc@1 Catalyst 0.929, Solvent 0.534, Reagent 0.530; Catalyst ties best baseline; outperforms REACON on Solvent and Reagent; paired bootstrap analysis shows statistically significant gains for solvent and reagent when retrieval is integrated with learned condition heads, compared to purely parametric models.

Conclusion: HiRes bridges predictive accuracy and chemical interpretability by offering a single representation that yields competitive recommendations and concrete chemical precedents necessary for practical synthesis planning.

Abstract: Reaction condition recommendation sits immediately after retrosynthetic disconnection selection, and in practice, chemists require both accurate predictions and the precedents that justify them. We present HiRes (Hierarchical Reaction Representations), a retrieval-augmented condition recommendation system whose learned reaction space serves as both a classifier feature and an inspectable precedent memory. The model combines a graph encoder, transformation-aware cross-attention, multi-stream reaction fusion, and a k-NN retrieval layer. HiRes achieves state-of-the-art performance among primary-slot USPTO-Condition models, reaching Catalyst, Solvent, and Reagent top-1 accuracies (Acc@1) of 0.929, 0.534, and 0.530 respectively. It ties the best reported baseline on Catalyst while outperforming models such as REACON on Solvent and Reagent. Furthermore, paired bootstrap analysis demonstrates that integrating retrieval with learned condition heads provides statistically significant gains for solvent and reagent selection over purely parametric approaches. Ultimately, HiRes bridges the gap between predictive accuracy and chemical interpretability, offering a single representation that supplies both competitive recommendations and the concrete chemical precedents necessary for practical synthesis planning.

</details>


### [304] [torchtune: PyTorch native post-training library](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21442)
*Mark Obozov,Maxime Griot,Joseph Cummings,Evan Smothers,Felipe Mello,Rafi Ayub,Philip John Bontrager,Salman Mohammadi,Ariel Kwiatkowski,Nathan Azrak,Mircea Mironenco*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Torchtune is a PyTorch-native library that streamlines LLM post-training, prioritizing modularity and transparency. It outperforms Axolotl and Unsloth in performance and memory across various settings, while remaining flexible for rapid research iteration and deployment-oriented workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern LLMs rely on multistage training, with post-training as the primary interface for adapting open-weight models. There is a need for a transparent, extensible, and hackable post-training framework that remains PyTorch-native rather than sacrificing flexibility for convenience.

Method: The paper outlines design principles implemented in torchtune, including model builders, training recipes, and a distributed training stack. It emphasizes modularity and direct access to underlying PyTorch components. The evaluation compares torchtune against Axolotl and Unsloth across representative post-training settings to assess performance and memory efficiency.

Result: Empirical results show strong performance and memory efficiency across multiple post-training settings, with torchtune offering the flexibility required for rapid research iteration.

Conclusion: Torchtune provides a practical foundation for reproducible LLM post-training research, combining PyTorch-native design with modular components that facilitate experimentation, deployment-ready workflows, and transparent, extensible pipelines.

Abstract: Modern LLMs typically require multistage training pipelines to achieve strong downstream performance, with post-training serving as the main interface for adapting open-weight models. We introduce torchtune, a PyTorch-native library designed to streamline the post-training lifecycle of LLMs, enabling efficient fine-tuning, experimentation, and deployment-oriented workflows. Unlike many existing fine-tuning frameworks, which often optimize for ease of use, specialized recipes, or hardware efficiency at the cost of transparency and extensibility, torchtune emphasizes modularity, hackability, and direct access to the underlying PyTorch components. In this paper, we present the design principles behind torchtune, describe how they are reflected in its model builders, training recipes, and distributed training stack, and evaluate the library across representative post-training settings. We compare against popular fine-tuning frameworks, including Axolotl and Unsloth, and show that torchtune provides strong performance and memory efficiency across many settings while remaining flexible enough for rapid research iteration. These results position torchtune as a practical foundation for reproducible LLMs post-training research.

</details>


### [305] [A New Framework to Analyse the Distributional Robustness of Deep Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21313)
*Divij Khaitan,Subhashis Banerjee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework to assess distributional robustness of neural networks by modeling layer weight–activation interactions with Bernoulli variables, using class separation as a robustness proxy; evaluated on CIFAR‑10 and ImageNet; can distinguish memorized from non-memorized models; activation-space diagnostics behave differently under shifts; offers model‑level robustness diagnostics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address brittleness of DNNs under distributional shifts and the lack of reliable, scalable diagnostics for representation structure and robustness. The work aims to diagnose whether robustness is rooted in weight-space or activation-space interactions and whether memorization correlates with robustness.

Method: Define Bernoulli-based diagnostics to capture interactions between layer weights and activations; use the separation between classes as a diagnostic proxy for robustness; perform path-based analyses in weight space; test on CIFAR‑10 and ImageNet; compare behavior under distribution shifts and between memorized vs non-memorized networks; attempt analogous experiments in activation space.

Result: Diagnostics distinguish between memorized and non-memorized networks in weight space; distribution shifts reduce class separation under the proposed path-based diagnostics; activation-space diagnostics do not generalize in the same way, suggesting different structural properties; the framework provides useful model-level diagnostics of representation structure and robustness.

Conclusion: The proposed Bernoulli-interaction framework offers a practical tool for diagnosing robustness and representation structure in neural networks, with evidence that distributional shifts impact the weight-space diagnostics and that activation-space properties may differ, highlighting nuanced sources of brittleness.

Abstract: Deep neural networks have achieved impressive performance on a variety of tasks, but their brittleness to distributional shifts remains a significant barrier to real-world deployment. In this paper, we propose a framework to analyse and quantify the distributional robustness of neural networks by studying the interactions between layer weights and activations. We model these interactions using Bernoulli distributions, using the separation between classes as a diagnostic proxy for robustness. We demonstrate the usefulness of this framework through models trained on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. We show that our proposed metrics can distinguish between networks that have memorised their training data and those that have not. We also perform analogous experiments in the activation space and find that the same properties do not hold up. Additionally, we investigate the behaviour of our metrics under various distribution shifts and show that these shifts reduce separation under our path-based diagnostics. Our results suggest that this framework provides useful model-level diagnostics of representation structure and robustness.

</details>


### [306] [CRAFT: Conflict-Resolved Aggregation for Federated Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21317)
*Ziqi Wang,Qiang Liu,Nils Thuerey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRAFT introduces a closed-form, geometry-based aggregation for federated learning that resolves client conflicts by projecting the global update onto a conflict-free reference direction, with layer-wise adaptation and theoretical support for common descent; empirically improves global accuracy and reduces client disparity while avoiding iterative solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In FL with non-iid data, naive averaging of client updates can harm certain clients due to conflicting updates, creating a bottleneck and degraded performance for some participants.

Method: Frame aggregation as a constrained optimization to find the update closest to a reference direction under conflict-free alignment constraints; derive a closed-form solution; apply layer-wise adaptation to handle conflicts at different feature granularities.

Result: The approach yields a common-descent structure, mitigates conflicts through projection geometry, and empirically improves global model accuracy and fairness across heterogeneous clients compared with state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: CRAFT provides an efficient, principled aggregation mechanism that mitigates client conflicts in FL, with theoretical justification and strong empirical gains; source code is provided.

Abstract: The aggregation of conflicting client updates remains a fundamental bottleneck in federated learning (FL) under heterogeneous data distributions. Naive averaging can produce a global update that improves the global objective while conflicting with specific clients, causing degradation for those clients. In this work, we propose CRAFT (Conflict-Resolved Aggregation for Federated Training), a new aggregation framework that treats the global update as a geometric correction problem. We formulate aggregation as finding the update closest to a reference direction while satisfying conflict-free alignment constraints. We derive a closed-form expression for the constrained optimization problem, avoiding the computational overhead of iterative solvers. Furthermore, we use a layer-wise adaptation to address conflicts at varying feature granularities. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that CRAFT promotes a common-descent structure and mitigates conflicts through its projection geometry. Extensive experiments on heterogeneous benchmarks demonstrate that CRAFT improves the accuracy of the global model while reducing performance disparity across clients compared with state-of-the-art baselines. The source code for CRAFT is available at https://github.com/tum-pbs/CRAFT.

</details>


### [307] [Optimized Federated Knowledge Distillation with Distributed Neural Architecture Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21322)
*Chaimaa Medjadji,Sylvain Kubler,Yves Le Traon,Guilain Leduc,Sadi Alawadi,Feras M. Awaysheh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedKDNAS couples on-device neural-architecture search with distillation to tackle non-IID data and device heterogeneity in FL, achieving better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs with minimal communication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Statistical heterogeneity (non-IID data) and system heterogeneity (varying device capabilities) in FL create conflicting demands on model accuracy and resource use. Fixed architectures force compromises, hurting real-world performance.

Method: Each client autonomously selects a lightweight, constraint-satisfying architecture. Local training uses a hybrid objective of supervised learning and knowledge distillation from server-provided targets. Clients share only predictions (logits) on a public reference set. The server aggregates these predictions, optionally blends them with a teacher model, to produce stable distillation targets for the next round.

Result: Evaluated on six datasets against six FL baselines (FedAvg, Ditto, FedMD, FedDF, FedDistill, Local-KD). FedKDNAS achieves superior Pareto efficiency, with up to 15% accuracy gains under non-IID data, about 28% reduction in client CPU usage, and up to 44x lower communication overhead, while preserving lightweight logit-based communication.

Conclusion: A distillation-driven on-device architecture search framework can substantially improve accuracy-efficiency trade-offs in heterogeneous FL settings, demonstrating strong Pareto improvements and efficient communication.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without centralizing data. However, real-world deployments must simultaneously address statistical heterogeneity across client data (non-IID), system heterogeneity in device capabilities, and communication efficiency. Existing FL approaches mitigate these challenges through improved aggregation, personalization, or knowledge distillation, but they almost universally assume a fixed client architecture, limiting adaptability to heterogeneous data complexity and hardware constraints. This architectural constraint often leads to suboptimal trade-offs between accuracy and efficiency in real-world FL systems. This work introduces FedKDNAS, a distillation-driven FL framework that combines client-side neural architecture selection with distillation of server-coordinated knowledge. Each client autonomously selects a lightweight model under accuracy-resource constraints. It then trains it locally using a hybrid objective combining supervised learning and knowledge distillation and shares only predictions on a public reference set. The server then aggregates and smooths these predictions, optionally combining them with a teacher model, to produce stable distillation targets for the next round. Extensive evaluation on six datasets against six representative FL baselines (FedAvg, Ditto, FedMD, FedDF, FedDistill, Local-KD) demonstrates that FedKDNAS consistently achieves superior Pareto efficiency, improving accuracy by up to 15\% under non-IID conditions, reducing client CPU usage by approximately 28\%, and decreasing communication overhead by up to 44 times while maintaining lightweight logit-based communication.

</details>


### [308] [Approximation Theory for Neural Networks: Old and New](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21451)
*Soumendu Sundar Mukherjee,Himasish Talukdar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Universal approximation theorems and quantitative bounds for neural networks; depth-width trade-offs; survey of single-hidden-layer density results and Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks (KANs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthesize and evaluate four decades of universality theory from qualitative density results to quantitative approximation rates, and to assess architectural effects (depth, width) and alternative paradigms like KANs.

Method: Expository survey: review classical density results for shallow nets, present quantitative bounds relating error to network size and target function smoothness, analyze depth–width trade-offs, discuss parameter efficiency in deep architectures, and survey Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks and their approximation properties.

Result: Highlights include that deep architectures can achieve superior parameter efficiency for structured function classes; many quantitative bounds relate approximation error to size and smoothness; KANs show promising approximation power and deserve further study.

Conclusion: The field has evolved into a rich quantitative framework where depth and architectural choices materially affect approximation power, with KANs offering an additional fruitful direction; the survey outlines current knowledge and suggests directions for future research.

Abstract: Universal approximation theorems provide a mathematical explanation for the expressive power of neural networks. They assert that, under mild conditions on the activation function, feedforward neural networks are dense in broad function classes, such as continuous functions on compact subsets of $\mathbb{R}^d$, $L^p$ spaces, or Sobolev spaces. Over the past four decades, these qualitative universality results have evolved into a rich quantitative theory addressing approximation rates, parameter efficiency, and the role of architectural features such as depth and width. This survey presents several glimpses into this theory. We review classical density results for single-hidden-layer networks, as well as quantitative bounds that relate approximation error to network size and smoothness assumptions on target functions. Particular emphasis is placed on depth--width trade-offs and on results demonstrating that deeper architectures can achieve superior parameter efficiency for structured function classes. In addition to standard feedforward neural networks, we also review recent developments on Kolmogorov--Arnold Networks (KANs), which offer an alternative architectural paradigm and whose approximation-theoretic properties have begun to attract significant theoretical attention.

</details>


### [309] [Fast and Stable Triangular Inversion for Delta-Rule Linear Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21325)
*Aleksandros Sobczyk,Gioele Gottardo,Christos K. Matzoros,Mirko De Vita,Filip Skogh,Anastasios Zouzias,Jiawei Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Systematic comparison of direct and iterative triangular inversion methods in Delta-Rule linear attention, focusing on numerical stability, computational complexity, and hardware efficiency; demonstrates practical gains on NPUs with up to 4.3x speed-up over SGLang while preserving end-to-end accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Triangular inversions in Delta-Rule linear attention are a bottleneck and highly sensitive to numerical errors, particularly in low-precision; there is a need for stable, hardware-friendly algorithms that balance speed and accuracy.

Method: Analyze a broad class of direct and iterative triangular inversion algorithms that leverage heavy matrix product structure; evaluate numerical stability, computational complexity, and hardware efficiency; perform rigorous experiments across precisions and compare against state-of-the-art SGLang implementations on NPUs.

Result: Experimental results validate the numerical stability and practical efficiency of the proposed approaches; achieved up to 4.3× speed-up on NPUs compared to SGLang for triangular inversion; end-to-end model accuracy is preserved; provides a detailed trade-off analysis and practical guidelines.

Conclusion: Matrix-product–friendly triangular inversion strategies can significantly improve hardware efficiency for linear attention without compromising accuracy; method selection should consider precision, hardware characteristics, and the desired balance between speed and numerical stability; findings support broader adoption in long-context transformer architectures.

Abstract: Linear attention has emerged as a cornerstone for efficient long-context architectures, as evidenced by its integration into state-of-the-art open-source models including Qwen3.5/3.6, Kimi Linear, and RWKV-7. Models that incorporate linear attention layers with the so-called Delta-Rule involve the inversion of triangular matrices as a core sub-routine. This operation often forms a performance bottleneck, and, due to its high-sensitivity to numerical errors, it can significantly deteriorate end-to-end model accuracy if it is not carefully implemented. This work provides a systematic analysis of both direct and iterative triangular inversion algorithms, targeting methods that are rich in matrix products, and, therefore, have the potential to efficiently utilize modern hardware. To that end, our analysis covers a broad spectrum of mathematical and practical aspects, with a heavy focus on numerical stability, computational complexity, and, ultimately, hardware efficiency and practical considerations. We provide a rigorous experimental evaluation to verify these properties in practical scenarios, and in low-precision floating-point representations, highlighting the strengths and limitations of each method. Performance benchmarks on NPUs reveal up to $4.3\times$ speed-up against the state-of-the-art implementations of SGLang for triangular matrix inversion, leading to significant performance improvements on the entire layer level, while maintaining full end-to-end model accuracy.

</details>


### [310] [Agent JIT Compilation for Latency-Optimizing Web Agent Planning and Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21470)
*Caleb Winston,Ron Yifeng Wang,Azalia Mirhoseini,Christos Kozyrakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation for computer-use agents (CUA) that converts task descriptions into executable code, enabling LLM calls, tool calls, and parallelization to reduce latency and tool-use errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current sequential fetch-screenshot-execute loops in CUAs incur high latency and error-prone tool usage; a compiled, parallelizable approach can improve speed and reliability.

Method: Three components: (1) JIT-Planner—generates multiple code plans, validates against tool specs, selects the minimum-cost candidate; (2) JIT-Scheduler—explores parallelization via Monte Carlo cost estimations using learned latency distributions; (3) invariant-enforcing tool protocol—pre/postconditions to reduce invalid tool usage.

Result: Evaluated on 5 web applications: JIT-Planner yields 10.4× speedup and +28% accuracy over Browser-Use; JIT-Scheduler yields 2.4× speedup and +9% accuracy over OpenAI CUA.

Conclusion: The JIT approach substantially improves the speed and reliability of computer-use agents, with demonstrated gains across multiple apps and potential for broader applicability.

Abstract: Computer-use agents (CUA) automate tasks specified with natural language such as "order the cheapest item from Taco Bell" by generating sequences of calls to tools such as click, type, and scroll on a browser. Current implementations follow a sequential fetch-screenshot-execute loop where each iteration requires an LLM call, resulting in high latency and frequent errors from incorrect tool use. We present agent just-in-time (JIT) compilation, an alternative that compiles task descriptions directly into executable code that is free to include LLM calls, tool calls, and parallelization. Our approach comprises three components: (1) JIT-Planner, which generates multiple code plans, validates each against tool specifications, and selects the minimum-cost candidate; (2) JIT-Scheduler, which explores parallelization strategies via Monte Carlo cost estimation from learned latency distributions; and (3) an invariant-enforcing tool protocol specifying precondition and postcondition state requirements that reduce the rate of generating plans with incorrect tool use. Across 5 web applications, JIT-Planner achieves $10.4\times$ speedup and $+28\%$ accuracy over Browser-Use, while JIT-Scheduler achieves $2.4\times$ speedup and $+9\%$ accuracy over OpenAI CUA.

</details>


### [311] [Classification of Single and Mixed Partial Discharges under Switching Voltage Using an AWA-CNN Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21352)
*Md Rafid Kaysar Shagor,Zannatul Ferdousy Mouri,Farhina Haque,Anindya Bijoy Das*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AWA (Amplitude-Width-Area) patterns map PD pulses under switching excitation into a visual representation where amplitude vs. area define axes and width is encoded by color, enabling multi-class PD source classification with CNNs achieving >96% accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Partial discharge analysis under switching-voltage excitation is more challenging than under sinusoidal excitation due to activity concentrated at voltage transitions; a compact, discriminative representation is needed to distinguish PD sources.

Method: Characterize time-domain PD pulses by amplitude, width, and area; construct AWA patterns with amplitude vs. area as coordinates and pulse width encoded by color; evaluate classification using Random Forest as baseline and CNNs (InceptionV3, ResNet-18).

Result: AWA patterns reveal source-dependent distributions; CNN classifiers achieve testing accuracy >96%, while Random Forest achieves 73.33% on the task of distinguishing six single and mixed PD sources (corona, internal, surface, and their combinations).

Conclusion: AWA patterns provide a visual, discriminative representation of PD pulses under switching excitation, enabling effective multi-class PD source classification.

Abstract: The growing use of fast-switching power electronics has made partial discharge (PD) analysis under switching-voltage excitation increasingly important, yet more challenging than under sinusoidal conditions due to activity concentrated at voltage transitions. This work presents an Amplitude-Width-Area (AWA) pattern representation for source-oriented PD analysis under switching-voltage excitation. In the proposed method, time domain PD pulses are characterized using pulse amplitude, width, and area, and mapped into a visual pattern where amplitude and area define the coordinate axes and width is encoded by color. The generated AWA patterns are used to distinguish six single and mixed PD source conditions: corona, internal, surface, corona+internal, corona+surface, and internal+surface. To evaluate the classification capability of the proposed representation, a Random Forest baseline and two Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models, InceptionV3 and ResNet-18, are compared. The AWA patterns show distinguishable source-dependent distributions, and CNN-based classification achieves testing accuracy above 96%, compared with 73.33% for Random Forest. The results indicate that AWA patterns provide a visual representation of PD pulses suitable for multi-class PD source classification under switching-voltage excitation.

</details>


### [312] [Quantifying Hyperparameter Transfer and the Importance of Embedding Layer Learning Rate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21486)
*Dayal Singh Kalra,Maissam Barkeshli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper frames hyperparameter transfer as a three-metric problem and shows that Maximal Update Parametrization (μP) improves transfer over standard parameterization (SP) largely due to embedding-layer learning-rate scaling; steps include ablations with AdamW, width, and weight decay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable reliable extrapolation of optimal optimization hyperparameters from small-scale to large-scale LLM training by quantifying transfer and explaining μP's practical benefits.

Method: Introduce three metrics: (1) scaling-law fit quality, (2) extrapolation robustness, (3) asymptotic loss penalty from parameterization. Conduct comprehensive ablations comparing μP vs SP under AdamW, analyze embedding-layer learning rate as a bottleneck, vary width, and evaluate weight decay, including a fixed token-per-parameter setting.

Result: μP yields higher-quality hyperparameter transfer; the main benefit over SP with AdamW comes from maximizing the embedding-layer LR. In SP, embedding LR bottlenecks training; increasing it by a factor equal to width to match μP smooths training and improves transfer. Weight decay improves scaling-law fits but reduces extrapolation robustness in fixed-token-per-parameter settings.

Conclusion: Parameterization choice critically shapes hyperparameter transfer; the practical advantage of μP arises largely from favorable embedding-layer LR scaling, suggesting practitioners should inspect layer-wise LR distributions and consider width-matched LR scaling. Theoretical explanations are still lacking, and the mixed effects of weight decay warrant careful regime-aware tuning.

Abstract: Hyperparameter transfer allows extrapolating optimal optimization hyperparameters from small to large scales, making it critical for training large language models (LLMs). This is done either by fitting a scaling law to the hyperparameters or by a judicious choice of parameterization, such as Maximal Update ($μ$P), that renders optimal hyperparameters approximately scale invariant. In this paper, we first develop a framework to quantify hyperparameter transfer through three metrics: (1) the quality of the scaling law fit, (2) the robustness to extrapolation errors, and (3) the asymptotic loss penalty due to choice of parameterization. Next, we investigate through a comprehensive series of ablations why $μ$P appears to offer high-quality learning rate transfer relative to standard parameterization (SP), as existing theory is inadequate. We find that the overwhelming benefit of $μ$P relative to SP when training with AdamW arises simply from maximizing the learning rate of the embedding layer. In SP, the embedding layer learning rate acts as a bottleneck that induces training instabilities; increasing it by a factor of width to match $μ$P dramatically smooths out training while improving hyperparameter transfer. We also find that weight decay improves the scaling law fits, while, in the fixed token-per-parameter setting, it hurts the robustness of the extrapolation.

</details>


### [313] [Variance Reduction for Expectations with Diffusion Teachers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21489)
*Jesse Bettencourt,Xindi Wu,Matan Atzmon,James Lucas,Jonathan Lorraine*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CARV introduces a compute-aware variance-accounting framework for diffusion-model teacher gradients, using a hierarchical Monte Carlo estimator with amortized upstream computation, timestep importance sampling, and stratified inverse-CDF to reduce variance and increase compute efficiency; yields 2-3x effective compute in text-to-3D distillation and attribution, with variance reductions notable in single-step distillation but not improving FID, indicating variance is not the bottleneck there.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Teacher gradients for downstream diffusion-based pipelines are MC expectations over noise levels and Gaussian samples; the dominant cost is expensive upstream computation (rendering, simulation, encoding). A compute-aware variance estimator can amortize this cost and reduce overall compute.

Method: Hierarchical Monte Carlo estimator that amortizes expensive upstream computation over cheap diffusion-noise resamples; employs timestep importance sampling; uses stratified-inverse-CDF construction to sharpen estimates.

Result: In text-to-3D distillation and attribution tasks, CARV achieves 2-3x effective compute multipliers (primarily from amortized reuse; ~25% additional from importance sampling and stratification). In single-step distillation, gradient variance is reduced by about an order of magnitude, but downstream FID does not improve, indicating MC variance is not the bottleneck in that regime.

Conclusion: CARV provides compute gains where MC variance and upstream costs dominate; when variance is not the bottleneck (e.g., single-step distillation with unchanged FID), gains are limited, highlighting regime-dependent applicability.

Abstract: Pretrained diffusion models serve as frozen teachers feeding downstream pipelines such as text-to-3D, single-step distillation, and data attribution. The teacher gradients these pipelines consume are Monte Carlo (MC) expectations over noise levels and Gaussian noise samples; their estimator variance dominates compute cost because each draw requires expensive upstream work (rendering, simulation, encoding). We introduce CARV, a compute-aware variance-accounting framework that motivates a hierarchical MC estimator: amortize the expensive upstream computation over cheap diffusion-noise resamples, sharpened by timestep importance sampling and a stratified-inverse-CDF construction. In our text-to-3D distillation and attribution experiments, CARV delivers 2-3x effective compute multipliers (most from amortized reuse; ~25% additional from IS+stratification) without changing the objective; in single-step distillation, the same techniques cut gradient variance by an order of magnitude but do not improve downstream FID, marking the regime where MC variance is no longer the bottleneck.

</details>


### [314] [Adaptive Signal Resuscitation: Channel-wise Post-Pruning Repair for Sparse Vision Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21426)
*Qishi Zhan,Ziheng Chen,Minxuan Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ASR is a training-free, channel-wise, variance-based repair for post-pruning that matches repair granularity to damage, improving high-sparsity CNN accuracy and outperforming layer-wise repair and BN recalibration across multiple datasets and architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: One-shot magnitude pruning causes severe accuracy collapse at high sparsity due to granularity mismatch: repairing an entire layer with a single correction can over-amplify damaged channels. Layer-wise repairs cannot adapt to channel-level disparities in post-pruning signal.

Method: Adaptive Signal Resuscitation (ASR) estimates a variance-matching correction for each output channel and stabilizes it with a data-driven shrinkage rule. It is training-free, applied before BatchNorm recalibration, and requires only forward passes on a small calibration set with no retraining. Works for both unstructured and structured sparsity.

Result: ASR generally improves over layer-wise repair across three datasets, four CNN architectures, and both sparsity settings. On ResNet-50 at 90% sparsity, ASR achieves 55.6% top-1 on CIFAR-10 versus 41.0% (layer-wise) and 28.0% (BN-only). Ablations show naive channel-wise variance matching is insufficient; shrinkage is essential for stability.

Conclusion: ASR effectively matches repair granularity to damage, enabling data-efficient, robust post-pruning repair across diverse architectures and sparsity patterns, with pronounced gains in high-sparsity regimes.

Abstract: One-shot magnitude pruning can cause severe accuracy collapse in the high-sparsity regime, even when the pruning mask preserves the largest weights. We argue that this failure reflects a granularity mismatch in post-pruning repair. Under global magnitude pruning, nearly collapsed channels can coexist with channels that retain informative activation variance within the same layer. Existing layer-wise activation repair methods apply a single correction to the whole layer, and can therefore over-amplify damaged channels while trying to restore the layer-level signal. We propose Adaptive Signal Resuscitation (ASR), a training-free channel-wise repair method that matches the granularity of repair to the granularity of damage. ASR estimates a variance-matching correction for each output channel and stabilizes it with a data-driven shrinkage rule, suppressing unreliable corrections for channels with weak post-pruning signal while preserving corrections for healthier channels. Applied before BatchNorm recalibration, ASR requires only forward passes on a small calibration set and no retraining. Across three datasets, four convolutional architectures, and both unstructured and structured sparsity settings, ASR generally improves over layer-wise repair, with the clearest gains in high-sparsity regimes. On ResNet-50 at 90% sparsity, ASR recovers 55.6% top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-10, compared with 41.0% for layer-wise repair and 28.0% for BatchNorm-only recalibration. Ablations show that naive channel-wise variance matching is insufficient, and that shrinkage stabilizes post-pruning repair.

</details>


### [315] [Polynomial-Time Robust Multiclass Linear Classification under Gaussian Marginals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21428)
*Ilias Diakonikolas,Giannis Iakovidis,Mingchen Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces efficient, dimension-free agnostic learning methods for multiclass linear classifiers under Gaussian marginals, overcoming obstructions in the multiclass perceptron and achieving polynomial-time guarantees with favorable error scaling in k and opt.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in algorithmic theory for agnostic multiclass linear classification with k≥3 under Gaussian inputs, seeking robust, sample-efficient, dimension-independent learning with provable error guarantees.

Method: Establish structural obstructions for the multiclass perceptron under Gaussian marginals; introduce a pairwise improper-learning framework that reduces multiclass problems to pairwise tasks; develop a localization-based framework; analyze geometrically regular k-class arrangements; derive error bounds and polynomial-time algorithms.

Result: Proves that the standard multiclass perceptron needs super-polynomial samples/updates; provides efficient learning algorithms with: (i) general-k bound: ~k^{3/2} sqrt(opt) + ε; (ii) k=3: O(opt) + ε; (iii) geometrically regular k-class classifiers: poly(k)·opt + ε; all with dimension-independent error guarantees in the stated regimes.

Conclusion: Advances the theory of agnostic multiclass learning under Gaussian marginals by providing scalable, provably robust learners; highlights that pairwise/improper and localization strategies circumvent previous obstructions; invites further study of distributions beyond Gaussian and tighter bounds.

Abstract: We study the task of agnostic learning of multiclass linear classifiers under the Gaussian distribution. Given labeled examples $(x, y)$ from a distribution over $\mathbb{R}^d \times [k]$, with Gaussian $x$-marginal, the goal is to output a hypothesis whose error is comparable to that of the best $k$-class linear classifier. While the binary case $k=2$ has a well-developed algorithmic theory, much less is known for $k \ge 3$. Even for $k=3$, prior robust algorithms incur exponential dependence on the inverse of the desired accuracy in both complexity and representation size. In this work, we develop new structural results for multiclass linear classifiers and use them to design fully polynomial-time robust learners with dimension-independent error guarantees. Our first result shows that the standard multiclass perceptron algorithm requires super-polynomially many samples and updates, even with clean labels and Gaussian marginals, revealing a basic obstruction absent in the binary case. Our main positive result is a pairwise improper-learning framework which yields an efficient learner with error $\widetilde O(k^{3/2}\sqrt{\mathrm{opt}})+ε$ for general $k$. Additionally, we develop a sharper localization-based framework which leads to error $O(\mathrm{opt})+ε$ for $k=3$, and error $\mathrm{poly}(k)\mathrm{opt}+ε$ for geometrically regular $k$-class linear classifiers.

</details>


### [316] [Gaussian Sheaf Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21435)
*André Ribeiro,Ana Luiza Tenório,Tiago da Silva,Diego Mesquita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GSNNs encode Gaussian node features on graphs via a cellular-sheaf Laplacian, preserving mean/covariance structure instead of naive vectorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard GNNs operate on vector-valued node features; when features are Gaussians (mean and covariance), simple concatenation loses geometric/algebraic structure. A sheaf-theoretic framework can preserve this structure and enable principled learning via a generalized Laplacian.

Method: Adapt cellular sheaves to Gaussian features and derive a new Laplacian that generalizes the sheaf Laplacian for Gaussian-distributed node attributes; integrate this into a neural framework (GSNNs) to perform message passing with inductive biases.

Result: The work presents a theoretical derivation of a generalized, structure-preserving Laplacian for Gaussian features and provides empirical validation on synthetic and real-world datasets, demonstrating the practical relevance of GSNNs.

Conclusion: GSNNs offer a principled, structure-preserving framework for graph learning with Gaussian node features, leveraging a generalized Laplacian to incorporate the geometry of means and covariances in relational data.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have become the de facto standard for learning on relational data. While traditional GNNs' message passing is well suited for vector-valued node features, there are cases in which node features are better represented by probability distributions than real vectors. Concretely, when node features are Gaussians, characterized by a mean and a covariance matrix, naively concatenating their parameters into a single vector and applying standard message passing discards the geometric and algebraic structure that governs means and covariances. We propose Gaussian Sheaf Neural Networks (GSNNs), a principled framework that incorporates these inductive biases into graph-based learning. Building on the theory of cellular sheaves, we derive a new Laplacian operator that generalizes the sheaf Laplacian to this setting and preserves its key properties. We complement our theoretical contributions with experiments on synthetic and real-world data that illustrate the practical relevance of GSNNs.

</details>


### [317] [Mitigating Label Bias with Interpretable Rubric Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21455)
*Calvin Isley,Johann D. Gaebler,Sharad Goel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Rubric embeddings offer an interpretable alternative to black-box embeddings for decision models trained on biased supervisory labels, reducing group disparities and improving cohort quality in admissions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground-truth labels are hard to obtain and historical evaluations reflect social biases; interpretable, domain-grounded representations can guard against biased proxy signals.

Method: Introduce rubric embeddings: features derived from expert-defined criteria aligned with the target construct; anchor predictions to semantically meaningful dimensions; provide theoretical guarantees under plausible conditions; evaluate on a dataset of master’s program applications.

Result: Models trained with rubric embeddings reduce group disparities and improve measures of cohort quality compared to baselines that rely on standard embeddings.

Conclusion: Domain-grounded, interpretable representations provide a practical approach to learning with biased labels, mitigating proxy biases while maintaining or improving performance.

Abstract: Statistical decision algorithms are increasingly deployed in domains where ground-truth labels are hard to obtain, such as hiring, university admissions, and content moderation. In these settings, models are typically trained on historical human evaluations -- for example, using past hiring decisions as a proxy for true applicant quality. However, if past evaluations unjustly favor certain groups, models trained on these labels may inherit those biases. To address this problem, we propose basing predictions on rubric embeddings, a representation framework that replaces standard black-box embeddings with features derived from expert-defined criteria that align with the underlying construct of interest. By anchoring predictions to semantically meaningful dimensions, this approach guards against biased proxy signals. We provide both theoretical and empirical evidence that rubric embeddings mitigate label bias under plausible conditions. Empirically, we evaluate our method on a novel dataset of applications to a large master's program. We find that models trained on rubric embeddings reduce group disparities while improving measures of cohort quality. Our results suggest that basing predictions on interpretable, domain-grounded representations offers a practical approach to learning in the presence of biased labels.

</details>


### [318] [A Machine Learning Framework for Weighted Least Squares GNSS Positioning based on Activation Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21461)
*Pin-Hsun Lee,Harry Leib*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML-enhanced WLS for GNSS positioning using activation-weighted quality scores improves accuracy in urban environments and shows regional transferability; sigmoid activation yields best results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban canyons cause GNSS signal obstruction, NLOS, and multipath, degrading pseudorange measurements. While multi-constellation GNSS increases observations, degraded signals can worsen positioning. A robust weighting scheme leveraging ML-predicted signal quality can mitigate errors.

Method: Train ensemble ML models on signal quality indicators to predict signal quality scores. Use activation functions to transform scores into WLS weights. Evaluate on real-world HK and Tokyo datasets across single- and multi-constellation setups, comparing activation functions.

Result: Sigmoid activation consistently yields the greatest improvement across ML models and constellation configurations. The algorithm substantially reduces positioning errors in both single- and multi-constellation scenarios and shows strong geographic transferability, performing comparably when trained on data from similarly urbanized regions.

Conclusion: The ML-activated weighting framework enhances GNSS PVT accuracy in urban canyons and is robust across regions and constellations, with sigmoid-based weighting offering the best performance.

Abstract: Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) are widely used to provide position, velocity, and timing (PVT) information for various applications, including transportation, location-based communication services, and intelligent agriculture. In urban canyons, high-rise buildings and narrow streets can cause signal obstruction, non-line-of-sight (NLOS) reception, and multipath effects that introduce errors in GNSS pseudorange measurements. Although multi-constellations GNSS effectively increase the number of available satellites, the inclusion of degraded signals can lead to severe positioning errors. This study proposes a machine learning framework for the weighted least squares (WLS) algorithm incorporating activation functions to enhance positioning accuracy. Several signal quality indicators are employed as training features for ensemble learning algorithms to identify poor quality signals by providing quality scores. Then, activation functions are employed to transform the machine learning predicted scores to appropriate weights for WLS positioning. To evaluate the performance of our approach, experiments are conducted using real-world datasets from Hong Kong and Tokyo urban areas. Comparative analysis of activation functions reveals that sigmoid functions consistently yield the greatest improvements with different machine learning algorithms and GNSS constellation configurations. The proposed algorithm demonstrates substantial reductions in positioning errors for both single- and multiconstellation scenarios. Furthermore, our results indicate that the proposed algorithm exhibits strong geographical transferability. The proposed algorithm maintains comparable level of performance when trained on data from other regions with similar levels of urbanization.

</details>


### [319] [DelTA: Discriminative Token Credit Assignment for Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21467)
*Kaiyi Zhang,Wei Wu,Yankai Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DelTA is a discriminative token credit assignment method for RLVR that reweights token-gradient directions to create more contrastive, side-specific centroids, improving policy updates; shows gains on math benchmarks and generalizes to code and other backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how response-level RLVR rewards translate to token-level probability updates and address dilution of discriminative directions by shared high-frequency patterns in token gradients; improve credit assignment to high-signal tokens.

Method: Adopt a discriminator view of RLVR where policy-gradient updates act as a linear discriminator over token-gradient vectors. Construct positive/negative centroids from advantage-weighted averaging. Identify that centroid estimation can be dominated by shared patterns. Propose DelTA to estimate per-token coefficients that amplify side-specific gradient directions and downweight common ones, reweighting a self-normalized RLVR surrogate to make centroids more contrastive and thus alter the update direction.

Result: DelTA outperforms strongest same-scale baselines on seven mathematical benchmarks, achieving an average gain of 3.26 points on Qwen3-8B-Base and 2.62 points on Qwen3-14B-Base. Additional experiments on code generation, a different backbone, and out-of-domain data indicate strong generalization.

Conclusion: DelTA provides a more discriminative token credit assignment for RLVR updates, yielding more effective update directions and better performance across tasks and models; demonstrates robustness across domains.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a central technique for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models. Despite its effectiveness, how response-level rewards translate into token-level probability changes remains poorly understood. We introduce a discriminator view of RLVR updates, showing that the policy-gradient update direction implicitly acts as a linear discriminator over token-gradient vectors and thereby determines which token probabilities are increased or decreased during learning. Under standard sequence-level RLVR, this discriminator is constructed from positive- and negative-side centroids formed by advantage-weighted averaging of token-gradient vectors. However, such centroid construction can be dominated by shared high-frequency patterns, such as formatting tokens, diluting sparse yet discriminative directions that better distinguish high-reward responses from low-reward ones. To address this limitation, we propose $\textbf{DelTA}$, a discriminative token credit assignment method that estimates token coefficients to amplify side-specific token-gradient directions and downweight shared or weakly discriminative ones. These coefficients reweight a self-normalized RLVR surrogate, making the effective side-wise centroids more contrastive and thereby reshaping the RLVR update direction. On seven mathematical benchmarks, DelTA outperforms the strongest same-scale baselines by 3.26 and 2.62 average points on Qwen3-8B-Base and Qwen3-14B-Base, respectively. Additional results on code generation, a different backbone, and out-of-domain evaluations further demonstrate the generalization ability of DelTA.

</details>


### [320] [You Only Need Minimal RLVR Training: Extrapolating LLMs via Rank-1 Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21468)
*Zhepei Wei,Xinyu Zhu,Wei-Lin Chen,Chengsong Huang,Jiaxin Huang,Yu Meng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RELEX extrapolates RL-based training updates using a rank-1 subspace, enabling accurate future checkpoints with far fewer training steps and without a learned model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate the geometry of RLVR weight trajectories and exploit low-rank, predictable dynamics to reduce compute by extrapolating future checkpoints.

Method: Estimate a rank-1 subspace from early parameter deltas, project subsequent updates onto it, and perform linear regression to predict future checkpoints; no training involved; evaluate on three Qwen models across in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks; compare to full RLVR and ablations.

Result: Most performance gains captured by rank-1 projection; projection magnitude grows nearly linearly with steps; RELEX matches or exceeds RLVR with only ~15% of training steps; can extrapolate 10–20x beyond observed window; ablations show higher rank or nonlinear models provide no extra benefit; denoising effect reduces stochastic optimization noise; code released.

Conclusion: A simple, compute-efficient extrapolation approach reveals a low-rank structure in RLVR updates; by extrapolating along a dominant direction, one can achieve RLVR-like performance with substantially less cost, implying broader applicability of low-rank dynamics in LLM fine-tuning.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a dominant paradigm for improving reasoning in large language models (LLMs), yet the underlying geometry of the resulting parameter trajectories remains underexplored. In this work, we demonstrate that RLVR weight trajectories are extremely low-rank and highly predictable. Specifically, we find that the majority of downstream performance gains are captured by a rank-1 approximation of the parameter deltas, where the magnitude of this projection evolves near-linearly with training steps. Motivated by this, we propose a simple and compute-efficient method RELEX (REinforcement Learning EXtrapolation), which estimates the rank-1 subspace from a short observation window and extrapolates future checkpoints via linear regression, with no learned model required. Across three models (i.e., Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B, Qwen3-4B-Base, and Qwen3-8B-Base), RELEX produces checkpoints that match or exceed RLVR performance on both in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks, requiring as few as 15% steps of full RLVR training. Remarkably, RELEX is able to extrapolate far beyond the observation window at no training cost, predicting checkpoints up to 10-20$\times$ beyond the observed prefix with continued improvement (e.g., observe only the first 50 steps and extrapolate to 1000 steps). Our ablation analysis confirms the minimalist sufficiency of RELEX: neither increasing the subspace rank nor employing non-linear modeling yields further gains in extrapolation. Finally, we show that RELEX's success stems from a "denoising" effect: by projecting updates onto the rank-1 subspace, the model discards stochastic optimization noise that would otherwise degrade performance during extrapolation. Our code is available at https://github.com/weizhepei/RELEX.

</details>


### [321] [Is Fixing Schema Graphs Necessary? Full-Resolution Graph Structure Learning for Relational Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21475)
*Yi Huang,Qingyun Sun,Jia Li,Xingcheng Fu,Jianxin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FROG: a learnable full-resolution graph structure learning framework for relational deep learning on relational databases, enabling tables to act as nodes/edges and jointly optimize structure and GNN representations with role-driven message passing and functional dependency regularization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Relational prediction over RDBs often relies on fixed, hand-crafted graph constructions that may misrepresent relational semantics. There is a need to learn both the graph structure and the neural representations to better capture inter-table and intra-table relations.

Method: Formulate relational structure learning as a learnable table-role modeling problem where tables contribute as nodes and edges in message passing. Implement role-driven message passing to capture relational semantics and jointly optimize graph structure and GNN representations. Introduce functional dependency constraints to regularize representations across table and entity levels for semantic consistency.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows FROG outperforms existing approaches on relational prediction tasks. The study also analyzes how learned table roles influence downstream tasks, providing insights into effective graph construction for RDL.

Conclusion: Jointly learning full-resolution graph structures with role-aware messaging and FD-based regularization improves relational deep learning on RDBs and offers interpretability into how table roles shape predictions.

Abstract: Relational prediction tasks are fundamental in many real-world applications, where data are naturally stored in relational databases (RDBs). Relational Deep Learning (RDL) addresses this problem by modeling RDBs as graphs and applying graph neural networks (GNNs) for end-to-end learning. However, the full-resolution property is commonly adopted as a design principle in graph construction for RDBs to preserve relational semantics, which leads most existing methods to rely on fixed graph structures. In this paper, we propose FROG, a Full-Resolution and Optimizable Graph Structure Learning} framework for RDL that formulates relational structure learning as a learnable table role modeling problem, allowing tables to contribute as nodes and edges in message passing. We further design role-driven message passing mechanisms to capture relational semantics, enabling joint optimization of graph structure and GNN representations. To ensure semantic consistency, we introduce functional dependency constraints that regularize representations across table and entity levels. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches and reveal how table roles impact downstream tasks, offering new insights into graph construction for RDL

</details>


### [322] [EvoStruct: Bridging Evolutionary and Structural Priors for Antibody CDR Design via Protein Language Model Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21485)
*Mansoor Ahmed,Sujin Lee,Umar Khayaz,Murray Patterson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EvoStruct integrates a frozen protein language model (PLM) with 3D structural context via an E(3)-equivariant GNN cross-attention adapter to solve vocabulary collapse in antibody CDR design, achieving state-of-the-art amino acid recovery, lower perplexity, and higher diversity and binding-pair correlation on CHIMERA-Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Equivariant GNNs for antibody CDR design suffer from vocabulary collapse due to learning amino acid distributions from limited structural data and ignoring evolutionary substitution patterns. The aim is to preserve this evolutionary information by coupling a PLM with structural context.

Method: EvoStruct uses a frozen PLM augmented by a cross-attention adapter that connects to an E(3)-equivariant GNN. It employs progressive PLM unfreezing and R-Drop consistency regularization to specifically address vocabulary collapse in CDR design.

Result: On CHIMERA-Bench, EvoStruct achieves the highest amino acid recovery and lowest perplexity among antibody design methods, improving sequence recovery by 16% and reducing perplexity by 43% versus best GNN baselines, while attaining 2.3x greater amino acid diversity and the highest binding-pair correlation with ground truth.

Conclusion: EvoStruct effectively mitigates vocabulary collapse by fusing evolutionary priors from PLMs with 3D structural reasoning, delivering state-of-the-art antibody CDR design performance on CHIMERA-Bench.

Abstract: Equivariant graph neural network (GNN) methods for antibody complementarity-determining region (CDR) design achieve the highest sequence recovery but suffer from severe vocabulary collapse. The current best GNN methods over-predict very few amino acids, such as tyrosine and glycine, while ignoring functionally important residues. We trace this failure to GNN encoders learning amino acid distributions de novo from limited structural data, discarding substitution patterns encoded in evolutionary databases. To resolve this, we propose EvoStruct, which bridges a frozen protein language model (PLM) with 3D structural context from an E(3)-equivariant GNN via a cross-attention adapter. Unlike prior PLM-structure adapters for general protein design, EvoStruct targets the vocabulary collapse problem specific to CDR design through progressive PLM unfreezing and R-Drop consistency regularization. On the CHIMERA-Bench dataset, EvoStruct achieves the highest amino acid recovery and lowest perplexity among several antibody design methods, improving sequence recovery by 16% and reducing perplexity by 43% relative to the best GNN baselines, while recovering 2.3x greater amino acid diversity and the highest binding-pair correlation with ground truth.

</details>


### [323] [Equilibrium Reasoners: Learning Attractors Enables Scalable Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21488)
*Benhao Huang,Zhengyang Geng,Zico Kolter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EqR introduces task-conditioned latent attractors to enable scalable, iterative reasoning. By increasing depth (iterations) and breadth (multiple initializations), the model converges to solution-aligned attractors, enabling large test-time compute to dramatically boost accuracy (e.g., Sudoku-Extreme).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how iterative latent models generalize beyond memorized patterns and why test-time compute scaling helps; provide a mechanistic explanation beyond empirical gains.

Method: Formalize Equilibrium Reasoners (EqR) that scale along two axes: depth (more iterations) and breadth (aggregating stochastic trajectories from multiple initializations). Train to shape latent dynamical systems toward stable, solution-aligned attractors without external verifiers or task-specific priors. Unroll up to very deep architectures (up to ~40k layers) and observe convergence toward attractors. Evaluate on tasks like Sudoku-Extreme.

Result: Test-time scaling yields strong gains, with harder cases benefiting from large scale; accuracy on Sudoku-Extreme rises from 2.6% (baseline feedforward) to over 99%. Gains correlate with convergence toward attractor landscapes.

Conclusion: Learning attractor landscapes provides a plausible mechanistic lens for scalable reasoning in iterative latent models and enables adaptive allocation of test-time compute based on task difficulty.

Abstract: Scaling test-time compute by iteratively updating a latent state has emerged as a powerful paradigm for reasoning. Yet the internal mechanisms that enable these iterative models to generalize beyond memorized patterns remain unclear. We hypothesize that generalizable reasoning arises from learning task-conditioned attractors: latent dynamical systems whose stable fixed points correspond to valid solutions.
  We formalize this process through Equilibrium Reasoners (EqR), which enable test-time scaling without external verifiers or task-specific priors. EqR scales internal dynamics along two axes: depth, by running more iterations, and breadth, by aggregating stochastic trajectories from multiple initializations. Empirically, gains from test-time scaling are tightly coupled with stronger convergence toward solution-aligned attractors.
  This attractor perspective allows neural networks to adaptively allocate test-time compute based on task difficulty. While simple cases converge within 1 to 5 iteration steps, harder cases benefit from massive test-time scaling. By unrolling up to the equivalent of 40,000 layers, scalable latent reasoning boosts accuracy from 2.6% for feedforward models to over 99% on Sudoku-Extreme. These results suggest that learned attractor landscapes provide a useful mechanistic lens for understanding scalable reasoning in iterative latent models.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [324] [Adaptive Human-Robot Collaboration for Masonry Construction Under Material and Assembly Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20264)
*Jutang Gao,Arash Adel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An adaptive masonry workflow using an end-effector projector for just-in-time, spatial guidance and laser-scanning feedback for pose correction to counter tolerance accumulation in human-robot collaboration; validated with brickwork case studies showing improved adhesive consistency, reduced application time, and maintained level courses with fewer collision-prone failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Construction robotics face limited robot–human communication and tolerance accumulation due to material and assembly uncertainties. The work proposes an adaptive, perception-enabled collaboration to maintain precision and robustness in masonry tasks.

Method: Two complementary mechanisms: (1) an end-effector-mounted projector delivering spatially registered, just-in-time guidance for manual adhesive application; (2) laser scanning for feedback-driven grasping and placement pose correction. Evaluated through full-scale bricklaying experiments across conventional running-bond and nonstandard configurations to assess impact on adhesion accuracy, placement time, and structural alignment.

Result: Projection guidance improved adhesive application consistency and reduced application time; laser-based correction maintained level courses and reduced collision-prone failures associated with open-loop execution. The integration of projection with feedback-driven adaptation, supported by as-built sensing, mitigates tolerance accumulation and enhances precision and robustness in human-robot collaborative construction.

Conclusion: Integrating spatial projection with feedback-driven adaptation enabled by material and as-built sensing can mitigate tolerance accumulation and improve precision and robustness in human-robot collaborative construction.

Abstract: Human-robot collaboration in construction is often challenged by limited robot-to-human communication and the need to adapt to tolerance accumulation arising from material and assembly uncertainties. We present an adaptive human-robot collaborative workflow for masonry construction that addresses communication limitations and tolerance accumulation, demonstrated through a brickwork case study in which a robot places bricks while a human applies adhesive. This workflow is enabled by two complementary mechanisms: 1) an end-effector-mounted projector that provides spatially registered, just-in-time projection guidance for manual adhesive application, and 2) laser scanning for feedback-driven grasping and placement pose correction. Together, these mechanisms enable adjustment of human and robotic actions in response to material variability and accumulated assembly tolerances. Full-scale experiments across conventional running-bond and nonstandard configurations demonstrate that projection guidance improves adhesive application consistency and reduces application time, while laser-based correction maintains level courses and avoids collision-prone failures associated with open-loop execution. These results indicate that integrating spatial projection with feedback-driven adaptation, enabled by material and as-built sensing, can mitigate tolerance accumulation and improve precision and robustness in human-robot collaborative construction.

</details>


### [325] [Terrestrial Soft Mobile Robots: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20304)
*Dimuthu D. K. Arachchige*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Comprehensive literature review on soft wheelless mobile robots, surveying locomotion strategies, actuation methods, modeling approaches, and control systems, plus a gap analysis and adoption roadmap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthesize and organize the rapidly evolving field of soft mobile robotics to provide researchers and practitioners with a consolidated reference, emphasizing wheelless terrestrial locomotion and its broad application potential.

Method: Systematic literature review and synthesis of existing work on soft wheelless terrestrial locomotion, including taxonomy and analysis of locomotion strategies, actuation methods, modeling approaches, and control systems; identification of open challenges and research gaps.

Result: A unified overview of the current state, including a taxonomy of approaches, key findings across locomotion strategies, actuators, models, and controllers, and a set of open challenges and directions that guide future research and practical deployment.

Conclusion: Soft wheelless mobile robots hold strong promise for diverse applications, but realizing widespread deployment requires addressing actuation, modeling, control, and reliability challenges; the article provides a valuable resource and roadmap for researchers and practitioners.

Abstract: Soft mobile robots have emerged as a promising area of research with potential applications in various disciplines including but not limited to search-and-rescue, service, surveillance, explorations, and manufacturing. In this article, we provide a comprehensive review of the current state of soft mobile robot research, focusing on wheelless terrestrial locomotive systems. We include past and present developments in locomotion strategies, actuation methods, modeling approaches, and control systems. Further, we identify key research challenges that must be overcome to enable the widespread adoption of soft mobile robots in various applications. Overall, this article provides a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners interested in the field of soft mobile robots and soft robotics.

</details>


### [326] [Proximal State Nudging: Reducing Skill Atrophy from AI Assistance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20355)
*Megha Srivastava,Jonathan Ouyang,Eric Zhou,Andrew Silva,Emily Sumner,Dorsa Sadigh,Yuchen Cui,Deepak Gopinath,Guy Rosman*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PSN is a learning-aware shared autonomy method that nudges users toward learnable states to optimize both skill development and task performance; in LunarLander with simulated learners, PSN outperforms baselines, and in CARLA with 60 participants across two tasks, PSN yields up to 7x unassisted-skill gains and 50% fewer collisions than self-practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Skill atrophy under AI-assisted shared control poses safety risks, as operators may not distinguish human inputs from autonomous corrections; there is a need to preserve and develop operator skills while leveraging AI performance.

Method: Proximal State Nudging (PSN) jointly optimizes learning and performance by nudging the user's state toward regions estimated to be most learnable, enabling learning-compatible shared autonomy. Evaluations include simulated LunarLander with virtual students and two CARLA driving tasks (High Performance Racing, Parallel Parking) with human subjects (n=60).

Result: PSN outperforms existing shared autonomy baselines in balancing unassisted skill improvement with overall shared performance in LunarLander; in CARLA, PSN achieves up to 7x larger gains in unassisted skill than standard blended autonomy and reduces collisions by 50% compared with unassisted self-practice.

Conclusion: PSN demonstrates promise for learning-aware shared autonomy, offering safety and learning benefits; represents one of the first human-subject evaluations of a planning framework that supports learning-compatible shared autonomy.

Abstract: Skill atrophy, the gradual decline of human capability under AI assistance, poses a safety risk in shared-control of semi-autonomous systems, where operators may be unable to distinguish their own inputs from autonomous corrections. We propose Proximal State Nudging (PSN), a shared autonomy algorithm that jointly optimizes for skill development and task performance by nudging users toward states estimated to be most learnable. We first show that PSN outperforms existing shared autonomy baselines in balancing student improvement in unassisted reward with overall shared performance, using simulated students in the classic LunarLander environment. We then present, to the best of our knowledge, the first human subject studies of a planner incorporating learning-compatible shared autonomy: across two driving tasks in the CARLA simulator (High Performance Racing and Parallel Parking, n = 60), PSN produces up to 7x larger gains in unassisted skill than standard blended shared autonomy, while incurring 50% fewer collisions than unassisted self-practice.

</details>


### [327] [SUGAR: A Scalable Human-Video-Driven Generalizable Humanoid Loco-Manipulation Learning Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20373)
*Tianshu Wu,Xiangqi Kong,Yue Chen,Qize Yu,Hang Ye,Jia Li,Yizhou Wang,Hao Dong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SUGAR is a data-driven, staged framework that converts diverse human videos into deployable humanoid loco-manipulation skills without task-specific rewards or reference motions, via automated priors extraction, a physics-based refiner, and hierarchical policy distillation achieving zero-shot real-world transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalizable whole-body loco-manipulation for humanoids is hampered by reward engineering, reliance on reference motions, or teleoperation. Human videos offer rich data but priors are imperfect due to occlusions and artifacts, limiting policy learning. A scalable pipeline to extract reliable priors and convert them into deployable skills is needed.

Method: Stage 1: automate extraction of kinematic interaction priors (human-object trajectories, contact labels) from unstructured videos. Stage 2: privileged physics-based refiner with a unified mimic reward and progressive state pool to refine priors into physically feasible, high-fidelity skills. Stage 3: distill refined skills into a hierarchical autonomous policy: a command generator and a command tracker.

Result: Evaluated on six loco-manipulation tasks in simulation and on real humanoid hardware. SUGAR outperforms reference-tracking baselines, scales with the amount of human video data, and achieves zero-shot real-world transfer with reliable closed-loop execution, autonomous failure recovery, and stable long-horizon performance under perturbations.

Conclusion: SUGAR demonstrates a scalable, end-to-end pipeline for converting diverse human video data into deployable loco-manipulation skills without task-specific reward design or reference motion conditioning, with demonstrated zero-shot transfer and performance gains that scale with data. Potential limitations include dependence on video quality and real-world variability.

Abstract: Building humanoid robots capable of generalizable whole-body loco-manipulation in the real world remains a fundamental challenge. Existing methods either rely on laborious task-specific reward engineering, rigidly replay reference motions that fail to generalize, or depend on costly teleoperation that limits scalability. While human videos capture diverse human behaviors, motion priors inferred from them are inherently imperfect, suffering from occlusion, contact artifacts, and retargeting errors that render them unsuitable for direct policy learning. To address this, we present SUGAR, a scalable data-driven framework that converts diverse human videos into deployable humanoid loco-manipulation skills, without any task-specific reward engineering or reference-motion conditioning at inference. SUGAR proceeds in three stages. First, a fully automated pipeline extracts kinematic interaction priors including human-object motion trajectories and contact labels from unstructured human videos. Second, a privileged physics-based refiner uses a unified mimic reward and progressive state pool to transform imperfect priors into physically feasible, high-fidelity skills. Third, refined skills are distilled into a hierarchical autonomous policy consisting of a command generator and a command tracker. We evaluate SUGAR on six representative loco-manipulation tasks in simulation and real-world humanoid hardware. Our method substantially outperforms reference-tracking baselines, and performance scales clearly with the amount of human video data. It also achieves zero-shot real-world transfer with reliable closed-loop execution, autonomous failure recovery, and stable long-horizon performance under external perturbations. Project Page: https://tianshuwu.github.io/sugar-humanoid/

</details>


### [328] [Scalable Multi-robot Motion Planning via Hierarchical Subproblem Expansion and Workspace Decomposition Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20395)
*Isaac Ngui,Courtney McBeth,James D. Motes,Marco Morales,Nancy M. Amato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A multi-robot motion planning method that uses an iteratively refined workspace decomposition with discrete search to coordinate robots, allowing planning in smaller decoupled configuration spaces and achieving large speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the computational burden of coordinating multiple robots by avoiding full joint-space search while maintaining collision avoidance, addressing the scalability bottleneck in multi-robot planning.

Method: Represent the workspace with a decomposition and perform discrete search to determine when robot coordination is necessary. Rather than composing robots into a joint configuration space from the outset, iteratively refine the workspace representation to enable planning in smaller, decoupled configuration spaces while maintaining coordination.

Result: Planning time is improved by up to an order of magnitude compared to approaches that rely on full joint-space search, by leveraging discrete workspace coordination and iterative refinement to decouple planning when possible.

Conclusion: Iterative workspace refinement coupled with discrete search provides substantial speedups in multi-robot motion planning, enabling scalable coordination without exhaustive joint-space search; the approach leverages workspace topology to inform coordination and refines it to enable decoupled planning.

Abstract: A fundamental challenge in multi-robot motion planning is achieving sufficient coordination to avoid inter-robot conflicts without incurring the large computational expense of searching the joint configuration space of the robot group. In this work, we present a method for multiple mobile robot motion planning that achieves an improvement in planning time up to an order of magnitude by leveraging the insight that we can use discrete search over a workspace decomposition to provide coordination between robots during planning. While prior work uses workspace topology to inform when coordination between robots is needed and then composes robots into their joint configuration space, we take a step further by iteratively refining our workspace representation to allow our planner to search smaller, decoupled configuration spaces.

</details>


### [329] [Spacetime Optimal-Transport Attention for Visuo-Haptic Imitation Learning of Contact-Rich Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20433)
*Yue Feng,Weicheng Huang,I-Ming Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SO-TA: a tri-modal fusion backbone using entropy-regularized optimal-transport attention to align force-pose derived sub-queries with visual patches, paired with a diffusion-based sequence policy; achieves high success on three contact-rich tasks and robustness to illumination, distractors, and occlusion, with interpretable OT heatmaps and modality diagnostics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-driven controllers for contact-rich tasks struggle due to the coupling of discontinuous contact dynamics, partial observability, and safety constraints. This work argues that a principled tri-modal fusion with structured inductive bias can improve robustness and explainability beyond uni-/bi-modal or naive cross-attention methods.

Method: Replace softmax patch-attention with entropy-regularized Optimal Transport (OT) alignment between force-pose derived sub-queries and visual patches (SO-TA). Use explicit marginal constraints as inductive bias to encourage conditioning-aware, stable spatial selection across modalities.
