Pair SO-TA with a diffusion-based sequence policy that maps observation windows to pose-action chunks.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three real-robot tasks (tight peg-in-hole, BCM wiring-connector insertion, curved-surface mark erasing) with ~200 rollouts per condition. Achieves 100% success on tight peg-in-hole vs 93% for cross-attention at matched capacity; retains 82.5% success under illumination, distractor, and partial-occlusion perturbations where a concatenation baseline drops to 43.5%. OT-derived patch heatmaps and leave-one-out modality-influence ratios provide interpretable diagnostics.

Conclusion: OT-guided tri-modal fusion offers a structured, interpretable mechanism for robust contact-rich manipulation, with a diffusion-based policy enabling sequence-level control. The approach yields strong task success and resilience to perceptual perturbations, and provides interpretable diagnostics via OT heatmaps and modality-influence analyses.

Abstract: Contact-rich manipulation tasks such as tight-clearance insertion, connector mating, polishing, and surface-conforming wiping remain difficult for data-driven controllers because they couple discontinuous contact dynamics, partial observability, and strict safety constraints. No single sensing modality suffices: vision supplies global context before contact, force/torque (F/T) feedback governs interaction after contact, and proprioceptive pose provides a consistent kinematic backbone. Most prior imitation-learning policies for contact-rich tasks operate on uni- or bi-modal signals, and the few that fuse three modalities typically adopt off-the-shelf attention modules with no explicit prior on how attention mass should be distributed across task-relevant regions. We present Spacetime Optimal-Transport Attention (SO-TA), a tri-modal fusion backbone that replaces softmax-normalized patch attention by an entropy-regularized Optimal Transport (OT) alignment between force-pose-derived sub-queries and visual patches. Explicit marginal constraints act as a structured inductive bias for contact-rich tasks, encouraging conditioning-aware spatial selection that is stable across illumination, distractors, and partial occlusion. SO-TA is paired with a diffusion-based sequence policy mapping observation windows to pose-action chunks. We evaluate SO-TA on three real-robot tasks: tight peg-in-hole assembly, BCM wiring-connector insertion, and curved-surface mark erasing. With ~200 rollouts per condition, SO-TA reaches 100% success on tight peg-in-hole versus 93% for cross-attention at matched capacity, and retains 82.5% success under illumination, distractor, and partial-occlusion perturbations where a concatenation baseline drops to 43.5%. OT-derived patch heatmaps and leave-one-out modality-influence ratios provide interpretable, phase-dependent diagnostics.

</details>


### [330] [Enhancing Graph-Based SLAM in GNSS-Denied environments by leveraging leg odometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20484)
*Léon Perruchot-Triboulet,Luc Jaulin,Kai Xiao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Parallel factor-graph SLAM augmentation: proprioceptive leg odometry forms a vertical anchor for LIO-SAM in GNSS-denied environments, dramatically reducing elevation drift and enabling convergence where the baseline fails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNSS-denied navigation for legged robots in geometrically sparse scenes suffers elevation drift; leveraging onboard proprioception can stabilize vertical estimation.

Method: Introduce a parallel kinematic lane driven by leg odometry, connected to the LiDAR-inertial lane via identity relative pose constraint with a selective noise model; extend LIO-SAM framework; implemented on Linxai D50 quad at over 1 km outdoor loops.

Result: Elevation drift reduced from >30 m to <0.3 m; convergence achieved in scenes where baseline fails; demonstrates vertical anchor efficacy.

Conclusion: Proprioceptive data onboard gait control provides a lightweight and effective vertical anchor for GNSS-denied SLAM in legged robots; potential for broader adoption.

Abstract: Autonomous navigation in GNSS-denied environments remains a core challenge for legged robots, where exteroceptive sensors such as LiDAR are prone to elevation drift in geometrically sparse or repetitive scenes. We present a factor graph architecture that augments the LIO-SAM framework with a parallel kinematic lane driven by proprioceptive leg odometry, coupled to the main LiDAR-inertial lane via an identity relative pose constraint with a selective noise model. Applied to a Linxai D50 quadruped platform across two outdoor loops totaling over one kilometer, our approach reduces elevation drift from over 30m to under 30cm and enables convergence in a scene where the baseline pipeline fails entirely. These results suggest that proprioceptive data, already computed onboard for gait control, constitutes a lightweight and effective vertical anchor for SLAM in GNSS-denied settings.

</details>


### [331] [The Yes-Man Syndrome: Benchmarking Abstention in Embodied Robotic Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20544)
*Doguhan Yeke,Elif Su Temirel,Ananth Shreekumar,Brandon Lee,Dongyan Xu,Z Berkay Celik*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A taxonomy-driven, image-grounded dataset for abstention in embodied robotics is proposed, via RoboAbstention, enabling auditable evaluation of vision-language model planners. It reveals large abstention gaps in frontier VLMs, while prompting-based methods substantially improve abstention but do not solve it, and the dataset is open-sourced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap that existing abstention benchmarks are text-only and fail to capture perceptual grounding and physical constraints in embodied robotics, where abstention must reflect ambiguity, infeasibility, false premises, or modality/context limits.

Method: Three-phase pipeline: (1) structured visual grounding to map instructions to perceptual features, (2) deterministic constraint derivation to identify physically feasible vs. infeasible/ambiguous cases, (3) controlled instruction generation using category-specific templates to create diverse, verifiable abstention data from images across five robotics datasets; evaluation of frontier VLMs with defensive prompting and in-context learning.

Result: Frontier models show substantial abstention weaknesses; best model Gemini 2.5 Flash abstains on 39.0% of 6,069 instructions; Gemini Robotics ER 1.6 Preview abstains on 16.5%. Prompting and in-context learning boost abstention dramatically (to 93.6% for ER 1.6 Preview and 88.6% for GPT-5.4 Mini), but no approach fully resolves abstention.

Conclusion: RoboAbstention provides a scalable, auditable resource for studying abstention in embodied planning, revealing persistent gaps and offering practical improvement strategies; the dataset is open-source for community use and evaluation.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are used as high-level planners for embodied agents, translating natural language instructions and visual observations into action plans. While prior work has studied abstention in LLMs, existing benchmarks are largely text-only and do not capture the perceptual grounding and physical constraints inherent to embodied robotics environments. In such settings, abstention requires recognizing when instructions are ambiguous, physically infeasible, based on false premises, or otherwise unresolvable given the available sensory modalities and context. To address this gap, we introduce a taxonomy to categorize abstention in the context of embodied robotics and present RoboAbstention, a scalable and auditable framework for generating abstention instructions grounded in images gathered from five robotics datasets. RoboAbstention instantiates the taxonomy through a three-phase pipeline: (1) structured visual grounding, (2) deterministic constraint derivation, and (3) controlled instruction generation via category-specific templates. This enables the construction of a diverse dataset with verifiable abstention conditions. We evaluate several frontier VLMs and find that all models exhibit significant weaknesses in abstention, including those with advanced reasoning capabilities. The best-performing model, Gemini 2.5 Flash, abstains on only 39.0% of our 6,069 benchmark instructions, while the embodied planner Gemini Robotics ER 1.6 Preview abstains on just 16.5%. We further explore methods for improving abstention in VLM planners, such as defensive prompting and in-context learning, and find that these interventions substantially improve performance, reaching 93.6% abstention rate for Gemini Robotics ER 1.6 Preview and 88.6% for GPT 5.4 Mini, yet no approach fully solves the problem. We open-source RoboAbstention at https://purseclab.github.io/RoboAbstention/.

</details>


### [332] [Fault-Tolerant, Rigidity-Preserving Control of Inflatable Truss Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20561)
*James Wade,Isaac Weaver,Mihai Stanciu,Nathan Usevitch*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Fault-tolerant control framework for inflatable isoperimetric trusses enabling operation under motor failures via (i) failure-aware kinematic optimization, (ii) discrete-time control barrier functions for rigidity and workspace optimization, (iii) closed-loop control with encoder feedback and state estimation; validated in a 2D testbed with significant workspace preservation and improved tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Isoperimetric trusses offer high strength-to-weight and reconfigurability, but actuation faults in the field threaten mission-critical performance; need a control framework that maintains functionality despite actuator failures.

Method: Three contributions: (1) extend kinematic optimization with equality constraints to disable failed actuators; (2) introduce discrete-time control barrier function constraints to guarantee rigidity while maximizing workspace under discrete-time control; (3) implement closed-loop position control using onboard encoders and a forward-kinematics based state estimator.

Result: Simulation and hardware experiments on 2D 6-actuator isoperimetric truss; under single-motor failures, >69% workspace preserved; closed-loop control yields >25% improvement in tracking accuracy.

Conclusion: The framework provides a foundation for robust/resilient isoperimetric truss robots operating under degraded actuation, combining kinematic optimization, DTCBF-based constraints, and state estimation to maintain performance.

Abstract: Isoperimetric robotic trusses can adapt to different tasks and environments because they have a high strength-to-weight ratio, can change their own shape dramatically, and can be reconfigured into a variety of different shapes. However, motor failures in operational environments can severely limit operational capabilities if not properly addressed. This paper presents a fault-tolerant control framework for an inflatable robotic truss that maintains functionality despite motor failures, shown through three key contributions. First, we extend the kinematic optimization to handle arbitrary combinations of motor failures by imposing equality constraints to ensure failed actuators are not used. Second, we introduce discrete-time control barrier function (DTCBF) constraints that mathematically guarantee structural rigidity while maximizing workspace utilization, a critical requirement for reliable operation of truss robots under discrete-time control. Third, we implement closed-loop position control using onboard encoder feedback and a forward kinematics-based state estimator, improving positional accuracy in the presence of disturbances. We validate our approach through simulation and hardware experiments on a 2D isoperimetric truss testbed. For a 2D configuration with 6 actuators, we demonstrate >69% workspace preservation under single-motor failures and a >25% improvement in tracking accuracy with closed-loop control. These results establish a foundation for more robust and resilient isoperimetric truss robots operating under degraded actuation.

</details>


### [333] [Conflict-Aware Active Perception and Control in 3D Gaussian Splatting Fields via Control Barrier Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20566)
*Amirhossein Mollaei Khass,Athanasios Cosse,Vivek Pandey,Nader Motee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Principled conflict-aware active perception in 3D Gaussian Splatting using AV@R-based CBF for safe, information-rich viewpoints; risk-aware EIG for next-best-view; perception barrier functions; a unified hard-safety, soft-perception QP with slack; simulations show improved safety and information gain over 3DGS baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Balancing safety and information gain in uncertain environments where informative views risk collisions near uncertain regions, using a 3D probabilistic scene representation (3DGS).

Method: Derive an AV@R-based control barrier function from 3DGS-collision risk to guarantee forward invariance of a safe set; formulate risk-aware expected information gain for next-best-view selection; introduce perception barrier functions aligning camera orientation with local information gradient; solve a unified quadratic program with hard safety constraints and soft perception constraints via slack variables.

Result: Simulation results indicate the proposed approach improves both safety (fewer collision risks) and information acquisition (higher information gain) relative to existing 3DGS-based methods.

Conclusion: A principled optimization-based framework reconciles safety and perception under uncertainty by coupling AV@R-based CBFs, risk-aware perception metrics, and barrier-guided control within a tractable QP, yielding safer and more informative robotic perception in uncertain environments.

Abstract: Active perception in uncertain environments requires robots to navigate safely while acquiring informative observations to reduce map uncertainty. These objectives inherently conflict, as informative viewpoints often lie near uncertain regions with higher collision risk. To address this challenge, we develop a conflict-aware active perception and control framework for robotic systems operating in environments represented by 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). Safety is enforced using a Control Barrier Function (CBF) derived from an Average Value-at-Risk AV@R collision-risk metric that accounts for geometric uncertainty and guarantees forward invariance of a safe set. To improve perception, we propose a risk-aware Expected Information Gain (EIG) formulation for selecting the next-best-view and introduce perception barrier functions that align the camera orientation with the local information-ascent direction. To obtain a tractable formulation for these conflicting safety and perception objectives, we propose a unified safety-critical, perception-aware quadratic program that enforces safety as a hard constraint while relaxing perception constraints through slack variables. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method improves both safety and information acquisition compared to existing 3DGS-based approaches.

</details>


### [334] [Intent-First Aerial V2V for Tactical Coordination and Separation: Protocol and Performance Under Density and Disturbance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20595)
*Mehrnaz Sabet*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: First controller-coupled V2V tactical neighborhood stack for dense UTM, combining refreshed state/intent beacons with event-triggered messages; evaluated in all-airborne sidelink C-V2X; shows improvements but needs guarded fallback at higher density; frames intent-first aerial V2V as bounded enabler for scalable coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense low-altitude aerial operations require fast, trusted local coordination beyond pre-flight plans and last-resort collision avoidance; current awareness approaches are insufficient for timely disturbance-driven separation.

Method: Developed all-airborne V2V stack (sidelink-class C-V2X) with authenticated freshness, integrated refreshed state and intent beacons, cooperative perception, degraded-mode assessment, and event-triggered coordination; stress-tested in scenario-driven, field-anchored setup with real-time infrastructure.

Result: Reduces stale-belief divergence, preserves observability, rejects invalid messages, suppresses false inferences, structures shared-resource coordination.

Conclusion: Stack provides viable layer for tactical separation in lower-to-moderate density; as density/impairment/complexity rise, need guarded fallback; intent-first aerial V2V acts as bounded enabler for scaling tactical coordination in urban airspace.

Abstract: Dense low-altitude aerial operations require more than pre-flight route coordination and last-resort collision avoidance. Once aircraft are airborne, disturbances can emerge on timescales shorter than strategic reauthorization can absorb, while collision avoidance is too late and disruptive to serve as routine traffic management. Although tactical separation is recognized as the intermediate layer, realizing it at scale requires a deployable neighborhood communication mechanism that provides fresh, trusted information for local coordination. This paper presents what is, to our knowledge, the first controller-coupled characterization of an all-airborne, sidelink-class, intent-first vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) tactical neighborhood exchange stack for dense Unmanned Aircraft System Traffic Management (UTM) operations. Unlike awareness-only broadcast, the proposed exchange combines refreshed state and intent beacons for local awareness, cooperative perception, and degraded-mode assessment with event-triggered messages for yielding, sequencing, release, and contingency coordination. We implement and evaluate this model on an all-airborne V2V stack using sidelink-class C-V2X modules with authenticated freshness checks. Evaluation uses a scenario-driven, high-volume stress campaign supported by real-time, field-anchored infrastructure. Results show that V2V reduces stale-belief divergence, preserves observability through cooperative perception, rejects invalid tactical messages, suppresses false local inference, and structures shared-resource coordination. The implemented stack provides a viable communication layer for tactical separation in lower-to-moderate regimes, but transitions toward guarded fallback as density, impairment, and complexity increase. These findings position intent-first aerial V2V as a bounded enabler for scaling tactical coordination in disturbance-driven urban airspace.

</details>


### [335] [Jointly Learning Predicates and Actions Enables Zero-Shot Skill Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20648)
*Benedict Quartey,Sebastian Castro,Eric Rosen,Wil Thomason,George Konidaris,Stefanie Tellex*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PACTS: joint generative model over action trajectories and predicate beliefs enabling robust skill composition and zero-shot planning in learning from demonstration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the generalization gap in LfD where policies model only action trajectories and lack symbolic reasoning for composing skills; enables reliable composition and execution monitoring via predicates.

Method: Introduce Predicate Action Skills (PACTS), closed-loop visuomotor policies that model a joint generative process over action and predicate belief trajectories, yielding coherent action–outcome rollouts within a single model. Joint generation improves both action generation and predicate classification and supports planning-based, zero-shot skill composition using online predicate predictions as a symbolic interface.

Result: Demonstrates zero-shot composition of learned skills via planning by leveraging online predicate predictions from PACTS as a symbolic interface for sequencing and monitoring execution; also reports improved representations for action generation and predicate classification due to joint training.

Conclusion: PACTS provide an integrated framework combining action and symbolic reasoning, enabling robust composition of demonstrated skills and planning-driven execution without retraining.

Abstract: Learning from Demonstration (LfD) enables robots to learn complex behaviors from expert examples, yet existing approaches often fail to generalize to new compositions of known skills without retraining. Modern generative policies model distributions over action trajectories alone, thus are unable to reason about the symbolic outcomes required for robust composition. We propose that skills should jointly model action trajectories and the symbolic outcomes they induce. To address this gap, we introduce Predicate Action Skills (PACTS), a class of closed-loop visuomotor policies that model skills as a joint generative process over action and predicate belief trajectories, producing coherent action-outcome rollouts within a single model. Jointly generating actions and predicates enables PACTS to learn internal representations that improve both action generation and predicate classification. Furthermore, we demonstrate zero-shot composition of learned skills via planning by leveraging online predicate predictions from PACTS as a symbolic interface for sequencing and monitoring execution. Project website: https://planpacts.github.io/

</details>


### [336] [A Semantic and Occlusion-Aware GM-PHD Filter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20666)
*Jovan Menezes,Mark Campbell*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces a Semantic-Occlusion Aware (S-OA) birth model for the GM-PHD tracker to incorporate occlusion regions and semantic priors via deep learning, improving object initialization in occlusion-heavy driving scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address misinitializations and latency in multi-object tracking under occlusion by leveraging semantic context and occlusion reasoning within the GM-PHD birth term.

Method: Define the GM-PHD birth terms using occlusion-aware regions and semantic information derived from deep learning; integrate into the standard GM-PHD framework; evaluate with Monte Carlo simulations and KITTI data; use latency from first detection to track initiation, mean absolute cardinality error, and OSPA as metrics; perform birth weight sensitivity analysis.

Result: The S-OA birth model reduces initialization delay in occlusion-heavy settings, matching or surpassing the strongest baseline in about 70% of cases; demonstrates improved or comparable tracking performance; includes a sensitivity analysis of birth model weights.

Conclusion: Incorporating occlusion reasoning and semantic priors into Bayesian tracking improves robustness and performance for autonomous driving, particularly in high-density scenarios.

Abstract: This paper proposes a new birth model including semantic information derived from deep learning to create an occlusion-aware Gaussian Mixture Probability Hypothesis Density (GM-PHD) filter. Unlike prior approaches that rely on simplistic or uniform assumptions, the proposed Semantic-Occlusion Aware (S-OA) birth model defines initialization terms by explicitly considering regions of occlusion and by leveraging semantic information about the environment. This enables the filter to accurately represent where new objects are more likely to appear, thereby improving tracking performance in complex and high-density driving scenarios. The method is evaluated through Monte Carlo simulations and experiments on the KITTI dataset. Performance is assessed by measuring the latency between first detection and track initiation, along with the mean absolute cardinality error and the Optimal Subpattern Assignment (OSPA) metric. Results demonstrate that the S-OA birth model reduces initialization delay in occlusion-heavy settings, matching or outperforming the strongest baseline in approximately 70% of cases. A sensitivity analysis of birth model weights is also provided. Overall, the findings underscore the benefits of integrating occlusion reasoning and semantic priors into Bayesian tracking frameworks for autonomous driving.

</details>


### [337] [GaussianDream: A Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian World Model for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20752)
*Zijian Zhang,Yuqing Jiang,Qian Cheng,Si Liu,Ding Zhao,Ping Luo,Weitao Zhou,Haibao Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces GaussianDream, a 3D Gaussian world-model plug-in for VLA policies that enables dense, decodable spatio-temporal supervision from robot trajectories to improve 3D geometry and short-horizon dynamics in imitation-based manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard action-imitation training provides limited explicit supervision of 3D geometry, dense structure, and rapid environment evolution, hindering physically precise manipulation.

Method: A feed-forward 3D Gaussian world-model that maps trajectories to structured spatial-temporal supervision. It combines current Gaussian reconstruction with horizon-conditioned future Gaussian prediction to produce a compact spatio-temporal prefix that is decodable into renderable 3D Gaussian states. Enables dense RGB, depth, and pseudo 3D scene-flow supervision without requiring test-time Gaussian decoding. At inference, only the learned prefix is used to condition action generation, avoiding rendering/rollouts during closed-loop control.

Result: Empirical evaluation on LIBERO, RoboCasa Human-50, and real-robot tasks shows strong performance: 98.4% average success on LIBERO, 52.6% on RoboCasa Human-50, and 50.0% in real-world evaluation.

Conclusion: GaussianDream provides dense supervision for vision-language-action policies without extra test-time decoding, achieving competitive to strong results across benchmarks and real robots, with efficient inference by discarding decoding heads.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies have advanced language-conditioned robotic manipulation by transferring semantic priors from pretrained vision-language models to action generation. Yet, standard action-imitation training often provides limited explicit supervision for 3D geometry, dense visual structure, and short-horizon environment evolution, which are critical for physically precise manipulation. We introduce \textbf{GaussianDream}, a feed-forward 3D Gaussian world-model plug-in that turns robot trajectories into structured spatial-temporal supervision. The key idea is to couple current Gaussian reconstruction with horizon-conditioned future Gaussian prediction during training, forcing a compact spatio-temporal prefix to be decodable into renderable 3D Gaussian states. This enables dense RGB rendering, depth, and pseudo 3D scene-flow supervision without requiring test-time Gaussian decoding. At inference, GaussianDream discards all auxiliary decoding heads and retains only the learned prefix to condition action generation, avoiding rendering, video rollout, or additional planning during closed-loop control. Experiments on LIBERO, RoboCasa Human-50, and real-robot tasks demonstrate strong and highly competitive performance, achieving \textbf{98.4\%} average success on LIBERO, \textbf{52.6\%} on RoboCasa Human-50, and \textbf{50.0\%} in real-world evaluation.

</details>


### [338] [VLA-REPLICA: A Low-Cost, Reproducible Benchmark for Real-World Evaluation of Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20774)
*Alex S. Huang,Jiahui Zhang,Shiqing Tang,Yu Xiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A low-cost, reproducible real-world benchmark (VLA-REPLICA) for Vision-Language-Action robotic manipulation, built from off-the-shelf parts, enabling consistent evaluation across labs with diverse tasks and a small adaptation dataset; includes in-distribution and out-of-distribution evaluation protocols; experiments show reproducibility and reveal strengths/limitations of VLA models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of accessible, reproducible, and diverse real-world benchmarks for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. Simulation benchmarks fail to capture real-world complexity, and existing real-world benchmarks are expensive, centralized, or limited in task diversity.

Method: Develop VLA-REPLICA as a low-cost, easily reproducible hardware platform assembled from off-the-shelf components; create a diverse suite of manipulation tasks; provide a small demonstration dataset for target-domain adaptation; establish evaluation protocols for both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings; perform experiments using imitation learning and state-of-the-art VLA models.

Result: Demonstrates model strengths and limitations and confirms reproducibility across independently constructed setups, indicating the benchmark's reliability and consistency.

Conclusion: VLA-REPLICA enables accessible, reproducible real-world benchmarking for VLA models, supporting robust evaluation and cross-lab comparability in robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong promise for general-purpose robotic manipulation, but their real-world evaluation remains limited by a lack of accessible, reproducible, and consistent benchmarks. Simulation benchmarks fail to capture real-world complexity, while existing real-world benchmarks often require expensive hardware, centralized evaluation, or are limited in task diversity. We introduce VLA-REPLICA, a low-cost, easily reproducible real-world benchmark for evaluating VLA models. Built from off-the-shelf components, our system can be quickly assembled and replicated across laboratories, providing a consistent environment for policy evaluation anywhere in the world. VLA-REPLICA includes a diverse suite of manipulation tasks and a small-scale demonstration dataset for target-domain adaptation, with real-world evaluation protocols for both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings. Experiments with imitation learning and state-of-the-art VLA models reveal model strengths and limitations, while consistent results across independently constructed setups demonstrate the reproducibility of our benchmark.

</details>


### [339] [CMC-Opt: Constraint Manifold with Corners for Inequality-Constrained Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20796)
*Yetong Zhang,Frank Dellaert*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A manifold-based optimization framework for robotics that encodes mixed equality and inequality constraints as constraint manifolds with corners, extends manifold optimization to this structure, and demonstrates success on large-scale kinodynamic planning by producing dynamically feasible trajectories where standard methods fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address optimization problems in robotics with mixed nonlinear equalities and inequalities by embedding the feasible set into a constrained state space and performing optimization directly on that space, avoiding penalties or projections.

Method: Introduce constraint manifolds with corners to represent the feasible state space; extend existing manifold optimization algorithms to operate on this non-smooth topological structure; apply the framework to kinodynamic planning problems.

Result: Validated on a large-scale kinodynamic planning task, achieving dynamically feasible trajectories and robustness where standard constrained optimization methods struggle.

Conclusion: The constraint-manifold-with-corners framework provides a powerful and robust approach to constrained robotics optimization by enabling unconstrained optimization techniques to operate directly on the feasible state space.

Abstract: We introduce a manifold-based framework for addressing optimization problems with equality and inequality constraints found in robotics. Our approach transforms the original problem into an unconstrained optimization problem directly on the constrained state space. To achieve this, we introduce ``constraint manifolds with corners" to represent the state space satisfying mixed nonlinear equality and inequality constraints. We further extend manifold optimization algorithms to operate on this new topological structure. We demonstrate the power and robustness of our framework in the context of a large-scale kinodynamic planning problem, successfully generating dynamically feasible trajectories where standard methods fail.

</details>


### [340] [Q-SpiRL: Quantum Spiking Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20801)
*Mohamed Khair Altrabulsi,Nouhaila Innan,Alberto Marchisio,Muhammad Kashif,Muhammad Shafique*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: QSNN-based quantum-enhanced spiking RL yields superior navigation performance with high success rate and efficient trajectories, validated on IBM hardware across 20x20–40x40 grid-worlds with static/dynamic obstacles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve robustness and efficiency of robot navigation in dynamic environments by combining spike-based temporal processing with quantum feature transformation in a reinforcement learning framework.

Method: Develop five agent families (tabular Q-learning, classical MLP, classical SNN, quantum-enhanced MLP, and quantum-enhanced spiking neural network) under a unified training/evaluation pipeline; QSNN is the central architecture combining spike-based temporal processing and variational quantum feature transformation; evaluate on 3 grid sizes (20x20, 30x30, 40x40) with static and dynamic obstacles; assess via success rate, success-weighted path length, path length, and turn rate under deterministic inference; validate on IBM quantum hardware.

Result: QSNN offers the strongest overall trade-off between task completion, trajectory efficiency, and motion smoothness, achieving up to 99% success rate in challenging settings with dynamic obstacles; maintains high path efficiency; demonstrates feasibility of running the hybrid policy on real quantum hardware.

Conclusion: Hybrid quantum-enhanced spiking policies are promising for robust, efficient navigation in dynamic environments; successful hardware demonstrations indicate practical feasibility and potential scalability.

Abstract: Adaptive robot navigation in dynamic environments requires policies that can reach the target reliably while producing efficient and stable trajectories. This paper presents Q-SpiRL, a quantum spiking reinforcement learning framework for obstacle-aware robot navigation. The framework develops and evaluates five agent families: tabular Q-learning, classical MLP, classical SNN, quantum-enhanced MLP (QMLP), and quantum-enhanced spiking neural network (QSNN). While all models are implemented under a unified training and evaluation pipeline, the QSNN is the central architecture of interest, as it combines spike-based temporal processing with variational quantum feature transformation. Experiments are conducted across three grid-world environments of increasing size, namely 20x20, 30x30, and 40x40, with both static and dynamic obstacles. Performance is assessed using success rate, success-weighted path length, path length, and turn rate under deterministic inference. Results show that QSNN achieves the strongest overall trade-off between task completion, trajectory efficiency, and motion smoothness, reaching up to 99% success rate while maintaining high path efficiency in the most challenging setting. Execution on IBM quantum hardware further demonstrates the feasibility of deploying the proposed hybrid policy under real-device conditions.

</details>


### [341] [Demo-JEPA: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for One-shot Cross-Embodiment Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20811)
*Jingyang He,Guangrun Li,Jieyu Zhang,Chengkai Hou,Zhengping Che,Shanghang Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Cross-embodiment imitation framework (Demo-JEPA) that maps demonstrations into a shared latent space and uses planning with the agent's own dynamics to realize subgoals, enabling flexible imitation across morphology differences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome action-space mismatch and embodiment-specific constraints in imitation learning by treating demonstrations as future goal specifications rather than action-by-action replication.

Method: JEPA-based world model translates source visual demonstrations into target-compatible latent trajectories in a shared predictive space; the target agent plans to realize these latent subgoals using its own forward dynamics; no action-level alignment required; operates with visual demonstrations and interaction data.

Result: Empirical results on RLBench and real-world tasks show competitive performance with specialized planners and strong generalization to unseen tasks and embodiments.

Conclusion: Demonstrations can encode intent as latent subgoals; decoupling intent from embodiment enables robust cross-embodiment imitation through latent planning.

Abstract: Robotic imitation learning is often treated as reproducing demonstrated actions, but actions are inherently embodiment-specific. When demonstrations come from humans or robots with different morphology, kinematics, or action spaces, this action-centric view requires shared action spaces, heuristic retargeting, or large-scale multi-embodiment co-training. We instead view demonstrations as implicit specifications of future goals: the target agent should infer what state the demonstrator is trying to realize, rather than how the demonstrator executes it. We propose Demo-JEPA, a cross-embodiment imitation framework that decouples demonstration intent from embodiment-specific execution. Built on a JEPA-based world model, Demo-JEPA translates source visual demonstrations into target-compatible future latent trajectories in a shared predictive representation space. The target agent then uses these latent trajectories as subgoals and realizes them through planning under its own learned forward dynamics. Because Demo-JEPA avoids action-level correspondence and requires only visual demonstrations plus the target agent's own interaction experience, it supports flexible imitation across heterogeneous embodiments. Experiments on RLBench and real-world manipulation tasks show that Demo-JEPA matches specialized in-domain planners and generalizes to unseen tasks and embodiment configurations where prior methods fail.

</details>


### [342] [SmoCap: Unified Scale-Pose Canonicalization with Proxy-Mapped Trust-Region QP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20850)
*Shihao Li,Naohiko Sugita*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SmoCap is a leakage-resistant, joint-scale canonicalization framework that couples morphology and posture in local trust-region QPs using a low-dimensional proxy map, stabilizing weakly observed motions and enabling dataset-scale, consistent motion capture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stage-wise scaling and inverse kinematics can cause morphology-posture compensation, yielding anatomically inconsistent yet numerically acceptable results, especially in weakly observed directions. A unified, leakage-resistant approach is needed for anatomically plausible, dataset-scale motion canonicalization.

Method: SmoCap solves a constrained trust-region QP with analytical proxy-mapped pose and scale Jacobians. A low-dimensional proxy map stabilizes weakly observed directions and coordinates tissue-like structures. Optional pre-solve provides warm starts in difficult configurations.

Result: Achieves 2.9° knee flexion RMSE against fluoroscopy; anthropometric endpoint error ~3%. Leakage audit shows reduced marker RMSE, FE error, and anthropometric endpoint error. Proxy coupling preserves expressive spine motion with minimal fitting error increase (+0.14 mm, +0.6%) in yoga ablation. Median marker RMSE ~20 mm; median frame runtime 0.204–0.332 ms with 2–3 iterations.

Conclusion: SmoCap delivers an externally validated, coupling-aware scale-pose framework that makes externally consistent motion canonicalization practical at dataset scale.

Abstract: Objective: Stage-wise workflows that separate model scaling and inverse kinematics can induce morphology-posture compensation, resulting in anatomically inconsistent yet numerically acceptable solutions, especially in weakly observed directions. We present SmoCap, a leakage-resistant canonicalization framework that estimates morphology and posture jointly in each local trust-region quadratic program (QP) within a sparse control subspace. Methods: SmoCap solves a constrained trust-region QP with analytical proxy-mapped pose and scale Jacobians. The low dimensional proxy map stabilizes weakly observed directions and drives coordinated structures. An optional pre-solve provides warm starts in difficult configurations. The framework is evaluated using cohort fluoroscopy knee motion, anthropometric ground truth, and extreme yoga sequences. Results: SmoCap achieved 2.9 degree knee flexion RMSE against fluoroscopy, and a pooled anthropometric endpoint error around 3%. In the leakage audit against segment wise scaling, SmoCap also reduced marker RMSE, FE error, and anthropometric endpoint error. Proxy coupling preserved expressive and coordinated spine motion with marginal fitting error increase (+0.14 mm, +0.6%) against baseline models in yoga ablation. Median marker RMSE was around 20 mm, and median runtime was 0.204-0.332 ms/frame, achieved with consistently 2-3 iterations. Conclusion: SmoCap provides an externally validated unified coupling-aware scale-pose framework, making externally consistent motion canonicalization practical at dataset scale.

</details>


### [343] [DISC: Decoupling Instruction from State-Conditioned Control via Policy Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20856)
*Hanxiang Ren,Pei Zhou,Xunzhe Zhou,Yanchao Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces DISC, a hypernetwork-based approach that generates a task-specific visuomotor policy solely from instruction, removing observation leakage caused by language-free shortcuts; demonstrates strong performance on LIBERO-90 and Meta-World, with few-shot adaptation, and outperforms entangled baselines and π0 without external pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language-conditioned manipulation policies suffer from observation leakage via task-state entanglement, where networks exploit scene-to-action shortcuts that bypass language grounding. DISC aims to ensure task-awareness originates from language while policy parameters are generated by language, eliminating leakage by design.

Method: A hypernetwork generates the entire parameter set of a task-specific visuomotor policy from the instruction. The policy itself never directly accesses language. A two-stage hypernetwork with a refinement stage encodes the structure of gradient-based optimization as a feed-forward inductive bias, yielding globally consistent parameters without performing actual gradient computation. Trained from scratch on standard data budgets. Evaluated on LIBERO-90, Meta-World, and a real-world benchmark with identical visual context.

Result: DISC outperforms all entangled baselines on LIBERO-90 and Meta-World, with advantages growing on long-horizon tasks; it surpasses the large-scale pretrained π0 despite no external pretraining data. In a real-world setting where all tasks share the same visual context, DISC substantially outperforms entangled alternatives. The hypernetwork learns a semantically structured parameter manifold enabling few-shot adaptation from minimal demonstrations and robust generalization across paraphrased instructions.

Conclusion: Language-generated policy parameters drive behavior; observation leakage is structurally eliminated. DISC provides a semantically structured, adaptable parameter space and achieves strong empirical performance without language-access at inference-time. Code is available at the provided URL.

Abstract: Language-conditioned manipulation policies typically process instructions and observations through shared network parameters. This task-state entanglement provides a pathway for observation leakage -- networks learn scene-to-action shortcuts that bypass language grounding entirely. DISC eliminates this failure structurally. Rather than conditioning a universal policy on language, DISC uses a hypernetwork to generate the entire parameter set of a task-specific visuomotor policy from the instruction alone. The generated policy never directly accesses language; therefore, its task-awareness must come from the language. Consequently, observation leakage has no pathway to emerge. On the other hand, generating coherent high-dimensional policy weights is itself a challenging problem. We address it with a two-stage hypernetwork whose refinement stage embeds the structure of gradient-based optimization as a feed-forward inductive bias, producing globally consistent parameters without actual gradient computation. Trained entirely from scratch on standard data budgets, DISC outperforms all entangled baselines on LIBERO-90 and Meta-World, with advantages that widen on complex, long-horizon tasks -- and surpasses the large-scale pretrained $π_0$ despite using no external pretraining data. On a real-world benchmark where all tasks share identical visual context, DISC substantially outperforms entangled alternatives, directly confirming that language-generated policy parameters, not visual shortcuts, drive behavior. The hypernetwork further learns a semantically structured parameter manifold that enables few-shot adaptation from minimal demonstrations and robust generalization across paraphrased instructions. Our code is available at: {https://github.com/ReNginx/DISC}.

</details>


### [344] [Mobile UMI: Cross-View Diffusion Policy with Decoupled Kinematics for Mobile Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20894)
*Haoran Huang,Haonan Dong,Huixu Dong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hardware-free Mobile UMI framework that decouples kinematics and aligns latency for mobile imitation learning using chest-wrist dual cameras, a ChArUco anchor, and an asynchronous receding-horizon executor, achieving 83.8% success over 100 trials per task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle two coupled bottlenecks in mobile imitation learning: locomotion-contaminated action labels and inference-induced execution latency on a moving base, without requiring robot hardware changes.

Method: Three components: (1) dual-camera capture of chest-centric global context and wrist-centric local interaction without a robot; (2) one-shot ChArUco-based spatial anchor unifies chest and hand frames; hand pose expressed relative to chest to yield decoupled SE(3) manipulation and SE(2) base trajectories; (3) asynchronous receding-horizon executor performs online state matching by realigning generated action chunks with current pose and discarding expired waypoints before execution.

Result: Average success rate 83.8% across four long-horizon household tasks with 100 trials per task. Chest-relative labels capture much of the improvement; online state matching closes the remainder. Outperforms ACT and Diffusion Policy baselines.

Conclusion: Explicit kinematic factorization plus state-level latency alignment effectively addresses both bottlenecks in mobile imitation learning without requiring changes to the underlying policy class.

Abstract: Mobile imitation learning on portable demonstration interfaces faces two coupled bottlenecks: locomotion-contaminated action labels and inference-induced execution latency on a continuously moving base. Recent wrist-mounted interfaces lower the cost of tabletop data collection, yet a single wrist view does not capture the global context required for base navigation. Adding a body-mounted camera entangles human walking with hand motion. Meanwhile, generative policies introduce hundreds of milliseconds of inference latency, during which the base advances past predicted waypoints, forcing backward corrections at action splices. This paper presents Mobile UMI, a hardware-free demonstration framework that addresses both gaps through three components. First, a dual-camera capture system records chest-centric global context and wrist-centric local interaction without any robot present. Second, a one-shot ChArUco-based spatial anchor unifies the chest and hand visual-inertial frames; the hand pose is then re-expressed relative to the chest to extract decoupled SE(3) manipulation and SE(2) base trajectories. Third, an asynchronous receding-horizon executor performs online state matching: each generated action chunk is realigned with the current physical pose so that expired waypoints are discarded before execution. The full system is evaluated on four long-horizon household tasks, achieving an average success rate of 83.8% over 100 trials per task. Controlled comparisons against ACT and Diffusion Policy show that the chest-relative label alone closes much of the gap; online state matching closes the remainder. These results indicate that, for mobile imitation learning under the tested conditions, explicit kinematic factorization combined with state-level latency alignment provides an effective solution without requiring architectural changes to the underlying policy class.

</details>


### [345] [SubTGraph: Large-Scale Subterranean Environment Synthesis with Controllable Topological Variability for Robotic Autonomy Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20917)
*F. Labra Caso,A. Saradagi,S. Fredriksson,S. Nordström,A. Koval,G. Nikolakopoulos*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SubTGraph provides a scalable, open-source framework to synthesize diverse subterranean environments for benchmarking robotic autonomy; it uses a cost-guided Dijkstra search over topometric tiles from the DARPA World Generator to generate many varied SubT worlds, validated via semantic segmentation, multi-agent path planning, and LIO SLAM, with a public repo and a database of 150 worlds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of large-scale, simulation-based benchmarking infrastructure for SubT autonomy, enabling rigorous statistical evaluation and reproducible experiments across different environment topologies and textures.

Method: Procedural world generation guided by a user-defined cost matrix; multi-level environment synthesis over topology, dimensionality, textures; uses topometric tiles from DARPA World Generator; solves with Dijkstra to connect tiles generating distinct environments; three case studies to assess different autonomy stack layers.

Result: Demonstrates utility across three robotics tasks and provides open-source code and a database of 150 underground worlds; showcases reproducibility and variability for robust autonomy validation.

Conclusion: SubTGraph fills a notable benchmarking gap by offering rapid, diverse, and reproducible subterranean environments; its open-source nature and large repository support rigorous evaluation and community adoption, though the approach depends on tile quality and cost-model design.

Abstract: Subterranean (SubT) environments have been a frontier for autonomous robotics, driven by the push for automation of mining operations and the interest in planetary exploration (Martian Lava Tubes). Due to the challenges involved in accessing real SubT environments, rigorous hardening of autonomy stacks in realistic simulation environments is critical. This article fills a well-known gap, which relates to the unavailability of a large-scale simulation-based benchmarking infrastructure for rigorous statistical evaluation of robotic autonomy, due to which it is common for SubT research articles to present validation results in a few environments at best.
  This article presents SubTGraph, a novel framework for rapid synthesis of multi-level SubT environments with high variability, incorporating user specifications related to topology, dimensionality, textures, etc., to generate distinct environments such as operational mines, natural caves and lava tubes. SubTGraph builds a cost matrix from user-specified structural constraints to guide the classical Dijkstra algorithm to procedurally generate SubT worlds utilizing topometric tiles from the DARPA World Generator. Three robotics case-studies are investigated to demonstrate the utility of SubTGraph for rigorous validation of different layers in the robotic autonomy stack. Structural semantic segmentation is validated against topometric ground truths, multi-agent path planning is widely tested for identification of patterns and trends in the algorithm behavior and LIO SLAM is stress-tested in challenging subterranean sections to identify failure cases. The SubTGraph world creation codebase is open-sourced (https://github.com/LTU-RAI/SubTGraph.git) along with a database consisting of 150 highly variable underground worlds.

</details>


### [346] [STEAM: A Training-Free Congestion-Aware Enhancement Framework for Decentralized Multi-Agent Path Finding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20929)
*Mingyang Feng,Mengnuo Zhang,Shaoyuan Li,Xiang Yin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Training-free, test-time enhancement STEAM improves decentralized MAPF by using cost-to-go maps to identify and mitigate congestion: spatially avoidable congestion via updated costs, spatial bottlenecks via temporal logit correction, and emergent congestion via density-aware logit correction; yields up to 60% success-rate gain with minor overhead, no retraining needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve performance of pretrained, decentralized MAPF policies at test time without retraining or central planners by addressing multiple congestion types (spatially avoidable, bottlenecks, and emergent).

Method: Roll out shortest paths from current cost-to-go maps to locate potential congestion. Update agent-specific costs to mitigate spatially avoidable congestion. Apply temporal logit correction to handle spatially unavoidable bottlenecks. Use density-aware logit correction using neighboring agents' corrected maps to reduce emergent congestion. No retraining; lightweight overhead.

Result: STEAM consistently improves key metrics (success rate, makespan, solution cost) across representative learning-based decentralized MAPF algorithms, with up to ~60% success-rate gains and minor computational overhead.

Conclusion: A practical, training-free augmentation for MAPF that improves performance by guiding policy execution with congestion-aware cost updates and logit corrections at test time.

Abstract: We propose STEAM (Spatial, Temporal, and Emergent congestion Awareness for MAPF), a training-free test-time enhancement framework for learning-based decentralized Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) in discrete environments. Given a pretrained decentralized policy, STEAM requires no retraining, architectural modification, or replacement by a centralized planner. Instead, it injects lightweight congestion-aware guidance into the original policy execution. STEAM first rolls out the shortest paths induced by the current cost-to-go maps to identify potential future congestion hotspots. Spatially avoidable congestion is mitigated by updating agent-specific cost-to-go information, while spatially unavoidable bottlenecks are handled through temporal logit correction. In addition, emergent local congestion is reduced by a density-aware logit correction based on neighboring agents' corrected cost-to-go maps. Extensive experiments on representative learning-based decentralized MAPF algorithms show that STEAM consistently improves success rate, makespan, and solution cost, with success-rate gains of up to 60% and only minor computational overhead. The implementation is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/STEAM-MAPF-7A62.

</details>


### [347] [WiXus: A Wheeled-Legged Robot with Wire-Driven Environmental Utilizing to Integrate Mobility and Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20932)
*Shintaro Inoue,Kento Kawaharazuka,Temma Suzuki,Sota Yuzaki,Kei Okada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: WiXus integrates a wheeled-legged robot with a wire-driven external support to repurpose legs as manipulators, enabling 3D mobility (e.g., cliff climbing) and object/tool manipulation, suggesting a design principle that uses environmental interaction to expand capabilities beyond locomotion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitation of wheeled-legged platforms which are primarily locomotion-focused by freeing legs from locomotion responsibilities via external support, increasing task-execution capability (manipulation, tool use).

Method: Develop a hybrid robot WiXus that fuses wheeled-legged drive with a wire-driven mechanism that interacts with the external environment. By suspending the body using wire actuation, legs function as arms. Demonstrate planar locomotion, 3D mobility (cliff climbing), object manipulation (rescue of a stuffed dog), and tool use (apple harvesting with loppers).

Result: Demonstrates coordinated actuation enabling 3D mobility and manipulation, validating that environment-assisted wire actuation can repurpose limbs and extend operational domain.

Conclusion: External environment leveraging via wire-driven actuation constitutes a new design principle for wheeled-legged robots, enabling broader task spaces and potential future applications.

Abstract: Wheeled-legged robots, which have wheels at their feet and achieve high mobility by coordinating wheel drive and leg drive, have been developed. These robots have been developed purely as platforms specialized for locomotion. Therefore, they do not have a means to repurpose their legs for roles other than locomotion, such as object manipulation or tool utilization. In this paper, we address the problem of how to draw out the potential task-execution capability of the legs by freeing them from the roles of locomotion through external body support. To this end, we propose and develop a new robot, WiXus, which fuses a wheeled-legged mechanism with a wire-driven mechanism that utilizes the external environment. The developed WiXus demonstrates not only planar locomotion with wheeled-legged drive, but also three-dimensional mobility such as cliff climbing by coordinating wire-driven and wheeled-legged actuation. Furthermore, by suspending the body with wire-driven actuation, WiXus successfully repurpose its legs as arms to perform object manipulation, (e.g., rescuing a dog (stuffed animal)), and tool utilization (e.g., harvesting an apple (mockup) with loppers). This study demonstrates that the approach of utilizing the environment with wire-driven actuation is a new design principle that extends the operational domain of wheeled-legged robots.

</details>


### [348] [Component Influence-Driven Fastener Reduction for Robotic Disassemblability-Aware Design Simplification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21026)
*Takuya Kiyokawa,Tomoki Ishikura,Shingo Hamada,Genichiro Matsuda,Kensuke Harada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A CAD-based CCC_graph analytical framework identifies and removes influential fasteners to improve robotic disassembly efficiency, using 3D heatmaps and stability checks; validated on seven appliances with substantial reductions in structural constraints (8–132) and travel distances (165–1675 mm).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Design for robotic remanufacturing: provide quantitative feedback to designers on how fasteners hinder robotic disassembly, enabling faster, safer, and more efficient disassembly-oriented design decisions.

Method: Construct CAD models and automatically generate Contact-Connection-Constraint (CCC) graphs; translate robotic disassembly sequence outcomes into component influence scores; visualize scores as 3D heatmaps on CAD geometry; simulate removal of highly influential fasteners; assess reductions in structural constraints, tool changes, and robot travel; enforce geometric stability to prevent unsafe modifications; validate on seven household appliances.

Result: Removal of recommended fasteners targeted redundancies and simplified structural dependencies, eliminating 8–132 structural constraints on the CCC graph across products; reduced unnecessary tool changes and shortened robot travel by 165–1675 mm where permissible.

Conclusion: The framework provides actionable redesign guidance to mitigate robotic hindrances through fastener reduction, offering a quantitative, visual, and safety-aware approach validated on real products.

Abstract: To accelerate automated remanufacturing, robotic disassembly must be considered during the product design phase. However, designers currently lack quantitative feedback to identify which structural elements hinder robotic operations. To address this, this study proposes an analytical framework that provides actionable redesign guidance focused on fastener reduction, as fasteners are numerous and ubiquitous components found in almost all manufactured products. Using a Computer-Aided Design (CAD) model and its automatically generated Contact-Connection-Constraint (CCC) graph, the framework translates robotic disassembly sequence planning outcomes into component influence scores. These scores reflect how often a component causes structural constraint violations or evaluation objective deteriorations in the robotic disassembly sequence. To visually highlight structural hindrances, the framework projects these scores onto the CAD geometry as 3D heatmaps. The system then analytically simulates the removal of highly influential fasteners. It reports the expected reductions in structural constraints, tool changes, and robot travel distances, while preventing structurally unsafe modifications by evaluating geometric stability metrics. Experiments on seven household appliances demonstrate that the framework successfully targets redundant fasteners. Removing the recommended fasteners simplified the structural dependencies by eliminating between 8 and 132 structural constraints on the graph depending on each product's structural configuration. Furthermore, it improved robotic operational efficiency by eliminating unnecessary tool change operations and shortening travel distances by 165 to 1675 millimeters wherever structurally permissible.

</details>


### [349] [Modeling and Control of a Pneumatic Morphing Soft Quadrotor based on the SOFA Framework for Dynamic Soft Robotic Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21031)
*F. Labra Caso,V. Sumathy,P. Ferrentino,V. Vanderborght,J. Haluska,G. Nikolakopoulos*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SOFA-based FEM for soft pneumatic quadrotor arms with tetrahedral mesh and elastic laws; periodic/error-based actuation; PID control; simulation validates morphing and dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of modeling and controlling soft, pneumatically actuated arms within a quadrotor while preserving the interpretable control structure of rigid-quadror dynamics.

Method: Discretize soft arms as tetrahedral mesh in SOFA under an elastic material law to generate internal forces; actuate pneumatic cavities with periodic and error-based signals; implement a proportional-integral controller to drive the arm to a target position and study morphing.

Result: Simulation results demonstrate the framework's effectiveness in capturing dynamics and enabling morphing through controlled actuation.

Conclusion: The approach retains traditional quadrotor dynamics interpretability while accurately modeling soft arm behavior, enabling controllable morphing validated by simulations.

Abstract: This article presents a novel SOFA based finite element method for the soft body modeling and the corresponding dynamic simulation and control of a pneumatic morphing soft quadrotor. The proposed modeling preserves the physical interpretability and control structure of traditional quadrotor dynamics, while capturing the complex, time-varying behavior of pneumatically actuated soft arms. In SOFA, the soft pneumatically actuated arms are discretized as a tetrahedral mesh following an elastic material law that produces internal forces adequate to the real dynamic behavior of the body. Pneumatic actuation governed by both periodic and error-based control signals is applied within the internal cavities to analyze the morphing capability. Finally, a proportional-integral controller is proposed to study the controlled dynamic behavior and morphing capabilities of the pneumatic arm, wherein the pneumatic actuation to the soft arm is controlled to achieve the desired target position. The simulation results show the effectiveness of the proposed novel modeling framework and the related controller design.

</details>


### [350] [Perception of Social Robots as Communication Partners in Healthcare for Older Adults](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21053)
*Hana Yamamoto,Carlotta Julia Mayer,Charlotte Raithel,Theresa Buchner,Christian Werner,Yasuhisa Hirata,Monika Eckstein,Katja Mombaur*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Robots matched humans as interaction partners for older adults; no stress difference; robots accepted; slightly lower HR indicating relaxed state; potential to reduce caregiver burden via structured tasks; design issue: appearance-content mismatch.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address global caregiver shortage and understand psychological/physiological effects of HRI on older adults; evaluate social robots as interaction partners and the effect of positive prompts.

Method: Comparative study with 35 participants aged 70+, multi-modal measurements (facial expressions, heart rate variability, and subjective questionnaires); compared robot-led vs human-led interactions and evaluated 'positive prompts'; tasks included structured health-sensing surveys.

Result: No significant differences in overall stress between robot and human interactions; robot accepted as a valid interaction partner based on facial expression analysis; physiological data showed slightly lower heart rates during robot interactions, suggesting a more relaxed state.

Conclusion: Social robots can engage older adults without inducing psychological strain and can reduce caregiver burden by performing structured tasks. Design improvement is needed to address the appearance-content mismatch to enable more natural interactions.

Abstract: Addressing the global caregiver shortage through socially assistive robots necessitates a deep understanding of their psychological and physiological impacts on older adults during human-robot interaction (HRI). This study addresses whether social robots can serve as effective interaction partners compared to humans, and if "positive prompts" can similarly enhance these interactions. We conducted a comparative study with 35 participants (aged 70+). Our multi-modal analysis, integrating facial expression data, heart rate variability, and subjective questionnaires, revealed no significant differences in overall stress levels between human and robot interactions. Facial expression analysis confirmed that the robot was accepted as a valid interaction partner, while physiological data showed slightly lower heart rates during robot interactions, suggesting a more relaxed state compared to human-led sessions. These findings indicate that social robots can engage older adults without inducing psychological strain and are capable of alleviating caregiver burden by performing structured tasks, such as health-sensing surveys. Future work should address the identified "appearance-content mismatch" in robot design to facilitate even more natural and effective interactions.

</details>


### [351] [Anomaly-Informed Confidence Calibration for Vision-Based Safety Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21109)
*Zhenjiang Mao,Jiawen Wu,Gabriel Wagner,Zhongzheng Zhang,Ivan Ruchkin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Anomaly-informed online calibration fuses perceptual (reconstruction error) and dynamics (epistemic uncertainty and control-stream) anomaly scores from a world model, applying test-time augmentation and lightweight temperature scaling to calibrate confidence for vision-based controllers under distribution shifts; achieves 37% improvement on DonkeyCar.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety-critical autonomous racing requires reliable uncertainty estimates. Existing anomaly signals focus on visual corruptions and miss dynamics anomalies (e.g., actuation bias, latency), leading to overconfidence under test-time shifts. A perception-dynamics gap motivates an online, retraining-free fusion and calibration approach.

Method: Extract two anomaly scores from a world model: a perceptual score from reconstruction error and a dynamics score from epistemic uncertainty and control-stream statistics; fuse these scores; use a lightweight temperature-scaling calibrator with test-time augmentation to calibrate predictions under shift; no retraining of the base predictor; evaluate on a physical DonkeyCar across four unseen anomaly protocols (darkness, blur, actuation bias, processing latency).

Result: Calibration error (ECE) reduced from 0.184 to 0.116, a 37% improvement over the best baseline, without modifying the base safety predictor.

Conclusion: Jointly leveraging perceptual and dynamics anomaly signals and applying online calibration with test-time augmentation yields more reliable confidence estimates under distribution shifts for vision-based autonomous control, preserving nominal performance while improving safety.

Abstract: Reliable confidence estimates are important for safely deploying vision-based controllers in autonomous racing, where safety predictions must be derived from camera images, yet modern predictors become dangerously overconfident under test-time distribution shifts. We identify a critical perception-dynamics gap in existing anomaly signals: widely used scores, such as autoencoder reconstruction error, capture visual corruptions but miss dynamics anomalies (e.g., actuation bias, latency), where images remain plausible while the trajectory degrades. To address this, we propose an Anomaly-Informed Online Calibration approach that, without retraining any model component, fuses two complementary anomaly scores extracted from a world model: a perceptual score from reconstruction error and a dynamics score from epistemic uncertainty and control-stream statistics. Based on these fused scores, a lightweight temperature-scaling calibrator leverages test-time augmentation to selectively reduce overconfidence under shift while preserving nominal-condition performance. Experiments on a physical DonkeyCar under four real-world anomaly protocols unseen during training (darkness, blur, actuation bias, processing latency) reduce average expected calibration error from 0.184 to 0.116, a 37% improvement over the best baseline, without modifying the base safety predictor.

</details>


### [352] [Benchmarking Empirical and Learning-Based Approaches for Feedforward Steering Control in Autonomous Racing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21111)
*Georg Jank,Mattia Piccinini,Sebastian Wenk,Phillip Pitschi,Johannes Betz,Boris Lohmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Open-loop accuracy favors learning-based feedforward; however, in closed-loop racing control, the proposed polynomial-based EHD method achieves the best robustness and lap times, highlighting the gap between open-loop metrics and full-stack performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess and benchmark feedforward steering controllers (two learning-based and two empirical) in a high-fidelity racing simulation to determine how open-loop predictions translate to closed-loop performance within a complete control stack.

Method: Implement four feedforward controllers (2 learning-based, 2 empirical); introduce a new EHD formulation using a polynomial surface fit to model velocity-dependent nonlinear steering with few parameters; evaluate in a high-fidelity Abu Dhabi Autonomous Racing League simulation with a double-track vehicle model; compare open-loop prediction errors and closed-loop path tracking and lap times; iterate tuning; provide code.

Result: Open-loop: learning-based controllers deliver lowest prediction errors; Closed-loop: enhanced accuracy does not yield better path tracking or lap times; EHD shows best closed-loop robustness and fastest lap times; overall, stack-level evaluation is essential.

Conclusion: Feedforward steering performance depends on closed-loop integration; high open-loop accuracy alone is insufficient; the polynomial EHD approach provides superior robustness and lap-time performance within the full trajectory planning/control stack; results are supported by open-source code.

Abstract: Feedforward steering control is a key component of hierarchical control architectures for autonomous racing. The goal is to reduce steering corrections from the feedback controllers by predicting the vehicle's inverse lateral dynamics. This paper presents a systematic benchmark of two learning-based and two empirical (analytical) feedforward steering controllers. We introduce a new \acf{ehd} formulation based on a polynomial surface fit that captures velocity-dependent nonlinear steering behavior with minimal parametrization. We test the feedforward controllers in a high-fidelity simulation framework based on the real-world Abu Dhabi Autonomous Racing League competition, using a high-fidelity double-track vehicle dynamics simulator. Open-loop evaluation shows that the learning-based controllers achieve the lowest prediction errors; however, closed-loop testing reveals that this improved accuracy does not translate into superior path tracking performance or lap times, even after iterative fine-tuning. In contrast, the proposed EHD approach achieves the best overall closed-loop robustness and lap time, highlighting the necessity of evaluating feedforward strategies within the complete trajectory planning and control software stack. Our code is available at https://github.com/TUMRT/steering_ff_control.

</details>


### [353] [Humanoid Whole-Body Manipulation via Active Spatial Brain and Generalizable Action Cerebellum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21133)
*Zhizhao Liang,Yi-Lin Wei,Xuhang Chen,Mu Lin,Yi-Xiang He,Zhexi Luo,Jun-Hui Liu,Kun-Yu Lin,Wei-Shi Zheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A generalizable humanoid loco-manipulation framework using multi-agent large models with an Active Spatial Brain (perception/plan) and a Generalizable Action Cerebellum (execution) to address spatial understanding and data-efficient action generation; validated on spatial manipulation tasks with strong performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcoming two main challenges in humanoid whole-body manipulation: (1) complex 3D spatial understanding in diverse environments, and (2) limited real-robot data hindering generalization of data-driven policies.

Method: Two-component framework: Active Spatial Brain for active spatial perception, decision-making, task planning and subtask decomposition; Generalizable Action Cerebellum for generating executable robot actions directly from the Brain's decisions, without reliance on task-specific real-robot data.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows strong performance on both spatial perception/understanding and real-robot task execution across diverse tasks and environments.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates generalizable loco-manipulation capabilities by decoupling perception/planning and action generation, leveraging large-model cognition to compensate for limited real-robot data.

Abstract: In this paper, we explore spatial-aware humanoid whole-body manipulation task. Compared with tabletop settings, this task poses two key challenges: 1) Spatial understanding is challenging in complex 3D environments with diverse spatial relations. 2) Action generation is difficult to generalize, as limited and costly real-robot data restricts data-driven models generalization. To address these challenges, we propose a generalizable humanoid loco-manipulation framework that leverages the spatial perception and action generation capabilities of multi-agent large models. Specifically, our framework includes two components: Active Spatial Brain for active spatial perception and decision-making, and Generalizable Action Cerebellum for executable robot action generation. The first component actively perceives the spatial scene and makes decisions on task planning and subtask decomposition. The second component generate executable robot actions based on the decisions made by the first module without needs of task-specific real robot data. To benchmark our framework, we design a set of spatial manipulation tasks from two perspectives: evaluating spatial perception and understanding, and assessing real-robot task performance. The results demonstrate strong performance on both aspects across diverse tasks and environments.

</details>


### [354] [Safety-Critical Control for Smoothed Implicit Contact Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21138)
*Haegu Lee,Yitaek Kim,Christoffer Sloth*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces a safety-centric framework for smoothed implicit contact dynamics: bounds contact force under relaxed complementarity, screens the smoothing parameter κ via boundary-focused rollouts, and uses a discrete-time CBF with a robust margin to eliminate force-violations in simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety-critical control in contact-rich tasks is challenged by relaxed contact constraints: smoothing improves optimization but can misestimate contact forces; there is a need for guarantees on safety despite κ-related approximation errors.

Method: 1) derive bounds on the actual contact force under relaxed complementarity; 2) show that constraint violations can be non-monotonic in κ; 3) introduce boundary-focused rollouts to screen κ by comparing safety margin with the force-approximation error; 4) develop a discrete-time CBF framework based on a first-order Taylor approximation of the implicitly defined contact force; 5) augment the safety constraint with a fixed robust margin to account for possible under-prediction; 6) validate via simulations on four contact-rich systems.

Result: Constraint violations are non-monotonic in κ; smaller κ reduces force-approximation error but does not uniformly improve safety; the proposed boundary-focused κ screening plus robust CBF eliminates the observed force violations in standard CBF.

Conclusion: Combining force bounding, κ screening, and a robust-discrete-time CBF yields improved safety guarantees for smoothed implicit contact dynamics in contact-rich tasks; the approach is validated in simulation and offers a practical pathway for safe planning/control where predefined contact modes are unavailable.

Abstract: Smoothed implicit contact dynamics enables gradient-based planning and control for contact-rich tasks without predefined mode sequences. However, safety-critical control remains challenging because implicit contact dynamics makes safety-filter design nontrivial. The smoothing parameter $κ$ relaxes contact complementarity constraints, which makes the dynamics smooth but affects the contact force. This paper provides a method for bounding the actual contact force despite the use of relaxed complementarity constraints. We show that constraint violations can be non-monotonic in $κ$. Smaller $κ$ reduces force-approximation error, but it does not necessarily improve safety performance.
