To address this issue, we introduce boundary-focused rollouts to screen $κ$ by comparing the safety margin with the approximation error. We then develop a discrete-time control barrier function (CBF) framework based on a first-order Taylor approximation of the implicitly defined contact force. To account for possible force under-prediction, we augment the resulting safety constraint with a fixed robust margin. Simulations on four contact-rich systems show that the proposed method eliminates force violations observed under a standard CBF.

</details>


### [355] [EllipseLIO: Adaptive LiDAR Inertial Odometry with an Ellipsoid Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21150)
*Rowan Border,Margarita Chli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EllipseLIO is a real-time LiDAR-inertial odometry method that generalizes across diverse scenarios by adaptively filtering scans and registering data according to sensor capabilities and environment, avoiding scenario-specific tuning. It achieves state-of-the-art performance across five challenging datasets, showing robustness (no divergence) and 38% lower odometry error on average compared to the second-best method; source code will be released on GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve robust LIO across heterogeneous environments and LiDAR sensors without manual retuning or scenario-specific configuration, addressing the fragility of traditional LIO methods when deployed in varied settings.

Method: Adaptive LiDAR scan filtering and registration components in EllipseLIO that adjust to sensor capabilities and environmental conditions in real time, enabling cross-scenario generalization without scenario-specific tuning.

Result: EllipseLIO attains the best overall performance across five diverse datasets, with ~38% lower odometry error on average than the second-best approach, and is the only method that did not exhibit divergence in any experiment.

Conclusion: EllipseLIO demonstrates robust, generalizable real-time LIO across heterogeneous environments and sensors, and its open-source release will facilitate reproducibility and wider adoption.

Abstract: LiDAR Inertial Odometry (LIO) is a critical component for many mobile robots that need to navigate without relying on external positioning (e.g., GPS). Platforms that operate autonomously in different environments and with heterogeneous LiDAR sensors require a LIO approach that can adapt to these different scenarios without human intervention.
  Existing LIO approaches can typically provide reliable and accurate odometry in scenarios with similar environments and sensors when suitably tuned. However, many approaches struggle to retain robust odometry across heterogeneous environments and sensors while using a consistent configuration.
  This paper presents EllipseLIO, a real-time LIO approach that generalises between scenarios by using methods for LiDAR scan filtering and registration that adapt to the sensor capabilities and environment without requiring scenario-specific tuning. Experiments with EllipseLIO and state-of-the-art LIO approaches on five datasets with diverse and challenging scenarios demonstrate that EllipseLIO is the best-performing approach overall. It achieves a 38% lower odometry error on average than the second-best approach and is the only approach that does not diverge in any experiment. An open-source version of EllipseLIO will be available at github.com/v4rl-ucy/ellipselio.

</details>


### [356] [A Terrain-Adaptive epsilon-Constraint MPC for Uneven Terrain Kinodynamic Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21188)
*Otobong Jerome,Geesara Kalathunga,Tiago Nascimento*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive epsilon-constrained MPC with a semi-parametric vehicle-terrain model to explore the Pareto front in real time for car-like navigation on uneven terrain, achieving improved multi-objective performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Balance competing objectives (path efficiency vs. pose stability) under terrain-induced disturbances and enable real-time Pareto-front exploration.

Method: An adaptive epsilon-constraint MPC where epsilon bounds are tuned from terrain descriptors; a semi-parametric model combining analytical vehicle dynamics with a Sparse Gaussian Process trained on the same terrain descriptors; evaluation against MPPI and GAKD baselines.

Result: Navigation success rate of 94%; maximum orientation deviation reduced by 24%; multi-objective trade-off quality improved by 23%.

Conclusion: Adaptive epsilon-MPC with terrain-informed bounds and a semi-parametric model effectively navigates uneven terrain, delivering superior Pareto trade-offs compared to the baselines.

Abstract: Kinodynamic planning for car-like vehicles on uneven terrain requires simultaneously optimizing competing objectives such as path efficiency and pose stability. This work presents an adaptive epsilon-constraint method integrated into a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework, where the epsilon bounds are dynamically adjusted based on terrain descriptors to explore the Pareto front in real time. To capture vehicle-terrain dynamics, we develop a semi-parametric model combining analytical vehicle dynamics with a Sparse Gaussian Process (SGP) trained on the same terrain descriptors. The proposed epsilon-MPC is evaluated against MPPI and GAKD baselines, achieving a 94% navigation success rate while reducing maximum orientation deviation by 24% and improving multi-objective trade-off quality by 23%.

</details>


### [357] [To Select or not to Select, that is the Question: Distilling Robot Skill Prediction into a Small Ensemble](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21242)
*Haechan Mark Bong,Simon Roy,Euhid Aman,Giovanni Beltrame*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A small ensemble of ~133M-parameter encoders trained on synthetic NL-to-skill data achieves 83.5% task-to-skill matching, outpacing large LLMs in zero-shot settings for fleet-level task routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As robot fleets become increasingly heterogeneous, efficient task routing requires mapping natural-language task descriptions to the physical capabilities needed. Lacking labeled NL-to-skill data, synthetic data generation with LLMs and auditing enables training.

Method: Create a synthetic task-to-skill dataset via LLM-assisted generation with targeted label auditing. Train an ensemble of two fine-tuned sentence encoders (mpnet + MiniLM) totaling ~133M parameters. Evaluate on a stratified 200-task dataset, comparing against Kimi K2 (1T MoE), GPT-OSS-120B, and Llama-4-Scout-17B under the same zero-shot prompts.

Result: The model achieves 83.5% task-to-skill matching, outperforming Kimi K2 at 72.0%, GPT-OSS-120B at 71.5%, and Llama-4-Scout-17B at 69.0% under the same zero-shot prompts.

Conclusion: For fixed robot skill taxonomies, small specialized models trained on synthetic data can outperform large general-purpose LLMs for fleet-level task routing.

Abstract: As robot fleets become more heterogeneous, including humanoids, rovers, quadrupeds, and drones, selecting the right robot for a task becomes a core systems problem. We study robot skill prediction: mapping a natural-language task description to the physical capabilities required to execute it, such as fly, wheels, legs, surface water, under water and hands. Since labelled data that maps natural-language task descriptions to robot's physical capabilities does not exist, we construct a synthetic task-to-skill dataset using LLM-assisted generation and targeted label auditing. Trained on this data, a ~133M-parameter ensemble of two fine-tuned sentence encoders (mpnet + MiniLM) reaches 83.5% task-to-skill matching on a stratified 200 task dataset, outperforming Kimi K2 (1T MoE) at 72.0%, GPT-OSS-120B at 71.5%, and Llama-4-Scout-17B at 69.0% under the same zero-shot prompt. These results suggest that, for fixed robot skill taxonomies, small specialized models trained on synthetic data can outperform much larger general-purpose LLMs for fleet-level task routing.

</details>


### [358] [Reinforcement Learning for Risk Adaptation via Differentiable CVaR Barrier Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21257)
*Xinyi Wang,Taekyung Kim,Bardh Hoxha,Georgios Fainekos,Dimitra Panagou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An end-to-end risk-adaptive framework for crowd navigation, integrating RL with a CVaR-based safety layer under Gaussian mixture obstacle motion to achieve safe, efficient, and generalizable performance under uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Crowd navigation under uncertain obstacle motion often leads to conservative or inefficient behavior; need context-aware risk adaptation to balance safety and efficiency.

Method: Model obstacle motion with a Gaussian mixture; train a policy via RL while a differentiable quadratic-program safety layer enforces CVaR barrier-based probabilistic safety constraints; jointly learn nominal control, risk level, and safety margin.

Result: Extensive experiments across dynamic, uncertain, crowded scenarios; varying obstacle densities and robot models; three out-of-distribution generalization tests; integrated RL+optimization approach yields strongest safety, efficiency, and generalization under uncertainty.

Conclusion: The framework delivers context-aware, risk-adaptive navigation that maintains safety while minimizing unnecessary conservatism; outperforms optimization-only and RL-only baselines in challenging uncertainty conditions.

Abstract: Planning through crowded environments under uncertain obstacle motions remains difficult, as stochastic interactions often induce overly conservative behavior or reduced efficiency. To address this challenge, we propose an end-to-end risk adaptation framework for crowd navigation under obstacle-motion uncertainty modeled by a Gaussian mixture model. The framework combines reinforcement learning~(RL) with a differentiable quadratic-program safety layer based on Conditional Value-at-Risk~(CVaR) barrier functions, jointly learning nominal control input, risk level, and safety margin and enforcing explicit probabilistic safety constraints. This design enables context-aware adaptation, promoting efficient behavior while invoking caution only when necessary. We conduct extensive evaluations in dynamic, uncertain, and crowded environments across varying obstacle densities and robot models, and further assess generalization under three out-of-distribution cases. Comparisons across optimization-based, RL-based, and integrated RL and optimization methods are provided, and the proposed method is shown to deliver the strongest overall performance in safety, efficiency, and generalization under uncertainty.

</details>


### [359] [Learning Structural Latent Points for Efficient Visual Representations in Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21258)
*Yicheng Jiang,Jiaxu Wang,Junhao He,Zesen Gan,Junhao Li,Qiang Zhang,Jingkai Sun,Jiahang Cao,Mingyuan Sun,Xiangyu Yue,Qiming Shao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid 3D representation method for embodied perception that merges implicit expressiveness with explicit structural priors by inserting a point-wise latent VAE into a point-cloud autoencoder’s latent space, regularized to a Gaussian prior, coupled with a lightweight 3DGS-based renderer; demonstrates improved task success, sample efficiency, and robustness across RLBench, ManiSkill2, and real robots, with ablations confirming each component’s importance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the dichotomy between purely implicit representations (high expressiveness but weak explicit structure) and purely explicit geometries (clear structure but limited resolution and generalization) in 3D-aware pretraining for embodied perception and manipulation.

Method: Insert a point-wise latent variational autoencoder into the latent space of a point-cloud autoencoder; jointly regularize point-wise features and coordinates toward a Gaussian prior to produce a compact latent that captures coarse structure and semantic cues; use a lightweight 3DGaussian-Splatting (3DGS) rendering pipeline; train end-to-end with standard autoencoder objectives plus VAE regularization; evaluate on RLBench, ManiSkill2, and a real-robot platform; perform ablations.

Result: Consistent improvements in task success, sample efficiency, and robustness to viewpoint and scene variations over strong baselines; ablations verify that each component contributes to performance gains.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid latent representation effectively combines the strengths of implicit expressiveness and explicit structural priors, aided by a lightweight 3DGS renderer, yielding robust, efficient 3D-aware pretraining for embodied tasks across simulated and real-world benchmarks.

Abstract: Current 3D-aware pretraining methods for embodied perception and manipulation are largely built on differentiable rendering frameworks, producing either fully implicit neural fields or fully explicit geometric primitives. Implicit representations, while expressive, lack explicit structural cues, whereas explicit ones preserve geometry but suffer from resolution limits and weak generalization. To address these limitations, we propose a novel pretraining framework that learns a hybrid representation-structural latent points. Specifically, we insert a point-wise latent variational autoencoder into the latent space of a point-cloud autoencoder, jointly regularizing point-wise features and coordinates toward a Gaussian prior. The resulting compact latent preserves coarse structural tendencies, which do not encode precise geometry but capture richer rough shape and semantic information, effectively combining the expressiveness of implicit representations with the structural priors of explicit ones. In addition, informed by shared design choices in prior work, we develop a streamlined, efficient 3DGS-based rendering pipeline that is deliberately kept lightweight, improving efficiency while leaving greater representational capacity to the front-end latent module. Extensive evaluations on RLBench, ManiSkill2, and a real-robot platform demonstrate consistent gains in task success, sample efficiency, and robustness to viewpoint and scene variations over strong baselines. Ablation studies further confirm that each component of our framework is critical to overall performance.

</details>


### [360] [Learning Robust Dexterous In-Hand Manipulation from Joint Sensors with Proprioceptive Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21330)
*Senlan Yao,Chenyu Yang,Jaehoon Kim,Aristotelis Sympetheros,Robert K. Katzschmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proprioceptive Transformer (PT) enables in-hand cube rotation using only joint sensing on a tendon-driven hand, via teacher-student distillation from privileged data; Transformer extracts environmental cues from proprioceptive history, achieving superior speed and cube-position estimation compared to baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate the sufficiency of joint sensing for dexterous manipulation, addressing: (i) whether motor encoders vs direct joint sensing provide better proprioception; (ii) how to infer environment from joint data; (iii) whether joint-only control can approach external-sensor-based performance.

Method: Train a teacher policy with privileged (exteroceptive) information using reinforcement learning; distill into Proprioceptive Transformer that operates solely on joint position/velocity histories; use Transformer to infer object state from temporal joint signals on a tendon-driven hand (ORCA).

Result: On real hardware (ORCA hand): PT achieves 3.1x faster cube rotation than baselines; PT attains 23.4% lower RMSE for cube position estimation than an MLP baseline, signaling improved extraction of exteroceptive info from proprioception.

Conclusion: Joint sensing, when combined with temporal modeling (Transformers) and distillation from privileged data, can enable competitive or superior real-world in-hand manipulation without external perception; this reduces reliance on vision/tactile sensing for certain tasks and demonstrates the value of leveraging readily available joint data.

Abstract: In-hand object manipulation is a fundamental yet challenging capability for dexterous robots. Despite significant progress in dexterous manipulation, existing approaches rely heavily on vision or tactile sensing to track object states, while joint sensing -- the most readily available modality on any robotic hand -- remains largely overlooked, particularly for tendon-driven hands. In this paper, we study how far joint sensing alone can go by asking: (i) whether motor encoders or direct joint sensing provides better proprioceptive feedback, (ii) how to extract environment information from joint measurements, and (iii) whether joint-only control can achieve competitive real-world performance without external perception. We present the Proprioceptive Transformer (PT), an exteroceptive-free approach for continuous cube rotation on a tendon-driven dexterous hand that uses only joint sensing feedback. A teacher policy is first trained via reinforcement learning with privileged object information, then distilled into PT, which operates solely on joint position and velocity histories. The Transformer architecture effectively extracts implicit object state information from temporal patterns in joint sensor readings. Experiments on the real ORCA hand show that our approach achieves 3.1x higher rotation speed than baselines. We also demonstrate that our PT achieves a 23.4% lower RMSE for cube position estimation than the MLP baseline, indicating superior extraction of exteroceptive information from proprioceptive sources.

</details>


### [361] [From swept contact to pose: Probe-aware registration via complementary-shape docking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21398)
*Chen Chen,Yunwen Li,Yifan Xu,Xiangjie Yan,Chang Shu,Jianxia Hou,Shiji Song,Xiang Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Calibration-free registration via complementary-shape docking between the object's geometry and the probe's swept volume. It uses a global 3D FFT-based search over low-discrepancy SO(3) samples followed by continuous SE(3) refinement with Lie-algebra updates and analytic contact sensitivities, achieving metric-grade accuracy without external sensors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optical registration incurs long calibration chains, line-of-sight constraints, and fabrication errors. A calibration-free alternative that leverages both contact and non-contact evidence is desirable for robust robotic manipulation.

Method: Reformulate registration as docking between the object's shape and the probe's swept volume (complementary shapes). Employ a global-to-local approach: 3D FFT correlation over low-discrepancy samples of SO(3) for global pose hypotheses, then continuous SE(3) refinement using Lie algebra updates and analytic contact sensitivities. Integrates contact and non-contact evidence throughout the pipeline.

Result: In simulation on free-form meshes, achieved translation accuracy sub-0.04 mm and rotation accuracy sub-0.4°. Demonstrated robustness to pose noise and contact loss. On a tooth-preparation robot, achieved 0.42 mm translation and 3.75° rotation, outperforming an optical-tracker-based registration while requiring no external sensors.

Conclusion: Presents a practical, precise, calibration-free registration strategy for surgical and industrial robots, avoiding fragile point correspondences and external sensing.

Abstract: Accurate registration between a prior model and the real scene is essential for high-precision robotic manipulation, yet optical methods suffer from long calibration chains, line-of-sight constraints, and fabrication errors. We propose a calibration-free alternative that reformulates contact registration as complementary-shape docking between the object and the probe's swept volume, explicitly accounting for probe geometry and leveraging both contact and non-contact evidence. Our solver integrates a global-to-local search via 3D FFT correlation over low-discrepancy SO(3) samples, then followed by continuous SE(3) refinement using Lie-algebra updates and analytic contact sensitivities. This pipeline yields efficient exploration and metric-grade convergence without fragile point correspondences. Simulation across free-form meshes achieved sub-0.04 mm and sub-0.4° accuracy and robustness to pose noise and contact loss. On a tooth-preparation robot, our method attained 0.42 mm and 3.75°, outperforming an optical tracker registration while requiring no external sensors. These results demonstrate a practical and precise registration strategy for surgical and industrial robots.

</details>


### [362] [MC-Risk: Multi-Component Risk Fields for Risk Identification and Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21406)
*Maximilian Link,Yingjie Xu,Yingbai Hu,Yinlong Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MC-Risk is a modular BEV risk field combining motorized-agent, VRU, and road-penalty components, achieving early, calibrated, and class-aware hazard localization and compatible with MPC planning; first standardized evaluation on RiskBench shows leading performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for interpretable, planner-aligned risk representations that yield early hazard cues and can be integrated with planning without retraining, addressing limitations of isotropic or opaque risk models.

Method: Three components: (i) motorized-agent field fusing a black-box multimodal trajectory predictor with an analytic Gaussian-torus that expands laterally with speed/curvature and attenuates with look-ahead; (ii) VRU risk field using forward-biased anisotropic kernel aligned to heading and speed; (iii) road penalty field leveraging HD-map topology to impose off-road penalties and lane-aware risk exposure for same/opposite directions. Evaluation on RiskBench's collision subset; plug-and-play MPC interface demonstrated by using the field as an MPC cost density for risk-aware trajectory generation.

Result: MC-Risk attains the best overall risk localization and the earliest hazard indication on RiskBench. It also enables risk-aware trajectory generation via an MPC cost density without additional training.

Conclusion: MC-Risk provides an interpretable, modular risk field that aligns with planning objectives and can be integrated into MPC-based planners, achieving strong risk localization and early hazard signaling; standard evaluation on RiskBench supports its efficacy.

Abstract: We present MC-Risk, a planner-aligned, multi-component risk field on a bird's-eye-view grid that yields early, calibrated, and class-aware risk localization. MC-Risk linearly composes three interpretable modules: (i) a motorized-agent field that fuses a black-box multimodal trajectory predictor with an analytic Gaussian-torus construction whose lateral width grows with speed/curvature and whose height attenuates with look-ahead; (ii) a VRU risk field that replaces isotropic pedestrian blobs with a forward-biased anisotropic kernel aligned to heading and speed; and (iii) a road penalty field that exploits full HD-map topology, imposing an off-road penalty and lane-aware risk exposure for same/opposite directions. We conduct, to our knowledge, the first standardized quantitative evaluation of a risk-field formulation on RiskBench's collision subset. MC-Risk attains the best overall risk localization and the earliest hazard indication. Finally, we demonstrate a plug-and-play planning interface by using the field as an MPC cost density, enabling risk-aware trajectory generation without additional training.

</details>


### [363] [PointACT: Vision-Language-Action Models with Multi-Scale Point-Action Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21414)
*Shizhe Chen,Paul Pacaud,Cordelia Schmid*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PointACT is a 3D-aware VLA policy that fuses hierarchical 3D point cloud features with 2D vision-language backbones via a multi-scale point-action interaction using bottleneck window self-attention, yielding consistent gains on LIBERO and RLBench, especially when 2D backbones are frozen or action policies are trained from scratch.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models rely heavily on 2D visual representations, which struggle with precise 3D geometry and spatial grounding needed for manipulation in real environments. Integrating 3D geometry into the action decoding process could improve robustness and accuracy.

Method: Introduce PointACT, a dual-system policy that integrates hierarchical 3D point cloud representations into action decoding. Use multi-scale point–action interaction with efficient bottleneck window self-attention to allow evolving action tokens to attend to local geometry and global scene structure. Evaluate on LIBERO and RLBench against monolithic and dual-system baselines, including point-cloud-augmented variants; perform extensive ablations.

Result: PointACT achieves consistent improvements across LIBERO and RLBench, including ~+10% success rate on RLBench-10Tasks over state-of-the-art pretrained VLAs. Gains are larger when the VLA backbone is frozen and the action expert is trained from scratch. Ablations indicate that tightly coupling hierarchical 3D geometry with pretrained 2D representations is crucial for robust, spatially grounded control.

Conclusion: Coupling hierarchical 3D geometry with 2D semantic representations is essential for robust, spatially grounded 3D manipulation. The work demonstrates the value of pretrained 3D representations for 3D-aware VLA policies and points to a promising direction for future VLA designs.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for general-purpose robotic manipulation by leveraging large pretrained vision-language backbones. However, most existing VLAs rely primarily on 2D visual representations, which limit their ability to reason about fine-grained geometry and spatial grounding - capabilities that are essential for precise and robust manipulation in 3D environments. In this paper, we propose PointACT, a dual-system 3D-aware VLA policy that integrates hierarchical 3D point cloud representations directly into the action decoding process. PointACT employs a multi-scale point-action interaction mechanism with efficient bottleneck window self-attention, enabling evolving action tokens to densely attend to both local geometric detail and global scene structure. We evaluate PointACT on the LIBERO and RLBench benchmarks and systematically compare it against monolithic and dual-system VLA baselines, including variants augmented with point cloud inputs. PointACT achieves consistent improvements across both benchmarks, increasing success rates by 10% on the challenging RLBench-10Tasks suite over state-of-the-art pretrained VLAs, with even larger gains when the vision-language backbone is frozen and the action expert is trained from scratch. Extensive ablation studies demonstrate that tightly coupling hierarchical 3D geometry with pretrained 2D semantic representations is critical for robust and spatially grounded robot control. Our results also highlight the promise of pretrained 3D representations for 3D-aware VLA policies.

</details>


### [364] [roto 2.0: The Robot Tactile Olympiad](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21429)
*Elle Miller,Jayaram Reddy,Ayush Deshmukh,Trevor McInroe,David Abel,Oisin Mac Aodha,Sethu Vijayakumar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces roto 2.0, a GPU-parallelised tactile RL benchmark spanning 4 robotic morphologies (16–24 DOF) for end-to-end blind manipulation using only proprioception and tactile sensing. Reports a large performance boost (13 Baoding ball rotations in 10 s) over previous methods and open-sources environments to lower barriers to entry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fragmented, orientation-centric tactile RL research lacks a standard, challenging benchmark that supports end-to-end blind control across diverse morphologies; need to unify evaluation and reduce tuning effort.

Method: GPU-accelerated simulation environments (roto 2.0) covering four morphologies; end-to-end blind RL with only proprioceptive and tactile inputs, no state information or distillation; benchmarking and baselines are open-sourced.

Result: Blind agents achieve 13 Baoding ball rotations in 10 seconds, purportedly an order-of-magnitude faster than state-of-the-art; robust baselines and open-source environments to facilitate rapid experimentation.

Conclusion: Roto 2.0 provides a standard, accessible platform to advance tactile-based RL by focusing on core algorithmic challenges rather than extensive tuning; broadens applicability across multiple morphologies.

Abstract: Tactile-based reinforcement learning (RL) is currently hindered by fragmented research and a focus on over-saturated orientation tasks. We introduce v2 of the Robot Tactile Olympiad (\texttt{roto 2.0}), a GPU-parallelised benchmark designed to standardise tactile-based RL across four distinct robotic morphologies (16-DOF to 24-DOF). Unlike prior benchmarks, roto focuses on end-to-end "blind" manipulation, utilising only proprioception and tactile sensing without state information or distillation. We demonstrate a significant performance leap, with our blind agents achieving 13 Baoding ball rotations in 10 seconds, an order of magnitude faster than current state-of-the-art speeds. By open-sourcing our environments and robustly tuned baselines, we reduce the barrier to entry and enable researchers to prioritise fundamental algorithmic challenges over tedious RL tuning. Website: https://elle-miller.github.io/roto/

</details>


### [365] [Lost in Fog: Sensor Perturbations Expose Reasoning Fragility in Driving VLAs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21446)
*Abhinaw Priyadershi,Jelena Frtunikj*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Consistency of Chain-of-Causation explanations under perturbations strongly predicts trajectory safety in vision-language-action autonomous driving; enabling CoC improves accuracy; perturbations yield near-linear degradation; suggests runtime monitoring of CoC for safer VLA deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The work addresses reliability of interpretable autonomous driving planners: explanations must remain trustworthy under real-world sensor degradation to support safe planning.

Method: A controlled perturbation study evaluating Alpamayo R1 (10B parameters) across 1,996 scenarios with eight sensor perturbations (Gaussian noise at four levels, two lighting extremes, two fog levels) totaling ~18,000 inference trials. Measured how Chain-of-Causation (CoC) explanations change under perturbation and how trajectory deviation correlates; performed a controlled ablation to assess the impact of enabling CoC generation on trajectory accuracy under matched inference settings.

Result: Trajectory deviation spikes by ~5.3× when CoC explanations change (21.8m vs 4.1m); correlation with perturbations is high (r=0.99 across attack types; r_pb=0.53 per sample); enabling CoC yields 11.8% improvement in trajectory accuracy (p<0.0001); degradation due to noise is approximately linear (R^2=0.957) across σ∈{10,30,50,70}; standard input preprocessing defenses provide only marginal relief.

Conclusion: CoC consistency is a quantitative proxy for planning safety and supports reasoning-based runtime monitoring to enable safer VLA deployment.

Abstract: Interpretable autonomous driving planners depend not only on generating explanations, but also on those explanations remaining reliable under real-world sensor degradation. In this paper we present a controlled perturbation study of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) robustness in autonomous driving, evaluating Alpamayo R1 (10B parameters) across 1,996 scenarios under eight sensor perturbations (Gaussian noise at four intensities, two lighting extremes, and two fog levels; ${\sim}18{,}000$ inference trials). We find that reasoning consistency is a high-fidelity indicator of trajectory reliability: when Chain-of-Causation (CoC) explanations change after perturbation, trajectory deviation spikes $5.3{\times}$ (21.8m vs 4.1m), with $r\!=\!0.99$ across attack types and $r_{pb}\!=\!0.53$ per-sample (Cohen's $d\!=\!1.12$). A controlled ablation provides evidence that enabling CoC generation is associated with improved trajectory accuracy (11.8% on average across conditions; $p < 0.0001$) under matched inference settings. Over the tested noise range ($σ\in \{10, 30, 50, 70\}$), degradation is approximately linear ($R^2\!=\!0.957$), while standard input preprocessing defenses provide only marginal relief. Together, these results establish CoC consistency as a quantitative proxy for planning safety and motivate reasoning-based runtime monitoring for safer VLA deployment.

</details>


### [366] [HITL-D: Human In The Loop Diffusion Assisted Shared Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21460)
*Riley Zilka,Sergey Khlynovskiy,Allie Wang,Martin Jagersand*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HITL-D merges diffusion-based autonomous policies with human-in-the-loop control to improve teleoperation performance and reduce workload in multi-step manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limited exploration of integrating human expertise with diffusion-based policies in shared control, aiming to reduce mental workload and improve performance.

Method: A shared-control framework (HITL-D) that updates end-effector orientation autonomously conditioned on a scene point cloud and the end-effector's Cartesian position, reducing joystick axes; combines diffusion-based policies with human input; evaluated in a multi-task user study (12 participants).

Result: In a multi-task user study, HITL-D reduced average task completion times by 40%, decreased perceived workload by 37%, and improved Likert-scale ratings for independence, intuitiveness, and confidence compared to traditional teleoperation.

Conclusion: HITL-D effectively integrates human expertise with autonomous diffusion assistance, improving both objective performance and subjective user experience in teleoperation; shows promise for generalization to insertion and fine manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Autonomous manipulation systems have achieved remarkable capabilities, yet the integration of human expertise with diffusion-based policies in shared control remains relatively unexplored. In this paper, we propose Human-In-The-Loop Diffusion (HITL-D), a shared control framework that enhances user performance in multi-step, insertion, and fine manipulation tasks. HITL-D leverages a novel combination of diffusion-based policies and human control to provide autonomous end effector orientation updates conditioned on a scene point cloud and the Cartesian position of the end effector. This approach reduces the number of joystick control axes required, thereby lowering mental workload. In a multi-task user study with 12 participants, HITL-D reduced average task completion times by 40%, decreased perceived workload by 37%, and improved Likert-scale ratings for independence, intuitiveness, and confidence compared to traditional teleoperation methods. These results demonstrate that HITL-D effectively integrates human expertise with autonomous assistance, improving both objective and subjective aspects of teleoperation.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [367] [SOLAR: A Self-Optimizing Open-Ended Autonomous Agent for Lifelong Learning and Continual Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20189)
*Nitin Vetcha,Dianbo Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SOLAR introduces a parameter-level meta-learning autonomous agent that self-improves to enable durable, test-time adaptation of LLMs under concept drift, using an evolving episodic memory of modification strategies; demonstrates stronger performance across diverse domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses concept drift and high cost of gradient-based adaptation in dynamic, non-stationary data streams. Aims to prevent catastrophic forgetting and reduce manual data curation by enabling autonomous lifelong adaptation.

Method: SOLAR is an open-ended autonomous agent that uses parameter-level meta-learning, treating model weights as an environment for exploration. It starts with a strong prior over common-sense knowledge for transfer learning, and employs multi-level reinforcement learning to autonomously discover adaptation strategies. It maintains an evolving knowledge base of valid modification strategies, acting as episodic memory to balance plasticity and stability.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows SOLAR outperforms strong baselines on a range of tasks requiring reasoning (common-sense, mathematical, medical, coding, social, logical).

Conclusion: Represents a significant step toward autonomous agents capable of lifelong adaptation in evolving environments, by coupling self-improvement, memory-guided plasticity-stability, and test-time adaptation.

Abstract: Despite the remarkable success of large language models (LLMs), they still face bottlenecks while deploying in dynamic, real-world settings with primary challenges being concept drift and the high cost of gradient-based adaptation. Traditional fine-tuning (FT) struggles to adapt to non-stationary data streams without resulting in catastrophic for getting or requiring extensive manual data curation. To address these limitations within the streaming and continual learning paradigm, we propose the Self-Optimizing Lifelong Autonomous Reasoner (SOLAR) which is an open-ended autonomous agent that leverages parameter-level meta-learning to self-improve, treating model weights as an environment for exploration. It initiates the process by consolidating a strong prior over common-sense knowledge making it effective for transfer-learning. By utilizing a multi-level reinforcement learning approach, SOLAR autonomously discovers adaptation strategies, enabling efficient test-time adaptation to unseen domains. Crucially, SOLAR maintains an evolving knowledge base of valid modification strategies, implicitly acting as an episodic memory buffer to balance plasticity (adaptation to new tasks) and stability (retention of meta-knowledge). Experiments demonstrate that SOLAR outperforms strong baselines on common-sense, mathematical, medical, coding, social and logical reasoning tasks, marking a significant step toward autonomous agents capable of lifelong adaptation in evolving environments.

</details>


### [368] [OSCToM: RL-Guided Adversarial Generation for High-Order Theory of Mind](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20423)
*Sharmin Sultana Srishty,Kazi Mahathir Rahman,Malaika Parizat Sakkhi,Samia Shahid Prianna,Shaikhul Islam Sinat*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OSCToM introduces an observer-self conflict Theory of Mind for LLMs, combining RL, an expanded DSL, and compositional surrogate models to generate nested belief conflicts. An 8B model (OSCToM-8B) yields strong results, notably 76% on FANToM vs 0.2% for ExploreToM, with improved data efficiency (~6x) and competitive performance on Hi-ToM and BigToM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address gaps in current ToM benchmarks that lack recursive beliefs and information asymmetries, particularly observer conflicts with their own beliefs. Enable smaller LMs to handle advanced cognitive reasoning through targeted data synthesis.

Method: Integrates reinforcement learning, an extended domain-specific language, and compositional surrogate models to synthesize observer-self conflicts, producing nested belief scenarios. Uses OSCToM-8B as the primary model; data-synthesis pipeline is designed for efficiency.

Result: OSCToM-8B achieves top performance among tested systems, outperforming ExploreToM on FANToM (76% vs 0.2%), and remains competitive on Hi-ToM and BigToM. Data-synthesis is ~6x more efficient than prior approaches.

Conclusion: Targeted data generation and modular modeling enable smaller LLMs to perform advanced recursive Theory of Mind tasks, advancing cognitive reasoning capabilities and efficiency. Code available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) perform well on many language tasks, but their Theory of Mind (ToM) reasoning is still uneven in complex social settings. Existing benchmarks, including ExploreToM, do not always test the recursive beliefs and information asymmetries that make these settings difficult. This paper presents OSCToM (Observer-Self Conflict Theory of Mind), an approach for modeling nested belief conflicts in LLM-based ToM tasks. The key case is one in which an observer's view of another agent conflicts with the observer's own belief state. Such cases go beyond simple perspective-taking and require recursive, multi-layered reasoning. OSCToM combines reinforcement learning (RL), an extended domain-specific language, and compositional surrogate models to generate observer-self conflicts. In our experiments, OSCToM-8B gives the best overall result among the systems tested. It improves on the reported ExploreToM results on FANToM and remains competitive on Hi-ToM and BigToM. On the information-asymmetric FANToM benchmark, OSCToM reaches 76% accuracy, compared with the 0.2% reported by ExploreToM. The data-synthesis procedure is also 6x more efficient, indicating that targeted training data can help smaller models handle advanced cognitive reasoning. The project code is available at https://github.com/sharminsrishty/osct.

</details>


### [369] [AgentCo-op: Retrieval-Based Synthesis of Interoperable Multi-Agent Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20425)
*Shuaike Shen,Wenduo Cheng,Shike Wang,Mingqian Ma,Jian Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentCo-op is a retrieval-based synthesis framework that composes reusable skills, tools, and external agents into executable workflows with typed artifact handoffs, plus bounded self-guided local repair; it demonstrates open-world genomics workflows and strong benchmark performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-ended scientific settings lack curated training data, reliable evaluation metrics, and standardized interfaces between tools and agents, making end-to-end automated workflow design difficult without exhaustive global topology search.

Method: Retriever-based composition of independently developed agents, tools, and typed artifacts; executable workflows with typed artifact handoffs; bounded self-guided local repair informed by execution evidence; ability to import searched workflows as structural priors and ground nodes with retrieved components; auditable workflows without redesign; avoidance of global topology search.

Result: Two open-world genomics case studies where AgentCo-op coordinates specialized agents for spatial transcriptomics and gene-set interpretation, and builds a parallel workflow for cross-modality marker analysis on single-cell multiome data; can improve imported workflows by grounding and local repair; on six coding/math/Q&A benchmarks it attains best results on four benchmarks and best average score under a unified backbone, with reduced per-task cost compared to multi-agent baselines.

Conclusion: Retrieval-based synthesis extends automated agentic workflow design beyond benchmark-optimized graphs to open-world workflows built from existing agents, tools, and typed artifacts; synthesis and search are complementary.

Abstract: Designing multi-agent workflows is especially difficult in open-ended scientific settings where tasks lack curated training sets, reliable scalar evaluation metrics, and standardized interfaces between existing tools and agents. We propose AgentCo-op, a retrieval-based synthesis framework that composes reusable skills, tools, and external agents into executable workflows through typed artifact handoffs, then applies bounded self-guided local repair to implicated components when execution evidence indicates failure. In two open-world genomics case studies, AgentCo-op composes independently developed scientific agents and external tool repositories into auditable workflows without redesigning them or running global topology search. It coordinates specialized agents for spatial transcriptomics and gene-set interpretation to enable collaborative discovery from spatial transcriptomics data, and builds a parallel workflow for cross-modality marker analysis on single-cell multiome data. AgentCo-op can also import a searched workflow as a structural prior and improve it by grounding nodes with retrieved components and applying local repair, showing that synthesis and search are complementary. On six coding, math, and question-answering benchmarks, AgentCo-op achieves the best result on four benchmarks and the best average score under a unified backbone setting, while consistently reducing per-task cost relative to multi-agent baselines. Together, these results suggest that retrieval-based synthesis can extend automated agentic workflow design beyond benchmark-optimized agent graphs to open-world workflows built from existing agents, tools, and typed artifacts.

</details>


### [370] [High Quality Embeddings for Horn Logic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20467)
*Yifan Zhang,Yasir White,Dean Clark,Joseph Sanchez,Jevon Lipsey,Ashely Hirst,Jeff Heflin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Triplet-loss embeddings for logical reasoning statements, with anchor enrichment, balanced positive/negative sampling, and hard-example mining; evaluated across KBs to link embedding properties with reasoning task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve downstream reasoning and search efficiency by learning embeddings that capture logical structure and relational semantics.

Method: Train embeddings using triplet loss guided by three strategies: (1) anchors more likely to repeat terms, (2) balanced easy/medium/hard positives/negatives, (3) periodic emphasis on hard examples during training.

Result: Empirical evaluation across multiple knowledge bases shows that the proposed sampling and hard-mining approaches affect embedding quality and that embedding characteristics correlate with task suitability; some improvements in downstream ranking tasks are observed.

Conclusion: Embedding properties matter for task-specific reasoning; the proposed strategies provide practical guidance for designing triplet-based embeddings for logical reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Neural networks can be trained to rank the choices made by logical reasoners, resulting in more efficient searches for answers. A key step in this process is creating useful embeddings, i.e., numeric representations of logical statements. This paper introduces and evaluates several approaches to creating embeddings that result in better downstream results. We train embeddings using triplet loss, which requires examples consisting of an anchor, a positive example, and a negative example. We introduce three ideas: generating anchors that are more likely to have repeated terms, generating positive and negative examples in a way that ensures a good balance between easy, medium, and hard examples, and periodically emphasizing the hardest examples during training. We conduct several experiments to evaluate this approach, including a comparison of different embeddings across different knowledge bases, in an attempt to identify what characteristics make an embedding well-suited to a particular reasoning task.

</details>


### [371] [\ECUAS{n}: A family of metrics for principled evaluation of uncertainty-augmented systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20490)
*Lautaro Estienne,Erik Ernst,Matías Vera,Pablo Piantanida,Luciana Ferrer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes ECUAS_n, a family of proper scoring rules for evaluating uncertainty-augmented (UA) systems; the parameter n tunes the balance between incorrect-prediction costs and uncertain predictions; demonstrated theoretical and empirical benefits on classification and generation tasks, including TriviaQA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CurrentUA evaluations separate prediction accuracy and uncertainty scores or rely on fixed rejection costs/coverage-risk curves, which inadequately reflect decision-making under uncertainty. There is a need for integrated, principled metrics that align with use-case costs.

Method: Introduce ECUAS_n as a parametric proper scoring rule that jointly evaluates predictions and predictive uncertainty; the parameter n controls the trade-off between misclassification costs and uncertainty quality. Provide theoretical properties (e.g., propriety, calibration implications) and empirical validation across diverse datasets, including generation tasks and a TriviaQA subset.

Result: ECUAS_n metrics offer a principled, decision-aware evaluation that outperforms traditional separate metrics and fixed-cost schemes in both theory and practice; flexible n allows tailoring to application-specific cost structures; demonstrated across classification and generation tasks.

Conclusion: ECUAS_n provide a unified framework for evaluating UA systems, enabling better decision-making guidance by incorporating uncertainty into the scoring rule; the approach generalizes across tasks and supports customized trade-offs via n.

Abstract: In high-stakes automated decision-making, access to predictive uncertainty is essential for enabling users -- human or downstream systems -- to accept or reject predictions based on application-specific cost trade-offs. Such uncertainty-augmented (UA) systems -- i.e., systems that output both predictions and uncertainty scores -- are currently being assessed in the literature in a variety of ways, using separate metrics to evaluate the predictions and the uncertainty scores, setting a cost function with a fixed rejection cost or integrating over a coverage-risk curve. We argue that these evaluation approaches are inadequate for assessing overall performance of the UA system for decision making under uncertainty and propose a novel family of metrics, \ECUAS{n}, formulated as proper scoring rules for the task of interest. The parameter $n$ controls the trade-off between the cost of incorrect predictions and imperfect uncertainties depending on the needs of the use-case. We demonstrate the advantages of the \ECUAS{n} metrics both theoretically and empirically, through experiments on diverse classification and generation datasets, including a manually annotated subset of TriviaQA.

</details>


### [372] [Open-World Evaluations for Measuring Frontier AI Capabilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20520)
*Sayash Kapoor,Peter Kirgis,Andrew Schwartz,Stephan Rabanser,J. J. Allaire,Rishi Bommasani,Harry Coppock,Magda Dubois,Gillian K Hadfield,Andrew B. Hall,Sara Hooker,Seth Lazar,Steve Newman,Dimitris Papailiopoulos,Shoshannah Tekofsky,Helen Toner,Cozmin Ududec,Arvind Narayanan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Open-world evaluations complement benchmarks by testing long-horizon, real-world tasks through qualitative analysis; CRUX is proposed to conduct such evaluations regularly, with a pilot task of developing and publishing a simple iOS app showing potential early warning of capabilities proceeding to widespread use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark tests can misstate deployed capabilities by privileging tasks that are easy to specify, grade, optimize, and run cheaply. Open-world evaluations aim to capture messy, real-world performance over longer horizons using small-sample qualitative assessments.

Method: Survey recent open-world evaluations to identify strengths and limitations; introduce CRUX (Collaborative Research for Updating AI eXpectations) as a framework for ongoing evaluations; implement a first instance where an AI agent develops and publishes a simple iOS app on the App Store, with only a single avoidable manual intervention.

Result: The agent completed the task with only one avoidable manual intervention, suggesting open-world evaluations can provide early warning of capabilities likely to become widespread and that open-world methods can detect capabilities not easily captured by benchmarks.

Conclusion: Recommend design and reporting practices for open-world evaluations and position CRUX as an ongoing project for regular, real-world capability assessment.

Abstract: Benchmark-based evaluation remains important for tracking frontier AI progress. But it can both overstate and understate deployed capability because it privileges tasks that can be precisely specified, automatically graded, easy to optimize for, and run with low budgets and short time horizons. We advocate for a complementary class of evaluations, which we term open-world evaluations: long-horizon, messy, real-world tasks assessed through small-sample qualitative analysis rather than benchmark-scale automation. In this paper we survey recent open-world evaluations, identify their strengths and limitations, and introduce CRUX (Collaborative Research for Updating AI eXpectations), a project for conducting such evaluations regularly. As a first instance, we task an AI agent with developing and publishing a simple iOS application to the Apple App Store. The agent completed the task with only a single avoidable manual intervention, suggesting that open-world evaluations can provide early warning of capabilities that may soon become widespread. We conclude with recommendations for designing and reporting open-world evals.

</details>


### [373] [AgentAtlas: Beyond Outcome Leaderboards for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20530)
*Parsa Mazaheri,Kasra Mazaheri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentAtlas proposes a multi-dimensional evaluation framework for deployable LLM agents, combining six-state control decisions, a nine-category trajectory-failure taxonomy with dual labels, a taxonomy-aware vs taxonomy-blind methodology, and a benchmark-coverage audit. A synthetic eight-model run demonstrates the impact of prompt structure on trajectory accuracy and shows no single model dominates across control, diagnosis, and tool-context metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent benchmarks are fragmented and rely on a single accuracy metric, which inadequately captures deployable reliability across control, planning, and tool-use. There is a need for a unified, taxonomy-aware evaluation protocol that exposes how much of observed capability comes from supervision rather than genuine capability.

Method: Introduce four components: (i) six-state control-decision taxonomy (Act/Ask/Refuse/Stop/Confirm/Recover); (ii) nine-category trajectory-failure taxonomy with two orthogonal labels (primary_error_source, impact); (iii) taxonomy-aware vs taxonomy-blind evaluation to separate supervision from model capability; (iv) benchmark-coverage audit mapping 15 benchmarks to 6 behavioral axes. Demonstrate via a small fixed eight-model synthetic run (1,342 items; 4 frontier closed/open-weight) under both prompt modes; assess impact of removing explicit label menu on trajectory accuracy.

Result: With label menu removed, trajectory accuracy drops by 14–40 percentage points, yielding a floor of 0.54–0.62 across model families; no model dominates across control accuracy, trajectory diagnosis, and tool-context utility retention.

Conclusion: The work serves as a measurement-protocol demonstration rather than a benchmark release, illustrating the value of multi-axis, taxonomy-aware evaluation for deployable agents and laying groundwork for more standardized benchmarking across agent behaviors.

Abstract: Large language model agents now act on codebases, browsers, operating systems, calendars, files, and tool ecosystems, but the benchmarks used to evaluate them are fragmented: each emphasizes a different unit of measurement (final task success, tool-call validity, repeated-pass consistency, trajectory safety, or attack robustness). A line of 2024-2025 work has converged on the diagnosis that a single accuracy column is no longer the right unit of comparison for deployable agents. AgentAtlas extends this line of work with four components: (i) a six-state control-decision taxonomy (Act / Ask / Refuse / Stop / Confirm / Recover); (ii) a nine-category trajectory-failure taxonomy with two orthogonal hierarchical labels (primary_error_source, impact); (iii) a taxonomy-aware vs. taxonomy-blind methodology that measures how much of a model's apparent capability comes from the supervision in the prompt; and (iv) a benchmark-coverage audit mapping fifteen agent benchmarks against six behavioral axes. To demonstrate the methodology we run a small fixed eight-model set (1,342 generated items, four frontier closed and four open-weight) under both prompt modes. Removing the explicit label menu drops every model's trajectory accuracy by 14-40 pp to a tight 0.54-0.62 floor regardless of family, and no single model wins on all three of control accuracy, trajectory diagnosis, and tool-context utility retention. We treat the synthetic run as a measurement-protocol demonstration, not a benchmark release.

</details>


### [374] [Personality Engineering with AI Agents: A New Methodology for Negotiation Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20554)
*Michelle A. Vaccaro,Jared R. Curhan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces 'personality engineering' to design and test AI negotiation agents via the interpersonal circumplex (warmth and dominance).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses persistent tensions in negotiation (empathizing vs asserting) and the lack of controlled test environments; argues AI agents enable precise, scalable experiments to test theory.

Method: Proposes a methodology to parameterize, manipulate, and evaluate negotiator personality in AI agents; uses the interpersonal circumplex as core coordinate system.

Result: Conceptual framework with theoretical grounding; no empirical results reported in abstract; provides a protocol and design guidance.

Conclusion: Offers a rigorous methodology for testing classic negotiation theories and a practical guide for configuring AI agent personalities.

Abstract: According to canonical negotiation theory, people's success in a negotiation depends on how well they balance competing demands--empathizing and asserting, demonstrating concern for other and concern for self, being soft on the people and hard on the problem. Yet people struggle to manage these tensions, so researchers have lacked the ability to rigorously test the field's prescriptions under controlled conditions. AI agents do not face the same limitations, and their precision, repertoire, consistency, and scalability enable a new class of experiments to contribute to negotiation theory. In this article, we introduce personality engineering: a methodology that uses AI agents to precisely parameterize, manipulate, and evaluate negotiator personality. We propose using the interpersonal circumplex--and its two core dimensions of warmth and dominance--as a foundational coordinate system for the field. This approach offers both a rigorous methodology for testing classic negotiation theories and a practical guide for designing the personalities of AI negotiation agents.

</details>


### [375] [Mahjax: A GPU-Accelerated Mahjong Simulator for Reinforcement Learning in JAX](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20577)
*Soichiro Nishimori,Shinri Okano,Keigo Habara,Sotetsu Koyamada,Eason Yu,Masashi Sugiyama*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A scalable, GPU-accelerated, vectorized Riichi Mahjong environment (Mahjax) in JAX enabling large-scale tabula-rasa RL with visualization, achieving very high throughput and enabling RL experiments to train agents against baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Riichi Mahjong presents multi-agent, imperfect-information, stochastic, high-dimensional RL challenges. Existing work relies on supervised pre-training from human logs; a fully tabula-rasa, scalable environment is needed to explore general RL methods (e.g., AlphaZero-style).

Method: A fully vectorized Riichi Mahjong environment implemented in JAX, Mahjax, designed for large-scale rollout parallelization on GPUs. Includes a high-quality visualization tool for debugging and interaction with trained agents. Evaluation uses two rule variants (no-red and red) and demonstrates hardware-accelerated throughput on eight NVIDIA A100 GPUs.

Result: Throughputs up to 2 million (no-red) and 1 million (red) steps per second on eight A100 GPUs. Demonstrates the environment’s utility for RL by training agents to improve rank against baseline policies.

Conclusion: Mahjax enables scalable tabula-rasa RL research in Riichi Mahjong, providing a high-performance, GPU-accelerated, fully vectorized environment with debugging visualizations, suitable for large-scale experiments and AlphaZero-like RL approaches.

Abstract: Riichi Mahjong is a multi-player, imperfect-information game characterized by stochasticity and high-dimensional state spaces. These attributes present a unique combination of challenges that mirror complex real-world decision-making problems in reinforcement learning. While prior research has heavily relied on supervised learning from human play logs to pre-train the policy, algorithms capable of learning \textit{tabula rasa} (from scratch) offer greater potential for general applicability, as evidenced by the AlphaZero lineage. To facilitate such research, we introduce \textbf{Mahjax}, a fully vectorized Riichi Mahjong environment implemented in JAX to enable large-scale rollout parallelization on Graphics Processing Units (GPUs). We also provide a high-quality visualization tool to streamline debugging and interaction with trained agents. Experimental results demonstrate that Mahjax achieves throughputs of up to \textbf{2 million} and \textbf{1 million steps per second} on eight NVIDIA A100 GPUs under the no-red and red rules, respectively. Furthermore, we validate the environment's utility for reinforcement learning by showing that agents can be trained effectively to improve their rank against baseline policies.

</details>


### [376] [From Automated to Autonomous: Hierarchical Agent-native Network Architecture (HANA)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20608)
*Binghan Wu,Shoufeng Wang,Yunxin Liu,Ya-Qin Zhang,Joseph Sifakis,Ye Ouyang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A hierarchical multi-agent reference architecture for Level 4/5 Autonomous Networks using a Dual-Driven Orchestrator, Executive Agents, and Public Memory; introduces agent self-awareness to combine strategic governance with fault recovery; validated in a 5G Core, achieving throughput under congestion and MTTR reduction of 86%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to move beyond rigid scripts and impart cognitive agency enabling robust operation under off-nominal conditions in autonomous networks.

Method: design of architecture components, self-aware agent coordination, and validation through case studies in a 5G Core environment.

Result: maintains critical throughput during congestion; MTTR reduced by 86%; demonstrates integration of strategic planning with operational resilience.

Conclusion: the approach shows promise for realizing higher levels of autonomy in networks and unified resilience through self-aware multi-agent orchestration.

Abstract: Realizing Level 4/5 Autonomous Networks (AN) demands a shift from static automation to agent-native intelligence. Current operations, reliant on rigid scripts, lack the cognitive agency to handle off-nominal conditions. To address this, this letter proposes a hierarchical multi-agent reference architecture enabling high-level autonomy. The framework features a Dual-Driven Orchestrator that coordinates specialized Executive Agents, supported by a shared Public Memory for unified domain knowledge. A key innovation is the integration of agent self-awareness, which empowers the system to harmonize deliberative strategic governance with reflexive fault recovery. We instantiate and validate this architecture within a 5G Core environment. Case studies demonstrate that the system sustains critical throughput under congestion and reduces Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) by 86%, confirming its efficacy in unifying strategic planning with operational resilience.

</details>


### [377] [COAgents: Multi-Agent Framework to Learn and Navigate Routing Problems Search Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20618)
*Oleksandr Yakovenko,Mahdi Mostajabdaveh,Cheikh Ahmed,Abdullah Ali Sivas,Xiaorui Li,Zirui Zhou,Mao Kun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: COAgents: a cooperative multi-agent search framework for VRP using a dynamic Partial Search Graph, with Node/Move/Jump agents guiding intensification and diversification; achieves strong results, especially on VRPTW, and code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VRPs are computationally intractable at scale; handcrafted heuristics struggle to generalize; end-to-end learn-to-search approaches may lack modular flexibility. A modular, problem-agnostic search-control framework could generalize better and adapt across tasks.

Method: Construct a Partial Search Graph dynamically during search where nodes are solutions and edges are either local refinements or large perturbations (jumps). Train a Node Selection Agent and a Move Selection Agent to guide intensification, and a Jump Agent to trigger diversified exploration. The framework separates problem-agnostic search control from compact, domain-specific encoding, enabling adaptability.

Result: On CVRP, COAgents is competitive with several learn-to-search baselines; on VRPTW, it achieves a new state-of-the-art among learning-based methods, reducing the gap to best-known solutions by 14% (N=100) and 44% (N=50) relative to POMO, and by 21% (N=100) and 40% (N=50) relative to ALNS.

Conclusion: Modular, graph-based search with specialized agents enables strong performance and adaptability across VRP variants, without sacrificing generality; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Although Vehicle Routing Problems (VRP) are essential to many real-world systems, they remain computationally intractable at scale due to their combinatorial complexity. Traditional heuristics rely on handcrafted rules for local improvements and occasional \textit{jumps} to escape local minima, but often struggle to generalize across diverse instances. We introduce \textbf{COAgents}, a cooperative multi-agent framework that models the search process as a graph: nodes represent solutions, and edges correspond to either local refinements or large perturbations for diversification (i.e., jumps). A \textit{Partial Search Graph} (PSG) is dynamically constructed during search, enabling COAgents to train a Node Selection Agent and a Move Selection Agent to guide intensification, and a Jump Agent to trigger well-timed explorations of new regions. Unlike end-to-end learning approaches, COAgents cleanly separates problem-agnostic search control from compact domain-specific encoding, facilitating adaptability across tasks. Extensive experiments on the CVRP and VRPTW benchmarks show that COAgents remains competitive with several learn-to-search baselines on CVRP and sets a new state of the art among learning-based methods on the more challenging VRPTW instances, reducing the gap to the best-known solutions by 14\% at $N\!=\!100$ and 44\% at $N\!=\!50$ relative to the strongest neural solver (POMO), and by 21\% and 40\% respectively relative to ALNS.
  Code is available at https://github.com/mahdims/COAgents.

</details>


### [378] [Evaluating Temporal Semantic Caching and Workflow Optimization in Agentic Plan-Execute Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20630)
*Alimurtaza Mustafa Merchant,Krish Veera,Sajal Kumar Goyla,Shambhawi Bhure,Dhaval Patel,Kaoutar El Maghraoui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes temporal semantic cache and MCP workflow optimizations for AssetOpsBench plan-execute pipelines; achieves substantial speedups; reveals correctness risks of semantic caching for parameter-rich industrial queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latency-sensitive industrial asset operations; existing LLM caching methods (KV-cache, embeddings) break when output validity depends on time, asset, or sensor parameters.

Method: Two optimization layers: (1) temporal semantic cache; (2) MCP workflow optimizations including disk-backed tool-discovery caching and dependency-aware parallel step execution.

Result: 1.67x speedup and ~40% median end-to-end latency reduction; temporal cache yields median 30.6x speedup on cache hits.

Conclusion: Highlights a concrete failure mode of pure semantic caching for parameter-rich industrial queries; caching choices can affect evaluation correctness in MCP-backed agent benchmarks; informs robust design of industrial agent caching.

Abstract: Industrial asset operations workflows are latency-sensitive because a single user query may require coordination over sensor data, work orders, failure modes, forecasting tools, and domain-specific agents. We evaluate this problem on AssetOpsBench (AOB), an industrial agent benchmark whose plan-execute pipeline exposes repeated overhead from tool discovery, LLM planning, MCP tool execution, and final summarization. Existing LLM caching techniques such as KV-cache reuse and embedding-based semantic caching were designed for chatbot serving and break down when output validity depends on time, asset, or sensor parameters. We propose two complementary optimization layers for AOB plan-execute pipelines: a temporal semantic cache and a set of MCP workflow optimizations combining disk-backed tool-discovery caching and dependency-aware parallel step execution. MCP workflow optimizations corresponded to a 1.67x speedup and reduced median end-to-end latency by about 40.0% while the temporal-cache benchmark achieved a median of 30.6x speedup on cache hits. Beyond the speedup, our results expose a concrete failure mode of pure semantic caching for parameter-rich industrial queries, providing a critical analysis of how caching choices interact with evaluation correctness in MCP-backed agent benchmarks.

</details>


### [379] [Declarative Data Services: Structured Agentic Discovery for Composing Data Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20690)
*Shanshan Ye,Duo Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DDS is a structured, multi-layer architecture for agentic data-system discovery that imposes typed contracts to bound search, enabling convergent deployment on real data-system stacks where unbounded agentic search fails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unbounded agentic discovery struggles with heterogeneous search spaces, unreliable verification (whether a deployed stack runs), and uneven pretraining of composition knowledge; a bounded, typed approach is needed.

Method: Four layered contracts at successive levels: intent, operator DAG, per-system skills, and runtime attribution. Sub-agents search each space; the framework channels knowledge forward as inline skill citations and backward signals as typed error messages. The architecture orchestrates bounded sub-searches to assemble a working stack.

Result: On a trading-backend workload, DDS converges where unbounded discovery does not; runtime failures are transformed into skill patches that the next deployment cites inline.

Conclusion: DDS is an early prototype demonstrating that structured, declarative data-service discovery can yield practical convergence in real-world data-system composition and provides lessons for future work.

Abstract: Agentic discovery has shown that LLM-driven search can find novel algorithms, designs, and code under benchmark conditions. Translating the paradigm to multi-system data backends surfaces a harder problem: the search space is heterogeneous, the verifier is whether a deployed stack actually runs, and composition knowledge is unevenly captured in pretraining. Unbounded agentic discovery, a coding agent iterating on failure-log feedback, fails to converge consistently on a working stack even when iteration and explicit composition knowledge are added. We propose Declarative Data Services (DDS), an architecture for structured agentic discovery of data-system compositions from declarative user intent. The framework owns four typed contracts at successive layers (intent, operator DAG, per-system skills, runtime attribution) that decompose the global search into bounded sub-searches; sub-agents search each typed space, while the framework provides the channels by which knowledge flows forward as inline skill citations and errors route backward as typed signals. As a proof of life on a trading-backend workload, DDS converges where unbounded discovery does not; runtime failures become skill patches that the next deployment cites inline. We position this as an early prototype reporting lessons from real-world data-system composition.

</details>


### [380] [VBFDD-Agent for Electric Vehicle Battery Fault Detection and Diagnosis: Descriptive Text Modeling of Battery Digital Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20742)
*Joey Chan,Zhen Chen,Ershun Pan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A descriptive text modeling approach for battery fault reports enables an interpretable, maintenance-oriented diagnostic agent (VBFDD-Agent) that integrates textual representations, case retrieval, manuals, and LLM reasoning to perform accurate anomaly detection and provide actionable maintenance recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of open-source battery fault report corpora and unified knowledge representation, enabling cross-domain adaptability and human–AI collaboration in battery fault diagnosis and maintenance.

Method: Construct a descriptive textual corpus from monitoring signals, statistical features, anomaly records, and state assessments; develop VBFDD-Agent that fuses descriptive battery-state texts, historical case retrieval, local maintenance manuals, and large language model reasoning to produce structured diagnostic results and maintenance guidance.

Result: Experiments show accurate anomaly monitoring based on descriptive textual representations, with flexible, efficient, and actionable maintenance suggestions; expert evaluation confirms practical value of the recommendations.

Conclusion: VBFDD-Agent extends battery diagnosis from mere label prediction to interpretable, maintenance-oriented decision support.

Abstract: With the rapid proliferation of electric vehicles, the safety and reliability of lithium-ion batteries have become critical concerns. Effective anomaly detection is essential for ensuring safe battery operation. However, as battery systems and operating scenarios become increasingly complex, battery fault diagnosis and maintenance require stronger cross-domain adaptability and human-AI collaboration. Traditional fault detection and diagnosis methods are usually designed for specific scenarios and predefined workflows, making them less effective in complex real-world applications.
  To address the scarcity of open-source battery fault report corpora and the lack of unified maintenance knowledge representation, this study proposes a descriptive text modeling approach for battery signal reports. Monitoring signals, statistical features, anomaly records, and state assessment results are transformed into structured and readable natural language descriptions, forming a language corpus for battery health diagnosis and maintenance.
  Based on this corpus, we propose VBFDD-Agent, a vehicle battery fault detection and diagnosis agent for automotive-grade battery systems. VBFDD-Agent integrates descriptive battery-state texts, historical case retrieval, local maintenance manuals, and large language model reasoning to generate structured diagnostic results and maintenance recommendations. Experiments show that the proposed framework can accurately perform anomaly monitoring based on descriptive textual representations and provide flexible, efficient, and actionable maintenance suggestions. Expert evaluation further confirms the practical value of the generated recommendations. Overall, VBFDD-Agent extends traditional battery diagnosis from label prediction to interpretable and maintenance-oriented decision support.

</details>


### [381] [Conflict-Aware Additive Guidance for Flow Models under Compositional Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20758)
*Xuehui Yu,Fucheng Cai,Meiyi Wang,Xiaopeng Fan,Harold Soh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Conflict-Aware Additive Guidance (g^car) for inference-time constraint guidance in diffusion/flow models, aiming to fix off-manifold drift when combining multiple constraints without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current guidance methods degrade when composing multiple external constraints due to gradient misalignment, causing off-manifold drift and reduced fidelity. A lightweight, learnable solution is needed to robustly combine constraints at inference.

Method: Introduce g^car, a lightweight, learnable additive guidance that actively detects and resolves gradient conflicts during constraint composition, thereby rectifying off-manifold drift without fine-tuning.

Result: Empirical validation across synthetic data, image editing, and generative decision-making shows that g^car improves generation fidelity and robustness to multiple constraints, outperforming baselines with modest compute. Code released.

Conclusion: g^car provides robust, scalable maintenance of the data manifold under multiple constraints in diffusion/flow generation, enabling high-fidelity generation with inference-time constraint composition.

Abstract: Inference-time guided sampling steers state-of-the-art diffusion and flow models without fine-tuning by interpreting the generation process as a controllable trajectory. This provides a simple and flexible way to inject external constraints (e.g., cost functions or pre-trained verifiers) for controlled generation. However, existing methods often fail when composing multiple constraints simultaneously, which leads to deviations from the true data manifold. In this work, we identify root causes of this off-manifold drift and find that the approximation error scales severely with gradient misalignment. Building on these findings, we propose Conflict-Aware Additive Guidance ($g^\text{car}$), a lightweight and learnable method, which actively rectifies off-manifold drift by dynamically detecting and resolving gradient conflicts. We validate $g^\text{car}$ across diverse domains, ranging from synthetic datasets and image editing to generative decision-making for planning and control. Our results demonstrate that $g^\text{car}$ effectively rectifies off-manifold drift, surpassing baselines in generation fidelity while using light compute. Code is available at https://github.com/yuxuehui/CAR-guidance.

</details>


### [382] [Interaction Locality in Hierarchical Recursive Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20784)
*Yosuke Miyanishi,Tetsuro Morimura*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces interaction locality, a task-geometry-aware metric for whether information flow stays within nearby cells/segments or crosses them, as a fingerprint of recursive and embodied spatial reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
