Motivation: Bridge local execution and global planning in spatial reasoning by providing a reproducible measurement of information flow with respect to task geometry across recursive models and embodied agents.

Method: Instantiate the framework with sparse-autoencoder feature ablations and finite-noise activation patching; evaluate structural Jacobian and attention checks (reported in appendix); apply to HRM and TRM (Maze-Hard, Sudoku Extreme, ARC-AGI) and MTU3D for 3D scene grounding.

Result: Activation patching reveals a clear locality fingerprint: high-level recurrent states write information within nearby cells or same-segment units; recursive updates accumulate local writes into broader solution structure; strongest pattern in TRM; MTU3D shows locality mostly at the transition to the grounding module rather than throughout the visual encoder.

Conclusion: Interaction locality provides a reproducible framework to diagnose how recursive and embodied spatial reasoning organize local vs. global information flow, linking observed local-to-global handoffs to model architecture (recursive dynamics vs. module boundaries).

Abstract: Spatial reasoning requires both location-bound computation and location-invariant structure: agents must make local moves while preserving route, object, or constraint-level plans. We propose interaction locality, a task-geometry-aware framework for measuring whether information flow stays within nearby cells or semantic segments, or crosses them. We instantiate the framework with sparse-autoencoder feature ablations and finite-noise activation patching, with structural Jacobian and attention checks reported in the appendix, and apply it to HRM and TRM, two compact hierarchical and recursive reasoning models, on Maze-Hard, Sudoku Extreme, and ARC-AGI. Across these models, activation patching gives the clearest architectural fingerprint: high-level recurrent states tend to write information within nearby cells or same-segment units, while repeated recursive updates accumulate these local writes into broader solution structure. This pattern holds across maze paths, Sudoku constraints, and ARC-AGI object neighborhoods, with the strongest concentration in TRM. To test whether interaction locality extends beyond toy-yet-challenging grid benchmarks, we also apply it to MTU3D, a large-scale embodied 3D scene-grounding model. In this MTU3D setting, causal spatial locality appears primarily at the transition where visual scene features are handed to the downstream grounding module, rather than uniformly throughout the visual encoder. This contrast suggests that the local-to-global handoff observed in HRM and TRM is tied to explicit recursive reasoning dynamics, while embodied 3D models may concentrate causal spatial structure at module boundaries. Interaction locality turns the intuitive local-execution/global-planning story into a reproducible measurement framework for recursive and embodied spatial reasoning.

</details>


### [383] [Conditional Equivalence of DPO and RLHF: Implicit Assumption, Failure Modes, and Provable Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20834)
*Zhiqin Yang,Yonggang Zhang,Wei Xue,Dong Fang,Bo Han,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DPO is not universally equivalent to RLHF; the equivalence fails when the RLHF-optimal policy does not align with human preferences, causing DPO to optimize relative advantage rather than absolute alignment, potentially converging to dispreferred responses. The authors introduce Constrained Preference Optimization (CPO) to enforce alignment, provide a geometric soft-margin interpretation, and show CPO achieves state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks with available code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify when DPO's claimed equivalence to RLHF holds and identify failure modes where it can misalign with human preferences. Propose a corrective framework with provable alignment guarantees.

Method: Theoretical analysis of DPO vs RLHF under an implicit alignment assumption; characterize violation conditions and undesirable solution spaces; introduce Constrained Preference Optimization (CPO) by adding constraints to RLHF; interpret results via soft-margin ranking with possibly negative targets; comprehensive experiments on standard benchmarks to validate CPO.

Result: Demonstrates that the equivalence is conditional; shows existence of undesirable solution space where DPO and RLHF diverge; DPO can minimize loss while preferring dispreferred responses; CPO achieves provable alignment and state-of-the-art performance; code provided at the linked repository.

Conclusion: DPO's guarantees are limited to specific conditions; CPO offers a principled, constrained alternative that preserves simplicity and achieves strong empirical performance, with a clear geometric interpretation of the optimization landscape.

Abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) has emerged as a popular alternative to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), offering theoretical equivalence with simpler implementation. We prove this equivalence is conditional rather than universal, depending on an implicit assumption frequently violated in practice: the RLHF-optimal policy must prefer human-preferred responses. When this assumption fails, DPO optimizes relative advantage over the reference policy rather than absolute alignment with human preferences, leading to pathological convergence where policies decrease DPO loss while preferring dispreferred responses. We characterize when this assumption is violated, show the existence of an undesirable solution space, and prove that DPO and RLHF optimize fundamentally different objectives in such cases. To address this, we introduce Constrained Preference Optimization (CPO), augmenting RLHF with constraints for provable alignment. We further provide a geometric interpretation through soft margin ranking, revealing that DPO implements margin ranking with potentially negative targets. Our theoretical analysis establishes when DPOs' guarantees hold and provides solutions preserving simplicity with provable alignment. Comprehensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that CPO achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code is available at: https://github.com/visitworld123/CPO.

</details>


### [384] [PlanningBench: Generating Scalable and Verifiable Planning Data for Evaluating and Training Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20873)
*Ziliang Zhao,Zenan Xu,Shuting Wang,Hongjin Qian,Yan Lei,Minda Hu,Zhao Wang,Shihan Dou,Zhicheng Dou,Pluto Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PlanningBench is a scalable, controllable data-generation framework for planning in LLMs, enabling evaluation and training via a taxonomy-guided, constraint-driven synthesis pipeline with verification, showing current models struggle with coupled constraints but RL on verified data improves generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing planning benchmarks are fixed collections and fail to cover diverse scenarios, rely on surface proxies, and lack scalable generation and verification. There is a need for controllable, verifiable planning data to diagnose and improve LLM planning abilities.

Method: Develop PlanningBench: real planning scenarios → taxonomy of >30 task types, subtasks, constraints, difficulty factors; constraint-driven synthesis pipeline that instantiates self-contained planning problems with adaptive difficulty, quality filtering, and instance-level verification checklists; enable generation, evaluation, and training data; apply RL on PlanningBench data.

Result: Evaluated open-source and closed-source frontier LLMs; models struggle to produce complete solutions under coupled constraints; RL on PlanningBench improves performance on unseen planning benchmarks and broader instruction-following tasks; determinate/well-specified solutions yield clearer rewards and stable training.

Conclusion: PlanningBench provides a controllable, scalable data source for diagnosing and improving generalizable planning abilities in LLMs, enabling better evaluation and training.

Abstract: Planning is a fundamental capability for large language models (LLMs) because such complex tasks require models to coordinate goals, constraints, resources, and long-term consequences into executable and verifiable solutions. Existing planning benchmarks, however, usually treat planning data as fixed collections of instances rather than controllable generation targets. This limits scenario coverage, ties difficulty to surface-level proxies rather than structural sources, and offers limited support for scalable generation, automatic verification, or planning-oriented training. We introduce PlanningBench, a framework for generating scalable, diverse, and verifiable planning data for both evaluation and training. PlanningBench starts from real planning scenarios and abstracts practical workflows into a structured taxonomy of more than 30 task types, subtasks, constraint families, and difficulty factors. Guided by this taxonomy, a constraint-driven synthesis pipeline instantiates self-contained planning problems with adaptive difficulty control, quality filtering, and instance-level verification checklists. This shifts planning data construction from fixed benchmark collection to controllable generation while preserving realistic task grounding. We use PlanningBench to evaluate open-source and closed-source frontier LLMs, and find that current models still struggle to produce complete solutions under coupled constraints. Beyond evaluation, reinforcement learning on verified PlanningBench data improves performance on unseen planning benchmarks and broader instruction-following tasks. Further analysis suggests that determinate or well-specified optimal solutions provide clearer reward signals and more stable training dynamics. Overall, PlanningBench provides a controllable source of planning data for diagnosing and improving generalizable planning abilities in LLMs.

</details>


### [385] [Governance by Construction for Generalist Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20874)
*Segev Shlomov,Iftach Shoham,Alon Oved,Ido Levy,Sami Marreed,Harold Ship,Offer Akrabi,Sergey Zeltyn,Avi Yaeli,Nir Mashkif*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A modular policy-as-code governance layer (CUGA) for generalist LLM agents that enforces governance across an agent's execution with five checkpoints (Intent Guard, Playbook, Tool Guide, Tool Approvals, Output Formatter) to deliver auditable, compliant, and safer behavior without model fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Production-grade autonomous agents must operate within constraints across tools and interfaces, guaranteeing what actions are allowed, when human oversight is required, and what information is exposed, while remaining domain-agnostic and deployable without retraining.

Method: Introduce a runtime governance architecture—the policy system CUGA—that interposes at five structural checkpoints inside the agent pipeline. It uses dynamic playbook injection, intent guards, tool-guides, human-in-the-loop approvals, and output formatting to enforce structured tool usage and safe reasoning, integrated with a generalist LLM agent without fine-tuning.

Result: Demonstrates in a healthcare scenario that dynamic policy injection and multi-layered interventions improve policy adherence and execution consistency, enable faster safe deployment, and provide auditable governance through typed primitives and structured tool sequences.

Conclusion: Embedding governance across the agent execution pipeline yields predictable, auditable, and compliant enterprise agentic systems, enabling safer deployment and continuous policy adherence without per-domain model retraining.

Abstract: Enterprise agents are increasingly expected to operate autonomously across tools and interfaces, yet production deployments require governance by construction. Systems must specify which actions are allowed, when human oversight is required, and what information may be exposed, without rebuilding the agent for each domain. This demo presents CUGA's policy system, a modular policy-as-code layer that composes with a generalist LLM agent to deliver predictable, auditable, and compliance-aware behavior in compound workflows without model fine-tuning. We present a runtime governance architecture that enforces policy interventions at every critical stage of execution. Rather than passively constraining behavior, policies intercept the agent at five structural checkpoints: upstream of planning (Intent Guard), within the system prompt to steer reasoning (Playbook), at the tool-call boundary to enforce proper usage (Tool Guide), outside the reasoning loop as a Human-in-the-Loop gate for high-risk actions (Tool Approvals), and at the output stage to filter and structure the final response (Output Formatter). Together, these stages embed governance continuously across the agent's execution pipeline rather than treating it as an afterthought. Using a healthcare scenario and a multi-layered enforcement intervention, the demo shows dynamic playbook injection for structured tool-sequence enforcement, intent guards that block malicious or accidental harmful requests, and human-in-the-loop tool approval checkpoints for potentially destructive actions. The artifact illustrates how typed governance primitives enable faster, safer deployment of enterprise agentic systems while improving policy adherence and execution consistency.

</details>


### [386] [For How Long Should We Be Punching? Learning Action Duration in Fighting Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20911)
*Hoang Hai Nguyen,Kurt Driessens,Dennis J. N. J. Soemers*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Jointly learning action and duration enables adaptive responsiveness in fighting-game RL; achieves competitive results to hand-tuned frame skips but lacks robustness and tends to favor high frame-skip, repetitive strategies against scripted bots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the fixed decision-interval limitation in real-time, fast-paced games by enabling the agent to decide both what to do and for how long, aiming for human-like timing and improved efficiency.

Method: Train agents in FightLadder to predict (action, duration) pairs; evaluate across various fixed frame-skip settings; compare against scripted built-in bots; analyze performance, responsiveness, and learned behavior; observe robustness and exploitable patterns.

Result: Learned timing can match performance of well-chosen fixed frame skips and promote repeatable action patterns; does not inherently guarantee robustness; in most cases, high frame skip (low responsiveness) yields best performance, enabling exploitative, repeated actions against scripted bots.

Conclusion: Learning action duration is a viable alternative to fixed frame skips but does not by itself ensure robustness; high-skip strategies can emerge, suggesting future work to enhance generalization and resistance to exploitation, possibly via diverse opponents or regularization.

Abstract: Fighting games such as Street Fighter II present unique challenges to reinforcement learning (RL) agents due to their fast-paced, real-time nature. In most RL frameworks, agents are hard-coded to make decisions at a fixed interval, typically every frame or every N frames. Although this design ensures timely responses, it restricts the agent's ability to adjust its reaction timing. Acting every frame grants frame-perfect reflexes, which are unrealistic compared to human players, whereas longer fixed intervals reduce computational cost but hinder responsiveness. We consider an alternative decision-making framework in which the agent learns not only what action to take but also for how long to execute it. By jointly predicting both action and duration, the agent can dynamically adapt its responsiveness to different situations in the game. We implement this method using the open-source FightLadder environment with agents trained against scripted built-in bots, systematically testing different frame skip configurations to analyze their influence on performance, responsiveness, and learned behavior. Experiments show that learned timing can match the performance of well-chosen fixed frame skips and encourages repeatable action patterns, but does not ensure robustness on its own. In most cases, we see agents performing best with consistently high frame skip values (i.e., low responsiveness). This strategy makes it easier to learn exploitative strategies where the same action is repeated over and over, which the scripted bots appear to be susceptible to.

</details>


### [387] [Playing Devil's Advocate: Off-the-Shelf Persona Vectors Rival Targeted Steering for Sycophancy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21006)
*Ishaan Kelkar,Nebras Alam,Vikram Kakaria,Madhur Panwar,Vasu Sharma,Maheep Chaudhary*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Off-the-shelf persona steering vectors can mitigate model sycophancy as well as, and in some cases nearly as well as, Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), with the effect being persona-level rather than a single steerable direction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sycophancy—the tendency of models to agree with users when they are wrong—remains a concern in instruction-tuned models. The study investigates whether generic persona vectors (trained for role-play) can substitute for label-based CAA in reducing sycophancy.

Method: Two instruction-tuned models are steered with off-the-shelf persona vectors representing doubt/scrutiny or agreement, and compared to CAA. The study measures reductions in sycophancy relative to CAA (to ~0.68x and ~0.98x of CAA's effect), while checking accuracy when the user is correct. It also analyzes asymmetry (agreeable personas) and the geometric relationship between persona vectors and sycophancy directions in activation space; code is released.

Result: Steering toward doubt or scrutiny personas reduces sycophancy to about 68% and 98% of CAA's effect, respectively, and unlike CAA, preserves accuracy when the user is correct. The effect is asymmetric: steering toward agreeable personas does not mirror the reduction. Geometrically, the persona vector is largely independent of the direction of sycophancy in activation space. Overall, sycophancy appears as a persona-level property rather than a single steerable direction.

Conclusion: Sycophancy is better understood as a persona-level phenomenon. Off-the-shelf persona steering can be a viable alternative to CAA, with robustness to correctness and asymmetric effects across persona types. The work suggests broader design principles for mitigating sycophancy through persona alignment and highlights the importance of evaluating the geometry of activation-space directions.

Abstract: We study the effect of different persona on \textbf{sycophancy}: model's agreement with users even when the user is incorrect. The standard mitigation, Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), derives a steering direction from labelled pairs of sycophantic and honest responses. This study evaluates whether off-the-shelf persona steering vectors, originally developed for general role-playing and not trained on sycophancy data, can serve as an alternative. In two instruction-tuned models, steering toward personas characterised by doubt or scrutiny reduces sycophancy to approximately $68\%$ and $98\%$ of CAA's effect, and, unlike CAA, maintains accuracy when the user is correct. The effect is also asymmetric: steering toward agreeable personas does not produce a mirror increase in sycophancy. Geometrically, the persona vector is largely independent of the direction of sycophancy in activation space. Collectively, these findings suggest that sycophancy is better understood as a persona-level property rather than a single steerable direction. We release our code here: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Sycophancy-Steering-9DF0/.

</details>


### [388] [AutoRPA: Efficient GUI Automation through LLM-Driven Code Synthesis from Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21082)
*Minghao Chen,Xinyi Hu,Zhou Yu,Yufei Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoRPA distills ReAct-style LLM decision logic into reusable, efficient RPA functions for GUI tasks, using a translator-builder pipeline and a hybrid repair strategy, achieving major token reductions and improved runtime and reusability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Repeated multi-step GUI tasks with LLM reasoning are inefficient; traditional RPA is fast but requires heavy manual development. Bridging the two can yield efficient, maintainable automation.

Method: Two-stage translator-builder pipeline: (1) a translator converts hard-coded ReAct actions into soft-coded procedures; (2) a builder synthesizes robust RPA functions via retrieval-augmented generation over multiple trajectories. A hybrid repair strategy during verification combines RPA execution with ReAct-based fallback for iterative refinement.

Result: RPA functions generated by AutoRPA solve similar tasks across GUI environments, with token usage reduced by 82%–96%, and substantial improvements in runtime efficiency and reusability.

Conclusion: AutoRPA effectively distills LLM-based decision logic into robust, reusable RPA functions, reducing dependence on repeated LLM reasoning and manual RPA development, with strong potential for scalable GUI automation.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) based agents have demonstrated proficiency in multi-step interactions with graphical user interfaces (GUIs). While most research focuses on improving single-task performance, practical scenarios often involve repetitive GUI tasks for which invoking LLM reasoning repeatedly, i.e., the ReAct paradigm, is inefficient. Prior to LLMs, traditional Robotic Process Automation (RPA) offers runtime efficiency but demands significant manual effort to develop and maintain. To bridge this gap, we propose AutoRPA, a framework that automatically distills the decision logic of ReAct-style agents into robust RPA functions. AutoRPA introduces two core innovations: (1) A translator-builder pipeline, where a translator agent converts hard-coded ReAct actions into soft-coded procedures, and a builder agent synthesizes robust RPA functions via retrieval-augmented generation over multiple trajectories; (2) A hybrid repair strategy during code verification, combining RPA execution with ReAct-based fallback for iterative refinement. Experiments across multiple GUI environments demonstrate that RPA functions generated by AutoRPA successfully solve similar tasks while reducing token usage by 82% to 96%, significantly improving runtime efficiency and reusability.

</details>


### [389] [ScenePilot: Controllable Boundary-Driven Critical Scenario Generation for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21168)
*Qiyu Ruan,Yuxuan Wang,He Li,Zhenning Li,Cheng-zhong Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ScenePilot introduces a feasibility-guided, boundary-driven framework for generating physically solvable yet failure-causing driving scenarios. It uses constrained multi-objective RL with an RSS-based feasibility score and an online AV-risk predictor, plus shielding to stay near the feasibility boundary. It yields higher collision rates on SafeBench across planners and enables adversarial fine-tuning to reduce downstream crashes; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety-critical evaluation of autonomous driving requires stress scenarios that are both physically plausible and challenging. Prior methods either yield physically unrealizable crashes or focus on controller limits, leaving a gap for boundary-band scenarios where the system fails despite feasible conditions.

Method: Formulated as constrained multi-objective reinforcement learning. Combines an RSS-derived physical-feasibility score σ with an online-learned AV-risk predictor Φ to drive scenario generation. Introduces step-level feasibility-aware shielding to keep exploration near the physical feasibility boundary and avoid infeasible artifacts.

Result: On SafeBench with multiple planners, ScenePilot achieves substantially higher collision rates (+6.2 percentage points) while maintaining physical validity. Adversarial fine-tuning on boundary-band scenarios consistently reduces downstream crash rates.

Conclusion: ScenePilot effectively identifies and exploits the boundary between physically feasible and failure-inducing scenarios, enabling robust stress testing and vulnerability mitigation for autonomous driving stacks; code is publicly available for replication.

Abstract: Safety-critical scenarios are central to evaluating autonomous driving systems, yet their rarity in naturalistic logs makes simulation-based stress testing indispensable. Most scenario generation methods treat surrounding agents as adversaries, but they either (i) induce failures without explicitly modeling vehicle-road physical limits, yielding visually extreme yet physically unsolvable crashes, or (ii) enforce physical feasibility or policy feasibility in isolation, which can over-focus on aggressive maneuvers or remain tied to a controller-dependent capability boundary. We propose ScenePilot, a feasibility-guided, boundary-driven framework that targets the boundary band: scenarios that are physically solvable in principle yet still cause the deployed autonomy stack to fail. We formulate generation as constrained multi-objective reinforcement learning, combining an RSS-derived physical-feasibility score $σ$ with an online-learned AV-risk predictor $Φ$, and introduce step-level feasibility-aware shielding to keep exploration near the feasibility boundary while avoiding infeasible artifacts. Experiments on SafeBench with multiple planners show that ScenePilot yields substantially higher collision rates (+6.2 percentage points) while preserving physical validity, and that adversarial fine-tuning on these boundary-band scenarios consistently reduces downstream crash rates. The code is available at https://github.com/QiyuRuan/ScenePilot.

</details>


### [390] [Insights Generator: Systematic Corpus-Level Trace Diagnostics for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21347)
*Akshay Manglik,Apaar Shanker,Kaustubh Deshpande,Jason Qin,Yash Maurya,Veronica Chatrath,Vijay S. Kalmath,Levi Lentz,Yuan,Xue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Insights Generator (IG) enables corpus-level trace diagnostics for LLM agents via a multi-agent scout-investigator architecture to produce evidence-backed, natural-language insights across trace groups; it improves downstream scaffold and coding-agent performance and achieves competitive detection coverage with high evidence quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual trace inspection relies on small trace subsets, misses cross-trace patterns, and scales poorly to long production traces; a corpus-level diagnostic framework is needed to reveal systematic behavioral patterns and provide grounded insights.

Method: IG proposes and tests hypotheses across a corpus of execution traces using a scout-investigator multi-agent architecture; it generates natural-language insights grounded in evidence, producing an evidence-backed insights report; evaluation includes rubric-based report assessment and downstream performance improvements; comparison across human-expert and coding-agent deployments.

Result: Human experts using IG reports improve scaffold performance by 30.4 percentage points over the unmodified baseline scaffold; coding agents leveraging IG-derived insights show consistent and stable gains; IG’s scout-investigator architecture achieves detection coverage comparable to competing approaches; domain experts rate IG reports as having leading depth and evidence quality.

Conclusion: IG provides scalable, evidence-backed corpus-level trace diagnostics that translate to measurable downstream performance gains and are perceived as high-quality by domain experts.

Abstract: Diagnosing failures in LLM agents remains largely manual. Practitioners inspect a small subset of execution traces, form ad-hoc hypotheses, and iterate. This process misses patterns that only emerge across trace populations and does not scale to production corpora where individual traces span tens of thousands of tokens. We formalize the problem of corpus-level trace diagnostics. Given a corpus of execution traces, the goal is to produce grounded natural-language insights that characterize systematic behavioral patterns across trace groups, each linked to supporting evidence. We present the Insights Generator (IG), a multi-agent system that answers diagnostic questions by proposing and testing hypotheses across the trace corpus to produce an evidence-backed insights report. We evaluate IG across qualitative and objective dimensions, spanning rubric-based report assessment and downstream performance improvements achieved by implementing IG insights. Human experts using IG reports improve scaffold performance by 30.4pp over the unmodified baseline scaffold, and coding agents leveraging IG-derived insights show consistent and stable gains. Across benchmarks, IG's scout-investigator architecture produces findings comparable in detection coverage to competing approaches, while domain experts rated IG reports as leading depth and evidence quality.

</details>


### [391] [Towards Resilient and Autonomous Networks: A BlueSky Vision on AI-Native 6G](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21395)
*Liang Wu,Kelly Wan,Mayank Darbari,Liangjie Hong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A BlueSky vision for 6G where AI is native, anchored by a foundation model and multi-agent orchestration, enabling autonomous, self-sustaining networks with two key directions: a unified backbone foundation model with edge-friendly distillation, and autonomous multi-agent diagnosis/maintenance/recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Escalating demand from autonomous driving and immersive experiences requires resilient, intelligent networks; 5G relies on scattered models not suited for multi-task, multi-modal optimization; need for AI-native networks that can self-manage.

Method: Proposes BlueSky vision: shift from Network for AI to AI for Network; leverage a core foundation model as backbone; use multi-agent systems to coordinate tasks; two directions: foundation model with distillation, and self-governing multi-agent diagnosis/maintenance/recovery; emphasis on edge deployment and multi-task optimization.

Result: Outlines a roadmap with two transformative directions; a unified backbone with compact edge models; autonomous agents for diagnosis/maintenance/recovery; integrates multi-modal data; aims for self-sustaining 6G.

Conclusion: 6G becomes intelligent, self-sustaining; AI-native networks; foundation-model-driven backbone; distributed autonomy; substantial shift from 5G.

Abstract: The proliferation of emerging applications, such as autonomous driving and immersive experiences, demands cellular networks that are not only faster, but fundamentally more resilient and autonomous. This paper presents a BlueSky vision on how Artificial Intelligence will be natively integrated into 6G, shifting the paradigm from \underline{Network for AI} to \underline{AI for Network}. We envision that, unlike 5G's reliance on scattered, ad-hoc models each trained for a single task, native AI in the 6G era will be anchored by a foundation model and and orchestrated via collaborative multi-agent systems, framing network management as a unified, multi-modal, multi-task optimization problem. Built on this vision, we outline two transformative directions. The first focuses on developing a 6G foundation model as a unified backbone, with task-specific knowledge distilled into compact models suited for diverse edge deployments. The second advances multi-agent systems designed to autonomously diagnose, maintain, and recover networks with minimal human intervention. These directions chart a roadmap for 6G to evolve into an intelligent, self-sustaining communication infrastructure.

</details>


### [392] [Teaching AI Through Benchmark Construction: QuestBench as a Course-Based Practice for Accountable Knowledge Work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21413)
*Haiyang Shen,Jiuzheng Wang,Taian Guo,Mugeng Liu,Wenchun Jing,Chongyang Pan,Siqi Zhong,Zhiyang Chen,Weichen Bi,Yudong Han,Xiaoying Bai,Yun Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A course-based approach to teaching AI through benchmark construction (QuestBench) that trains students to test and judge AI outputs by creating verifiable, expert-level benchmark questions across disciplines; evaluation reveals systemic failures in current models and fosters responsible knowledge stewardship.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move AI education beyond using AI as a productivity tool toward critical evaluation and verification, enabling students to become responsible knowledge actors who understand what trustworthy AI answers require.

Method: A course-based practice where students translate disciplinary knowledge into verifiable questions, peer-review designs for ambiguity/shortcuts, and evaluate AI systems on the tasks; development of QuestBench, a 256-question benchmark spanning 14 humanities/social-science domains.

Result: Evaluation on 13 AI systems shows a mean question-level pass rate of 16.85%, with the best system (GPT-5.5) achieving 57.58%; the results reveal hidden failures where fluent, source-backed answers miss the correct query, source, term, or evidence standard; five student reflections suggest professional knowledge can be a basis for judging AI outputs.

Conclusion: QuestBench serves as both a reusable benchmark artifact and a classroom setting to educate students on maintaining responsible knowledge sovereignty in the AI era, highlighting the need to align AI outputs with rigorous knowledge standards.

Abstract: As AI becomes part of everyday learning, many courses teach students to use it mainly as a productivity tool: how to prompt, search, summarize, write, code, and use tools more efficiently. We argue that AI education also needs a setting in which students learn to test AI and understand their own role in judging machine-produced knowledge. To this end, we introduce a course-based practice that teaches AI through benchmark construction, using deep research systems as a concrete example of AI-era knowledge work. Students turn disciplinary knowledge into verifiable expert-level questions, review one another's designs for ambiguity and shortcuts, and evaluate AI systems on the resulting tasks. This activity gives students direct exposure to a powerful tool while asking them to specify what a trustworthy answer would require. The produced benchmark, QuestBench, consists of 256 questions across 14 humanities and social-science domains. Evaluation on QuestBench shows that student-designed tasks reveal hidden failures in current deep research systems: across thirteen evaluated systems, the mean question-level pass rate is only 16.85%, and the best-performing system, GPT-5.5, reaches a 57.58% pass rate. The failures are educationally useful because they show how fluent, source-backed answers can still miss the right query, source, term, or evidence standard. Reflections from five student contributors suggest that benchmark construction can help students see professional knowledge not only as content AI may retrieve, but as the basis for judging AI outputs. We present QuestBench as a benchmark artifact and as a reusable classroom setting for a larger educational question: how students can remain responsible knowledge actors as AI enters learning and professional work. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/PKUAIWeb/QuestBench/tree/main.

</details>


### [393] [PALS: Power-Aware LLM Serving for Mixture-of-Experts Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21427)
*Can Hankendi,Rana Shahout,Minlan Yu,Ayse K. Coskun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PALS introduces a power-aware runtime for LLM inference that treats GPU power caps as a controllable knob, jointly optimizing power caps with batch size to improve energy efficiency while meeting throughput targets; validated in vLLM on multi-GPU dense and MoE models, achieving up to 26.3% energy savings and 4x–7x reduction in QoS violations under power constraints without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GPU power is a dynamic, controllable resource that significantly impacts energy use and efficiency in real-time LLM serving. Traditional systems treat power as a static constraint, leaving potential energy savings untapped. A runtime that actively manages power can improve energy proportionality and grid responsiveness without sacrificing performance.

Method: Develop PALS, a power-aware runtime integrated into the vLLM serving framework. Use lightweight offline power–performance models and a feedback-driven controller to jointly adjust GPU power caps and software parameters (e.g., batch size) to meet throughput targets while maximizing energy efficiency. Validate on multi-GPU setups with dense and mixture-of-experts models; no model retraining or API changes required.

Result: Energy efficiency improved by up to 26.3%. QoS violations under power constraints reduced by 4x–7x. Demonstrates ability to track dynamic power budgets and apply power control across both dense and MoE models in multi-GPU systems.

Conclusion: Integrating power control into LLM inference runtimes is viable and beneficial, enabling energy-proportional and grid-interactive AI systems without retraining. PALS demonstrates a practical approach to dynamically allocate power to meet performance targets while reducing energy use.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) inference has become a dominant workload in modern data centers, driving significant GPU utilization and energy consumption. While prior systems optimize throughput and latency by batching, scheduling, and parallelism, they largely treat GPU power as a static constraint rather than a controllable resource. In this paper, we present a power-aware runtime for LLM serving, PALS, that treats GPU power caps as a first-class control knob and jointly optimizes them with software parameters such as batch size. The system combines lightweight offline power-performance models with a feedback-driven controller to select configurations that satisfy throughput targets while maximizing energy efficiency. We implement PALS within an existing LLM serving framework, vLLM, demonstrating that it requires no model retraining or API changes. Across multi-GPU systems and both dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) models, PALS improves energy efficiency by up to 26.3%, reduces QoS violations by 4x to 7x under power constraints, and tracks dynamic power budgets. These results highlight the potential of integrating power control directly into LLM inference runtimes, enabling energy-proportional and grid-interactive AI systems.

</details>


### [394] [Mind the Sim-to-Real Gap & Think Like a Scientist](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21458)
*Harsh Parikh,Gabriel Levin-Konigsberg,Dominique Perrault-Joncas,Alexander Volfovsky*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework for supplementing a biased simulator with experiments to optimize sequential policies; decomposes simulator error, separates value gap into local and reachability components, and introduces Fisher-SEP for variance-minimizing simulation-aided experimentation; illustrated on a vending-machine supply chain and HIV testing scenario.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world sequential decision problems suffer from simulator calibration drift; experiments are unbiased but costly; a principled method is needed to combine them efficiently.

Method: Extended simulation lemma; calibration–deployment shift identified via randomization; residual parametric component; decomposition of value gap; Fisher-SEP minimizing posterior predictive variance; specializations for reward-only and transition-only; two case studies.

Result: Value error splits into identifiable calibration–deployment shift plus irreducible residual; value gap splits into local and reachability components; reachability bounded away from zero without exploratory experimentation; Fisher-SEP reduces posterior variance; empirical regimes: front-loaded experimentation helps with long horizon; designed exploration needed to reach poorly surveilled regions.

Conclusion: Provides a principled, data-efficient approach to simulation-aided experimentation in sequential decision problems, with clear regimes and practical guidelines for when to invest in experiments and how to design exploration.

Abstract: Suppose a planner has a pre-trained simulator of a sequential decision problem and the option to run real experiments in the field. The simulator is cheap to query but inherits confounding and drift from its calibration data. Experimentation is unbiased but consumes one real unit per trial. We study when, and how, the planner should supplement the simulator with experiments. We give three results. First, an extended simulation lemma decomposes the simulator's value error into a calibration--deployment shift that randomization can identify and a parametric residual that no further interaction can reduce. Second, the value gap between the simulator-optimal policy and the optimum splits into a local component, on states the deployed policy already visits, and a reachability component, on states it does not. The reachability component stays bounded away from zero at any horizon under purely passive learning. Third, we propose Fisher-SEP, a simulation-aided experimental policy (SEP) that minimizes the posterior predictive variance of a target policy's value, with reward-only and transition-only specializations. Two case studies illustrate the regimes. In a vending-machine supply chain, front-loaded experimentation overtakes posterior updating once the horizon is long enough to amortize the pilot. In an HIV mobile-testing example with a corridor that separates a well-surveilled region from a poorly-surveilled one, only designed exploration reaches the poorly-surveilled region.

</details>


### [395] [AiraXiv: An AI-Driven Open-Access Platform for Human and AI Scientists](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21481)
*Junshu Pan,Panzhong Lu,Yixuan Weng,Qiyao Sun,Fang Guo,Zijie Yang,Qiji Zhou,Yue Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes AiraXiv, an AI-driven, open-access publishing platform where humans and AIs co-author and iteratively refine papers; validated by real-world deployment as ICAIS 2025 submission platform; aims for fast, inclusive, scalable AI-era publishing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rising AI-generated and human research outputs strain traditional publishing systems, increasing submissions, reviewer workload, and venue sizes; need continuous, feedback-driven publishing with AI-assisted analysis and review.

Method: Development of AiraXiv built on open preprints, AI-augmented analysis and review, and a Model Context Protocol (MCP) for AI interactions; provides an interactive UI for human researchers and AI-enabled workflows; real-world deployment as the ICAIS 2025 submission platform.

Result: Demonstrated real-world deployment as ICAIS 2025 submission platform; evidence of fast, inclusive, and scalable research infrastructure in the AI era.

Conclusion: An AI-era publishing paradigm enabling iterative, feedback-driven paper evolution with human and AI collaboration; AiraXiv is a viable, publicly available platform with potential to transform scholarly publishing, pending broader validation.

Abstract: Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) have accelerated the growth of both human-authored and AI-generated research outputs, placing increasing strain on traditional academic publishing systems and challenging the scalability of conference- and journal-centered paradigms amid rising submission volumes, reviewer workload, and venue size. To address these challenges, we explore an AI-era publishing paradigm in which both human and AI scientists participate as authors and readers, and papers evolve through continuous, feedback-driven iteration. We propose AiraXiv, an AI-driven open-access platform built on open preprints, AI-augmented analysis and review, and reader feedback. AiraXiv supports human scientists through an interactive UI and AI scientists through Model Context Protocol (MCP)-based interactions. We validate AiraXiv through real-world deployments, including serving as the submission platform for ICAIS 2025, demonstrating its potential as a fast, inclusive, and scalable research infrastructure for the AI era. AiraXiv is publicly available at https://airaxiv.com.

</details>


### [396] [DeepWeb-Bench: A Deep Research Benchmark Demanding Massive Cross-Source Evidence and Long-Horizon Derivation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21482)
*Sixiong Xie,Zhuofan Shi,Haiyang Shen,Jiuzheng Wang,Siqi Zhong,Mugeng Liu,Chongyang Pan,Peilun Jia,Baoqing Sun,Xiang Jing,Yun Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DeepWeb-Bench is a deep-research benchmark for frontier language models, emphasizing large-scale evidence gathering, cross-source reconciliation, and long-horizon derivation; it introduces four capability families and source-provenance auditing, and reveals distinct failure modes and domain specialization across nine frontier models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To distinguish frontier model capabilities beyond existing benchmarks by stressing multi-source retrieval, derivation, reasoning, and calibration in long-horizon tasks with verifiable provenance.

Method: Define four capability axes (Retrieval, Derivation, Reasoning, Calibration) as task dimensions; construct tasks demanding massive evidence collection, cross-source reconciliation, and long-horizon inference; annotate tasks with source provenance and disclosure levels; evaluate nine frontier models; analyze error causes and cross-model agreement.

Result: Key findings: retrieval errors are 12-14% of failures; derivation and calibration account for >70%; strong vs weak models fail differently (incomplete derivation vs hallucinated precision); across-domain specialization with low cross-model agreement (rho=0.61) and per-case disagreement up to 18.8 percentage points; public release with data, rubrics, and evaluation code.

Conclusion: DeepWeb-Bench advances evaluation by challenging current frontier models with evidence-intensive, provenance-audited tasks, revealing distinct error modes and domain specialization; results imply focus on improving derivation and calibration, and support broader, auditable benchmarking among frontier models.

Abstract: Deep research, in which an agent searches the open web, collects evidence, and derives an answer through extended reasoning, is a prominent use case for frontier language models. Frontier deep research products score high on existing benchmarks, making it difficult to distinguish their capabilities from current evaluation data alone. We introduce DeepWeb-Bench, a deep research benchmark that is substantially harder than existing benchmarks for the current frontier. Difficulty comes from three properties of the data itself: each task requires massive evidence collection, cross-source reconciliation, and long-horizon multi-step derivation. We represent these three sources of difficulty as four capability families (Retrieval, Derivation, Reasoning, and Calibration) and report results sliced by family. Every reference answer is accompanied by a source-provenance record with four disclosure levels and cross-source checks where available, making scores easier to audit against the underlying evidence. We evaluate DeepWeb-Bench on nine frontier models and report three findings: (1) retrieval is not the bottleneck, as retrieval failures account for only 12-14% of errors while derivation and calibration failures account for over 70%; (2) strong and weak models fail in qualitatively different ways, with strong models' errors dominated by incomplete derivation and weak models' by hallucinated precision; and (3) models exhibit genuine specialization across domains, with cross-model agreement of only rho = 0.61 and per-case disagreement reaching 18.8 percentage points. The public benchmark release includes the data, rubrics, and evaluation code.

</details>
